answer_text: I refer the Member to my answer to AQW 43567/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the impact of recent fuel cost increases on Translink.
answer_text: Recent fuel cost increases have not had a direct operational impact on Translink due to the company’s ongoing approach to managing market volatility. Translink regularly reviews its fuel requirements, operating assumptions and hedging arrangements to minimise exposure to fluctuating fuel prices. It currently has 80% of its diesel requirement for 2026/27 hedged, providing cost certainty to an extent and delivering an estimated £7 million in cost savings if the hedge was not in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the work being carried out to improve footpath surfaces in and around Derry City Centre.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether any assessment has been made of any savings realised by local councils that have adopted council residents-only policies at their recycling centres.
answer_text: Local councils have operational responsibility for the management of their own Household Recycling Centres and policies. My Department does not hold information on savings realised by councils and has made no such assessment.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans her Department has to increase the frequency of trains serving the North West Transport Hub on Sundays.
answer_text: I fully recognise the need to address regional imbalance and better connect our communities including those within the Northwest. I have received the business case for the enhanced services and will consider these as part of my budget decisions on the Department’s Final Budget for this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any work being undertaken on the rehabilitation of perpetrators of domestic abuse.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS), in supporting the rehabilitation of offenders, recognises the need to offer offence specific interventions.  In relation to domestic abuse, NIPS Psychology Services deliver the Building Better Relationships (BBR) programme, which is designed for individuals convicted of intimate partner violence. In addition, counselling sessions are offered to individuals in custody who have disclosed experiences of sexual or domestic abuse. Relationship-focused interventions are also delivered in partnership with the Probation Board for Northern Ireland (PBNI). These interventions are provided through a combination of one-to-one and group-based formats.Beyond NIPS Psychology-led provision, PBNI delivers Respectful Relationships interventions, while Prisoner Development Units facilitate White Ribbon programmes aimed at challenging attitudes and behaviours associated with domestic abuse. NIPS also provides funding to Nexus to deliver one-to-one counselling sessions for individuals in custody who have disclosed sexual or domestic abuse.In 2025/26, NIPS benefited from funding through the Domestic Sexual Abuse Strategy (DSA) which supported the delivery of four Safety in Relationships programmes for perpetrators of domestic abuse. These programmes focus on accountability, power and control, emotional regulation, healthy communications, empathy and planning for non-violent futures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any engagement that has taken place with the Public Prosecution Service and the Police Service of Northern Ireland, to improve evidence gathering in domestic abuse cases.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of robust evidence gathering in domestic abuse cases, particularly given the complex and often hidden nature of these offences.The investigation of offences and the gathering of evidence are, as you will be aware, operational matters for the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI). Similarly, the independent Public Prosecution Service for Northern Ireland (PPS) is responsible for bringing cases to court and all matters relevant to the prosecution of cases.  As indicated in AQW 43352/22-27 and AQW 43471/22-27, both the PSNI and PPS operate independently within their respective functions. The Member may, therefore, wish to write directly to those organisations on these matters.However, my Department has regular engagement with colleagues in the PPS and PSNI in relation to domestic abuse and, in addition, has submitted a bid to the Transformation Fund in relation to a package of measures that would support work to end violence against women and girls by tackling domestic and sexual abuse.  The bid includes funding for a number of PSNI-led initiatives that would support the operational response to domestic and sexual abuse.  We await a final decision on funding allocations related to this bid.I will continue to work with partners across the justice system to help ensure a robust and effective response to domestic abuse.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any targeted interventions being developed to address the high levels of child poverty in the Derry City and Strabane area, where rates remain at 24 per cent; and (ii) how these will be prioritised within the forthcoming Anti-Poverty Strategy.
answer_text: While the proportion of children in Derry City and Strabane in relative poverty (after housing costs) has decreased from 24% in 2023/24 to 23% based on the most recent figures from 2024/25, there is still much more work to be done and there are a number of interventions, both in operation and in development within my Department that will impact directly and indirectly on tackling child poverty.  Through the Community Support Programme, DfC funds councils to provide community based generalist advice that meets local need. In 2025/26, Derry City and Strabane District Council (DCSDC) received approximately £711k for advice services. This includes the additional provision funded under Welfare Mitigations measures, to provide extra support for people impacted by Welfare changes. DCSDC match fund the core advice grant by approximately £274k. Advice Northwest are sub-contracted by Advice NI to deliver the face-to-face NI Debt Advice Service in the council.In addition to this, DfC launched an Integrated Advice Partnership Fund in November 2024 with the objective to improve the uptake of free, independent regulated debt advice through a collaborative partnership approach. The programme has recently been extended to March 2027 and the Department will provide DCSDC with approximately £39k to fund initiatives under this programme in 2026/27 in addition to the £151k received across 2024-2026.DfC also funds Social Supermarket projects, with £399k allocated to DCSDC in 2025/26, offering food support and wraparound assistance to address the underlying issues contributing to financial hardship.In tackling child poverty, we must focus on lifting families out of poverty and ensuring our young people have the best possible start in life irrespective of their socio-economic status. Final decisions on the content and implementation of the Anti-Poverty Strategy will be subject to Executive agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether there are any plans to enhance training or guidance for investigators and prosecutors in handling domestic abuse cases, with a view to strengthening case files and increasing prosecution rates.
answer_text: The provision of training and operational guidance for investigators and prosecutors is a matter for the independent Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) and the Public Prosecution Service for Northern Ireland (PPS). It would therefore not be appropriate for me to comment on the training delivered by those organisations or the guidance used.However, I am committed to ensuring that the justice system is equipped to respond effectively to domestic abuse. My Department has introduced a range of measures to support this, including the domestic abuse offence under the Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (Northern Ireland) 2021, which is supported by statutory training requirements for relevant criminal justice agencies.Under that Act, the PSNI, PPS and other specified bodies are required to provide training to ensure a clear understanding of domestic abuse, including coercive and controlling behaviour and its impact on victims. While prosecution decisions remain a matter for the independent PPS, I will continue to work with partners to ensure that victims are supported and that the system as a whole is as effective as possible in delivering justice.Separately, my Department is also leading work, with partners, to replace the existing Multi-Agency Risk Assessment Conference (MARAC) arrangements with a new Domestic Violence & Abuse Risk Response (DVARR) model.  The focus of the model is on protection and risk reduction in high-risk cases of domestic abuse. As part of the planned work to introduce DVARR a significant programme of training will be rolled out to all DVARR partners.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail when the proposed action plan to address wastewater constraints and unlock capacity for new social housing will be circulated to the Executive.
answer_text: I intend to bring the first Housing Supply Strategy Action Plan, targeting a range of whole system issues, to the Executive in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on Phase 2 of the A6 Caw to Drumahoe Dualling Scheme, in conjunction with the Mobuoy Remediation Project.
answer_text: My Department is currently working with the NIEA and other stakeholders, to ensure that any remediation proposals for the site of the illegal waste at Mobuoy and I formally approved the Department’s response to the recent public consultation on the Draft Remediation Strategy which closed on 2 October 2025, following continued collaborative working between my officials and their DAERA counterparts.We must now await finalisation of the DAERA strategy and the associated timeline for its implementation.  Once this is confirmed, my officials can determine the most efficient delivery mechanism for the progression of the scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 42328/22-27, whether her Department will engage with the PSNI or the National Police Chief’s Council, to consider the deployment of Family Liaison Officers in the case of drug-related deaths.
answer_text: As you will be aware, the Police Service (PSNI) are operationally independent of the Department of Justice (DoJ); as such the arrangements in relation to the deployment of Family Liaison Officers are an operational matter for which they are responsible.As per my response to AQW 42328/22-27, you may wish to liaise with them directly in terms of the arrangements that typically apply in respect of drug-related deaths; including any support for families brokered through such engagement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any summer schemes that will be provided by the Education Authority for children with special educational needs this summer.
answer_text: Following confirmation from the Health Minister on 27 March that nursing support will be provided at every summer scheme location, officials in the Education Authority are continuing to liaise with individual schools and the Special Schools Strategic Liaison Committee to identify those schools that will deliver schemes in 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of domestic abuse cases referred to the Public Prosecution Service; and (ii) the number of those cases that did not proceed due to insufficient evidence, for each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Department does not hold the information requested.  In relation to part (i) of the question, the recording of information on domestic abuse cases referred to the Public Prosecution Service by the police is a matter for the Chief Constable.  You may, therefore, wish to direct any further query relating to the information requested in part (i) of the question to the PSNI.  In relation to part (ii) of the question, the recording of information on number of domestic abuse cases referred to the Public Prosecution Service that did not proceed due to insufficient evidence is a matter for the Public Prosecution Service.  You may, therefore, wish to direct any further query relating to the information requested in part (ii) of the question to the Public Prosecution Service (PPS).  My Department continues to work closely with criminal justice partners, including the PPS and the PSNI, to improve the overall response to domestic abuse. I am committed to ensuring that the justice system provides robust protection and reassurance to all those affected by domestic abuse.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the evidential threshold applied by the Public Prosecution Service in cases of domestic abuse; and (ii) any assessment she has made of its appropriateness, given the complex and coercive nature of such offences.
answer_text: I recognise the serious and complex nature of domestic abuse, including the challenges posed by coercive and controlling behaviour, and the importance of ensuring that cases are assessed appropriately within the justice system.The application of evidential thresholds in individual cases is a matter for the independent Public Prosecution Service for Northern Ireland. The PPS applies the Test for Prosecution, which requires both an evidential test (i.e. whether there is a reasonable prospect of conviction) and a public interest test.Given its operational independence, it would not be appropriate for me to comment on the application of that test in individual cases or to direct how the PPS exercises its functions. The Member may wish to raise any specific questions on evidential thresholds directly with the PPS.For our part, my Department continues to work closely with criminal justice partners, including the PPS, to improve the overall response to domestic abuse.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any additional measures being considered to better support and protect women and girls who report domestic abuse, especially where cases do not meet the prosecution threshold.
answer_text: I recognise the serious and complex nature of domestic abuse, and the importance of ensuring that all victims, including women and girls, have access to effective protection and support when needed. It is essential that regardless of prosecution decisions, where there is ongoing risk or abuse, that victims feel enabled and encouraged to report and to seek support. The Department is progressing work to establish a pilot for Domestic Abuse Protection Notices and Orders (DAPNs/Os), which are intended to provide additional protections for victims of domestic abuse. Work is ongoing with justice partners to develop a police-led pilot, informed by learning from other jurisdictions, which will include, where appropriate, positive requirements for perpetrators. This will represent a significant step forward in strengthening protections for victims, including by reducing the need for victims themselves to apply for protective measures.My Department is also progressing a number of operational and policy initiatives aimed at strengthening the response to domestic abuse. This includes working to establish new Domestic Violence and Abuse Risk Response (DVARR) arrangements to replace existing Multi-Agency Risk Assessment Conferences (MARAC).  The enhanced model, which includes changes to structure and governance, will focus on protection and risk reduction in high risk cases of domestic abuse, and implementation is being planned around an extensive training programme for key agency representatives. Current preventative measures include the Domestic Violence and Abuse Disclosure Scheme (DVADS) which enables individuals to request information about a current or former partner’s history of abusive behaviour. This scheme empowers individuals to make informed decisions about their relationships and enhances early intervention to prevent harm.Continued engagement with the voluntary and community sector ensures that victims and survivors are supported throughout their interaction with the justice system by a range of professional advocacy, legal and witness support services.Victims are able to access a range of dedicated support services to help them navigate the justice system. Statutory and voluntary partners work closely together to ensure help is available.  My Department funds a number of key support services. ASSIST NI provides specialist, tailored and trauma-informed advocacy support to high-risk victims as they progress through the criminal justice system. Each victim referred to ASSIST NI is assigned a dedicated support worker who acts as a single point of contact, helping them understand processes, engage with services, and receive updates on their case, reducing the need to repeatedly recount their experience. Victim Support Northern Ireland (VSNI) also offers broader support to all victims, including emotional support, advice, help with Criminal Injuries Compensation, and signposting. Its Witness Service supports victims through the court process, helping

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps her Department is taking to improve the availability and accessibility of public transport at night in the Foyle constituency, particularly in the context of tackling violence against women and girls.
answer_text: My Department recognises the importance of ensuring that public transport in the Foyle constituency is safe, accessible and available in the evening, particularly in the context of tackling violence against women and girls.Translink undertakes a range of safety measures and works with partners through a multiagency approach to support safer public spaces, including on public transport. This includes membership of the Safer Socialising Steering Group within the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Directorate, and the rollout of training for frontline staff, developed with White Ribbon and the PSNI, to reinforce a zerotolerance approach to antisocial behaviour and harassment.As part of this work, Translink is contributing to research with Queen’s University Belfast and The Executive Office and is participating in design and technology initiatives aimed at improving safety for women and girls. Translink is also engaging with the Department of Justice and the Department for Communities on improving access to public transport for individuals fleeing domestic violence. In the Foyle area, it continues to work closely with Derry City and Strabane District Council and partners in the women’s, community and voluntary sectors.To improve accessibility and safety in the evenings, from 30 June 2025 Translink moved all Foyle Metro services that previously departed Foyle Street after 19:00 into the main bus station. This provides enhanced staff, security and CCTV presence. Evening and weekend services continue to operate across a wide range of routes, ensuring that public transport remains available at night for communities across the Foyle area. Translink maintains a zerotolerance approach to antisocial behaviour and works closely with the PSNI and its Safe Transport Teams to address any issues that arise.My officials will continue to work closely with Translink to support safe, accessible and reliable evening public transport across the network, including in the Foyle area, particularly to improve safety for women and girls.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps that are being taken to ensure that frontline health services, including Emergency Departments and GP services, are equipped to identify patients experiencing domestic abuse and coercive control.
answer_text: My Department has taken a number of steps to ensure that frontline health services, including Emergency Departments and GP services, are equipped to identify patients experiencing domestic abuse and coercive control.In September 2024 my Department, in collaboration with the Department of Justice, launched the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy 2024–2031. Under Pillar 2 (Prevention), the Strategy commits to building capacity among people working in frontline and publicfacing roles to identify and respond effectively to domestic and/or sexual abuse at the earliest opportunity.All Health and Social Care Trusts provide mandatory safeguarding training that includes domestic abuse and coercive control, supported by additional specialist training for Emergency Department staff and those working with individuals and families affected by domestic abuse. The Permanent Secretary has written to the Chief Executives of our ALBs to encourage uptake of the Domestic Abuse Context and Change e-Learning training across Health and Social Care.My Department is also piloting the Identification and Referral to Improve Safety (IRIS) programme in General Practice in Belfast and the Newry and District GP Federation areas. The programme is an evidencebased intervention aimed at strengthening the primary care response to domestic and sexual violence, thereby improving the safety, wellbeing and quality of life of survivors. Any further role out of IRIS will be subject to funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether there are any plans to introduce a temporary variation to home to school transport contracts, in response to the increase of the cost of fuel.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the operation and delivery of home to school transport, including the management of contracts it holds with private transport operators. It has advised me that it has no plans at present to uplift the contracted rates agreed with private operators, however it will continue to monitor developments in fuel costs to assess any potential implications for current home to school transport arrangements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has any plans to resurface roads in the Garden City area, in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: Details of the resurfacing schemes planned for the 2026/27 financial year will be published in my Department’s annual report to Council.My officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on any engagement with the UK Government regarding construction products policy, including how his Department is ensuring that Northern Ireland’s position is appropriately recognised and addressed, in the context of responsibility for defective materials.
answer_text: The UK Government launched its Construction Products Reform White Paper on 25 February 2026, and it sets out major reforms to the UK’s regulatory framework for construction products, including the introduction of a General Safety Requirement and a strengthened testing/oversight regime.The previous Minister for Building Safety in England and myself have worked closely with my Department officials and officials at Ministry of Housing Communities and Local Government to ensure Northern Ireland’s unique position is appropriately recognised in the development of the policy and will continue to work closely with them to ensure that the implementation of the reforms work to improve our citizen’s safety.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether if he will review mileage rates for domiciliary care, given recent rises in fuel costs.
answer_text: As part of negotiations to conclude industrial action undertaken during 2023, my Department, employer representatives and Trade Union Side undertook to consider a regional system of mileage reimbursement which might better reflect the integrated nature of the HSC service and the rurality of many of our population requiring care at home.Following these negotiations, a Departmental circular, which issued on 20 May 2024, informed HSC employers that the primary rate of mileage had increased, with effect from 1 April 2024 to 58 pence per mile for staff employed within HSC under Agenda for Change (AfC) terms and conditions.It is important to note that when AfC mileage rates were increased in 2024, fuel prices were at similar levels to those currently prevailing. Staff have, to some extent, benefited from lower fuel prices over the past couple of years as the mileage rates paid have remained the same. I will, however, continue to give this important issue close attention, especially at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 30156/22-27, whether the Registrar General has received any update relating to changes to the Human Fertilisation and Embryology Act 2008, in order to address issues facing couples who receive IVF treatment outside the UK.

To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for an update on her engagement with law enforcement to address the risks associated with the online radicalisation of young men, particularly through misogynist extremism and incel-related content; and (ii) to detail any steps she is taking to prevent the spread of such harmful ideologies and the potential threat they pose to women and girls.
answer_text: I recognise the growing risks associated with the online radicalisation of young men, including the influence of misogynistic extremism and incelrelated content. Such ideologies can contribute to harmful attitudes and behaviours which pose real dangers to women and girls.  I remain engaged with law enforcement and a wide range of other partners from all sectors in efforts to end this growing culture of misogyny and extremism. Our work in this area includes collaboration with the PSNI and The Executive Office to develop and deliver the first phase of the Power to Change campaign. Phase two will be delivered in an interactive, practical, empowering and non-judgmental format, supported by ongoing community engagement led by police.My Department has also led work to address and challenge rape myths. Rape myths, along with wider misogynistic attitudes, distort public understanding of sexual crime and contribute to an environment in which harmful behaviours can be minimised or excused. Working alongside partners including the PSNI,  Victim Support, Nexus, Women’s Aid and academics, my Departments have developed a social media based campaign aimed at challenging commonly held misconceptions about consent, sexual offending and rape. I launched this campaign on 9 March 2026 under the tagline “Rape Myths = Real Harm.” The campaign features a series of graphics that challenge commonly held misconceptions, highlighting that factors such as what a person wears, how they behave, or their consumption of alcohol or drugs can never negate the need for unambiguous consent before engaging in any sexual activity. Importantly, I am grateful that the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) and the Department of Education have also agreed to support the campaign and make its resources available to teachers through the RSE Hub.Separately, my Department is progressing policy work in relation to hate crime and is assessing the suitability of the inclusion of sex/gender as a protected characteristic in hate crime legislation; this includes the consideration of misogyny offences in the hate crime framework. Policy decisions will be informed by findings from the Phase 2 public consultation which is scheduled to take place in the next mandate. It is important that future policy decisions on gender/sex/misogyny should be considered within the broader Ending Violence Against Women and Girls (EVAWG) policy context and the TEO-led EVAWG Strategic Framework to enhance protections for women and girls at risk from violence, abuse and harassment. Of course, legislation alone will not address the impact of misogynistic behaviour, nor can my Department tackle this issue alone. A cross departmental holistic approach, focused on prevention and education is needed to effectively deal with the consequences of misogynistic attitudes, behaviours and beliefs. On a more general basis, although aligning with work relating to tackling violence against women and girls, I ha

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps being taken within the school curriculum to address the rise in misogyny, and to ensure that issues such as coercive control, respect and healthy relationships are being effectively taught across all schools.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the composite answer to AQW 38707/22-27, AQW 38708/22-27, AQW 38709/22-27, AQW 38710/22-27, and AQW 38711/22-27, provided to Claire Sugden MLA, East Londonderry, Independent, on 3 February 2026. The answer is contained in the weekly answer booklet w/e 6 February 2026.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the rollout of the coercive control campaign as part of the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls framework.
answer_text: The Head, Shoulders, Knees and Toes (H.S.K.T.) coercive control campaign consists of two key strands: 1) a targeted awareness and information campaign and 2) training of frontline workers.  A pilot phase of the H.S.K.T campaign was rolled out over a three-month period from September to December 2025.  This was supported by a training programme to 47 frontline workers across a range of 21 cross sectoral organisations. Following successful evaluation of the pilot, EVAWG officials have been working in partnership with colleagues at Ulster University to scope and make preparations for a full regional campaign rollout. A Training Toolkit and Resource Kit have been developed.  These were used to train a further 60 frontline workers from over 30 organisations in preparation for the Campaign rollout at a recent training event held at Ulster University’s Coleraine Campus on Friday 20th March.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, in relation to the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategy and ongoing work with the Police Service of Northern Ireland through the Safer Socialising Steering Group, to detail any specific actions being taken to ensure that reports of domestic abuse are responded to promptly and consistently across all communities, in particular within nationalist areas such as Creggan in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: Our Ending Violence Against Women and Girls’ (EVAWG) officials engage with the PSNI regularly through formal and informal structures to consider emerging issues and identify areas of collaboration. The EVAWG Safer Socialising Steering Group (SSSG) brings together partners across government, policing and the hospitality sector to support safer socialising and enable closer strategic collaboration between key stakeholders on related issues. The Department of Justice and Department of Health have joint responsibility for the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy and related action plans which provide a framework for improving protection and support for victims.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the review of Primary Social Need points as part of the Housing Selection scheme, and in particular how these reforms will improve access to housing for victims of domestic abuse.
answer_text: The outcome of the review was received by the Department on 24 March and is under consideration. 		A key objective of this review is to ensure the Scheme more effectively reflects the needs of the most vulnerable applicants, including victims of domestic abuse, by improving how housing need is assessed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement he has had with sports clubs in relation to tackling domestic abuse, misogyny and violence against women and girls, within the last three years.
answer_text: While the Executive Office has lead policy responsibility for the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategic Framework my department is committed to ensuring that everyone can participate in sport in a safe and welcoming environment. Sport NI an arms-length body of my department, supports a range of initiatives that engages sports clubs in addressing domestic abuse, misogyny and violence against women and girls and has advised me as follows:“Following requests for training in this area, Sport NI offered Keeping Adults Safe in Sport training to governing bodies in January and March 2025, which was facilitated by Volunteer Now.Sport NI then offered governing bodies Domestic Abuse in Sport Awareness Sessions (including Safe Leave Act) in November 2025, which was facilitated by Women’s Aid Federation NI. 36 participants across 26 Governing bodies attended the two sessions. Feedback welcomed the guidance provided and indicated they would be interested in future training in this area.Sport NI have launched an adult safeguarding service for the sports sector with Volunteer Now, which will operate until March 2027. This will provide guidance, advice and support to the sports sector, including sports clubs, regarding adult safeguarding queries including violence against women and girls. Contact details can be found on their website at https://www.sportni.net/performance/safe-sport/adult-safeguarding/.Sport NI are also a member of Safeguarding in Sport Strategic Group where EVAWG strategy forms part of the discussions around the work the group does.  Originally this group’s name was Safeguarding Children in Sport Strategic Group, however given the continued concern around adult safeguarding in sport including the violence against women and girls, the name was changed to ensure everyone (adults and children alike) are safeguarded in sport.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps being taken to ensure that victims of domestic abuse can access safe and appropriate housing in a timely manner.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:‘The Housing Executive has published a Domestic Abuse Action Plan with the 2025/26 delivery guided by Year 2 Actions which can be found on the Northern Ireland Housing Executive website. The Action Plan has been developed to ensure staff can provide the best support to tenants, customers and colleagues and are equipped with the knowledge to signpost to appropriate services when required. The work of the action plan has been supported by a significant training programme which further assists staff across a number of work areas in identifying potential signs of domestic abuse with a view to ensuring that in such cases, appropriate action is taken to support the household accessing safe and appropriate housing.Additionally, the Supporting People Programme continues to provide grant funding for housing-related support services through refuge accommodation, for those in housing need, and through Floating Support services for those who choose to remain at home or in a community setting. Through support planning, these services assist individuals in making informed choices about their accommodation, including support to secure appropriate housing and to maintain or sustain a home. This may also include assistance to access other relevant agencies and services.The Housing Executive remains one of the largest funders of domestic abuse support services in Northern Ireland and values its partnerships with the voluntary and community sector, which enable the delivery of services to women as well as children impacted by domestic abuse. The Housing Executive has adopted a strategic approach to improve and develop a suite of services for women experiencing homelessness that are accessible, flexible and person centred.  Key priorities have included reducing levels of repeat homelessness; addressing a mismatch between customer needs and available accommodation; increased provision for those with complex needs; and increased move on options, with support to help individuals transition to independent living.’

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any engagement she has had with the Police Service of Northern Ireland, to ensure a timely and consistent response to breaches of non-molestation orders, particularly in cases involving perpetrators of domestic abuse.
answer_text: I recognise that breaches of non-molestation orders can create additional fear and retraumatise individuals who are already in vulnerable and potentially dangerous situations. I am committed to ensuring that the justice system offers robust protection and reassurance to those affected by domestic abuse. I have met with the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) on numerous occasions since resuming office, including in recent weeks following recent tragic deaths.  As Minister of Justice, I am determined to do all I can to ensure the criminal justice system responds as effectively as it can to prevent harm and protect victims.However, as the Member will be aware, the Department of Finance holds policy responsibility for the Family Homes and Domestic Violence (Northern Ireland) Order 1998, under which non-molestation orders are made.The operational response to breaches of non-molestation orders, including the timeliness and consistency of that response, is a matter for the PSNI, which must exercise its operational independence in responding to incidents and enforcing court orders; however, my Department also engages regularly and closely with PSNI and wider criminal justice partners on issues relating to the response to domestic abuse, including how we collectively strengthen protections for victims and improve system responses. It is important to emphasise that any breach of a court order, including a non-molestation order, should be dealt with through the proper judicial process. Sentencing in individual cases is, and must remain, a matter for the independent judiciary, who are required to consider the specific circumstances of each case, including any aggravating factors such as repeated or serious breaches.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 41757/22-27, to detail (i) the number of meetings held under the North South Ministerial Council Transport sectoral workstream on road safety, since its establishment; (ii) the specific joint actions or policy initiatives agreed at those meetings; (iii) whether any joint targets for casualty reduction have been set on a cross-border basis; and (iv) whether she has discussed with her counterpart in the Department of Transport, the Road Safety Commissioner model or equivalent statutory accountability mechanisms.
answer_text: Following my answer to AQW 41757/22-27, I can advise that:(i) Since the establishment of the NSMC Transport sectoral workstream on road safety in February 2025, there has been one sectoral meeting on 23rd October 2025, with another upcoming meeting in May 2026.(ii) At the meeting on 23rd October 2025, I welcomed and agreed with Ministers O’Brien TD and Minister Nesbitt MLA several road safety initiatives, details of which can be found in the Joint Communiqué of the meeting at: https://www.northsouthministerialcouncil.org/publications/transport-joint-communique-23-october-2025”. (iii) Joint targets have not been set on a cross-border bases. Each jurisdiction has a live Road Safety Strategy with individual targets.  These Strategies are however based on the same ‘Safe Systems’ Approach and have a target in common and that is to reduce the number of road deaths by 50% by 2030.  (iv) a Road Safety Commissioner model or equivalent statutory accountability mechanism is not an action in either jurisdiction’s current Road Safety Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on improvements to Altnagelvin Area Hospital's Emergency Department; and (ii) whether the plans include the provision of additional rooms to support vulnerable, neurodiverse patients and those who are immunosuppressed.
To ask the Minister of Finance to provide the annual budget allocation for each Department, for each of the last ten years.
answer_text: The annual budgets for departments for the past ten years are published in the Executive’s annual Budget plans, available on the Department of Finance website at:www.finance-ni.gov.uk/topics/budget-plansInformation on in-year monitoring allocations for the same period is available at:www.finance-ni.gov.uk/topics/year-monitoring

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in relation to reform of the Rates Support Grant, whether his Department has given consideration to the potential ring-fencing of funds, and the introduction of a minimum allocation threshold, to guarantee a baseline level of support each year.
answer_text: The Rates Support Grant (RSG) is a statutory grant, but its budget allocation is discretionary. Any funding allocated to the RSG, including the ability to set a minimum allocation baseline, must be considered in the context of my overall Departmental budget position. The Budget for my Department in 2026/27 has not yet been agreed, therefore I am not in the position to confirm the level of funding available for the upcoming financial year.It would be a matter for the Executive to consider ring-fencing the RSG in future budgets.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of Personal Independence Payment applications made, in the last 12 months, broken down by the number that have been (i) successful; and (ii) unsuccessful, at (a) mandatory reconsideration; and (b) tribunal stage.
answer_text: The most recent Personal Independence Payment statistics were published in February 2026 and covered the period up to 30 November 2025.(i) (a) The total number of applications to Personal Independence Payment that were successful upon mandatory reconsideration from 1 April 2025 to 30 November 2025 was 1,390.(ii) (a) The total number of initial applications to Personal Independence Payment that were unsuccessful upon mandatory reconsideration from 1 April 2025 to 30 November 2025 was 4,770.	(i) (ii) (b) My Department does not hold appeals information in the format requested.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any preparations that are underway within the health and social care system in response to recent concerns in relation to meningitis.
answer_text: The PHA is attending UKHSA Incident Management Team meetings to monitor the outbreak in Kent and consider the public health implications for Northern Ireland.  The risk to the NI public is currently low however we are closely monitoring the situation.PHA issued a letter to HSC Trusts, Primary Care GPs, Community Pharmacy and GP Out of Hours providers to provide an update on the outbreak in England, and to outline actions required of HSC services in NI.Information on the potential number of students from Northern Ireland who may return from Kent University has been provided to PHA by UKHSA.  Arrangements are being made to ensure returning students can access recommended antibiotics or vaccines whilst in Northern Ireland.The public have been urged to remain vigilant for the signs and symptoms of meningitis and to act fast if they suspect they may be symptomatic.  Further information is available at: PHA statement - meningitis | HSC Public Health Agency and on NI Direct at Meningitis | nidirect

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the implementation of the Law Commission recommendations on Land Law Reform and Ground Rents.
answer_text: I refer the member to the answer previously given in response to AQW 17252/22-27. Officials will keep the issue under review.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the establishment of an Infrastructure Commission.

To ask the Minister of Finance whether any consideration has been given to providing rates relief to homeowners in unadopted developments, who are paying full rates but do not benefit from adopted roads, drainage or other public infrastructure services.
answer_text: Rates are a property tax as opposed to a charge for specific services provided to individual ratepayers. The regional rate element of the bill supplements the Executive’s funding for front line public services. In relation to the district rate element of the bill, this is based on expenditure levels at a local council level but again is not a direct charge for specific services provided to any individual ratepayer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on planned legislative change, relating to property management companies.
answer_text: My Department published a scoping report in October 2025 following which I announced that further work would commence when resources and expertise were in place. The necessary resource to set up a small team to take this work forward is being finalised, and my Department will source officials with relevant skills and expertise to continue policy development.Part of that process will involve stakeholder engagement that I expect to begin shortly after a team is in place. Further policy work will be undertaken with a view to legislation being ready for consideration in the early part of the next mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement he has had with the Minister of Health in relation to creating a health and housing pathway for individuals with complex needs, particularly those suffering with addiction.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive’s Corporate Strategy recognises the interrelationship between housing and health and the major role that housing can play in reducing health inequalities. This is reflected in the work we do both at strategic and operational levels.  The Housing Executive maintains regular engagement with the Department of Health to support the development of coordinated health and housing pathways for individuals with complex needs, including those affected by addiction.The Supporting People (SP) Programme, administered by the Housing Executive on behalf of the Department for Communities, grant funds housing support services that enable vulnerable people to live as independently as possible in the community. The programme is focused on four thematic areas working with young people, older people, people with a disability (including mental health and learning disabilities), and people experiencing homelessness, including those with alcohol and drug related issues.Engagement with the Department of Health is facilitated through the SP Joint Policy Forum and through SP decision making structures. The Joint Policy Forum, chaired by the Department for Communities and convened quarterly, provides a formal mechanism for engagement between the Department for Communities, the Department of Health, the Department of Justice and the Housing Executive on matters relating to the SP Programme.Regular engagement also takes place through the SP decision making structures, including quarterly Regional Thematic Groups aligned to each thematic area and the biannual SP Strategic Advisory Board. The Department of Health play an active role in these structures which provide ongoing opportunities for collaboration on issues relating to housing related support for those with complex needs.Furthermore, the Housing Executive supports health and housing pathways through the delivery of the Complex Lives which has provided coordinated, multiagency support for people experiencing chronic homelessness and who present with a range of needs which have included mental health issues, physical health problems, brain injury, learning disability and addiction. In delivering the Complex Lives model, a weekly multi-agency, multi-disciplinary team meeting is held, with partners (including health), who work together to agree a single support plan for those individuals who are currently at risk”.  The Complex Lives model is designed to provide a ‘whole system’ partnership delivery model that joins up support services across housing, health, criminal justice, employment, skills and other support together in a shared, agreed, and sustainable way. Through working with our partners, one of which is the Belfast Health & Social Care Trust, the model seeks to remove barriers to treatment, improve access to health supp

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the works at St Eugene's Primary School, Derry, including an estimated timescale for the completion of the works.
answer_text: The tenders for the roofing works at St Eugene’s Primary School were returned on 16 March 2026 and these are currently being evaluated by the Education Authority. It is anticipated that the works will begin on site in May 2026. A programme for the works will be agreed when a contractor is appointed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has given any consideration to resurfacing the Northland Road in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: Details of the resurfacing schemes planned for the 2026/27 financial year will be included in my Department’s Annual Report to Derry and Strabane District Council. My officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 39299/22-27, for an update on the resurfacing of the Strand Road.
answer_text: Preliminary preparatory work commenced on Monday 16th February 2026, with cleaning and inspection of the drainage along the Strand Road and footway repair works currently ongoing. My officials are currently in discussion with the University about the provision of ducts for fiber optic connections across the Strand Road and other service connections which will need to be completed before the road is resurfaced.The main carriageway resurfacing works are now planned to start in May 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether any assessment has been made on the impact of recent fuel cost rises on the delivery of domiciliary care.

To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 41584/22-27, whether any assessment has been made on the impact of the delay implementing the Real Living Wage, in particular for the delivery of domiciliary care within the Western Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps her Department is taking, in conjunction with NI Water and local councils, to address ongoing sewage and drainage issues within unadopted developments.
answer_text: Unadopted developments are private developments, and it is the responsibility of the developer to ensure the site meets the required sewage and drainage standards for adoption to the local wastewater network. Neither my Department nor NI Water have the legal remit to carry out works on an unadopted development. NI Water acts as a Statutory Consultee in the planning process and will provide wastewater impact assessments and advice to local councils on planning conditions which need to be met by developers. NI Water has a developer-led process in place to receive applications for adoption. NI Water have advised that the current number of unadopted sites progressing through the adoption process is 1,599. This includes 243 sites with agreements and bonds under Article 17 of the Drainage (NI) Order (1973) and 1,356 sites with agreements and bonds under Article 161 of the Water and Sewerage Services (NI) Order 2006.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the total value of developer bonds, currently in place for unadopted developments; and (ii) how often her Department has called in bonds, in the last ten years, where developers failed to complete required works.
answer_text: The Department’s Private Streets Database currently records a total of 3,042 "bonded extents", which are standalone agreements and do not necessarily correspond to an individual development as a development can be made up of one or any number of bonded agreements.  It is generally accepted that if a bonded extent remains unadopted, there will be some degree of infrastructure works required to bring the development to an adoptable standard.The Department currently holds a total of £144,470,855 in developer bonds for unadopted developments, however we do not record information on how often these bonds have been called in over the last ten years.I recognise the impact that unadopted streets can have on householders.  In September 2025, I introduced enhanced enforcement procedures to ensure roads infrastructure in new developments can be adopted into the public road network.    These enhanced enforcement procedures will significantly reduce the risk of developments currently under construction entering the backlog.Furthermore, in 2023, the Department increased the bond calculation rates to ensure that bond coverage would be sufficient to complete construction works should a developer default.  This represented the first increase since 2008 and links rates to the term contract Price Fluctuation Factor which ensures bond calculation rates are increased annually in line with construction inflation.  This will facilitate the Department’s intervention in those cases where a developer does not complete the required works by ensuring sufficient funding is available to allow the Department to deliver the outstanding work required to progress adoption.All private streets sites are the responsibility of the developer until they are formally adopted into the public road network.  Where works have been completed to standard, my officials arrange final inspections and progress adoption. Where works remain incomplete, officials actively engage with developers to encourage compliance and completion to the required specification.There are historic unadopted backlog sites defined as uncompleted developments, where at least 80% of the residences are occupied.  Progressing these sites is challenging as, in many cases, there is no developer to enforce against, or the remaining bond value is insufficient to cover the cost of the required remedial work.  The Department is not funded, nor under a legislative requirement, to bridge any funding shortfall or to bring streets up to standard.There are no plans for a targeted scheme to assist residents in this situation, however I assure you I remain committed to progressing adoptions as quickly as developer compliance and resources permit, and to continue to engage with residents where difficulties arise.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 41299/22-27, to detail the number of times the road surface in the Ballymagroarty area, Derry, have been assessed within the last five years.
answer_text: High trafficked roads within Ballymagroarty are inspected on a monthly basis and medium trafficked roads are inspected on a 3 monthly basis. Routine inspections within the Ballymagroarty area will continue in line my Department’s Limited Service maintenance policy and any defects that meet the intervention level, will be programmed for repair.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of new build social homes completed within the last 12 months, in each constituency area.
answer_text: The number of new build social homes completed within the last twelve months, in each constituency area, are listed in table below.Parliamentary Constituency CompletionsEast Antrim37North Antrim220South Antrim74Upper Bann36East Belfast24North Belfast24South Belfast100West Belfast0North Down43South Down1Fermanagh and South Tyrone78Foyle18Lagan Valley120East Londonderry48Mid Ulster5Newry and Armagh56Strangford0West Tyrone74 958

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions his Department plans to take in response to the 19 Recommendations outlined in the February 2026 ADHD Needs Assessment Report.
answer_text: The final ADHD Needs Assessment Report was published on 12 February 2026, noting that any decisions on future commissioning of ADHD services would be in the context of overall affordability.Officials have commenced an options appraisal process to identify what options could potentially be implemented in the short to medium term to help address the need for ADHD services.  The outcome of this work will inform the development of an implementation plan to address the recommendations in the Needs Assessment report, which is already underway.  It is recognised that several of the recommendations can only be progressed in the context of a future commissioned service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the number of managerial roles within the Civil Service; and (ii) the cumulative annual salary cost associated with these roles, for each of the last ten years.
answer_text: The HR system does not include a specific field identifying a post as a “managerial” role.  Managerial responsibilities can fall across several grades, typically from Executive Officer II upwards, depending on business need. As a result, the Department does not hold an official count of “managerial roles”.In the absence of a discrete managerial category, published statistics on staff in post by analogous grade provide the most consistent basis for understanding staffing structures. Published statistics on the number of staff in post broken down by analogous grade are available in Table 3 (a) in the Employment in the NICS – 1 April Tables for 2025 to 2016. All reports are on the NISRA website.Published statistics on the mean full-time equivalent salary broken down by analogous grade are available in Table 1b in the Pay in the NICS publication for 2025 to 2016. All reports are available on the NISRA website.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of managerial roles, in each Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) the cumulative annual salary cost associated with those roles, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: (i) the number of managerial roles, in each Health and Social Care TrustInformation on the whole-time equivalent (WTE) of staff employed in the Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts is published by my Department on a quarterly basis.  The latest information available relates to the position at 31 December 2025 and can be found at the link below.https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-health-and-social-care-hsc-workforce-statistics-december-2025Due to the varied nature of individual roles within the entire Health and Social Care (HSC) workforce, many staff across all Agenda for Change (AfC) pay bands will have management responsibilities.  Therefore, a breakdown of all managerial roles cannot be provided.  Information on the whole-time equivalent of staff employed at each (AfC) pay band in each HSC Trust, as at 31 December in each of the last ten years, is detailed in the tables below.
