question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps she is taking to support households struggling with the cost of home heating oil, particularly in light of rising prices and the impact this is having on families.

To ask the Minister of Finance to detail a timeline for introduction of regulations setting out the procedures for pooling resources under the Children’s Services Co-operation Act (Northern Ireland) 2015.
answer_text: The recent review into barriers to the implementation of the Children’s Services Co-operation Act, completed in December 2025, noted that the recent provision of guidance provides an important framework for pooling budgets without restricting departmental flexibility. My department published guidance for departments in April 2025 (updated in March 2026) and NILGA published guidance for local government in March 2025.  Whilst the provision exists for my department to introduce regulation, strengthening and promoting current guidance as well as learning from the NICS Test and Learn initiative is, in my view, a proportionate first step. My officials will continue to monitor the impact of these developments on the pooling of resources, and if necessary, I will then consider the need for regulations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the current pathway for emergency dental care for vulnerable patients, including those with learning disabilities; and (ii) any measures that are being taken to ensure timely and accessible treatment for these groups, in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: (i)Vulnerable children, young people and adults receive dental care across a range of settings in Northern Ireland: General Dental Services (GDS), Community Dental Services (CDS) and the Hospital Dental Services (HDS). All dental services may have a role in the provision of emergency care to a vulnerable patient, depending on their specific clinical needs.For vulnerable patients who are registered with a Health Service dentist and are suitable for treatment within general dental practice, emergency or urgent dental conditions should be directed to their own dental practice during weekdays. At weekends and bank holidays, patients should contact their practice for details of outofhours arrangements. Any dental practice providing Health Service treatment is required to offer prompt emergency care to its registered patients.For vulnerable patients who are not registered with a Health Service dentist but remain suitable for treatment in general dental practice, emergency or urgent care may be accessed through the Dental Access Scheme (DAS). This scheme provides weekday access for unregistered patients with emergency, urgent or pressing dental needs. Within the Western Trust area, there are currently four operating DAS practices—three in Limavady and one in Strabane.Vulnerable patients—including people with learning disabilities, physical disabilities, complex medical needs, mental health conditions, housebound individuals and those living in residential care—may receive care through the Community Dental Service (CDS). The CDS accepts referrals for patients whose needs cannot be managed safely or effectively in general dental practice. The CDS operates as a referralonly specialist service. Patients must be referred by a General Dental Practitioner (GDP) or another Health and Social Care professional. In the case of a patient requiring emergency care, the GDP may submit a CDS referral form clearly indicating the need for emergency treatment. For vulnerable patients who are not registered with a dentist, referrals may also be made by other Health and Social Care professionals involved in their care. To ensure that the most urgent cases are treated as quickly as possible, CDS teams across HSC Trusts operate a strict clinical triage and prioritisation process.Where dental treatment involves patients with complex medical conditions or severe special needs, treatment may occur in hospitalbased dental units equipped for sedation and general anaesthesia. This pathway ensures timely care when communitybased provision is not clinically appropriate.(ii)In 2025/26 my Department invested £7 million in General Dental Services, which play an integral role in supporting timely and accessible dental care for vulnerable groups.  This investment includes:The Enhanced Child Examination Scheme;Continuation of a 30% fee enhancement for essential Health Service dental treatments;£1.6 million in additional support for Health Service dentists to maintain service capacity;Expan

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on improvements to the Emergency Department at Altnagelvin Area Hospital.
answer_text: I witnessed first-hand the need for additional investment in the Emergency Department at Altnagelvin Hospital when I visited last January. The Western Health and Social Care Trust has been allocated £0.5million in capital funding this year for works to the Emergency Department to improve patient/staff experience. These include the provision of additional toilet and shower facilities, additional vending area, and an upgrade of the current staff kitchen/rest area.In order for the full Emergency Department project to proceed it is necessary for the required capital funding to be committed, in the context of the competing priorities.  I am sure members appreciate the severe financial challenges facing the Health & Social Care sector in recent years which means that my Department continues to have insufficient funding to take forward the capital projects that have been proposed as quickly as it would wish.The current Budget process is ongoing and will set the capital budgets for NI departments over the next four years, 2026/27 to 2029/30.  In January 2026 the Finance Minister published his draft Budget proposals which included £1.8billion in capital funding for DoH over the next four years.  This level of funding will allow DoH to meet its inescapable capital pressures and progress most of its previously partially committed projects over the Budget period, including Altnagelvin Emergency Department, with some delay to reflect the funding available each year.Once the NI Executive has agreed to the Budget capital allocations for NI departments over the next four years, the DoH 10-year Capital Investment Plan will be developed setting out the projects and schemes in the Health & Social Care sector to be funded over the next decade.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) any family liaison support currently available to families of prisoners with learning disabilities and other additional needs; and (ii) whether her Department plans to enhance these supports, to ensure families are appropriately informed and supported throughout the custodial process.
answer_text: The Department’s Strengthening Family Relations Strategy provides the framework for maintaining and supporting family relationships during prison custody. The Strategy recognises that all decisions and actions relating to a family should be dealt with on a case-by-case basis and emphasises a personcentred approach to family contact, with a focus on maintaining relationships. (i)  The Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS) has a duty of care to all people living in prison in Northern Ireland. They part fund NIACRO’s Family Links programme, which offers practical and emotional support, advocacy and advice to families and children who have a family member in prison. This programme is tailored to individual needs, including those with intellectual/learning disability (ID/LD).   NIPS Family Officers also engage with, support and provide reassurance to families of prisoners. Family Officers receive information from families on actions that can assist individuals with additional needs and meet with families on an individual basis to discuss any queries or concerns they may have.The NIPS works collaboratively with the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (the Trust) whose Healthcare in Prison team includes learning disability nurses. The Trust advises NIPS on individuals who have an ID/LD upon their committal to prison.  In collaboration with others, NIPS ensures that appropriate support is provided to individuals with additional needs. NIPS also promotes family engagement by facilitating in person visits, telephone contact, letters, virtual visits and family events.(ii) The Department is currently developing a 2026/2027 Action Plan for the Strengthening Family Relations Strategy. This development will consider any enhancements required to the supports currently in place to ensure that families are appropriately informed and supported throughout the custodial process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department will take to reform legislation so that Northern Ireland provides the same level of legal recognition, protection, and support for young carers as the rest of the UK.
answer_text: The support of carers, and young carers, is an important societal issue affecting many children and young people and their families. The HSC commissions the Regional Young Carers Service, a targeted regional service delivered by Action for Children and Barnardos working in partnership with Health and Social Care Trusts to assess the needs and  provide support to young carers. Schools also play a crucial role in ensuring young carers receive personal, emotional, and educational support, and the Department of Education and Education Authority provide guidance to schools to promote identifying and supporting young carers in educational settings. To inform cross departmental engagement the Department of Health established a Cross Departmental Senior Officials Group for Carers. Young carers recently presented to this  group and shared their lived experiences and views on the issues impacting young carers.The integrated health and social care system in Northern Ireland is distinct and different from arrangements in England, Scotland, and Wales. Other jurisdictions have in place provisions for children who are young carers, however, common across jurisdictions including in Northern Ireland is the focus on supporting the wellbeing of children who are young carers. The Department has recently published the Reforming Adult Social Care and Support Delivery Plan 2026-2029. This plan sets out the commitment to review legislative provisions for adult and young carers and to develop recommendations by 2028.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the criteria for accessing secondary care for the treatment of endometriosis.
answer_text: All Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts currently provide care for women with endometriosis, with referrals managed under the care of a consultant gynaecologist. Gynaecology services are delivered through multidisciplinary teams to ensure holistic, responsive and appropriate care throughout the patient journey. Each member of the team whether a consultant, nurse or allied health professional acts as a specialist within their own discipline.A Northern Ireland Endometriosis Pathway has been agreed and was developed in partnership with stakeholders across both primary and secondary care. This pathway has been shared with Trust Gynaecology Units and the Gynaecology Provider Collaborative. It sets out the patient journey from initial presentation in primary care, through enhanced primary care services and local endometriosis provision, and onwards to the developing Regional Endometriosis Centres, as well as covering posttreatment followup.The diagnostic pathway includes assessment of symptoms, investigations to determine severity and condition progression, and the planning of appropriate next steps. Management options range from ongoing monitoring to hormonal therapies and/or surgical intervention, with consideration of fertility needs throughout.The Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) is working closely with the Gynaecology Provider Collaborative to invest across all five Trusts. This investment aims to enhance diagnostic capacity, increase the number of laparoscopy sessions, and expand clinical appointment availability. In addition, work is progressing to develop two Regional Endometriosis Centres within the Western and Belfast Trusts. These centres will provide complex surgical care for women who require specialist intervention, supported by multidisciplinary teams including colorectal and urological surgeons.The model being developed aligns with the national Endometriosis Pathway however, available funding will not allow for full implementation of all pathway components at this stage. As a result, development will proceed on a phased basis to ensure services can be expanded safely and sustainably.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he intends to undertake a wider review of the Child Maintenance Service, particularly in relation to delays and the adequacy of support for single-parent families.

To ask the Minister of Education, in relation to the Teachers’ Pay Agreement, whether his Department considered plans to implement a phased payment, instead of a lump sum, to ensure that teachers in receipt of Universal Credit are able to benefit fully from any backdated payment.
answer_text: In agreeing the Teachers’ Pay Award with teaching unions, the Department and teacher employers agreed to pay the arrears due to all teachers as soon as was practical after agreement was ratified by unions. In discussing any implications from the payment of the arrears with unions, the Department made clear that payments would be paid in line with normal payment processes.  The Department’s teachers’ payroll complied with all relevant HMRC legislation and guidelines in making the payment of arrears.The processing of any pay award is complex, with many different calculations required to ensure that all salary-based pay, allowances and other statutory and occupational payments are revised to ensure teachers will receive their correct payment of arrears. The Department’s Teachers’ Pay Team administers pay for approximately 23,000 teachers and phasing the payments due to staff as a result of a pay deal would require significant time to process and would significantly delay the pay award to staff.Any additional income may have unwelcome implications for an individual in receipt of income assessed benefits. Individuals in receipt of Universal Credit should seek advice from their work coach or contact the service centre on 0800 328 5644, as neither my Department, nor individual employers can offer advice on benefit entitlements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients removed from the gynaecology waiting list in the last 12 months, broken down by (i) the reason for their removal; and (ii) Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) whether Police Service of Northern Ireland Family Liaison Officers provide support to families bereaved as a result of drug-related deaths; and (ii) any support mechanisms currently available to such families.
answer_text: Every single drug-related death is a tragedy for our society, and a cause of such trauma and loss for those family members and loved ones most affected. I am aware that the PSNI adhere to National Police Chief’s Council Guidance when considering the deployment of Family Liaison Officers and that these generally occur in the event of: homicide; fatal road traffic collisions; mass fatalities; rail fatalities; and critical incidents where family liaison might enhance the effectiveness of the police response.As you will be aware, the police are operationally independent of the Department; as such you may wish to liaise with them directly in terms of the arrangements that typically apply in respect of drug-related deaths; including any support for families brokered through such engagement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement his Department has had with the Department for Infrastructure regarding public realm schemes in the Foyle constituency, particularly issues raised around the reduction of available residents' parking, in the last 12 months.
answer_text: My Department has engaged regularly with the Department for Infrastructure (DfI) throughout the development and delivery of the Inner Walled City public realm scheme in the Foyle constituency. This engagement has included discussion of issues raised regarding changes to residents’ parking provision over the past 12 months.DfI is represented on the Project Board for the scheme, which meets frequently to review progress and address operational matters, including traffic management and parking. My Department will continue to work closely with DfI and other stakeholders to ensure that the needs of residents, local businesses and the wider community are fully considered as the project progresses.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when she will answer AQW 39295/22-27.
answer_text: I answered AQW 39295/22-27 on 13 March 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the waiting times for access to detox and addiction support services, in the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients on the waiting list for detox and addiction support services, in the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has had any discussions with the Minister of Education regarding the increase in school meal prices, in particular the impact on secondary school pupils from low-income households who are not eligible for free school meals.
answer_text: Setting prices for school meals is the responsibility of the Department of Education.  I have not had any discussions with the Minister of Education on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the revised business case, submitted by Translink for the provision of additional services on the North West rail corridor.
answer_text: I fully recognise the need to address regional imbalance and better connect our communities including those within the Northwest. I have received the business case for the enhanced services and will consider these as part of my budget decisions on the Department’s Final Budget for the coming year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 38120/22-27, to detail what the £44,625 million mulit-year bid for Belfast to Derry enhancements, as part of the delivery of the All-Island Strategic Rail Review, entails.
answer_text: At the time of the previous response, the information presented was based on the bids submitted in response to the Budget 2026/27-2029/30 Information Gathering Exercise.  Figures supplied by Translink at that time requested £44.625m for the Belfast to Derry enhancements project, which in turn formed part of the £220.055m multi-year bid for the All-Island Strategic Rail Review (AISRR).  Translink is progressing the necessary technical modelling and the development of a Strategic Outline Business Case (SOC) to assess options for improving services on the corridor. The SOC is currently scheduled for completion by Summer 2026, and its findings, will inform any future decisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there are any plans to provide additional rooms within the Emergency Department at Altnagelvin Area Hospital to support (i) neurodiverse patients; and (ii) those who are immunosuppressed.
answer_text: The Western Health and Social Care Trust have advised that they have no plans to provide additional rooms within the current emergency department at Altnagelvin Area Hospital to support neurodiverse patients or those who are immunosuppressed. The Trust has also advised that given the age and the general layout of the department there would be no practical solution within the current footprint to provide for these additional rooms.Currently the Altnagelvin ED has one small sensory space funded for adults with additional learning needs, but it is utilised by both adults and children. This space will also be used for patients who are neurodiverse, if the space is available at the time of attendance. Altnagelvin ED prioritises immunosuppressed patients for isolation rooms if possible. The ED has a total of 5 isolation rooms, (cubicles with a closed door). They have no rooms with ensuite facilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how the Children’s Services Co-operation Act (Northern Ireland) 2015 is being used to support cross-departmental work on free school meals and potential expansion of this provision.
answer_text: The Children’s Services Co-operation Act (Northern Ireland) 2015 supports improved co-operation between government departments in Northern Ireland in order to improve the well-being of children and young people. In line with this approach, my Department actively engages in cross-departmental partnerships to support the provision of free school meals (FSM) and school food more widely.  The Department of Health (DoH) is jointly responsible with my Department for the Food in Schools policy to help ensure children and young people are educated to make informed healthy food choices and that such food choices are available at school, including for those entitled to FSM. This is overseen by the Food in Schools Forum, including representatives of DoH and the Public Health Agency. My Department works with the Department for the Economy in order to ensure a consistent approach to FSM eligibility for students in further education colleges. Both my officials and Education Authority staff work with the Department for Communities (DfC) to ensure efficient processing of FSM applications linked to benefits entitlement. DfC officials also provided data to inform my Department’s review of the eligibility criteria for FSM and uniform grants.My officials also engage regularly with officials from Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) on the Northern Ireland Food Strategy Framework and its associated action plan. These departments played an important role on the cross-departmental working group that supported my Department’s review of the eligibility criteria for FSM and uniform grants. Following the completion of this review, I increased the FSM income threshold for those in receipt of Universal Credit, indexed to consumer price inflation. This ensures continued access for those in need and will be uprated annually. Due to the severe budgetary constraints facing both my Department and the wider Executive, further expansion of access to FSM is currently not affordable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 40924/22-27, to detail (i) the reason why the Housing Executive does not record the number of applicants who seek emergency accommodation but cannot be accommodated; and (ii) whether he will ensure this information is formally collected and published, to better understand the scale of unmet housing need.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“(i) The Housing Executive does record information relating to applicants seeking temporary accommodation who subsequently do not take up a placement. However, it is not captured in a way that enables us to extract it other than by undertaking a significant manual exercise to review all individual case records as it is recorded in the notes on the applicant’s file. (ii) Data in relation to homelessness and social housing waiting lists published in the quarterly Northern Ireland Housing Bulletin and the biannual Northern Ireland Homelessness Bulletin provides an understanding of unmet housing need.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in relation to the 600 homes planned for temporary accommodation, whether his Department will consider the use of modular homes.
answer_text: The proposal is for NIHE Landlord to acquire properties from the general market. The types of property to be purchased and locations will be aligned with need.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement he has had with his counterparts in Westminster regarding the introduction of a protected minimum floor within Universal Credit, for self-employed claimants.
answer_text: I have not had any engagement with my counterparts in Westminster regarding the introduction of a protected minimum floor within Universal Credit for self-employed claimants.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department will prioritise plans to extend benefit cap mitigations beyond 2028, to provide certainty for those impacted.
answer_text: I refer you to my response to AQW 38517/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she is aware of any issues raised, in the past three years, about potential conflicts of interest of any members of the judiciary in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: You will be aware that you wrote to me on 4 March 2026 regarding a perceived conflict of interest in relation to the judiciary, raised by one of your constituents, and I replied to you on 11 March 2026.I am not aware of any other issues raised about potential conflicts of interest of any members of the judiciary in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update for the resumption of the stocking programme for the Public Angling Estate.
answer_text: My Department restarted the annual stocking programme for the Public Angling Estate in December 2025, following favourable sampling results at Movanagher Fish Farm, which allowed stocking in compliance with the Wildlife Northern Ireland Order 1985.Stocking information is published on the DAERA website.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether it is permitted for members of the judiciary in Northern Ireland to have commercial interests.
answer_text: The Statement of Ethics for judicial office holders which provides guidance on commercial interests along with their terms and conditions of appointment can be found on the Judiciary NI website Judicial Conduct and Complaints | Judiciary NI.

answer_text: The table below sets out the number of Penalty Charge Notices (PCNs) issued in 2024 and 2025. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) whether she has considered appointing a dedicated Road Safety Commissioner with statutory authority and a mandate, to deliver casualty reduction targets; (ii) whether her Department has made any assessment of governance models in Norway and Sweden, where designated lead agencies hold road safety targets; and (iii) any engagement her Department has had with the Department of Transport in the south, on all-island coordination of road safety strategy.
answer_text: You will be aware that on the 27 January 2026 I launched the latest Road Safety Action Plan to 2027 that supports the work of the Road Safety Strategy to 2030.  In this, I have set out my key road safety priorities and on-going projects. (i)  I am not considering appointing a Road Safety Commissioner at this time.   My Department chairs the Road Safety Strategic Forum which steers the work of the Road Safety Strategy.  Members include senior representatives from government departments and agencies, and our road safety partners in the emergency services. (ii) Whilst my officials keep abreast of road safety interventions in general in neighbouring jurisdictions and internationally, designating lead agencies to hold road safety targets is not part of the current Road Safety Strategy governance plan.(iii) My officials meet regularly with their counterparts in the South on specific policy areas and more generally on our respective road safety strategies. It is contained in both current action plans that collaborative work on road safety would continue. My predecessor John O’Dowd approved road safety as an official workstream of the NSMC Transport sectoral.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how high risk routes are identified and prioritised for inclusion in the speed camera programme, including the criteria used to target areas with the highest incidence of serious and fatal collisions.
answer_text: The policy regarding speed detection cameras is the responsibility of the Northern Ireland Road Safety Partnership, and I suggest that you contact the Partnership directly for this information.Their contact details are:Northern Ireland Road Safety Partnership42 Montgomery RoadBelfast BT6 9LD

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has explored (i) the potential; and (ii) a programme, to redesign the highest risk road sections across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: All major road projects are subject to a robust appraisal process, which considers impacts on society, the economy, and the environment, as well as on public finances. Road safety is a key consideration within this process, with specific assessment of changes in the number and severity of collisions and casualties, underlining its importance in scheme development and prioritisation.Alongside this, my Department also delivers an annual programme of Local Transport Safety Measures (LTSM), which includes collision remedial works, road alignment improvements, trafficcalming schemes, vehiclerestraint systems, active travel enhancements and parttime 20mph speed limits at schools. These schemes focus on locations with identified safety concerns, informed by PSNI collisioncluster analysis, and are developed and prioritised in line with current policy to maximise the delivery of road safety benefits where they are most needed.My Department’s Road Safety Strategy to 2030 embraces the Safe System approach to road safety improvement. The internationally recognised Safe System approach is recognised as the optimal, best practice approach to achieving significant reductions in road user fatalities and serious injuries.Under the Safe System approach, ‘safe roads and roadsides’ aims to support correct road use through providing ‘self-explaining’ roads (i.e. they are designed so that the driver is aware of what is expected of them and behaves appropriately) and forgiving roadsides (i.e. roadsides that minimize the impact of collisions) so as to reduce the risk of collisions occurring, and the severity of injuries if a collision does occur. Safe roads and roadsides involves the planning, design and operation of roads and roadsides so that roads are predictable and forgiving of errors. The Safe System approach seeks to ensure that safety features are incorporated into the road design from the outset.   There is also an emphasis on a proactive approach to road safety, with improvements made to improve both the actual and perceived risks of road safety. Collision hot spots are identified, and targeted engineering measures taken to remedy them, e.g. by improving road surfaces, removing roadside obstacles to vision, or installing traffic lights. In January 2026 I launched the latest Action Plan that supports the Strategy. It sets out 11 Strategic Interventions that aim to make the biggest impact to the current and emerging road safety issues. Intervention 10 states ‘We will plan, develop and deliver road engineering and infrastructure improvement projects that will include steps to reduce the number of people killed or seriously injured in road traffic collisions.’

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 36425/22-27, for an update on the further analytical work undertaken by his officials, to determine the wider impact of the removal of the two-child limit.
answer_text: As of November 2025, in Northern Ireland, 13,700 households were impacted by the two-child policy. There were 47,690 children in the impacted households; of these, 17,450 were not eligible for the child element in Universal Credit (UC). Of the 13,700 households impacted by the two-child policy at November 2025, 560 were affected by the Benefit Cap. Following the removal of the two-child policy, an additional 880 households are estimated to be impacted, bringing the total households impacted by the Benefit Cap to an estimated 1,440.  As of November 2025, there were 1,580 households in receipt of transitional element who were impacted by the two-child limit. Following the removal of the policy, it is estimated that 1,050 households will receive an increase in their UC entitlement that is greater than the value of their current transitional element. This will reduce their transitional element award to zero, but their overall UC award will increase. The remaining 520 households are expected to have their transitional element reduced, and while these households will see no monetary increase in their award, their underlying entitlement will have increased due to payment of the additional child element.Figures are extracted from the Universal Credit Full Service system (UCFS) at November 2025. Due to retrospection of UCFS data these figures are subject to change. Due to instances of missing data around children declarations and dates of birth please treat these figures as estimates. All figures are rounded to the nearest ten. Totals may not sum due to rounding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of the significant increase in oil prices over the past week, and Northern Ireland’s over-reliance on home heating oil, to detail any immediate actions he plans to take to protect low-income and vulnerable households from fuel poverty.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the pressures that rising energy costs place on households, especially given Northern Ireland’s ongoing dependence on home heating oil. My Department remains firmly committed to providing practical assistance to those most at risk of fuel poverty.Where a sudden and unaffordable rise in heating costs places someone in an extreme or exceptional situation, Discretionary Support may be able to assist. This includes interestfree loans to help with fuel costs and, in specific circumstances, nonrepayable grants for essential living expenses.The NI Energy Advice Service, funded by my Department and delivered by the Housing Executive, continues to offer free and impartial advice. It has now handled almost 75,000 enquiries, helping many households’ access energyefficiency support, including through the Affordable Warmth Scheme, which has invested over £140 million since 2014 to improve more than 31,000 lowincome homes.While the Oil Buying Network has been temporarily suspended due to market volatility, engagement with suppliers is ongoing with a view to restoring it when conditions stabilise.Alongside immediate support, my Department’s Warm Healthy Homes Strategy sets out a longterm, crossgovernment approach to tackling fuel poverty. A new Warm Healthy Homes Fund will launch next year to support lowincome and vulnerable households living in the least energyefficient homes, informed by public consultation planned for Spring 2026.I encourage anyone facing immediate risks to their health or wellbeing, such as being unable to afford essential heating or basic living expenses, to contact my Department’s Make the Call service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement he has had with the Minister for Infrastructure regarding the draft Disability and Work Strategy, specifically in relation to improving access to public transport for people with disabilities.
answer_text: I have not discussed the draft Disability and Work Strategy directly with the Minister for Infrastructure. Officials from the Department for Infrastructure have been part of the cross-departmental working group to develop the Executive Disability Strategy,  and Outcome 2 focuses on improving access to public transport for people with disabilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any expenditure on security staff provision for Housing Executive offices, in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of incident reports filed, when two healthcare assessments and a mental health triage assessment were not completed within seven days of an individual's arrival into custody, for each of the last three years.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any steps being taken to address repeat perpetrators of anti-social behaviour in social housing; and (ii) whether any assessment has been made of the impact of such behaviour on the number of transfer requests in areas with high levels of anti-social behaviour.
answer_text: I am bringing forward a Housing (Amendment) Bill to strengthen social landlords’ powers to deal more effectively with repeat perpetrators of anti-social behaviour. The Bill is expected to include provisions for injunctions against unacceptable behaviour, grounds for possession and changes to eligibility for social housing and Full Duty homelessness status where serious unacceptable behaviour has occurred. I have sought input from the Chief Executive of the Housing Executive, who has advised as follows:“No assessment of the type suggested has been undertaken.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department intends to (i) review; and (ii) replace, Small Pockets of Deprivation and Neighbourhood Renewal Funding allocations, as part of the People and Place Review.
answer_text: My Departments Review of the Executive’s People and Place Strategy (2003), which included a review of its programmes, including Neighbourhood Renewal, Areas at Risk and Small Pockets of Deprivation, has concluded with the development of a Strategic Framework draft that has been submitted to me for my consideration.It is not anticipated that any changes to the existing programmes of Neighbourhood Renewal, Areas at Risk or Small Pockets of Deprivation will occur in the 26/27 financial year.Once approval from the Executive has been given, it is envisaged that an extended transition phase will apply to ensure that there is a smooth transition for communities from the previous strategy to the new.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the road markings for the loading bay on Shipquay Street, in the Foyle constituency, will be amended to Loading Only, in order to facilitate effective enforcement against improperly parked vehicles.
answer_text: The incorrectly marked “Loading Bay” marking on Shipquay Street was amended on the morning of 4th March 2026 to “Loading Only”.  Our parking enforcement team is now issuing advisory notices and will start to enforce fully from 16th March.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the rationale as to why additional funding has not been provided to independent homecare providers to meet the Government-imposed increase in Employer National Insurance Contributions for 2025-26.
answer_text: My Department provides funding to HSC Trusts on an annual basis for inflationary uplifts for a range of independent sector social care providers.  HSC Trusts commission services directly from these providers.Prior to the beginning of the 2025/26 financial year, I approved additional funding for HSC organisations, primarily Trusts, in respect of these inflationary uplifts, including the home care sector, which were effective from April 2025.These uplifts were intended not only to take account of statutory increases in for example, the National Living Wage, but also changes to Employer National Insurance contributions (NIC), which were introduced in April 2025.  These changes increased the rate of Employer NIC to 15% from 13.8% and also lowered the threshold above which Employer NIC is payable from £9,100 to £5,000 per year.  The regional home care provider hourly rate increased by 6.7%, from £20.01 per hour in 2024/25 to £21.35 per hour in 2025/26.  Part of this increase related to the April 2025 changes in Employer National Insurance contributions (NIC), both rate and threshold.In 2025/26, over £16m was provided to HSC Trusts to facilitate payment of home care providers in respect of the hourly rate increase to £21.35.  Of this funding, over £4.8m related to support for the change in the Employer NIC rate and threshold from April 2025.Whilst I appreciate that the provision of a blanket uplift will not always address the changing costs of each individual independent sector provider, I am satisfied that the level of funding provided is sufficient to cover the cost of paying staff the National Living Wage and the associated Employers National Insurance contributions at the revised rates.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the rationale as to why an uplift to deliver the Real Living Wage for independent domiciliary care workers and the wider social care sector has not been implemented.
answer_text: Severe budgetary pressures meant it was not possible to implement the Real Living Wage (RLW) for independent sector care workers during 2025/26. However, I remain committed to progressing the RLW implementation at the earliest affordable opportunity, and it remains a key priority for the 2026/27 planning process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps his Department is taking to safeguard and enhance Small Pockets of Deprivation funding, recognising that it supports many youth and community organisations.
answer_text: The Small Pockets of Deprivation (SPOD) programme is delivered under  ‘People and Place: A Strategy for Neighbourhood Renewal’ and is administered by the Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE) on behalf of the Department. It was included in the scope of the People and Place review, which has now concluded. A draft strategic framework has been developed and is currently under consideration.It is not anticipated that any changes to the existing programme will take place in the 26/27 financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the Medicines Regulatory Group, to detail the number of (i) investigations; (ii) prosecutions; and (iii) pending prosecutions, around the unlawful possession, importation, advertisement or supply of unlicensed prescription medicinal products for use in the non-surgical cosmetic sector, in the last 12 months.
answer_text: The risk to public safety posed by the unlawful distribution and misuse of unauthorised medicinal products within the non-surgical cosmetic sector in Northern Ireland remains an ongoing issue and my Department remains committed in its efforts to combat unlawful promotion, supply or misuse of medicines, including those identified for use in the non-surgical cosmetic sector. My Department cannot disclose all information in respect of its investigative work. However, I can confirm during the last 12-month period my Department’s Medicines Regulatory Group (MRG) has conducted 57 investigations focusing on the unlawful possession, advertisement and/or supply of falsified or unlicensed medicinal products for use within the non-surgical cosmetic sector. During this period, one person has been successfully prosecuted and a further ten prosecutions are currently pending.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any steps his Department is taking to introduce a moratorium on the eviction of individuals and families from properties managed by Mears PLC on behalf of the UK Home Office, until the end of Ramadan.
answer_text: Immigration, including the Move On policy, is not devolved and is a matter for the Home Office.  Officials regularly liaise with Home Office on matters relating to Move Ons, however have not been advised of any policy changes due to Ramadan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps her Department is taking to improve safety at the junction of Coshquin Estate and the Buncrana Road, in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: I am advised that officials have assessed this location and advance junction warning signs have been provided and ‘SLOW’ road markings added on the Buncrana Road approaches to Benview Road.  One of these new advance warning signs has since suffered storm damage and will be replaced.  The road markings will also be renewed as part of white line maintenance.Concerns regarding driver behaviour can also be reported to the PSNI using their non-emergency 101 number.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans her Department has to address the poor road conditions in the Ballymagroarty area of the Foyle constituency.

answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“There currently is no change in the position as advised in AQW 33837/22-27 response given in October 2025.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any progress that has been made in implementing regulations under the Health and Social Care (Control of Data Processing) Act (Northern Ireland) 2016, including the development of any regional opt-out mechanism for the secondary use of health and social care data.
answer_text: My Department is currently progressing work to bring a "Bill of Amendment" that will seek to address the issues that have been identified with the Health and Social Care (Control of Data Processing) Act (Northern Ireland) 2016.  The timetable for introduction will remain subject to the NI Assembly’s legislative schedule and processes.In enablement of the Act 2016, the implementation of regulations will include an opt out mechanism for HSC service users. The HSC opt-out system will let service users view or change their preferences on whether their confidential patient information can be used for research and planning.  It is intended that the Business Service Organisation Northern Ireland Digital Identity Service (NIDIS) will manage this service. The Statutory Committee will monitor the opt-out process and can be contacted directly by the public as guided by legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Fundamental Review of Social Housing Allocations, including the removal of the no detriment rule.
answer_text: The Fundamental Review of Social Housing Allocations is now in its final phase. Ten of the twenty proposals have been successfully implemented to date. The remaining scheme changes will take effect at the closure of the project, which is currently planned for April 2027. The exact details of the changes required to the rules of the Selection Scheme to end the practice of “no detriment” are still under consideration.
