question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will engage further with deaf and disabled people to deliberate over changes to the Draft Disability Strategy.
answer_text: I remain fully committed to a meaningful and ongoing engagement process with Deaf and disabled people on a range of issues, including the Disability Strategy. Public consultation on the Strategy is continuing, and my officials have developed a range of accessible engagement opportunities to ensure that as many people as possible can contribute. Officials are currently engaging with Deaf and disabled people and representative organisations across Northern Ireland on the draft Strategy, both online and in person, as I recognise that lived experience is essential to shaping a Strategy that is practical, inclusive and rights based. All feedback received will be carefully considered and fed into further Strategy development, the final content of which remains subject to approval by the Northern Ireland Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of housing applications that have seen a reduction in housing points, since the removal of the no detriment policy, as part of the Fundamental Review of Social Housing Allocations, in each constituency area.
answer_text: There have been no housing applications that have seen a reduction in housing points, as the removal of the no detriment policy does not take place until April 2027.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any engagement he has had in the last 12 months regarding improved pay and conditions for classroom assistants, particularly in relation to ending term-time only contracts,.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA), as employing authority for the majority of classroom assistants in Northern Ireland, has established protocols for regular engagement with trade unions on pay and conditions of support staff.  This includes Joint Negotiating Council (JNC) meetings and the Pay, Terms and Conditions and HR Policy Central Forum.  Departmental officials attend these regular meetings.The current position in relation to the use of term-time contracts for classroom assistants is set out in the ‘Term Time Agreement 2001’. This offers classroom assistants a choice of contract when they commence employment, including the option of a term-time contract.As part of the SEND Reform Plan, we are progressing a strategic review of the classroom assistant employment model. This will examine contracts, job descriptions and a range of other employment-related matters including qualifications, professional standards and professional development. A review of the Term-Time Agreement has been identified as a key part of this work. Initial discussions have taken place with Trade Union side, who are a member of the EA Classroom Assistant Employment Model Project Group.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the renovation project at the Triangle Estate in the Foyle constituency, including a review of the original proposals.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive have carried out extensive remedial works to Waterside Triangle to alleviate the water ingress issues through the Response Maintenance Contract; subsequently the original brief for the Planned Maintenance scheme has since been reviewed with new proposals outlined to provide a long-term solution for the external elements of the properties. To date, we have engaged with the appointed Consultant to review and agree on the revised proposals. The proposals are currently going through the internal approval process and subject to receiving the relevant approvals is it proposed to tender the works in the summer. Subject to a successful procurement exercise and obtaining the relevant approvals, it is anticipated that works would commence in the early part of 2027.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether delayed hospital discharge data is being analysed alongside sheltered accommodation and social housing capacity, specifically in relation to elderly patients.
answer_text: Delayed hospital discharge data is not analysed alongside sheltered accommodation and social housing capacity, in relation to older people. There are regionally agreed "Delayed Transfers of Care” codes utilised by Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts to record and monitor the reason for an individual’s delay in being discharged from hospital, when they are considered medically optimised.  Within these codes are options to identify an individual’s delay relating specifically to housing issues.  In the event, an individual is experiencing a delayed discharge from hospital, secondary to a housing issue the health and social care team will work collaboratively, on a case by case basis, with individuals and other key agencies, such as the Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE) who have the statutory responsibility for housing, including homelessness and social housing assessment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any steps her Department has taken to assist young adults leaving care to access their Child Trust Funds, when a parent can no longer manage their finances at the age of 18.
answer_text: The Department has not taken any specific steps to assist young people leaving care to access their child trust funds. Any such person leaving care can apply to access his or her child trust fund in the same way as any other young person.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has had any engagement with (i) the Department of Health; and (ii) the Housing Executive, regarding strategic alignment between health and housing policy, particularly in relation to early intervention and preventative support services for older people.
answer_text: My Department works in close partnership with the Department of Health (DoH) and the Housing Executive across a range of shared strategic frameworks designed to enhance outcomes for older people and advance preventative health and wellbeing priorities. Housing is recognised as a fundamental determinant of health, and both Departments along with the Housing Executive place strong emphasis on aligning policies to strengthen the delivery of early intervention and communitybased care.An established Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) between DfC and DoH sets out the key working arrangements and responsibilities within and between both Departments. This MoU specifically covers provision of accessible and appropriate housing for people with disabilities, including older people, and underpins a collaborative approach to housing strategy and policy development.DfC jointly chairs the Interdepartmental Accessible Homes Strategic Forum alongside the DoH. Through this Forum, which also comprises representation from the Housing Executive, we provide strategic leadership, oversight, and assurance to ensure that operational service delivery is consistently aligned with wider Departmental policy objectives and the overarching vision for accessible, sustainable homes.The Supporting People (SP) Programme, administered by the Housing Executive on behalf of my Department, grant funds housing support services that enable vulnerable people to live as independently as possible in the community. The programme is focused on four thematic areas working with young people, older people, people with a disability (including mental health and learning disabilities), and people experiencing homelessness.Engagement with DoH is facilitated through the SP Joint Policy Forum and through SP decision making structures. The Joint Policy Forum, chaired by my Department and convened quarterly, provides a formal mechanism for engagement between the DfC, DoH, the Department of Justice and the Housing Executive on matters relating to the SP Programme.Regular engagement also takes place through the SP decision making structures, including quarterly Regional Thematic Groups aligned to each thematic area and the biannual SP Strategic Advisory Board. DoH play an active role in these structures which provide ongoing opportunities for collaboration on issues relating to housing related support for older people.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has had any engagement with the Department for Communities regarding the strategic alignment between health and housing policy, particularly in relation to early intervention and preventative support services for older people.
answer_text: My Department works jointly with the Department for Communities (DFC) across several strategic frameworks aimed at improving outcomes for older people and supporting preventative health and wellbeing objectives. Housing is recognised as a key determinant of health, and the alignment of policy streams is essential to the delivery of early intervention and community-based care.The Department of Health and Department for Communities have an established Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) which sets out the key working arrangements and responsibilities within and between both Departments. The MoU specifically covers accessible housing provision for people with disabilities, including older people, and supports a collaborative approach to housing strategy and policy.My Department, co-chairs the Interdepartmental Accessible Homes Strategic Forum with the Department for Communities. The Forum provides strategic oversight and an assurance framework to ensure that operational service delivery is consistently aligned with wider policy and strategic objectives.A programme of work also continues to progress through the Regional Assistive Technology and Equipment Oversight Board, alongside collaborative Housing and Health partnerships spanning all housing tenures. Together, these programmes are strengthening coordination, streamlining processes, and enhancing service integration to ensure that housing adaptations are delivered in a timely and effective manner in support of the wider early intervention and prevention agenda. These initiatives are of particular importance to older people.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 39804/22-27, to detail the waiting times for red flag breast cancer assessments, for each month from September 2025 to February 2026.
answer_text: My Department is only able to comment on official, validated statistics that have been published through the established reporting process.  Any more recent operational data held by Health and Social Care Trusts remains unvalidated and therefore not suitable for release.Official Statistics for patients seen following an urgent breast cancer referral, by month, are published on a quarterly basis in the ‘Northern Ireland Cancer Waiting Times’ bulletin’s associated Excel file. The most recent issue of this bulletin has a reporting period of 1 July to 30 September 2025 and is available to view at:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/cancer-waiting-timesThe next issue of the ‘Northern Ireland Cancer Waiting Times’ bulletin will have a reporting period of 1 October to 31 December 2025 and will be published on 2 April 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of individuals who have died in non-standard temporary accommodation, broken down by type of accommodation, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“We are aware of one occasion in the past five years in which an individual has died in non-standard temporary accommodation with this death having occurred in 2022/23.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the (i) number; and (ii) value, of projects supported through departmental match funding under the European Social Fund, which have not received equivalent support under the UK Shared Prosperity Fund.
answer_text: None. Funding for European Social Fund Projects ended in March 2023 and did not continue under the UK Shared Prosperity Fund.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the value of European Social Fund match funding provided by his Department, in each financial year from 2016-2017 to 2022-2023.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the establishment of an Infrastructure Commission.
answer_text: Decisions on the way forward will be a matter for the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any actions the Loughs Agency is taking to protect wild Atlantic salmon stocks, to prevent further decline of salmon populations.
answer_text: Loughs Agency utilises its powers under the Foyle Fisheries Acts 1952 to protect all life stages of Atlantic salmon and preserve stocks and prevent further declines in populations. These powers range from protecting migrating salmon from the sea to their spawning grounds by carrying out patrols to apprehend illegal fishers and seize illegal nets, to regulating the angling effort to ensure stocks are not over-exploited. The Agency uses a suite of scientific monitoring systems which provide real-time and predicted stock assessments to inform management decisions and set conditions for commercial and rod fisheries in the rivers in the Foyle and Carlingford catchments. The Agency also monitors and protects the water quality and habitat features to ensure the optimum survival of all life stages of the species, as well as using its powers to prosecute for illegal fishing, pollution and habitat damage in both jurisdictions.Loughs Agency plays an active role at the North Atlantic Salmon Conservation Organisation (NASCO) Council and is a member of a number of working groups and expert committees, all tasked with assessing trends across the Atlantic salmon range and finding solutions and best practice for continuity of approach to management and protection.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the publication of the Mental Health Outcomes Framework.
To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the annual match funding provided collectively by Departments under the European Social Fund in each year from 2016-2017 to 2022-2023.
answer_text: ANSWERThe detail requested in the Member’s question was answered by the former Minister for the Economy, Conor Murphy, on 03 July 2024 and the information contained in that response has not changed. I therefore refer the member to AQW 13040/22-77.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number (i) of applications for emergency accommodation made to the Housing Executive; and (ii) of these which could not be accommodated, within the last 18 months.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The information requested is not readily available from Housing Executive systems. The Housing Executive will seek to offer suitable temporary accommodation to those to whom a duty is owed under the Housing (NI) Order 1988. From 1st April 2024 - 30th September 2025, there were 17,846 placements made to temporary accommodation.While notes are made on individual applicants’ files it is not possible for the Housing Executive to readily identify which households have declined an offer of temporary accommodation, or those who have requested temporary accommodation and are not owed a statutory duty without carrying out a case-by-case review of each application.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the total value of departmental contributions to comparable programmes under the UK Shared Prosperity Fund, in each year since its introduction.
answer_text: There have been no contributions from the Department of Finance to programmes under the Shared Prosperity Fund. This is a British Government fund and my Department does not deliver any programmes associated with it.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the rationale as to why there is no routine public reporting of waiting time and activity data for (a) adult mental health services; (b) psychological services talking therapies; (c) counselling provision; (d) eating disorder services; (e) electroconvulsive therapy services; and (f) wider community mental health services; and (ii) whether his Department intends to introduce regular publication of such data, including any timeframe for doing so.
answer_text: HSC Trusts developed, over many years, its own legacy systems and coding structures, which produced variations in definitions, reporting practice and levels of completeness.  For this reason, we have not had a unified regionwide dataset covering activity, demand, capacity and outcomes.Today, Trust-level information is now drawn from the new encompass workflows.  During the encompass rollout, temporary discrepancies have arisen as historic data is cleansed, workflows aligned and staff re-trained.  This is normal in a transformation of this scale.  Some Trusts have, understandably, reported lower confidence in certain waitingtimes and activity metrics during the early phase.  Governance has been strengthened and validation intensified, while we stabilise new reporting logic. Encompass region-wide rollout concluded in May 2025, and we are now in a structured two-year stabilisation and optimisation phase replacing dozens of legacy systems with consistent coded workflows.  As encompass matures it will provide comparable regional data for planning, public reporting and accountability. Encompass reports are now being used to extract monthly waiting time data, with each trust validating its figures against regionally agreed System Oversight Meansures definitions and timelines, during this validation period.  As confidence levels rise we will transition from internal management information to regular verified reporting that is comparable across the region.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the Severe Weather Emergency Protocol, including (i) the decision-making process; (ii) the authority responsible for activating it; and (iii) any mechanisms used to communicate its implementation to (a) individuals experiencing homelessness; and (b) homelessness support organisations.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“(i) The Housing Executive reviews forecast weather conditions. A SWEP will be considered on each occasion on which a weather warning is issued by the Met Office.(ii) There is no statutory obligation for the Housing Executive to implement a SWEP. The Housing Executive implements the SWEP to ensure availability of accommodation to individuals sleeping rough when there is an increased risk of death due to the weather e.g. temperatures fall near or below freezing.(iii)(a) Social media channels provide details of relevant contact numbers for the Housing Executive, Welcome Organisation and First Housing to ensure any rough sleepers are directed to funded services so that the appropriate advice, assistance, and support can be provided.(iii)(b) The Housing Executive notifies funded partners with outreach services such as the Welcome Organisation in Belfast and First Housing and Support Services in Londonderry. Outside of the two main cities, where a rough sleeper is identified, local staff will take all necessary steps to make contact and make an offer of temporary accommodation for the duration of SWEP.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 35235/22-27, whether the Housing Executive report on processes and controls over both block booked, and routinely used non-standard accommodation, as per recommendation three of the NI Audit Office report on homelessness, will be made publicly available.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The processes and controls, that have been formalised and documented, relate to staff guidance and, consistent with all other staff guidance documents, are not publicly available. The Housing Executive is happy to respond to requests for any specific guidance documents if contacted directly.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will consider introducing Benedict’s Law, a proposed safeguarding measure to address the gap in protections for children with allergies.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer to AQW 41034/22-27 published in the Weekly Answer Booklet for week ending 6th March 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the development of a paediatric stroke pathway.
answer_text: The Health and Social Care system in Northern Ireland follows a national clinical guideline for diagnosis, management and rehabilitation of Stroke in Childhood, which was developed by the Royal College of Paediatrics and Child Health in 2017 and updated in 2021. While the above assists with the recognition and management of stroke in childhood closer working relationships between adult clinicians and paediatricians would be beneficial.The Child Health Partnership is currently establishing a Clinical Advisory Group, chaired by a senior clinician.  This will be made up of paediatric clinical leaders across NI and they will consider a wide range of issues including the development and adoption of specific pathways to aid clinical management.There will be a number of potential areas for consideration by this group and they will decide using best clinical guidance on what the key priorities will be for the Group to consider.  The issue of a regional pathway for managing stroke in children will be raised once the group is convened.  They will then decide on next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount of compensation that her Department has paid for damage to cars, due to potholes or other road defects, in Foyle, for each of last five years.
answer_text: The management information my Department holds in relation to roads related public liability claims can be found online at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/road-asset-maintenance-directorate-management-information-roads-related-claims-period-20202021-20242025. Please note that this information is recorded in financial years and by Roads Section Office which broadly align with Council areas. In addition, members should be cognisant of the caveats associated with each of the tables of management information available via this link.Table 7 in the management information details claim expenditure for the last five full financial years broken down by Section Office, claim type and expenditure type. Claim figures have been extracted from DfI's Claims Management System, whereas the financial data has been extracted from the NICS accounting system, Account NI. Unfortunately, as the NICS Accounting System does not record the cause of incidents that lead to compensation payments, it is not possible to disaggregate the amount of compensation paid out by defect type.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of hospital bed-days for those with an eating disorder; and (ii) the number of emergency eating disorder cases, in the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his Department's work to improve access to community and day treatments for eating disorders.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 27826/22-27, to detail when this information will be deposited in the Assembly library.
answer_text: This information was deposited in the Assembly Library on 17th February 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has considered introducing legislation to restrict or ban access to social media platforms, for children under the age of 16.
answer_text: Telecommunications is a reserved matter for which the UK Government has responsibility, which means I do not have the authority to legislate directly in this area.  I note with interest the ongoing consultation by the UK Government on children’s social media use and look forward to the findings of that consultation. Where the consultation identifies gaps in the legal framework, I will engage with the UK Government to ensure that Northern Ireland’s specific issues are considered in the context of any legislative developments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the implementation plan for Action 29d of the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 to enhance regional eating disorder services; and (ii) whether this provision will be secured before the end of the 2022-27 Assembly mandate.
answer_text: (i) Action 29D of the Mental Health Strategy 2021–2031 sets out a clear commitment to strengthening and enhancing eating disorder services through improved community-based provision and a more consistent regional model of care. A Business Case to enhance community-based eating disorder services has been developed and has received partial funding. This has enabled the establishment of an Eating Disorder Managed Care Network (EDMCN) to support the delivery of this commitment.The Implementation Plan identifies four key Strategic Areas; Communication and Training, Access to Services and Workforce, Research, Data and Outcomes and Co-Production, Co-Design and Co-Delivery. These areas align closely with the ambitions of the Mental Health Strategy and set out a credible pathway for improving the quality, accessibility and consistency of eating disorder services across Northern Ireland. (ii) The Mental Health Strategy Deliverability Review has highlighted the impact of significant funding constraints on the pace of implementation. A clear timeframe for further review will be set once there is greater certainty regarding available funding. In recognition of current financial pressures, the Plan prioritises short- and medium-term actions that can be delivered at low or no cost, ensuring progress continues while work to secure further funding is ongoing.Improving access to community and day treatments for eating disorders remains a key priority under Action 29d. The Eating Disorder Managed Care Network’s Strategic Framework and Implementation Plan provides the essential foundation for advancing these improvements. The plan’s focus on standardising regional pathways, strengthening workforce capability and embedding consistent supervision and reflective practice ensures that, once resources become available, the region will be well positioned to expand community and day treatment provision in a safe, effective and sustainable way.(i) To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of hospital bed-days for those with an eating disorder, in the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust:Adult Mental Health Service Admissions:TrustYearDaysBHSCT2019852020674202149202234120231112024020250NHSCT202067020219332022503202378620245312025779SEHSCT2020/20213352021/202210832022/20235332023/20242872024/2025654WHSCT2020/20212442021/20221622022/20231302023/2024582024/2025134The Southern Health and Social Care Trust have provided its data below and advised that it cannot be disaggregated by individual year, as doing so could risk identifying individuals.Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service Admissions Beechcroft operates as Northern Ireland’s regional Child and Adolescent Mental Health inpatient unit. As such, the data relating to children and young people is collected and reported on a regional basis in the table below.Year No. of psychiatric hospital admissions for children presenting with eating disorders2020/2021102021/2022152022/2023222023/202

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the amount raised through the carrier bag levy; and (ii) how it has been distributed, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: Since the Carrier Bag Levy started in 2013, proceeds have totalled £79.07 million. Annual statistics since 2013 can be found at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/northern-ireland-carrier-bag-levy-statisticsIn keeping with its original intention, since the implementation of the Levy, DAERA have used its proceeds to support additional environmental projects, with the added focus of alignment with the strategic environmental outcomes in the Environmental Improvement Plan published in 2024.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she has given any consideration to the introduction of a minimum of five-years before the expiry of gift cards, similar to the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: Given the shortened mandate and the scale of my legislative programme it would not be possible to introduce this legislation in the current mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the progress of the Deposit Return Scheme.
answer_text: The UK Deposit Management Organisation launched the Deposit Return Scheme’s brand name, Exchange for Change, and logo in January 2026 helping to strengthen the scheme’s identity across the UK.Exchange for Change is making progress to ensure successful scheme launch in October 2027, particularly in establishing the necessary infrastructure and appointing key leadership positions. It is also actively engaging with industry stakeholders and suppliers on operational planning as the scheme progresses.  Alongside continued engagement with colleagues across the UK, my officials and Exchange for Change are actively collaborating with Ireland’s scheme administrator, Re-Turn, to draw on existing learning and regarding operational coordination on an all-Ireland basis. This engagement aims to ensure reduced burdens on industry and to avoid any unintended consequences of the separate schemes.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement she has had with the PSNI regarding the use of e-scooters.
answer_text: It is currently illegal to use e-scooters on public roads and public spaces here . Anyone using an e-scooter illegally may be subject to enforcement action by the PSNI.My officials have held discussions with the PSNI on e-scooter use and enforcement at the Road Safety Strategic Forum. The group meets regularly and was established to address road safety issues and support the  Road Safety Strategy to 2030. In addition, the issue of e-scooter use and enforcement has been raised by my officials at the PSNI-led silver command meeting.The Department is exploring measures to raise awareness around the dangers associated with e-scooters. The Department is also monitoring the progress of a pilot scheme for rental e-scooters in Britain, as well as the impact of the legislative changes in the south which made the use of e-scooters legal and regulated their use on public roads from May 2024. This evidence base can help inform the department of what steps to take in relation to the registration and regulation of e-scooters.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when she will answer AQW 37906/22-27.
answer_text: I answered AQW 37906/22-27 on 24 February 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the decline of the hedgehog population.
answer_text: There is currently no reliable population estimate for Hedgehogs in either Northern Ireland or the Republic of Ireland, nor sufficient data to establish population trends. Records submitted to data recording centres show that the species has a widespread range across all counties. Accordingly, the most recent Irish Red List for Terrestrial Mammals (Marnell, Looney & Lawton 2019) regards the conservation status of hedgehogs on the island of Ireland as being of ‘least concern’. While there is clearer evidence of a decline in Great Britain, decline in Northern Ireland is also suspected.  Ulster Wildlife is carrying out work to increase baseline data on the local hedgehog population.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any increases she proposes to make to public transport fares.
answer_text: I chose not to approve any fare increases for public transport in 2025-26.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any work being done to facilitate more purpose-built student accommodation as part of the expansion of the Ulster University campus in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: I remain committed ensuring the availability of high-quality Purpose-Built Student Accommodation to support the expansion of Ulster University’s Magee campus and achieve the ambition of 10,000 students studying there.I recently established an expert working group, chaired by the Department’s Permanent Secretary, to develop a coordinated strategy for delivering sufficient, affordable, and highquality student accommodation to support the expanded Magee Campus. The work of this group will focus on identifying effective delivery mechanisms, funding models, and partnerships that will deliver the student accommodation the city needs, as well as addressing any barriers that exist.It will set out a phased delivery roadmap aligned to the Magee expansion timeline and ensure that all proposed accommodation options promote accessibility and sustainability.The group met for the first time on 26 January 2026 and next meeting is scheduled for 16 March 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) how his Department currently monitors the impact of the Neighbourhood Renewal Programme across different areas; and (ii) any evidence that it is reducing social and economic inequalities.
answer_text: (i) The Department monitors impact of the Neighbourhood Renewal Programme through an Outcomes Based Approach. All funded organisations report against standardised performance measures aligned to the Programme’s four renewal pillars: Community, Economic, Social and Physical Renewal. Returns are validated through routine monitoring and consolidated into an annual Programme-level Outcomes Report Card.(ii) Evidence drawn from the Programme’s outcomes-based reporting in 2024/25 highlighted that over 2,500 local community groups were supported; more than 5,500 adults volunteered in their communities; 30,000 children & young people participated in projects to improve their educational attainment; 1,500 people were helped to secure employment through targeted training and employability interventions; and community groups were able to secure over £40 million in additional external funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Lidl planning application at Crescent Link, in the Foyle constituency.
To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the Local Growth Fund.
answer_text: The Local Growth Fund is due to commence on the 1 April 2026 and unfortunately, British Government Ministers have still, at this late stage, not indicated whether they will rectify the Capital-intensive funding profile with a more appropriate Resource allocation.  The Voluntary and Community Sector, who I met on 4 February, have been very clear that this cut in Resource funding will have a very damaging impact on their organisations and the services they provide. I have raised this matter with Ministers in Westminster repeatedly over many months, and on 22 January, the Executive jointly wrote to Steve Reed, Secretary of State for Housing, Communities and Local Government calling for urgent action on this.  In doing so, we have been clear that it is unrealistic for Westminster to expect the Executive to step in and address harm that they have very clearly caused with their approach to Local Growth at a time when the Executive’s budget position is so constrained and falls far short of what is needed to deliver public services.  Disappointingly, Steve Reed has not yet responded.Notwithstanding this, my officials, with support from the other Executive Departments, have been engaging collaboratively with the NIO on the development of a Local Growth Investment Plan to ensure that this is in place when decisions on funding are made, and to maximise the impact and value for money of the funding that is available. Rest assured, I will continue to press British Government Ministers on this.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the funding allocations for the Neighbourhood Renewal Programme in each local council area, in each of the last eight years.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any requirements the Housing Executive places on temporary accommodation landlords, in relation to electrical safety, including certification of Electrical Installation Condition Reports.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Providers of single let properties are responsible for all aspects of maintenance and repair of their properties, including ensuring Electrical Installation Condition Reports are carried out. Acquisition of single let properties is subject to the provision of relevant documentation, which includes Electrical Installation Condition Reports.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, since he updated the Corporate Identity Guidelines with regards his Department’s letter head, to detail the number of pieces of Ministerial correspondence which included (i) a trilingual language logo; (ii) single language logo; and (iii) an abbreviated single language logo.
answer_text: I am unable to provide the information requested, as records are not maintained in a way that allows my Department to identify the number of letters issued. The cost implications of undertaking a manual review of all Ministerial correspondence are disproportionate and would exceed the advisory threshold and would also be a ridiculous waste of my officials time

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether (i) Electrical Installation Condition Reports for temporary accommodation properties are monitored by the Housing Executive; and (ii) such inspections are routinely carried out.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“(i) Acquisition of single let properties is subject to provision of relevant documentation which currently includes Electrical Installation Condition Reports.(ii) Providers of single let properties are responsible for ensuring Electrical Installation Condition Reports and the relevant inspections are carried out, and the Housing Executive engages with providers to ensure they continue to meet required standards in this regard.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of temporary accommodation properties used by the Housing Executive, that have had an Electrical Installation Condition Report carried out, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The information requested is not readily available from the Housing Executive systems. In order to establish the number of single let properties that have had an Electrical Installation Condition Report carried out, the Housing Executive would need to carry out a manual review of records held regarding all current, and past, single let properties. This would be a significant number of records to account for over the duration requested.Providers of single let properties are responsible for all aspects of maintenance and repair of their properties, including ensuring Electrical Installation Condition Reports are carried out. Acquisition of single let properties is subject to the provision of relevant documentation, which includes Electrical Installation Condition Reports.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of fires that occurred in Housing Executive properties; and (ii) the number of these fires determined to have been caused by electrical faults, in each of the last five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement his Department has had with community garden associations, in each of the last five years.

answer_text: There are currently no plans to update the Allotments Act (Northern Ireland) 1932, therefore I have not made any assessment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of complaints received by the Housing Executive, from individuals, or families, placed in non-standard accommodation, who did not have access to a fridge, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive has not received any suitability reviews or final stage complaints from 2022 to the present that specifically relate to access to a fridge in non-standard accommodation. The Housing Executive would need to carry out a manual review of records to establish this information prior to 2022.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department is aware of any instances in which hotels, used to provide emergency accommodation for homeless individuals and families, have removed fridges from rooms.
answer_text: This issue has not been raised with the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of ADHD patients who have received medication via NHS shared care arrangements, in the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The HSC Business Services Organisation BSO holds data on prescriptions prescribed in primary care and dispensed by a community pharmacist, dispensing doctor or appliance contractor in Northern Ireland.The table below sets out information on individuals in Northern Ireland who were dispensed medication used in the treatment of Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) in primary care between April 2020 and March 2025 - the most recent complete year data available.BSO data does not capture the indication for which the drug has been prescribed/dispensed. Medications prescribed in the management of ADHD may also be prescribed for other conditions.As many of the drugs available to manage ADHD are graded as ‘amber’ it is recommended that the responsibility for prescribing be transferred from secondary to primary care with the agreement of an individual GP once agreed shared care arrangements have been established.The majority of patients will have a shared care arrangement in place. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) whether any consideration has been given to introducing Awaab’s Law, in Northern Ireland; (ii) any steps his Department has taken to address damp and mould in (a) private; and (b) social tenancies.
answer_text: I am currently considering the introduction of Awaab’s Law in Northern Ireland following the Assembly debate on 24th February. However, existing measures and standards provide similar protections. Across all housing tenures, Environmental Health Officers have powers to address damp and mould by enforcing the statutory minimum fitness standard. In respect of the private rented sector, my Department is currently progressing a programme of reform which includes the development of Minimum Energy Efficiency Standards which will help improve thermal comfort and potentially address issues of damp and mould in the poorest quality homes.In respect of the social sector, I am currently consulting on proposals to revise and enhance the Decent Homes Standard. Proposals include an enhanced approach to damp and mould, and the introduction of requirements to risk assess and triage repairs for investigation, including timeframes, with emergency and urgent repairs prioritised appropriately. This approach will align with the principles of Awaab’s Law. Regarding steps taken to date, my Department issued formal damp and mould guidance to all social housing providers in January 2024. Since 2023/24, my Department’s Housing Regulation Branch has undertaken periodic surveys aimed at ensuring that Registered Housing Associations are appropriately managing all cases of damp and mould within their stock, have policies and procedures in place to address damp and mould and provide regular reports to their respective boards.The Housing Executive regularly reports to my Department on management of damp and mould within its stock. The need for investment is well-documented, and achieving access to borrowing for the Housing Executive is my highest priority. My recent approval of the Housing Executive's annual rent increase will see an additional £16.8M raised, which will directly support investment in its housing stock.

answer_text: I have previously provided a response to this question and would refer you to AQW 35660/22-27, which was answered on 8 January 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of discussions he has held in relation to defective concrete blocks; (ii) the bodies, agencies or stakeholders involved in those discussions, in the last three years; and (iii) the current position on exploring potential avenues of redress for homeowners affected by defective blocks in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Whilst I have had no direct meetings my officials have engaged with stakeholders in the matter of defective blocks.  My officials have engaged with the following over the last 3 years, Derry and Strabane District Council, Irish Ministry for Housing Local Government & Heritage, The Housing Agency (which administers the redress scheme in the Republic of Ireland) and the UK Ministry for Housing, Communities and Local Government. There has also been engagement with the resident affected by defective blocks as well as elected representatives.  To date my Department has been advised of 8 cases of defective blocks, 6 of which have been remediated by the social housing provider responsible for the properties and the others by the private property owners.  The number of cases identified does not suggest a systemic issue is prevalent with concrete blocks within Northern Ireland.  As such it does not would not warrant a redress scheme at this point in time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has assessed the impact of the removal of the two-child limit on transitional protections.
answer_text: Subject to the consent of the Assembly to the Legislative Consent Motion, the Universal Credit two-child limit will be removed from April 2026. Following this change, households who are currently affected by the two-child policy will see an increase in their underlying Universal Credit entitlement. This is expected to reduce any Universal Credit transitional element that may be in payment. My officials are currently undertaking analytical work to determine the impact of this change.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) any pathway in place to ensure timely access to prescribed medication for newly received prisoners, particularly those dependent on antipsychotic or other mental health-related medication; and (ii) any safeguards in place to prevent the interruption of treatment on entry into custody.
answer_text: Upon committal into custody, each individual receives an initial healthcare assessment, which includes identifying their currently prescribed medication.  The Primary Care Nurse will identify any medications deemed critical and will ensure these are prescribed, supplied, and administered as soon as possible. Where a patient brings their own medications into custody, there is an established process for verification, prescribing, and administration/supply. In situations where immediate verification of medication is not possible, an appropriately qualified prescriber may, based on risk assessment and clinical judgement, provide interim or emergency prescribing. These pathways are followed for individuals prescribed antipsychotic or other mental health-related medications to ensure accurate identification and reconciliation of prescribed treatments. A number of safeguards are implemented to minimise the risk of treatment interruption when a person enters custody. These include conducting two healthcare assessments and a mental health triage assessment within seven days of arrival into custody.  Where patients do not consent or are unable to complete these assessments within the timeframes,  this will be escalated and an incident report is completed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in relation to the Special Rules for Terminal Illness, to detail the number of applicants who died before receiving their first benefit payment, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department does not hold the requested information.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when her Department will grant the necessary approvals for the upgrade of Caw Roundabout, in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: The upgrade and signalisation of Caw Roundabout is a transport mitigation measure arising out of the adjacent H30 Rosses Gate residential development. As a developer led scheme, Southbank Square is responsible for design and construction of the works. My Department’s role is to ensure the design meets the required standards and the final built road layout is safe for all road users.My officials continue to work with the developer to progress the detailed design of the Caw Roundabout upgrade. I met with the developer on site to discuss the proposals and have committed additional resource to ensure a timely review of all elements of the detailed design once they have submitted the required information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in relation to school absences, particularly chronic absences, to detail any steps his Department has taken to ensure that the new school administration system, Education Information Solutions, enables a more detailed recording of absence, including where absences are linked to poor mental health or neurodivergent-related need.
answer_text: The new Management Information System Bromcom has been designed and developed to meet the attendance code requirements as outlined by my Department.  My officials issue attendance coding guidelines through circulars and policy to schools and any can be reflected in the Bromcom system through a change control that will be charged to EdIS and the Department. At the start of this academic year, my officials introduced a new attendance code for Emotional Based School Non-Attendance and temporary exemption to the curriculum, allowing us to quantify reduced attendance or non-attendance at school where the avoidance is driven by emotional distress.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 34631/22-27, whether her Department will consider expanding the assessment of a one-way system, to include Central Drive, Creggan, in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: I have asked officials to consider the introduction of a one-way system on Creggan Heights.  However, I would caution that one-way systems tend to increase travel distances for residents and may cause confusion for drivers and visitors. One-way systems also have a tendency to increase traffic speeds in the direction of travel.The inclusion of a one-way system to include Central Drive is likely to place more traffic on the already tightly parked Creggan Heights and my officials would not generally consider this proposal without evidence of widespread local support for such a measure.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps that are being taken to address the increasing waiting times for red flag breast cancer assessments.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail current waiting times for red flag breast cancer assessments, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Due to the implementation of the Regional waiting list, breast waiting times are now reported regionally and no longer on an individual Trust basis.Official Statistics for patients seen following an urgent breast cancer referral are published on a quarterly basis in the ‘Northern Ireland Cancer Waiting Times’ bulletin. The most recent issue of this bulletin has a reporting period of 1 July to 30 September 2025 and is available to view at: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/cancer-waiting-times Figures for breast cancer patients seen within 14 days following an urgent referral are published in Table 5. A new regional breast waiting list was launched on 8 May 2025 and a breakdown by Trust is no longer applicable.My Department is actively monitoring the situation and taking immediate steps to improve access to timely and appropriate care. We remain fully committed to working closely with Trusts and stakeholders to implement sustainable solutions that safeguard the delivery of high-quality breast services. Patients can be assured that addressing these challenges is our top priority, and we are focused on ensuring timely care for everyone who needs it.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any actions her Department is taking to ensure that residents living within the Derry City Walls, particularly along Magazine Street, including those with mobility issues, have access to safe and reliable parking.
answer_text: I am aware that the Department for Communities (DfC) has funded a public realm scheme in Magazine Street and adjoining areas which will provide an improved setting for the Historic City Walls. My officials are working with their counterparts in DfC to consider public concerns raised as a result of this work and determine if further interventions may be appropriate both during and after the construction work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any supports available to prisoners suffering from drug and alcohol addiction.
answer_text: The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust is responsible for healthcare in NI prisons. They ensure that there is:•	Access to addiction medicine clinicians and wider healthcare support;•	Continuity of care for withdrawal management, maintenance therapies, mental health support, and physical health needs; and•	Integration with wider community services upon release (including referrals to community addiction teams).The Trust contracts with Start 360 who run the Alcohol and Drugs: Empowering People Through Therapy (AD:EPT) programme, and provide harm reduction and safety measures such as:•	Core harm reduction awareness;•	Addictions assessment and individualised care planning;•	One to one casework;•	Behaviour change counselling;•	Relapse prevention sessions;•	Pre-release support for continuity of care;•	Failed drug test review sessions;•	Delivery of approved substance misuse programmes; and•	Harm reduction education and paraphernalia management.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any assessment he has made of the impact on the community and voluntary sector, should the Local Growth Fund remain underfunded.
answer_text: I am aware of the significant impacts, including potential job losses, that the reduced financial allocations proposed by the UK Government under the Local Growth Fund will have on community and voluntary sector organisations.These organisations are delivering important services to support those furthest from the labour market and I will continue to engage with other Executive Ministers and with the UK Government to seek a resolution to this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any practical mechanisms his Department will take to allow the continued supply of essential veterinary medicines from Great Britain to Northern Ireland; (ii) any measures to address excessive price disparities; and (iii) any interim protections for animals already established on long-term prescription treatments.
answer_text: In line with the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the supply of veterinary medicines to Northern Ireland falls under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra). Furthermore, the Veterinary Medicines Directorate (an Executive Agency of Defra) is the Competent Authority for veterinary medicines in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients who have died while waiting for their first consultant-led appointment, in the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: My Department does not hold data in a format that would allow me to report this information. There is currently no routine reporting of deaths occurring while patients are on a waiting list for an initial consultant appointment, nor is there a regional agreement on how such events should be defined or recorded. Capturing this information would require a standardised definition and consistent reporting processes across all Health and Social Care Trusts, which are not currently in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any engagement he has had with Executive colleagues regarding contingency arrangements, should the UK Government fail to provide sufficient funding for the Local Growth Fund, particularly in light of the reduced £9.3 million allocation for Economic Inactivity 2026-2027.
answer_text: I discussed the current situation regarding the Local Growth Fund with my Ministerial colleagues at the Executive meeting on 22 January 2026, after which we collectively wrote to Steve Reed, Secretary of State for Housing, Communities and Local Government, calling on him to work with his Ministerial colleagues to rectify the profile of the Local Growth Fund here with a more favourable Resource allocation immediately.I have been very clear that the current Resource funding shortfall is a direct consequence of Brexit and the loss of EU funding, which led to reduced funding by the British Government for SPF and its successor the Local Growth Fund. I have also made it clear that it is unrealistic for Westminster to expect the Executive to step in and address the harm that it has caused with its failed approach to Local Growth. This is particularly the case at a time when the Executive’s Budget position is already highly constrained and falls short of what is needed to deliver sustainable public services. While we await Mr Reed’s response, I wrote to Executive Colleagues on 12 February asking them to consider if there are opportunities within their departments to support the sector in other ways and suggesting they meet with sector representatives to discuss this. My officials also continue to engage with their colleagues across departments on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 36504/22-27, to detail the number of Serious Adverse Incidents raised in relation to missed abnormal cervical screening tests in the Western Health and Social Care Trust, in the last 12 months.
answer_text: The Western Health and Social Care Trust advise that from 1 January 2025 to 31 December 2025, there was one Serious Adverse Incident registered in relation to a missed abnormal cervical screening test.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, following the Arts Council survey published in October 2025, which shows a 30 per cent decline in the number of performances over the past three years, due to rising running costs, to detail any interim measures that will be introduced to protect arts organisations in the absence of a fully funded arts strategy.
answer_text: The 2024/25 Annual Investment Survey carried out by the Arts Council focuses on organisations funded through the Annual Funding Programme and Lottery Funded Programme providing a key insight into the arts sector.I will continue to support the arts sector by funding the Arts Council which has a strategic priority to strengthen the investment environment and create the conditions for a more financially stable arts sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has given further consideration to issuing a public consultation into mandatory licensing for non-surgical cosmetic procedures.
answer_text: My Department has no current plans to issue a public consultation into mandatory licensing for non-surgical cosmetic procedures.The risk to public safety posed by the unlawful distribution and misuse of unauthorised medicinal products within the non-surgical cosmetic sector in Northern Ireland is a serious issue. My Department continues to take decisive action to combat illegal promotion, supply or misuse of medicines and to alert the public about the dangers of using unauthorised medicines outside of the regulated supply chain. We remain vigilant and continue to work with key partner agencies to monitor the illicit marketplace and will continue to take effective action where necessary to protect public safety in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, in relation to the recent Civil Service recruitment drive in the north west for Administrative Officer posts, to detail (i) the number of candidates currently on the merit list; and (ii) the expected timeframe for candidates to be informed of the outcome.
answer_text: (i) 218 candidates have been offered a post to date, with 449 candidates on the merit list.(ii) All candidates have been informed of the outcome and are aware they are on a merit list for vacancies when they arise.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps he will take to ensure that Housing Executive decant properties, which have been left vacant because planned maintenance schemes have not been funded or have been delayed, are being effectively utilised.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in relation to the resurfacing of the Strand Road, Derry, whether her Department will consider conducting works at night.
answer_text: Officials will be meeting with the contractor, PSNI and other key stake holders to discuss how best to deliver the Strand Road resurfacing scheme.  All options, such as nighttime working and restricted working hours will be considered to ensure the scheme is delivered safely whilst also minimising disruption to road users, local businesses and residents.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of new social homes which have been delayed, in each local council area, due to (i) outstanding planning; or (ii) water connection issues, in each of the last 3 years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive manages the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) on behalf of the Department for Communities. Each SHDP year, schemes projected to start on site can move into future programme years for a wide range of reasons, including (but not limited to) delays in securing Planning Approval, NI Water approval processes, land title issues, conveyancing/legal matters, viability challenges, and wider development constraints. Given these multiple and often concurrent factors, it is not possible to isolate with precision the extent to which any slippage in scheme start dates is attributable solely to Planning timeframes or NI Water connection approvals. NI Water related approvals can also affect projected completion and handover timelines, further complicating attribution.Similarly, delays in the Planning process can arise from several sources—for example, extended response times from statutory consultees, requests for additional information, or delays on the part of the applicant or agent. These variables make it challenging to provide a definitive or comprehensive analysis of Planning performance against statutory or non-statutory target timeframes for new social housing proposals. It is also important to note that Planning and NI Water processes can be interlinked. For example, Planning Approval may be dependent on the progression of Wastewater Impact Assessments or confirmation of network capacity from NI Water. In such cases, delays cannot reasonably be attributed to a single organisation, as the sequencing and dependencies are shared.Despite these inherent uncertainties, SHDP performance for 2022/23–2024/25 demonstrates that annual targets for new social housing starts and completions have been achieved within available capital budgets. This reflects the Housing Executive’s proactive management of the programme, including the ability to mitigate delays by bringing forward alternative deliverable schemes when individual proposals encounter issues. Across all of this activity, early and proactive engagement is critical to delivery. The Housing Executive maintains excellent working relationships with NI Water and Local Councils, and these strong partnerships, combined with open communication and collaborative problem solving are essential in navigating system constraints, progressing assessments efficiently, and maximising social housing delivery each year.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the impact that the proposed delivery of the Local Growth Fund will have on mental health services and support.
answer_text: I recognise the considerable concern across the community and voluntary sector regarding the proposed delivery model for the Local Growth Fund and its implications for services that play a vital role in supporting mental health and wellbeing in Northern Ireland.At present, there remains no confirmed delivery model for the Fund in Northern Ireland, and the lack of clarity continues to create uncertainty for all organisations including those that provide early intervention and preventative mental health and wellbeing support. One of the most significant issues is the proposed 70% capital and 30% revenue funding split, which sector leaders have consistently highlighted as being misaligned with the needs of Northern Ireland.  Mental health services delivered through the voluntary and community sector are predominantly revenuebased, relying on skilled staff and sustained, personcentred interventions rather than capital projects. A model weighted so heavily towards capital risks undermining precisely those services that prevent mental illhealth and reduce pressure on statutory services. The Executive has already unanimously expressed its view that urgent changes are required to the Fund to ensure a more favourable revenue allocation and to safeguard vital, peoplefocused services delivered across our communities. We have made clear that reducing the revenue available would be devastating for interventions that promote local growth, increase productivity, and support community resilience, including mental health and wellbeing. I therefore urge the Finance Minister to bring forward clarity on the delivery model for Northern Ireland, and to work with us to ensure a funding profile that reflects our needs. Protecting communitybased and preventative mental health provision is essential not only for individuals and families, but for the wider health and social care system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement he has had with the Minister of Finance regarding the Local Growth Fund in 2026-2027.
answer_text: I wrote to the Minister of Finance on 27 November 2025 highlighting my concerns regarding the significant impact on the provision of employability services arising from the UK Government’s proposed financial allocations under the Local Growth Fund and the emphasis on mainly Capital funding.I also attended the Executive meeting on 17 December where I and my Executive colleagues discussed a paper, provided by the Minister of Finance, outlining possible options and approaches for the allocation of the Resource Funding for 2026/27.Officials from my Department are in regular contact with the Department of Finance through the Future Funding Policy Inter-Departmental Working Group.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on Rates Support Grant funding, following his meeting with the Minister of Finance on 29 January 2026.
answer_text: I met with the Finance Minister on 29 January 2026 and discussed a wide range of budgetary issues, including the Rates Support Grant. No agreement has been reached on my Department’s budget for 2026/27, therefore I have no further update.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department will introduce the four new earned income disregards into housing benefit for supported housing and temporary accommodation residents, scheduled to be introduced in England, Scotland and Wales in Autumn 2026.
answer_text: In line with the principle of parity, it is anticipated that my Department will introduce these new disregards at the same time as the Department for Work and Pensions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of people employed in the hospitality industry, in each local council area, for the last five years.
answer_text: The latest available data on the number of employee jobs in various industry sectors by council area is included as part of the Business Register & Employment Survey (BRES).  The 2022 iteration of BRES is the most recent survey with a breakdown which includes the number of employee jobs by district council area and industry section.The data can be found at the following link:	https://www.nisra.gov.uk/statistics/annual-employee-jobs-surveys/business-register-and-employment-survey

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans her Department has to increase park and ride provision in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: Enhancing connectivity is a key priority of the Department through public transport and active travel, including opportunities for advanced park and ride schemes. As such, funding requirements for park and ride schemes throughout the North continue to be submitted on an annual basis. My Department has identified a list of priority bus and rail-based Park and Ride sites and further potential sites will be considered within the new Transport Strategy and Transport Plans continuing to be developed.Many of these sites are at various stages of development. Whilst I am supportive of expanding Park and Ride schemes where needed, the member will be aware that my Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years. As the capital budget for my Department in 2025/26 is less than required it is critical that available funding continues to be prioritised accordingly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount spent on the patching of the Strand Road, Derry, in July 2025.
answer_text: I am pleased to confirm that footway repair works in advance of the main carriageway resurfacing will commence on Monday 16th February 2026. The resurfacing scheme will include the section of Strand Road between Queens Quay Roundabout and Meadowbank Avenue.Subject to favourable weather conditions, the works will be substantially complete by the end of April 2026, however the Department will keep the public informed of any change.The requested financial information is considered commercially sensitive.  Disclosure of such data could enable others, particularly economic competitors, to identify commercial rates and costs associated with these works, which may potentially undermine the legitimate economic interests of the Department and its contractors.However, once the scheme is complete, my Department will be able to provide the overall cost of the work.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail a timeframe when resurfacing of the Strand Road, Derry, will begin.
answer_text: I am pleased to confirm that footway repair works in advance of the main carriageway resurfacing will commence on Monday 16th February 2026. The resurfacing scheme will include the section of Strand Road between Queens Quay Roundabout and Meadowbank Avenue.Subject to favourable weather conditions, the works will be substantially complete by the end of April 2026, however the Department will keep the public informed of any change.The requested financial information is considered commercially sensitive.  Disclosure of such data could enable others, particularly economic competitors, to identify commercial rates and costs associated with these works, which may potentially undermine the legitimate economic interests of the Department and its contractors.However, once the scheme is complete, my Department will be able to provide the overall cost of the work.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the impact of NI Reval2026 on the hospitality industry.
answer_text: I welcome the Finance Minister's decision to halt the Reval 2026 process and to engage with businesses and others on a fair and equitable way forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the reason why non-returned ballots are considered as negative responses in the Residents Parking Scheme policy.
answer_text: Securing an overall majority of residents’ support for a residents parking zone through affirmative responses ensures that the buy-in threshold is based on clear and measurable support from residents and businesses affected by a proposed scheme, rather than assumptions about unreturned ballots. This is a fundamental part of the demand-led application and assessment process to ensure that more schemes can be delivered across the North going forward and reflects a balanced approach that underpins that schemes are community driven while also being deliverable and sustainable. I would strongly encourage anyone who wishes to get a residents parking scheme to ensure that they respond to the ballots.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any work that has been carried out to improve public transport links to the Ulster University campus, in Foyle.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Currently, Foyle Metro services provide a combined 5 minute frequency to the University of Ulster campus at Magee and this offers opportunities for interchange with other bus and rail services in Foyle Street Station and the North West Transport Hub. This enables connectivity to an extensive network of bus services including Goldline and rural routes for onward journeys as well as the rail network. Connectivity between University of Ulster campuses at Magee and Coleraine is also possible via service 234 connecting the communities Eglington, Greysteel, Ballykelly and Limavady along the route. Translink will continue to work closely with the Magee Campus Expansion Programme regarding future service provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will launch an independent review into Carer's Allowance overpayments, similar to the Sayce Review, commissioned by the Department for Work and Pensions.
answer_text: The remit of the Sayce Review was primarily focused on carers’ experiences in England and Wales, however my Department arranged for stakeholders in Northern Ireland to also provide input. As my Department typically mirrors Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) benefit legislation, processes, and systems, it is expected that the findings of and responses to the UK Review will also be applicable to Northern Ireland. My officials are currently working with their counterparts in DWP to consider the Review’s recommendations, including the need for a reassessment exercise to address potential error in historic earnings-related overpayment cases, and to make plans for implementation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how the £4 million funding allocation, specifically targeted at the rural road network, will be spent.
answer_text: The recently announced additional funding for my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme will deliver additional resurfacing schemes, with c£4m additional funding allocated to the Roads Recovery Fund (RRF) to continue to address the poor condition of our road network. This funding has now been allocated across all four of my Department’s road divisions.The detail on the location of the resurfacing and RRF schemes will be included within the Department’s Annual Report which will be presented to Councils in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in relation to Graduated Driver Licensing changes, whether her Department will engage with the Minister of Justice to ensure that the PSNI has the necessary resources and capacity, to effectively enforce the proposed changes for new drivers.
answer_text: During the development of the Graduated Driver Licensing (GDL) proposals, my Department was in contact with the PSNI in their capacity as a GDL stakeholder.  Their input helped shape the GDL provisions within the Road Traffic (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2016.My Department continues to engage with the PSNI, through the Road Safety Forum; meetings with senior officers in the Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) and Roads Policing; and monthly meetings with the DVA PSNI Liaison Officer.  We also engage with DoJ through the Road Safety Forum, and the February 2026 meeting will have a specific update on GDL.Resourcing and capacity are a matter for the PSNI.
