question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in relation to Bunscoil Cholmcille in the Foyle Constituency, to detail (i) his Department's (a) plan; (b) timeline; and (c) funding mechanism, for replacing the existing huts and classrooms; and (ii) whether it will be delivered as part of a defined project, rather than an open-ended rolling programme.
answer_text: A business case, with estimated costs of £1.6m, was approved on 10 April 2024 for a significant accommodation scheme which will provide Bunscoil Cholmcille with a 160m² Multi-Purpose Hall (MPH), a 28m² school meals servery, toilet provision, administration facilities and ancillary space.  This is a mix of traditional and modular build.  The works also includes the demolition of the existing administration unit, a mobile classroom and the current pupil toilet block.The tenders for an Integrated Consultant Team (ICT), to take forward the detailed design works, return on 23 January 2026 and are currently being assessed.  The appointment is anticipated in May 2026.A master plan for replacing the modular classrooms has been incorporated into the scope of works for the ICT. This approach will ensure the efficient use of space and support of a phased replacement of the modular classrooms. Funding for the modular classroom replacement project will be drawn from the Minor Works capital budget and will be subject to capital availability. The timeframe for commencement of construction will not be known until the ICT is appointed and a project timeline created. This will be subject to planning approval and construction tender approval processes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in relation to approved works for Bunscoil Cholmcille in the Foyle Constituency, to detail (i) when construction will commence on the approved multi-purpose hall scheme; and (ii) the confirmed construction programme.
answer_text: The timeframe for commencement of construction will not be known until the Integrated Consultancy Team (ICT) is appointed and a project timeline created. This will be subject to planning approval and construction tender approval processes.Tenders returned on 23 January 2026 and will now procced through the tender assessment process, subject to available resources, to determine the winning bid.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) the cost of maintaining the former Regional Government Headquarters Site, in Ballymena, in the last 12 months; (ii) a breakdown of these costs; and (iii) any future plans for the site.
answer_text: The Regional Government Headquarters (RGHQ) in Ballymena is a scheduled historic monument considered worthy of protection under the Historic Monument and Archaeological Objects (NI) Order 1995.The Civil Contingencies Division (CCD) in the Executive Office manages the RGHQ in line with the Protocol for the Care of the Government Historic Estate.The following table provides a breakdown of the costs incurred in terms of protecting and maintaining the premises between 1 January 2025 and 31 December 2025.Expense TypeAmountSite visits (mileage expenses)£365.46Maintenance / Repairs / Security£7,299.59Electricity£12,518.03DoF CPD fees£1,636.25Total£21,819.33Officials are working with the Department for Communities Historic Environment Division and the Department of Finance Construction and Procurement Delivery Section to determine the works necessary to return the premises to a safe state which will enable disposal. Those works will include the removal of mould and other contaminants hazardous to health.The undertaking of the works will rely on sufficient funding being made available.

answer_text: Discretionary Support applications are governed by the Discretionary Support Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2016 which can be accessed via Legislation.gov.uk. In respect of claims disallowed due to not meeting the criteria, the reasons include:Application withdrawn by claimant,Claimant not deemed to be in an exceptional or crisis situation which presents a significant risk to the health safety or wellbeing of the claimant,Other resources are available to the claimant,Discretionary Support payment (grant for living expenses) received in previous 12 months.The table below provides a breakdown of unsuccessful claimants who were deemed ineligible in the current financial year due to having received a Discretionary Support payment (grant for living expenses) within the previous 12 months:MonthDisallowed*Grant for living expenses received in the last 12 monthsApr 202520428May 202523638June 202525334July 202529735Aug 202539220Sept 202561629* Disallowed figures include claims that have been withdrawn by claimantFigures are subject to final reconciliation checks prior to publishing in Annual report 25/26

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 38104/22-27, whether officials reviewed the assessment of options provided by the inter-departmental working group in early 2022.
answer_text: Decisions will be a matter for the Executive in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 37609/22-27, whether his Department will implement the routine reporting of deaths occurring whilst patients are waiting in emergency departments.
answer_text: There are no immediate plans to publish statistics relating to the routine reporting of deaths occurring whilst patents are waiting in emergency departments. My Department does not currently hold data in a format that would allow me to report this information.I remain committed to ongoing improvement and transparency in emergency care services. My Department will continue to work with Trusts, GIRFT and professional bodies to strengthen data quality and to identify opportunities to improve monitoring and analysis as part of ongoing service improvement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of items of (i) correspondence; and (ii) complaints that he has received from housing associations, regarding delays to social housing developments arising from water and wastewater infrastructure constraints, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: While this issue has been raised in strategic engagement and discussions with Housing Associations, I have not directly received any correspondence or complaints from Housing Associations regarding delays to social housing developments arising from water and wastewater infrastructure constraints, in each of the last five years. The operational delivery of the Social Housing Development Programme is for Northern Ireland Housing Executive, who engage directly with Housing Associations regularly including regarding timelines for development.The impact of water infrastructure capacity restrictions on residential development is a key barrier to supply and one that is regularly highlighted in strategic discussions between the Department and stakeholders regarding housing delivery.Departmental officials have and will continue to engage with statutory colleagues to establish options to address wastewater capacity issues impacting on housing supply, including within the Development Programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any further support that will be made available to assist local businesses facing financial pressure, due to rising business rates.
answer_text: 75% of non-domestic properties are currently entitled to some form of rate relief, which amounts to around a quarter of a billion pounds worth of support.I am in the process of consulting on enhancements to the Small Business Rate Relief scheme for 2026/27, a scheme which already supports almost 30,000 businesses. The Draft Budget consultation includes a proposal to allocate £10 million in 2026-27 for potential enhancements to the scheme supporting small businesses. Subject to approval, this funding will assist small businesses that are integral to our local communities.  The ongoing cost pressures for businesses are also recognised and reflected in my Draft Multi-Year Budget with a lower uplift for non-domestic ratepayers of 3%, an uplift below the current rate of inflation.My Department has also restored and extended the Back in Business and rural ATM schemes to support businesses and town centres. Industrial derating policy also applies here, providing over £75 million of support. That measure is unique to this jurisdiction and supports over 4,400 manufacturers, a sector which provides around 90,000 jobs within our local economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of items of (i) correspondence; and (ii) complaints that she has received from housing associations, regarding delays to social housing developments arising from water and wastewater infrastructure constraints, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: (i) I have identified six pieces of correspondence in the past five years from housing associations, relating to delays raising from water and wastewater constraints, as set out in the table below:YearNumber of pieces of correspondence2025320242202302022120210(ii) I have not identified any official complaints from housing associations, relating to delays raising from water and wastewater constraints, during this time period.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when she will answer AQW 36946/22-27.
answer_text: I answered AQW 36946/22-27 on 22 January 2026. Further to that answer, I am pleased to confirm that footway repair works in advance of the main carriageway resurfacing will commence on Monday 16th February 2026. The resurfacing scheme will include the section of Strand Road between Queens Quay Roundabout and Meadowbank Avenue.  Subject to favourable weather conditions, the works will be substantially complete by the end of April 2026, however the Department will keep the public informed of any change.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he will consider a rates reduction for businesses in the Diamond, in the Foyle constituency, that have been impacted by hoarding and accessibility concerns arising from conservation works at the nearby Austins Building.
answer_text: The rating and valuation system is determined by established case law and a long-standing statutory framework that supports the stability of the tax base. Individual businesses affected may apply to the District Valuer requesting a review of their Net Annual Value (NAV) to determine if a temporary allowance is warranted. The degree to which the NAV of premises in a locality may be affected is based on the duration of the works required and the severity of impact on the business. In advance of applications from affected businesses, Land & Property Services (LPS) has contacted the Inner City Trust in relation to their planned works and is currently gathering relevant information.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the number of times the Executive Working Group on Mental Wellbeing, Resilience and Suicide Prevention has met in the last two years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the publication of the Anti-Poverty Strategy.
answer_text: Work continues at pace on the Executive’s Anti-Poverty Strategy following the public consultation which ran from 17 June until 19 September 2025. Consultation responses have been analysed by DfC Officials and compiled into a summary report, which has been shared with Executive Ministers for consideration.When this is process is complete, the Anti-Poverty Strategy will be updated, where necessary, before sharing with the Executive for approval. While the Department for Communities is leading on the Anti-Poverty Strategy, it remains an Executive Strategy and final decisions on the timescales for publication will be subject to Executive approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has given further consideration to introducing a Living Over the Shops initiative.
answer_text: A review of the Living Over the Shops scheme piloted by the Northern Ireland Housing Executive in 2002 found that significant barriers remained to delivering homes above retail units, including high adaptation costs, planning and insurance constraints, access issues and building layouts. The review concluded that the scheme made a limited contribution to housing supply or regeneration and there are therefore no current plans to reinstate it. However, the Department is taking forward wider work, aligned to the Housing Supply Strategy, which will consider housing-led regeneration and the reuse of existing buildings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will consider proposals, put forward by the Pothole Partnership UK, around introducing a five year warranty for pothole repairs.
answer_text: On 2nd December, I was pleased to launch our new Road Maintenance Strategy, which is now open for public consultation. The main objectives of the strategy are to improve the overall condition of the road network and enhance safety and quality, by delivering a targeted programme of intelligent maintenance investment and a sustainable maintenance regime. I have also committed to investing £1.3 million in the provision of a ground-breaking full digital survey of the North’s entire road network. This will provide a level of detail that will allow my officials to take data-driven, efficient decisions and interventions.The warranty period is a contractual issue, and while data from the new Road Maintenance Strategy will be of use in an assessment of the value of increased warranty periods, implementation would only be possible as new contracts are let. This Strategy alone will not, however, counter the years of under-investment, and I am committed to working alongside my Executive colleagues to secure appropriate investment in our Infrastructure, and contribute to the delivery of the priorities set out in the agreed Programme for Government.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 37326/22-27, to detail how an easement of £4,449,505 occurred in 2024-25.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the information requested from the Chief Executive who has advised me as follows:    “Local Housing Allowance (LHA) remained frozen at the 2020/21 level in the financial years 2021/22, 2022/23 and 2023/24. LHA was uplifted in April 2024. “As a result of the uplifting in LHA in the 2024/25 financial year, a number of customers no longer had a shortfall between their eligible rent and the rent charged by their landlord, with their full rent charge being met by Housing Benefit or the housing costs element of Universal Credit. This resulted in a reduction in demand for Discretionary Housing Payments in the financial year, therefore the full allocation was not required.“Throughout the 2024/25 financial year the NIHE Housing Benefit Team worked to ensure that customers were aware of the DHP scheme to help maximise uptake by working with the Housing Executive’s Communications Team to develop a bespoke communications plan for DHPs. This included the issuing of a press release to all media and a social media plan with bespoke graphics to ensure NIHE raised awareness of the scheme to help and support as many people as possible.“In addition, in January 2025 the NIHE Housing Benefit Team wrote to 18,681 customers in receipt of Housing Benefit and living in the private rented sector to advise them that if they had a shortfall between the rent charged by their landlord and the rent used to calculate their Housing Benefit award, they may be entitled to additional financial help with their rent charges through the DHP scheme, and advised customers of how to make an application.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of cancer patients who have died while on a waiting list for (i) diagnosis; and (ii) treatment, in the last three years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Department does not collect or publish statistics on the number of cancer patients who died while on a waiting list for (i) diagnosis or (ii) treatment.In relation to part (i), it is not possible to robustly identify individuals as “cancer patients” if they die before a diagnosis is confirmed. Where an individual die before diagnostic assessment is completed, there is no verified cancer diagnosis on which to base classification for statistical or reporting purposes. Consequently, this information cannot be produced in a meaningful or reliable way using current administrative data.In relation to part (ii), while Health and Social Care Trust information systems may record that a patient is removed from a waiting list due to death, this information is not collected, standardised or qualityassured for analytical or statistical reporting. It does not provide detail on the cause or circumstances of death, nor does it allow confirmation that death occurred while a patient was actively awaiting cancer treatment, or that it was related to their cancer diagnosis. The use and interpretation of such administrative information also varies across services and over time, limiting its reliability and comparability.Mortality data for Northern Ireland are collected through death registrations by the General Register Office and published by the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (NISRA). Cancer incidence, survival and mortality statistics are produced by the Northern Ireland Cancer Registry (NICR). These datasets are not routinely linked to waiting list information held by Health and Social Care Trusts in a way that would allow the figures requested to be derived to an acceptable statistical standard.Producing this information would require a large-scale study linking deaths to cancer diagnoses, involving a retrospective audit of each patient’s health and care pathway. This would be labourintensive, costly, and timeconsuming. In addition, cancer registrations are only fully verified and confirmed by the Northern Ireland Cancer Registry (NICR) after a time lag (currently more than two years), which further limits the timeliness of such analysis. There are currently no plans to undertake an audit or research project to generate this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps his Department is taking to improve gaps in support for pupils with ADHD, including to prevent disengagement and absences from school.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised the Graduated Response Framework (GRF) sets out clear expectations for all mainstream schools in providing high quality, early intervention for pupils with emerging or identified Special Educational Needs (SEN), including attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD). It ensures that school staff follow a structured, evidence based approach to identifying needs, planning support, implementing strategies, and reviewing progress. The EA Local IMPACT Teams (LIT) model is currently building on legacy services’ models of support for pupils identified as having ADHD within existing staffing resources. Since January 2025, all Special Educational Needs Co-ordinators (SENCos) can use the new online ‘Request for Involvement’ (RFI) portal to seek support for children and young people with special educational needs, including ADHD, from EA SEND Services and to request training to develop staff knowledge and understanding of ADHD and how to support pupils diagnosed, awaiting assessment.When pupils also have low attendance or Emotional Based School Non-Attendance (EBSNA) LIT staff collaborate with EA colleagues in Educational Psychology and the Education Welfare Service to provide support.The EA and associated guidance define early intervention for EBSNA as the process of identifying and addressing the emotional, social, and environmental factors that contribute to a pupil’s difficulty in attending school as soon as concerns arise. Early intervention is recognised as the most effective approach to preventing escalation and supporting positive outcomes.

answer_text: As of the end of November 2025, the number of children and young people waiting for an autism assessment across HSC Trusts is as follows: Number of children and young people waiting for autism assessment broken down by HSC Trust*HSC Trust WaitlistBHSCT 4,868NHSCT 5,027SEHSCT 2,620SHSCT 1,841WHSCT 2,773     *As part of the encompass roll-out, all Health and Social Care Trusts have now gone live with a new electronic patient record system. question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they will consider establishing a cross-departmental taskforce to address homelessness among (i) care leavers; and (ii) individuals with complex needs.
answer_text: Along with Minister Nesbitt I recently launched the New Foundations Programme to address homelessness among Care Leavers.The Homelessness Strategy Steering Group is cross-departmental and includes representatives from the voluntary and community sector.  My officials continue to engage with colleagues in relation to supporting those with complex needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he intends to review the Common Funding Scheme.
answer_text: I will decide on carrying out a review of the Common Funding Scheme as part of my wider consideration of the recommendations arising from the Independent Review of Baseline School Funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any urgent actions being taken to improve whole-system patient flow in order to reduce pressures in Emergency Departments; including (i) seven-day discharge services; (ii) social care capacity; and (iii) shared responsibility for patient flow, across hospital departments.
answer_text: My Department recognises the significant pressures facing Emergency Departments (EDs) and the impact these have on ambulance handover times and wholesystem patient flow. Through the Overarching Winter Preparedness Plan 2025–26, urgent actions have been implemented across hospitals, community services and social care to reduce these pressures.(i) Sevenday discharge servicesImproving discharge processes, including sevenday working, is central to improving patient flow. Trusts are strengthening Early Review Teams, discharge coordination and allied health professional capacity to support timely senior decisionmaking and reduce avoidable delays.Additional shortstay inpatient beds and intermediate care beds are supporting stepdown care, alongside expanded Same Day Emergency Care and ambulatory pathways across acute medicine, respiratory, cardiology and surgical specialties. Frailty at the Front Door services are also in place to improve early decisionmaking for older patients and avoid unnecessary admissions.(ii) Social care capacityWithin the Winter Preparedness Plan, my Department committed to more robust implementation of measures to improve social care flow including focusing on areas to address social care capacity.  Measures outlined below are being undertaken in collaboration with health and social care Trusts.Early Review Teams, introduced in 2023–24 and expanded since, have reviewed over 4,100 individuals, resulting in reduced or no ongoing home care need for almost half, freeing up over 12,000 weekly care hours and improving system efficiency. This early review team model continues to demonstrate the benefits for individuals in receipt of home care by ensuring critical care needs are being met and by optimising autonomy and independence. Regional consistency has also been implemented for the protection of care packages during hospital admission, ensuring packages are available immediately when patients are medically fit for discharge. This approach was implemented across all Trusts in Quarter 3 and is supporting faster discharge. It is considered that adopting a consistent approach across all Trusts will assist in expediting discharge arrangements for those patients assessed as being medically fit to leave hospital.Digital solutions are strengthening capacity management, including Careline Live, now live across all five Trusts, providing realtime oversight of home care capacity, and a regional digital platform for care home bed availability, currently in development and due to be in place in 2026–27. It is acknowledged that on occasions not all the hours per week saved through the Early Review Team and Careline Live initiatives can be fully recycled as the capacity available may not match the geographical areas of unmet need.The Trusted Assessor model has now been introduced across all Trusts to support timely discharge to care homes. The underlying principle of the Trusted Assessor role is associated with promoting safe and timely discharges

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will extend the benefit cap mitigations indefinitely, to ensure families are not adversely impacted by the removal of the two-child benefit cap.
answer_text: It will be for the Executive to agree funding for the extension of those welfare mitigations schemes that are due to end in March 2028.

answer_text: The biannual Northern Ireland Homelessness Bulletin April - September 2025, contains information on a range of areas relating to homelessness and is available at: https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-homelessness-bulletin-april-september-2025. Whilst the data is not available in the format you have requested, Section 1 provides details on homelessness presentations including Table 1.1 Homeless presenters by reason. Table 1.1C provides further detail on the reasons for loss of private rented accommodation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of times he has met with his counterparts in the Department for Work and Pensions, in relation to Carer's Allowance overpayments; and (ii) any actions being taken to (a) support affected claimants; and b) promote awareness of appeal options.
answer_text: I met with Sir Stephen Timms, Minister for Social Security in the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) on 25 November 2025 to discuss a number of issues including Carers Allowance overpayments. In addition, my officials engage monthly with their DWP counterparts on this matter.  (a) My Department introduced the Verification of Earnings & Pensions (VEP) service for CA claims in Northern Ireland from May 2025. This service helps to identify overpayments at the earliest opportunity, thus preventing them from accumulating over time. A carer experiencing financial difficulty because of an overpayment can seek advice from the Department’s Debt Management team to agree a sustainable repayment plan or to access other debt recovery discretion. The Department is reviewing customer communications to ensure customers are aware of the importance of reporting changes promptly and the methods in place for reporting a change. (b) Claimants are informed of their dispute and appeal rights when notified of the outcome any decision on their claim. In addition, overpayment notification letters advise that in certain exceptional circumstances, the Department may waive recovery of all or part of an overpayment. This information is also available on NI Direct.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 36575/22-27, to detail the locations of the two Cavity Wall Insulation Schemes, comprising of 179 homes in the Derry and Strabane area.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows: “The first scheme located within the Ballymagroaty estate, comprises of 119 dwellings. This project commenced on site on 19 January 2026.The second scheme located within the Creggan estate area, comprises of 60 dwellings. Internal approvals for this scheme are scheduled for 9 February 2026, following which work is intended to commence imminently afterwards.”

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 24819/22-27, to detail whether any further consideration has been given to including Ashfield Terrace and Kerrs Terrace, Creggan Road, within the Foyle constituency, in the Urban Villages Initiative.
answer_text: Urban Villages continues to deliver in the Bogside, Fountain and Bishop Street areas. As part of future planning, all areas are being considered to determine where the programme’s investment and support could have the greatest impact.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 32006/22-27, for an update on the inclusion of parents of sick children in the Healthcare Travel Costs Scheme.

To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant AQW 35113/22-27, to detail the findings of his Department's assessment of the impact of the House Sales Scheme on levels of housing stock and homelessness.
answer_text: The Department’s assessment is that there have been steeply declining sales since 2011. 208 Housing Executive social homes were sold last year, down from 356 in 2023/24. The scheme has minimal impact on levels of housing stock and no direct impact on homelessness.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will make any changes to ensure that the Homelessness Strategy Steering Group is independently chaired, in order to strengthen accountability.
answer_text: The Public Accounts Committee is progressing an inquiry into homelessness in Northern Ireland. I will consider any recommendations arising from their report in due course including those in relation to governance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the Proposed Draft Budget 2026-29/30 contains sufficient resource for Mobuoy remediation.
answer_text: The public consultation on the draft remediation strategy for the Mobuoy site closed on 2 October 2025. The views of the consultees are being carefully considered to inform any necessary amendments to the draft remediation strategy. My department’s appointed Integrated Consultancy Team is progressing the technical review of the responses.Any necessary updates to the strategy will be determined further to the assessment of the consultation responses and an updated cost estimate for delivery of the strategy will follow. Any implementation plan and timetable for remediation remains subject to the outcome of the public consultation and will be considered in the context of the Department’s responsibility to protect water quality and the challenging constraints of public finances.The process to deliver any remediation strategy, obtain Executive approval and undertake the work will take a number of years. There will be important considerations to be made on the availability of funding and the realistic timescales required to deliver a remediation solution.Safeguarding public health, ensuring safe drinking water, and reducing the environmental impact of the Mobuoy site is of paramount importance to me. To that end, NIEA continues to implement a comprehensive Environmental Monitoring Programme at the site. The projected Resource DEL to deliver this very important Environmental Monitoring Programme and the planning towards remediation are included in my Proposed Draft Budget 2026-29/30.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 34500/22-27, to detail the number of successful candidates that have now taken up employment.
answer_text: A total of 218 successful candidates for the recent Civil Service Administrative Officer North West pilot competition have been made offers to commence employment from 26 January 2026 onwards.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has considered replicating the East Belfast Mission's Hosford Community Homes project, to tackle housing need across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Department is working closely with East Belfast Mission on its Community Homes project by providing support through the Loan to Acquire Move-on Accommodation (LAMA) programme with Financial Transactions Capital. My ambition is to continue to expand LAMA to provide support to those who need it.Full details of the Loan to Acquire Move-on Accommodation which was launched in December 2024 and be found at this link - https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/articles/loan-acquire-move-accommodation-lama

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of Carer's Allowance overpayments; (ii) the cost of these overpayments; (iii) the amount of money that has been recouped; and (iv) the number of mandatory reconsiderations requested, in the last twelve months.
answer_text: There were 8,541 Carer’s Allowance overpayments in the last 12 months1  There total value of recoverable Carer’s Allowance overpayments is £9,586,665 in the last twelve months1£1,927,361 has been recouped in respect of Carer’s Allowance overpayments in the last twelve months2346 mandatory reconsiderations have been requested in the last twelve months3Please note that the data provided in the answers above is taken from operational administrative systems. Therefore, the data itself is not quality assured to the standard of published Official Statistics. The data provided covers the period from 01 January 2025 to 31 December 2025. 1The high volume and value reflects the work completed using the Verify Earnings and Pensions system which has identified older overpayments that had previously gone unreported and undetected.2Overpayments may not be recovered (either partially or fully) in the same year as they have been referred for recovery. The figures provided show the total value of Carers Allowance overpayments recovered during this period, regardless of the year referred.3The figure provided is the total number of mandatory reconsideration requests received by the Carers Allowance Branch during this period. Separate figures are not held for requests against the entitlement decision and the overpayment recoverability decision as one request often covers both scenarios.

The Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) allocates ambulance crews flexibly to ensure safe and effective coverage across Northern Ireland. Daily crew numbers vary across Trust areas due to operational factors such as call demand, staff availability and regional deployment priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will consider installing grit boxes at Campsie Industrial Estate, Derry.
answer_text: Salt bins are provided for use by the public, on a self-help basis on public roads which do not qualify for inclusion on the gritting schedule, providing the necessary criteria are met.Residents and proprietors can make an application to the Department to request the installation of a salt bin in their area however, provision of new salt boxes is not automatic and may be refused if the location in question is not on the publicly maintained road network; the gradient of the road in question is less than 5%; or if reasonable alternative routes are available.When considering requests for the provision of a salt bin, my officials carry out an evaluation using a criteria based system, which takes into account various factors including topography, traffic volume, road gradient/geometry, alternative routes as well as other factors such as residential usage and community facilities including schools, hospitals and care homes for the elderly.My officials have carried out an assessment for the provision of grit boxes at Campsie Industrial Estate and unfortunately it does not meet the current criteria and a salt bin cannot be provided.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of patients on the waiting list for orthopaedic surgery; and (ii) the longest waiting times, in the last two years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Waiting list statistics, broken down by specialty, Trust, and waiting time cohort are published each quarter on the Department of Health’s webpage. This information can be accessed using the following webpage address:  https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/topics/hospital-waiting-times-statistics.The Elective Care Framework sets out the strategic roadmap for tackling the backlog of patients waiting for surgery and, while core capacity is being developed, my Department will continue to ensure that additional capacity is secured to help address our current backlog. This will include the funding of additional in-house waiting list initiative work and the ongoing partnership arrangement with the independent sector for primary joint replacement surgery

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps his Department is taking to support the arts sector, in the North West.
answer_text: The Arts Council, an arm’s length body of my Department, is the responsible body for the distribution of funding and development of the arts in Northern Ireland, which includes supporting the arts in the North West.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail which Health and Social Care Trusts offer non-violent resistance training for parents of children with behavioural needs.
answer_text: As part of a Regional Consultation with parents during Summer 2025, it emerged that families would benefit from training in managing challenging behaviours.  The HSC Trusts approached the Challenging Behaviour Foundation and they have been identified to deliver this training.  They will be facilitating workshops co-facilitated by a parent with lived experience which will focus on understanding the challenging behaviours and supporting behaviour change employing positive behaviour support methods.     The training will commence in March 2026 and conclude in June 2026 with all 5 HSC Trusts having the opportunity to train 37 parents. 25 training sessions will be delivered on a face to face basis and 12 online.  Each of the HSC Trusts are currently in the process of identifying parents and carers to participate in the training.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the establishment of an Infrastructure Commission.
answer_text: Decisions on the way forward will be a matter for the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Translink business case for service enhancements on the North West rail corridor, including the provision of an hourly Sunday service between Derry and Coleraine.
answer_text: I fully recognise the need to address regional imbalance and better connect our communities including those within the Northwest. Translink submitted a revised business case in September 2025 for the provision of additional services in the North West rail corridor. This is being considered by my officials.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the All-Island Strategic Rail Review.
answer_text: The Project Prioritisation Project was published in December. This is the first step in progressing the implementation of the Rail Review. It sets out, at a high level, the proposed sequencing of selected projects, identifying early interventions to be delivered in the coming years and major projects to be progressed. The Strategy was published on 15 December 2025 and is accessible at www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/rail-project-prioritisation-strategy-2025.Translink have concluded the feasibility studies linked to the recommendations in the Rail Review. Completion of these studies is a critical first milestone in defining the projects and their viability. A summary report of the outcomes from the studies was published on 17 December 2025 and is accessible at www.translink.co.uk/shapingthefuture.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 36750/22-27, to detail any engagement he has had with the Minister for Communities in the current mandate. around addressing the issue of empty homes,
answer_text: I have engaged with the Minister for Communities in the context of the Executive Housing Supply Strategy 2024 – 2039. Housing, and empty homes within that context, is a multi-faceted issue that is led by the Communities Minister at the Executive through that Strategy. A link to the copy of that Strategy is provided: -https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/housing-supply-strategy-2024-2039

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 30045/22-27, for an update on his Department's commissioned ADHD needs assessment.
answer_text: The final needs assessment was received by the Department on 30 November 2025 (in line with the revised agreed timeframe for completion) and subsequently published on 12 February 2026, noting that any decisions on future commissioning of ADHD services would be in the context of overall affordability.Officials have commenced an options appraisal process to identify what options could potentially be implemented in the short to medium term to help address the need for ADHD services.  The outcome of this work will inform the development of an implementation plan, which is already underway.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of Freedom of Information requests received by his Department, broken down by (i) answered; (ii) partially answered; and (iii) refused, including reasons given for each refusal or partial response, in each of the last four years.
answer_text: Due to the large volume of detail required to provide an answer to your question, I have arranged for this to be placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to expand the role and powers of community pharmacies.
answer_text: The Community Pharmacy Strategic Plan 2030, published in May 2024, was developed in partnership with Community Pharmacy Northern Ireland (CPNI) and outlines the vision and strategic priorities for community pharmacy up to 2030. The strategic plan aims to expand the role of community pharmacists and fully utilise skills of pharmacy teams through the delivery of an increased range of clinical services.  Central to this vision is the progressive implementation of independent prescribing and advanced practice within community pharmacy, enabling pharmacists to provide more patient-centred care and respond effectively to population health needs. The plan also outlines how reforms across service delivery, workforce, legislation and digital infrastructure will support this enhanced clinical role within Health and Social Care.Furthermore, my Department has recently finalised a new three-year Commissioning Plan for 2025-2028, in partnership with CPNI. These new commissioning arrangements will progress the ambitions set out in my Department’s strategic plan to expand the roles for community pharmacy.  Recent expansions to community pharmacy services include Pharmacy First Sore Throat Service and an enhanced Community Pharmacy Palliative Care Network.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 36422/22-27, to provide an update on the details and expected timescale for the establishment of specialist endoscopy centres within the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: My Department is committed to ensuring that we have the right model of care, in the right place, at the right time. In this context, we have secured and delivered a Getting it Right First Time report on gynaecology and are progressing the implementation of its recommendations.A Regional Gynaecology Forum was established to take this work forward. The establishment of Regional Endometriosis Centres in the Western Trust and Belfast Trust, along with the enhancement of local services across all five Trusts, is a key priority.All HSC Trusts provide care for endometriosis, with women referred under the care of a consultant gynaecologist. Gynaecology services are delivered through multidisciplinary teams to ensure holistic, responsive, and appropriate care throughout the patient journey. Each team member—whether a consultant, nurse, or other professional—functions as a specialist within their discipline. Plans and investment for endometriosis services will commence in 2026. Business Cases relating to both the Regional Centres and local service models are expected to be submitted to the SPPG by March 2026 for consideration.More complex endometriosis care will be provided in the Belfast and Western Trusts, supported by multidisciplinary teams of surgeons, including colorectal and urological consultants.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to expand availability of counselling and talking therapies for people experiencing depression and anxiety, in order to reduce reliance on antidepressants.
answer_text: The Mental Health Strategy (MHS) fully recognises the benefits of non-pharmaceutical supports, for example talking therapies and social prescribing, for individuals struggling with their mental health, including those impacted by depression and anxiety. Actions 5 and 19 of the MHS respectively commit to expanding hubs for talking therapies and to further embedding psychological therapies across all steps of care. Talking therapies are available in each Health and Social Care Trust, as well as through various community and voluntary (C&V) sector  organisations. The Public Health Agency currently fund a range of counselling interventions and talking therapies for individuals. These services are placed at various steps across the Stepped Care Model of service provision and include Self Harm Intervention Project (SHIP), Lifeline, the Rainbow Project, and a range of community-based interventions.  A Psychological Professions Forum for NI was launched in October 2025 by the Department’s interim Chief Psychological Professions Officer, to help ensure psychological knowledge and expertise is embedded throughout the health and social care system. A key aim of the Forum is to advocate for the integration of psychological approaches in prevention, treatment, and recovery across all care settings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any work her Department has undertaken, to date, in relation to the regulation of e-scooters.
answer_text: It remains illegal to use e-scooters on public roads and public spaces here anyone using an e-scooter illegally may be subject to enforcement action by the PSNI. The Department is exploring measures to raise awareness around the dangers associated with e-scooters. The Department is also monitoring the progress of a pilot scheme for rental e-scooters in Britain, as well as the impact of the legislative changes in the south which made the use of e-scooters legal and regulated their use on public roads from May 2024. This evidence base can help inform the department of what steps to take in relation to the registration and regulation of e-scooters.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department will consider expanding the eligibility criteria for the JobStart Scheme, to include unemployed individuals who are not in receipt of a working-age benefit due to household income.
answer_text: To make the best use of available funding in a time of constrained budgets the current JobStart scheme focuses on working age benefit claimants. There are no plans to further expand this eligibility criteria.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any criteria her Department uses to determine eligibility for the provision of a salt bin.
answer_text: I refer the Member to his previous AQW 37436 / 22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any efforts his Department has made to tackle empty homes; and (ii) any engagement he has had with his Executive colleagues, regarding this issue.
answer_text: Tackling empty homes is a priority for my Department as part of its wider work to increase housing supply, support regeneration and improve outcomes for communities. My officials are taking forward work to identify potential policy interventions that could be utilised in Northern Ireland to bring vacant properties back into use.My officials have engaged with their colleagues in the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) and the Department of Finance (DoF) in support of this work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has had any engagement with the Department for Communities regarding home adaptations and Disabled Facilities Grant funding, as key elements of preventing avoidable hospital admissions and delayed discharges, particularly among the elderly population.
answer_text: My Department recognises that accessible home environments are critical to delivering upon the preventative health agenda, reducing unnecessary hospital admissions, and avoiding delayed discharges. To ensure effective partnership working, my Department actively engages with the Department for Communities and the Housing Executive on the delivery of home adaptations and the administration of Disabled Facilities Grant (DFG) funding. A member of my Department is the current co-chair (alongside DfC) for the Interdepartmental Accessible Homes Strategic Forum, which sets strategy and policy while driving improved collaboration and decision-making on home adaptations. This work is supported by housing and health partnership forums between HSC Trusts and housing providers. In addition, my Department is represented on the Department for Communities’ Project Board for the Review of Administered Grants, including the DFG.  This collaboration has resulted in a pilot scheme for accelerated adaptations, seeking to improve timeframes for individuals with critical needs.  This, alongside discussions with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive on how housing can support the implementation of the Neighbourhood Care Model, aims to ensure alignment with broader health and social care reforms.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any analysis his Department has undertaken of the impact that increasing school meal costs in post-primary schools will have on low-income families, who do not qualify for free school meal provision.
answer_text: In taking its decision to raise the price of a school meal for those not entitled to free school meals, the Education Authority (EA) recognised that children and young people having access to a nutritious lunch positively impacted both their educational and health outcomes.The EA acknowledged that if a child is no longer be able to access a healthy lunch, it could result in a negative impact on these outcomes for any such children. However, a family not entitled to free school meals retains the choice of whether to purchase a school meal or to make their own arrangements, such as providing a packed lunch. To support parents in this, the Public Health Agency (PHA) publication “Are you packing a healthy lunch?” provides advice and guidance to parents to ensure their child is able to access an affordable, healthy and nutritionally balanced packed lunch. Consequently, if a family decides not to pay for a school meal, the availability of this guidance on preparing a healthy packed lunch means that all parents should be able to ensure that their children are able to access an affordable, healthy lunch, either through the canteen or a packed lunch, without it having an adverse impact on their educational and health outcomes.The PHA publication is available at:www.publichealth.hscni.net/publications/are-you-packing-healthy-lunch-english-and-irish-translation

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any support available to parents who are unable to secure an NHS dental place for young children, particularly where a child has identified speech and language difficulties and has been advised by a speech and language therapist to be seen by a dentist.
answer_text: There is no specific pathway for children with identified speech and language difficulties to attend a dentist under Health Service arrangements. While I am acutely aware of the challenges in accessing a Health Service appointment with a dentist, my Department has been supporting dental practices to register children aged between 0 and 10 years under the Enhanced Child Dental Examination Scheme. Since June 2024, more than 67,000 children have been registered and dentists supported to provide individual oral health advice and fluoride application to these children.  Children may be referred by other Health Professionals to the Community Dental Scheme if they are unsuitable for the primary care setting due to their particular health needs. Good oral health in early years can impact on speech development, eating and the overall health of young children.  In recognition of this, I have provided funding for the refresh and expansion of Happy Smiles, an evidence-based oral health and supervised toothbrushing programme which will be offered to children in Primary one, two and three in schools located in the 20% most deprived areas within each Health and social Care Trust in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will undertake a review of the current eligibility criteria for the provision of grit boxes.
answer_text: Salt bins are provided for use by the public, on a self-help basis on public roads which do not qualify for inclusion on the gritting schedule, providing the necessary criteria are met.Residents and proprietors can make an application to the Department to request the installation of a salt bin in their area however, provision of new salt boxes is not automatic and may be refused if the location in question is not on the publicly maintained road network; the gradient of the road in question is less than 5%; or if reasonable alternative routes are available.When considering requests for the provision of a salt bin, my officials carry out an evaluation using a criteria based system, which takes into account various factors including topography, traffic volume, road gradient/geometry, alternative routes as well as other factors such as residential usage and community facilities including schools, hospitals and care homes for the elderly.The current criteria for the provision of salt bins is considered appropriate, and therefore there are no plans to review it at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any measures her Department is taking to address repeat flooding issues along the Northland Road, in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information and have been advised as follows:(i)    NI Water staff attended this site to investigate flooding on 7 December. It was identified that a manhole cover had been dislodged during an extreme rainfall event. Initial works to make the manhole area safe were completed overnight on 7/8 December. During this work, NI Water’s contractor also ‘jetted’ the sewer to remove an obstruction. The existing manhole cover was subsequently replaced with a hinged manhole cover. (ii)  Investigations were undertaken on 11 and 17 December and included CCTV inspection of the sewer network. During these investigations, roots were identified within the sewer and were partially removed using a root - cutting jet.(iii)  Further investigations on 22 January highlighted that excavation is required to replace approximately 5 metres of the sewer network on Northland Road. These works are now being processed by NI Water’s contractor although a start date is not yet available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of temporary accommodation placements, including Houses in Multiple Occupation, broken down by type; and (ii) the number of these which provide access to kitchen facilities, in each local council area, in the last 12 months.
answer_text: The biannual Northern Ireland Homelessness Bulletin October 2024 - March 2025, published on 12 June 2025, contains information on a range of areas relating to homelessness and is available at: https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-homelessness-bulletin-october-2024-march-2025 	Temporary accommodation placements broken down by type of accommodation are available in section 3.2 of the accompanying tables. 		Whilst the information you have requested cannot be provided in the format you have requested, licensed HMOs are required to provide kitchen facilities as per the Houses in Multiple Occupation (Living Accommodation Standard) Regulations (NI) 2019, available at:  https://www.legislation.gov.uk/nisr/2019/31/made

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps being taken to ensure that his Department's crackdown on benefit fraud ensures claimants who incur overpayments due to official error, or genuine oversight are not named publicly.
answer_text: The names of those convicted of benefit fraud are publicised following a successful conviction in court. Those who incur benefit overpayments due to a genuine oversight or official error are not subject to prosecution and therefore are not named publicly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of (i) grit boxes in the Foyle constituency; and (ii) these grit boxes that were replenished on Monday 5 January 2026.
answer_text: During periods of sustained adverse weather, such as that experienced recently, many of my officials expend significant efforts to salt the Primary Road Network, to ensure the safety of the travelling public.  These efforts must be directed to areas which deliver the most benefit to the greatest number of people, and as such the replenishment of grit boxes is completed when resource is available or can be safely redirected from primary gritting actions.My Department currently has 349 grit boxes placed within Foyle constituency for use by the public on a self-help basis.All grit boxes were replenished prior to the start of the winter season, with 51 grit boxes replenished on Monday the 5th January 2026. Work has been continuing since that date to replenish all the remaining grit boxes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients who have died while waiting, in each Emergency Department, in the last three years.
answer_text: My Department does not hold data in a format that would allow me to report this information. There is currently no routine reporting of deaths occurring while patients are waiting in emergency departments, nor is there a regional agreement on how such events should be defined or recorded. Capturing this information would require a standardised definition and consistent reporting processes across all Health and Social Care Trusts, which are not currently in place.Patient safety remains an absolute priority for my Department and for Trusts. Emergency departments continually review clinical processes, escalation protocols, and staffing arrangements to ensure that critically ill patients receive timely and appropriate care. I am committed to ongoing improvement and transparency in emergency care services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will explore the implementation of a publicly accessible system in Northern Ireland, similar to Scotland’s Trunk Road Gritter Tracker, to allow road users to view gritting activity in real time.
answer_text: In recent years, there have been a number of anti-social incidents involving gritting vehicles from my Department. These incidents have endangered both the vehicle drivers and those who rely on the gritting operation to safely use the road network. Therefore, I do not believe it would be prudent to implement a publicly accessible system to allow road users to view gritting activity in real time at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any efforts his Department has made to ensure Disabled Facilities Grant funding aligns with increasing materials and constructions costs; and (ii) when the funding thresholds were last reviewed.
answer_text: In February 2023, in response to rising materials and construction costs, the maximum amount payable on award of a mandatory Disabled Facilities Grant (DFG) was increased from £25,000 to £35,000. The discretionary “top-up” facility was also increased to £35,000, bringing the total maximum amount payable from £50,000 to £70,000. In line with the legislation governing the DFG, the Housing Executive prepares a Schedule of Works (SoW) for each application based on the Occupational Therapist’s recommendations of assessed need. This ensures that the required adaptations are clearly defined and tailored to the applicant’s circumstances. The costings applied to each SoW are determined using the Housing Executive’s internal Schedule of Rates. This is revised annually to reflect movements in construction industry costs, including labour, plant and materials, and is updated using Building Cost Information Service (BCIS) average building cost indices projected for the year ahead. This ensures that the rates used in valuing grant-aided works remain aligned with prevailing construction costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any measures her Department will take to address ongoing flooding issues along the Foyle Road, in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: I have been advised by NI Water that it is undertaking two projects to alleviate pressure on the Foyle Road sewer network and improve overall sewage flows.   This will include a full desilting of a trunk sewer between Coshowen and Craigavon Bridge and the diversion of a main sewer connection directly to Coshowen Wastewater Pumping Station.  NI Water expect to have the sewer diversion work completed by March 26 and the desilting completed as soon as possible. As there does appear to be a reoccurring flooding problem in the area associated with higher tide levels in the Foyle River, most recently on the 14th December, NI Water will monitor the location that has been prone to flooding after the two projects have been completed. Road gullies are cleaned along Foyle Road, in accordance with my Department’s limited-service maintenance policy. Officials have reviewed the carriageway drainage system on Foyle Road and are satisfied that it is operating effectively.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly,through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or throughwaterline@niwater.com . Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) why there is no out of hours crisis for people aged over 65 in the Western Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) similar services available in other Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: (i)There is no dedicated out of hours crisis service for people aged over 65 in the Western Health and Social Care Trust. Demand for this service is at a level that does not justify the levels of investment required, to develop a 24/7 model of crisis response for over 65’s.  Older Person’s Mental Health Services in WHSCT have never felt the need to propose a 24/7 model of crisis care.  (ii)The Regional Emergency Social Work Service (RESWS) offers a crisis service across the region and inclusive of The Western Health and Social Care Trust including those citizens over 65 years. A regional mental health liaison and crisis response care network has been stood up to address regional variation in mental health service provision and delivery.The development of a regionally consistent model of Mental Health Crisis Services has been identified as an area of work within the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 and is a priority area within the recent Review of Deliverability of the Mental Health Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the criteria for a grit box to be placed in a street or area.
answer_text: My Department commits significant resources to maintaining approximately 5,600 salt bins that are provided for use by the public, on a self-help basis on public roads which do not qualify for inclusion on the gritting schedule, providing the necessary criteria are met.Residents can make an application to the Department to request the installation of a salt bin in their area however, provision of new salt boxes is not automatic and may be refused if the location in question is not on the publicly maintained road network; the gradient of the road in question is less than 5%; or if reasonable alternative routes are available.When considering requests for the provision of a salt bin, my officials carry out an evaluation using a criteria based system, which takes into account various factors including topography, traffic volume, road gradient/geometry, alternative routes as well as other factors such as residential usage and community facilities including schools, hospitals and care homes for the elderly.

answer_text: My Department does not hold maintenance expenditure by individual areas, however details of the annual expenditure by District Council are reported in the Annual Report to local Council.The Derry City & Strabane Council Reports for the period 2020-2025 can be found on my Department’s website:Derry City and Strabane District Council 2020 - Annual ReportDerry City and Strabane District Council 2021 - Annual ReportDerry City and Strabane District Council 2022 - Annual ReportDerry City and Strabane District Council 2023 - Annual ReportDerry City and Strabane District Council 2024 - Annual ReportDerry City and Strabane District Council 2025 - Annual Report

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the projected timeline for redevelopment of Fort George Site in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: The Western Health and Social Care Trust (WHSCT) submitted a full planning application for a healthcare hub and multi-storey car park including associated site works, in August 2025, and is currently awaiting a planning determination. The Department, which is responsible for the remainder of the site, submitted a Proposal of Application Notice (PAN) for a mixed-use development on 2 December 2025. The PAN process will run for at least 12 weeks, with a public consultation event scheduled in January 2026. It is anticipated that the formal outline planning application for the DfC lands at Fort George will be submitted in Spring 2026.  Subject to planning approval, the Department intends to issue a development brief and invite tenders during 2027.  Timelines are indicative and may vary depending on planning outcomes and market engagement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any engagement his Department had with the taxi industry before implementing a 10 per cent cut to school journey fares.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the operation and delivery of home to school transport and as the Centre of Procurement Expertise (CoPE) for the education sector, the EA holds direct responsibility for the procurement and management of school transport contracts with private taxi operators. Rather than implementing a cut to school journey fares, the EA has asked taxi operators to voluntarily modify their contracts, reducing their invoiced rates by 10% and it assures me it is taking a balanced and considered approach in reviewing the responses received. As these arrangements are contractual matters managed by the EA, my Department does not have a role in direct engagement with the taxi industry on this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the estimated amount of unclaimed pension benefits, in the last three years.
answer_text: My Department does not hold this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether any consideration has been given to need for a third vehicular traffic bridge for Derry.
answer_text: My officials are currently developing a suite of transport plans, including the North West Transport Plan (NWTP), which covers your constituency.The NWTP will provide the framework for local transport policy and investment decisions up to 2035. Its aim is to ensure that the transport network meets the needs of people and businesses living, working, and visiting Derry City and Strabane, both now and in the future.The plan is being prepared using a modelled, evidence-based approach to identify the most effective interventions and will ultimately set out a comprehensive range of measures across all transport modes.At present, the plan is at the long-listing stage, where all potential transport measures are being assessed against its objectives. Specific schemes have not yet been confirmed, as the modelling and option appraisal process is still ongoing.I would hope to be in a position to publish the draft NWTP for public consultation in 2026 however, in the meantime further information on the development of the plan can be found on my Department’s website: North West Transport Plan 2035 | Department for Infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update of the Lidl planning application at Crescent Link, in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: My Department’s retail assessment and a review of the applicant’s retail assessment is completed. Work is concluding to finalise a report and recommendation which will balance all material considerations to ensure a robust decision is reached.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the review of her pothole repair policy.
answer_text: On 2nd December, I was pleased to launch the new Road Maintenance Strategy which is now open for public consultation and in support of the new approach I have also commissioned a full digital road survey of all roads in the North. Using advanced AI, HD imaging and roadscape modelling technology, a detailed digital twin of every road will be captured and used to not only identify and prioritise urgent maintenance to address defects such as potholes, but also perform lifecycle modelling that will provide clear long-term projections of how the road network will perform under different investment scenarios, helping to plan investments more effectively, reduce the carbon impact from maintenance and optimise the safety and lifespan of its road assets.We are committed to raising the standard of maintenance across the network, ensuring that interventions are timely, durable, and delivered to consistently high specifications. This will reduce long-term costs, improve user experience, and enhance safety.The digital road survey and the analysis of the data it produces, is an investment in the future of our road network enabling a better understanding of the condition of that network and therefore inform strategic decision making going forward.Whilst the strategy and survey will provide greater detail to inform strategic decision making, this will also continue to be supported by the reporting of potholes and surface defects via the online portal.  Maintenance staff will continue to inspect and make-safe defects in accordance with our policy to ensure the safety of the travelling public.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the estimated costs of the proposed digital mapping project for road defects.
answer_text: On 2nd December, I was pleased to launch the new Road Maintenance Strategy which is now open for public consultation.The main objectives of the strategy are to improve the overall condition of the road network and enhance safety and quality, by delivering a targeted programme of intelligent maintenance investment and a sustainable maintenance regime. This means instead of filling one or two potholes and skipping over others, there will be more patch resurfacing and when this is done, the repairs will last longer because they will be better quality.  This means filling more potholes, where it is needed most.Through this new strategy, my Department will ensure that the road maintenance function is organised to improve day to day effectiveness and enhance our ability to respond to incidents and weather events.Along with this strategy my Department is investing £1.3m to deliver a complete digital survey of the entire road network in the North. This will provide a much greater level of understanding of road condition and allow my officials to take data-driven decisions and deliver more focused maintenance interventions saving money in the longer term.The value of deeper, more accurate information to support intelligent road maintenance is widely recognised. A growing number of Local Authorities are already benefiting from this type of roadscaping technology, however, the North is the first to collect such a vast data set to produce a jurisdiction-wide road condition baseline. It’s a landmark initiative that, in just six months, will give my Department an unprecedented understanding of its road infrastructure to better target maintenance, reduce costs, and react quickly to future opportunities and innovations.The survey will be completed by the end of the 25/26 financial year and data will then be processed over a period of a couple of months and used to develop the future structural maintenance programme and inform future in-year monitoring rounds for 2026/27.However, it is important to recognize that these measures on their own will not address the consequences of years of under investment and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made to improve the quality of our road network and contribute to the delivery of the priorities set out in the agreed Programme for Government.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of emergency respite requests that have been (i) received; and (ii) approved, in the last three years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: (i) With regard to the number of emergency respite requests that have been received within the last three years - the five HSC Trusts have provided the detail below:Belfast HSC Trust:The Belfast HSC are unable to provide this data, this would require a manual trawl of all cases as this data is not held.Northern HSC Trust:The Northern HSC Trust are unable to provide this data as this data is not held and would require a manual trawl of all cases.South Eastern HSC Trust:The South Eastern HSC Trust has reported that for Adult Services, Primary Care, Older People Services and Children’s Services – a manual count of records would be required by the Trust to attempt to acquire this information, and this would not be feasible at this time.Southern HSC Trust:The Southern HSC Trust has reported that across Adult Services (inclusive of Mental Health Services, Physical Disability Services, Adult Community Services, Learning Disability Services) - there is no system code for recording emergency respite on Paris (previous information system) or Encompass (new information system operational in SHSCT from 08 May 2025), and therefore it is not possible for the Trust to provide this information without conducting a manual trawl of records. This manual exercise cannot be conducted within the current timeframe.Within Children’s Services, the Southern HSC Trust report that there has been a total of 24 requests for emergency respite within the last 3 years. Western HSC Trust:The Western HSC Trust are unable to provide information with regard to emergency respite requests within Adult Services. The Western HSC Trust has provided detail regarding requests that have been received within Children’s Services for emergency overnight respite. The Western HSC Trust has reported that there has been a total of 15 emergency overnight respite requests received across the previous 3-year period.(ii) The number of emergency respite requests that have been approved within the last 3 years is as follows:Belfast HSC Trust:The Belfast HSC Trust are unable to provide this data as this would require a manual trawl through all cases as this data is not held.Northern HSC Trust:The Northern HSC Trust are unable to provide an answer on emergency respite approval or provision, as this data is not held.South Eastern HSC Trust:As outlined in the first section of this answer - the South Eastern HSC Trust has reported that for Adult Services, Primary Care, Older People Services and Children’s Services – a manual trawl of records would be required by the Trust to attempt to acquire this information, and this would not be feasible at this time.Southern HSC Trust:The Southern HSC Trust had received a total of 24 requests for emergency respite within Children’s Services across the last 3-year period. 22 of these requests were approved and fulfilled via a mixture of residential and fostering short breaks provision for children with a disability. The other requests were de-escalated through the provis
