question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the process for securing an adult care package, including the points of contact to access these services, in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Each of the Health and Social Care Trusts have a ‘brokerage’ system in place for sourcing packages of care where requests for a new or increase to a current package of care are sent by the keyworker to the Brokerage Team who then share the request with their own internal home care service and the independent sector providers.  Any of the agencies that have capacity to meet the package of care notify the Trust Brokerage Team.  The team then inform the keyworker who notifies and agrees this with the individual and their family, the care agency is then informed and a commencement date agreed.When the care agency starts providing a package of care the individual will be given details about the provider including contact details to assist communication on an ongoing basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of instances where dementia patients could not be safely discharged from hospital due to a lack of suitable care arrangements, in the last three years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The data on dementia patients that could not be safely discharged due to lack of suitable care arrangements is currently not available. The Health and Social Care Trusts have now gone live with a new electronic patient record system, Encompass. As the Trusts continue to transition to completely digitised records, the data generated within the context of service delivery is subject to a process of extensive validation. Until this process is complete, there may be gaps in the data that Trusts are able to submit for reporting purposes.I acknowledge that there is a significant issue in regards to securing placements for people with dementia awaiting discharge from hospital. This is an issue that is currently being considered by the Provider Collaborative Forum for Enhanced Care. The forum is testing a concept of offering an enhanced care rate to the independent care home sector for dementia placements. The intention of this exercise is to determine whether this will create additional capacity in the independent care home sector, effect more timely discharge from hospital for patients with dementia and ease pressure on hospital beds over this winter period.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of adult respite beds, in the last three years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Requests for respite/short breaks are assessed and managed by Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) in accordance with assessed need. Respite beds are not ringfenced therefore data is not collected on the number of adult respite beds. The use of respite beds is taken from the overall bed stock within care homes.Respite/short breaks activity is collated on the number of adult carers who receive a short break from the person they care for. The table below outlines the adult carers short break activity across the last three financial years by Trust and by programme of care.Adult Carers – Short breaks22/2323/2424/25HSCTELDMHLDP&SDELDMHLDP&SDELDMHLDP&SDBelfast            772             503             754             160          1,290             597             796             190     Northern         1,879               69             637             179          1,633               52             581             109          2,907               76             633             171 South Eastern            831                 4             468             110             246                 1             417               92             207                 1             517               69 Southern            815             108             494               57             785             108             555               67             710             101             548               48 Western            395               79             198               57             409               71             250               30             375               96             256               30 TOTAL         4,692             763          2,551             563          4,363             829          2,599             488          4,199             274          1,954             318 Please note caveats to the data• BHSCT - due to the implementation of ENCOMPASS, activity is outstanding since Q1 24/25.• NHSCT - due to the implementation of ENCOMPASS, activity is outstanding since Q3 24/25 (Only Qtr 1 and Qtr 2 included for 24/25 year for Hours and position reported at end September '24 for Number of Adult Carers)• SEHSCT – the reduction in activity is due to a number of factors including some care homes declining to accept short break bookings as priority is given to temporary and permanent placements.  This is particularly the case for respite breaks in care homes for dementia.  Trust staff, therefore, explore alternative short break options to placement such as increase in homecare hours for a respite period at home alongside support from family members, and use of a Self-Directed Support option, such as managed budget, to top up the care package to support the carer need for a short break.   Keyworkers also explore options for carers to access regular breaks from caring such as the provision of day care and a sitting service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether his Department has assessed the impact that the reduction in grant funding provided to housing associations will have on areas of greatest housing need.
answer_text: Grant funding has not been reduced. Following the December monitoring round, the allocation for the Social Housing Development Programme has increased to in excess of £207m, which is the largest allocation of budget to the Programme.		Social housing is only built in areas of housing need. In some areas, the amount of grant per unit will decrease. Other areas will see an increase. Our assessment indicates that although there may be reductions in some areas, housing targets remain achievable. It will be for each individual housing association to decide themselves how much private finance they can borrow and how much risk can they take on.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of instances where hospital discharge meetings or multidisciplinary team meetings were not carried out on behalf of a patient, in the last 12 months, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Trust information systems do not have coding to identify information in respect of the number of instances where hospital discharge meetings or multidisciplinary team meetings were not carried out on behalf of a patient, in the last 12 months.Securing this information would likely require a specific search of all patient records in each of the 12 monthly periods requested. Whilst I am unable to provide an estimate of the timeframe for this, I understand the scale of search required would involve redirection of staff time with a likely impact on direct patient care or the identification of funding to support a specific initiative over and above core hours.Whilst appreciating the concerns in respect of this issue, I would caution against asking the service to direct resource to undertake this work given the current pressures on the service, the scale of the ask and the potential for erroneous results as outlined.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of local bus routes that have been (i) cancelled; or (ii) reduced, in each local council area, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:In accordance with Translink’s retention and disposal policy regarding timetable design and schedules, Translink do not hold the relevant records for the last three years.  Translink can, however, provide details for the last two years as follows: From 1st Sept 2025 no routes have been cancelled. During this period some minor timetable amendments have taken place in accordance with the Public Service Agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 35465/22-27, in relation to palliative social workers, to detail the number that are assigned to assist cancer patients, in each of the last three years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Palliative care social workers employed in the Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts support service users with a range of palliative conditions, including those with a cancer diagnosis and an assessed specialist palliative need.  Patients are assessed on their palliative needs, not on any specific diagnosis that leads to palliative needs, therefore a figure for the number of palliative social workers specifically assigned to cancer patients cannot be provided.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients that sought emergency treatment, in the last month, broken down by each Emergency Department.
answer_text: Emergency Department attendances (new and unplanned) for November 2025 are shown in Table 1 below. Table 1: Emergency Department Attendances – November 2025TrustEmergency DepartmentTotal Attendances November 2025BelfastChildren4,119Mater (inc MIU)3,640Royal7,110NorthernAntrim (inc MIU)7,784Causeway4,249South EasternUlster7,062SouthernCraigavon6,905Daisy Hill4,730WesternAltnagelvin4,739South West Acute3,351Grand Total53,689This data was sourced from Epic EC1 report ID 671186 and is pending final validation.Emergency Departments (EDs) continue to face significant pressures driven by high attendance numbers, complex patient needs, and delays in hospital discharge. To address these challenges, my Department has implemented a range of measures aimed at improving patient flow and reducing waiting times:Expansion of alternatives to ED attendance: Urgent Care Centres, Phone First services, and Minor Injury Units provide timely care for less acute cases.Additional staffing: 20 emergency medicine consultants have been appointed in the past year, with recruitment underway for a further nine consultants across Trusts.Winter Plan initiatives: Enhanced discharge processes, rapid access clinics, and increased community-based capacity through Hospital at Home services.Development of Same Day Emergency Care (SDEC) and ambulatory models across specialties such as Frailty, Respiratory, Cardiology, Surgical, and Acute Medicine.Improved diagnostics and discharge coordination to support timely care and earlier discharge.These actions form part of a broader system-wide approach to ensure patients receive timely, safe, and effective care. While influenza and RSV cases are rising, vaccination uptake remains strong, with 484,000 residents vaccinated as of 8 December 2025, including 81% of those aged over 80.My Department remains committed to supporting ED staff, who continue to work tirelessly under extremely challenging conditions, and to sustaining services through both immediate measures and longer-term reforms.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the potential resurfacing of the Strand Road, in the Foyle constituency, including whether a recent survey of the road surface has been conducted.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government and this has created significant challenges in our ability to maintain the road network in a safe condition.  While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.  The resurfacing of the Strand Road will be kept under consideration should further funding become available.On 2nd December, I was pleased to launch the new Road Maintenance Strategy which is now open for public consultation and in support of the new approach I have also commissioned a full digital road survey of all roads in the North.The digital road survey and the analysis of the data it produces, is an investment in the future of our road network enabling a better understanding of the condition of that network and therefore inform strategic decision making going forward.Whilst the strategy and survey will provide greater detail to inform strategic decision making, this will also continue to be supported by the reporting of potholes and surface defects via the online portal and maintenance staff will continue to inspect and make safe defects in accordance with our policy to ensure the safety of the travelling public.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how the proposed digital mapping project for road defects (i) differs from; and (ii) will operate alongside, the public online reporting portal.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government and this has created significant challenges in our ability to maintain the road network in a safe condition.  On 2nd December, I was pleased to launch the new Road Maintenance Strategy which is now open for public consultation and in support of the new approach I have also commissioned a full digital road survey of all roads in the North. Using advanced AI, HD imaging and roadscape modelling technology, a detailed digital twin of every road will be captured and used to not only identify and prioritise urgent maintenance, but also perform lifecycle modelling that will provide clear long-term projections of how the road network will perform under different investment scenarios, helping  to plan investments more effectively, reduce the carbon impact from maintenance and optimise the safety and lifespan of its road assets. The digital road survey and the analysis of the data it produces, is an investment in the future of our road network enabling a better understanding of the condition of that network and therefore inform strategic decision making going forward.Whilst the strategy and survey will provide greater detail to inform strategic decision making, this will also continue to be supported by the reporting of potholes and surface defects via the online portal.  Maintenance staff will continue to inspect and make safe defects in accordance with our policy to ensure the safety of the travelling public.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any avenues available to support patients with disabilities and on low incomes, in meeting the costs of medically prescribed cannabis.
answer_text: There are a small number of cannabis-based medicinal products that have been licensed by the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) and approved by the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE). Such medicines are available, where clinically appropriate, to people in NI. These medicines can only be prescribed by a hospital specialist and supplied from a hospital pharmacy. Patients are not required to pay for medicines supplied by HSC Trusts.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps he is taking to address governance and bullying concerns raised through whistleblowing complaints by Housing Executive employees.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has a Raising Concerns – Whistleblowing Policy which is publicly available on its website and provides guidance for both staff and members of the public on how they can raise concerns, and how these will be processed and addressed by the Housing Executive.While the Housing Executive operates this independently from the Department for Communities, they are required to notify the Department’s Raising a Concern Team of concerns received to support governance and oversight.  Whistleblowing is also discussed and reported to the Department through its quarterly accountability meetings with the Housing Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail why applicants to the Civil Service Administrative Officer recruitment drive in the North West were invited to apply for posts under different working patterns, and across multiple areas, when positions were only available for Alternative Working Patterns in Area 2.
answer_text: The Pilot AO recruitment in the North West generated a merit list to fill current and future vacancies. Applicants indicated preferred working patterns and locations to allow flexibility throughout the life cycle of the merit list. This approach was clearly outlined in the candidate information booklet, which stated: “For this competition, merit lists will also reflect location and working pattern preferences. This competition will be used to fill a number of current vacancies. It may also be used to fill further vacancies which may arise during the lifetime of this recruitment exercise.”The recruitment website for the North West competition advised: “Please note: The majority of vacancies will be in the Department for Communities, Alternative Working Pattern posts.”Of the candidates who were successful as part of the AO Pilot recruitment, 238 selected Alternative Working Pattern (AWP) Area 2 as a preference. These candidates have been invited to pre-employment checks and to complete onboarding forms, of which 225 candidates have now completed the onboarding process, where they will progress to permanent appointments. Vacancies for AO positions in other areas and departments will be reviewed as they become available, and candidates will be drawn from the merit list created as a result of the Pilot AO competition. Following the success of this competition, the North West maintains a strong merit list to support efficient and timely candidate placement over the coming weeks and months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of patients who have had their domiciliary care package reviewed; and subsequently (ii) the number of packages that were (a) reduced; or (b) fully withdrawn, in the last three years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Whilst the 5 Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts undertake reviews on an ongoing basis, the correlation of these reviews with the outcomes you are requesting is not collected in a reportable format.  The outcome of an individual’s review will be documented on a case by case basis within the body of their personal health and social care record.  However, since October 2023, my Department provided funding to 5 HSC Trusts to implement an Early Review Service.  Early Review Teams were introduced via an initial pilot in October 2023 across all 5 Trusts and in 2024/2025, 4 Trusts proceeded with implementation across Older People’s programmes of care.  The remaining Trust fully implemented the model in 2025/2026.  The work of the Early Review Teams involves a systematic review of commissioned home care support, provided by both statutory and independent care providers, at a timely interval. This approach is driven by an intention of enhancing individuals’ autonomy and directing care where it is most needed.  The Early Review model focuses on a review within 8 weeks of a new or increased home care package being implemented, when an individual is discharged from hospital and/or living in a community setting.  There are exclusions in the criteria, for example, individuals receiving end of life care and those that meet the criteria for an existing rehabilitation pathway.   The consequence of not having a timely review is that a home care package would continue to be delivered in line with what was established at the outset notwithstanding the fact that an individual may no longer require the full level of support outlined in the initial assessment of need.  This in turn may serve not only to impact the individuals’ level of independence but also to impact the availability of care for others that do require it, including those waiting in hospitals settings, in a care home or in the community with limited interim care plan arrangements.I have provided the outcome figures for this service, for the period, 01 October 2023 to 31 March 2025 in the tables below.  During the initial 18 months, data collection sets varied across Trusts and therefore it has been challenging to gain consistencies in reporting the measurements in the manner below and that should be taken into consideration.  The 2025/2026 data is still being validated by Trusts and is not yet available for reporting purposes.  Work has been undertaken across my Department and all Trusts to harmonise datasets and formalise the returns process. Table 101 October 2023 – March 2024WHSCTNHSCTSEHSCTSHSCTBHSCTReviews Completed89701698876Home Care Packages Remained the Same 5743312530Home Care Packages Increased 04315Home Care Packages Decreased2912584837Home Care Packages Ceased31177144Table 201 April 2024 – 31 March 2025WHSCTNHSCTSEHSCTSHSCTBHSCTReviews Completed	2020585381342Home Care Packages Remained the Same 1140123155153Home Care Packages Increased 4081314Home Care Packages Decreased820171202152Home Car

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 35465/22-27, to detail any efforts being made to increase the number of palliative care social workers, in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Palliative Care in Partnership Clinical Engagement Group submitted the SPC Palliative Care Workforce Planning Report to the Department Workforce Strategy Programme Board in October 2020. The report found there to be a total of 16.89 WTE (20 headcount) Palliative Care Social Workers working across providers (HSC Trusts and hospices) in Northern Ireland and projected the need for Palliative Care Social Workers to rise to 28.3WTE to meet the population needs in 2024 (3.7WTE per 250,000 population).The report also highlighted the need for a postgraduate pathway to be developed in order to train Palliative Care Social Workers to meet future demand and the need for postgraduate opportunities to also be developed for SPC Allied Health Professionals and Palliative Care Pharmacists.   Following Covid-19, the SPC Workforce Planning Report was updated in light of learning from the pandemic. The report recognised the development of a Specialist Award Level Postgraduate Diploma for Palliative Care Social Workers by Queens University. This was a part-time two-year course and 14 students started the course in September 2023 - 7 students were funded by Department, six were funded by their employers and one was part employer funded/ part self funded.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any areas where grit box requests have been approved, within the Foyle constituency, over the last three years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of tenants in temporary accommodation who (i) do not have access to kitchen facilities; and (ii) do not have access to a washing machine, in each local council area, within the last 12 months.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive does not record the number of placements in temporary accommodation specifically by access to kitchen and laundry facilities.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any immediate steps being taken to address the structural roof safety concerns, which have caused the temporary closure of Nazareth House Primary School, in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: The temporary closure of Nazareth House Primary School was necessary to ensure the health and safety of the pupils, staff and parents. Years of underinvestment in our ageing school estate have left us facing difficult decisions and, in this case, the only option was to temporarily close the school.The Education Authority (EA) has commenced planning for urgent remedial works to make the roof of the main school building safe, with the aim of allowing the whole Nazareth House school community to return to learning on their own site by half-term break in February 2026.Following intensive work with the school leadership, the Council for Catholic Maintained Schools (CCMS) and the EA, it has been agreed that from 7 January, pupils and staff of Nazareth House Primary School, including the nursery, will share a site with St Anne’s Primary School. In parallel with these temporary contingency arrangements and the remedial works, my Department has commenced planning for the more extensive roofing works, which will be prioritised for delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of empty homes, in each local council area, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Land & Property Services (LPS) does not maintain a register of 'empty homes'.  Information is published on Open Data NI by LPS detailing the Housing Stock (excluding Caravans, Domestic Garages, and Domestic Stores) that are recorded as vacant in the rating system at 1st April each year, broken down by District Council area: Portal | Housing Stock, by District Council, recorded as vacant	As rates on domestic properties are charged at 100% irrespective of whether the property is occupied or not, ratepayers may not inform LPS of a property becoming vacant, therefore these figures may not reflect accurately an ‘empty homes’ position.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether he plans to commission an independent audit of the Housing Executive’s handling of (i) construction contracts; and (ii) compliance with adjudicator decisions, given High Court criticisms.
answer_text: No.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the works at the Caw Roundabout, in the Foyle constituency, will commence as planned in early 2026.
answer_text: The upgrade and signalisation of Caw Roundabout is a transport mitigation measure arising out of the adjacent H30 Rosses Gate residential development. As a developer led scheme, Southbank Square is responsible for design and construction of the works. My Department’s role is to ensure the design meets the required standards and is safe for all road users.The works are to be in place prior to occupation of more than 262 dwellings and prior to the end of November 2026. In order to meet their delivery programme, the developer has indicated their intention to commence works in early 2026.My officials continue to work with the developer to progress the detailed design of the Caw Roundabout upgrade. It is currently with the developer to submit amended plans and information to enable approval of the detailed design.This should provide a significant improvement in both road safety and traffic progression on the road network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any preventative flooding measures her Department has undertaken in the Ivy Mead area of the Foyle constituency, within the last five years; and (ii) any further measures planned to mitigate future flood risk.
answer_text: Development of potential flood alleviation proposals for Drumahoe is complex and remains ongoing. Consultants are currently preparing an Outline Business Case (OBC) for the scheme and progressing with the detailed design. The current programme indicates that the OBC will be received in spring 2026 with detailed design work to be completed by the end of 2026. Extensive upgrade works to two existing culvert inlet screen structures in Drumahoe has already been completed following flooding here in Summer 2022. Blockage of these screens compound flood risk and completion of these works will assist in reducing the current risk ahead of the main flood alleviation scheme. Officials from my Department’s Rivers Directorate inspect and maintain the Burnagibbagh River on an annual basis.  The last inspection was carried out on 24 January 2025 with the identified maintenance works completed between 24 January 2025 and 6 March 2025. In addition, my Department operates an on-call duty and flooding officer rota system 24 hours a day, 365 days a year with staff on standby to respond to flooding. Routine cyclical inspections also take place on drainage infrastructure, the maintenance of these is usually increased before and during significant periods of heavy rainfall. Sandbag stores at operational depots, and those at high-risk locations close to communities at known flood risk, are fully stocked in preparation for any potential flooding, two of which are nearby at Three Mile House and Ronnie V’s licensed premises. If the threat for any potential flooding increases, sandbags can be placed on mobile units to enable a timely response as necessary.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) why his Department stated it was unable to provide data on antipsychotic prescribing rates in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust when the Business Services Organisation has confirmed that this information is readily available; and (ii) any steps being taken to address ongoing issues in mental health data transparency and reporting.
answer_text: (i)The Epic prescribing system may have the functionality to provide antipsychotic prescribing data, however we need to ensure appropriate governance when interpreting the data and therefore the previous request could not be met within the timeframe.(ii)It is envisaged that the encompass system will support HSC in becoming a national leader in the provision of regionally consistent mental health data. There has been a phased approach to roll-out across the Trusts, beginning with SET in November 2023 and completing with WHSCT and SHSCT in May 2025. A Regional Reporting Group (RRG) has been established, supported by SPPG, Informatics and Trust representatives, to oversee data quality, standardisation of definitions, and the incorporation of agreed mental health outcome and activity measures into the system.Steps are actively being taken to improve data transparency and reporting across mental health services; however, this work is taking place within the context of well-documented workforce pressures and significant operational demand. Trusts must balance the time required for detailed data validation with the need to maintain safe service delivery, and focussed work is ongoing within high-pressure areas such as Community Mental Health (waiting lists), Psychological services and CAMHS to strengthen data capture and reporting within encompass. As a result, improvements will be incremental rather than immediate.Nonetheless, once the initial bedding-in period and validation processes are fully completed, encompass offers substantial potential to strengthen reporting, enhance regional comparability, and support more timely and accurate mental health data. The current work underway is crucial to ensuring that the system produces reliable outputs that can be used confidently for planning, performance and service improvement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 33107/22-27, to detail the rational as to why no properties within the Derry and Strabane area have been included in this scheme.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“In the absence of sufficient funding to deliver a full programme of CWI replacement/remediation across our stock, we committed in our Cavity Wall Insulation Action Plan to investigate and address instances of suspected cavity wall insulation failure that were having a detrimental impact on the structure of a property.  In the first instance a pilot programme of some 300 dwellings was identified in which to test our revised CWI extraction and refill process.  These dwellings were identified in consultation with our local Area Offices.This year sees the start of a follow up CWI programme in which we had intended to address approximately 3,000 properties per year over 3 years i.e. 9,000 dwellings in total. We have identified a number of schemes totalling 1,232 homes in the Derry and Strabane area to be included in that programme and are aiming to get two of these schemes comprising 179 homes on site in this financial year.   Unfortunately, however, there is some uncertainty at this time as to when the other schemes will start as our funding position for investment in our stock is such that we have had to initiate a review of our investment priorities and forward 5-year programme.  It is highly likely that delivery of this CWI programme will have to be undertaken over a longer period of time or, indeed, some of it deferred until our funding position significantly improves.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any assessment he has made of the level of public money associated with the Housing Executive’s non-compliance with adjudicator decisions in construction disputes, which has resulted in enforcement actions in the High Court.
answer_text: This is a matter for NIHE, I have made no such assessment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of invitations that have been withdrawn, since his visit to Israel.
answer_text: One.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of leaseholders under a management company; and (ii) the maintenance fees paid, in each of the last five years, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“All service charges collected for any maintenance works completed, in regard to Housing Executive leases, are collected by the Housing Executive directly. The Housing Executive does not use management companies”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether his Department has minimum standards for non-standard accommodation regarding access to essential appliances such as (i) fridges; (ii) cooking facilities; and (iii) laundry.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive has no minimum standard in relation to fridges, cooking facilities, and laundry.The Housing Executive secures non-standard accommodation in the same manner as any other customer group, whereby the respective Hotel/B&B is subject to the relevant checks and accreditation via Tourism NI.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 36254/22-27, to detail the number of Ministerial visits or engagements which have been rescheduled, since his return from Israel.
answer_text: Since the 31 October no Ministerial visits or engagements have been rescheduled.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of families currently living in (i) non-standard; or (ii) temporary, accommodation who do not have access to their own fridge.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive does not record this information for non-standard accommodation.Those in single lets or supporting people funded temporary accommodation have access to fridges.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether all non-standard accommodation providers are required to ensure families have access to their own fridge, for safe food storage.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive secures non-standard accommodation in the same manner as any other customer group, whereby the respective Hotel/B&B is subject to the relevant checks and accreditation via Tourism NI. There are no further standards imposed regarding access to facilities due to the nature of non-standard accommodation and its intended short-term use to meet our statutory duty in the absence of other options.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of serious adverse incidents raised in relation to missed abnormal cervical screening tests in the Western Health and Social Care Trust, in each of the last 5 years.
answer_text: The number of Serious Adverse Incidents (SAIs) raised in relation to unsatisfactory cervical screening test results in the Western Health and Social Care Trust, in each of the last 5 years are as follows: Year - (this is the year the SAI was reported)Total number of SAI's relating to Cervical Screening 2020 32021 42022 02023 22024 5It should be noted that the Western Trust had reported a higher number of category 3 (unsatisfactory) outcomes compared to other Trusts, which were subsequently taken forward under the Trust’s SAI procedure.  Following further investigation, it was concluded that the Trust had been applying a different threshold than others when categorising cervical cancer audits with an ‘unsatisfactory’ outcome.  In late 2024 the PHA issued updated guidance to all Trusts to support greater consistency in the categorisation of audit outcomes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether any further consideration has been given to expanding the threshold for Education Maintenance Allowance.
answer_text: The policy in respect of Education Maintenance Allowance (EMA) is a joint policy between my Department and the Department for the Economy (DfE). I have not had any discussions with the DfE Minister regarding any expansion of the eligibility thresholds for EMA payments in Northern Ireland.I recognise that the Education Maintenance Allowance provides valuable support to enable young people to remain in education or training. As work continues to bring forward legislation in the current mandate that will require young people to remain in education or training until age 18, my officials will continue to discuss support for these young people with DfE officials.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will establish a lived-experience advisory group, made up of people directly affected by poverty and the welfare system, to help shape and inform future departmental policy.
answer_text: The Executive’s draft Anti-Poverty Strategy includes a commitment which is focused on addressing the real-life impacts of poverty based on a range of evidence including lived experience.I anticipate that throughout the lifespan of the Strategy as individual Department’s develop and deliver actions, real life experience will help shape and inform future departmental policy to ensure a joined up and sustainable approach to delivery.The creation of a specific lived-experience advisory group as part of the Anti-Poverty Strategy is ultimately a matter for the Executive to decide.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any (i) action he will take, within this mandate, to address child poverty; and (ii) engagement he has had with other Departments in relation to this issue.
answer_text: Within this mandate I will bring an Anti-Poverty Strategy to the Executive for consideration. The draft Anti-Poverty Strategy aims to address poverty and the impacts of socio-economic disadvantage in Northern Ireland, including children and young people. The Strategy sets out the  Executive’s commitment to a joined up, long term approach to addressing poverty.I have written to my Executive colleagues in relation to the next steps in the development of an action plan to ensure delivery of the Anti-Poverty Strategy

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, given the incoming removal of the two-child policy, to detail whether his Department has made any assessment on the impacts of this change to Universal Credit awards.
answer_text: At August 2025 there were 17,600 children that were not eligible for the child element of Universal Credit because of the limits imposed by the two-child limit. My officials are undertaking further analytical work to determine the wider impact of this change.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to address the delays for laparoscopic diagnosis of endometriosis.
answer_text: There is currently considerable work underway across the health and care system to try to reduce waiting times and develop pathways for gynaecology specific conditions such as endometriosis in each Trust across Northern Ireland.The Department has established a Regional HSC Gynaecology Services Forum, with Trusts, the Public Health Agency, and General Practitioner representation. This forum is overseeing ongoing service improvement, and the implementation and development of gynaecology service pathways at primary, intermediate, secondary and tertiary care levels. as well as overseeing the implementation of the “Getting It Right First Time” (GIRFT) Gynaecology Report. An Endometriosis Clinical Pathway Task and Finish Group is working to design a standardised pathway for patients with minor, intermediate, or severe diagnoses. This pathway will ensure services meet women’s needs at different stages of the condition and will minimise duplication across Northern Ireland.Investment is planned in each of the five Trusts to enhance local endometriosis services, including additional laparoscopy sessions. Further investment will support the development of two Endometriosis Centres in the Western and Belfast Trusts.Additional non-recurrent funding has been approved for waiting list initiatives—both independent sector and in-house—to reduce waiting times for patients requiring red-flag and other time-critical assessments and treatments across a range of specialties, including gynaecology. However, I acknowledge that the current financial position may make progress difficult for non-red-flag or non-urgent cases such as endometriosis, unless clinically indicated.My officials will continue to work with partners to strengthen pathways and improve care for women affected by endometriosis and other gynaecological conditions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has engaged with the Minister of Finance to ringfence funds garnered through the Soft Drinks Industry Levy to address health conditions, such as obesity and type 2 diabetes.
answer_text: The Soft Drink Industry Levy (SDIL) came into effect across the UK from 6 April 2018 and was designed to help tackle childhood obesity by encouraging the reformulation of drinks to reduce levels of added sugar, as well as portion size reduction and the marketing of low sugar alternatives. It was introduced into law across the UK in the Finance Act 2017, with commencement on 6 April 2018 established in The Soft Drinks Industry Levy Regulations 2018. 		Since its announcement in 2016, the SDIL successfully led to extensive product reformulation, with a 46% average reduction in sugar in soft drinks in scope of the levy between 2015 and 2020. Between 2015 and 2019, 65% of soft drinks that contained more than 5g sugar per 100ml reformulated to below 5g. 		The UK Government recently announced it will reduce the current lower threshold at which SDIL applies from 5g of total sugar per 100ml to 4.5g of total sugar per 100ml. In addition, the current exemption for milk-based drinks and milk substitute drinks with added sugar will also be removed. These changes will come into operation from January 2028.		It is important to note that the SDIL is a UK-wide levy, and as such, is an excepted matter where powers to legislate remain with the UK Parliament. The UK Government does not formally link the funds that are generated by Soft Drinks Industry Levy to any specific spending. Officials from my Department have also confirmed with their counterparts across the UK that monies raised via the levy are not ringfenced for any particular area of spending in any of the 4 UK nations.		My Department had previously explored the potential to use funds equivalent to those likely to have been raised through the SDIL in Northern Ireland for tackling obesity and other public health related issues but given there was no budget line this is not possible. 		However, my Department recently published the new “Healthy Futures obesity strategic framework Healthy Futures obesity strategic framework for Northern Ireland | Department of Health, work will soon commence to set up refreshed structures for the implementation of the strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, in relation to the 2024 Civil Service recruitment competition, to detail (i) the number of candidates disqualified for alleged proxy assistance; and (ii) whether there is a formal appeals mechanism for those impacted.
answer_text: Refer to AQW 27824/22-27. Interview decisions made by panels for external recruitment or internal competitions cannot be appealed. Any candidate who wishes to make a complaint about any aspect of their application can do so via the published complaint procedure which can be found on the NICS Recruitment website.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether his Department will consider a scheme that would provide financial support for tenancy deposits to support victims of domestic abuse in securing housing in the private sector.
answer_text: The additional ringfenced allocation of £2.5m for strategic homelessness prevention in 2025/26 enabled the reinstatement of the Homelessness Prevention Fund, which has supported delivery of tenancy deposit schemes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will review the traffic light sequencing at the Culmore Road Roundabout at Madam's Bank Road, in the Foyle constituency, which is currently contributing to traffic issues.
answer_text: The signalised junction at the Culmore Road Roundabout relies on accurate detection of vehicles on each approach to operate at optimum efficiency. Vehicle detection is carried out through loops which are embedded below the road surface sending information to the signal controller for the traffic light sequencing.During the extensive works to upgrade the waste water infrastructure in the city earlier this year the associated excavation of the road compromised some of the detector loops.New loops have recently been installed and the signals should now be operating as intended.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he has engaged with the Minister of Health to ring-fence funds garnered through the Soft Drinks Industry Levy to address health conditions, such as obesity and type 2 diabetes.
answer_text: As previously advised, funds generated by the Soft Drinks Industry Levy are not formally linked by the British Government to any specific spending. Instead, allocations for health initiatives within Whitehall departments are determined through Spending Reviews.The Executive receives a Barnett consequential based on any changes in funding provided to Whitehall departments. For Spending Reviews this is provided at a departmental level rather than for specific programmes. My department is not informed of how much the Executive receives as a result of the Levy. As Barnett consequentials are unhypothecated, it is for the Executive to determine how this funding is allocated, in line with local priorities.The Minister of Health holds primary policy responsibility for health-related matters, including obesity and type 2 diabetes, and has discretion to prioritise his departmental budget in accordance his own priorities.  As Finance Minister, I am willing to consider proposals which will help to address these issues.  It would be for the Executive to agree to the ring-fencing of such funds and the quantum of funding to be allocated.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of Ministerial visits and engagements which have been (i) cancelled; or (ii) rescheduled since, his return from Israel.
answer_text: Since the 31 October 2025, no engagements have been cancelled. My Private Office routinely reschedules diary commitments to accommodate changing circumstances.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 33322/22-27, whether there are plans to assess temporary accommodation beyond the point of acquisition, to ensure they remain safe and of good quality.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The development of a temporary accommodation standards framework is a commitment within the Strategic Action Plan for Temporary Accommodation 2022-27. The regularity of assessments regarding property standards will be considered as part of the development.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of speech and language therapists employed within stroke units, in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Information provided by Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts on the number (whole-time equivalent) of speech and language therapists employed at 30 September 2025, and how many are employed in stroke units, is detailed in the table below. Note that Trusts will also have additional speech and language therapists from community services providing in-reach services to stroke units.HSC TrustSpeech and language therapists employed (WTE) at 30 September 2025Of which: numbers employed in stroke unitsBelfast161.13.7Northern114.2  0.8 South Eastern111.60.8 Southern117.03.2Western91.23.0Source: Information & Analysis Directorate, DoH

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, in relation to the recent Civil Service Administrative Officer recruitment drive in the North West, to detail (i) the number of applicants who declared that they have a disability at registration stage; (ii) the number of these applicants who were subsequently invited to interview; and (iii) the purpose of collecting information regarding disabilities at such an early stage.
answer_text: Candidates were not asked to confirm if they had a disability on the expression of interest form.  However, they were asked to indicate if they wished their application to be considered under the Guaranteed Interview Scheme (GIS). 512 candidates indicated they wished to be considered under GIS.178 GIS candidates have been invited to interview to date. A GIS is a positive action measure which enhances access to interview for   disabled applicants.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, in relation to the recent Civil Service Administrative Officer recruitment drive in the North West, to detail the number of applicants aged 50 and over who (i) submitted an expression of interest; and (ii) were subsequently invited to interview.
answer_text: As is the case with large scale general service recruitment competitions my Department will be publishing a full breakdown of the Section 75 groups once the competition concludes. This will be available on the Open Data portal in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will implement a staggered income threshold to replace the single income threshold in the eligibility requirements for Discretionary Support.
answer_text: I have no plans to introduce staggered income thresholds in Discretionary Support.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will consider the installation of a pedestrian crossing for St Eithne's Primary School, in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. We are acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads, particularly around schools.As a review of this location has not previously been undertaken, I have asked my officials to carry out an assessment at St Eithne's Primary School in accordance with the department’s policy on the provision of controlled crossings.  The proposal will then be scored against other schemes within the area and if considered to be high priority, it will be included in a future works programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will consider implementing a 30 mph speed limit along the Creggan Road, from Grangemore Park to the Glen Road, within the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users. I have asked officials to undertake a review of the existing speed limit on the Creggan Road in line with my Department’s policy. My Department is also intending to launch a public consultation in the near future on a number of speed related matters.  The outcomes of this consultation will be used to inform a final Speed Limit Review Report which will be published in due course.   If you have concerns regarding driver behaviour, these should be directed to the PSNI using their non-emergency 101 number.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 20096/22-27, for an update on any progress on the steps being taken to address concerns in relation to the use of LED lights on vehicles.
answer_text: As you will be aware from the response to AQW 20096/22-27, the British Department for Transport (DfT) Vehicle Certification Agency (VCA) is responsible for certifying all on road vehicles here and advised that all vehicle headlamps are designed and tested to comply with internationally recognised standards. In response to public concern, DfT awarded Transport Research Laboratory (TRL) a research contract to investigate the factors that cause glare from the headlights of oncoming vehicles, and how to address this.TRL published their findings on 5 November 2025 and DfT officials have advised that they are currently conducting a review of the findings.DfT have also confirmed that they plan to undertake further targeted vehicle-based research to help inform future international lighting regulations and consider what more can be done domestically. My officials will continue to monitor the progress of this ongoing research and will advise of any implications or advice for road users here once DfT have completed their review of the findings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of (i) respite beds; and (ii) short-term break placements, that have been availed of for elderly care, in the last three years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Requests for respite/short breaks are assessed and managed by Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) in accordance with assessed need. Respite beds are not ringfenced therefore data is not collected on the number of adult respite beds. The use of respite beds is taken from the overall bed stock within care homes.Respite/short breaks activity is collated on the number of adult carers who receive a short break from the person they care for. The table below outlines the adult carers short break activity across the last three financial years by Trust and by programme of care.The table below shows the number of adult carers for the Elderly who received a short break HSCT22/2323/2424/25Belfast                     772                   1,290  Northern                  1,879                   1,633                   2,907 South Eastern                     831                      246                      207 Southern                     815                      785                      710 Western                     395                      409                      375 TOTAL                  4,692                   4,363                   4,199 It is important to note the caveats to the data below• BHSCT - due to the implementation of ENCOMPASS, activity is outstanding since Q1 24/25.• NHSCT - due to the implementation of ENCOMPASS, activity is outstanding since Q3 24/25 (Only Qtr 1 and Qtr 2 included for 24/25 year for Hours and position reported at end September '24 for Number of Adult Carers)• SEHSCT – the reduction in activity is due to a number of factors including some care homes declining to accept short break bookings as priority is given to temporary and permanent placements.  This is particularly the case for respite breaks in care homes for dementia.  Trust staff, therefore, explore alternative short break options to placement such as increase in homecare hours for a respite period at home alongside support from family members, and use of a Self-Directed Support option, such as managed budget, to top up the care package to support the carer need for a short break.   Keyworkers also explore options for carers to access regular breaks from caring such as the provision of day care and a sitting service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the areas in the Foyle constituency which have passed an assessment for a grit box and currently do not have one in place.
answer_text: My officials have confirmed that all requests for salt boxes in the Foyle constituency which have passed the assessment process have a salt box in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement her Department has had with the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs and the Department for Economy, to ensure there is minimal disturbance to coastal communities and marine environments, in respect of the potential entry point into Lough Foyle for offshore renewable energy developments.
answer_text: The Department for the Economy has established the Offshore Renewable Energy Action Plan (OREAP) Steering Group which brings together key delivery partners to develop and deliver the action plan for offshore renewable energy in the North of Ireland. My Departmental planning officials are involved in this group.The OREAP will ensure that offshore renewable energy is situated in the most sustainable locations and that they are connected in the most efficient ways.I and my officials will continue to collaborate with the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, the Department for the Economy and relevant stakeholders in relation to the matter of offshore renewable energy infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department will bring forward a regulatory framework for letting agents, to address concerns around unfair fees and to improve transparency for tenants.
answer_text: I have previously set out my priorities for Private Rented Sector reform in this mandate, which will focus resources on those areas of reform which will have a greater impact in tackling the complex challenges of the private rented sector. This includes introducing minimum standards for fire and electrical safety, increasing notice to quit periods and enhancing the Landlord Registration Scheme. Accordingly, I do not plan to introduce a regulatory framework for letting agents in the limited remaining time in this mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any efforts made in conjunction with the North South Ministerial Council to address challenges surrounding the Loughs Agency’s ability to carry out effective enforcement within Lough Foyle, in particular to aquaculture trestles moving towards the Republic of Ireland shoreline.
answer_text: The jurisdictional issue regarding Lough Foyle is not within the competence of the Northern Ireland Assembly, nor therefore, the North South Ministerial Council. It is a matter for the UK and Irish Governments. Until an agreement between the UK and Irish Governments on the management of aquaculture in Lough Foyle is reached, the regulation of aquaculture in the Foyle and Carlingford areas remains the responsibility of my Department in respect of the Northern Ireland shoreline and the Department of Agriculture, Food and the Marine for the Republic of Ireland shoreline.  My Department continues to monitor aquaculture activities in Lough Foyle, in conjunction with the Loughs Agency, and, in March 2025, took action to remove unauthorised aquaculture trestles from the Northern Ireland shoreline.  The Joint Secretary (North) of the North South Ministerial Council Joint Secretariat facilitated a meeting between my Department and the UK Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Office (FCDO) in September 2024 to address the challenges with unregulated aquaculture and the Loughs Agency’s ability to establish a regulatory regime for aquaculture, particularly in relation to Lough Foyle.  My Department had further engagement with the FCDO on this matter in February to April 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any work being done to protect designated protected sites in Lough Foyle from disturbance associated with bait digging and intertidal shellfish collections.
answer_text: Bait digging and intertidal shellfish gathering are not specifically listed as notifiable activities in the Lough Foyle Area of Special Scientific Interest (ASSI) citation, however, they may fall under certain operations that do require Departmental consent under the Environment (NI) Order 2002. In some cases, there may be an existing right or practice to collect bait. For this matter, permission is likely to be needed from RSPB as occupier of a lease and /or The Crown Estate as landowner. This may also require Departmental consent, and I would encourage landowners, lease occupiers or third parties involved to contact my officials within the NIEA consenting case work team in advance for advice. My officials have been engaging directly with landowners within Lough Foyle to implement signage advising public of the protected status and the prohibition of collection and digging for bait without consent from the Department. Any reports of unconsented bait digging and intertidal shellfish gathering have been taken forward by my officials for investigation.Furthermore, with regards to intertidal shellfish collections, my Department has a statutory obligation under the Fisheries Act 2020 and the Joint Fisheries Statement to prepare and publish a fisheries management plan (FMP) for the hand-gathering of shellfish in Northern Ireland.  The FMP must be published by end of 2027.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how the Northern Ireland Environment Agency has decided the types of planning applications that can be included in consultation response prioritisation lists, invited from each local council.
answer_text: A non-statutory trial for prioritisation of planning consultations has been established between my officials in the Natural Environment Division (NED) of NIEA and local planning authorities.  Applications requested for prioritisation are identified collectively by the local planning authorities and officials are not involved in the selection process.NED can accept most application types for prioritisation requests from local planning authorities. However, windfarms and associated grid connection infrastructure proposals are excluded, as they are presently not considered appropriate for prioritisation given their complex nature and the need for these cases to be considered by a small specialist team within NED.  This position may change as the scheme is still in its trial stage and is subject to review by both parties.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the average time it takes the Northern Ireland Environment Agency to provide its final substantive response to consultations, regarding planning applications for wind farms.
answer_text: DAERA provides responses to planning application consultations at a departmental level covering advice from NIEA and DAERA Marine and Fisheries Division.  From the 1 April to 24 November 2025, the average time taken to issue a final substantive response to consultations on Statutory and Non-Statutory planning applications for wind farms, is 44 days.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to outline the support it can provide to improve public information about the work of Committees in the Assembly.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how the Northern Ireland Environment Agency’s decision to specifically exclude renewable energy projects from local councils’ lists of priority applications that require a consultation response aligns with his Department’s responsibility to deliver climate change targets.
answer_text: These complex cases are dealt with by a small specialist team within NED that currently has no capacity to prioritise one case above another. This team will receive additional resources in the first quarter of 2026/27 as an outcome of current scientific recruitment competitions.  In the meantime, these specialist NED officials and teams elsewhere within my Department continue to progress applications for renewable energy, outside of the prioritisation scheme.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline her plans to ease the financial pressures on local businesses.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the legal basis on which the Northern Ireland Environment Agency can refuse to prioritise consultation responses for renewable energy planning applications, when inviting lists of priority planning applications from each local council.
answer_text: There is no statutory prioritisation process in the Northern Ireland planning system and no legislative requirement to prioritise development types.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail why renewable energy planning applications are being excluded by the Northern Ireland Environment Agency, when inviting lists of priority planning applications that require consultation responses, from each local council.
answer_text: Renewable energy consultations including windfarms and associated grid connection infrastructures are presently not considered appropriate for prioritisation given their complex nature and the need for these cases to be considered by a small specialist team within NIEA.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the threshold assessment process, used in the recent Administrative Officer competition, for applicants who submitted expressions of interest.
answer_text: The purpose of the expression of interest for the Civil Service Administrative Officer (AO) North West posts is to assess whether applicants met the minimum eligibility criteria including age, right to work and the pre-agreed preferred experience requirements for the role. This involved a self-assessment, which asked candidates to rate their level of experience against a set of predefined administrative and organisational criteria that are relevant to AO duties. Each criterion carried a predetermined weighting reflecting its importance to the AO role. The candidate’s total score was calculated by combining their responses across all seven criteria and meeting the minimum eligibility criteria. The top scoring candidates, over 950, have been invited to interview. Due to the high volume of applicants, those who did not meet the agreed score threshold have not been advanced to the next stage at this time. However, their applications will be retained for consideration if further opportunities arise in the North West.  They will remain on the waiting list and could be brought forward to interview at a later stage should further vacancies arise over the course of the next 12 months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for a breakdown of (i) the number of (a) agency; and (b) temporary, staff employed by the NI Civil Service; (iii) the average length of time they have been in post; and (iii) the number of these staff who have subsequently been offered a permanent role within the NI Civil Service, in each local council area, over the last five years.
answer_text: (a) The number of agency workers Year as at 31/03 of each yearNumber of Agency Workers2021252120222930202329752024401520254939(b) The number of temporary staff employed by the NI Civil Service over the last five years is available in the published 1 April Employment Statistics reports. Figures for 2024 and 2025 are available in the notes on Table 1 and figures for 2021 – 2023 are available in Table 1c of the publications.	(ii)  The Department does not hold the average length of time agency workers have been on assignment to NICS.(iii) The Department does not hold information on the number of agency workers who have subsequently been offered a permanent role within the NI Civil Service, in each local council area, over the last five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether the Universal Credit Bill changes made by the UK Government from April 2026 in relation to the health element for new claimants, will apply to claimants in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The changes to the health element for new claimants from  April 2026  made under the UK Government’s Universal Credit Act 2025 will apply in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether a soft-launch recruitment process has been considered, that would give priority to existing NI Civil Service staff working in temporary or agency roles, when filling upcoming permanent positions.
answer_text: In line with the Civil Service Commissioners Recruitment Code, we are committed toappointments on merit, in fair and open competition. The selection process used in each recruitment competition will ensure that only those candidates who have demonstrated sufficient capability for the roles are considered for appointment to NICS posts. Recruitment Agency workers are welcome to apply for advertised roles where they believe they meet the eligibility criteria. The introduction to a “soft launch” recruitment process would be contrary to our commitment to adhere to the merit principle.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail geographical breakdown of the number of electric vehicle charge points, both (i) current; and (ii) planned.
answer_text: The provision and operation of EV charging infrastructure is a commercially led activity in the North. Responsibility for the maintenance, development and utilisation of charge points rests solely with the Charge Point Operators (CPOs). There has been significant investment from CPOs in EV infrastructure over the last two years. The publicly accessible commercial charge point network now has over 730 chargers in place, a growth in EV charging provision of 89% since April 2023. The Rapid/Ultra Rapid public charging network, which offers the fastest, most convenient method of charging has grown by over 460% here in the same period. Relevant CPOs are best placed to advise you on the position of their current installations and on their plans to install further charge points within their wider networks.With regards to planned installations, Local Councils have availed of funding for the installation of charge points under the Office for Zero Emission Vehicles (OZEV) ‘On Street Residential Charge Point Scheme’. This project, part funded by my Department and WEEV, is set to install up to 250 charge points across the North.The Irish Government’s ‘Shared Island Sports Club EV Charging Scheme’ will also be delivering EV chargers at 48 sports clubs in the North bringing further charge points to community settings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will establish a public land agency, to maximise the use of public land for the delivery of sustainable and affordable housing.
answer_text: I am currently finalising a paper for Executive consideration which will set out proposals to make better use of public land assets to support the supply of affordable housing. I have no plans to establish a public land agency.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the amount of the NI Housing Executive’s reserves used to reduce the cost of temporary accommodation; (ii) how those funds were applied; and (iii) by the amount overall temporary accommodation costs were reduced as a result, in the last 12 months.
answer_text: NIHE Landlord reserves have not been used to reduce the cost of temporary accommodation to date.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of times she has (i) met with; or (ii) received correspondence from, the Minister for Communities in relation to wastewater capacity, in the last 12 months.
answer_text: In the last 12 months, in relation to wastewater capacity, I have:met with the Minister for Communities once; and received one piece of correspondence from the Minister for Communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) whether his Department has conducted their own value for money exercise into temporary accommodation; and (ii) any steps taken to reduce spend in this area following the publication of the Centre for Homelessness Impact October 2024 report Spending on Temporary Accommodation: is it Value for Money.
answer_text: My Department has not undertaken its own value-for-money exercise into temporary accommodation. Expenditure in this area was subject to scrutiny in the recent Northern Ireland Audit Office (NIAO) report, ‘Homelessness in Northern Ireland’ and work is progressing to implement the recommendations contained within the NIAO report.The Housing Executive has engaged with the Centre for Homelessness Impact (CHI) to conduct an independent value for money exercise that resulted in the ‘Value for money in homelessness: A spotlight on temporary accommodation in Northern Ireland’ report, published in May 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of (i) Freedom of Information requests received by his Department in relation to the Northern Ireland Football Fund; (iii) requests that have been answered; and (iii) these requests that were refused, including the reasons for refusal.
answer_text: (i) My Department has received a total of 56 Freedom of Information Requests since March 2022 relating to the Northern Ireland Football Fund (and its predecessor the Sub Regional Stadia Programme).  (ii) Of the 56 FOI requests received, 53 have been answered and three requests are currently in progress.  Six requests were answered in full.(iii) 16 requests were refused in part as some information was considered exempt from disclosure under:Section 35 Formulation of government policy;Section 36 Prejudice to the effective conduct of public affairs; andSection 40 Personal data.31 requests were refused in full as the information was considered exempt from disclosure under:Section 35 Formulation of government policy;Section 36 Prejudice to the effective conduct of public affairs;Section 43 Commercial interests;Responding to the request would breach the costs limit; orThe information requested was not held by the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will consider extending health bursary funding to post-graduate medical students at Queen's University, Belfast.
answer_text: My officials are continuing to review the bursary funding arrangements for pre-registration health and social care students. The review is intended to inform options for a public consultation. I am unable to provide any further detail until the review is finalised and options developed for consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any steps his Department is taking to improve access to Changing Places facilities in hospitals; and (ii) whether there are any plans to make such facilities mandatory in all new builds and refurbishments.
answer_text: In light of the severe financial challenges facing the Northern Ireland Health & Social Care (HSC) sector, as well as the competing demands on the capital budget, the Department has no plans to centrally fund the provision of Changing Places facilities in any existing hospitals or health centres where currently not provided. HSC Trusts are however provided with general capital funding monies and may consider the provision of these facilities from this allocation based on their own assessment of need and options available.Since June 2022 amendments to building regulations require that all new build hospitals and primary care buildings and those whole hospital or primary care buildings formed by a material change of use of existing facilities should provide at least one changing places toilet.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the rationale for not implementing a ban on no fault evictions, in line with other UK regions.
answer_text: As I have previously stated, the reason I have no plans to ban no fault evictions at this time is because my Department is already taking forward work, as mandated by the Assembly, to increase security of tenure through the introduction of much longer notice to quit periods. This change will greatly improve security of tenure for individuals and families in the private rented sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients who have attended Emergency Departments presenting with pain and complications caused by endometriosis, in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any processes in place to ensure that landlords providing temporary accommodation to the Northern Ireland Housing Executive are addressing repair issues raised by tenants.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Single let temporary accommodation placements are provided under license arrangements between the occupant and providers, who are responsible for property maintenance and repairs. If repairs are not addressed, Housing Executive staff may act as an intermediary to try to resolve issues and engage with the provider to ensure repairs are completed. Where significant disrepair persists, the Housing Executive may offer alternative accommodation and remove the property from its portfolio either permanently, or until repairs are completed.”
