They may provide training to families but we would not have that detail and this inquiry would need to be directed to CPI themselves.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether (i) he has had any engagement with the Irish Government in relation to their Vacant Property Refurbishment Grant Scheme; and (ii) his Department has considered implementing a similar initiative.
answer_text: My officials are taking forward work to examine empty homes approaches with a view to determining policy interventions which could be utilised in Northern Ireland to bring vacant properties back into use.  As part of work to consider approaches used elsewhere, my officials have engaged with officials in the Irish Government to discuss its vacant property grant scheme.I have asked my officials to prepare a report on this initiative for future consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of complaints received about the condition of temporary accommodation provided by the Northern Ireland Housing Executive, in each local council area,  in each of the last five years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive received seven final stage complaints relating to conditions in temporary accommodation, since January 2025.It is not possible for the Housing Executive to extract the details in the format you requested, without carrying out a significant manual exercise to review all cases.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any discussions he has had with the Minister for Communities regarding the Rates Support Grant, within the last 12 months.
answer_text: The Rates Support Grant provides financial support, on an annual basis, to those councils whose needs exceed their wealth relative to other councils. The Rates Support Grant is a statutory grant; however, the level of funding is not set in legislation. As a result, allocations to this grant are the responsibility of the Minister for Communities and he must decide what can be afforded. The information gathering exercise in relation to the multi-year Budget provided the Communities Minister with an opportunity to highlight any additional requirements in relation to the Grant, which can then be considered by the Executive when agreeing a Budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in relation to the £150,000 awarded to Mid and East Antrim Council for festive lighting, to detail (i) whether there was an application process involved; and (ii) how other councils can access similar support.
answer_text: I can confirm that my Department provided £135,000 investment in response to an application for funding from Mid and East Antrim Borough Council for the enhancement and improvement of festive lighting in Ballymena, Carrickfergus and Larne.It is for individual Councils, working in partnership with Development Offices, to identify their priorities for town centre regeneration funding and submit applications. Funding for festive enhancements has also been provided to other Councils in current and recent years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 32861/22-27, to provide the estimated annual cost of providing a universal breakfast club scheme for primary schools.
answer_text: As stated in my response to AQW 32861/22-27, the Department of Education is currently operating under severe budgetary constraints and has no plans to introduce universal breakfast club provision for all primary schools in Northern Ireland. For that reason, officials have not explored the estimated annual cost of providing a universal breakfast club scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 32190/22-27, to detail how (i) transparency; (ii) accountability; and (iii) value for money, can be ensured in the absence of a formal procurement process, or oversight mechanism, for temporary accommodation provision by the Housing Executive.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows.“Work has recently been completed to deliver Recommendation 3 of the NIAO Report on Homelessness whereby the Housing Executive has formalised and documented its processes and controls over both block booked and routinely used non-standard accommodation.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the most recent cost estimate of mitigating the two-child limit; and (ii) any engagement he has had with his UK Government counterparts regarding the potential tapering of this policy.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer given to AQW 33732/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will work with the Ministers of (i) Finance; and (ii) Health, to identify a mechanism that will ensure health and social care staff in receipt of Universal Credit are not financially penalised by receiving a lump sum back payment under the 2025-2026 pay award.
answer_text: My Department does not have any current plans to review how health and social care staff who are in receipt of Universal Credit are impacted following their 2025-2026 backdated pay awards.  Universal Credit is designed to take account of a claimant’s earnings reported by His Majesty’s Revenue and Customs in the assessment period in which they are received. Where a claimant receives a backdated payment, this is taken into account in the assessment period it was paid and not for the period it covers. The rules on the treatment of backdated pay awards apply equally to all Universal Credit claimants who receive earned income. The payable amount of Universal Credit will decrease by 55p for each £1 a claimant earns until their earnings are too high to get the benefit.  Some people will qualify for a work allowance which enables them to earn a certain amount of money before the taper rate applies in the benefit. A person’s claim will not close if their earnings exceed their Universal Credit eligibility in one assessment period and the claim will remain open with payment resuming in the next assessment period, subject to surplus earnings rules and provided eligibility conditions continue to be met.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will work with the (i) Minister for Communities; and (ii) Minister of Finance, to identify a mechanism that will ensure NHS staff in receipt of Universal Credit are not financially penalised by receiving a lump sum back payment under the 2025-26 pay award.
answer_text: While I always remain open to collaborative engagement with my Executive colleagues, I do not consider that this is a feasible proposal. These matters were previously considered by officials, both from within my Department, and from their colleagues in the Departments of Communities and Finance, when implementing the HSC Staff Recognition Award made in response to the pandemic response, and in subsequent years due to late implementation of pay awards. The issues which prevented such measures then remain unchanged today. The processing of any pay award is complex, with many different calculations required to ensure that all salary-based allowances are paid at the correct rate and that staff will receive their correct payment of arrears. Employers are not aware of who, or who is not, in receipt of income-based benefits among the c75,000 staff in HSC. Any process would therefore require individuals to self-identify and for manual procedures to be introduced. Phasing the payments due to staff as a result of the 2025/26 pay deal, even if this were feasible, would require significant time for the Business Services Organisation to process and would delay the pay award significantly.Individual Benefits entitlement, and the income to be considered when assessing, is established in legislation. Within that, any backdated lump sum of pay is considered as income for the month in which it is received. It is easy to consider that a phasing might be beneficial, however the reality is that paying in instalments may in fact result in further detriment, as individuals near the threshold may have entitlement removed for an even longer period. Neither employers, nor even individuals, would be able to predict the outcome of such measures. It would further not be feasible for Universal Credit (UC) assessors to examine each case on an individual basis to determine the impact of apportioned payments, nor is it possible to disregard back pay, as this would require a legislative amendment, and any measure taken to do so would impact on the allocation to DfC from DWP, who are responsible for UC.I remain of the view that the only way in which such issues may be avoided in future years is the establishment of long-term, realistic, budgetary allocations which allow for improved planning.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any research undertaken by his Department, on the impact of the 20 per cent increase in school meal costs on families and schools; (ii) the estimated financial savings to his Department arising from this measure; and (iii) any alternative cost-saving options that were explored prior to introducing this increase.
answer_text: In taking its decision to raise its school meal prices, the Education Authority (EA) recognised that this decision may create challenges for low-income families who are not entitled to free school meals. Children entitled to free school meals would not be impacted by the EA’s decision.It is important to note that such families retain the choice of whether to purchase a school meal or to make their own arrangements, such as providing a packed lunch. To support parents in this, the Public Health Agency (PHA) publication “Are you packing a healthy lunch?” provides advice and guidance to parents to help them prepare an affordable, healthy and nutritionally balanced packed lunch. All parents should therefore be able to ensure that their children are able to access a healthy lunch, either through the canteen or a packed lunch. The PHA publication is available at:  https://www.publichealth.hscni.net/publications/are-you-packing-healthy-lunch-english-and-irish-translation.  The EA’s estimate for the overall savings that would result from its decision over the next three years financial years is £1.7m in 2024/25, £5.4m in 2025/26 and £5.7m in 2027/28. These estimates are based on the price increase coming into effect in January 2026. The EA considered the only alternative to a price rise would be to reduce the food offering provided to pupils and it did not consider this to be a viable option. The EA continues to work to ensure its catering service operates as efficiently as possible. A key aspect of this work is to maximise footfall of pupils paying for a school meal. The additional income from more pupils choosing to take a school meal reduces the overall net cost to the EA of operating its school meal service. A range of initiatives taken forward by the EA have seen increasing pupil numbers as a result and it will continue to try to maximise pupil footfall in future. Having maintained its school meals prices at the same level since 2017/18, despite significant rises in its production costs over that period, the EA determined that a price increase for school meals was unavoidable in light of the anticipated shortfall in the EA’s overall budget this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of (i) Scotland and Wales’s decision to end their Right to Buy Schemes to protect social housing stock; and (ii) any learning for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I have made no assessment of the decisions to end the Right to Buy Schemes in Scotland in 2016 and in Wales in 2019; or whether there is specific learning for Northern Ireland from those jurisdictions’ decisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether his Department has assessed the impact of the House Sales Scheme on current levels of (i) housing stock; and (ii) homelessness.
answer_text: Yes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the provision of up to three funded cycles of IVF as per the New Decade, New Approach commitment.
answer_text: I remain fully supportive of the New Decade, New Approach commitment to provide up to three funded cycles of IVF. Although the expansion in 2024 to one full cycle was welcomed, it was not possible to expand the service further due to funding constraints.My Department is continuing to work with the Regional Fertility Centre to explore the feasibility of expanding current capacity from within available resources. This is extremely challenging, as any expansion must be both affordable and deliverable without detriment to current provision and waiting times, which the team has worked hard to maintain after the disruption of the Covid pandemic. While I am cautious about raising expectations at this stage, I am assured that all feasible options for moving to a second cycle are being actively considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the 2018 Department for Communities consultation regarding the future of the House Sales Scheme, which showed that the majority of stakeholders who responded were in favour of ending the scheme for all social tenants.
answer_text: That consultation was run as part of the Department’s work to achieve reclassification of Registered Housing Associations as Private Non-Financial Corporations by the Office for National Statistics. It informed the development of the Housing (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2020.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any engagement his Department is having with schools, to support families in managing the rising cost of the school day, including reducing financial pressures, during the ongoing cost of living crisis.
answer_text: My Department and the Education Authority (EA) continue to undertake a range of actions, many either directly or indirectly involving schools, to support parents and carers with the costs of their children accessing their education. The EA continues to provide support to low-income families through the provision of free school meals (FSM) and uniform grants (UG). I increased the income threshold in 2025/26 for both supports to £15,390, and that it would be uplifted by consumer price inflation in subsequent years. The EA undertakes significant work to maximise the number of applications for FSM and UG. In parallel with the EA’s FSM/UG promotional initiatives aimed directly at parents, it also works in close partnership with schools to enable them to promote the application process amongst their school community. The EA provides weekly management information to all schools to help them engage with specific families who may have previously been in receipt of FSM. The EA provides promotional materials to schools that are relevant to all families. An example of these is a social media toolkit that was sent to all schools on how to make parents aware of the FSM and UG application process. Letters were issue by the EA to Principals in the summer, enabling them to identify those who received FSM/UG in the previous year but had not yet applied so that they can engage with the family directly. A dedicated EA email address for schools was also launched to enable school enquiries to be prioritised. In relation to the costs of school uniforms, I have introduced the School Uniforms (Guidelines and Allowances) Bill to address affordability of school uniforms.  I have shared the guidelines that the Bill will give statutory effect to with schools to assist them in ensuring that affordability – alongside comfort and practicality - is at the centre of their school uniform policies for the 2026/27 school year. In May 2025, I announced that eligibility for the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) would be extended to include school-age children from 1 September 2025. The NICSS provides a 15% subsidy on the costs of before and after-school activities, and when combined with TFC, parents can now save up to 32% on their overall bill.In June 2025, my Department wrote to all schools inviting them to register for NICSS. Schools receive a payment to cover initial setup costs and ongoing administration costs which will help encourage uptake, reduce financial pressures on families, and help schools offer affordable wraparound provision for families.I have also prioritised the transitioning all pre-school provision over time to 22.5 hours per week, with access to FSM for eligible children.  105 pre-school settings made this change from September 2025 and intend to approve at least 100 more from September 2026, subject to consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the dualling of A2 Buncrana Road.
answer_text: Major Road schemes are an important part of the work that is delivered by my Department as we seek to reduce journey times, increase reliability and improve road safety.As part of my announcement on 20 June 2025, the A2 Buncrana Road scheme will be considered as part of the North West Transport Plan.My officials are working collaboratively with Derry City and Strabane District Council and other stakeholders to develop the North West Transport Plan. The NWTP is currently at an early stage and will be developed in line with the Plan’s vision and objectives. It is anticipated that public consultation will commence in Spring 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the estimated number of children who are eligible for (i) Free School Meals; and (ii) the Uniform Grant, but are not currently claiming them, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: Free school meals (FSM) and uniform grant (UG) entitlement is based, in the vast majority of cases, on the applicant being in receipt of relevant benefits, such as Universal Credit. If an applicant has chosen, for whatever reason, not to apply for a relevant benefit, this would prevent a successful application being made to the Education Authority for FSM or UG, irrespective of their financial circumstances. It is therefore not possible to reliably estimate the number of children who would be eligible for free school meals and uniform grants but have not applied.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any engagement his Department is undertaking with (i) schools; (ii) parents; and (iii) community organisations, to encourage greater uptake of Free School Meals and the Uniform Grant for eligible families.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the administration of applications for free school meals (FSM) and uniform grants (UG) on behalf of the Department. It undertakes a broad-based campaign of promotion and marketing activities to maximise the uptake of FSM and UG. These activities are primarily focussed at the opening months of the application period each year. The EA issued a press release on 7 July promoting uptake of these supports, its social media campaign ran throughout the summer and the EA website contains videos, frequently asked questions and other relevant information to assist and encourage parents to apply. Parents who were in receipt of FSM/UG in the previous year and who had not yet applied for the new school year were sent reminder emails by the EA throughout July and August encouraging them to apply. Families who had commenced, but not completed, an application were sent twice weekly emails until the school census date encouraging them to complete their application.  In recognising that schools play an important role in engaging directly with the parents of its pupils, the EA provides promotional materials that are relevant to all families. It also provides weekly management information to each school which can be used to engage with specific families who may have previously been in receipt of FSM. An example of these promotional materials is a social media toolkit that was sent to all schools before the application portal opened providing advice on how to make parents aware of the FSM and UG application process. Schools were asked to use the toolkit in communications with their parent community throughout the summer period, encouraging families to apply as early as possible. The EA issued a letter to all school Principals in August 2025 and again in September 2025, enabling them to identify those who received FSM/UG in the previous year but had not yet applied in order that they can engage with the family directly if they believe they may still be eligible for FSM. A dedicated EA email address for the sole use of schools was launched to enable school enquiries to be prioritised in order to help determine if parents have applied or children are eligible so that schools can inform their parents and encourage applications.A letter and poster were sent via the EA Equality Service to 189 organisations across Northern Ireland, including community organisations that engage directly with families. This contained links for the EA website and EA Connect log-in page for applicants to apply.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 18437/22-27, whether further consideration has been given to implementing a men's health policy similar to the Healthy Ireland - National Men's Health Action Plan.
answer_text: The position regarding the development of a Northern Ireland Men’s Health Strategy or Action Plan remains unchanged from that given in my answer provided for AQW 18437/22-27. The financial position of the Department remains extremely challenging.The Public Health Agency (PHA) supports a wide range of men’s health initiatives which focus on providing services to those experiencing limited or restricted access to services. These reduce social isolation and promote opportunities for social connection and health enhancing behaviours. Initiatives supported by PHA include: the Farm Families Health Check Programme; funding for Cancer Focus NI (CFNI) to coordinate the implementation of the Skin Cancer Prevention Strategy Action Plan; funding for  Groundwork NI to provide a coordination and support service to Men’s Sheds across the region; support and funding for HSC Trusts and community and voluntary networks and groups to plan, promote and facilitate events during Men’s Health Week each year; funding for a range of locally based physical activity programmes under the banner Older and Active; Community Gardening and Growing initiatives, and funding for programmes across the region which provide opportunities for men to engage with and participate in community gardens, allotments, growing initiatives and outdoor Green Gym sessions.I remain supportive of work to improve Men’s Health and have engaged with a variety of men’s groups since my appointment as Minister. Both myself, and the Chief Medical Officer, will provide supportive videos for the Belfast Men’s Health Group event being held on 19 November to mark International Men’s Day.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her Department's assessment of the suitability and safety of footpaths in the Newmarket Street, in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: The gradient and configuration of footways on Newmarket Street in Foyle constituency are constrained by the general topography of the streetscape.  Regrettably, this means that the footway profiles are steeper than those generally recommended in current design standards.  My Department is investigating options to improve the slip resistance of the paving slabs laid within the footways to enhance pedestrian safety.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when the Walled City Public Realm Scheme will be completed.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has responsibility for the footpath network in Newmarket Street, in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: The footway on Newmarket Street is adopted by my Department from the kerb to the adjoining building facades or overhang.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his Department's assessment of the suitability and safety of footpaths in the Newmarket Street area, in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: Due to ongoing legal proceedings related to incidents in the Newmarket Street area, it would be inappropriate to provide further detail at this time. However, I would emphasise that pedestrian health and safety in all areas under my Department’s responsibility remains a key priority.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether his Department has responsibility for the footpath network in Newmarket Street, in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: The Department for Communities is not responsible for the footpath network on Newmarket Street in the Foyle Constituency. Responsibility for public footpaths falls to the Department for Infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when her Department will provide a replacement bollard at the top entrance of Foyleside, within the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: My Department does not own or maintain the entrance to Foyleside Shopping Centre.  The bollards at this entrance are located within privately owned property and any replacement will be the responsibility of the owner.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether the age of eligibility for the Tax-Free childcare scheme is under 12, and under 17 for children with disabilities, as per the NI Direct website.
answer_text: To be eligible for Tax-Free Childcare a child must be aged 11 or under, with eligibility ending on 1 September following their 11th birthday.  For children with a disability, eligibility continues until the 1 September after their 16th birthday.Full eligibility details, including financial limits and qualifying conditions, are clearly outlined on the guide titled “Tax-Free Childcare” available at gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the total revenue collected from Penalty Charge Notices, issued for driving in bus lanes, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The revenue from Moving Traffic Offence (MTO) penalties issued due to bus lane infringements, for the past five financial years is as follows:Financial Year£'k2020-21    4282021-22    713 2022-23    947 2023-24    999 2024-251,144

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail whether the amendment relating to The Civil Registration Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2012 will apply to parents of children conceived through in-vitro-fertilisation, outside of the United Kingdom.
answer_text: The amendment relating to The Civil Registration Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2012 in the Deaths, Still-Births and Baby Loss Bill is designed to include provisions that will ensure that both unmarried and non-civil partnered couples have access to the same registration procedures, regardless of whether they are a same-sex or heterosexual couple.  It will not make any changes to the entitlement to be named on a birth certificate so it does not address the birth registration issues facing couples who do not receive IVF treatment at a licenced clinic here or in England, Scotland and Wales.  The Registrar General has met with officials from the Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC) to highlight the current birth registration position for those couples who are neither married nor in a civil partnership, who receive IVF treatment elsewhere and the impact the Human Fertilisation and Embryology Act 2008 has on affected families here. Discussions are ongoing to consider how the Act might be changed to address the situation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has considered increasing the gully maintenance schedule from once a year to twice annually, in light of the increasing frequency of flooding incidents.
answer_text: My Department places high importance on the need to adequately maintain the road network through day-to-day essential maintenance activities such as gully emptying, to maintain the structural integrity of the road network. However, allocations of funding to maintain our roads have been less than required for many years and as a result, my Department has only been able to deliver a limited service across a range of functions including gully emptying.My Department’s current Limited-Service Policy aims to inspect and clean, where necessary, all gullies once annually.Further ad-hoc cleaning may also be undertaken to address any specific localised issues as required, within available budgets, and in advance of and in response to severe weather events. However, in recognition of the importance of this area of work, I have allocated further funding to deliver additional works in the form of cleaning and maintenance of drainage lines and manholes.In preparation for Storm Amy, for example, my officials completed proactive culvert screen maintenance work. This preventative work helped minimise disruption and contributed to protecting hundreds of homes from flooding.The value of our infrastructure cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made so my Department can deliver important aspects of road maintenance, such as gully cleaning, ensuring public safety and an efficient transport network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will consider imposing a moratorium on the Northern Ireland Housing Executive's House Sales Scheme.
answer_text: I have no plans at present to introduce a moratorium on the Housing Executive’s House Sales Scheme

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 3223/22-27, to provide an update on the extension of double yellow lines along the Duncreggan Road, within the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: My officials are actively engaged with local stakeholders regarding traffic management and parking issues in the Duncreggan Road area of the city as part of the Ulster University Magee Campus expansion.  The provision of parking restrictions will be considered within this wider context.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department will consider the introduction of legislation to prohibit the sharing of photos of road traffic collisions.
answer_text: ANSWER This is an important issue that cuts across the responsibilities of many Departments and organisations, including my own, and I am committed to ensuring that my Department engages to consider how best we might provide support.  Therefore, I have agreed to participate in a proposed roundtable discussion convened by Road Victim Support Northern Ireland – Donegal, to consider appropriate measures to prevent the unauthorised recording and sharing of photos of road traffic collisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of vacancies within the Civil Service, including temporary posts, broken down by constituency area, in each of last three years.
answer_text: My Department does not maintain records of vacancies by constituency, however, the table below gives the number of staff vacancies, including temporary posts, in the NICS at the beginning of each of the last three financial years, broken down by city/ town.City/TownNumber of vacancies at beginning of 2025/26Number of vacancies at beginning of 2024/25Number of vacancies at beginning of 2023/24Antrim582056Armagh232232Ballinamallard1026Ballykelly2193173Ballymena1567676Ballymoney1357Ballynahinch823Banbridge965Bangor976459Belfast320920291420Brussels100Bushmills333Carrickfergus313741Castlewellan001Coleraine662835Cookstown682447Craigavon452730Crumlin100Cullybackey300Derry- DC110Location to be confirmed16010485Grand Total548631472529Source:NICSHRWhen a department identifies a vacant post it needs filled, the request is referred to NICSHR to initiate the process to fill the post. NICSHR liaises with all departments on an ongoing basis to seek to prioritise resourcing activity within allocated financial and staff resources. Therefore, details of the total number of vacant posts in each department is not held centrally by NICSHR or the wider Department of Finance. Each Civil Service department is responsible for managing its resources – both financial and staff. Each department determines its individual workforce needs and is responsible for ensuring that any additional staffing resources are affordable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any consideration her Department has given to the introduction of (i) traffic calming measures; or (ii) a one-way system, for the Creggan Heights area of the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. However, my Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government and limited budget resources mean we must prioritise our interventions to those locations deemed most in need.I am aware that Creggan Heights currently benefits from traffic calming in the form of road humps. I have asked officials to consider the introduction of a one-way system on Creggan Heights.  However, I would caution that one-way systems tend to increase travel distances for residents and may cause confusion for drivers and visitors. In certain circumstances, one-way systems may also increase the speeds of vehicles using the street, as drivers no longer expect to be met by oncoming traffic, which may cause concern for pedestrians, cyclists, and other vulnerable road users and as such an important consideration will also be the level of support from local residents for such a proposal.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of people who were successful in previous Civil Service recruitment exercises and are currently on a live merit list, awaiting an offer of employment.
answer_text: Following clarification, the Member has confirmed that he is content for the reply to this question to relate to the merit list for the 2024 General Service Administrative Officer competition.As at the end of October 2025, there are 1,141 candidates remaining on the merit list for the 2024 General Service Administrative Officer competition for locations throughout the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of agency workers currently employed in the Civil Service, in the North West.
answer_text: The table below details the number of agency workers on assignment in NICS in the North West.  According to our information at month ending October 2025: Location Number of Agency Workers on assignment (Headcount)Derry/Londonderry (including Lisahally)759Ballykelly103Limavady29Coleraine80Ballymoney5Omagh40Strabane101026

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of current Civil Service vacancies in (i) Foyle; (ii) East Derry; and (iii) West Tyrone.
answer_text: My Department does not maintain records of vacancies by constituency, however, the tables below gives the number of staff vacancies in towns within the Foyle, East Derry and West Tyrone constituencies as at the end of October 2025.Foyle ConstituencyTownNumber of vacancies at all gradesDerry/Londonderry520East Derry ConstituencyTownNumber of vacancies at all gradesBallykelly250Bushmills5Coleraine54Garvagh4Limavady11Total324West Tyrone ConstituencyTownNumber of vacancies at all gradesOmagh67Strabane16Total83Source:NICSHRWhen a department identifies a vacant post it needs filled, the request is referred to NICSHR to initiate the process to fill the post. NICSHR liaises with all departments on an ongoing basis to seek to prioritise resourcing activity within allocated financial and staff resources. Therefore, details of the total number of vacant posts in each department is not held centrally by NICSHR or the wider Department of Finance. Each Civil Service department is responsible for managing its resources – both financial and staff. Each department determines its individual workforce needs and is responsible for ensuring that any additional staffing resources are affordable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail when successful candidates in the recently publicised Civil Service recruitment campaign, in the North West, will commence employment.
answer_text: On 28 October 2025, my Department launched a new approach to recruitment for Administrative Officers, for vacancies in the North West. This new pilot recruitment competition signals a move from traditional, annual campaigns to a more agile and dynamic approach to recruitment.Following the registration and self-assessment process, applicants will be invited to attend a recruitment event where they will undertake an interview and have the opportunity to discuss the roles available across a number of departments.It is anticipated that successful candidates from this campaign will take up employment from January 2026 onwards.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of the impact the impending recruitment of over 200 Civil Service Administrative Officers in the North West, will have on candidates currently on live merit lists, awaiting offers of employment.
answer_text: On 28 October 2025, my Department launched a new approach to recruitment for Administrative Officers, for vacancies in the North West.  The decision to run this pilot reflects workforce planning considerations, including the need to address persistent vacancy levels and improve access to opportunities in the North West while maintaining the NICS commitment to fair and open competition.My Department is currently in the process of completing a round of allocations from the merit list for the 2024 General Service Administrative Officer for successful candidates who selected the North West as a locational preference (Areas 2, 4 and 5 as specified in the Candidate Information Booklet for the competition). Most offices operate a flexi-time system between 8am and 6pm (Standard Working Pattern (SWP)), while some Administrative Officer roles require an Alternative Working Pattern (AWP), involving shifts between 8am and 8pm Monday to Friday, and in certain business areas, 9am to 5pm on one Saturday in every four.  All candidates from the 2024 General Service Administrative Officer competition who selected AWP in Area 2 as a preference in the North West have been allocated to a vacancy, however some candidates who selected AWP in Areas 4 and 5 or SWP in all Areas, will remain unplaced due to limited vacancies in this allocation round for posts that offer these working patterns. These individuals will be considered first for any new North West vacancies, in line with the merit principle, as they were successful earlier.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he will consider rates relief, for businesses affected by the collapsed building on Pump Street, in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: ANSWERThe rating and valuation system is determined by established case law and a long-standing statutory framework that supports the stability of the tax base. Businesses affected may apply to the District Valuer requesting a review of their Net Annual Value (NAV) to determine if a temporary allowance is warranted. The degree to which the NAV of premises in a locality may be affected is based on the duration of the works required and the severity of impact on the business. In advance of applications from affected businesses, Land & Property Services (LPS) is currently gathering information on the recent events in the locality and has reached out to the owners of the damaged property and Derry City & Strabane District Council for further information and timescales of works.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any actions being taken to address the continued delays in red flag breast cancer services within the Western Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) the average waiting times for these services, in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: (i) Further AQW 30046 and 30049, with the introduction of a single regional list, actions to address delays in breast assessment are being implemented regionally.  To address the current delays in red flag breast assessment my Department is working with all Trusts to deliver additional regional clinics. During October there was an additional 9 evening and weekend sessions, providing an additional 374 assessments.  Further evening and weekend sessions are in planning for November and December. Trusts have also been directed to prioritise red flag referrals within existing breast service appointment capacity.To support the reform of breast services, including assessment services across the region, the Elective Care Framework (ECF) has identified investment of £5million for Breast Services to enhance HSC Trusts capacity.  My Department is working collaboratively with HSC trusts to explore innovation and best practice from other jurisdictions who have faced similar challenges. (ii) With the new Regional waiting list, breast waiting times are now reported regionally. Official Northern Ireland Cancer Waiting Times statistics are published quarterly and can be accessed at https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/cancer-waiting-times. The most recent data (April-June 2025) is available at: https://datavis.nisra.gov.uk/health/ni-cancer-waiting-times-apr-jun-25.html.This release includes regional reporting for “urgent breast cancer referrals”.  However, data from all five Trusts is not yet available for this period due to implementation of the Encompass system in the Southern and Western HSC Trusts.  The next quarterly publication (July-September 2025), scheduled for release on 15 January 2026, will include a regional 14-day performance figure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will commission a cost of a child study.
answer_text: I have no plans to commission a cost of a child study.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of former Housing Executive properties, sold under the House Sales Scheme, which are now in the private rental market.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has confirmed that it does not hold this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has considered the introduction of a Child Poverty and Wellbeing Office, similar to the model introduced by the Irish Government, to enhance cross-departmental action.
answer_text: I have no plans to introduce a Child Poverty and Wellbeing Office.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any action her Department is taking to improve pedestrian safety on Alder Road in the Foyle Constituency, given the recent increase in local footfall from new housing developments; and (ii) whether consideration will be given to the installation of street lighting, footpaths or other safety measures, along this route.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. We are acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads, particularly around schools.However, my Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government and limited budget resources mean we must prioritise our interventions to those locations deemed most in need.My officials will undertake a review of the existing pedestrian facilities on the Alder Road and consider the potential for provision of additional safety measures when assessed against other competing priority schemes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any actions her Department is taking to increase the number of planning staff, in order to reduce delays within the planning system.
answer_text: My Department recognises the critical importance of a well-resourced planning system in supporting sustainable development and economic growth. In response to ongoing staffing challenges, my Department in partnership with Local Government and Belfast Metropolitan College, has developed a bespoke Trainee Planner Programme which is scheduled to commence in January 2026. This initiative aims to address current staffing shortages and future-proof the profession by creating a structured pathway into planning as a career. Successful candidates will begin as trainees and participate in a structured three-year programme that combines in-house training with academic study. In addition, my officials are currently working on a number of external recruitment competitions seeking a range of professional and technical planning staff, to fill several vacancies within this financial year.  My Department also offers placement opportunities for students, providing valuable work-place learning experience to encourage future professionals to enter the sector. Officials continue to collaborate and engage with local universities and colleges to promote planning as a career of choice.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the establishment of an Infrastructure Commission.
answer_text: Decisions on the way forward on this matter will be for the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail whether any consideration has been given to implementing a package of measures to address the cost of living.
answer_text: I recognise the ongoing impact and pressure that the cost of living crisis is having, particularly for the most vulnerable in our society.  However, due to funding constraints, the Executive is limited in its ability to implement measures to ease these pressures.  The levers which can make the biggest difference sit with the British Government.  That is why I have pressed Treasury for funding that will support the delivery of public services our people rightly deserve and for policy decisions that will make a meaningful difference in their daily lives.At recent meetings with both the Chancellor and the Chief Secretary to the Treasury, I underscored the importance of fair and progressive tax policies in the forthcoming Autumn Budget, advocating that all tax and spend decisions fully consider their impact on our citizens and local businesses.  I have called for changes to the Tax-Free Childcare scheme, increasing the support rate from 20% to 30% and reviewing the £2,000 annual cap per child. Affordable childcare is essential to improving labour market participation and driving economic growth, yet high costs continue to deter many families from entering or remaining in employment.  In response to this the Executive has introduced a 15% top-up subsidy to the existing Tax-Free Childcare scheme for eligible families.  I have also pressed Treasury for a reversal of the Two-Child Benefit Cap.  The decision to limit Universal Credit and Child Tax Credit to two children per household has exacerbated child poverty and placed additional strain on already vulnerable families.I remain committed to advocating for policies that promote fairness, protect the vulnerable, and ensure that every family has the opportunity to thrive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps her Department is taking to increase resources within the planning system, to accelerate the development of renewable energy projects.
answer_text: My Department has been working collectively over the last number of years with local government, who are responsible for the determination of over 99% of planning applications, and other key stakeholders to improve the efficiency and effectiveness of the planning system in the North, through the Planning Improvement Programme. The measures and initiatives therein aim to improve the development management process, and ensure planning applications, including those for renewable energy projects, are progressed in a more efficient way.A well-resourced planning system is critically important in supporting sustainable development and economic growth across all sectors. As part of the planning improvement agenda and in response to ongoing staffing challenges my Department, in partnership with local government and Belfast Metropolitan College, has developed a bespoke Trainee Planner Programme which is scheduled to commence in January 2026. This initiative aims to address current staffing shortages, increase resources and future-proof the profession by creating a structured pathway into planning as a career. Successful candidates will begin as trainees and participate in a structured three-year programme that combines in-house training with academic study. In addition, my officials are currently working on a number of external recruitment competitions seeking a range of professional and technical planning staff, to fill several Departmental vacancies within this financial year. My Department also offers placement opportunities for students, providing valuable work-place learning experience to encourage future professionals to enter the sector. Officials continue to collaborate and regularly engage with local universities and colleges to promote planning as a career of choice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration given to protecting people living with dementia in the Overarching Winter Preparedness Plan 2025-26.
answer_text: Earlier this year, under the leadership of the Chief Medical Officer and Chief Nursing Officer, I brought together senior leaders from across the Health and Social Care system to learn from last winter, as we plan for winter 2025/26. I was clear that I wanted a whole system approach looking at what could be achieved both in advance of this winter and in the longer-term.   This process identified the need for a particular focus on interventions to improve the care of those older people living with dementia, frailty and other co-morbidities, who have been identified as some of the most vulnerable to the risks associated with the winter period.  Seven improvement projects have been established which focus on reducing hospital demand by supporting older people, including those with dementia, to stay well and receive appropriate care in the community. This includes improving the use of advance clinical care planning and enhancing pathways for patients, ensuring the right care is delivered in the right place, first time.  The Department recognises that winter preparedness cannot simply focus on primary and secondary health care settings, but that a significant and critical contributor to the delivery of effective services must be focused on ensuring that appropriate care is provided in social care settings and in people’s own homes.  The Department is currently leading a series of cross-sector roundtable discussions to better understand and address unmet need in the community care sector with a particular focus on the provision of more timely access to care whether this is at home or in a care home. Whilst the steps we are taking will help mitigate winter pressures, it is important to note that we are operating in an incredibly difficult environment, with significant financial challenges that are bringing additional risks, including the risk of industrial action.   I published the Winter Preparedness Plan in October and, as in previous years, we will again call upon the public to do everything possible to help protect our system, including utilising community services, where available, and getting vaccinated, if eligible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the estimated cost of waste tourism at recycling centres.
answer_text: Local councils in Northern Ireland have responsibility for the operations and management of their own Household Waste Recycling Centres. Individual local councils are best placed to advise on such information, if they collate it.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the estimated cost to local councils of processing waste emanating from the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: My department does not collect quantifiable data on the estimated costs to local councils relating to the processing of waste originating from the Republic of Ireland. Individual local councils are best placed to advise on such information, if they collate it.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the engagement she has had with the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs on progressing Phase 2 of the A6 Derry to Dungiven Dualling scheme, within the last 12 months.
answer_text: I refer the Member to my answer to AQW 33919/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail recycling rates, in each of the last five years, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: Information regarding Local Authority Collected (LAC) municipal waste and household waste recycling rates are publicly available online via the DAERA website (www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/northern-ireland-local-authority-collected-municipal-waste-management-statistics).
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of (i) successful; and (ii) unsuccessful, applications received under the Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme since its launch in June 2025.
answer_text: In respect of your request for some information in relation to uptake, from Scheme commencement 2 June 2025 until 23 October 2025, there has been a total of 502 applications received; 388 of those applications have been successfully prior approved. 4 applications have not been approved as they were retrospective claims for surgery. The remainder of applications are being held as they did not meet the criteria or did not supply sufficient information in order to process the application fully. Where that is the case, prospective patients have been contacted to support full completion.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 31863/22-27.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide an update on the draft Optimum Remediation Strategy for the Mobuoy site, including an estimated timeline for implementation.
answer_text: The public consultation on the draft Remediation Strategy for the Mobuoy site closed on 2 October 2025.NIEA are now carefully considering the consultation responses and will determine any necessary amendments to the draft Remediation Strategy. Any implementation plan and timetable for remediation remains subject to the outcome of the public consultation and will be considered in the context of the department’s responsibility to protect water quality and the challenging constraints of public finances.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the review, requested by her predecessor, with regard to establishing the use of Derry alongside Londonderry on road signs.
answer_text: My Department has carried out some preliminary work on a review of naming conventions used on traffic signs as was requested by my predecessor, however, more work still needs to be taken forward to finalise the review.  I can confirm that I remain committed in delivering this review and I will ask officials to continue to progress its development.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what role his Department can have in preventing homelessness, given the findings of Simon Community’s October 2025 report Childhood Adversity and Homelessness in Northern Ireland: Breaking the Cycle, which show that institutional contact such as being in care significantly heightens a person’s risk of becoming homeless.
answer_text: The Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 places a range of statutory duties on Trusts in respect of those who have left, or will be leaving, care.  As a result, many young people continue to receive support beyond the age of 18. Where the individual is in education or training, this support can continue up to age 24.Changes are being introduced in respect of care leavers as part of the implementation of the Adoption and Children Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, and are aimed at strengthening arrangements for providing advice, assistance and support to eligible young people as they leave care. This includes measures which will reduce the risk of homelessness for these individuals – placing on a statutory basis the ‘Going the Extra Mile (GEM)’ scheme which allows young people to remain in their foster home beyond their 18th birthday, and publication of information about services to assist care leavers in preparing for adulthood and independent living, including services relating to accommodation. Following a review of Jointly Commissioned Supported Accommodation (i.e. accommodation for young people which is commissioned jointly by the NIHE and HSC Trusts), where young people can remain from 16 to 25, Local Planning Partnerships have been established in all areas of Northern Ireland. The remit of the partnerships is to identify and meet the accommodation, housing and support needs of young people in, or leaving, care and improve transition planning for care leavers.  Additionally, to improve transition planning for young people in care and to help prevent homelessness among care leavers, the Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE) and my Department have collaboratively developed the strategic ‘New Foundations’ action plan.  This will enable all relevant government departments and statutory agencies to act together as the Corporate Family for children and young people preparing to leave care and making the transition to adulthood, in line with the approach set out in the strategy for looked after children, ‘A Life Deserved – ‘Caring’ for Children and Young People in Northern Ireland’.In tandem, my Department is also working with the NIHE in its partnership with Homewards NI—as part of a programme put in place by the Royal Foundation to demonstrate that it is possible to end homelessness—to develop proposals to focus specifically on early support and interventions which will prevent homelessness occurring for young care leavers.  This work is ongoing.My Department has also established a programme of reform, to address a range of known challenges within children’s social care services. A Residential Care Capacity Workstream has been established by the Children’s Social Care Services Strategic Reform Programme Board to focus on recommendations made by the Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services regarding expansion of accessible accommodation within the positive post 18-service & public sector provision of additional smaller children’s

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the delivery of the Intermediate Rent Pilot.
answer_text: Progress has been made on confirming the Intermediate Rent delivery proposal, funding package, and the conditions of funding. Officials are working closely with Maple & May to finalise details of the loan. I expect to award FTC loan funding to Maple & May in early 2026, with the first homes ready to let within the next 12 months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement she has had with the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs on progressing Phase 2 of the A6 Derry to Dungiven scheme, within the last 12 months.
answer_text: There has been continued collaborative working between my officials and their DAERA counterparts in relation the progressing Phase 2 of the A6 Derry to Dungiven Scheme.As you will be aware, part of the works for Phase 2 of the A6 Derry to Dungiven Flagship scheme, between Caw and Drumahoe, will encroach on the illegal waste site at Mobuoy. I have formally approved the department’s response to the recent public consultation on the Draft Remediation Strategy which closed on 2 October 2025.  I have also issued a letter of support to Minister Muir, emphasising the importance of the continued collaboration between our departments.A recent meeting with Minister Muir was organised for 5 September 2025, however due to diary commitments I was unable to attend, with my officials providing representation for the department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the correlation between the low number of residential care beds in the Western Health and Social Care Trust and the long waits at Altnagelvin Emergency Department.
answer_text: The Department acknowledges that pressures within the social care system, including the availability of residential care beds, can have a direct impact on patient flow through acute hospital settings. Within this context, the Department is aware that the Western Health and Social Care Trust has the lowest number of registered care home beds at 2079 total beds. This low number of residential care beds contributes to challenges in discharging patients who are medically fit but require ongoing support in a care setting. Delayed discharges can result in longer stays in hospital wards, which in turn reduce bed availability for patients awaiting admission from the Emergency Department at Altnagelvin Hospital. The Department recognises that improving access to residential care is a key component in alleviating hospital pressures.  Work is ongoing to enhance discharge planning, increase homecare capacity, and improve coordination between hospital and community services to mitigate these delays.To address these pressures, the Department has supported the introduction of a range of initiatives to maximise both homecare and care home capacity which should support with patient flow from our hospitals, streamline the hospital discharge process and improve patient outcomes. These include Early Review Teams to review new homecare packages, Care Line Live an electronic call rostering system, the use of Trusted Assessors to streamline the discharge arrangements from hospital to care homes and a digital Care Home Bed Availability App, which will enable real-time visibility of bed availability across care homes. Whilst multiple factors contribute to emergency department performance, the Department continues to work with Trusts to strengthen discharge pathways, expand community care capacity, and enhance digital infrastructure to support timely transitions of care.

answer_text: The table provided at Annex A provides a count of Non-Domestic Occupiers at 31 March for years 2023 to 2025 by Parliamentary Constituency that had a balance of rates remaining to be paid that carried forward into the next rating year.
