question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, given the findings of Simon Community’s October 2025 report Childhood Adversity and Homelessness in Northern Ireland: Breaking the Cycle, whether he plans to introduce a statutory duty for prevention on public agencies that support people at risk of homelessness.
answer_text: I welcome the work done by The Simon Community in their report ‘Childhood Adversity and Homelessness in Northern Ireland: Breaking the Cycle’ (October 2025).  		There are currently no plans for further legislation to be brought forward placing a statutory prevention duty on public agencies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 26011/22-27, to provide a timeframe for the beginning of each phase of the Housing Executive's Creggan Heights Multi-Elemental Improvement project.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Phase 1 – Creggan Heights MEI will start on the 5th January 2026. The start date of Rinmore, Westway and Lone Moor schemes remains uncertain at this stage and will be subject to the availability of finance.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will consider introducing a poverty act, similar to the Child Poverty (Scotland) Act 2017, to protect key poverty reduction targets and departmental duties in law.
answer_text: My officials are currently analysing and considering all responses received during the recent public consultation on the Executive’s Anti-Poverty Strategy and I will discuss the outcome of this analysis with my Executive colleagues in due course, however, I do not propose to bring forward an Anti-Poverty Act at this time.Decisions on the final content of the Anti-Poverty Strategy will be subject to agreement by the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the resurfacing works in the Ballymagroarty area, in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.While there are many roads within the Foyle area that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.Details of the resurfacing schemes planned for the 2025/26 financial year will be included in my Department’s Annual Report to Derry and Strabane District Council.Officials will continue to undertake regular inspections of the road network and repair any defects in accordance with the Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has made any assessment of the potential for a child payment to support low-income families.
answer_text: I refer the member to the response issued to AQW 32532/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the rational for carers being excluded from the draft Anti-Poverty Strategy.
answer_text: I am aware of the essential role carers play in our society and I know that carer-related issues have been raised during the public consultation on the draft Anti-Poverty Strategy which closed on 19 September. My officials are currently analysing and considering all responses received and I will report the outcome of this analysis with my Executive colleagues.Decisions on the final content of the Anti-Poverty Strategy will be subject to agreement by the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement he has had with his counterparts in Westminster regarding the two-child limit.
answer_text: The two-child limit has been discussed both formally and informally by me and by my officials during engagement with UK Government representatives and Department for Work and Pensions’ (DWP) officials. The UK Government has announced that the two-child limit will be abolished from April 2026.  My officials are engaging with DWP in terms of the legislative requirements which need to be brought forward to remove the restriction in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has made any assessment of the cost of the removal of the two-child limit.
answer_text: The UK Government announced the removal of the two-child limit from April 2026.My officials are currently working to assess the full impact of this change in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of (i) successful; and (ii) unsuccessful, applications to the Universal Credit New Claims Grant since the fund was renamed in March 2025, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The table below provides a breakdown of those successful, unsuccessful and withdrawn applications to the Universal Credit New Claims Grant from April 2025 to September 2025. Due to system limitations a breakdown by constituency is not available.MonthAwarded*Disallowed**Apr 2025860204May 2025985236June 20251235253July 20251078297Aug 20251320392Sept 20251977616						*Whilst a claim may be awarded it may not result in payment as the claimant may reject the award.** Disallowed figures include claims that have been withdrawn by claimant***Figures are subject to final reconciliation checks prior to publishing in Annual report 25/26

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he is working with the Department for Communities to strengthen engagement between the health and social housing sectors, to improve access to the healthcare and support services for elderly tenants.
answer_text: My Department has put in place a Memorandum of Understanding with the Department for Communities (DfC) which defines key working arrangements and responsibilities relating to housing strategy and policy for people with disabilities, including older people. My officials have also established an Interdepartmental Accessible Homes Strategic Forum with DfC which provides a platform for relevant stakeholders to engage and identify areas to collaborate and improve service delivery across housing tenure. Membership of the forum includes DfC, Public Health Agency, HSC Trusts, Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE), Northern Ireland Federation of Housing Associations (NIFHA), and service users. The participation of NIHE and NIFHA ensures that the social housing sector is well represented in the engagement process.The forum has been in place since 2022 and has helped to improve engagement across sectors to ensure we are working collaboratively to improve access to suitable accessible housing. This in turn helps to improve access to healthcare and supportive services for elderly tenants.The forum is underpinned by Interagency Partnership forums between Trusts and Social Housing providers which ensures clear communication pathways between strategy, policy, and operational service delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 31752/22-27, to detail the total cost of these missed or cancelled appointments.
answer_text: Calculating the total cost of missed or cancelled appointments depends on a variety of factors as fixed costs associated with outpatient activity still apply. In addition, missed or cancelled appointments may be rebooked and therefore no financial loss may occur. As such, it would be inappropriate and misleading to use the unit cost quoted to extrapolate the total cost for missed appointments. However, as a guide, the Northern Ireland average marginal unit cost of outpatient attendances is £220.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any action being taken to support private tenants driven into debt, by rising rent costs.
answer_text: In April, I introduced legislation to restrict the frequency of rent increases to once in every 12-months, with a three-month written notice. This will assist low-income tenants by providing assurances that they will not face multiple or unexpected rent increases over a short period of time. Freezing Local Housing Allowance means that many low-income households may be facing a growing shortfall between housing support and housing costs. Last year, I wrote to the Secretary of State for Work and Pensions on this matter, and I will continue to advocate for LHA rates to keep track of the market rents.I would also highlight that private rented tenants who are facing hardship can apply for help through the Discretionary Housing Payment and Discretionary Support schemes. My Department also funds Housing Rights which provides a wide range of services including providing support to tenants and signposting to debt advice agencies. It also provides a meditation service to help landlords and tenants resolve issues and sustain tenancies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the implementation of the recommendations of the Discretionary Support review panel.
answer_text: I am considering the recommendations from the Independent Review Panel for changes to Discretionary Support. These will be assessed within the context of ongoing financial constraint, with a focus to ensuring that budgets remain sustainable over the coming years.However, a number of operational recommendations that could be delivered reasonably quickly without legislative change, for example, call backs and information on right of review, were implemented by December 2021.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) whether his Department has given any further consideration to introducing a holiday hunger payment; and (ii) any research his Department has undertaken on the prevalence and impact of holiday hunger in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The ringfenced funding that was previously provided to enable my Department to make school holiday food grant payments on behalf of the Executive ceased in 2023. In the absence of such funding and the ongoing severe budgetary pressures on the education sector, I am not in a position to reinstate this scheme.My Department has not undertaken any research on the prevalence and impact of holiday hunger in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has considered the use of reinforced material, such as hot rolled asphalt with graphene, for pothole repair.
answer_text: My Department has considered the potential use of graphene pellets as an additive in hot rolled asphalt mixes. This emerging technology has been discussed with pavement engineers from National Highways and the Department’s partner consultants as part of our ongoing review of innovative materials for road construction and maintenance.The Department continues to explore a range of low-carbon technologies and alternative binders, including bio-based materials, as part of its commitment to sustainable infrastructure. These options are being assessed for technical feasibility, environmental benefit, and compatibility with existing industry standards and practices.My officials will remain engaged with industry and research partners to evaluate the suitability of graphene and other innovative materials for future use on the road network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 30977/22-27, to detail whether his Department plans to assess (i) the financial hardship experienced by homeless individuals living in non-standard accommodation; and (ii) the extent to which the lack of cooking facilities in this type of accommodation may be pushing people further into debt.
answer_text: Non-standard temporary accommodation (hotels and B&Bs) is only used in the absence of other options and for as short a duration as possible and I have no plans for such assessments. The Housing Executive has a team of Financial Inclusion Managers to assist customers with money worries, debt or benefits advice and my Department has allocated £2.86m in 2025/26 to support Social Supermarket projects in each Council area. Social Supermarkets aim to alleviate food insecurity by seeking to address the underlying reasons impacting on a person’s ability to access food whilst also meeting that immediate need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has given any consideration to the introduction of a national allergy strategy.

To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 30977/22-27, to detail the number of applicants in non-standard accommodation, who have availed of support from Financial Inclusion Managers, in each of the last three years, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive commenced recording referral reasons for Financial Inclusion Managers in 2024/25 but does not record this information by council area.In 2024/25 a total of 53 referrals were made to Financial Inclusion Managers in which placement in non-standard temporary accommodation was recorded as a reason for referral.  This represented circa 5% of the total number of referrals received.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 31634/22-27, to detail whether consideration has been given to introducing a primary school counselling programme similar to the Healthy Happy Minds initiative, to help address chronic absenteeism.
answer_text: Regular school attendance is a vital protective factor for children. When children and young people are in school, they are supported by trusted professionals who can identify and respond to emerging wellbeing or safeguarding concerns. Absenteeism removes this protective oversight and increases vulnerability. Understanding the underlying reason for non-attendance is essential to developing effective interventions. My department is finalising a new attendance strategy which I intend to consult on shortly. In parallel, I have increased investment to the Education Authority Education Welfare Service to support its Transformation Plan. This will enable a shift in focus towards improving educational outcomes, safety, wellbeing and early intervention. While the Healthy Happy Minds pilot concluded in March 2023, it was not evaluated on the basis of its impact on attendance. Any future provision for a centrally funded counselling service for primary schools must be considered in conjunction with health colleagues to ensure alignment with wider mental health services and to identify the most effective delivery model for younger children.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether any options have been explored to provide food vouchers to homeless families staying in non-standard, or temporary accommodation, without access to kitchen facilities.
answer_text: £2.86m was allocated in 2025/26 to support Social Supermarkets, which provide wraparound services, such as debt and benefits advice, budgeting, healthy eating, cooking on a budget and training and volunteering opportunities, alongside access to food. Homeless Connect also runs FareShare NI, which redistributes surplus food to charities supporting homeless individuals and families. While this is not a voucher system, it does provide access to food through partner organisations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on her Department's efforts to address unadopted developments, including unadopted laneways.
answer_text: On the 25th September I announced the introduction of new Private Streets Enforcement Guidance for staff.  This guidance will greatly reduce the risk of developments, constructed from January 2024, remaining unadopted.  My officials continue to monitor the progress of all housing developments and engage with developers to ensure timely adoption through the normal Private Streets process without the need to engage in enforcement action.  This route accounts for more than 92% of developments which are successfully adopted.Unadopted laneways are not the responsibility of my Department.  However, where a lane which serves 5 or more dwellings is brought to an adoptable standard by the adjacent householders the Department may consider adoption if it is in the public interest.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail whether any consideration has been given to ring-fencing additional Barnett consequential funding from the Warm Homes Plan, to tackle fuel poverty.
answer_text: ANSWERBarnett consequentials provided at a Spending Review are identified at Whitehall department level rather than individual programme level there is no transparency on the level of consequentials relating specifically to the Warm Homes Plan. It is worth noting that the Barnett formula only applies to changes to Whitehall departments budget, announcements funded from existing departmental baselines do not give rise to consequentials.As Barnett consequentials are unhypothecated, it is a matter for the Executive to agree how this funding will be allocated as part of the multi-year Budget, including whether or not to ring-fence funding for a specific purpose.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department will consider reviewing the Houses in Multiple Occupation Act (Northern Ireland) 2016, to reduce the number of households required to define a property as a house in multiple occupation, from three to two.
answer_text: The definition of a house in multiple occupation (HMO) in contained in the Houses in Multiple Occupation (Northern Ireland) 2016 was arrived at following extensive consultation and research, including a previous judicial review, to achieve a balanced approach to the potential risks to occupants. The definition is similar to that contained in Scottish legislation. There are currently no plans to review this definition.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether his Department will review the Houses in Multiple Occupation Act (Northern Ireland) 2016, to reconsider the definition of what constitutes a family, with a view to limiting this definition to immediate family members only.
answer_text: The definition of “family” used for the purpose of defining a house in multiple occupation includes a couple, parent, grandparent, child, grandchild, brother, sister, uncle, aunt, nephew or niece, including stepchildren and half blood relatives. This is to ensure that the law recognises the fact that members of an extended family living under the same roof normally comprise a single household which should not be subject to the full regulatory regime prescribed for HMOs. There are currently no plans to review this definition.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 19768/22-27, to detail whether his Department has given any consideration to implementing a scheme similar to the Scottish Empty Homes Partnership, to tackle the issue of empty homes.
answer_text: I refer the member to my previous answer to AQW 30028/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether regular assessments of the (i) quality; and (ii) condition, of temporary accommodation, provided by the Northern Ireland Housing Executive are being conducted.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“In the acquisition of Single Lets the Housing Executive will inspect the property at the point of acquisition and if the inspection is unsatisfactory or specific criteria is not met, the Housing Executive will not use the property.For placements in non-standard accommodation (B&B/Hotels), the Housing Executive secures the accommodation in the same manner as any other customer group, whereby the respective Hotel/B&B is subject to the relevant checks and accreditation via Tourism NI.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of injury claims submitted in relation to the Newmarket Street and Orchard Street area in the Foyle Constituency, arising from falls caused by slippery or uneven footpaths; and (ii) any actions being taken to (a) address these issues; and (b) prevent further injuries.
answer_text: As my Department is involved in legal proceedings relating to incidents on Newmarket Street/Orchard Street it would not be appropriate to provide detail on this specific case at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, given the findings of the Simon Community’s recent report, Childhood Adversity and Homelessness in Northern Ireland 'Breaking the Cycle, to detail any plans his Department has to ensure increased resources for homelessness prevention, across all Government Departments.
answer_text: ANSWERI attended the launch of the Simon Community’s report on 7 October 2025. Hearing from those with the lived experience of homelessness and the challenges they have faced has provided valuable insight into the problems we are facing and the need to address these.Our Programme for Government sets a clear priority in relation to tackling the issues around social housing and homelessness. In addition, a key component of the Executive approved Housing Supply Strategy is the prevention of homelessness   Responsibility for driving reform in this area falls to the Communities Minister, in the first instance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether data is available on the number of deaths by suicide that occurred in a given year, as opposed to those that were registered in that year.
answer_text: ANSWERNISRA collates data on death registrations as collected by the General Register Office.  Deaths are coded and reported according to the World Health Organisation (WHO) International Classification of Diseases (ICD tenth revision).  Any death suspected to be a suicide must be referred to the Coroner’s Service and can only be registered once the coroner has completed their investigation. As a result, there is often a lag between the date of death and the date of registration.  Registration of a suicide death can therefore take many months or even years.NISRA routinely publish figures on suicides by year of registration and year of occurrence.  Over time, annual data based on the date of occurrence become more accurate but in the most recent years data will be incomplete as not all occurrences will yet be registered to enable them to be incorporated in the official figures. Most suicide deaths (98 per cent) are registered within three years of the death occurring.The information requested on the number of suicides by year of occurrence can be found in Table 16 of NISRA’s Suicide Statistics 2023 Tables. More up to date suicide occurrence figures, incorporating registration information up to 30th June 2025, are planned for publication on the 20th November 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 31752/22-27, whether his Department plans to collect information categorising the reasons for hospital appointment cancellations in order to gain a better understanding of the underlying issues.
answer_text: HSC Trusts have all confirmed they do currently collect information categorising the reasons for cancellations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his assessment that families in Northern Ireland are being disproportionately impacted by the two-child limit, given the prevalence of larger families.
answer_text: I am aware that the 2024 Audit Office Report on Child Poverty stated that families in Northern Ireland tend to be larger than those in the rest of the UK and indicated that it has been suggested therefore that the two-child policy has had a disproportionate impact on families here.I have repeatedly made clear my views on the policy. I am strongly opposed to the two-child limit. I do not believe that the policy has met its stated aims and there is no doubt that it inevitably contributes to child poverty.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount spent on road maintenance within the Ballymagroarty Estate, Foyle constituency, in the last ten years.
answer_text: My Department does not hold the information in the format requested. Expenditure on road maintenance is not recorded per housing estate or constituency area. However, the maintenance spend for the Derry & Strabane District Council area for the last 5 years is detailed in the table below.YearCapital Spend Resource Spend 2024-20256,305,5272,391,5962023-20246,542,4001,489,2332022-20238,662,7221,471,7222021-20225,494,636969,7582020-20215,854,006952,223Further information is available within the Department’s Annual Report to Derry City and Strabane District Council.There have been no capital resurfacing schemes within the Ballymagroarty Estate in the last 10 years. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether he intends to introduce a statutory fuel poverty target.
answer_text: The consultation on the draft Fuel Poverty Strategy did not include statutory targets. Instead, it proposed that progress towards the Strategy’s objectives will be reported through annual Ministerial statements, informed by my Department’s annual report on fuel poverty. This approach will be evaluated as part of the Strategy’s five-year review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in relation to the Fuel Poverty Strategy, when the consultation report and finalised strategy will be published.
answer_text: The summary of responses to the Fuel Poverty consultation is due to be published imminently.I expect to receive the final Strategy document in the coming weeks. Subject to my clearance, I will bring the strategy to the Executive for approval

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the estimated cost for her proposed Flood Forecasting Centre.
answer_text: My Department is actively exploring the creation of a Flood Forecasting Service and we have completed a strategic Outline Business Case which demonstrates it will provide benefit here. On that basis, I have approved funding for this financial year (2025/26) to further explore flood forecasting capabilities here, and this investment marks a vital first step towards building a state-of-the-art flood forecasting service that will strengthen our resilience and help protect communities across the North. The next stage of appraisal is the production of a programme outline business case in the hope of securing the necessary financial approvals to advance to subsequent stages of development. The development of flood forecasting services is complex, with several interdependent strands including data requirements, new hydrometric infrastructure, IT systems, such as communication platforms and forecasting model builds.The full costs for the development and future operation of a Flood Forecasting Service will be developed and refined as part of the Outline Business Case.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the total spend on the Cavity Wall Insulation Action Plan; and (ii) the number of properties who availed of this scheme, in each of the last five years, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:	“The Cavity Wall Insulation (CWI) Action Plan did not commence until the 2024/25 financial year, beginning with a small-scale pilot programme. As such, no works were undertaken prior to this period under the CWI Action Plan.		Please find below a breakdown of the programme to date, detailing the number of dwellings addressed to date and associated spend, by local council area:		Council	Dwellings	£	Causeway Coast & Glens	40	109,856	Mid & East Antrim	44	117,844	Ards & North Down	103	230,949	Belfast City	107	258,514”

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for an update on the energy labelling scheme, which is compatible with EU standards; and (ii) to detail the engagement her Department has had with (a) manufacturers; (b) consumers; and (c) regulatory authorities, to provide clarity and mitigate additional costs.
answer_text: The Energy Labelling Scheme is a reserved matter falling to the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero and the Office for Product Safety and Standards. It is the responsibility of those departments to engage with regulatory authorities. My department meets regularly with industry representatives as part of its Dual Market Access Forum to discuss issues associated with regulation, compliance and potential divergence across a range of areas. The Consumer Council is responsible for consumer engagement but as the Energy Labelling Scheme is a reserved matter, the Consumer Council does not have any data or consumer insights in relation to this Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will consider installing traffic lights at Beech Hill View and the Glenshane Road in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: My Department is in the process of procuring a Feasibility Study to assess the H25 development's transport impact. This study will dictate what, if any, improvements are required to the A6 between the development's access and the city.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the engagement she has had with the Minister for Communities regarding the draft Disability and Work Strategy, specifically in relation to improving access to public transport for people with disabilities.
answer_text: I have had no direct engagement with the Minister for Communities regarding the draft Disability and Work Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there are any plans to expand ADR testing capacity at Newbuildings test centre, Derry.
answer_text: The ADR/ CDG (Carriage of Dangerous Goods) tests are conducted on vehicles that transport dangerous goods and are currently available at the Driver & Vehicle Agency (DVA) test centres in Belfast, Cookstown, Coleraine and Mallusk.The DVA is currently reviewing demand for ADR/ CDG testing across its network of test centres, with a view to expanding the service where resources and capacity allow.  As part of this process, site visits are being carried out at several locations, including Newbuildings, to assess their suitability to offer ADR/ CDG testing.  Any updates regarding additional locations for ADR/ CDG testing will be communicated through the DVA’s normal channels.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the percentage of consultation responses to the draft Anti-Poverty Strategy that highlighted the need to address, or mitigate, the impact of the two-child limit.
answer_text: The Public Consultation on the Executive’s Anti-Poverty Strategy closed on 19 September and analysis of all responses received is ongoing. Once this is completed, I will share the outcome of this analysis with my Executive colleagues, and we will take forward any further development as appropriate before finalising the Strategy for Executive consideration and approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will produce an overarching strategy statement on active travel, that clearly sets out (i) its objectives; (ii) a timetable for delivery; and (iii) the contribution of each major component of the active travel programme.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Audit Office published their report on Active Travel in Northern Ireland on 30 September 2025. Recommendation 3 of this report refers to your question. I can confirm that the report is being considered very carefully and a response to the recommendations will be provided in line with the established process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has explored the possibility of introducing a free breakfast club programme for primary schools.
answer_text: Breakfast clubs play a vital role in supporting children’s health and wellbeing by promoting access to nutritious food, encouraging regular eating habits, and helping pupils start the day ready to learn and thrive in school. However, due to significant budgetary constraints, my Department does not currently have plans to support the establishment of breakfast clubs in all primary schools across Northern Ireland.Despite this, in the 2025/26 financial year, over £7 million has been allocated to schools through the Extended Schools programme. This programme represents the largest source of Department of Education-funded breakfast club provision in Northern Ireland. In 2023/24, a total of 169 breakfast clubs were delivered through Extended Schools and Extended Schools clusters, providing valuable support to children and families in participating communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the digital mobile working solution for gully maintenance.
answer_text: Gully assets and spatial locations, of which there are approximately 650,000, are published on the Open Data NI website: https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/@transportni/drainage-asset.Work to progress a digital mobile working solution is at an advanced stage following several phases of testing and feedback, and input from experienced road maintenance officials and environmental contractors.A small number of issues still remain for resolution to ensure the update does not impact on other critical applications in use. Once resolved, it is planned to test in a pilot area before wider implementation.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they will write to the UK Foreign Secretary, concerning the attacks on the Global Sumud Flotilla, reiterating the lawful humanitarian nature of the mission, and encouraging the UK government to use all diplomatic powers to guarantee safe passage for those on the flotilla.

To ask the Minister for Communities, in relation to legislation preventing landlords from increasing rent more than once every twelve months, to outline (i) any enforcement measures in place; and (ii) the number of times these enforcement measures have been used against landlords since the implementation of these changes.
answer_text: Where a landlord attempts to increase rent more than once in every 12-month period, then this action will have no legal effect and a landlord cannot enforce the increase in court, nor can he harass the tenant, or he will be liable in the same way as if he tried to harass the tenant for any other money that is not due to him.None, as above.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline any efforts being made to increase respite capacity at Rosebud Cottages, in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: The Western HSC Trust are commissioned for 4 short break beds in Rosebud. At present, this facility is paused to short breaks, due to an ongoing need to provide emergency medium / long term residential care.The Trust has sought agreement with RQIA to operate a hybrid model of service provision should risk assessment allow this. This risk is reviewed weekly, however, at present due to the complexity of need - the level of risk is determined to be too high to allow this hybrid service model to occur. The Trust are actively working to support reunification through the Care Planning process, which will allow Rosebud to recommence short break provision.Additional financial resources have been provided via Ministerial priorities, however, this funding has been directed to enhancing family support, clinical and therapeutic services and intensive support. Increasing the number of short break beds above the 4 commissioned beds is not an option for the Trust given the limitations of the physical building.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any research his Department has conducted into food poverty in Northern Ireland, including identification of the most at-risk (i) groups; and (ii) geographic areas, in each of the last 5 years.
answer_text: In 2020, the Department for Communities (DfC) funded a food poverty and Covid-19 survey through YouGov to gain an understanding of the implications of Covid-19 on householders’ ability to access and afford sufficient food. This research focused on the impact of Covid-19 on the whole population, and did not identify the most at-risk groups and most at-risk geographic areas in Northern Ireland. The report can be found at https://pure.ulster.ac.uk/en/publications/food-insecurity-and-covid-19-summary-results-to-department-for-coFor the last five years, the Department has published annual Accredited Official Statistics on food security in Northern Ireland. The table below shows the proportion of food secure and insecure households in Northern Ireland over the last 5 years.Household food security status of all households, 2019/20-2023/24, Northern Ireland 2019/202020/212021/222022/232023/24Food Secure93%95%95%91%91%Food Insecure7%5%5%9%9%Further analysis shows that certain groups are more at-risk of food insecurity than others, for example those living with a disabled adult (particularly where the disabled adult is under pension age), those in rented accommodation (particularly those in social rented accommodation), and those in households receiving income-related benefits. Data is not published at geographic levels lower than Northern Ireland level. Further information can be found at https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/topics/family-resources-survey-and-poverty-analysis

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether any consideration has been given to introducing a statutory right to food.
answer_text: A right to food is one of the guiding principles of the Executive’s Northern Ireland Food Strategy Framework.  Within the 2025-27 Action Plan, there is an action to develop a cross-departmental approach to applying the ‘Right to Food’ guiding principle.  This action is being led by the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs.  My Department is committed to contributing in a collaborative manner.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of properties which have benefitted from the Northern Ireland Housing Executive Cavity Wall Insulation Action Plan, in each of the last three years, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: Please refer to my answer to AQW 33107/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any steps his Department is taking to support carers at risk of poverty.
answer_text: Carer’s Allowance is the main benefit support targeted towards certain unpaid carers, aiming to provide a measure of financial support for people who give up the opportunity of full-time employment to provide regular and substantial care.  Depending on individual circumstances, carers on a low income may be able to access additional financial support through income-related benefits, such as Universal Credit, and associated carer premiums/elements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on her Department's actions to address inconsiderate pavement parking.
answer_text: Following the public consultation on options to tackle inconsiderate pavement parking and the publication of the subsequent findings report, my officials have developed potential next steps for my consideration, and I plan to issue a formal response once I have determined the best way forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the powers that the PSNI has in relation to the drinking of alcohol in public places.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the impact that digestate spreading has on water quality, particularly in the Lough Neagh catchment area.
answer_text: Applying anaerobic digestate and other nutrient-rich material can worsen eutrophication in Lough Neagh and other waterways, if not used in compliance with regulations. The 2019 update to the Nutrient Action Programme Regulations limits use to crop needs and sets rules for storage and application methods, to reduce pollution risk.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any research his Department has undertaken in relation to the impact of the two-child limit on poverty indicators.
To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 26574/22-27, to provide an update on bursary arrangements for allied health students.
answer_text: My officials are continuing to review the existing funding arrangements for commissioned and non-commissioned tertiary level courses, including allied health courses, which lead to careers within the HSC. This review will help inform the development of options for a public consultation which I remain hopeful to launch later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will prioritise the Lisfannon Park area of the Foyle constituency, for inclusion in a residents' parking scheme.
answer_text: I am currently considering my officials’ recommendations in relation to residents’ parking schemes that provide a streamlined approach for considering, assessing and prioritising requests for schemes going forward. It is my intention to make an announcement as to how future residents’ parking schemes will be assessed by my Department in due course.   In the meantime, my Department will continue to develop the schemes already commenced such as in the Bogside in Derry and Iveagh area of Belfast.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will consider the introduction of a child payment scheme for low-income families with children under the age of 16, similar to the provision made by the Scottish Government.
answer_text: Introduction of an equivalent child payment scheme to Scotland was considered by the Independent Advisory Panel but was not included as a recommendation in their Report on future welfare mitigations.My Department is not considering the introduction of a child payment scheme for low-income families.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will consider introducing variable parking on the sides of streets, to allow residents in built-up areas to be permitted to park on one side of the street, based on different times, to help facilitate street cleaning and gully maintenance.
answer_text: My Department has no plans to introduce variable parking measures to facilitate street cleaning and gully maintenance.If parked vehicles are causing an obstruction to the cyclical gully cleaning programme, these are generally noted by the contractor and a further attempt to clean will be made soon after.  If further attempts to clean the gullies are still unsuccessful then, in particular problem areas, plans may be made to enter the area at weekends or evenings, as resources permit. In the case of individual gullies which cannot be accessed, they may be left to the next cyclical clean unless there is a specific complaint.My department is aware of difficulties accessing gullies in some areas within the Foyle constituency and we will continue to engage with residents to complete the necessary maintenance works. If the Member is aware of specific problem sites, my officials are happy to look into this further.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the new paediatric department for Altnagelvin Area Hosptial.
answer_text: Department of Health (DoH) and Department of Finance (DoF) approval to the project was granted in June 2023 at an estimated capital cost of £18.1 million. Planning approval was secured, and the project progressed through the design stages. The tender process concluded in May 2024. A Full Business Case (FBC) was developed to secure approval to an updated capital cost of £22.1 million and an annual additional revenue requirement of £724,000. £1.3 million capital has been spent to date. The FBC was submitted to the Department in December 2024 and it is currently with the Western Trust for updating following feedback from DoH officials in February 2025. FBC approval will allow the Trust to proceed to contract award. As part of the recent Budget 2026 information gathering exercise a capital budget funding requirement of £11.7 million was identified for this project in 2026/27 followed by £10.5 million in 2027/28. Overall, the level of funding requirements identified for Inescapable capital projects in 2026/27 was £445 million compared with a projected budget of £420 million which implies that there will be limited scope to allocate funding next year to Partially Committed Projects such as the paediatric department at Altnagelvin Hospital, depending on the outcome of Budget 2026.  Although it is expected that the project will be included in the ten year DoH Capital Investment Plan, subject to approval, it is likely that there will be some delay.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an an update on the local growth fund which is due to replace the Shared Prosperity Fund in March 2026.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Local Growth Fund had been expected to be the successor to the Shared Prosperity Fund (SPF). The June Spending Review announced £46 million annually for three years and detailed that NIO would work with the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government (MHCLG) to implement the Local Growth Fund, in partnership with the Executive.For months now, I have been pressing the British Government to fulfil their commitment to partnership working on Local Growth. While there has been little information to date, I learned recently that unilateral decisions have been made by the British Government that the SPF would end without a proper successor, and instead the new Local Growth Fund will be heavily skewed towards Capital, with only 33% Resource funding . This is significantly lower than the SPF, which was 75% Resource-based.I recently met with representatives of the Voluntary and Community Sector to discuss this issue. I heard from them about the risk of significant harm to organisations delivering important services currently funded using Resource under the SPF. The current position taken by Whitehall risks a significant number of jobs across the sector, and risks damage to the ecosystem of small organisations that deliver vital services to our communities. This reduction in Resource funding will inevitably result in added pressure across our Departments at a time of already massively constrained budgets.I have raised my concerns, and those of the sector, directly with Hilary Benn, who has committed to arranging a trilateral meeting involving the MHCLG Secretary of State to explore any potential flexibility on the funding split. I anticipate this meeting will take place in the near future.

Table 2 below outlines SPPG routinely collect data on the number of permanent and deciduous extractions under GA for each of the CDS Health and Social Care Trusts from 2018/2019 until 2022/2023 (the most recent year for which figures are available). (SPPG) The table shows that there were significantly more teeth extracted in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust than in any other Trust. The main reason for this is that the South Eastern Trust has considerably more dental general anaesthetic sessions than any other Trust.  In addition, there is a longstanding arrangement whereby patients living in the northern and western parts of the Belfast Trust area are seen in the South Eastern Trust if they require a dental general anaesthetic.  Therefore, some of the patients included in the South Eastern Trust figures actually reside in the Belfast Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline his assessment of the social and economic impact of the two-child limit under Universal Credit, on families.
answer_text: My Department has not carried out an assessment of the social and economic impact of the two-child limit under Universal Credit, on families.

answer_text: Neither the Department nor HSC Pension Service hold information on the number of people who have applied for, or been granted, ill-health retirement due to their long COVID status.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide an update on the Regional Obesity Management Service.
answer_text: In May of this year, I announced the establishment of a Regional Obesity Management Service for Northern Ireland. My Department acknowledges that full implementation of this service will require consideration of financial and service requirements, including clinical capacity, specialist support and the development of a coordinated and sustainable service model and care pathways. The first phase of the ROMS, scheduled to commence early 2026, will focus on the development of a community-based service, where adult patients will have access to lifestyle support to ensure maximum effectiveness, as well as obesity management medication, if clinically appropriate. Further phases will be developed, subject to funding.I have instructed my officials within the Strategic Planning and Performance Group, to develop an implementation plan which outlines a timescale for rollout that provides equitable, sustainable and affordable access to this technology and which is targeted to optimise outcomes for those with the highest clinical need.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any efforts his Department has made to tackle empty homes, and if he intends to review the Empty Homes Strategy.
answer_text: I refer the member to my previous answer to AQW 30028/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any measures his Department is taking to address access to illicit or counterfeit weight-loss injections.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the general availability and use of unlicensed, falsified or sub-standard weight-loss injections in circulation outside the normal healthcare arena.The regulatory regime for human medicines is set out in the Human Medicines Regulations 2012 ("the HMRs"). The HMRs provide a comprehensive scheme for regulating human medicines that covers, amongst other areas, their licensing, manufacture, import, labelling, distribution, advertising and sale and supply.My Department works to ensure continuing compliance with existing laws and guidance. Where evidence or information suggests unlawful practice is occurring or a breach of the HMRs is identified, my Department’s Medicines Regulatory Group (MRG) will consider a range of enforcement options proportionate to the circumstances to achieve compliance, including for example, through the issuing of warning or advice, referral to professional bodies, or prosecution. My Department remains committed to taking all possible steps to combat the illegal promotion, supply or misuse of weight-loss products and to alerting the public about the dangers of using such prescription medicines outside of the regulated supply chain. Working closely with key enforcement partners, MRG continues to actively monitor the marketplace, taking robust action where necessary.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the schools that currently (i) employ, or have access to, a school nurse; and (ii) do not have access to a school nurse, broken down by Local Council Area.
answer_text: There are currently 12 schools which have a school nurse employed by the Education Authority. The relevant schools are listed in the table below broken down by council area.Council AreaSchool(s)1Antrim and Newtownabbey Ballyclare Secondary School*Ards and North DownBangor Academy and Sixth Form CollegeGlenlola Collegiate, BangorCauseway Coast and GlensCross and Passion College, BallycastleDerry City and StrabaneHoly Cross CollegeLisburn and CastlereaghFort Hill Integrated CollegeLaurelhill Community College, LisburnMid and East AntrimLarne High School2Dunclug College, BallymenaSt Killian’s College, Ballymena Mid UlsterHoly Trinity College, CookstownNewry, Mourne and DownSt. Mark’s High School, WarrenpointNotes:1 Excludes Voluntary Grammar, Grant Maintained and Other Maintained sectors, as the information is not held centrally.2 The school nurse role is carried out by two individuals as part of a job share arrangement.All schools have access to the nursing service provided by the Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) and paediatric nurses, who support schools where this specialist role is required, for example in Special Schools. Each HSCT has its own operational model, with some nurses based directly in schools. These nurses deliver the Northern Ireland immunisation programme in schools, offer health promotion time and support schools with pupil care plans that do not require specialist paediatric nurses.The table below lists the schools in which HSCT employed nurses are based:Council AreaSchool(s)Ards and North DownClifton SchoolBelfast City Fleming Fulton SchoolGlenveagh SchoolMitchell House SchoolOakwood SchoolLisburn City and CastlereaghParkview SchoolTor Bank School

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline (i) any steps his Department is taking within this mandate to ensure security of tenure within the Private Rented Sector; and (ii) why a ban on no fault evictions has not been considered.
answer_text: As I have previously stated, my Department is taking forward work, as mandated by the Assembly, to increase security of tenure through the introduction of much longer notice to quit periods. This change will greatly improve security of tenure for individuals and families in the private rented sector. I have no plans to ban no fault evictions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline whether there is a procurement process in place to ensure value for money in the Northern Ireland Housing Executive’s use of providers of non-standard accommodation.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The use of non-standard accommodation is not subject to a formal procurement process. These arrangements are on an ad-hoc basis to respond quickly and flexibly to urgent housing need. Internal controls are in place to ensure expenditure is subject to proportionate checks and robust financial oversight.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 30490/22-27, to detail any plans his Department has to engage with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive to ensure transfer requests for this reason are recorded as a reportable category in the My Housing Executive digital tenant portal.
answer_text: I have asked my officials to engage with the NIHE on this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 27904/22-27, for an update on efforts to secure additional funding to enable a new Action Plan and Implementation Plan to be published in relation to the Protect Life 2 Suicide Prevention Strategy.
answer_text: The new Protect Life 2 Action Plan and Implementation Plan were published on 1st July 2025. The Action and Implementation plans can be found at https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/protect-life-2-suicide-prevention-strategy. £300k of funding has been reallocated to take forward the first year of the new action plan. Funding is not secured beyond existing provision for future years in light of current and expected budgetary constraints for the HSC. The £300k in year 1 will allow PHA to support and facilitate the five Protect Life Implementation Groups (PLIGs) in the HSC Trust areas and to take forward work in relation to the development and provision of a postvention service for under 18 year olds.My officials and their colleagues in PHA are working together to ensure that years 2 and 3 of the Action Plan and Implementation Plan can deliver while remaining affordable within existing resources. The Department will endeavour to achieve as many actions as possible within the plans over future years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans his Department has to provide funding for the Diploma in Specialist Support for Teaching and Learning in Schools, in light of the withdrawal of Skill Up funding previously provided by the Department for the Economy.
answer_text: The Department has no plans to provide funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail why he has not met with the Northlands Addiction Treatment Centre, in Foyle.
answer_text: I want to acknowledge the important role that Northlands play in addressing the harms of substance use, and to supporting vulnerable people right across Northern Ireland.In terms of the commissioning of substance use services, the outworking of the Tier 4 Review is ongoing, and this work includes a market engagement process which will afford key stakeholders, including the Northlands Centre, the opportunity to liaise with officials, in order to develop a sustainable regional delivery model for Tier 4 services that delivers high quality, safe and equitable series.While that regional developmental work is underway, it would be not be appropriate for me to meet with potential individual providers. That said, once this work has concluded I would be delighted to visit Northlands to meet with staff, service users and to see the important work undertaken.I also understand that in the meantime, Departmental officials have offered to meet and discuss funding matters with Northlands.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the rationale for withdrawing funding under the Skill Up programme for the Diploma in Specialist Support for Teaching and Learning in Schools.
answer_text: To support my key priorities such as the Magee expansion, it was necessary to make reductions across Departmental programmes. Regrettably, this included the Skill Up initiative, which funded the Diploma in Specialist Support for Teaching and Learning in Schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline how her Department intends to address the skills gap, created by the removal of Skill Up funding for the Diploma in Specialist Support for Teaching and Learning in Schools.
answer_text: The aim of Skill Up is to support a wide range of subject areas that directly address the skills gap in key sectors relating to my Economic Vision including, Health and Social Care and Child Care, Hospitality and Tourism, Software and Digital, FinTech and Financial Services, Advanced Manufacturing and Green Skills. Available funding has been allocated to best address skills gaps to benefit employment opportunities and enhance a continuously growing and diverse economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the steps her Department is taking to facilitate access to apprenticeship placements, ensuring that opportunities are available across a range of sectors.
answer_text: My Department’s ApprenticeshipsNI and Higher Level Apprenticeship programmes are demand-led, with employers establishing apprenticeship opportunities in line with business need.   		Both programmes are witnessing increasing numbers with participation at an all time high.  For example, the most recently published statistics show that total starts on the ApprenticeshipsNI programme increased by 70% between 2019/20 and 2023/24 and by 48% between 2022/23 and 2023/24.  		Given the success and growing popularity of apprenticeships over the past number of years, securing employment can be competitive in specific sectors and occupations. My Department actively encourages employers to take on apprentices through a range of promotional activities. These include advertising campaigns across TV, radio, and social media, timed to coincide with key points in the academic calendar. 		My Department also engages directly with potential employers and learners at events such as job fairs and career insight days. These efforts aim to raise awareness of the benefits of apprenticeships, increase visibility of available programmes, and ultimately boost participation across all sectors, including trades. By highlighting the financial supports available and showcasing success stories, employers see the value in investing in apprenticeships as a way to build a skilled and sustainable workforce.		The Apprenticeship Action Plan recognises the need to encourage more employers to consider recruiting apprentices and includes deliverables such as piloting Apprenticeship Navigators to support SMEs employing their first apprentices and commissioning research to quantify the financial impact of the apprenticeship system on individual businesses and the economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any steps his Department is taking to improve access to dental care for NHS patients; and (ii) what advice would he provide to patients who cannot seek registration with a dental practice.
answer_text: General Dental Practitioners are independent contractors, and unlike General Medical Practitioners, are under no obligation to register new Health Service patients and are able to decide which patients they will treat under Health Service arrangements.I acknowledge the challenges that many patients are facing in trying to get Health Service dental care when needed, and I am committed to taking meaningful action to ensure the service performs for both patients and dental professionals.In regards to the steps taken to improve access to dental care, on 22 May 2025 I approved a £7m investment in General Dental Services for 2025/26 which includes:£4.5m to fund the continuation of a 30% increase to fees within the Statement of Dental Remuneration related to fillings, extractions and root canal treatment for 2025/26. These treatments were selected as they are clinically beneficial, frequently provided and consistently given as an example of a treatment which costs more to carry out than the previous level of remuneration.£2m over the next two years to fund the continuation of the Enhanced Child Examination scheme, which involves a one-off payment of £16.44 for each child aged between 0-10 years who is newly registered with a GDS dentist and has an enhanced examination completed, which includes an examination, dental disease risk assessment, individualised oral health advice, and age-specific fluoride application.£1.6m to fund the establishment of a payment to dentists who continue to provide Health Service care to incentivise them to maintain their service level.In addition, the Dental Access Scheme is funded until 2027, and continues to provide access for unregistered patients with an urgent or pressing oral health need. Since it commenced on 1st August last year, it has allowed more than 27,000 high-need patients to receive treatment.  From the information you have provided your constituent may be eligible to be seen under this scheme. There are currently 37 practices participating in the scheme including four in the Western Trust area.  Further details, including the participating practices, can be found at https://online.hscni.net/dental-access-scheme/ While these additional investment interventions are necessary to stabilise General Dental Services in the short term, I am clear that, in common with other services, it will require sustained effort to ensure sustainability over the longer term. The next step is the completion of a cost-of-service review, due by the end of March 2026, which will inform the future of General Dental Services.In the interim, our key priorities continue to be ensuring that patients in need, particularly unregistered patients, have access to care; that capacity within the service is focused on priority treatments; and supporting those practitioners and practices who remain committed to providing health service care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide an update on the inclusion of parents with sick children in the Healthcare Travel Costs Scheme.
answer_text: My department is currently focused on the final stages of smooth rollout of the changes to Help with Health Costs (HwHC) to reinstate automatic passporting for people on Universal Credit who are eligible to receive financial help towards the cost of dental treatment, eyesight tests, glasses or contact lens, and travel costs for HSC treatment on referral by a doctor or dentist.  The commencement of a wider review of the provisions within these Regulations and associated HwHC scheme will be considered once this urgent work is completed and in line with available resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline why kickboxing has been removed from the list of approved activities for GCSE Physical Education.
answer_text: The current CCEA GCSE Physical Education specification allows for assessment of Kickboxing. It is as an approved activity for GCSE Physical Education Component 3: Individual Performance in Physical Activities and Sports.This is detailed in the CCEA Teacher Guidance for GCSE Physical Education – see the link below:https://ccea.org.uk/downloads/docs/Support/Teacher Guidance/2024/Component 3%3A Teachers Guide to Individual Performance in Physical Activities and Sports_2.pdfThe guidance lists all approved activities (refer to page 3 and pages 73-89).  CCEA keeps this list under review and also provides schools with the opportunity to submit a request for an activity, not on the CCEA list, for assessment. Applications must be received in June prior to the first year of a candidate’s study.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he is aware of hospital appointments being cancelled due to consultants opting to deliver private clinics.
answer_text: Trusts have confirmed consultants are job planned to deliver as per NHS contract. Any private work must be completed outside of these periods.Due to this, HSC Trusts have confirmed they are unable to capture this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the allocation made by his Department for Home Improvement Grants, for each of the last five years, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 31862/22-27, the information has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any steps her Department has taken to assist young adults, aged between 16 and 21, with learning or intellectual disabilities, to access their Child Trust Funds, when a parent can no longer manage their finances at the age of 18.
answer_text: Where a young adult lacks capacity to manage their child trust fund when it matures, as with any asset, it is necessary for a parent to seek legal authority to manage it on their behalf by making an application for controllership to the Office of Care and Protection of the High Court. However, in some cases, for example, where the child trust fund is the only asset, the court may make a short procedure order (a ‘one-off’ order) instead.   My Department has reviewed and enhanced the information and guidance available to the public on the Department’s website on how to access a child trust fund for a young person lacking mental capacity, which can be found here: https://www.justice-ni.gov.uk/articles/accessing-child-trust-fund-young-person-lacking-capacity. While there are fees applicable to a controllership application, information on the availability of fee exemptions and remissions and how to apply for them is also available on the Department’s website. I have also called for better government co-operation to improve access to guidance and information more generally for parents of young people lacking capacity transitioning to adulthood, and my Department has committed to collaborating on a cross-departmental basis to examine how access to information can be improved. This will include information about accessing child trust funds.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the restoration of automatic passporting under the Help With Health Costs Scheme.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail why he declined an invitation to visit St Eugene's Primary School in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: I have many competing demands on my diary and was unable to accept the invitation to visit St Eugene’s Primary School.In my response to the invitation, I acknowledged that it was a challenging time for the school community and expressed gratitude for the actions taken to reconfigure the site and enable the school to 	re-open swiftly after the essential closure of the junior school building. I also provided a substantial update on the remedial roof works.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide a breakdown of the number of hospital appointments cancelled by consultants, in the last three years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Outpatient activity data, which includes cancelled appointments, is published annually in August by financial year. Information on cancellations is not categorised by appointments cancelled by consultants and this information is not routinely collected. The most recent publication on cancellations and categories included in the information which is routinely collected was released on 7th August 2025 for 2024/25. Along with this publication, the excel file provides historical data. This file can be found at the following link:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/hospital-statistics-outpatient-activity-statistics-202425 1 In the tabs ‘Face-to-Face Reasons for Cancel’ and ‘DPC Reasons for Cancel’ (Day Case Procedure Centre data) in column F, the number of outpatient appointments cancelled due to the consultant being unavailable are broken down  by HSC Trust and specialty and whether or not the data was taken from encompass. This is presented alongside the other hospital and patient cancellation reasons.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in relation to stadia funding under the Northern Ireland Football Fund, whether clubs were informed in advance that they would only be ranked against others within their own tier.
answer_text: The scoring matrix and guidance notes were published on the Department’s webpage 30 January and remain available showing the criteria set for applicants and how applications would be scored. On page 5 of the guidance notes it clearly states that a tiered approach means that similar sized projects will be scored against each other. This is repeated on Page 35 which states that applications will be scored and ranked within their relevant tier. On Page 36 it states that I will consider advice and decide on the number of projects, from each tier, who will move forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail an estimate on the funding required to support the delivery of the Anti-Poverty Strategy.
answer_text: The Anti-Poverty Strategy Public Consultation closed on 19 Sept. My officials are currently analysing and considering all responses received throughout the public consultation process. While the Department for Communities is leading on the Anti-Poverty Strategy, it remains an Executive Strategy, and I will share the outcome of this analysis with my Executive colleagues. The Executive will take forward any further development as appropriate before finalising the Strategy. Departments will be required to ensure that any actions they contribute to the Strategy are adequately funded and resourced, therefore an estimate cannot be provided at this stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 14948/22-27, (i) to provide an update on the exercise that was planned for July August 2024; (ii) what this process entailed; (iii) the number of properties involved, broken down by local council area; and (iii) when a repeat exercise will take place.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“(i) The exercise carried out in July/August 2024 did not identify any issues in terms of occupancy or quality of non-standard accommodation and therefore 100% of properties were deemed reasonable.(ii)	As per AQW 10879 the purpose of such inspections was to ensure non-standard accommodation was being used by those placed in such accommodation. Staff would have raised any concerns in respect of the quality of accommodation should there have been any.(iii)	A total of 47 distinct premises were visited in July/August 2024 with the table below providing a breakdown of the number of premises by Council area.Council AreaNumber of visitsAntrim and Newtownabbey3Ards and North Down1Armagh, Banbridge, and Craigavon1Belfast9Causeway Coast and Glens4Derry and Strabane20Fermanagh and Omagh 8Newry, Mourne and Down1(iv)	Repeat exercises were conducted in January/February 2025 and July/August 2025.”
