question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any research his Department has undertaken in relation to the impact of the two-child limit on poverty indicators.
answer_text: My Department has not conducted any research in relation to the impact of the two-child limit on poverty indicators.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 12078/22-27, (i) for an update on the costings for the introduction of a pregnancy and baby payment; and (ii) whether consideration has been given to a mitigation payment for claimants in receipt of Maternity Allowance who are disadvantaged by the Universal Credit system.
answer_text: The Better Start Grant, which included a pregnancy and baby payment, was one of the recommendations from the Independent Advisory Panel Report on future welfare mitigations.  Updated cost projections for these recommendations have been shared with the Executive and the Committee for Communities and have been presented to the Assembly.It will be for the Executive to decide on any future mitigation package and to consider the allocation of the necessary additional funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) what savings were achieved by the removal of trilingual branding from departmental correspondence; (ii) when this decision was made; and (iii) what advice he received from his officials before proceeding with this removal.
answer_text: It is not possible to quantity potential future savings - the guidance is applied as part of the normal replacement cycles25 June 2025I received information and design options from my officials on the suite of Departmental logos.  All versions of the Departmental logo including a single language logo, an abbreviated single language logo, and a refreshed trilingual language logo, remain in use.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister in relation to the Inquiry (Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses) and Redress Scheme Bill (NIA 15/22-27), to outline (i) why workhouses have been excluded; (ii) why Nazareth House has been removed from the list; (iii) if the inquiry will continue to examine the pathways and practices used in these institutions; and (iv) if foster care survivors can be guaranteed inclusion without requiring a separate inquiry.
answer_text: No institution has been listed for the inquiry yet, as this will be done in secondary legislation. However, workhouses are in the title of the Bill and one of the institutional types. The standardised payment is currently based on admission to a Mother and Baby Institution or Magdalene Laundry. Unlike workhouses, these had a gender specific focus.  This list may be amended by regulations, subject to Assembly approval. Nazareth House was a children’s home examined in the HIA inquiry and was not listed in either the QUB / UU research on these institutions or the Truth Recovery public consultation.The inquiry will examine the pathways and practices of the list of institutions, which will be outlined in secondary legislation.   Foster care may be relevant as a pathway and practice, for example, if a person was placed in foster care from an institution. But as outlined in the consultation, the policy intent is not to examine the entire adoption or care system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans he has to introduce legislation similar to Awaab's Law.
answer_text: I refer the Member to my previous answer on this topic in AQW 22292/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline any consideration given to the impact that the removal of AS levels will have on students from Northern Ireland applying to higher education through the Central Applications Office process in the Republic of Ireland.

To ask the Minister for Communities to outline (i) what assessment his Department has made around the impact of the Universal Credit system on claimants who are paid on a four-weekly basis; and (ii) what steps are being taken to mitigate the risk of debt and hardship for those affected.
answer_text: The impact of Universal Credit monthly payments is that the Universal Credit amount will reduce in an assessment period where the household has received two payments of four-weekly earnings. Claimants who are paid on a four-weekly basis will have one assessment period in a twelve-month cycle, which will contain two wage payments.Universal Credit staff are trained and supported by guidance to explain to claimants how four-weekly payments will affect their Universal Credit award. Those who may need budgeting or debt support are signposted to the appropriate tools and services.Financial support may also be available to anyone in need of short-term financial assistance via the Discretionary Support Scheme, which provides support to those in financial need or require short term assistance in crisis or emergency situations. Further details are available via https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/campaigns/finance-support or by calling Freephone 0800 587 2750.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration given to providing additional staffing resources in care homes to support residents experiencing sundowning, particularly those prone to wandering and at risk of falls during the night.
answer_text: The Department of Health recognises the complex and evolving needs of care home residents, particularly those living with dementia who may experience sundowning, increased agitation, and a heightened risk of wandering and falls during evening and night-time hours.The Department works in close partnership with a range of organisations to support care homes in meeting the needs of residents with complex behaviours. This includes collaboration with the Health and Social Care Trusts, the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG), the Public Health Agency (PHA), and the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA). These bodies collectively contribute to service planning, workforce development, clinical in-reach, and regulatory oversight. The Department also supports the development of integrated care models through the Integrated Care System Northern Ireland (ICSNI), which brings together health and social care providers, service users, and carers to co-design services that are responsive to local needs.As part of our commitment to improving care home provision, the Department has developed the Enhancing Clinical Care Framework (ECCF), which aims to ensure that residents receive timely, person-centred, and multidisciplinary support in line with their individual needs. This includes consideration of behavioural and psychological symptoms of dementia (BPSD), such as sundowning, and the associated risks these present. The Framework promotes:Integrated clinical in-reach from nursing, medical, and allied health professionalsAnticipatory care planning to identify and mitigate risks such as night-time wanderingCollaborative working between care home staff, GPs, district nurses, and social care teamsIn addition, the Department continues to work with Health and Social Care Trusts and the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA) to ensure that staffing levels in care homes are appropriate to meet residents’ needs. While staffing decisions are primarily the responsibility of individual care home providers, the Department is actively exploring ways to support workforce resilience and capacity, including through the Delivering Care programme and wider Adult Social Care Reform. The Department acknowledges the importance of night-time supervision and tailored support for residents at risk of falls and wandering. We will continue to engage with stakeholders, including care home providers and advocacy groups, to assess how best to strengthen staffing models and ensure safe, dignified care for all residents.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of applications for (i) free school meals; and (ii) the school uniform grant, which have been (a) delayed; and (b) deemed unsuccessful due to families being homeless and residing in emergency accommodation, in each of the last three years, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the determination of entitlement to free school meals (FSM) and uniform grants (UG) following the submission of an application and evidence of the applicant’s entitlement to a relevant benefit at the address listed on the application. Families without a permanent address are able to access both FSM and UG if they meet the relevant eligibility criteria. In support of this, the EA helpdesk is able to offer help and guidance to applicants with no permanent home address on the relevant information required to submit an application. The eligibility criteria for FSM and UG allow the children in families seeking asylum to access these supports if they are being assisted by the Home Office National Asylum Support Service. Where a household does not meet the means-tested criteria for FSM, if their child presents at school hungry, schools can request the provision of FSM on humanitarian grounds. The EA has confirmed that no applications made in the last three years for FSM or UG have been delayed or deemed unsuccessful due to families being homeless or residing in emergency accommodation.

answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the determination of entitlement to free school meals (FSM) and uniform grants (UG) following the submission of an application and evidence of the applicant’s entitlement to a relevant benefit at the address listed on the application. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what assessment his Department has made of the impact that ongoing difficulties in NHS patients accessing dental care is having on oral health outcomes.
answer_text: I acknowledge the challenges facing many patients, particularly those not registered with a dental practice, in accessing dental care when they need it. Access to Health Service dentistry remains a priority for me, and my Department has specifically targeted investment this year at improving access for priority groups.The determinants of oral health are largely linked to lifestyle factors such as frequency of intake of dietary sugar, smoking, alcohol use and good oral hygiene behaviours such as daily tooth-brushing with a fluoride toothpaste. Given that most of these factors can be controlled outside of a visit to the dentist, it is not possible to directly link oral health outcomes of the population solely to access to health service dental services. However, dental attendance provides an opportunity to reinforce good oral health behaviours, carry out opportunistic oral cancer screening in adults and detect dental disease at an early stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps his Department is taking to support contributory Employment and Support Allowance claimants who have been left worse off following a migration to Universal Credit.
answer_text: Only individuals receiving income-related Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) will be asked to migrate to Universal Credit (UC) as part of the Department’s Move to UC exercise. Those receiving contribution-based ESA only will not be asked to migrate to Universal Credit. Contribution-based ESA remains outside the scope of the managed migration process and will continue to be paid separately, provided the claimant remains eligible.Transitional Protection is available to all claimants who migrate from income related ESA to Universal Credit (UC). If UC entitlement is less than entitlement on the existing benefit, the difference is paid as top-up so that claimants are not worse off financially at the point of migration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the resurfacing of Strand Road, in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.While there are many roads within the Derry City & Strabane District Council area that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.Details of the resurfacing schemes planned for the 2025/26 financial year will be included in my Department’s Annual Report to Council.My officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline any plans in place to improve and expand access to genetic testing and related counselling services for people with motor neurone disease.
answer_text: Northern Ireland operates a single regional genetics service, which is based within the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust. The service handles genetic testing and counselling requests, with patient referrals coming from GPs or other primary care providers.The breakdown of waiting times to access counselling services in relation to genetic testing for motor neurone disease (MND) by HSC Trust is not available. In terms of predictive testing, MND patients and their families would be triaged for urgent Genetic Counselling (GC) appointments. Currently, there are 147 patients on the urgent GC waiting list as a whole. The waiting times range from 0 to 130 days, with a median wait time of 45 days.Genomic testing for MND diagnosis is conducted through the regional neurology service. Patients with suspected genetic MND are referred to a specialist neurologist, who can then offer genetic counselling and testing. The establishment of the Genomics Medicine Centre in 2015 to advance genomic medicine offers improved diagnosis and understanding of rare diseases, like MND. Furthermore, my Department is currently consulting on the final report of the neurology review which includes recommendations for the optimum configuration of neurology services for the next 10-15 years which will have an impact on the development of MND services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the full range of options considered, including all recommendations and advice received from officials, prior to deciding on the number of projects to be taken forward for stadia funding in each tier of the Northern Ireland Football Fund.
answer_text: I will not be publishing this information at this stage. The Department will fully consider requests for any information made in line with terms of the relevant legislation.

answer_text: Information provided by the Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts on the number of consultant psychiatrists employed at 31 August in each of the last two years is detailed in the table below:Consultant Psychiatrists Employed, by HSC TrustDateNumber / WTEHSC TrustBelfastNorthernSouth EasternSouthernWestern31-Aug-24Number  7035302716WTE61.527.826.123.814.931-Aug-25Number  7138302418WTE62.132.926.721.816.2Source: Information & Analysis Directorate, DoHA number of actions are being progressed to address these challenges:A Mental Health Workforce Review Report was published in July 2023, setting out 18 recommendations to address workforce challenges across statutory mental health services. Work is currently being progressed to deliver a number of key recommendations in relation to both costing the proposed future workforce profile and development of a future recruitment and training plan;In parallel, my Department has established a Psychiatry Workforce Task and Finish Group to identify a range of actions to help alleviate the pressures within the psychiatry workforce in the short to medium term, including addressing geographical inconsistencies where possible;  Following a formal application from the Western Trust, my Department recently approved an enhanced Recruitment and Retention Premia rate of up to 20% to help attract consultant psychiatrists to hard-to-fill posts; As part of work to increase the attractiveness of Northern Ireland as a place within which to pursue medical education and support the retention of resident doctors, my officials, along with Trust Chief Executives, are developing a transparent method to align training post distribution with service needs, with a pilot being taken forward in Core Psychiatry this year;And as part of a significant package of training expansions I announced in May 2025, I confirmed an additional 26 new medical specialty training posts, including 6 in Core Psychiatry.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail which organisations the Department provided direct support to in completing applications for stadia funding under the Northern Ireland Football Fund.
answer_text: Application Guidance and the scoring matrix was provided to all eligible clubs on the Northern Ireland Football Fund website - The Northern Ireland Football Fund Performance Programme documents | Department for CommunitiesMy officials arranged Application Information Sessions on the 4th, 5th and 6th Feb 2025 at venues across Northern Ireland. All eligible applicants were invited to these sessions.Eligible applicants were also given the opportunity to email the Northern Ireland Football Fund if further clarification was required prior to the application closure date.Any communication given in all these instances was clarification on the application guidance and process. answer_text: The information requested is regularly published on the Open Data NI portal at Portal | Ministers Meeting

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the average cost to repair a pothole by her Department, compared with the average cost of a repair carried out by external contractors.
answer_text: My Department does not hold information on the average cost of repairing a pothole as the cost for repairing potholes falls within the Department’s budget for Patching. This term is applied to small scale works necessary to keep roads in a satisfactory state of repair, including infilling potholes but also the repair of small areas affected by cracking or settlement.As the materials, specifications, quantities and methods used vary from location to location there is no single method or technique used in the repair of surface defects and as such it is not possible to provide a single unit cost for repairs carried out by either our internal or external contractors.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline what action is taken when his Department is notified of an unlicensed House in Multiple Occupation.
answer_text: Any report of an unlicensed HMO made to my Department would be passed to the NIHMO Unit in Belfast City Council for action. The NIHMO unit investigations include visiting properties and other information gathering, such as, taking witness statements, requesting tenancy agreements and the identification of documentation etc. If the property is deemed to be in HMO usage, officers will contact the owner and/or managing agent to establish if they have a reasonable excuse for occupying the property as an HMO without a licence. If no reasonable excuse is received, or the excuse is not acceptable, (and subject to the seriousness of the breach and the past record of the person) the council may either issue a Fixed Penalty Notice or proceed to prosecution.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has explored options to expand accommodation provision for new parents whose babies require neonatal care at the Royal Maternity Hospital in Belfast, particularly for families travelling from other parts of Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Within the Royal Jubilee Maternity Hospital in Belfast there are currently two parents’ bedrooms within the current Neonatal Intensive Care Unit (NICU) floor. This will increase to seven bedrooms in the new maternity building.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline (i) what level of cooperation currently exists between the Houses in Multiple Occupation licensing team and planning authorities; and (ii) any measures which are being taken to strengthen and streamline this process.
answer_text: (i) On receipt of an HMO licence application (whether it is a new or renewal application) the NIHMO Unit will contact the local council planning team to inform that an HMO licence application has been received, and to ascertain if planning permission for HMO usage or a Certificate of Lawful Existing Use or Development (CLEUD) has been granted. On request from the planning department the NIHMO Unit will provide a record of the findings (including inspection records) to assist planning colleagues in any planning or CLUED applications they may receive, or to assist in any enforcement action they are considering. (ii) I am not aware of any measures currently being taken.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of licences for Houses of Multiple Occupation awarded, for each of the last four years, broken down by local council area.
To ask the Minister for Communities to provide an update on the Fundamental Review of Allocations review of the Housing Selection Scheme.
answer_text: The Fundamental Review of Allocations (FRA) is being implemented over four Stages. Stages 1, 2 & 3 are complete, with 10 of the 20 proposals implemented. Departmental officials are continuing to work with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive on the remaining 10 proposals as well as a review of the Primary Social Needs (PSN) points and criteria.Further information can be found at The Housing Executive - Fundamental Review of Allocations.

answer_text: This data is not held centrally in the Department of Health, however information provided by the Health and Social Care Trusts on the whole-time Equivalent (WTE) staff in Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services by HSC Trust in 2022, 2024 and 2025, is detailed in the table below:HSC whole-time Equivalent (WTE) staff in Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services by HSC Trust in 2022, 2024 and 2025HSC TrustWTE 2022WTE 2024WTE 2025Belfast192.0214.7200.4 Northern87.276.084.8 South Eastern *- - - Southern109.8100.998.6Western53.258.6 68.0*BHSCT deliver CAMHS services on behalf of SEHSCT.This data reflects staff numbers collected to inform the Mental Health Workforce review as part of Action 32 of the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any assessment his Department has made of the financial hardship experienced by homeless individuals and their families, as a result of being placed in non-standard accommodation.
answer_text: While no assessment has been made, the Housing Executive funds a team of Financial Inclusion Managers to assist customers with money worries, debt or benefits advice.  The team aim to assist customers to maximise their income, reduce financial stress, and sustain current accommodation and/or prepare for a new permanent home, assisting in the prevention of homelessness.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will consider reducing the age at which women are invited for cervical smear tests.
answer_text: The age thresholds for population screening programmes are based on recommendations from the UK National Screening Committee (UK NSC), an independent scientific advisory committee which advises the four nations, on all aspects of screening. The UK NSC regularly reviews its recommendations on screening as new research and evidence becomes available. When considering who to screen, the benefits of offering a screening programme are weighed up against the possible harms. The UK NSC only recommends screening when it has concluded that the evidence shows that the benefits of screening outweigh the potential harms, for each age cohort.  For cervical cancer screening, currently women aged 25-49 years old are invited for cervical screening every three years, and those aged 50-64 years old are invited every five years. Screening younger women (under 25) is not recommended by the UK NSC because cervical cancer is very rare in this age group, and unnecessary treatment can cause significant anxiety and potential harm, such as a higher risk of premature birth in future pregnancies.Regardless of age, anyone experiencing any symptoms of concern should contact their GP immediately.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any assessment her Department has made of the impact of houses of multiple occupancy on parking pressures and traffic congestion in residential areas.
answer_text: Planning applications proposing houses of multiple occupation, including any requirement for car parking, is the responsibility of the local planning authority. While on-street parking surveys submitted in support of such planning applications are considered by my Department from a road safety and traffic progression perspective, it is a matter for the local council planning authority to determine the acceptability of such planning applications.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail if consideration has been given to reinstating martial arts within the list of approved activities for GCSE Physical Education, given their exclusion in 2018 due to assessment issues.
answer_text: The current CCEA GCSE Physical Education specification allows for assessment of Martial Arts. It is as an approved activity for GCSE Physical Education Component 3: Individual Performance in Physical Activities and Sports.This is detailed in the CCEA Teacher Guidance for GCSE Physical Education – see the link below:https://ccea.org.uk/downloads/docs/Support/Teacher Guidance/2024/Component 3%3A Teachers Guide to Individual Performance in Physical Activities and Sports_2.pdfThe guidance lists all approved activities (refer to page 3 and pages 73-89).  CCEA keeps this list under review and also provides schools with the opportunity to submit a request for an activity, not on the CCEA list, for assessment. Applications must be received in June prior to the first year of a candidate’s study.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the progress of the Social Housing Development Programme, including (i) the number of homes completed within the last twelve months; and (ii) the current funding situation for delivery in 2025-26.
answer_text: For the period 2024/25, 1,410 social homes have been completed.The current budget, following June Monitoring, is £177.5M. It is estimated this will enable around 1,150 social housing starts in 2025/26. I will continue to bid for further funding to enable the commencement of 2,000 new social homes this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide an update on the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031, including (i) the current funding position; and (ii) the 45 per cent uplift in staff for statutory services.
answer_text: The Mental Health Strategy 2021 - 2031 Funding Plan recognised that implementation of its 35 actions would require a significant investment, estimated at £1.2 billion over ten years (based on 2021 costs).  However, to date, no additional funding has been provided to my Department to support implementation of the Strategy and so existing Departmental resources have been used to progress many of the actions.  A total of £12.3 million, has been utilised across 20 specific MHS actions from 2021 to the end of 2024/25. This represents only 16% of the funding identified (£76.93m) as required for the Mental Health Strategy by the end of 2024/25.The absence of dedicated additional funding has necessitated a partial and phased implementation and, undoubtably, progress has been constrained in areas due to the challenging financial position.  These efforts have enabled the advancement of a substantial number of MHS actions, including: •	The development and implementation of a three-year action plan focused on early intervention and prevention. •	The establishment of the infrastructure to support a regionally consistent Mental Health Service. •	Targeted investment in Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS). •	The rollout of a region-wide perinatal mental health service; and •	Enhanced support for crisis and specialist services, such as those for personality disorders and eating disorders. A major factor in the delivery of the Strategy is to ensure that the limited resources available are used to the best effect.   A Mental Health Workforce Review Report published in July 2023 made 18 recommendations.  In particular, the Report recommended a 45% increase in the current combined Child and Adult Mental Health Strategy and Adult workforce, bringing the workforce number from 4237 Whole Time Equivalent (WTE) posts to 6140 WTE, subject to future budget availability.A co-produced implementation plan has been drafted, and a number of recommendations have been prioritised for implementation.In particular, Recommendations 1 (to cost the future workforce profile set out in the Mental Health Workforce Review) and 2 (to undertake a prioritisation of service developments to inform a future recruitment plan) Have been prioritised following a commitment given to the Public Accounts Committee.My officials have established a task and finish group and engaged external support to progress both recommendations.  This work is well underway, with a final report expected in the Autumn. However, I must be clear that any increase in the future workforce profile for statutory mental health services will be dependent on the availability of the required budget to fund the increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to improve access to timely diagnosis and treatment for polycystic ovary syndrome.
answer_text: Women’s health, including services for those suffering from conditions such as Polycystic Ovary Syndrome and Endometriosis, is a priority for my Department.Following a Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) review, the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) have established a regional forum, with Trust, GP and PHA representation, to oversee ongoing service improvement and implementation at a local and regional level in Gynaecology services.The aim of the review was to identify areas where improvements could be made in the extensive waiting lists for gynaecology services, and to ensure that patients are treated as quickly as possible to ensure best possible outcomes, this includes those women suffering from conditions such as Polycystic Ovary Syndrome.Work to develop standardised clinical pathways for common gynaecology conditions is underway, with mapping from a patient’s initial presentation at GP services into enhanced GP Service Hubs, secondary, regional and tertiary services. These new pathways will benefit both patients and clinicians ensuring that patients are treated by the right person, at the right time, in the right place. These changes will of course require additional funding to achieve.Gynaecology Services is an area which has been identified for additional funding need and Trusts are working to address the long waiting times through validation, additional clinics, daycases and inpatients activity.In addition, the Department recognises that there is a clear need for improved public awareness, information and education on women’s health matters from an early age and across the life course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of women currently diagnosed with polycystic ovary syndrome; and (ii) the average waiting time for diagnosis, in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: At present, there is no mechanism within Trust data systems to extract figures specific to polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) diagnoses. Women presenting with symptoms suggestive of PCOS are assessed within the general gynaecology outpatient services, rather than through a dedicated PCOS clinic. As such, diagnostic coding and reporting are embedded within broader gynaecological pathways, making it difficult to isolate PCOS specific data.With regards to waiting times, the timeframe to diagnosis is directly linked to the waiting period for new gynaecology outpatient appointments. These waits are influenced by overall service demand, capacity and acuity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on (i) the introduction of a ban on the use of Trimethylbenzoyl Diphenylphosphine Oxide, given growing health and environmental concerns associated with this substance; and (ii) when this will apply in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Consumer Safety is a reserved matter sitting with the Department for Business and Trade and the Office for Product Safety and Standards. The remit of this question is outside the scope of the Department of Health.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the rationale for withdrawing funding, under the Skill Up programme, for the Level 3 Teaching Assistant qualification.
answer_text: In order to finance my key priorities, such as the Magee expansion, reductions were made to Departmental programmes. Unfortunately, this included Skill Up which funds the Level 3 Teaching Assistant qualification.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) why his Department's forms, including responses to Assembly Questions, which previously included Irish and Ulster Scots alongside English now appear in English only; and (ii) whether this change applies across all forms used by the Department.
answer_text: My Department’s corporate identity guidelines include a single language logo, an abbreviated single language logo and a refreshed trilingual language logo which is used when correspondence relates to Irish or Ulster-Scots language, identity or culture.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of Health and Social Care Trust-owned properties being used for social housing tenants, broken down by (a) sheltered housing; (b) supported living for people with learning disabilities; and (c) accommodation for people with mental health needs, in each of the last three years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts have a responsibility to provide housing accommodation for people with learning disabilities and mental health needs. This includes providing housing-related support services to help people live as independently as possible in the community. The number of Health and Social Care Trust-owned properties being used, broken down by type and Trust, in each of the last three years, is shown in the table below: Sheltered Housing(b)Supported Living for learning disabilities(c)Accommodation for people with mental health needs Year22/2323/2424/2522/2323/2424/2522/2323/2424/25BHSCT*000191914444NHSCT000000000SHSCT000333000WHSCT000222333SEHSCT000111121212Total000252520191919* Note one of the properties contains 25 one bedroom apartments with 24 hour care and support also 5 properties closed at the end of 2023 and are currently vacant pending redevelopment by a housing association.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of times (i) the Central Housing Forum has met; and (ii) his Department's officials have met with the Forum, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has confirmed that the Central Housing Forum has had 10 meetings in each of the past five years, with a total of 50 meetings over this period.I attended a Community Conference run by the Housing Executive and the Central Housing Forum in 2024. Officials also were in attendance. Officials from the Department have not met with the Central Housing Forum in the last five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) an update on funding for the Affordable Warmth Scheme; and (ii) if it will be delivered within this financial year.
answer_text: I committed £7.5m to the scheme this year. This covered £5m of committed approvals from last year and £2.5m for new approvals this year, meaning approximately 480 new approvals can be issued by NIHE to those in greatest need. I requested additional funding for the AWS in the June Monitoring Round, however, that bid was not met. I will continue to press for additional funding in this financial year for this important scheme through the monitoring round process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the traffic calming and road safety measures for Sandbank Cottages in the Lenamore area of the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users.  The Department considers all requests for improvements to the road network as part of the Local Transportation and Safety Measures (LTSM) programme.A traffic survey has already been carried out on the Lenamore Road. However, due to pressures on staff, the survey data has not yet been assessed in detail. Once it is assessed, the findings will be considered in line with current policies and any potentially viable schemes will be considered for inclusion in the Traffic Calming Works Programme for 2026/2027, subject to the availability of funding. Concerns regarding driver behaviour such as speeding should be directed to the PSNI using their non-emergency 101 number.One of the best ways to ensure the safety of our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury on roads by 2050 is by changing driver behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe.  The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps she is taking to address the long-standing staff resourcing issues within her Department, given ongoing concerns from industry stakeholders and from within her Department, that staffing shortages are impacting its capacity to deliver essential works and services through existing contracts and the current procurement process.
answer_text: My department is taking action to tackle long-standing staff resourcing challenges that have affected our capacity to deliver essential works and services. In collaboration with NICS HR, who are responsible for all NICS recruitment, we are implementing a strategic approach to attempt to address these issues.Recruitment initiatives to fill critical vacancies have been prioritised, resulting in the launch of 18 Professional & Technical (P&T) campaigns and the successful appointment of approximately 300 staff. NICS HR indicate that approximately 400 new staff will be offered appointments in my department in the near future. To further enhance internal capacity, my department is pursuing innovative recruitment models. Notably, my department took a leading role in developing and delivering a bespoke P&T Skills Academy focused on Civil Engineering career pathways, and plans are being developed to run this again. This initiative complements the existing Civil Engineering Apprenticeship and Graduate Trainee programme that were already in place within my department.  Additionally, my department has committed to 32 apprenticeships through the Operational Delivery Apprenticeship Scheme (ODAS), with scope for future expansion. These roles are primarily aimed at supporting operational delivery across the organisation.Preparations are well advanced for a new Trainee Planner grade, aimed at bringing new talent into the planning profession. This initiative is intended to support future staffing requirements by providing existing general service personnel with relevant skills for specialised departmental roles.To strengthen governance and oversight, my department established a dedicated People Development and Workforce Planning Directorate, overseen by a People Sub-Committee reporting directly to the Departmental Board to manage workforce planning and mitigate capacity risks. The DfI People Strategy (2024–2026) sets out our objectives to build capacity, support capability and strengthen our culture as an organisation, with a focus on inclusive leadership, and promoting staff wellbeing. This strategy is fully aligned with the broader NICS People Strategy, ensuring a cohesive framework for workforce development, talent management, and organisational resilience across the NICS.Workforce planning is being further strengthened through the adoption of the NICS Strategic Workforce Planning Guide, which incorporates succession planning and skills gap analysis. Structured career pathways and targeted training programmes are being introduced to support staff development and retention.  To promote staff wellbeing and resilience, my department is advancing career development initiatives and offering comprehensive personal and professional development opportunities to staff across all disciplines.Despite these proactive measures, significant challenges remain. Budgetary constraints and historic underinvestment continue to constrain recruitment and retention, parti

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail a breakdown of the number of transfer requests submitted to the Housing Executive by tenants citing anti-social behaviour by neighbours as a reason, in each constituency for the last three years.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive of the Housing Executive and she has responded as follows.“The specified application/transfer reason is not a reportable category in the Housing Executive’s Housing Management System (HMS).”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on proposals for traffic calming measures on Temple Road, Strathfoyle.
answer_text: I can advise that traffic calming measures at Temple Road, Strathfoyle have been included on the delivery programme for 2025/26 as part of the Local Transport and Safety Measures for the Derry City and Strabane Council area programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on the estimated costs associated with the (a) structural modelling; and (b) installation, of the suicide prevention barriers on the Foyle Bridge; and (ii) to outline how these figures were determined.
answer_text: My Department is continuing to engage with stakeholders, including the Mental Health Champion and the Cross Departmental Coordinated Action Group, to explore options for progressing suicide prevention infrastructure on the Foyle Bridge.The initial estimate of £800k for structural modelling was an early projection based on similar infrastructure projects. Officials undertook a detailed estimate in 2024 and this cost was assessed at approximately £415k.  My Department is now working with its partner consultant to further refine this estimate. The type of proposed suicide deterrent measures, including railings will be dependent on the structural modelling, but may cost in the region of £10m. My Department is not currently funded to undertake this work and I am actively pursuing potential funding sources, including cross-border opportunities such as the Shared Island Fund.While funding remains a challenge, my Department views the structural assessment as a critical first step and will continue to work with stakeholders to ensure that the necessary groundwork is in place to deliver enhanced safety infrastructure at this location.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide an update on the implementation of the National Joint Council pay award for 2025-2026.
answer_text: The Housing Executive submitted interim pay remits for 2025-26 earlier this year seeking approval to implement incremental pay progression for its staff. Following approval these were processed by the Housing Executive in its May pay. Subsequently the Housing Executive submitted full pay remits to the Department on 14 August 2025 seeking approval to apply incremental progression and revalorisation for the 2025/26 financial year. These are in the final stages of review, and I have been assured that, when the Department’s considerations are complete, the Housing Executive will progress matters as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any steps his Department will take to support benefit claimants who have lost their Rate Relief entitlement as a result of the Universal Credit managed migration process.
answer_text: My Department does not have responsibility for entitlement to, or the administration of, Rate Rebate. This is a matter for the Department of Finance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department considered the context of the Assembly debate on the active travel amendment to the Climate Change (No.2) Bill when revising the definition of active travel spend from around two per cent for new active travel schemes to a reported five to six per cent of the transport budget.
answer_text: The member will appreciate that it is incumbent on my department to consider all of its obligations in relation to Section 22 of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 legislation. I can confirm that my department did consider the context of the assembly debate in doing that.My department has not changed what it considers to be active travel, which is travelling by physically active means such as walking, wheeling, or cycling. What has changed since the introduction of the Act is that, for the first time, there is a need to capture and report all active travel expenditure funded from overall transport budgets across my department in line with Section 22 of the Act.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance if his Department intends to review the Rate Relief Regulations (NI) 2017 to include benefit claimants who have been disadvantaged following the Universal Credit managed migration process.
answer_text: The Rate Relief Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2017, which provide for the rate rebate replacement scheme, have been continually monitored by my Department since they were introduced in September 2017 to align with the Department for Communities (DfC) introduction of Universal Credit. The policy, and its operation, was also the subject of three public consultation exercises. In 2022 my Department published an independent interim review of the rate rebate replacement scheme and has made a series of amending Statutory Rules to adjust the scheme. The rate rebate replacement scheme won’t reach a stable or consistent caseload until the Department for Communities (DfC) completes its migration to Universal Credit. Responsibility for the managed migration process, including Transitional Protection for those moving from legacy benefits, lies with DfC - not with my Department.The Executive continues to protect claimants here from the 10% austerity cut to consequential funding applied by Treasury for this support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline any action being taken to address the shortage in the number of psychiatrists in the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will consider front-loading the Phase 2 works of the A6 Londonderry to Dungiven Dualling scheme at Caw rather than Maydown.
answer_text: Part of the works for Phase 2 of the A6 Derry to Dungiven Flagship scheme, between Caw and Drumahoe, will encroach on the illegal waste site at Mobuoy. My Department has been working collaboratively with our colleagues in DAERA who have launched a public consultation on their Draft Optimum Remediation Strategy on 13 June 2025.  The consultation period closes on 2nd October 2025 and my officials are currently considering this document and will provide response to the consultation process as necessary.In order to progress Phase 2 of this major road infrastructure project, we must await finalisation of the DAERA strategy and the associated timeline for its implementation.  Once this is confirmed my officials can determine the most efficient delivery mechanism for the progression of the scheme, which will include consideration of the construction sequencing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on efforts to improve access to (i) Megestrol; and (ii) Medroxyprogesterone.
answer_text: Medicines are manufactured as part of a highly regulated, complex and global industry; therefore, medicine shortages are not uncommon and can occur for a variety of reasons.  If a medicine supply disruption does occur, there are tried and tested tools and processes that can be implemented to ensure patients have access to appropriate treatments.Earlier this year there was a supply disruption reported by the marketing authorisation holder of Megace® (megestrol) tablets. A Medicine Supply Notification, MPS 3351, was issued to healthcare professionals on 25 March 2025 providing them with advice and guidance on the actions to take - https://bso.hscni.net/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/MPS-3351-Supply-Alert-Megestrol-Megace-160mg-tabs.pdf. However, this supply disruption has now been resolved.The marketing authorisation holder of medroxyprogesterone tablets discontinued their branded version of this product, Provera®, but are producing a generic equivalent. This information was communicated to general practices and community pharmacies in Northern Ireland on 1 July 2025, with advice to switch patients from the branded version to generic equivalents.Both megestrol and medroxyprogesterone tablets are currently available for local community pharmacies to order from local wholesalers. If a patient is experiencing a medicine supply disruption they should contact their healthcare professional for advice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the efforts being made to address the number of drug-related and drug-misuse deaths; and (ii) what engagement he has had with the Department of Education around improving awareness.
answer_text: My Department continues to lead on the cross-departmental strategy, Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery, which aims to reduce the harm for these groups through actions that place emphasis on harm reduction support measures for people who use alcohol and other drugs. The Substance Use Strategic Commissioning and Implementation Plan was subsequently published and sets out the current priorities for substance use service delivery.  I have provided some examples below. Drug & Alcohol Monitoring & Information System (DAMIS) DAMIS is an “early warning system” designed to identity, verify and assess emerging threats and potential trends in drug and alcohol use within Northern Ireland. The purpose of DAMIS is to share information via email amongst people who work within substance use services, who can provide information on emerging issues at a very early stage (such as a sudden increase in a particular drug being used or emergence of substances with unexpected unpleasant or dangerous effects) and are ideally placed to provide targeted harm reduction information to their clients. Much of the information sent out through DAMIS is practical advice aimed at reducing the harms to people from their drug use. If it is appropriate for information from DAMIS to be shared with the general public, PHA Communications adapt this information in terms of public and/or targeted health messaging.Take Home Naloxone Programme (THN) and Low Threshold Services Naloxone is an opioid antagonist, which temporarily and rapidly reverses the effects of heroin and other opioids. Since 2012, the Public Health Agency (PHA) has funded the Take Home Naloxone (THN) programme, which aims to supply THN packs to those at risk of opioid overdose as well as their friends, family and people who work with those at risk of opioid overdose. PHA currently fund a Harm Reduction training programme which includes Naloxone Administration Training for members of the public and Naloxone Suppliers Training for those staff who work within Take Home Naloxone sites. On 2 December 2024, legislation changed to widen the remit of who can supply naloxone without a prescription. This is particularly significant to those working with people experiencing homelessness who were previously excluded. A number of these organisations have come forward since the new legislation has been passed and are in the process of completing the governance requirements of the programme. Low Threshold Services provide direct harm reduction interventions (such as needle and syringe exchange, naloxone provision and safer injecting advice). Services also include interventions to reduce levels of substance use and interventions that encourage and support service users to reduce the amount of substances that they use and to use substances in a safer way.Targeted Prevention and the Youth Engagement Services (YES)The PHA commission Targeted Prevention Services for young people who had been identified as using substances or being

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what steps are being taken to improve endometriosis care.
answer_text: There is currently considerable work underway across the health and care system to try to reduce waiting times and develop pathways for gynaecology specific conditions such as endometriosis in each Trust across Northern Ireland.The Department has established a Regional HSC Gynaecology Services Forum, with Trusts, the Public Health Agency, and General Practitioner representation. This forum is overseeing ongoing service improvement, and the implementation and development of gynaecology service pathways at primary, intermediate, secondary and tertiary care levels. as well as overseeing the implementation of the “Getting It Right First Time” (GIRFT) Gynaecology Report. A Task and Finish group has been established which is working to develop a pathway for endometriosis, for patients that have minor, intermediate or severe diagnosis.  The development of a pathway will ensure that services are available to meet the needs of women at specific aspects of the condition’s progression.My officials are also working to establish a Regional Engagement Forum, this will allow the Department to garner expert user feedback from stakeholders.Additional funding has also been approved non-recurrently for waiting list initiatives (both independent sector and in-house) to help reduce waiting times for patients requiring red flag and other time critical assessments and treatments across a range of specialties, including gynaecology. Although I acknowledge that the current financial position may make progress difficult for non-red flag or time critical assessments and treatment such as endometriosis, unless clinically indicated.My officials will continue to explore options to strengthen pathways to improve care for women suffering from endometriosis and other gynaecology conditions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any consideration given to implementing Neonatal Care Leave and Pay entitlement.
answer_text: Between July and September 2024, the public consultation on the ‘Good Jobs’ Employment Rights Bill sought to gather opinions on a range of proposed employment law changes, including the creation of a new statutory entitlement to neonatal care leave and pay for eligible working parents in the North of Ireland. 	Following analysis of the consultation responses, on 28 April 2025 I announced my intention to include the introduction of an entitlement to Neonatal Care Leave and Pay, on the same basis as that available in Britain, in my forthcoming Employment Rights Bill.			More details in respect of the consultation and proposed entitlement can be found in the Consultation Response and Way Forward documents, which can be accessed via the following link: Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill Public Consultation Response | Department for the Economy	My officials are currently working with the Ofice of Legislative Counsel to draft the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill, with the intention that it will be introduced in the Assembly in early 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide an update on the ban on conversion therapy.
answer_text: Work has continued within my Department to inform policy around conversion practices. I am considering potential further actions, including options to ensure engagement with all those who have an interest.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 21749/22-27, if the Registrar General has received any update relating to changes to the Human Fertilisation and Embryology Act 2008, in order to address problems facing couples who receive IVF treatment outside the UK.
answer_text: The Registrar General continues to liaise with his counterparts in England, Wales and Scotland to ascertain if legislative change is required to the Human Fertilisation and Embryology (HFE) Act 2008 to address birth registration problems facing couples who receive IVF treatment outside the UK.  To press the matter, the Registrar General has written to the Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC) seeking a meeting. He will use this opportunity to highlight the current situation and the impact the HFE Act has on families here and to consider how the Act might be changed to address the situation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail a breakdown of Supporting People Programme funding allocations in each constituency area, for each of the last five years.

However, please see below information on funding per council area. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail what action is being taken to increase funding for organisations supported via the Supporting People programme, including First Housing Aid and Support Services, within the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: In 2024/25, I allocated a recurrent 6.4% uplift for Supporting People providers, including those operating in the Foyle constituency.  This uplift is reflected in the 2025/26 funding allocations along with an additional allocation of £1.003m for new services and the extension of current services.  Funding was allocated by Northern Ireland Housing Executive to North West Intensive Support Services for additional units to be provided this year.The Department submitted a bid to the Department of Finance at June monitoring round for £2.2m in respect of increased Employers’ National Insurance Contribution by Supporting People providers. Unfortunately, this bid was not met. The Department will continue to work closely with NIHE to manage the Supporting People budget framework.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the Urban Villages project at Meenan Square.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail current waiting times for red flag breast assessments, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Red Flag breast assessments are equivalent to urgent breast cancer referrals.  The most recent official statistics for median waiting times for urgent breast cancer referrals, by Health and Social Care Trust, are presented in Table 1 which follows.Table 1. Median waiting times for urgent breast cancer referralsat quarter end March 2025, by HSC Trust.HSC TrustMedian wait time (days) 2, 3Median wait time (weeks) 2, 3Belfast 1355.0Northern 1578.1South Eastern 1223.1Southern263.7Western101.4Source: Department of Health Hospital Waits Information Branch, Cancer Waiting Times Dataset.Notes:1 Data sourced from the encompass system. 2 The figures above refer to waiting times for urgent breast cancer referrals as at the reporting date. They do not indicate completed waiting times or expected future waiting times.3 To avoid distortion due to long waits, the Department reports on the median (the ‘middle’ waiting time) instead of mean waiting time.Due to validation process required to ensure data accuracy and completeness, there is a time lag in reporting.  Therefore, data beyond March 2025 is not yet available.  The next Cancer Waiting Time Statistics bulletin, covering the period of 1 April to 30 June 2025, is scheduled for publication on 2 October 2025.I acknowledge that the current waiting times for red flag breast cancer are lengthy and growing for adults across Northern Ireland. This situation has arisen due to several factors, including funding that falls short of objectively assessed need and previous missed opportunities to reform health and social care delivery. I am committed to addressing this, subject to available funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the steps being taken to address delays in red flag services, specifically prolonged waits for red flag breast cancer services in the Western Health and Social Care Trust area; and (ii) what modelling was carried out on the likely impact for each Health and Social Care Trust prior to the introduction of the regional waiting list model.
answer_text: (i) To address longstanding inequalities in red flag waiting times, a single regional waiting list for breast cancer assessments was introduced on 8 May 2025.  This system, ensures patients are offered the earliest available appointment, regardless of home Trust.  This change has led to improvements in waiting times in some Trusts while others have experienced delay. However, it has successfully eliminated disparities in access, helping to ensure that every patient is treated fairly and consistently across the region.  I fully acknowledge that the regional approach, while promoting fairness in access does not yet meet the 14-day target for red flag breast assessment services.  Our current focus is now on reducing overall waiting times so that every patient across Northern Ireland can be seen within the recommended timeframe. Achieving this requires a broader, coordinated effort to address the gap between demand and available capacity. To support this, £5 million in recurrent funding has been allocated through my Department’s Elective Care Framework (ECF) Implementation Plan.  My Department is working closely with Trusts to increase capacity.  In the short-term waits will be addressed using:additional waiting list initiatives clinics and increased use of independent sector capacity (ii) To support the delivery of this regional waiting list demand and capacity modelling was undertaken prior to implementation. This identified a shortfall of approximately 4 funded breast assessment clinics per week (3,900 patients), and an additional temporary operational gap of 1.3 clinics per week (1,200 patients) related to workforce pressures and expected to be resolved in early 2026.  I acknowledge that the current waiting times for red flag breast assessment patients remain lengthy and the deep concern regarding this for patients across Northern Ireland.  This situation reflects a combination of funding shortfalls of objectively assessed need and missed opportunities for system reform.   However, I want to assure you that improving access to breast cancer assessment services remains a key priority for the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 21904/22-27, for an update on the commissioning of adult ADHD services.
answer_text: There is currently no commissioned service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) in Northern Ireland. ADHD provision has evolved across Trusts in response to population demand and expertise within HSC teams. There is some provision available across the Health and Social Care system, but there are inconsistencies and lengthy waiting lists across Trusts.The Department of Health commissioned a needs assessment on ADHD in early February 2025, the findings of which will help inform options for a commissioned ADHD service across Trusts, subject to future budget availability.  The needs assessment, which covers both adults and children, will aim to determine the level of demand for a commissioned service, as well as recommendations for what such a service might look like, including potential implementation and staffing costs. While the initial estimated completion date was end June 2025, due to the desire and demand for further stakeholder engagement, information gathering and analysis required, it is likely that the work will now be completed by November 2025. Given the importance of this work in informing future ADHD service provision, it is important that sufficient time is given to ensure a robust evidence base and proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when she will answer AQW 28854/22-27.
answer_text: I answered AQW 28854/22-27 on 2 July 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans he has to (i) reinstate; or (ii) build upon, the Job Start 50+ scheme, given its potential benefits in addressing barriers to employment for older people and the ongoing impact of age-related discrimination in the labour market.
answer_text: I refer the member to the answer provided in respect of AQW 26436/22-27

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 12681/22-27, to detail (i) an update on the investigation of allocation to different areas; and (ii) a breakdown of the Centralised Temporary Accommodation spend per council area, for each of the last three years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“It has not been possible to progress with the investigation of temporary accommodation costs as outlined in AQW 12681/22-27.  This is due to sustained demands placed on staff responsible for the provision of homelessness services.To provide a comprehensive breakdown of expenditure by Local Council area for previous years would involve a manual analysis of all individual placements to identify the associated Local Council. For context, this would involve reviewing in excess of 45,000 temporary accommodation placement records.The Housing Executive recognises the usefulness of this information and has amended its information collection processes to ensure that this is captured going forward to enable future reporting.For now, the focus remains on using our limited resources to provide operational support to customers presenting as homeless and ensuring that we can, where necessary, provide interim temporary accommodation solutions.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what steps his Department is taking to address concerns around community pharmacies charging patients for the delivery of prescriptions; and (ii) whether any support or intervention is planned to assist community pharmacies or patients who may be unable to afford this provision.
answer_text: My Department funds community pharmacy to provide a range of commissioned HSC services through an agreed 3-year Commissioning Plan.Prescription delivery services are not a commissioned service in the current commissioning plan. Some, but not all, pharmacies have built this aspect into their business model and have done so for many years.Decisions as to whether to offer, withdraw or charge for such non-commissioned services are a commercial matter for individual pharmacies and therefore out with my control.The Commissioning Plan for 2025-2028 is currently being developed in partnership between Community Pharmacy Northern Ireland (CPNI) and Departmental officials. CPNI is the representative body for community pharmacy contractors in Northern Ireland.These new commissioning arrangements will progress the ambitions set out by my Department’s strategic plan for community pharmacy which outlines a vision for delivery of more clinical services that utilise the skills of pharmacy teams and offer safe, convenient and faster access to care. The vision builds on the foundations laid by the 2022-25 commissioning plan and aims to develop more patient centred community pharmacy services incorporating independent prescribing and advanced practice, to support the goals of the health and social care service and meet population health needs.Baseline funding for community pharmacies has increased significantly in the seven-year period from 2018/19 to 2025/26 by 54.7m (which equates to 52.6% increase overall). For 2025/26 the opening financial envelope has increased to £158.7m and is £11.7m (8%) higher than 2024/25.
