question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the total number of social workers employed by Older Peoples' Services in each of the last three years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) any urgent action his Department is taking to prevent the closure of the Community Crisis Intervention Service in Derry, given its role in suicide prevention; and (ii) whether he plans to replace the Mental Health Support Fund.
answer_text: The Community Crisis Intervention Service in Derry commenced with funding from the Mental Health Support Fund. The Mental Health Support Fund was a one-off, non-recurring fund for a period of three years, from April 2021 to March 2024. All monies allocated to the Mental Health Support Fund have now been exhausted.The Department remains in a significantly challenging budgetary position, with a funding gap of some £600 million, therefore, there is currently no capacity within the current budget to replace the fund.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail what steps are being taken to improve safety measures at Sacred Heart Primary School within the Foyle constituency, following multiple recent security alerts in the area.
answer_text: Following the most recent security alert at Sacred Heart Primary School, departmental officials contacted the school to explain the minor works process and advised the school to submit any required applications to address safety concerns. Officers from the Education Authority’s (EA) Maintenance Service have visited the school site and identified potential security works in the form of CCTV and external gates, which will be progressed via the Emergency Works process. EA Maintenance Officers are also reviewing a section of external fencing at the school.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether Lisfannon Park, in the Foyle constituency, will be assessed for inclusion in a residents' parking scheme.
answer_text: My Department published The Residents’ Parking Review Report on 29 November 2024, which can be viewed at https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/residents-parking-review-report. Following the publication of the report there has been a significant level of interest for the establishment of residents’ parking zones across the north. Given the level of demand, and ongoing budgetary and resource constraints, I have asked my officials to expedite the development of an application and assessment process for schemes to ensure that any requests can be appropriately considered. This process will take into account the findings and recommendations of the report and provide a streamlined approach for considering, assessing and prioritising requests for residents parking schemes going forward.It is my intention to make an announcement as to how future schemes will be assessed by my Department over the coming months.   In the meantime, my Department will continue to develop the schemes already commenced such as in the Bogside in Derry and Iveagh area of Belfast.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the the number of patients currently in hospital who are medically fit for discharge but are awaiting placement in a care home or other suitable care setting; and (ii) the average length of time these patients are waiting for an appropriate placement, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The following is a snapshot on the 21 August 2025TrustNumber Medically Fit for discharge but awaiting placement in a care home or other suitable care settingAvg. Length of time waiting for an appropriate. Placement (Days)BHSCT6837NHSCT4422SEHSCT2416SHSCT23SHSCT were not able to provide data on averages but rather days delayed range.0-62WHSCT8357It is important to note that delayed discharge data is fluid and changes from day to day and throughout the day.  In reviewing the above data, it is important to note that there is a variance in care home bed availability throughout Northern Ireland.  In addition, there is variance in the category of care home beds available regionally.  The above data is in relation to acute hospital setting delays, this may differ from their usual Trust of residence.In recognition of the impact of care home capacity on hospital flow, the Department of Health is leading on the reform of adult social care under the auspices of the Social Care Collaborative Forum.  The Maximising Capacity Workstream is working to make the most effective use of the available regional care home resource with the introduction of the Trusted Assessor role which allows for assessment for care home suitability to be done 7 days a week and therefore not delaying discharge at the weekend for example.  Also, work is currently underway on a more efficient care home app, to ensure all available capacity in care homes is recorded on the app which is available to professionals and which ensures individuals can be matched with the appropriate placement and hospital discharge can be expedited.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans he has to improve the provision of wheelchair accessible taxis.
answer_text: I acknowledge that there has been a decrease in the availability of wheelchair accessible taxis. Under the current taxi legislative framework in the North, there is no requirement for taxi operators to have a minimum number of wheelchair accessible vehicles in their fleet and the option to include these types of vehicles is at the discretion of the operators and drivers who are directly responsible for complying with the equality legislation. I recently announced a phased review of Taxi Policy and Legislation and in the second phase of this review, I have asked officials to consider options for improving access to wheelchair accessible taxis.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the steps taken to support individuals experiencing homelessness in Derry, particularly those with addiction issues, including those placed in emergency accommodation from outside the area, who have subsequently been excluded from Housing Executive support due to behaviour or other circumstances.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive has a statutory duty to provide temporary accommodation to homeless applicants under the Housing (NI) Order 1988 Order, and Applicants’ support needs, including addiction issues, are taken into account when determining the suitability of temporary accommodation placements.The Housing Executive will work with Applicants to provide additional necessary support from other agencies which can involve referrals to floating support funded by the Housing Executive via the Supporting People programme.The Housing Executive does not have a duty to provide temporary accommodation to ineligible persons. Individuals who are ineligible for assistance, for example due to unacceptable behaviour, or those otherwise not owed the full housing duty, can avail of advice and assistance from the Housing Executive. This can include information regarding self-referral hostels and referrals to floating support services as outlined above.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the action her Department is taking to ensure fair and equitable pay for all NI Water staff, including non-frontline staff, following the industrial action vote by NIPSA members in response to the ongoing pay disparity for the 2023-24 period.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for NI Water, and it is for NI Water to negotiate pay offers with its appointed Unions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) how many Housing Associations offer a voluntary House Sales Scheme for their tenants; and (ii) how many social homes have been sold per year by Housing Associations, since the statutory scheme closed for tenants in August 2022.
answer_text: The Housing Association House Sales Scheme closed in August 2022. The Housing (Amendment) Act (NI) 2020 did contain provision for registered Housing Associations to bring forward their own voluntary house sales schemes.  To date, no Housing Association has made DfC aware of any schemes they intend to operate and neither has any funding been designated by DfC for any such schemes.The numbers of remaining sales, under the House Sales Scheme from 2022 until present are detailed below:YEARHOUSE SALES BY HOUSING ASSOCIATIONS2021/20221602022/20232522023/20241252024/2025Not yet available

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the planned renovation project at the Triangle Estate, Waterside in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:‘The Housing Executive can advise that the works carried out by our response maintenance contractor have now been completed. This included balcony walkway repairs, parapet wall repairs, underside concrete repairs, roof repairs, gutter repairs, and rain penetration repairs. We are pleased to confirm that the issues of water ingress now appear to have been resolved, and these works are covered by guarantees. In relation to the improvement scheme that has been approved for the complex, we are continuing to review our position. Challenges remain around decanting and leaseholder buybacks, which are key considerations in the implementation of the scheme.Since our previous update, we had tasked our consultant with reviewing our original proposals in light of the completed response maintenance works. As a result, further options have now been proposed.Senior representatives from the Housing Executive are scheduled to meet on 3rd July 2025 to further deliberate these proposals. This meeting will consider the challenges faced and aim to determine the most advantageous way forward.’

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the specific environmental and carbon reduction requirements that were included in the procurement process for the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme, including expectations around (a) construction materials; (b) vehicles used; and (c) low-carbon technologies; and (ii) the information on these elements which were submitted as part of the evidence presented to the courts.
answer_text: The contracts for the design, development and potential construction of the A5WTC were awarded in 2009 and set up as a 2 Phase contract.  The requirements would have been in line with the DoF Procurement Policy and Guidance current at the time.  Throughout the course of the project, the environmental and carbon assessments were updated and formally documented within the Environmental Statement.  A carbon management process was a requirement within the proposed construction contract conditions, along with the requirement for the contractors to actively manage and optimise whole life carbon in accordance with PAS 2080:2023 Carbon Management in Buildings and Infrastructure.  This means that GHG emissions would be assessed at key stages throughout the project, and mitigation measures identified and considered to reduce the GHG emissions associated with the proposed scheme where possible. A summary of this process is set out in section 5.3.16 of the 2024 Departmental Statement.Measures include the need for contractors to consider the use of low carbon materials, low carbon fuels, electric vehicles, energy efficiency measures for plant (idle-reduction policies and more efficient machinery), increased planting, and use of electricity and renewable generation on site compounds. In addition to the carbon management requirements set by the Department, the proposed contractors have also provided their own corporate commitments and policies around sustainability and carbon management.All of this information was made available to the court. Link to Environmental Statement can be found at https://www.a5wtc.com/pages/environmental-statement Link to Departmental Statement can be found at https://www.a5wtc.com/pages/departmental-statements

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 27823/22-27, to detail how much of the £712,851 was spent solely on speakers.
answer_text: The public address system was procured by Translink’s main contractor for the project as a whole system and the cost for the speakers is therefore not known.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 26204/22-27, to detail the cost of these pothole repairs on the Strand Road, for each of the last five years.
answer_text: The repairs on the Strand Road were carried out by my Department’s internal Operations and Maintenance contractor who do not record the costs of individual repairs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she has any plans to engage with Ulster University, Derry, regarding its ongoing expansion and the need to address the associated infrastructure needs, including (i) residents’ parking schemes; (ii) road improvements; and (iii) access to public transport.
answer_text: On the 29 November 2024, my predecessor Minister O’Dowd committed that the Department would give consideration to a residents parking scheme in the vicinity of the Ulster University campus at Magee.  Earlier this year, at a specially convened meeting of the university community engagement forum, officials engaged with stakeholders to outline how a Residents’ Parking scheme may operate.  Development of this potential scheme is ongoing.At an operational level, my officials continue to engage with the University on road improvements through the taskforce community engagement forum and as a consultee in the planning process.  Officials also attended the Magee Expansion Infrastructure and Transport Sub Group Initial meeting on the 30th June.  Translink are also represented on Ulster University Travel and Transport Working Group who meet quarterly to promote and develop sustainable transport links.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the total number of dementia care home beds available; and (ii) the number of these currently vacant or available,  broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: (i). The Department of Health contacted RQIA as they hold a register for care home beds in Northern Ireland.  If a care home is registered solely for the care category dementia, then all of the care home’s beds are for dementia patients. However, the majority of homes are registered for dementia and other categories of care. This is to ensure that when a resident is being admitted to a Care Home that the home have the skills and ability to meet the needs of the resident. In some cases, the care home will have a condition of registration stating how many beds they have for dementia, but this will not always be the case. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether (i) glyphosate-based herbicides are currently being used by her Department, or its contractors, for weed control on public roads and verges; and (ii) consideration is being given to phasing out such products in favour of more environmentally sustainable alternatives.
answer_text: My Department uses external contractors to carry out weed control on hard surfaces for the control of weeds to ensure road safety, to prevent structural damage to infrastructure assets, to aid drainage and to fulfil legislative requirements on the control of noxious weeds and invasive species. The herbicide products suitable for use must be in accordance with the Plant Protection Products Regulations (NI) 2011 and the Sustainable Use Regulations 2012. My Department is exploring opportunities to phase out of the use of glyphosate-based herbicides for weed control on the road network and verges through addressing ongoing actions in the All-Ireland Pollinator Plan. Officials are taking this forward with their counterparts in DAERA aligning with the technical guidelines for maintaining roadsides for pollinators and participating in the Pollinator Monitoring Scheme.Alternative environmentally sustainable treatment options like the use of mechanical weed control, biological controls and organic mulches amongst others are reviewed on an ongoing basis as part of my Department’s drive for continuous improvement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 17732/22-27, for an update on (i) the implementation of National Institute for Health and Care Excellence TA 943; and (ii) the integration of advanced continuous glucose monitoring technologies.
answer_text: Following the endorsement of the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence TA943 as applicable to Northern Ireland, my Department has requested work to be undertaken to understand the implications.   Overall provision; priority groups; system costs; workforce and communication requirements, to both enable and support, are being considered. Funding is required for devices in addition to upskilling and recruiting the workforce needed to offer Hybrid Closed Loop safely, effectively and equitably across the region.  I have recently supported the establishment of a clinical working group to work with relevant stakeholders across Health and Social Care to develop an implementation plan which outlines an appropriate timescale for rollout. This plan should provide equitable, sustainable and affordable access to this technology, which is targeted to optimise outcomes for those groups most likely to benefit.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant AQW 15942/22-27, for an update on extending the discount period for Penalty Charge Notices.
answer_text: All recipients of a Penalty Charge Notice (PCN) have the right of challenge. Should a recipient wish to make a challenge within 14 days of its issue, the 50% discount period will not expire while the challenge is being considered.Due to other critical priorities within my Department, work in relation to considering the extension of the discount period has not yet been progressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of care homes that are equipped to accommodate residents with vascular dementia in each of the last three years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) what steps are being taken to improve access to specialist care for individuals living with this condition.
answer_text: (i)	Care home beds for dementia are categorised with RQIA as DE for Dementia as part of the registrations process. As such there is no specific data held in relation to dementia sub-types such as vascular dementia.  If a care home is registered solely for the care category dementia, then all of the care home’s beds are for dementia patients. However, the majority of homes are registered for dementia and other categories of care. This is to ensure that when a resident is being admitted to a Care Home that the home have the skills and ability to meet the needs of the resident. Based on the above caveats, RQIA are able to provide this banding of minimum and maximum numbers of care home beds for people with a dementia as per the tables below.Registered Care Home Places/Beds as at 30.09.2024 Service TypeDementia Beds inHomes Registeredfor Category DEOnly*Dementia Beds in HomesRegistered for Category DEand Other Categories**Total Identified Dementia BedsNursing (NH)5591,7992,358Residential (RC)9645411,505Grand Total1,5232,3403,863*Maximum approved places in homes registered solely for dementia**Confirmed dementia places in homes registered for DE and other categories of careService TypeBeds in Homes Registered forCategory DE and OtherCategories with no breakdownfor Dementia***Nursing (NH)633Residential (RC)1,108Grand Total1,741***Maximum approved places in homes registered for dementia and other care categories but where there is no breakdown in numbers between categoriesService TypeMinimumNumber ofDementiaBedsMaximumNumber ofDementiaBeds****Total Registered BedsMin % DEMax % DENursing (NH)2,3582,99110,57022%28%Residential (RC)1,5052,6135,26429%50%Grand Total3,8635,60415,83424%35%****Combined total for confirmed dementia places and places in homes which have category DE but no confirmed number of places under this category(ii)	The Western Health and Social Care Trust have established a Community Discharge Hub that facilitates discharges to Care Homes and has employed a Care Home Liaison Nurse as part of the Hub to facilitate appropriate placements for those whose needs are Dementia Nursing or Dementia Residential.The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust has introduced a Pilot for a Dementia Behaviour Outreach Service in the Lisburn area. The aim of this service is to offer care and management of the behavioural and psychological symptoms of dementia (BPSD) for patients within their own home setting avoiding unnecessary admission to hospital. This MDT team allows rapid access to specialist services for patients pre and post diagnosis as well as offering education and training to care staff in the appropriate management of dementia in the care home setting. Following the evaluation of the pilot the Trust is progressing a Business Case to build upon and expand this service Trust wide.The Northern Health & Social Care Trust has developed a Dementia Home Support Team (DHST) which is a multidisciplinary team that provides behavioural assessment and

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the average pupil absenteeism rate for (a) primary and (b) post-primary pupils; and (ii) what percentage of these absences were categorised as chronic absenteeism, in each local council area, in the last three years.
answer_text: (i) Pupils’ attendance at school is recorded twice a day and recorded as half day sessions. The table show shows the percentage of all half day sessions recorded as absent in primary and post primary schools. Chronic absence has been defined as 10% or more of a pupil’s total half day sessions recorded as absent by primary and post primary schools. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline (i) the rationale behind the requirement for individuals making representations on licence applications for Houses in Multiple Occupation to provide their name and address; and (ii) whether there are any plans to review this requirement, in light of concerns about potential intimidation.
answer_text: Individuals making representations on licence applications for Houses in Multiple Occupation are required to provide their name and address in order to verify that the representations are genuinely from individuals affected by the application for a licence and are not frivolous or vexatious. This also allows the council to contact individuals to acknowledge the representation and to request further information if necessary. There are no plans to review this requirement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 12416/22-27, for an update on the Education Information Solutions programme; and whether data on the number of pupils on long-term absence, who are experiencing mental health problems, is now available.
answer_text: Attendance codes as required by DE Policy will be implemented in the existing and future School Management Systems (SIMS and Bromcom), this includes the introduction of additional codes for Emotionally Based School Non-Attendance and Temporary Exceptions to the Curriculum with development scheduled for Summer 2025.  From the start of the new academic year commencing in September, schools will have the ability to record these absence codes.  Development is ongoing and scheduled to complete in advance of September to ensure that changes to attendance codes are also updated for centralised attendance reporting to DE.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the (i) total amount; and (ii) percentage of the annual transport budget allocated to active travel, in each of the last two financial years.
answer_text: The overall active travel spend is made up of Transport Road Asset Management (TRAM) active travel spend and public transport active travel spend. TRAM spend includes dedicated active travel projects, maintenance and reconstruction of existing footways and cycleways, traffic signal pedestrian crossings, minor pedestrian enhancements, 50% of street lighting costs, and staff costs. In previous years my department has largely only reported dedicated active travel projects.The overall transport budget in each of 2023/24 and 2024/25 was approximately £0.9 billion. The active travel spend for each of the financial years was in the region of £50m and between 5-6% of the transport budget. Figures for 2024/25 are provisional and subject to change. Expenditure reflects final budget allocations.  Expenditure is determined by budget availability and capacity in any given year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in relation to the Inner Walled City Public Realm Scheme, whether he will consider providing residents with permits for free or reduced-rate parking at Bishop Street car park.
answer_text: My Department does not have the vires to issue car parking permits.  Local councils have responsibility for managing off-street parking in Northern Ireland, while on-street parking is the responsibility of the Department for Infrastructure (DfI).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for her assessment of how road defects, including potholes impact road safety, particularly for cyclists; and (ii) how these issues impact the roll-out of active travel initiatives.
answer_text: My Department places a high importance on the need to adequately maintain the road network.Routine maintenance activities such as carriageway, footway and cycleway defect repair, grass cutting and weed control, gully emptying and maintenance of street lighting and traffic signals. all contribute to road safety, including for cyclists, and have been affected by underfunding over many years.Whilst the overall budgetary position remains challenging, and difficult decisions need to be made around prioritisation of investment, I remain committed to increasing overall investment in active travel by developing improved active travel networks and upgrading and maintaining existing assets so that they remain safe and attractive for all road users.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will consider prioritising the Duncreggan Road and the surrounding areas within the Foyle constituency, for a residents' parking scheme, given the planned expansion of Ulster University.
answer_text: My officials are currently working with key stakeholders to identify the potential areas that may benefit from a residents' parking scheme near the vicinity of Ulster University Magee campus.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will consider including special provisions for key workers, such as healthcare staff, in the allocation of permits under upcoming residents' parking schemes.
answer_text: My Department currently provides several ways to accommodate the parking needs of health carers in a residents' parking zone. These include the use of a health carers’ permit which may be issued to the local Health and Social Care Trust, where the Trust applies for permits on behalf of staff, using a visitors' permit obtained from a resident or through a paid parking event within a mixed use scheme. In addition, a permit shall not be required for the vehicles of medical practitioners actively engaged in the pursuance of statutory powers or duties. This approach has worked well in the Rugby Road/College Park Avenue scheme in Belfast and resultantly, my Department currently has no plans to introduce any additional provisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the amount of funding that has been allocated in the 2025-26 financial year, specifically for residents’ parking schemes; and (ii) the number of potential schemes supported.
answer_text: Whilst I have not taken any decisions on the 2025-26 Budget, I remain committed to continuing to develop the residents’ parking schemes already commenced such as in the Bogside in Derry and Iveagh area of Belfast and will consider schemes to address the emerging pressures with on-street parking in the vicinity of the new Ulster University campus in Belfast and the Magee campus.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has considered extending double yellow lines along the Student Village side of Duncreggan Road, Foyle, to address persistent parking issues.
answer_text: My officials are actively engaged with local stakeholders regarding traffic management and parking issues in the Duncreggan Road area of the city as part of the Ulster University Magee Campus expansion.  The provision of parking restrictions will be considered within this wider context.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide a timescale for the Traffic Calming Scheme, Temple Road, Strathfoyle , including both the commencement and indicative completion dates.
answer_text: The traffic calming scheme for Temple Road, Strathfoyle, is well advanced and will be considered for inclusion in the Local Transport and Safety Measures for the Derry City and Strabane Council area programme.  However, at this stage it is not possible to provide an indication of a potential commencement date until budget allocations have been confirmed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to clarify which forms of identification are accepted by the online Universal Credit journal system for migrating claimants.
answer_text: The provision of information as proof of identity is part of the process of making a claim to Universal Credit and not part of ongoing engagement through a claimant’s journal.   Claimants will be asked to verify their identity as part of their online claim using 2 forms of the following information, a valid UK passport, a UK driving licence, recent self-assessment tax returns, credit references or records e.g. information about credit cards or phone contracts. Where online verification has not been possible a range of other forms of identity information will be offered by Universal Credit telephony agents and in the Jobs and Benefits offices to help claimants verify their identity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the total number of gullies cleaned; and (ii) the number that could not be cleaned due to obstructions, such as parked cars, in each of the last three years, within the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: My Department does not hold gully information which specifically relates to the Foyle constituency area.  However, figures are available for the Cityside and Waterside areas and is summarised in the below tables for each of the last three years.Cityside Urban202220232024AprilOctoberJulyMayNovemberCleaned85668483838675858318Not Cleaned17751872196928302097Waterside Urban202220232024JanuaryJuneAprilNovemberAugustCleaned1088811233106261039310257Not Cleaned1311966157318061942The Department does not retain information in relation to the number of gullies that could not be cleaned due to obstructions, such as parked cars.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the total number of private driving tests conducted; and (ii) the number of driving test examiners employed at each test centre, in the last three years.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) publish Official National Statistics every quarter.  The most recent publication for the period from 1 January to 31 March 2025 can be accessed through the following link, DfI Driver, Vehicle, Operator, and Enforcement Statistics - 2024-25 - Quarter Four | Department for Infrastructure.  The total number of private car driving tests conducted in each of the last three years can be found at Section 4, Table 4.3 of this publication.The DVA has an obligation to comply with the code of practice for official statistics and other data protection legislation, therefore, only regional figures will be provided in respect of driving examiners and dual role examiners.  This is to protect the identity of individual driving examiners working within each testing location, given the small numbers at each site. The DVA has confirmed that they currently have 88 driving examiners across their network of test centres.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of private driving tests carried out over weekends, at each test centre, over the last 12 months.
answer_text: The table below shows the number of Category B (private car) driving tests conducted over weekends, at each test centre, for the period from 1 June 2024 to 31 May 2025. During this period 36 candidates failed to attend a booked appointment.Test centreTests conducted at weekendsAltnagelvin*49Armagh26Ballymena60Balmoral*362Coleraine217Craigavon*234Downpatrick226Enniskillen11Hydebank84Larne243Lisburn119Newry*175Newtownards*358Omagh19Grand Total2183*Test centres that offered Sunday appointmentsDriving test appointments are only offered on Sundays at certain test centres, where it is suitable to do so without compromising the integrity of the test.  As the average waiting time for a Category B driving test is now around 29 days, the DVA is not currently offering driving tests on Sundays as there is no demand for them.  This position will be kept under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for a breakdown of the number of young people not in education, employment or training, in each of the last five years, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: Estimates of the number of young people aged 16-24 not in education, employment or training (NEET) are sourced from the Labour Force Survey (LFS).  Figure 1 below shows the number of NEET people covering the period 2019 to 2023. The number of respondents to the survey for the NEET group is too small to allow a breakdown by Local Government District (LGD) for each year.Figure 1:  Number of NEET people in NI 2019-2023Number of NEET people in NI201923,000202022,000202121,000202217,000202313,000

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i)  the number of individuals migrating to Universal Credit that have incurred an overpayment in the last five years; and (ii) what steps are being taken to prevent such overpayments in the future.
answer_text: In the 5 years ending 17th June 2025, 14,796 claimants that moved as part of the move to Universal Credit exercise had an overpayment raised.The approach adopted by my Department to help prevent overpayments includes educating claimants to report changes to their circumstances as soon as they occur, further development of the Universal Credit computer system and its integration with other government systems and an ongoing programme of prevention and detection activities to address fraud and error within Universal Credit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any consideration he is giving to extending free school meal and uniform grant eligibility to include all families in receipt of Universal Credit, in light of the recent policy change in England.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer to AQW 28407/22-27, which has been published in the weekly answer booklet for week ending 20th June 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will introduce a strategy for Myalgic Encephalomyelitis and Chronic Fatigue Syndrome.
answer_text: The Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC) interim delivery plan on Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome (ME/CFS) in England and associated public consultation document were both published on 9th August 2023.  My officials engaged with colleagues in the DHSC throughout the development of the interim delivery plan. My officials have reviewed the consultation responses provided by Northern Ireland residents to understand the experiences of people with ME/CFS here. My officials continue to engage with colleagues in the DHSC regarding the development of a final delivery plan which I have been advised is planned for publication in the coming weeks.Following publication of the final delivery plan my officials will consider, in the context of local services and available resources, whether to develop a specific local cross departmental ME/CFS action plan.  It is important to recognise that the timing in terms of developing any such plan will depend on available resources.To improve access to services for people with ME/CFS, my Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group, in collaboration with the Public Health Agency, is actively exploring the potential of including new ME/CFS referrals within the new Long Covid service model. It is anticipated that the new service will be implemented in Autumn 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) to outline the amount of funding that will be allocated in the 2025-26 financial year, to support the review of post-school (a) education; (b) skills; and (c) training provision for individuals with special educational needs; and (ii) to clarify what educational or support pathways are being made available for individuals who lack the capacity to engage in further education-based provision.
answer_text: In the current year, my Departmental review team is funded through an allocation of approximately £320k from our Skills Fund. The current disability support across our existing programmes and provision will continue as in previous years, while funding bids will be submitted as required for the commitments in the reform programme.	Our review has found that leavers from Special Schools and Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools typically progress onto one of two pathways:  	A Department for the Economy-funded pathway involving Further Education or Training provision, orA Department of Health-funded pathway such as day opportunities or day care  My proposals to enhance learner support through the statutory Assessment and Support Model in Further Education, as well as expanding and improving the provision, are intended to enable more young people with Special Educational Needs to choose our Colleges for their next step.  Our Skills for Life and Work programme is already a popular option for young people with Special Educational Needs, with more than half of the participants having disability related support needs.  The current Apprenticeship Inclusion Challenge Fund Pilots will test approaches to improve participation for people with disabilities and we also intend to develop and pilot a new model for apprentices with Special Educational Needs.  However, there are also many young people and their families who consider that the pathways funded by the Department of Health, particularly day opportunities, are a more appropriate fit for them.  As I indicated in my Statement, there is a need for significant investment in both the Economy and Health funded pathways.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps which are being taken to improve the (i) availability; and (ii) accessibility, of temporary accommodation for applicants who are wheelchair users or have limited mobility.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive will seek to secure suitable options within the existing portfolio of temporary accommodation for anyone presenting with disabilities and / or mobility issues, including wheelchair users.Where suitable options are not readily available within voluntary and community sector hostels, the Housing Executive will seek bespoke arrangements such as wheelchair accessible single let properties.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline whether the Barnett Consequentials arising from the UK Government’s £13.2 billion Warm Homes Plan will be ringfenced to tackle fuel poverty.
answer_text: ANSWERBarnett consequentials provided at a Spending Review are identified at Whitehall departmental level rather than individual programme level.  Therefore, it is not possible to specify how much Barnett this announcement has provided the Executive.As a former Minister you will also be aware, Barnett consequentials are unhypothecated.  As such, it is a matter for the Executive to agree how this funding will be allocated considering all competing priorities.

answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that it does not hold the information requested broken down by constituency. The EA has provided the information below detailing the costs for taxi and bus transport for each of the last five years. These figures are not yet available for 2024/25. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any measures which are in place to ensure efficient use of resources relating to taxi and bus provision for transport to school, particularly in cases where pupils from the same street are being transported separately.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the operation and delivery of home to school transport, including route planning. It always aims to maximise vehicle occupancy across all modes of transport to ensure that school transport services operate as efficiently and economically as possible. Different modes of transport are required due to the varying needs of pupils, the level of demand and the geographical spread of pupils eligible for transport assistance. Where private bus and taxi services are required, they are contracted through a public procurement process to operate a specific route for a defined period and these routes are reviewed to ensure they remain appropriate, especially as new pupils can become eligible for assistance throughout the year. While it may appear that resources are duplicated in certain streets or areas, this can be necessary for a variety of reasons.  These include meeting the individual needs of pupils who must travel alone, vehicle capacity constraints and managing the number of pupil escorts required and accommodating varying school start and finishing times or the need to follow specific routes tailored to pupils’ needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of faults recorded; and (ii) the costs for repair to the traffic signals at Bradleys Pass in the Foyle constituency, since their installation.
answer_text: My Department’s signal fault database indicates that there have been 18 faults recorded at Bradleys Pass since 2018.The database does not record the cost of individual signal repairs in a readily accessible format and any attempt to do so would require an extensive exercise to be conducted across the Department which might only be expected to produce data with a limited degree of exactitude, and this exercise itself would very likely involve disproportionate costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how the £100 million Reinvestment and Reform Initiative Borrowing for social housing outlined in the Programme for Government earmarked allocations is allocated within the Department for Communities Budget 2025-26.
answer_text: My Department has directed £168.5m of its 2025-26 capital budget to the delivery of new social homes in line with the Programme for Government. This includes £100m of Reinvestment and Reform Initiative funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will consider introducing legislation to regulate private parking firms operating in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Whilst my Department is responsible for on-street parking enforcement across the north, it has no responsibility for the operation and management of private car parks nor the enforcement companies that operate within them.There is no legislation which specifically applies to privately owned car parks and my Department currently has no plans to introduce legislation in this area.However, any organisation that operates parking on private land and requests keeper details from the DVLA must be approved by an Accredited Trade Association such as the Approved Operator Scheme managed by the British Parking Association. Members of this scheme must adhere to the BPA AOS Code of Practice for the control and enforcement of parking on private land and unregulated public car parks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline (i) when the traffic signals at (a) Bradleys Pass; and (b) the Buncrana Road and Springtown Road intersection, in the Foyle constituency, were installed; and (ii) whether the traffic signals were both installed by the same contractor.
answer_text: The signals at the Buncrana Road / Springtown Road junction were installed in July 2021 while those at Bradleys Pass were installed in September 2015.The records associated with the installation of the traffic signals have been lawfully destroyed through application of the Department's Retention and Disposal Schedule, made under the Public Records Act (NI) 1923 and Disposal of Documents Order 1925.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any consideration he is giving to introducing a dedicated outreach programme for pension-age individuals, to ensure they are accessing their benefit entitlements.
answer_text: My Department’s Make the Call Service (MtC) offers free and confidential needs assessments for individuals to check for potential entitlements to social security benefits, additional support and services. The MtC Community Outreach team also conducts home visits, providing face to face assistance and support with the completion of benefit claim forms. MtC also has an extensive range of partnerships with community-based organisations and other government departments which extends its reach and accessibility. Community-based Outreach Officers work with local community and voluntary groups to raise awareness of potential benefit entitlements and provide practical help in accessing them. MtC services are available for all citizens, including those of pension age.  The Pension Centre undertakes a range of activities to promote uptake of Pension Credit among pension-age individuals in conjunction with MtC,  including regular social media campaigns; distribution of information leaflets to libraries and GP surgeries; collaboration with other statutory bodies such as the Northern Ireland Housing Executive and Northern Ireland Health and Social Care Trusts; and discussing Pension credit entitlement when a person makes their State Pension claim. The Pension Centre continues to seek opportunities to encourage Pension Credit applications by way of regular engagement with community and voluntary stakeholder groups to help raise awareness and encourage Pension Credit uptake. The Pension Centre is also currently planning an advertising campaign which will focus on radio and press channels which is due to launch later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the total amount of underclaimed pension benefits, for each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department does not hold the information requested.  For Pension Credit, the most recently published experimental statistics were released in September 2022 and provided estimates for the 2019/20 financial year. The analysis estimated that there was £1.1m per week in unclaimed Pension Credit in 2019/20. My Department is not in a position to update these statistics at present due to data constraints relating to State Pension data. The Department is working closely with the Department for Work and Pensions to resolve these issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the restructuring of red flag breast services; and (ii) whether it is delivering improved outcomes for patients in the Western health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: (i) In response to regional disparities and increasing waiting times for red flag breast services, my department has implemented a regional waiting list for breast cancer assessment.  This initiative is part of a broader review of Breast Services.  This regional approach became operational on 8 May 2025, and aims to ensure patients have access to the earliest available appointment across all Trusts. This system ensures that all patients referred for breast assessment across the region experience a similar wait. Most individuals will still be seen at their nearest hospital, where earlier appointments are available, patients may be offered an appointment at any of the acute hospitals.  Following assessment, patients are be referred back to their home Trust for treatment if required. (ii)  At this stage it is too soon to obtain data to determine if restructuring is delivering improved outcomes for patients in the Western Health & Social Care Trust.  My department will continue to monitor and evaluate the impact of these changes

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline what security and safeguarding measures are in place for Housing Executive staff, particularly frontline staff dealing with homelessness presentations.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive provides a range of safety and security measures for our people, including those providing frontline services. Our approach is risk based and includes the use of dynamic risk assessments as appropriate.Physical security measures are in place across our estate including access control, security personnel on site, provision of secure interview rooms, alarms to seek assistance, CCTV etc.In terms of personal safety, lone workers are provided with SOS Fobs and receive personal safety training.  All staff are required to report any incidents of abusive behaviour, to enable investigation and actions to prevent further similar incidents.We have a number of related organisational policies in place including lone working, the prevention and management of abusive behaviour, and we are currently recruiting a new post for Safety and Security Manager. All staff are required to complete safeguarding training, while our frontline staff also receive an additional tailored course.  In addition to internal support, colleagues negatively impacted by abusive behaviour, can also avail of confidential, external, and independent support.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether individuals can submit compensation claims for damage caused by potholes that have been spray-painted or marked for repair; or (ii) whether such markings remove liability before repairs are carried out.
answer_text: An individual may make a claim for compensation for damage to their vehicle regardless of whether a defect is spray-painted or marked out for repair. Defects which are deemed to be actionable under my Department’s Road Maintenance Standards for Safety are marked out or spray-painted by highway inspectors, where it is safe for the inspector to do so. The purpose of the marking is to highlight the location of the required repair to our internal or external contractor. If it is not safe to do so due to traffic volumes etc, the inspector is only required to provide clear instructions to facilitate the identification of the precise location of the defect requiring repair.Each claim made against my Department is investigated on its own merits. If the investigation confirms my Department is not legally liable for the damage being claimed, the case will be rejected.  Legal liability is ultimately decided by the courts, however, the courts’ consideration of a claim will include whether my Department’s Road Maintenance Standards for Safety were adhered to. Although the marking out or spray-painting of a defect doesn’t remove liability for a roads related public liability claim, my officials will consider, inter alia, whether the associated repair response time was met.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the estimated remediation and clean-up costs for the Mobuoy site in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Long term remediation at the Mobuoy has not commenced. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will consider supporting additional breast clinic capacity, previously provided by the Western Health and Social Care Trust to support other regions.
To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 26807/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 26807/22-27 was answered on 24 June 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the average waiting time for red flag breast assessments; and (ii) the number of patients on a red flag waiting list in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 19771/22-27, for an update on the early access programme for Givinostat for boys with Duchenne muscular dystrophy.
answer_text: Compassionate use schemes such as Early Access Programmes (EAP) permit health providers to make new licensed treatments available to patients free of charge while they are being assessed for clinical and cost-effectiveness by the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE).  They do not however provide any additional resources which are often required to safely administer the drug and monitor patients, as is the case with givinostat. Whilst it is an operational decision for health trusts to participate in an EAP and to identify the additional resources required prior to a recommendation by NICE, all our Health and Social Care Trusts are constrained by the severe budgetary pressures currently facing the Health and Social Care (HSC) system and the urgent need to reduce costs.  It is my understanding that in light of this it has not been possible for the Belfast Trust to identify the additional funding that would be required to support the EAP for givinostat. I am however acutely aware of the time sensitive need to access givinostat promptly, and so I have requested that the Belfast Trust put plans in place to enable implementation to proceed at pace in the event of a positive recommendation by NICE this Autumn.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) when each planned scheme for properties in Westway, in the Foyle constituency, was initially recorded by the Housing Executive; (ii) the reasons for delays; and (iii) why other schemes have been prioritised ahead of them.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 28350/22-27, the information has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any steps being taken to address the misuse of social housing, including by tenants of both the Housing Executive and housing associations; and (ii) any efforts in place to ensure that allocated properties are being occupied as intended.
answer_text: With regards Housing Executive properties this issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive of the Housing Executive, who has advised me as follows.“The Housing Executive is committed to addressing all misuse of its properties and does this through a range of measures designed to prevent, detect and address fraudulent activity and misuse.  All Housing Executive tenants are bound by their General Conditions of Tenancy, and the organisation takes steps to address any known breaches of these tenancy obligations in line with relevant legislative powers and provisions.  In addition, the Housing Executive have a Tenancy Fraud/Misuse Action Plan, this was developed to ensure an effective response to tenancy fraud and actions were designed to reduce the risk of tenancy fraud and help ensure that best use is made of social housing stock.”All Registered Housing Associations have individual Tenancy Fraud policies agreed by their Boards, to ensure that all tenancy agreements are adhered to. My Department is currently undertaking a review of tenancy fraud procedures and processes, which will enable further measures to address the misuse of social housing. These measures may include new legislation and policies, and awareness-raising activities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 14515/22-27, for an update on (i) the number of homes identified as affected by defective blocks, broken down by local council area; and (ii) whether the Housing Executive is currently investigating any of its properties suspected to be impacted by defective blocks.
answer_text: stated in my response to AWQ 14515/22-27, seven MICA-affected houses (one private, six social) have been identified, all within Derry City and Strabane District Council.	The Housing Executive regularly surveys its stock and currently has no properties suspected of MICA related defects under investigation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there has been any underspend in the road infrastructure budget for the last three financial years.
answer_text: My Department has fully spent all of the budget allocations made to road infrastructure for each of the past three financial years with the exception of a reported underspend of £16.2m in 2024-25 which related primarily to the A5 capital project scheme due to a legal challenge. Also, in relation to ring-fenced non-cash resource budgets in respect of accounting estimates for depreciation, my Department reported an underspend of c£4.2m in 2024-25 which related primarily to roads related depreciation. This is a non-cash underspend and as such did not affect spending capacity for the delivery of essential road maintenance services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what efforts are being made to improve access to social worker services in the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Western Health and Social Care Trust has been engaged in a range of activities designed to stabilise their social work workforce and improve access to services. In June 2023, all Trusts ceased the use of social workers employed by recruitment agencies and began the mass recruitment of newly qualified social workers into vacant posts. In 2023 and 2024 this resulted in an additional 128 social workers being employed permanently in the Western Trust. The 2025 recruitment process is currently underway and it is anticipated that a further 52 vacancies will be filled in the coming months. These additional staff have significantly increased the Trust’s capacity to plan and deliver more effective social work services. The Western Trust is also undertaking a range of actions designed to support and retain social workers in their roles, which will contribute to the long-term stabilisation of the social work workforce and improve access to the services they provide.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of plans by NHS England to roll-out specialist mental health crisis centres to reduce crowding in accident and emergency departments.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the average compensation paid for damage to a vehicle; and (ii) the average waiting time for payment, for each of the last five years.
answer_text: The average compensation paid for damage to a vehicle for each of the last full five financial years is detailed in the table.  Compensation paid in respect of vehicle damage will include claims that were dealt with internally by my Department, claims where legal proceedings were issued but settled before court, and claims where a court award was made.Financial yearAverage compensation2024/2025£254.682023/2024£278.432022/2023£261.452021/2022£240.492020/2021£313.81The average time between receipt of a vehicle damage claim and compensation being paid for each of the last full five financial years is detailed in the table below. In addition, once formal legal proceedings issue on a claim, timescales are outside of my Department’s control and are determined by the courts.Financial yearAverage time (calendar days)2024/2025912023/2024832022/20231092021/20221262020/2021143

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail what additional welfare mitigations he intends to introduce within this mandate.
answer_text: The updated estimated costs for each of the recommendations from the Independent Advisory Panel Report on future welfare mitigations were shared with the Executive and the Committee for Communities on 10 March 2025, and presented to the Assembly on 13 March 2025.It will be for the Executive to decide on the introduction of a future mitigation package and to consider the allocation of the necessary additional funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail: (i) what reforms he is currently considering in the private rental sector; and (ii) what measures he intends to implement within this mandate to improve affordability, security, and standards for tenants.
answer_text: I announced my programme of private rented sector reform last year. This programme contains key measures to address the issues of affordability, security and standards in a balanced way. Crucially, this programme will be delivered in the current mandate. Measures within this programme which I have already delivered are: Establishing minimum standards for fire and electrical safety;Commencing the power to restrict rent increases to once in every 12 months, with a three month notice period; andThe transfer of the Landlord Registration Scheme to councils.                   The remaining measures to be delivered in this mandate are: The introduction of longer notice to quit periods; Amending Landlord Registration Regulations to make the scheme a more meaningful tool in raising property standards; and Establishing Minimum Energy Efficiency Standards for the Private Rented Sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities if he is aware of any ongoing difficulties within the Northern Ireland Housing Executive and housing associations in respect of the accurate filtering and matching of applications from ground floor or mobility-needs applicants accessing appropriate housing options.
answer_text: Difficulties within the Northern Ireland Housing Executive in respect to the accurate filtering and matching of applications is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows.“The Housing Executive is not aware of any specific issues or difficulties in relation to this.”Housing Regulation Branch within DfC has not noted any issues within Housing Associations in respect of the accurate filtering and matching of applications from ground floor or mobility-needs applicants accessing appropriate housing options.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the total number of faults recorded; and (ii) the total costs for repair to the traffic signals at the Buncrana Road and Springtown Road intersection at Lidl, Derry, since the traffic lights were installed.
answer_text: Since the signals at this junction became the department’s responsibility to maintain there have been 14 faults recorded with a total repair cost of £2,956.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme eligibility criteria, whether the two year waiting time includes the time waiting for a first consultant-led appointment.
answer_text: The Scheme has now launched 2 June 2025 and applies the following strict eligibility criteria:Patients who are ordinarily resident in Northern Ireland; And Patients who are on a treatment or surgical waiting list within HSC services in Northern Ireland for 2 years or more.The two years wait refers to the date the patient was boarded for surgery with the respective HSC Trust in Northern Ireland. It does not include the wait time ahead of a patient’s first consultant led appointment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of potholes and road defects (a) reported; and (b) repaired, in the Fir Road and Mansefield Grove area in the Foyle constituency, in the last five years; and (ii) why this area has not been included in the two-year resurfacing programme for the Derry and Strabane District Council area.
answer_text: The table provides information on the defects notified to my Department and the subsequent repairs carried out in line with my Department’s maintenance policy over the last five years.YearDefects NotifiedDefects Repaired20201212202124222022636220235453202420142025 (to date)11My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government and this has had a significant impact on its ability to deliver essential services such as carriageway resurfacing.Whilst there are many roads which would undoubtedly benefit from investment, due to limited budgets, resurfacing and maintenance work must be taken forward through the prioritisation of those areas deemed most in need of intervention.Details of the resurfacing schemes planned for the 2025/26 financial year will be included in my Department’s Annual Report to Council.My officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance if he will consider reforms for leaseholders, including: (i) the introduction of a legal right to service-charge transparency; (ii) the creation of a housing tribunal to resolve disputes; and (iii) a fee cap to protect leaseholders from excessive charges.
answer_text: I have recently received a scoping paper prepared by my officials which explores the regulation of shared property managers. That paper contains a detailed analysis of the issues and outlines potential options that relate to the matters raised in this question. I am currently considering those options and am exploring with officials how best to progress this work in the context of available resources and competing priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount paid in compensation claims for damage to vehicles caused by potholes or road defects along the Strand Road in the Foyle constituency, within the last five years.
answer_text: Over the last full five financial years, 2020/2021 to 2024/2025, my Department has paid £1,798.15 in vehicle damage compensation for incidents that occurred at Strand Road, Derry.The NICS Accounting System does not record the cause of incidents that lead to compensation payments, therefore it is not possible to disaggregate the amount of compensation paid out by defect type.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 6767/22-27, for an update on whether consideration has been given to the inclusion of halts at (i) Strathfoyle; (ii) City of Derry Airport; and (iii) Ballykelly, as part of the Phase 3 Derry to Coleraine rail upgrade.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:As per response to AQW 6767/22-27 We are continuing to progress the Coleraine to Derry Phase 3 project with intention to commence works in 2026/27, subject to availability of funding. The Phase 3 project business case is focused on renewal of the track and line speed recovery and does not include any additional halts at (i) Strathfoyle; (ii) City of Derry Airport; or (iii) Ballykelly. The consideration of halts at Strathfoyle, City of Derry Airport and Ballykelly are part of a wider Belfast to Derry Route enhancements Programme. We are continuing to progress with the required modelling which will inform requirements to enhance the service on this line. This study should be concluded by the end of 2025. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 18281/22-27, whether he will expand the Dental Access Scheme to include the Foyle constituency, given oral health inequalities and poor access to NHS dental care in the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: A Northern Ireland-wide Dental Access Scheme (DAS) commenced on 1st August 2024.  This new scheme aims to improve access (during the working week) for unregistered patients experiencing an emergency, urgent, or pressing dental condition.  A key objective of the scheme is to include practices from those areas where access difficulties are most acute, such as the Western area.  All Northern Ireland dental practices were given the opportunity to apply to join the scheme but unfortunately no practices from Derry came forward.There are currently 38 practices participating in the scheme.  Details of the scheme (including participating practices) can be viewed at:  https://online.hscni.net/dental-access-scheme/ There are currently 2 practices who are delivering the DAS in the Western area, these are: Catherine Street Dental Care, LimavadyStrabane Family Dental Care, StrabaneEarly indications are that the scheme is helping to address the dental needs of unregistered patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the estimated number of: (i) people in who will be affected by the proposed cuts to Personal Independence Payment; and (ii)  households projected to be pushed into poverty by these cuts.
answer_text: During the second reading of the Universal Credit and Personal Independence Payment Bill in Parliament, the UK Government announced a significant change to its welfare plans, stating that any future reform of the eligibility conditions for Personal Independence Payment will now be subject to the outcome of a review in relation to the assessment criteria.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the estimated number of deaf and disabled people who will be affected if the current rate of Limited Capability for Work and Work-Related Activity is halved under proposed reforms.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer to AQW 27173/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline: (i) what specific reforms are being introduced to the Discretionary Support Grant to prevent fraud; and (ii) how the Department will ensure that these measures do not create additional barriers for genuine applicants in urgent need of support.
answer_text: I have tasked officials with exploring options to reform the administration of the Discretionary Support Scheme to ensure it protects the public purse from the risk of fraud. In doing so I will ensure the Scheme also continues to seek new ways to improve and enhance its service and that it is delivering the right money to the right people at the right time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline: (i) the number of Universal Credit claimants currently in receipt of Limited Capability for Work- Related Activities; and (ii) of these, how many claimants will be at a financial loss when the rate is frozen for individuals who were receiving the benefit before April 2026.
answer_text: (i) The most recently available data (February 2025) indicates that there were 70,860 Universal Credit Claimants in receipt of the Limited Capability for Work and Work Related Activity element as part of their award of Universal Credit.(ii) During the legislative passage of the Universal Credit Bill, the UK Government made a number of amendments to the initial proposals.  The Limited Capability for Work and Work Related Activities element of Universal Credit will now not be frozen for claimants who were in receipt of the element prior to April 2026 but will rise in line with the consumer prices index.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of claimants currently in receipt of contributory Employment and Support Allowance, who are at risk of losing the benefit under the proposed time-limiting changes.
answer_text: I refer to the answer provided in respect of AQW 27171/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline: (i) how many Personal Independence Payment (PIP) claimants also fall within (a) an Employment and Support Allowance support group or (b) the Universal Credit Limited Capability for Work-Related Activity component; and (ii) any research his Department has conducted into the number of individuals at risk of losing these benefits under the PIP proposals in the UK Government's Green Paper Pathways to Work: Reforming Benefits and Support to Get Britain Working.
answer_text: (i) The most recently available data indicates that:a) there were approximately 71,400 PIP claimants who were also in the Employment and Support Allowance Support Group (as of February 2025), andb) there were approximately 49,110 PIP claimants that also fell within the Universal Credit Limited Capability for Work and Work Related Activity element (as of August 2024).(ii) Impact analysis of the UK Government PIP proposals has been published: https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/universal-credit-and-personal-independence-payment-bill-impact-analysis-tables-30-june-2025  However, the UK Government has decided not to proceed with the proposed changes to PIP at this time. Any future reform of the PIP eligibility criteria will be based on the outcome of a review of the PIP assessment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail an estimate of the number of young people who could be impacted when eligibility for Universal Credit Limited Capability for Work-Related Activity is raised to 22 years old.
answer_text: I refer the member to the answer provided to AQW 26794/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 20918/22-27, to detail an update on (i) the results of the Gambling Prevalence Survey; and (ii) the introduction of a levy on land-based gambling operators.
answer_text: The results from the 2024 Gambling Prevalence Survey were published on 30 April and are available on the Department’s website at the following link: https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/prevalence-gambling-northern-ireland-2024In relation to the introduction of a local industry levy, I refer the Member to the answer to AQW 22856/22-27.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the Urban Villages project at Meenan Square.
answer_text: The Meenan Square site is currently under the ownership of Apex Housing Association. Subject to a successful outcome of the planning application, it is estimated that the project works will begin on site in Spring 2026.The redevelopment will transform a largely derelict site into a vibrant shared space. Once completed, the site will include a range of facilities, commercial units, social housing and residential apartments which will significantly improve the physical environment, as well as building capacity within the local community to harness wider economic and social benefits.Urban Villages recently started a capacity building programme with Meenan Square Developments Limited, to ensure successful management and operation of the new facilities. This support will continue throughout the planning process and beyond. Ensuring the long-term sustainability of the area is a key outcome for the Executive Office.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) why the publication of the Protect Life 2 - Suicide Prevention Strategy has been delayed; (ii) a timeline for publication; and (iii) the total current funding shortfall.
answer_text: The Protect Life 2 Suicide Strategy was published on 10 September 2019. The strategy has been extended until the end of 2027 with the option of a further extension to 2029. The new Action Plan and Implementation Plan were agreed at the Executive Working Group on Mental Wellbeing, Resilience and Suicide Prevention on 3 April, subject to funding issues being resolved.  The Department currently provides funding of up to £12m per annum for implementation of the strategy and despite the significant financial challenges it is facing in 2025/26, the Department is working hard to develop a plan to provide additional funding for this year, which would enable the new Action Plan and Implementation Plan to be published.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what assessment has been made of the impact on Altnagelvin Hospital following plans to remove emergency general surgery from Causeway Hospital.
answer_text: My Department is aware that proposed changes stemming from the Northern Trust General Surgery Consultation process could potentially have an impact on Altnagelvin Area Hospital and Western Trust services.As part of their consultation process, the Northern Trust considered potential implications for Western Trust and concluded that planned mitigations within their preferred model will avoid any large-scale transfer of unscheduled surgical demand from Causeway to Altnagelvin.Under the Northern Trust proposal any patients attending Causeway Emergency Department (ED) requiring onward transfer for surgical admission, will be exclusively transferred to Antrim Area Hospital. The Northern Trust has estimated that under the proposed model, the volume of patients which would bypass Causeway Hospital by ambulance to be no more than 2 patients per week, with the majority going to Antrim. The Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) may bring a small proportion of these patients to Altnagelvin depending on pick-up location. However, other than these bypass patients, NIAS would continue to bring the current profile of patients to Causeway ED, including those with possible surgical pathology.The Northern Trust estimated that under the proposed model, with a small number of exceptions there would be no change to the profile of patients who will be managed through Causeway ED. Causeway would still be providing assessment and treatment for patients with surgical presentations, and for most trauma cases. Given the distribution of the Causeway catchment population this is more likely to impact on Antrim than Altnagelvin. Initial Northern Trust modelling based on catchment population suggested this might result in 300 additional walk-in patients per year to Altnagelvin ED. This modelling has been shared with the Western Trust.The Northern Trust Project Board for the development of the General Surgery Consultation included a representative from the Western Trust. Northern Trust have committed to monitoring any impact post-implementation should the proposal be approved by the Department of Health.I am still considering my response to the Northern Trust proposal, the potential impact on Altnagelvin and other HSC services will be considered as part of my final decision.
