answer_text: 1929 successful candidates at interview2951 unsuccessful candidates at interview. 189 candidates who were disqualified for using notes or assistance from a proxy during the interview process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the total costs of the public announcement systems, including speakers, at Belfast Grand Central Station.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:The cost of the public address system including speakers at Grand Central Station was £712,851. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the depth threshold for pothole repairs for each of the last ten years; and (ii) whether the same threshold is applied consistently across all DfI Roads Divisions.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial position for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. In response, my Department has had to reduce its routine road maintenance activities to a limited service, which prioritises delivery of essential services for maintaining public safety and protecting the transport network. Consequently, only the highest priority defects across the network are currently being repaired.Over the past ten years, there have been a number for changes to the guidance for limited service to permit the routine road maintenance activities to be undertaken in line with available resources. The depth threshold for pothole repairs, known as the intervention levels are based on the usage levels for both carriageways and footways. The number of iterations of the guidance along with the various usage categories means that there have been a large number of intervention levels over the past ten years. The current policy regarding Road Maintenance Standards for Safety – Limited Service is DEM 160/21 and can be found at:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/road-maintenance-standards-for-safety-limited-service-in-2021-22-dem-160-21.pdfThe tables regarding the changes in the defects to be instructed for repair over the past 10 years have been deposited in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients on autism spectrum disorder assessment waiting lists in each of the last three years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: As part of the encompass roll-out, the Health and Social Care Trusts have now gone live with a new electronic patient record system. As these Trusts continue to transition to completely digitised records, their data is undergoing extensive validation. Until this process is complete, there may be gaps in the data they are able to submit for reporting purposes.Figures provided are active waits at month end for diagnostic assessment.NA - Figures not available due to on-going encompass implementation.Number of Children Waiting for Diagnostic Assessment Waiting as at:HSC TrustBelfastNorthernSouth EasternSouthernWestern31st March 2025NANANA1,6102,68631st March 20244,0234,269NA1,1602,00031st March 20233,0663,6991605751,577Number of Adults Waiting for Diagnostic Assessment Waiting as at:HSC TrustBelfastNorthernSouth EasternSouthernWestern31st March 2025NANA83848448131st March 2024546127668743337531st March 2023503839463372244Waiting lists for autism assessment remain a challenge. The Department acknowledges that this is not acceptable and can have a profound impact on mental health.Department officials are working with colleagues from the Public Health Agency and Child Health to develop a regionally consistent and defined neurodevelopmental pathway for children and young people that complies with NICE guidelines.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the criteria used by her Department to determine which areas and road networks are given priority for repairs.
answer_text: Article 8 of the Roads (Northern Ireland) Order 1993 places a duty on my Department to maintain all public roads and footways in reasonable condition.  To comply with this obligation, my department has in place a set of maintenance standards, which establish the frequencies for road inspections dependent on traffic volumes, and specify response times for the repair of defects.Inspection frequencies vary from daily cycles for motorways, to six-monthly cycles for carriageways and footways carrying low volumes of traffic.  Response times specified for the repair of defects are dependent on the severity of the defect and range from one calendar day to the inclusion of the defect in the next work programme for that particular route. In addition to response maintenance, my department also implements a programme of planned maintenance on its existing rural and urban road and footway networks to ensure that these assets remain safe and serviceable to users. The distribution of funding allocations for road maintenance, are made on the basis of need which aims to ensure, as far as practicable, an equitable distribution of funds across the whole of the North. Priorities are assessed on the basis of information obtained from condition surveys, other works programmes and professional engineering assessments.  This information is used to prepare work programmes, which are subsequently presented to the various District Councils at their Spring/Autumn meetings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide an update on (i) any efforts to mitigate against the impact of the two-child policy on low-income households; and (ii) any engagement he has had with the UK Government on this issue.
answer_text: I am committed to addressing poverty and its impacts. I am pleased that the Executive has agreed the draft Anti-Poverty Strategy which provides a framework across Government to ensure that we target actions and funding together to make a real and lasting difference to people’s lives. I am also engaging with the UK Government on wider anti-poverty initiatives, including representing Northern Ireland on the Four Nations Ministerial Group on Child Poverty which is exploring all available levers, including considering social security reforms, to drive forward short and long-term actions across government to reduce child poverty. My officials continue to engage with officials from the Department for Work and Pensions across these issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any research his Department has conducted into how the two-child policy disproportionately affects working families, compared to Great Britain.
answer_text: My Department has not conducted any research into how the two-child policy disproportionately affects working families, compared to Great Britain.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail what efforts are being made by her Department to tackle repeat flooding incidents along the Foyle Road, Derry.
answer_text: NI Water has advised that it does not have multiple reports of flooding on the Foyle Road.NI Water has completed a Culmore Drainage Area Plan (DAP) which did not identify a requirement for any capital interventions to address out of sewer flooding from NI Water infrastructure in this area.  NI Water has undertaken some storm separation work in the area and further investigations are ongoing in the area to identify other potential opportunities for storm separation. I can confirm that gullies are cleaned along Foyle Road, Derry, in accordance with my Department’s limited-service maintenance policy.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com . Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the minor works application for Broadbridge Primary School to address urgent safety issues relating to access routes for pupils with Special Educational Needs, following concerns regarding the front entrance and car park.
answer_text: Whilst Broadbridge Primary School applied for CCTV and access control works under the 2017 Minor Works call, the scheme has not progressed due to capital budget constraints. The school subsequently submitted an Unavoidable Minor Works Application to the Department in April 2025, requesting the creation of a secure lobby and provision of new fencing to secure the playground at the rear of the school. Departmental officials are currently working with the school to obtain appropriate evidence to support the application and have arranged for the Education Authority’s Health and Safety adviser to visit the school in early June.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister if they will reconsider the decision not to award funding via the Central Good Relations Fund to Greater Shantallow Community Arts in Foyle.
answer_text: The Central Good Relations Fund (CGRF) is a competitive, merit-based funding programme. Each application is subject to a robust assessment process, led by officials, and funding is allocated in merit list order until the available budget is exhausted.The CGRF has an appeals process for organisations to avail of, should they wish to challenge the status of their application.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the total number of applicants who are currently unable to move into social housing properties due to the homes being incorrectly categorised as wheelchair accessible or not adequately adapted to meet their needs, in each local council area.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive does not hold the requested information”.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly. Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 24738/22-27, whether she is in a position to provide an indication of where a shared path for Alder Road might rank in terms of prioritisation for investment.
answer_text: Development of the active travel route assessment tool is still ongoing in line with the response provided to AQW 24738/22-27 and I remain committed to assessing a potential route on Alder Road once that development work is complete.Consideration of the Active Travel Delivery Plan priority routes in Derry will be key to understanding where a shared path on Alder Road might rank in terms of prioritisation for investment against those other routes. Officials are currently reviewing responses from the public consultation on the Active Travel Delivery Plan with the aim of presenting a final plan for my consideration in the autumn.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she plans to increase public transport fares.
answer_text: As with any year, a decision on a potential increase in public transport fares is made in conjunction with wider budgetary considerations.I have not yet taken decisions on my Department’s 2025-26 budget outcome.  In doing so I will, of course, consider the responses received on the Equality Impact Consultation on my Department’s Draft 2025-26 Budget.In any budget situation, there will be finite resources for allocation and this includes public transport. Translink’s Draft 2025-26 Resource Budget for the purposes of the EQIA is challenging.The outworkings of the final budget and its potential impacts will influence any decision on fares this financial year.  These budgetary considerations will be balanced by wider consideration of an uplift in fares on the affordability, accessibility and modal shift potential of our public transport network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline what regulations or oversight mechanisms are in place to ensure Occupational Therapists are involved in the design and planning of new social housing developments, particularly in relation to the delivery of (i) Lifetime Homes and (iii) adaptable housing.
answer_text: Lifetime Homes consists of standards that have been developed to ensure that a home is flexible, adaptable and accessible; and that there is added comfort, convenience and safety for tenants and visitors. Occupational Therapists have no role to play as all new build social homes are currently built to Lifetime Homes Standards, ensuring that homes can be easily adapted to meet the changing needs of tenants.  This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive work collaboratively with Health and Social Care Trust Occupational Therapists in a range of areas related to housing for people with disabilities. This includes collaboration at strategic level through the Interdepartmental Accessible Homes Strategic Forum and operationally through Regional Partnership Forums across tenure. There is also frequent joint working of cases to ensure that housing design meets the assessed need of the service user with Occupational Therapists regularly consulted on the appropriate allocation of accessible social housing.In the adaptation of homes Trust Occupational Therapists and the Executive co-produced an Interdepartmental Housing Adaptations Design Toolkit (relaunched 2022). The Toolkit includes communication processes between OT and the Housing Executive as well as setting out regionally agreed design standards for adaptable housing. The Toolkit is unique to Northern Ireland and ensures a regionally consistent approach to housing adaptations provision.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the total number of applicants on the social housing waiting list for a (a) two-bed; (b) three-bed; and (c) 4-bed, ground floor property, in each of the last five years per local council area.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 27553/22-27 the information has been placed in the assembly library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline what action his Department is taking to (i) increase the availability of ground floor social homes; and (ii) ensure that adaptable or 'life homes' within the social housing stock are being included as offers for ground-floor applicants.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“(i)	The provision of ground floor social homes through the Social Housing Development Programme is via either ground floor apartments (as part of a larger apartment scheme) or bungalow provision, which can be either General Needs or Wheelchair Standards provision. Bungalow provision is normally only included in cases where an alternative design solution would not be appropriate for the need or where specific Planning restrictions apply. Please note that all New Build social housing is required to be designed to Lifetime Homes Standard, to ensure the flexibility of the property for potential future adaptation. The Rules and guidance around allocations detail that “dwelling must be reasonably suitable having regard to the particular needs of the Applicant or a member of the Applicant’s household.” In general, the property must be suitable for individuals at the time of allocation, however, the wording of the Rule provides some flexibility in allowing decision makers to come to an informed decision around the suitability of any property viz-a-viz the needs of those on the Waiting List”.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly at  Public.Affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk 	Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make the best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the total number of unoccupied (i)  two-bed; (ii) three-bed; and (iii) four-bed, ground floor properties in each of the last five years per local council area.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 27556/22-27, the information has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities if he will consider extending the Freedom of Information Act 2000 to include housing associations in the list of named Public Authorities, to ensure transparency on issues related to social housing potential contributing factors leading to homelessness and transfer requests.
answer_text: I have no plans to seek to extend the Freedom of Information Act 2000 to include Housing Associations in the list of named Public Authorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he has had any engagement with the Minister of Health in relation to air quality-related deaths.
answer_text: My officials work closely with Department of Health’s Chief Environmental Health Officer to improve public health and reduce air pollution related deaths. Engagement includes work on the Clean Air Strategy, air quality legislation, World Health Organisation guidelines, and public pollution alerts. Five meetings have taken place since November 2024.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will commit to reviewing current legislation governing the sale and marketing of infant formula, specifically to consider allowing the use of loyalty points, vouchers and gift cards as valid forms of payment.
answer_text: The Competition and Markets Authority recently published its final report on the infant formula and follow-on formula market study. The report makes a series of recommendations to governments across the UK, including a recommendation to amend, as necessary, the guidance to the infant formula and follow-on formula regulations (and, if necessary and feasible, amend the infant formula and follow-on formula regulations) to ensure that parents can use gift cards, vouchers, loyalty points and coupons in lieu of cash to purchase infant formula.Officials in my Department are currently working closely with the Food Standards Agency in Northern Ireland to assess the CMA report recommendations. Officials are also engaging with counterparts across England, Scotland and Wales to consider the report. This work has not yet concluded.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of staff employed to provide autism spectrum disorder assessments in each of the last three years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The number of whole-time equivalent staff employed to provide autism spectrum disorder assessments in each of the last 3 years broken down by Health and Social Care Trust is as follows:Children’s ServicesHSC TrustNo of StaffFY 22/23FY 23/24FY 24/25Belfast Health and Social Care Trust3.9(7.80 total wte)5.98(11.97 total wte)6.43(12.87 total wte)Northern Health and Social Care Trust24.8024.8024.80Southern Health and Social Care Trust9.807.8011.40South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust4.9(8.3 total wte)4.4(7.6 total wte)4.9(7.9 total wte)Western Health and Social Care Trust8.178.728.72Adult ServicesHSC TrustNo of StaffFY 22/23FY 23/24FY 24/25Belfast Health and Social Care Trust1.5(3.03 total wte)1.77(3.54 total wte)3.85(4.28 total wte)Northern Health and Social Care Trust5.905.905.90Southern Health and Social Care Trust223South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust3.413.214.01Western Health and Social Care TrustNot providedThe figures provided by Trusts above are approximate as at March of each financial year and assume all staff are in post and not on maternity leave, secondment, training and competencies for diagnostics etc.Not all Trusts have been able to provide a detailed breakdown of staff specifically involved in delivering diagnostic assessments for Autism. This is due to the integrated nature of their services, where teams often deliver a wide range of autism-related support — including diagnostic assessments, pre- and post-diagnosis intervention and training and consultation for other health and social care professionals Trusts also advise that there are other staff employed within their service who are not directly employed in delivering assessments but who would contribute to the supervision, triage, admin, contact calls, intervention and indirect clinical forms of assessment (e.g. school observations).At the time of request, Western Health and Social Care Trust were unable to provide figures on diagnosticians within their Adult services. Belfast Health and Social Care Trust advise that they also have 4 staff within their Community Paediatrics service who presently undertake autism spectrum disorder assessment within a complex caseload.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the total heating costs at Stormont buildings over the last five financial years; and (ii) what steps are being taken to improve energy efficiency across the estate.
answer_text: ANSWER(i) The heating costs for each of the last five financial years for those buildings on the Stormont Estate for which Department of Finance (DoF) is responsible are:Financial Year Stormont Estate Heating Costs £2020/2021361,107.722021/2022698,703.782022/2023717,128.352023/2024473,698.132024/2025416,858.09(ii) In 2020/2021, approximately 500 external lights on the Stormont Estate grounds were upgraded to LED which uses significantly less energy and has a longer lifespan. The 3 large gas boilers which supply heat to Castle Buildings were upgraded to high efficiency units. Phase 2 of the NICS Office Estate Strategy aims to deliver a greener, modern NICS Office Estate by 2035 through improved Energy Efficiency. With regard to the Stormont Estate, procurement is underway to deliver a 400kVA photovoltaic installation by 2027, which will provide renewable energy to the DoF managed buildings.At Castle Buildings, a refurbishment project is ongoing that will include the replacement of the roof at Block B with enhanced insulation to meet net zero standards. Work has also been initiated to investigate and develop options to upgrade both the Castle Buildings exterior “envelope” and systems, to move to net zero standards.My Department plans to commence the procurement of a carbon baselining exercise in 2025/26, for the retained Office Estate, including our buildings on the Stormont Estate. This will help inform and enable additional efficiency projects with a view to further reducing energy usage and de-carbonising the Office Estate.  For the longer term, my Department has been supporting Department for Economy (DfE) in a pilot project to investigate the potential future application of shallow geothermal technology to provide sustainable low carbon renewable heating and cooling systems. It is hoped that this will inform the potential for a future geothermal heat network to replace fossil fuel heating systems at some buildings on the Stormont Estate, as well as helping to reduce and stabilise our energy costs. The work will also improve our understanding of the contribution that geothermal energy might make in decarbonising how we heat our buildings generally and help inform future projects in the public and private sectors and in domestic settings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has engaged with the Minister of Finance to ring-fence funds garnered through the Soft Drinks Industry Levy to address health conditions such as obesity and type 2 diabetes.
answer_text: The Soft Drink Industry Levy (SDIL) came into effect across the UK from 6 April 2018. The UK Government does not formally link the funds that are generated by Soft Drinks Industry Levy to any specific spending. Rather, spending in Whitehall departments for children’s food and to promote children’s health is allocated through Spending Reviews.		The Executive receives a Barnett consequential on any changes in funding provided to Whitehall departments. For Spending Reviews this is provided at a departmental level rather than by specific programme. The Minister of Finance has confirmed that his Department is not informed of how much the Executive receives as a result of the Levy. 		In light of my focus on improving health and addressing health inequalities, it would be my preference to specifically allocate such funds against relevant areas of expenditure. My Department had previously explored the potential to use funds equivalent to those likely to have been raised through the SDIL in Northern Ireland for tackling obesity and other public health related issues but given there was no budget line this did not prove to be possible. 		My Department recently published a consultation analysis report on a new, co-produced obesity strategic framework, ‘Healthy Futures’. Work continues to develop a final strategic framework and associated action plan, and to identify the costs and funding to implement the strategic framework in full.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 20652/22-27. whether her Department will consider introducing a requirement for authorities to notify residents if a person convicted of sexual offences against children is living in their area.
answer_text: I am not currently considering introduction of a requirement for authorities to notify residents if a person convicted of sexual offences against children is living in their area.My previous response outlines the mechanisms already in place enabling PSNI and partner agencies to monitor and manage convicted sex offenders in the community.  These mechanisms are sufficient and proportionate to the protection and safeguarding of children in any given locale.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide a breakdown of the total costs of school maintenance and repairs for (i) maintained and (ii) controlled schools in Foyle, for each of the last eight years, including 2025/26.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has statutory responsibility for undertaking repairs/maintenance work in maintained and controlled schools.  My Department allocates the EA resource funding each financial year to take this forward.Details of the final outturn for schools’ maintenance expenditure in each of the last seven years for maintained and controlled schools in the Foyle constituency has been provided in a table by the EA, which I have deposited in the Assembly Library. Please note that the 2024/25 maintenance figure is provisional outturn expenditure and is likely to be subject to change.  The EA’s 2025/26 schools’ maintenance allocation is not split across individual schools but allocated by the EA on a needs basis as the works are prioritised.  Landlord maintenance activities are not included as they are funded by schools from their LMS budget and Voluntary Grammar and Grant Maintained Integrated Schools are responsible for their own maintenance works.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance if his Department has considered ring-fencing revenue from the Soft Drinks Industry Levy to fund public health programmes, particularly those tackling obesity and type 2 diabetes.
answer_text: ANSWERFunds that are generated by the Soft Drinks Industry Levy are not formally linked by the British Government to any specific spending. Rather, spending in Whitehall departments for children’s food and to promote children’s health is allocated through Spending Reviews. The Executive receives a Barnett consequential on any changes in funding provided to Whitehall departments. For Spending Reviews this is provided at a departmental level rather than by specific programme. My department is not informed of how much the Executive receives as a result of the Levy. The Minister of Health has primary policy responsibility for health-related issues such as obesity prevention and treatment; and has the ability to prioritise his departmental budget in line with Ministerial priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to provide an estimated breakdown of the revenue generated from the Soft Drinks Industry Levy (sugar tax) via Barnett Consequential, over the past five years.
answer_text: Funds that are generated by the Soft Drinks Industry Levy are not formally linked by the British Government to any specific spending. Rather, spending in Whitehall departments for children’s food and to promote children’s health is allocated through Spending Reviews. The Executive receives a Barnett consequential on any changes in funding provided to Whitehall departments. For Spending Reviews this is provided at a departmental level rather than by specific programme. My department is not informed of how much the Executive receives as a result of the Levy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will consider additional investment for the Foyle constituency to repair potholes and the deterioration across the road infrastructure.
answer_text: My officials are currently working on a new Road Maintenance Strategy for my consideration in the coming weeks. We will focus on delivering higher-quality repairs instead of spreading resources too thinly, ensuring a more reliable and safer road network where we intervene.We need a new approach to how the road network is maintained, one that focuses our limited resources in a way that balances safety and value for money. It should be data driven and supported by sound engineering judgement and address the problem in a meaningful and sustainable manner.The well-discussed funding shortfall, and the years of Limited Service, have seen the condition of roads across the network deteriorate. In distributing the resources available for road repairs and resurfacing programmes, allocations relating to the areas covered by the Department’s four Roads Divisions are made on the basis of need, using a range of weighted indicators tailored to each maintenance activity.  The Divisions also use a range of indicators when apportioning budget across District Council areas to ensure, as far as possible, an equitable distribution of funds according to need.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the (i) mean amount of time; and (ii) median amount of time on the social housing waiting list for applicant households on (a) 31 March; (b) 30 June; (c) 30 September; and (d) 31 December, in each of the last five years including the year to date in 2025, broken down by council area and local Housing Executive office.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW/27279//22-27 the information has been placed in the assembly library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) whether cervical screening results are routinely retained, including in cases where no HPV is detected; and (ii) how long are these records held and accessible for clinical audit or patient request.
answer_text: All cervical screening results are routinely retained for all samples, regardless of whether the sample is negative or positive and the result is entered into the patient’s record.Laboratories follow the Royal College of Pathologists’ (RCPath) guidelines on the retention and storage of records and specimens. The guidelines can be viewed at: https://www.rcpath.org/static/049ea966-df5c-4a9f-9353ba24a69bb808/The-retention-and-storage-of-pathological-records-and-specimens-5th-edition.pdf Where a sample is negative for HPV, the guideline relating to liquid samples is followed (paragraph 95 of the RCPath guidelines refers).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) what assessment has her Department made of the condition of the paving on Foyle Street, Foyle; and (ii) what urgent action is being taken to address the uneven surfaces to mitigate the risk of trips and falls for pedestrians.
answer_text: Foyle Street is a highly trafficked urban route and is inspected monthly in accordance with my Department’s Limited Service maintenance policy. Any defects identified that meet the current intervention level will be programmed for repair.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 26713/22-27, for a breakdown of the total number of active pothole enquiries, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Data showing potholes reported by the public is regularly published and available for public viewing through the OpenDataNI portal at https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/dataset/pothole-enquiries which can be downloaded and filtered by date, geographical area, road name, local district council, and status.

A table has been provided which shows a breakdown of location by electoral ward. Due to the volume of information, this has been placed in the Assembly Library.

An unsatisfactory audit outcome is applied where there have been significant process or management shortcomings that constitute a patient safety incident. question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any steps being taken to ensure that residents have access to an online platform for reporting trees, pests and diseases, similar to Tree Check in the Republic of Ireland and TreeAlert in Great Britain.
answer_text: The TreeCheck platform, has previously provided for users in Northern Ireland to link to its functionality. Due to developments in internet access arrangements, previous linkages are no longer available. Work is actively underway to re-establish the necessary digital integration between DAERA and the Department of Agriculture, Food and the Marine (DAFM). Technical teams are collaborating to identify and resolve the underlying issues affecting connectivity, data exchange and system security.My department is committed to restoring this useful tool, and in the meantime, reporting of trees, pests and diseases can still be made via email planthealth@daera-ni.gov.uk.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) why pupils with a statement of special educational need, who had applied to attend St Brigid’s College, Foyle, in a mainstream setting have not yet received confirmation of their post-primary placement; and (ii) any steps being taken to ensure timely placements for all pupils with special educational needs.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:To date, the Statutory Assessment and Review Service (SARS) have confirmed eight placements for Year 8 children within the Specialist Provision for Social Communication (SPSC) class in St Brigid’s College for September 2025.  However, given the capacity of this class, not all pupils who expressed a preference to attend this setting were able to be accommodated.  SARS continue to liaise with the school directly in relation to the placement of children with Statements in the mainstream classes in St Brigid’s College.  On 8th and 9th May, representatives from SARS called parents to explain the delay and to reassure them that SARS continue to work in their child’s best interests to secure an appropriate placement.  Parents will also have received a subsequent email confirming the same.St Brigid’s College has raised concerns in relation to the number of children with Statements seeking Year 8 placement for September 2025 and SARS understand that the school have shared these concerns with prospective parents.  SARS and other relevant EA services are working directly with the school to address these matters and remain committed to securing placements as soon as possible. In the interim, SARS staff will provide updates to parents directly on each child’s individual placement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) why diagnoses of ADHD and autism made by private practitioners are not routinely recognised within the health and social care system; and (ii) what steps are being taken to ensure that individuals with privately obtained diagnoses can access appropriate NHS support services.
answer_text: (i) why diagnoses of ADHD and autism made by private practitioners are not routinely recognised within the health and social care system;Diagnoses of ADHD and autism made by private practitioners are recognised within the health and social care system if they meet NICE guidelines.(ii) what steps are being taken to ensure that individuals with privately obtained diagnoses can access appropriate NHS support services.Trusts evaluate the private assessment process against compliance with National Institute of Health and Care Excellence (NICE) guidelines. If the diagnostic assessment complies with NICE, the diagnosis will be accepted and ongoing support will be provided. If the assessment does not meet with NICE guidelines, a clinician will complete the diagnosis. Service users accepted onto an ADHD/ASD waiting list with a private diagnosis are placed on a waiting list in chronological order of their referral, in line with Integrated Elective Access Protocol (IEAP) guidelines. They are also advised that they will remain under private health provision, including paying for treatments, such as prescriptions, until they reach their advised appointment date. Having a private diagnosis will not change their position on the waiting list for a consultant appointment.Drugs prescribed to treat ADHD are controlled and it is advised that they are prescribed initially by a Consultant. With a private ADHD diagnosis anyone requesting a prescription through the HSC, will have to be physically assessed by the HSC prescribing clinician to assess their medical need.  Drug treatment for children, young people and adults with ADHD should always form part of a comprehensive treatment plan that includes psychological, behavioural and educational advice and interventions (or occupational needs in the case of adults). If prescribing under shared care agreements it should be clear where responsibility for prescribing lies.The former HSCB, now SPPG, wrote to private health providers and GP’s in June 2021 of their duty to inform patients that there may be ongoing costs associated with their private health care treatment until such time as they are seen by a HSC clinician.I hope this information is helpful and provides some clarity to you on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 16480/22-27, if he has had or intends to have any engagement with the Minister of Health regarding homeless individuals presenting to Emergency Departments for support.
answer_text: Whilst I have not had any direct engagement with the Minister of Health on this matter, my officials continue to work closely with their counterparts in the Department for Health.There is a clear need to ensure individuals experiencing homelessness are supported to access the most appropriate support at the earliest opportunity. This includes signposting to the Housing Executive or other relevant homelessness services, where suitable interventions can be provided.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any engagement he has had with the Minister for Communities regarding homeless individuals presenting at Emergency Departments (EDs) in search of refuge; and (ii) whether the numbers of individuals presenting to EDs for this reason are recorded.
answer_text: Whilst there has been no specific engagement with the Minister for Communities on the issue of homeless individuals presenting at Emergency Departments (EDs) in search of refuge, my Department continues to work collaboratively with cross-Departmental officials in a number of workstreams to address the impact of homelessness. Homelessness is a complex issue, which cuts across a wide range of areas and impacts on our most vulnerable individuals as a society. My Department will continue to work collaboratively with stakeholders to further understand how to address the impact of homelessness across that cohort.The five Health and Social Care Trusts have stated that there is no formal mechanism in place to record the numbers of homeless individuals presenting to EDs across the region.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline (i) why Echo Echo Dance Theatre Company in Foyle were not granted support via the Arts Council of Northern Ireland's Annual Funding Programme despite meeting the eligibility criteria; and (ii) who is responsible for the decision-making process regarding the allocation of this funding.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Arts Council.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:	“As is the case with all Arts Council funding programmes, an application must meet the eligibility requirements of the given scheme in order to be considered for funding.  However, eligibility is not a guarantee of funding.  The Annual Funding Programme (AFP) is a highly competitive programme: applications are assessed against the published criteria and awards made to those organisations which best demonstrate an ability to deliver on the objectives of the programme.  While unsuccessful in their 2025/26 AFP application, Echo Echo were offered an award of over £28k to assist in their transition out of the AFP portfolio.  It is the policy of the Arts Council not to release details regarding the reason for an applicant’s rejection from any scheme to any third party, unless the rejected applicant indicates their permission for such disclosure.  The Arts Council will be happy to release specific details regarding the Echo Echo decision directly to Mr Durkan should Echo Echo indicate their permission for ACNI to do so. Applications are initially assessed by individual art form officers, then presented to and discussed at a series of Moderations - collective decision-making meetings which determine the final recommendation for each application.  These recommendations are then presented to the Arts Council of Northern Ireland Board who make the final decisions on all AFP recommendations.”	For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Arts Council directly. Making direct contact with the Arts Council will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the steps his Department is taking to address the increasing rates of drug and alcohol addiction, particularly in the North West.
answer_text: As the Minister of Health, I am aware of the harms caused by substance use. The Department of Health has developed a comprehensive approach to address drug and alcohol addiction across Northern Ireland, including the North West. The DOH Substance Use Strategy, and the HSC Strategic Commissioning and Implementation Plan for Substance Use, set both strategic direction and priority actions which address the impact of substance use in our Region. Our current priorities are informed by comprehensive stakeholder engagement and supported by additional research on population demand and current provision, this includes the Western Trust Needs Assessment for Substance Use; the Review of Inpatient Detoxification and Residential Rehabilitation Services and the Review of local information sharing and decision-making groups and the Drug and Alcohol Co-Ordination Teams. The Public Health Agency and SPPG are working closely with the Community and Voluntary Sector and Health and Social Care Trusts to implement the actions identified in the Strategic Commissioning Plan, with the aim that the people of our Region, including the North West, can access the right services, at the right time, delivered by the right people. In the absence of additional finance to support many of the required actions within the Plan, focus has been given to those actions which can proceed without additional resource. These include the development of improved service pathways across preventative, treatment, crisis and recovery services. This work will enable increased access to services, better service user experience and consistency of approach. This direction is supported by the Western Needs Assessment which evidences the benefits of linking existing services in the WHSCT area in a more integrated way and streamlining referral processes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline (i) the policy of the Education Authority in resect of accepting recommendations from privately commissioned educational psychology assessments; and (ii) what steps are being taken to ensure that students who receive private diagnoses, particularly for ADHD, can access reasonable adjustments and additional support at school.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:The Educational Psychology Service (EPS) acknowledges that parents have the right to seek private assessments to better understand their child’s needs. All psychological reports, whether commissioned privately or through the EPS, should be considered in the best interests of the child.  Private assessments where the child is known to the EPS will only be considered if conducted by a practitioner registered with the Health and Care Professions Council (HCPC). The report must demonstrate compliance with ethical, legal, and professional standards, including informed consent and appropriate use of psychometric tools. The full range of supports and available teacher professional learning relating to supporting pupils with social, behavioural, emotional and wellbeing needs, such as ADHD is detailed in the EA Behaviour Plan and also on the Children and Young Peoples’ Services (CYPS) Training Programme which can be reviewed on the following link: EA Bookings (easds.org.uk) My departments SEN and Medical Categories Guidance document sets out the categories under which pupils with special educational needs (SEN) and medical conditions, should be recorded.  Medical diagnoses will be recorded on the Medical Register and if an associated SEN has been identified, i.e. those requiring special educational provision to be made, will also be recorded on the SEN register.The Graduated Response Framework sets out the learning continuum for a range of educational needs and identifies examples of the types of intervention and support available from whole school educational provision to special educational provision.  This is the process schools should employ when assessing the needs of children and young people and providing appropriate provision regardless of diagnosis.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 23900/22-27, for an update on the review of the pothole repair policy as part of the Road Maintenance Strategy.
answer_text: I am currently considering a new Road Maintenance Strategy that I hope to implement in the coming weeks. Until such times as sufficient funding is made available it will not be possible to change the current intervention level however, we will focus on delivering higher quality repairs instead of spreading resources too thinly, ensuring a more reliable and safer road network where we intervene.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when his Department will confirm the number of funded allied health student places that have been allocated to Ulster University, Derry for the 2025-26 academic year.

To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) an update on discussions between his Department and the Irish Department of Foreign Affairs regarding parental consent requirements for passport applications which differ between Northern Ireland and the Republic of Ireland, specifically for single parents; and (ii) whether any steps are being considered to align these processes.
answer_text: ANSWERThe law governing the issuing of Irish passports, the Passports Act 2008, requires that a passport application on behalf of a child includes the consent of each person who is a guardian of that child. Applications for an Irish child passport from this jurisdiction require the consent of those with parental responsibility for the child. Changes to practice relating to the 2008 Act would be a matter for the Dublin Government, but I agree that this can cause difficulties for single parents in particular where an absent person has parental responsibility but is not contactable.An initial contact by my officials with their Department of Foreign Affairs (DFA) colleagues did not result in any resolution of this matter. However, my officials are considering the issue further and will re-engage with DFA in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether potholes and road surface defects, reported via NI Direct, are removed from the online map once they are marked for repair or only after the repair has been completed.
answer_text: The Report-a-Fault system receives over 100,000 customer enquiries relating to road and lighting defects/ issues each year.  The system generate a reference number and will provide the customer with an acknowledgement email, if a valid address is provided on the form.  The acknowledgement email a reference number which will provide a status update via the link http://reportfaultproblem.nidirect.gov.uk/track.html.Active enquiries are displayed on the map for a limited period of time to allow other members of the public to add their details to an existing enquiry if required.  However, this is not an indication that the fault has been investigated/ actioned, and will disappear after a set timeframe.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update for automatic support being provided under the Help with Health Costs scheme for Universal Credit recipients.
answer_text: As outlined in my answer to AQW 26575/22-27, the public consultation on the proposed eligibility criteria for passporting Universal Credit recipients closed on 10 March 2025. 		Officials are currently analysing the responses and compiling a Consultation Summary Report which will be published on the Departmental website in the coming weeks. It is intended that the legislative process will be completed by summer.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what assessment he has made of the results of the 'Music Fans' Voice' fan-led survey and review, which was initiated by the UK Parliament's Culture, Media and Sport Select Committee.
answer_text: Although not focused primarily on the position in Northern Ireland, the findings in the survey are a useful addition to the positions and evidence provided through my officials’ engagement with other music sector interests.  	While many of the issues identified are for others in government and in the music industry to consider, the survey offers an important and valuable perspective which can help inform work currently underway to develop a new arts policy for Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide an update on the current provision of Regional Obesity Management Services, including any plans to meet increasing demand for specialist weight management support.
answer_text: On 21 May 2025, I announced my plans to establish a Regional Obesity Management Service (ROMS). Work is currently being taken forward by my Department to assess patient numbers and to develop a service specification which will set out access criteria. Implementation will be taken forward in a phased basis and in a carefully managed way to ensure that it operates effectively. In its first phase, which is expected to commence early 2026, the focus will be on the provision of medical injections for weight loss, with associated lifestyle support to ensure maximum effectiveness. As work progresses, there may be further phases to this service development to include other interventions, such as bariatric (weight loss) surgery, subject to funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the rationale for the differing response times to traffic signal repairs, as set out in the Road Asset Maintenance policy, including why faults in the Belfast area are addressed within four hours, while elsewhere the target response time is 24 hours.
answer_text: Traffic signal faults are responded to on the basis of the maintenance category assigned to them. Depending on the category selected, response times for urgent faults can range from 2 – 24 hours. Maintenance category selection, as a whole, is based on the criticality of a junction and the complexity of the associated transport network.  Unfortunately, due to the higher costs associated with shorter response times, resources do not permit the allocation of the shortest response times to every junction.The target response times referred to specifically relate to Key Performance Indicators set out in the existing term contract for maintenance of traffic signal equipment.  It is intended that a new contract will be procured within the next 12 months which will include a review of these targets.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline (i) how many households in the private rented sector are in receipt of Local Housing Allowance (LHA) through (a) universal credit housing costs and (b) benefit; (ii) how many of those households have rents that exceed their LHA; and (iii) of these households, what is the median average gap between the full rent amount and the LHA, from April 2024 to the most recent period for which data is available and broken down by Broad Rental Market Area.
answer_text: (i) The number of households in the private rented sector in receipt of Local Housing Allowance (LHA) through (a) Universal Credit (UC) and (b) Housing Benefit (HB) at May 2024 and August 2024 are shown below.(a) Universal Credit(b) Housing benefitTotal households at May 202436,63921,548Total households at August 202438,37920,670(ii) The number of these households that exceed their LHA at May 2024 and August 2024 are shown below.(a) Universal Credit(b) Housing benefitTotal households at May 202416,9888,365Total households at August 202418,6058,577	(iii) Of households that have rents that exceed their LHA, the median average gap between full rent amount and the LHA broken down by Broad Rental Market Area at May 2024 and August 2024 are shown below.		May 2024LHA areaMedian Monthly difference (UC)Median Monthly difference (HB)Belfast BRMA£94.53£154.31Lough Neagh lower BRMA£77.03£72.04lough Neagh Upper BRMA£84.83£66.45North BRMA£64.15£67.40North West BRMA£70.61£64.83South BRMA£74.86£65.15South East BRMA£83.98£104.65South West BRMA£84.72£60.15Unknown£147.64		August 2024LHA areaMedian Monthly difference (UC)Median Monthly difference (HB)Belfast BRMA£94.53£141.16Lough Neagh lower BRMA£78.36£76.53lough Neagh Upper BRMA£84.83£71.07North BRMA£64.15£71.59North West BRMA£71.39£68.34South BRMA£78.54£72.52South East BRMA£85.28£105.99South West BRMA£84.72£62.49Unknown£229.64Data sources: Universal Credit Full Service system (UCFS) – August 2024. Note that due to retrospection of UCFS data UC figures are subject to change.NIHE Housing Benefit Data – May 2024 and August 2024	In producing the geographical analysis, records were attributed to an LHA area and Broad Rental Market Area based on their postcode. Not all records can be correctly allocated using this method and some cannot be allocated at all. All figures are rounded to the nearest pound.Note that the rate of LHA used to calculate Housing Benefit/Universal Credit housing costs award depends on the area where the customer chooses to live, who lives with them and the number of bedrooms they need.  This may have an impact on the median monthly difference as some customers may be under-occupying their property and will therefore have a larger shortfall between their eligible rent used to calculate their Housing Benefit/Universal Credit housing costs award and the contractual rent charged by their landlord.The median figures above are an average across all household size requirements (number of bedrooms needed). The median difference will be affected by both the household size requirements and the Broad Rental Market Area (BRMA) rates within each parliamentary constituency. This should also be considered when drawing comparisons between UC and HB due to the differences in their caseload characteristics.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the criteria used to assess applications for the Core Grant Funding Scheme, including how decisions are made.
answer_text: The Core Grant Funding Scheme for 2025-26 was co-designed in 2024 with Community and Voluntary Sector representatives, and included the following scoring criteria:Evidence of how an organisation supports the department’s strategic objectives;Evidence that an organisation is involved in carrying out relevant activities;Evidence that an organisation will deliver clear outcomes, including how those will be measured and the targets that will be met;Evidence that an organisation has delivered similar outcomes previously; Evidence that an organisation has suitable monitoring and control systems in place, and suitably qualified and experienced staff; andEvidence that an organisation will work in partnership across the statutory, community and voluntary sectors. These scoring criteria and the application process are listed and explained in the guidance for applicants, available on the departmental website.Applications were individually assessed and scored by assessment panel members who then met to agree a final panel score for each criteria.  The panel was chaired by a Grade 7 senior manager within the Department, supported by two separate officials from the Staff Officer or Deputy Principal Grade.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the development of a paediatric stroke pathway.

To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the reasons for the Northern Ireland Civil Service's failure to meet response deadlines for successful applicants in the Administrative Officer recruitment competition, which was launched in August 2024 with interviews held in November 2024.
answer_text: There are several stages in the recruitment process that need to be completed before successful applicants are notified.  The large number of candidates for this AO competition resulted in some delay in issuing results to successful candidates.  All stages have now been completed, and the paperwork is being finalised.The merit list is anticipated by 16th May 2025, candidate results would then issue in the following week.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the review of the Help with Health Costs, specifically the automatic passporting for claimants in receipt of Universal Credit.
answer_text: The public consultation on the proposed eligibility criteria for passporting Universal Credit (UC) recipients closed on 10 March 2025. I welcome the openness, feedback and engagement from all who have participated in the consultation.		Officials are currently analysing the responses and compiling a Consultation Summary Report which will be published on the Departmental website in the coming weeks. It is intended that the legislative process will be completed by summer.				My aim is to ensure that those most in need automatically receive help to access these very important health services. 		As you are aware, whilst the legislative process is ongoing, an interim measure remains in place for UC recipients who need financial assistance with their health costs. This is via the Health Service Low Income Scheme (LIS), where UC claimants are required to apply annually to gain a potential exemption or remission from their health costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide an update on planned changes to bursary arrangements for healthcare students, including allied health students.
answer_text: My Department is reviewing the breadth of current funding provided in support of healthcare related courses, including funded and unfunded allied health courses, which lead to a career within HSC. This review is intended to inform options for a public consultation. I would hope to be in a position to launch that consultation in late summer/early autumn.It is not possible to indicate what future changes will be until the consultation has been completed and responses considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline any steps that have been taken in schools in Foyle to counter increasing tensions between communities.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps he is taking to implement the findings of the UK fan-led survey of music.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on her plans to review the Taxis Act (Northern Ireland) 2008.
answer_text: As you are aware I have recently asked officials to undertake a phased review of taxi policy and legislation, commencing with a review of the Class C taxi licensing system. To help understand the views of the taxi industry, in relation to the Class C taxi licensing system, I have agreed to meet with a number of key stakeholders over the coming weeks, including representatives from the wedding car industry, funeral directors and associated businesses, private hire operators working in Class C and private hire operators working in Class A. My Officials are also planning on gathering information from across all four taxi classes via survey sampling, to help inform policy development for this review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans he has to support grassroots music venues.
answer_text: My Department supports the existence and growth of grassroots music venues primarily through the funding distributed via my Department’s Arm’s Length Body, the Arts Council of Northern Ireland.  I wrote to the Arts Council setting out my expectations for 2025/26 and emphasised the importance of working with district councils and others to make the maximum possible impact with finite funding that is available.  I have also written to local councils, and I expect the needs of grassroots music venues will form part of the upcoming discussions.A recent UK fan-led survey of music provided views on the importance of grass roots venues.  My officials have reviewed the findings as part of the development phase of a new arts policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 21904/22-27.

To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the return of the Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme; and (ii) whether he has requested funding from his Executive colleagues for this initiative.
answer_text: As outlined in my statement to the Assembly, I plan to establish a new Waiting List Reduction Reimbursement Scheme, as part of a package of initiatives to tackle hospital care backlogs.The Scheme will commence in June 2025 and will give patients, who are ordinarily resident in Northern Ireland, the option to seek and pay for treatment in the private sector and have the cost reimbursed, up to the cost of the treatment to HSC in Northern Ireland.  Patients will only be able to access the Scheme once they have waited on an HSC hospital treatment waiting list for two years.The new Scheme will be initiated on a phased approach.  The first phase will allow reimbursements for treatment in the Republic of Ireland (RoI). The second phase will be introduced at a later date and will see the geographical scope of the Scheme expanded to all countries in the European Union (EU).An initial £10m will be invested in the Scheme for this financial year.  It is estimated that approximately 1,400 patients could benefit from this investment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Anti-Poverty Strategy.
answer_text: Following on from the Executive’s approval of the draft Anti-Poverty Strategy on 15 May, the public consultation has now launched.Final decisions on the content and implementation of the Anti-Poverty Strategy will be subject to Executive agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any efforts being made to (i) address delays with autism diagnosis; and (ii) increase the numbers of staff within the Western Health and Social Care Trust's Autism Services.
answer_text: Waiting lists for autism assessment remain a challenge across the region. My Department acknowledges that this is not acceptable and to better understand pressures from a regional and whole system perspective, recently recruited a project lead for Adult Autism (March 2025) to take forward regional work to improve the management of waiting lists. Currently the lead is undertaking scoping and engagement work with Health and Social Care Trusts, community and voluntary organisations and service users/carers to map existing diagnostic pathways and processes before co-developing an action plan to help address regional variation in service delivery.Children’s Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) assessment and diagnostic pathways and interventions will be addressed through the implementation of the HSC Children’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework. The Framework is due to go out for public consultation in the coming weeks. Departmental officials are working with colleagues from the Public Health Agency and Child Health to develop a regionally consistent and defined neurodevelopmental pathway for children and young people that complies with National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) guidelines. WHSCT Adult Services reports that the demand for adult autism assessments has greatly exceeded their capacity in recent years. The Trust has capacity for about 60 new assessments per year, but demand has been three times higher than this. The service is continually making efforts to reduce delays including contracting extra staff when possible, effectively triaging all referrals received and streamlining processes. Unfortunately, despite these best efforts, impact has been limited due to underlying resource constraints. WHSCT Children's Services report that since 2016, the demand for autism assessments for children and young people has been much higher than the service's capacity. The Trust can conduct 420 assessments per year, but received 950 referrals in the period April 2024 to March 2025. WHSCT advise that more investment is needed to reduce delays in autism diagnosis.WHSCT Adult Services has reviewed the staff complement needed to meet the growing demand for adult autism assessments and has submitted a business case to my Department regarding its significant demand/ capacity challenges. Commissioners have advised that in the current financial constraints funding is not currently available. Within the Adult Learning Disability pathway, WHSCT have employed one Speech and Language Therapist and are actively recruiting one Clinical Psychologist to help ensure individuals with intellectual disability and suspected autism can receive a timely diagnosis. WHSCT Children's Autism Service has consistently highlighted the significant growth in demand at the monthly Heads of Service meetings with Commissioners. Commissioners have advised that in the current financial constraints funding is not currently available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 21030/22-27, whether a further structural survey has been conducted on the Strand Road, Derry.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the areas impacted by the various phases of the Housing Executive's Creggan Heights Multi-Elemental Improvement project; (ii) the number of properties impacted; (iii) the estimated time frame for completion of this project; and (iv) any impact this project will have on the delivery of home adaptations under the Disabled Facilities Grant within Creggan.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Please see table below showing (i) The areas impacted by the various phases of the Housing Executive’s Creggan Heights Multi-Elemental Improvement project (ii) The number of properties impacted and (iii) The estimated time frame for completion of this projectSchemeNr of DwellingsProgramme YearEstimated time for completionPhase 1 – Creggan MEI (Heights)10825/2628/29Phase 2 -Creggan MEI (Rinmore)6227/2829/30Phase 3 -Westway, Lower Creggan4328/2930/31Lone Moor Road Water Ingress3330/3131/32It should be noted that the timing of the Rinmore, Westway and Lone Moor schemes is indicative at this stage and subject to our annual programme roll-on exercises, the availability of finance, the necessary scheme approvals being obtained, and the appropriate contractor capacity being available to carry out the works.(iv) Any impact this project will have on the delivery of home adaption under the Disabled Facilities Grant within Creggan We don’t foresee Disabled Facilities Grants being impacted by our works. Disabled Facilities Grants applications for privately owned properties within these areas are administered on an individual basis when referred to the Housing Executive from the WHSCT. We assess the technical feasibility of each property based on the OT request and the available space and archetypeRegarding the impact on major adaptations to NIHE owned properties within the areas, following a review by NIHE Asset Management and Landlord Services, we would advise that it is not viable to proceed with the majority of our major adaptation works/ recommendations which require extensions and/or significant alterations for properties affected by the various phases of the Creggan MEI projects. Assessment of remedial works is required to address a range of issues associated with the physical condition of NIHE properties in the identified areas. These assessments will be completed by upcoming Multi Element Improvement schemes in various phases of MEI works. Unfortunately, until such time as each of these phases is complete, the majority of our major adaptations have been put on hold.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the Dunluce Family Centre, Ballymagroarty, Derry, will receive support from the Pathway Fund in the 2025-26 financial year.
answer_text: Early Years (EYO) which administers the Pathway Fund on behalf of the Department, advises that notification was issued to Dunluce Family Centre on 11 April 2025, confirming an award from the Pathway Fund for 2025-26.  The setting has benefitted from the increase I have made to the maximum Stream A award from £30,000 to £40,000 in 2025-26.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether staff in the Western Division are getting overtime or additional hours to carry out pothole repair works outside of peak traffic times.
answer_text: Work activities, such as the repair of potholes, are typically carried out during normal working hours. However, there are occasions when this is not practicable as it would endanger the health and safety of our staff.  In these circumstances, where activities during the normal working day may expose our staff to increased risk, works may be scheduled during off-peak hours, evenings, or weekends to minimise this risk and ensure a safer working environmentOur records indicate that circa. 1,900 hours of overtime were completed on this activity across the 7 Roads depots within Western Division.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 25762/22-27, whether an indicative time frame for adoption can be provided.
answer_text: The adoption of sites is a developer-led process requiring the developer to submit a request for a preliminary certificate.  My officials continue to work with the developer of Barleyfields to ensure the site is bought to an adoptable standard suitable for the issue of a preliminary certificate.Once the site is completed to an adoptable standard and a preliminary certificate issued there will be a 12 month maintenance period before final adoption.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the estimated funding required to bring Northern Ireland’s wastewater infrastructure up to the required standard that will protect the environment and support economic development.
answer_text: NI Water’s funding requirements is subject to regulation and is defined in the Utility Regulator’s Price Control process. The current Price Control is PC21, covering the investment period of 2021-28. The Utility Regulator has determined that NI Water requires capital funding of £2.4bn for the full price control period, to maintain existing services and deliver the upgrades required to meet its quality, environmental and service level obligations. I will continue to work with my Executive colleagues to secure investment for the delivery of essential water and wastewater services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to estimate how much of the current £800 million funding shortfall, identified by NI Water during the current price control period, will be addressed by her Department’s three pronged approach to the issue.
answer_text: The three-pronged approach is ongoing as I (i) continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment for NI Water, (ii) bring forward new legislation in relation to sustainable drainage systems (SuDS), and (iii) examine the changes required to bring forward developer contributions. While the outcomes are subject to further consultation and budget decisions, I am confident that this approach will yield positive outcomes. It should be noted that £379m of the £800m figure submitted by NI Water is described as the impact of Real Price Effects (RPE) which is the additional inflationary impact applied in the supply of services and skills to NI Water.  The Utility Regulator did not include NI Water’s RPE in their final mid-term review determination, but instead is undertaking some further work  to establish why some of NI Water’s capital costs are higher than predicted, despite the lack of evidence for cost pressures above inflation.
