question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any support services in place for individuals struggling with heroin addiction in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: My Department and the Public Health Agency (PHA) currently commission/fund a wide range of substance use services focused on meeting the needs of children, young people, adults and families/carers which are available in each HSC Trust area across Northern Ireland. These services are provided by the community, voluntary and statutory sectors and span all 4 tiers of substance use provision including prevention, early intervention, assessment, treatment and recovery services. They are underpinned by key policy drivers such as the Substance Use Strategy, Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery 2021-31, the HSC Substance Use Strategic Commissioning and Implementation Plan 2024-2028, the Mental Health Strategy 2021-31, and Protect Life 2.All HSC Trust areas provide specialist support and treatment to those with heroin addiction.. This support is provided via the Community Addiction Teams (CAT) and Opiate Substitution Treatment (OST) Teams. This includes people who are in prison where CAT and OST services are provided by South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.Following assessment and in collaboration with the patient, these specialist services may prescribe an opiate replacement to stabilise the patient and prevent harm to their health and well-being. The CAT and OST teams in Trusts can prescribe directly, and in conjunction with a community pharmacist, they ensure supervised dispensing of replacement opiates. A detoxification service is provided where indicated.Additional services available to patients in HSC Trust areas in partnership with the PHA and the community and voluntary sector include: Harm reduction programs, including the administration of Naloxone pens, needle and syringe exchange services Blood borne virus screening and immunisationMotivational interventions  Mental health interventions and co-working for people with co-occurring mental health and substance use issues  An Inclusion Health Nurse led service is also available for individuals who are experiencing homelessness. This service is available to individuals living in hostel accommodation and actively street sleeping.  The service works to improve the health and health outcomes of those experiencing homelessness, and using substances, including heroin.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any steps her Department is taking to stem the overspend on major capital projects; and (ii) any engagement she has had with Executive colleagues in relation to the delivery of an infrastructure commission.
answer_text: I am reassured to note that my department already possesses a great deal of expertise in its delivery of major infrastructure schemes and is working collaboratively with other departments and the Strategic Investment Board in preparation for the agreement of an Investment Strategy (ISNI).This Strategy will determine the capital projects my department will take forward over the ten years of the plan, and will include a range of 12 enabling actions designed to improve our ability to plan for, procure and deliver these schemes.My department has also established a Major Projects Committee to strengthen project assurance and oversight of the major capital projects being delivery across the department and its Arm’s Length Bodies.  In support of this Committee, and to promote best practice and guidance in programme and project delivery, my department has a dedicated Portfolio, Programme and Project Office (P3O).I recognise the benefits of a more holistic approach to infrastructure planning and am committed to working with my Executive colleagues to agree a way forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the total estimated cost savings resulting from the 2015 local government reform, which reduced the number of councils from 26 to 11.
answer_text: On 7 November 2024, my Department published the report: - “Impact of local government reform on service delivery and cost effectiveness in Northern Ireland”(https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/impact-local-government-reform-service-delivery-and-cost-effectiveness). The report identified savings of circa £21.5m, however, this did not cover all the savings made due to difficulties obtaining supporting data. The review period reflects the initial seven-year period since the formation of the 11 new councils.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 23806/22-27, to detail how much has been spent on this provision to date.
answer_text: To date, payments totalling £17.8 million have been made to people eligible for the Pension Age Fuel Support Payment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the total number of Houses in Multiple Occupation (HMOs); and (ii) the number of Fixed Penalty Notices issued to unlicensed HMOs in each of the last three years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: According to the Houses in Multiple Occupation (HMO) Unit, operated by Belfast City Council on behalf of all councils in NI, there are 3542 licensed HMOs in NI. This figure is provided as of 31 March 2025. The HMO Unit does not record information on Fixed Penalty Notices (FPNs) by constituency. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for her assessment of the accuracy of existing flood maps; and (ii) to detail when flood maps were last updated, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Detailed flood hazard and risk maps for rivers and coastal areas have been produced using the best available modelling techniques, tools and data at the time of creation. These maps are published at a scale of 1 in 5,000 and are available here: Flood Maps NI | Department for Infrastructure.However, any changes in local topography, new developments, drainage infrastructure or shifts in waterways over time may lead to differences in estimated flood risk.  Therefore, flood maps are reviewed, and if necessary, updated as required on a six-year rolling programme. They can also be updated periodically following any significant changes, for example, after the construction of a new flood alleviation scheme. Details of updates can be viewed on the terms and conditions screen of the Flood Maps NI viewer which is available at this link: Flood Maps (NI). We do not hold detail of updates broken down by constituency.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the potential adoption of the Barleyfields site, Culmore, Derry, given that the developer, Taggart Homes, is no longer in operation.
answer_text: I can confirm that the Department holds road bonds for the Barleyfields development and that construction of the remaining parts of the site are progressing well.My officials will continue to liaise with the developer, Kania Developments Limited & Luham Developments Limited, to progress the adoption of the Barleyfields site.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of (i) full-time; and (ii) part-time nursery admissions granted in the last five years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: A copy of the table containing the information requested has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he intends to (i) review admissions criteria for pre-school and primary school applications; and (ii) increase the number of available places.
answer_text: Pre-school and primary school admissions criteria are set by individual settings, taking into account the relevant legislation and guidance provided by my Department.The EA has advised that at this stage in the pre-school admissions process, 99.9% of primary children and 97% of pre-school target aged children whose parents applied for a place have already received an offer of a funded place in a setting of their preference.The EA has further advised that sufficient pre-school places are available for those children who have not yet received the offer of a funded place in a setting of their parents’ preference.  With the small number of primary applicants still to secure a place, sufficient capacity also remains available for these children.The pre-school admissions process has two stages and stage two remains ongoing at this time.  I would encourage parents of children who have not yet received an offer of a funded place to engage with the admissions process to the end and provide further preferences when invited to do so in order to maximise the chances of their child being offered a place in one of their preferred settings.  Should additional funded pre-school places be required in local areas to meet demand, the EA will adjust allocations to settings accordingly.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they will consider extending the opening hours for the public toilets at the Ebrington site.
answer_text: The current opening hours continue to ensure that the public toilets are still available during peak usage times.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 24473/22-27, whether there are any planned increases of the mileage allowance for domiciliary care staff employed through an agency.
answer_text: As set out in the response to AQW 24473/22-27, no revision is planned to the current rates for mileage for any staff groups travelling to deliver care on behalf of HSC.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of admissions for (a) pre-schools; (b) primary schools, in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency; and (ii) how many of these were deemed socially disadvantaged.
answer_text: A copy of the table containing the information requested has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any urgent measures being taken to improve bus safety for school children; and (ii) any engagement she has had with the Minister of Education in relation to safety improvements.
answer_text: I am committed to considering any initiatives that can improve road safety, which is a priority for me as Minister.I have asked my officials to look at all the options that may be available to improve safety for school children and to report back to me as a matter of urgency; following which I will decide on next steps. I have also asked my officials to reinforce the messaging to help keep all road users safe, especially the vulnerable.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, given the recent findings of the Northern Ireland Audit Office report highlighting a £3 billion overspend on major projects, to detail the immediate steps they will take to expedite the delivery of an Infrastructure Commission.
answer_text: TEO officials are currently considering the Public Accounts Committee (PAC) report published on 3rd April, and we note that the updated NIAO report highlights a significant overspend on major capital projects. Work is already underway to begin to address the challenges and tackle the causes of delays in major projects.An Infrastructure Commission is one possible mechanism for long term planning and contributing to discussion of how we address persistent infrastructure challenges. We are awaiting further advice from officials and any decisions will be a matter for the Executive in due course.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide an update on any progress to establish an Infrastructure Commission, following the creation of a cross-departmental working group in early 2022.
answer_text: A draft paper was produced by the inter-departmental working group in early 2022 which set out an initial assessment of options.  The working group was then stood down.Officials are reviewing this assessment alongside other developments and will provide us with advice on the next steps.  Decisions on the way forward will be a matter for the Executive in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any urgent measures being taken to improve bus safety for school children; and (ii) any engagement he has had with the Minister for Infrastructure in relation to safety improvements.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the operation and delivery of home to school transport and it engages regularly with Translink on a range of public transport safety initiatives, including its safety bus that visits schools across Northern Ireland. The EA already has a number of safety measures in place on its own yellow bus fleet, including special lighting on all EA school buses which alert other road users to exercise caution as school children may be alighting or disembarking the vehicle. I am giving careful consideration to school transport policy and operations in general and exploring the need for a review in some areas. This includes exploring any measures that my Department or the EA could introduce to improve bus safety for school children. Whilst road safety is within the remit of the Department for Infrastructure (DfI), I fully support a cross-departmental approach on developing any measures which would improve the safety of children on our roads and I completely endorse DfI’s current road safety community campaign ‘Share the Road to Zero’. The responsibility for road traffic legislation and the specialist expertise required to develop new road safety measures rests with DfI. My officials have been liaising with DfI on this issue and I have asked that they work closely together and provide any support needed in the development of options to improve road safety for school children.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the Education Authority intends to assume responsibility for the laneway which provides access to the nursery at Rosemount Primary School, Derry.
answer_text: To enable the Department to pay capital grant-aid for works on a school premises, the premises must be vested. The laneway which provides access to the nursery at Rosemount Primary School is not currently vested. Officials have been engaging with the Departmental Solicitor’s Office and the school trustees’ solicitor in an attempt to ascertain who owns the laneway. This land is unregistered, so it is proving to be a complex and time consuming process. Officials will continue to engage with the solicitors and the school in an attempt to bring this matter to a conclusion.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department intends to fund the Foyle Cup Tournament 2025.
answer_text: Responsibility for tourism event funding and support is undertaken by Tourism NI. Applications to Tourism NI’s International Tourism Events Fund (ITEF) 2025-26 scheme have been through a robust assessment and approval process.  The results of the application process will be published shortly.

answer_text: The Regional Government Headquarters is a scheduled historic monument considered worthy of protection under Section 3 of the Historic Monument and Archaeological Objects (NI) Order 1995.The following tables provide a breakdown of the costs incurred in terms of protecting and maintaining the premises since April 2022: April 2022 – March 2023Description CostElectricity£9,937.00Maintenance / Repairs£12,800.79Flood Repairs£30,716.00TOTAL SPEND  £53,453.79April 2023 – March 2024Description CostTravel£240.25Electricity  £8,466.02Maintenance / Repairs£4,094.13CCTV Camera£78.00CPD Fees (Flood Repair)£5,082.30Decontamination Works  £224,730.72TOTAL SPEND£242,691.42April 2024 – 13 March 2025Description CostTravel£282.50Electricity£7,134.51Maintenance / Repairs£6,062.99CCTV Camera£71.50CPD Fees (Flood Repair) £1,159.89TOTAL SPEND£14,711.39

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any steps being taken to prevent problem parking at Ebrington Square to ensure the safety of pedestrians.
answer_text: The Department is delighted that local people are enjoying Ebrington and coming to the site to work, exercise, or dine in one of the many businesses. We want to ensure the site remains accessible for local people and there is a large car park just a few steps from the main square which can be used by the public while they visit.We do continue to encourage the public to park and drive responsibly and to assist us in ensuring Ebrington remains accessible and safe for all users we have put in place speed ramps and signage to enhance safety. Further measures will be put in place in the coming weeks and months. We ask that all those using the site assist us in keeping the site safe for all users by parking responsibly in the designated areas, driving at 5mph and with hazard lights on, and leaving the drop off and blue badge spaces for those who need them most.Ebrington is also accessible by public transport, walking or cycling and we would encourage those visiting the site to use sustainable travel if possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail her Department's process for prioritising funding for road and footpath maintenance and resurfacing schemes, including (i) which constituency areas are given priority; and (ii) whether specific criteria is in place.
answer_text: In distributing the resources available for road repairs and resurfacing programmes, allocations relating to the areas covered by the Department’s four Roads Divisions have historically been made on the basis of need, using a range of weighted indicators tailored to each maintenance activity.  The Divisions have also used a range of indicators when apportioning budget across District Council areas to ensure, as far as possible, an equitable distribution of funds according to need.The well-discussed funding shortfall, and the years of Limited Service, have seen the condition of our roads deteriorate and its time we tried something different. That’s why a new approach to road maintenance is one of my Foundations.We need a new approach to how the road network is maintained, one that focuses our limited resources in a way that balances safety and value for money. It should be data driven and supported by sound engineering judgement and address the problem in a meaningful and sustainable manner.My officials are currently working on a new Road Maintenance Strategy that I will be considering in detail in the coming weeks. We will focus on delivering higher-quality repairs instead of spreading resources too thinly, ensuring a more reliable and safer road network where we intervene.We will use modern technology to direct our investments where they are needed most, ensuring smarter maintenance decisions and will future proof our roads to meet the changing needs of our society including active travel, reducing the carbon footprint of our operations, and ensuring that the network is resilient after adverse weather events.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the (i) processes; and (ii) safeguards in place to ensure individuals with a learning disability can access social housing.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The needs of some applicants who approach the Housing Executive, such as those with a learning disability or those with intense care and/or support needs (‘Complex Needs’) caused by some other condition, may be such that general needs accommodation alone will not be adequate for their needs. In addition to obtaining permanent accommodation, these applicants also need to have their care and support needs addressed as part of any housing solution. Applicants, who may be potential Complex Needs (CN) applicants, will be referred directly from Health and Social Services or may be identified following a housing needs assessment. Each of these cases needs to be assessed on its own merits, under a multi-agency approach between housing and Health and Social Services professionals to reach an agreement on the most suitable re-housing options for them, with the best interests of the individual in mind. There are two main options for these customers. Supported Housing Examples of CN applicants who require the supported housing option may include persons with severe learning difficulties, frail elderly persons, and persons with chronic debilitating mental health conditions; including those who lack capacity. Participating Landlords (housing associations) owning supported housing schemes provide not only accommodation to tenants with complex needs but also, directly or indirectly, the intensive care and support that these tenants require within a supported housing environment. The day-to-day management of supported housing schemes and the delivery of care and support to residents may be undertaken the landlord or by a third party with whom the landlord has entered into a management agreement. CN applicants who require supported housing will be considered without reference to points and apart from the general needs waiting list. When suitable supported accommodation becomes available, and it is determined by the relevant housing association and its joint management partner that vacancy is suitable for the applicant they will be offered a place in the scheme. 2) General Needs Accommodation Where supported housing is not required, general needs accommodation may be a suitable re-housing option for some CN applicants. Given the intensive care and support needs that these applicants will have, general needs accommodation will normally only be suited to those in receipt of an agreed, tailored care package, available and delivered to them by Health and Social Services at the time of any allocation. Applicants must comply with usual application requirements of Housing Selection Scheme (HSS) and allocations of general needs dwellings are also made under HSS rules. Applicants wishing to avail of general needs accommodation must have capacity to make a housing/homelessness application and enter in

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he intends to introduce legislation similar to Awaab's Law.
answer_text: I refer the Member to my previous answer on this topic in AQW 22292/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the publication of an anti-poverty strategy.
answer_text: I have shared an Executive Paper including a draft Anti-Poverty Strategy with Executive colleagues for them to consider.	Final decisions on the content and implementation of the Anti-Poverty Strategy will be subject to Executive agreement and subject to public consultation prior to publication of the final draft.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance when successful applicants to the recent Civil Service Administrative Officer recruitment competition will be notified.
answer_text: ANSWERWe are currently at the final stages of compiling the merit list and are planning to contact candidates with results this month.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there are any plans to ensure the Translink concession passes includes discounts on return tickets, given that it currently only applies to half-price single fares, which may limit its usefulness for regular commuters.
answer_text: Since the inception of free travel under the Concessionary Fares Scheme in 2002 it has been the case that SmartPass holders must obtain a single ticket for each leg of their journey by bus or train.  However, I am keen to look into this and have asked my officials to do so.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any steps being taken to ensure all government departments have stronger financial oversight of arm’-length bodies, such as NI Water and the Housing Executive, to prevent budget overruns and improve transparency in the use of public funds.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Department of Finance has published several pieces of guidance which provide advice to departments around how to manage their relationship with Arm’s Length Bodies, including the Code of Good Practice for Partnerships between Departments and Arm’s Length Bodies. In addition, the Department of Finance monitors the budget position of each department on an ongoing basis through the monthly outturn and forecast outturn exercise. It is for each Minister and Permanent Secretary, as accounting officer, to ensure that the budget of their department, including any Arm’s Length Bodies, is managed in line with the allocations agreed by the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the projected total cost of the forensic investigation into NI Water's £3 million overspend; and (ii) the expected time frame for the completion and publication of its findings.
answer_text: The expected timeframe for completion of the investigation, as outlined in my oral statement, is six weeks, and the findings will be published as soon as possible thereafter. The cost of the investigation is withheld as disclosure would be likely to prejudice the commercial interests of the external provider carrying out the investigation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 24042/22-27, for her assessment of the impact of delays in the delivery of the Living with Water Programme.
answer_text: The 12-year delivery timescale set out in the published Living With Water in Belfast Plan is no longer achievable due to NI Water’s elements of the Plan increasing by over 50% within 2 years of publication. This means that the delivery of the social, environmental and economic benefits anticipated from the Plan will take longer to achieve than originally estimated, and the pace will be determined by budget availability. It is for NI Water to identify which of its schemes in the Plan can be taken forward within its budget allocation, alongside other projects. At this time, NI Water has taken the decision to pause work on the major upgrades to the wastewater infrastructure around Belfast Lough. I remain committed to achieving the objectives of the Belfast Plan to develop and implement integrated drainage solutions, including blue/green infrastructure, and will continue to make the case for additional funding for water and wastewater services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline any engagement he has had with the Minister for Infrastructure regarding a potential overspend by NI Water.

To ask the Minister of Education, further to the publication in 2022 of a Queen’s University of Belfast research paper, Is academic selection in Northern Ireland a barrier to social cohesion, to outline whether his Department will take any action to abolish academic selection.
To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the future of the Collon Terrace Housing Executive office and its staff.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive of the Housing Executive, who has advised me as follows.“Through the Housing Executive’s Customer Outlet and Workspace Strategy, the Housing Executive is reviewing all its accommodation requirements across Northern Ireland in a structured manner between 2023/24 and 2028/29.How customers interact with the Housing Executive has changed significantly over recent years, with footfall figures falling by circa 70% over 10 years. Some offices are old, inefficient and no longer fit for purpose.  The Housing Executive need to ensure that it continues to meet the needs of tenants, customers and people, while making the best use of tenant and public monies. In June 2023 the Housing Executive successfully consolidated its Lanyon and Adelaide Street offices in Belfast and is currently finalising plans for its South Region. The Housing Executive have now developed plans to consolidate the four offices in Derry/Londonderry into one central city centre location at Carlisle House. This includes the Collon Terrace office, which will also be impacted by the planned Buncrana Road upgrade.The Housing Executive believes that Carlisle House best meets its needs, provides good access for tenants, customers and staff and enables it to bring all of its people and services together in one place within the City Centre. A consultation exercise concerning these plans is currently underway.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly with regard to matters that are their responsibility. Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 12079/22-27, for an update on the work of the Transitions Working Group to improve pathways for young people aged over 19 years old with special educational needs into further education or employment, including the outcome of the survey of practitioners and parents.
answer_text: The Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Delivery Plan sets out a wide range of actions proposed to transform SEN provision in Northern Ireland and to provide better support for children and young people with SEN. This includes a specific action in relation to transition out of education into adulthood which is a result of the cross departmental Transitions Working Group (TWG).As part of the annual review of the young person’s statement of special educational needs, school leaders will create a transitions plan and update this every year from Year 10 in preparation for the young person leaving school. Stakeholders along with the young person and their parent or carer have the opportunity to contribute to this process.Stakeholders may include health and social care services, careers service, educational psychology and Education Authority as appropriate. Depending on the young person’s individual circumstances opportunities such as visits, and open days are identified to support or ascertain appropriate pathways.To better understand how the transition process works in practice, the TWG ran a survey from 31 January until 13 March 2024, 241 responses were received. The survey was supplemented by further engagement with young people going through the transition process; with parents and carers via a series of workshops; and, through a number of schools visits and with service providers who provide employment and training opportunities for young people with SEN when they leave school. The survey and engagement sessions identified a number of areas for improvement within the transition process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 12620/22-27, for an update on actions to improve post-19 special educational needs provision.
answer_text: The previous Economy Minister tasked officials with reviewing the skills and training provision for school leavers with special educational needs, and the relevant legislative protections, to provide recommendations for improvements.  My officials have taken a twin track approach to the work. They have carried out a desk-based review of a range of relevant reports, research, reviews and recommendations, as well as commissioning a piece of comparative work from Stranmillis University College on the different legislative arrangements across the Britain and Ireland.  In parallel, they have undertaken more than 80 meetings and engagements with stakeholders including schools, parents, young people, colleges, training providers, voluntary and community sector, social enterprises, representative organisations and counterparts from other jurisdictions.  This work is being drawn to a close now and my officials will report to me in shortly.  Once I have had an opportunity to consider their findings and advice, I expect I will bring the issues to the Executive for an informed discussion on the next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, following the announcement of £2 billion for social and affordable housing from the UK Government, to detail (i) the Barnett consequentials for Northern Ireland; and (ii) whether this funding will be ring-fenced for housing.
answer_text: The Executive will receive Barnet consequentials on any additional funding provided to Whitehall department because of this announcement. These will be confirmed as part of the Spending Review, which is due to be announced in June.Since consequentials provided at a Spending Review are identified at the total Whitehall departmental level rather than individual programmes, it will not be possible to specify how much this announcement has provided.Barnett consequentials are unhypothecated and it will be for the Executive to agree how this funding is allocated in line with current needs and priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any research conducted by his Department in relation to the link between lower literacy levels and areas of high deprivation.
answer_text: In September 2020, my Department commissioned an expert panel to produce the A Fair Start – Final Report & Action Plan. However, the expert panel was appointed to examine the links between educational underachievement and socio-economic background more broadly, rather than to consider the specific issue of links between lower literacy and areas of high deprivation. My Department has also commissioned Stranmillis University College to produce an independent evaluation of the Extended Schools Programme, which aims to improve levels of educational achievement for disadvantaged children and young people by providing the additional support that they might need to help them reach their full potential. The evaluation includes an examination of international best practice in relation to tackling educational disadvantage and will conclude in September 2025.I have committed, within the TransformED Northern Ireland Strategy, to the development of a new literacy strategy. It is essential that this new strategy is developed and based on international best practice, informed by the needs of pupils in Northern Ireland and that it builds on examples of current effective delivery in our local schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the average literacy levels at the end of Key Stage 2 for (i) boys; and (ii) girls, for each of the last seven years.
answer_text: Regrettably, our system has limited data on how our children are performing in literacy. End of Key Stage assessment results were submitted for around 17% of primary school pupils in 2017-18 and around 14% in 2018-19 due to industrial action short of strike. These outcomes were then weighted to account for non-response bias. The best statistical estimate is that 73.4 percent of boys and 82.2 percent of girls achieved the expected Level 4 or above in Communication in 2017 -18. In 2018-19, the best statistical estimate is that 74.2 percent of boys and 83.4 percent of girls achieved the expected level or above in Communication. End of Key Stage assessment arrangements were not operational from 2019-20 to 2023-24.  Pupils in Northern Ireland also participate in the Progress in International Reading Literacy Study (PIRLS) which assesses Key Stage 2 pupils in reading every five years. This is a study of Year 6 pupils’ attainment in reading. Pupils in Northern Ireland performed very well in the last study in 2021 significantly outperforming those in 52 of the 56 other PIRLS participating countries and being significantly outperformed by pupils in only two other countries. In PIRLS 2021, the average reading score for girls (578) was statistically significantly higher than the score for boys (553). This was also the case in the previous cycle in 2016 when the average score for girls was 574 and the average score for boys was 555.The study demonstrates that girls have continuously performed significantly better than boys and that the gender gap has increased over time, driven by a steady increase in girls’ average attainment. I have introduced a new system level check in literacy and numeracy from the 2025-26 academic year to ensure we have robust and accurate annual information on our pupils’ literacy levels at the end of each Key Stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail all planned maintenance schemes for Housing Executive properties, including home adaptations, scheduled for the Creggan area, Derry, in the next five years.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 25030/ 22-27, the information has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when planned resurfacing works at Crescent Link, Derry, will be carried out.
answer_text: The planned resurfacing work on the A514 Crescent Link, Derry from the Kilfennan Link Roundabout to the junction with Knightbridge is programmed to commence on 7th April 2025 and be completed by 11th April 2025.The scheme will be undertaken at night to minimise disruption to traffic. Works will commence at 7pm each evening and the road reopened to traffic from 7am each morning.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any counselling or support services in place within each Health and Social Care Trust for staff, especially those who worked during the Covid pandemic.
answer_text: All HSC Trusts currently provide a range of health and wellbeing services and resources that are available to all staff.  Services include the provision of telephone support helplines and counselling provided through Employee Assistance Programmes and on-line resources to support healthy bodies and minds.  Where further support for staff is required, including psychological support, this is also available through referral to HSC Trust Occupational Health Services.  Support for issues arising specifically from working during the Covid pandemic are provided through these services.  To further support this provision, I launched ‘Strengthening our Core: A Regional Framework for HSC Staff Health and Wellbeing in the Workplace’ on 11 September 2024 which is a valuable resource that assists HSC Trusts in their planning and implementation of effective processes and resources for improving staff health, wellbeing and safety at work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of days the Rosebud Short Break Unit (i) faced temporary closure; and (ii) was unable to function at full capacity in the last three years.
answer_text: Rosebud Short breaks unit in the Western Trust is commissioned for 4 short break beds, the unit operates on a prioritisation of needs basis and there are often times when bed occupancy is significantly reduced due to the complexity of the young people availing of short breaks.  Some of these young people require a higher ratio of staffing or singular occupancy stays (i.e. no other children in the unit at the same time). Therefore, to measure against full capacity can often be misleading.YearPeriods of Closure2022Closed 22nd October 2021 – 10th August 2022 – due to the need to accommodate two separate medium to long term complex placements Reopened part time on August 2022Reopened full time on November 20222023Reduced Short Break capacity 12th September 2023 – 26th September 2023 to accommodate an emergency placement for Young Person (A) with complex needsReduced Short Break capacity 28th September 2023 -18th October 2023 to accommodate the same Young person with complex needsReduced Short Break capacity 27th October 2023 – 6th November 2023 to accommodate the same Young Person with complex needsReduced Short Break capacity 23rd November 2023 – 27th December 2023 to accommodate the same Young Person with complex needs2024From 4th January 2024 – continued reduced short break capacity due to need to accommodate the same Young Person (A) with complex needs for 4 nights every week. Young Person (B) admitted to Rosebud on Medium/Long Term basis effective 5th April 2024 – Reduced capacity continuedYoung Person (A) admitted to Rosebud on M/LT basis effective 19th April 2024 – further reduction in capacityYoung Person (C) admitted to Rosebud on M/LT basis effective 1st August 2024.   At this point Short break provision was fully suspended.Rosebud reopened on a part time on 2nd December 2024 following the transition of YP (A,B &C) to Jasmine Lodge CWD Residential Unit2025Rosebud continues to operate on a part time basis due to staffing pressures however the unit has incrementally increased provision since December and will be open 6 days per week by early May 2025.  It is envisaged the service will be fully operational by end of June 2025Planned Admission levelsFebruary 2025– 18 overnights delivered March 2025 – 30 overnights will be delivered April 2025 – 46 overnights May 2025– 62 overnights – 340% planned increase on current provision

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the adoption of Ivy Mead, Derry.
answer_text: My Department has recently been informed by NIW that the sewer/storm infrastructure is suitable for adoption.Officials are working with the developer to resolve any outstanding defects on the roads and footpaths. This work is ongoing and should be completed in the coming weeks.Once the defects are repaired the developer will be required to provide drawings and make a formal request for adoption. The adoption of Ivy Mead Mews will then be processed.It should be noted that Ivy Mead Close, which is at the end of Ivy Mead and under a separate bond, has a service strip infringement which prevents adoption taking place until it is removed.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they will consider the inclusion of Ashfield Terrace and Kerrs Terrace, Creggan Road, Derry, in the Urban Villages Initiative.
answer_text: We acknowledge the significant impact the Urban Villages programme has had in the North West. Successful delivery of the remaining capital projects will increase access to shared space facilities and continue to promote integration. To date, Urban Villages has focused on the Bogside, Fountain, and Bishop Street areas.The successor programme to Urban Villages will take account of lessons learned from current Urban Village areas as well as the learning and best practice from the Collaboration Test and Learn pilots. We look forward to updating Members once future Urban Villages areas have been identified.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any provisions in place to support prospective social tenants who have limited capacity to sign a tenancy agreement.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will consider introducing a shared-use path along Alder Road, Derry, to facilitate safer pedestrian and active travel to Foyle Golf Centre.
answer_text: My department is currently updating its active travel design guidance. This work includes development of a route assessment framework for Active Travel schemes and my officials will assess this route once that framework is fully developed and implemented. The new assessment framework will place a greater emphasis on the potential to encourage modal shift, including consideration of the population being served, the places it will connect to, and the level of improvement between the existing and proposed provision.Until the route is assessed under the emerging Active Travel Route Assessment it is not possible to give an indication of where it might rank in terms of prioritisation for investment. I expect this assessment to undertaken within the next 6 months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) when the Mental Capacity Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 will be implemented in full; and (ii) when provisions which fall under housing and tenancy agreements will be introduced.
answer_text: (i) The Mental Capacity Act (NI) 2016 (MCA) was brought forward by the Department of Health and the Department of Justice and has been implemented in a phased approach, with provisions relating to deprivation of liberty, research and money and valuables commenced to date. There are currently no indicative timings for full commencement of the MCA.  However, my Department is currently working collaboratively with Department of Justice officials to develop an updated implementation plan to better understand the resource need for full MCA implementation, which will inform Departmental decision making. (ii) The Department is not aware of MCA provisions falling under housing and tenancy agreements. Parts 5, 6 & 7 of the MCA relate to how property can be dealt with for people who lack capacity, which could include the legal interest that a tenant has in a rented property. Like other provisions, there is no indicative timeframe for commencement of these Parts.

To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) whether Certificates of Unenforceability can be issued in Northern Ireland in cases where a seller has knowingly sold a defective vehicle; and (ii) the protections in place for consumers.
answer_text: (i) An application for a Certificate of Unenforceability may be made after the Enforcement of Judgments Office has completed an assessment of the debtor’s current financial position and determined that no further enforcement orders can be made.Prior to the issue of a Certificate of Unenforceability, the Enforcement of Judgments Office will issue a notice to both the debtor and the creditor to give them an opportunity of being heard as to why a Certificate of Unenforceability should not be granted.  Details of all Certificates of Unenforceability are published in compliance with Section 83 of the Judgment Enforcement Rules (NI) 1981 in Belfast Gazette, including debtor name, amount owing and the date the Certificate of Unenforceability was granted.If a creditor becomes aware that the debtor’s circumstances have changed, they may request the Enforcement of Judgments Office to review the case and to issue appropriate enforcement orders.  As per Article 21 of the Judgments Enforcement (Northern Ireland) Order 1981, an application must be made within 12 years from the date on which the Certificate of Unenforceability was granted and may be made during the last six years of that period only by leave of the Office.(ii) Officials from the Department of the Economy have advised that when a consumer buys a new or second-hand car from a car dealer, the Consumer Rights Act 2015 (CRA15) stipulates that they can expect it to be of satisfactory quality, be fit for purpose and meet any description given to it.If the vehicle does not meet one of these standards, the CRA15 gives consumers the right to request a repair or replacement of the vehicle, and in some instances may allow consumers to return the vehicle and reject it, particularly if a fault develops within the first 30 days after purchase.Some car dealerships and garages have signed up to alternative dispute resolution schemes which means that, if a complaint is not getting resolved, consumers can refer it to an impartial third party, such as the Motor Ombudsman -https://www.themotorombudsman.org/consumers/make-a-complaint.When a consumer buys a car on finance or with a credit card, they have additional protections under section 75 of the Consumer Credit Act 1974. This makes the finance or credit card company jointly liable for any breach of contract. Consumers can take legal action for breaches of CRA15, such as being sold a faulty car, for up to six years after the date of the contract. Consumers should seek legal advice before considering court action, especially when the cost of the vehicle is above the £5000 limit of the small claims court.If a car trader does not comply with consumer protection laws, they may face enforcement action by the Department for the Economy’s Trading Standards Service (TSS). TSS can investigate complaints alleging unfair trading practices and criminal breaches of consumer protection legislation, taking appropriate enforcement action when necessary.Any consumer that has a co

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has conducted research or engaged with the Department for Communities in relation to the impact of lengthy waiting lists on the deterioration of patients’ health, increased long-term sickness and greater reliance on welfare support.
answer_text: As you may be aware, my Department is currently developing a Waiting Well policy to support patients who are on waiting lists, to ensure they are supported to better self-manage the condition they have been referred for, as well as their wider health and well-being needs.My officials plan on engaging with a range of stakeholders, including DfC, as this work progresses.In the meantime I regularly engage with Executive colleagues on the issue of Northern Ireland’s wholly unacceptable waiting times.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) her Department's enforcement responsibility in terms of unauthorised signage on public roads or footways; and (ii) the total number of enforcement actions which have taken place in the last five years.
answer_text: The illegal erection of advertising signs along the public road is an offence under The Roads (Northern Ireland) Order 1993. Where signs of any kind are erected illegally on DfI property or unlawfully affixed to the surface of a road, action may be taken by my Department under Article 87 of the Roads (NI) Order 1993, to try and remove them. However, during recent years my Department has faced significant budget shortfall and must carefully consider how best to use its limited resources. In order to ensure that the highest priority, safety related maintenance can be undertaken, my Department targets the limited resources available towards road safety related matters. The removal of advertising signage is considered low priority and therefore will only be carried out as resources permit / where there is a road safety implication.My Department does not hold records of the number of enforcement notices issued in relation unauthorised signs or advertisements.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any consultation that took place with the residents and lessees before the decision to remove security from the Ebrington site.
answer_text: The Department remains committed to ensuring Ebrington continues to be a safe and shared space for everyone to enjoy and security provision at the site via an extensive CCTV network remains in place.  This aligns with security provision within the city centre.  Much like the city centre, Ebrington is a hub of activity with a wide range of both day and night-time businesses, many of which have their own security provision in place. The guard presence was first reduced from 24hr to 12hr in January 2024, with no evidence of impact on site safety.    The Department meets site businesses regularly and tenants were made aware of proposed changes in guard provision.  The Department also meets regularly with resident representatives and the reduction in guards has not been raised as a significant issue. While ensuring the site remains safe for both tenants and residents, the Department is also mindful of the costs to maintain and manage a site of this scale and works to ensure costs incurred are necessary, fair and reasonable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to improve awareness and training among GPs and other healthcare professionals to ensure earlier recognition and referral for suspected endometriosis cases.
answer_text: My Department does not hold lists or information regarding GPs and their training.  As independent contractors, and in order to provide the services that they are contracting for, GPs will be required to meet the required training and competency standards.All doctors wishing to become GPs undertake training covering a wide range of conditions including endometriosis.GPs are ultimately responsible for ensuring that they maintain their own continuous professional development (CPD) in order to keep their knowledge, skills and training up to date. This includes participation in annual appraisal and in Revalidation, the requirements of which are set by the General Medical Council (GMC). Every licensed doctor must revalidate to show that they are fit to practise and to maintain their licence to work in the UK.My Department have endorsed the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) guidelines on the diagnosis and management of endometriosis.  Last year the National Confidential Enquiry into Patient Outcome and Death (NCEPOD) published A Long and Painful Road, a review of the quality of care provided to adult patients diagnosed with endometriosis and this was shared with all GP practices.I have a number of initiatives which are designed to ensure that GPs and Practices can access training readily and at a time that is convenient.GPNI was established during the Covid-19 pandemic to ensure effective communication was maintained, with all those working within GP Primary Care. This service is commissioned from the Eastern GP Federation Support Unit and allocation for this service for 2024/25 is £50.5k. The GPNI website and webinars are now widely accepted as a key regional communications platform designed to improve dissemination of, and access to, information for Primary Care practitioners.Practice Based Learning (PBL) is a programme of learning sessions for GP’s and the wider General Practice staff.  The aim of these PBL is to allow dedicated time to be set aside for educational sessions to contribute to the continued professional development of GP’s and their Practice Staff. Provision of PBL was previously developed by the then HSCB for all GP Practices, however from March 2020 SPPG commission the provision of PBL from the Federations for their member Practices.  PBL is facilitated by local GP Federation Support Units and £178k has been allocated for PBL for the 24/25 year.  They have provided a cost-effective programme of ongoing learning for their GPs, nurses and wider Practice staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the aftercare and support available for women who suffer a miscarriage, and their partners, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Please see below response provided by each Health and Social Care Trust:Belfast HSCT TrustWomen are cared for within different departments depending on their gestation and their clinical condition on presenting at the hospital.  These include the Maternity Assessment Unit (MAU), the Early Pregnancy Unit (EPU) and the Delivery suite.  Some women will be seen in the MAU, which provides care to women who require assessment and or additional care at various gestations as appropriate.  Some women may need to remain in the MAU until their physical condition is stabilised prior to transfer to theatre and or onto a different department.  Women are also supported through miscarriage in the Early Pregnancy Unit.  Specially trained midwives who offer transvaginal ultrasound and bereavement support are available in the unit Monday – Friday 08:00-16:00.  In addition to this, the service has two Consultant Obstetricians and a Speciality Doctor who provide cover three mornings weekly. The midwives provide counselling in relation to the options for the management of miscarriage and provide care for medical management both as an outpatient or an inpatient option.  Surgical management of miscarriage is also offered.  Women who chose this option will be cared for in the ward setting of EPU where they are transferred to theatre for the procedure and returned to the ward to receive post-operative care from the midwives within EPU.   For women who chose medical management of miscarriage they will remain under the care of EPU for a number of weeks until the miscarriage is fully resolved.  This can involve additional visits to the unit or virtual appointments over the phone.All midwives, doctors and maternity support workers provide bereavement care.  Some women will need to return to the EPU for ongoing care and they are cared for in a small private area with private toilet facilities.  South Eastern HSC TrustSouth Eastern Trust provide Early Pregnancy Service 7 days a week for women with early pregnancy issues.  Women who experience miscarriage  can access support from the bereavement team who cover gynaecology, maternity and neonatal.Northern HSC TrustWomen and families experiencing miscarriage or a stillbirth have the support of the multidisciplinary team, the Trust Bereavement coordinator, the Bereavement Support Midwife, specialist midwives and specialist nurses who work collaboratively.  The service provides support and guidance to all parents who experience pregnancy loss at any stage of pregnancy, miscarriage, stillbirth, early neonatal death, termination for medical reasons, and pregnancy after loss.In the clinical setting bereaved parents are cared for in their recently opened bereavement room.  ‘The Willow Suite’ is a dedicated space for parents and families following the loss of a baby after 20 weeks providing an appropriately safe and sensitive environment, which upholds privacy and dignity whilst giving the opportunity to grieve and spend time with their

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration given to increasing support with mileage costs for domiciliary care staff.
answer_text: As part of negotiations to conclude industrial action undertaken during 2023, my Department, employer representatives and Trade Union Side undertook to consider a regional system of mileage reimbursement which might better reflect the integrated nature of the HSC service and the rurality of many of our population requiring care at home.Following these negotiations, a Departmental circular, which issued on 20 May 2024, informed HSC employers that the primary rate of mileage had increased, with effect from 1 April 2024 to 58 pence per mile for staff employed within HSC under Agenda for Change (AfC) terms and conditions.The primary rate is payable for the first 4,500 miles travelled in any one financial year (1 April – 31 March). Additionally, the secondary mileage rate (for each mile travelled over 4,500 miles during the financial year) has maintained at 30p per mile.It was agreed that a review of the rates may be requested either by employers or Trade Unions at future meetings of the Joint Negotiating Committee Forum held with my Department and HSC employers, should the prevailing costs of motoring increase or decrease.The Department is not considering any further revision of the published rates at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the A2 Buncrana Road Improvement Scheme.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of tonnes of (i) untreated wastewater; and (ii) sewage that was discharged by NI Water into cross-border (a) rivers; and (ii) loughs in 2023-24.
answer_text: Further to my answer to AQW 24403 22-27, dated 27 March 2025, a full answer is now set out below.This is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:NI Water does not have volumetric measurement at storm overflows. To monitor and record spills, Event Duration Monitors (EDMs) are being installed across NI Water’s wastewater network over a multi-year investment programme. EDMs measure the number of times spills occur however, they do not measure spill volumes. NI Water’s drainage modelling provides a prediction of the volume of wastewater that spills in an average rainfall year.  There are 629 storm overflows within the NIEA’s seven cross-border Local Management Areas (LMAs), of these 30% are covered by NI Water's drainage models. The total average annual predicted wastewater spill volume within the identified seven LMAs, for modelled overflows, is 3,137,271m3, which equates to approximately 3.1 million tonnes.NI Water does not discharge sewage, rather after wastewater has been treated at wastewater treatment works, the final treated effluent is discharged through outfall pipes. NI Water does not have instrumentation at all wastewater treatment works and therefore cannot report on all continuous treated discharges.There are 286 wastewater treatment works within the seven identified cross-border LMAs, of these 15 % have flow measurement equipment. The 2024 measured volume of treated volume within the identified seven cross-border LMAs is 38,805,198m3, which equates to approximately 38.8 million tonnes. A programme for further installation of flow measurement instrumentation is under development, subject to the availability of funding.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will consider establishing a right for all social housing tenants to request permission to keep a pet and ensuring that such requests cannot be unreasonably refused.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive and for registered Housing Associations. With regard to the Housing Executive, I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows.“The Housing Executive’s position on pets is set out in clauses 9 and 10 of Part 1 (Tenant’s Obligations) of the General Conditions of Tenancy. Upon signing a tenancy agreement and accepting these conditions, our tenants agree: ‘9. Not to keep any domestic pets without the consent in writing of the Area Manager (if the dwelling is a flat or maisonette). 10. Not to keep more than one domestic pet without the consent in writing of the Area Manager (if the dwelling is not a flat or maisonette).’Coupled with these specific conditions relating to ownership of pets, responsible ownership of the same is provided for by clause 5 of Part 1 (Tenant’s Obligations) whereby tenants agree ‘not to do or permit or suffer to be done in the dwelling or within the curtilage or neighbourhood of the dwelling any act or thing which is or may be an annoyance or nuisance to the occupiers of any neighbouring or adjoining premises’. If our tenants observe the above obligations, they may quietly enjoy their dwelling, and keep a pet/pets without any interruption by the Housing Executive.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly with regard to matters that are their responsibility. Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.With regard to Housing Association tenants any policies related to the keeping of pets in their properties is an operational matter for each Housing Association to determine.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any steps his Department has taken to ensure the NIEA can regulate and monitor pollution incidents caused by NI Water.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) regulates discharges to the water environment under the Water (NI) Order (as amended) 1999. These Consents require dischargers to treat sewage to a level that can be sustained by the receiving environment and to maintain their treatments to avoid pollution.Where an operator falls below the standards of its Discharge Consent (under the Water (NI) Order 1999, then DAERA’s Enforcement Policy is applied in these circumstances and / or where the operator has been the cause of a pollution incident.It is NIEA’s policy to pursue formal enforcement action where the polluter is identified and where the pollution has had a High or Medium severity environmental impact. In the inclusive period 2021 to 2024, NIEA has responded to 360 reports of water pollution where, from investigation, the source was confirmed as a NI Water asset.Successful prosecution action against NI Water over the past 18 years, since their formation on 1 April 2007, has resulted in fines totalling £266,900. The cause of pollution incidents and lessons that are learned to minimise the risk of recurrence, are discussed at regular meetings held between NIEA and NI Water. Addressing water pollution remains a priority for my Department and all reports are investigated. The NIEA Incident Hotline enables the public to report incidents of pollution at any time of the day.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the cost of unoccupied (i) land; and (ii) buildings owned by their Department in each of the last three years.
answer_text: There are six buildings at Ebrington which are currently unoccupied, but all have an identified use and/or a tenant in the process of taking up occupation. The total cost for maintaining the unoccupied buildings at Ebrington over the past three years is £38,581. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer (i) AQW 23052/22-27; and (ii) AQW 23053/22-27.
answer_text: (i) AQW 23052/22-27 and (ii) AQW 23053/22-27 were answered on 31 March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the number of drug-related deaths in Northern Ireland, including the reliability and accuracy of recorded figures.
answer_text: The annual Drug-Related and Drug Misuse Deaths in Northern Ireland release published by the Northern Ireland Statistics & Research Agency (NISRA) presents statistics on the most recent official death registration data available on drug-related mortality across NI. These statistics are derived from cause of death information recorded when a death is registered and show the number of such deaths registered during each calendar year in Northern Ireland. The figures are typically reported in terms of three-year rolling averages to reflect some delays in registration of deaths due to Coroner’s investigations. While responsibility for collating this data sits outside my Department’s direct remit, the definition used for drug misuse deaths aligns with that used by the Office for National Statistics and I am reassured by NISRA’s documented processes in relation to their robust quality assurance steps.While there has been a marked reduction in such deaths since 2020, falling from 218 to 169 in 2023, NISRA’s figures show an overall increase of 47% in drug-related deaths compared to a decade ago. The position clearly remains unacceptable and each of these deaths is preventable. In addition, I have set out my commitment to reducing health inequalities, and these figures highlight once again the disproportionate impact of substance use on our most deprived communities with drug-related deaths almost 6 times higher in the most deprived areas versus the least deprived.My Department uses these official drug-related death statistics to guide policy and monitor the Executive’s substance use strategy Preventing Harm Empowering Recovery, the aim of which is to reduce the level of alcohol and drug-related harm through a range of cross-departmental actions and supports. While such data is essential in informing this work, my concerns in this area extend beyond the numbers. Every substance use related death brings heartbreak and distress for friends, families and communities. As I have frequently highlighted, given the links of substance use to trauma, poverty, poor mental health and other social factors, addressing drug related harms needs a holistic approach and prioritisation of this issue by the entire NI Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 23904/22-27, whether this recognition certificate scheme will be backdated.
answer_text: I plan to introduce the Deaths, Still births and Baby Loss Bill into the Assembly in the new few weeks. This will be the starting point for the Baby Loss certificate scheme: details of how the scheme will operate will be set out in secondary legislation, which I also expect to be considered by the Assembly.  It is very important to me that the Baby Loss certificate scheme meets the needs of families here, and I intend to consult on the question of the backdating of the scheme and a range of other matters. General Register Office continues to work with colleagues in the Department of Health to plan for this and the introduction of the Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on steps to improve access to NHS dental services; and (ii) for his assessment of the impact this is having on oral health outcomes.
answer_text: (i) I acknowledge the challenges facing many patients, particularly those not registered with a dental practice, in accessing dental care when they need it.Access to Health Service dentistry remains a priority for me, and my Department has specifically targeted investment this year at improving access for priority groups.An investment package of £9.2m has supported General Dental Services in 2024/25, including:£1m for newly registered child patients through the reestablishment of the Enhanced Child Examination Scheme;£4.3m to fund a 30% enhancement to fees paid to dentists for priority treatments; and£3.9m for the treatment of high priority unregistered patients through a Dental Access Scheme. There are currently 29 practices participating in the Dental Access Scheme.The Dental Access Scheme commenced on 1st August last year and has since allowed nearly 11,000 high-need patients to receive treatment. Efforts to improve access for all patients will continue to be the short-term focus as dental reform is progressed.I met with the British Dental Association last month to discuss the issues facing the industry, and officials held a follow-up meeting on investment options for 2025/26 targeted at improving access.While these additional investment interventions are necessary to stabilise General Dental Services in the short term, I am clear that, in common with other services, it will require sustained effort to ensure sustainability over the longer term.(ii) The determinants of oral health are largely linked to lifestyle factors such as a diet high in sugar, smoking, alcohol use and good oral hygiene behaviours such as daily tooth-brushing with a fluoride toothpaste. Given that most of these factors can be controlled outside of a visit to the dentist, it is not possible to directly link oral health outcomes of the population to access to health service dental services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) whether his Department collaborates with other agencies, such as law enforcement and social services, to gather comprehensive data on drug-related deaths; and (ii) any steps being taken to improve information sharing between agencies to identify trends and risk factors.
answer_text: While official drug death statistics are compiled by the Northern Ireland Statistics & Research Agency (NISRA), my Department is involved in extensive inter-agency collaboration on data and trends relating to substance use and the harms it causes. Trends in drug death statistics feature on the agenda for the UK Drugs Ministerial meetings which I attend along with the Justice Minister, providing an opportunity to work collaboratively and identify emerging risks relating to substance use. My officials participate regularly in Four Nations discussions on drug-related deaths and across a range of related issues. They are also fully engaged with their counterparts in Ireland, through the British-Irish Council Work Sector on Alcohol & Drugs and other forums.The number of drug-related deaths (and drugs involved) is a standing item on the Organised Crime Task Force (OCTF) Drugs Subgroup which is attended by my officials. Similarly, the Chair of the OCTF Drugs Subgroup sits on the Substance Use Strategy Programme Board and at the most recent meeting shared up-to-date analysis of its work with partner agencies such as the UK Border Force and the National Crime Agency to reduce the supply of illicit and harmful substances along with a summary of the drugs, and patterns of use, observed in local communities. There are also regular information sharing meetings between my Department, the Public Health Agency, the voluntary and community sector, PSNI and Border Force in relation to emerging threats.Additionally, my Department oversees the Drug & Alcohol Monitoring & Information System (DAMIS) with support from lead partners the Public Health Agency, the Department of Justice, the Police Service of Northern Ireland, and Forensic Science Northern Ireland. DAMIS is an “early warning system” designed to find out about emerging trends in drug use, so we can act quickly and provide relevant information or advice to those who misuse substances. The kind of information DAMIS collects includes: sudden increase in the use of a particular drug; drugs being consumed in new ways; new drugs becoming available; and the emergence of substances with unexpected unpleasant or dangerous effects.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any measures being taken to improve the recording of suicide and drug-related deaths in Northern Ireland, including efforts to examine cases where both issues intersect, ensuring that data is accurately captured for more effective policy planning and the development of support services.
answer_text: ANSWERNISRA publishes quarterly, provisional counts of registered deaths due to suicide and drug-related causes. The annual final accredited official statistics in turn provide fully quality-assured information at a more granular level. NISRA collates data on death registrations as collected via the death certificate by the General Register Office (GRO). As such, the registration data is deemed to have complete population coverage as it is a legal requirement to register all such events that occur here. Supporting information regarding the circumstances of a death is separately provided by the NI Coroner’s service, including details that relate to intent (whether the death was accidental, self-inflicted, or undetermined) or in the case of drug-related deaths, the types of drugs involved in the death. NISRA uses the definitions for both suicide, and drug-related and drug misuse deaths as employed by the Office for National Statistics. NISRA performs robust quality assurance processes and checks on all deaths data as outlined in the Quality Assurance of Administrative Data (QAAD) for Deaths Data in Northern Ireland. These include, trend analysis, bounds checking, internal within- and across- record consistency and visual analysis of data. To inform users about the quality of drug-related and drug misuse statistics, NISRA has also published a drug-related deaths information paper which details the approach to produce the statistics and the key quality dimensions and potential limitations of the output. The quality of NI drug-related deaths is deemed to be high, aided by the comparatively small numbers relative to other jurisdictions and the opportunity to address data gaps at record level. For example, in 2023, 22.9 (1,245) per cent of drug-poisoning deaths registered in England and Wales had no drug type recorded on the death certificate. In comparison, for NI, 8.9 (15) per cent had no drug type information recorded.The review of suicide statistics by NISRA and the NI Coroner’s Service for the data period 2015-2020 highlighted issues relating to the recording and reporting of ‘intent’ information. This comprehensive review resulted in significant improvements to reporting of suicide statistics incorporating improvements to the record management system in Coroner’s Service; closer working relationships between all parties involved in the information capture, processing and reporting of suicide deaths; increased quality assurance checks at each stage; increased documentation of processes; and a focus on the importance of establishing the intent of a death integrated into training for newly appointed coroners. As part of the suicide statistics, analysis is provided on method of suicide which includes numbers of suicides due to drug poisoning.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any engagement he has had with Executive colleagues in relation to the potential adverse effect of the increase in employers' National Insurance contributions on special educational needs and disabilities transport provision, given the significant impact these changes could have on children’s ability to attend school.
answer_text: School transport is one of many vital services provided by private sector employers on behalf of the public sector and I am concerned about the impact the recent Budget may have on the cost to employers of providing those services. The impact on employers of the increase in National Insurance Contributions (NIC) will vary depending on the size of the business, how many it employs and how it manages the new arrangements. I understand that a number of the private bus and taxi operators, who hold contracts with the Education Authority (EA) to provide school transport, are smaller businesses whose NIC liability may not increase or are self-employed and therefore unaffected. The EA does not, however, hold information that would allow it to identify the private bus and taxi operators who will be impacted or to make a detailed assessment of the extent to which this may increase their operating costs. The EA works closely with its transport operators and has advised that to date none have raised concerns about the rise in operating costs due to the increase in NICs, however it will engage with any who are impacted and for whom this presents difficulties.  It is clear that the increase in employers NICs has the potential to increase the already significant cost of the school transport service, however the picture is still emerging. At this stage, I believe the increase in operational costs of those transport operators who are impacted can be managed by the EA through the annual tendering process and I do not anticipate that this will result in children who are transported by private bus and taxi being unable to attend school.
