question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the factors contributing to the 127 per cent increase in the number of households on the social housing waiting list over the last decade.
answer_text: Lack of supplyThe economic and social consequences of the pandemic which are still evidentThe shortage of suitable accommodation and the problems of funding homelessness servicesIncreasing demand for temporary accommodationA lack of housing supply to facilitate move on from temporary accommodationThe finite nature of the Supporting People budgetThe increasing complexity of need among clients.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 23147/22-27, whether he is aware of any efforts to make home insurance data available at the postcode level to enable a more accurate assessment of the situation.
answer_text: ANSWERComprehensive data on home insurance premiums are held by insurance companies, which is why I pressed the Association of British Insurers (ABI), as their industry representative body, on the need for local data at my meeting with them on 13 February 2025.The ABI indicated to me that they will raise this directly with their member organisations. My officials continue to follow this up.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when the Northern Ireland Environment Agency’s Waste Water Reform Programme will be implemented.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Environment Agency has initiated its waste-water regulatory reform programme. The roadmap and timelines supporting this programme are currently under review in addition to the necessary resources to support its delivery.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the status of the Living with Water Programme, including its current funding position.
answer_text: Due to the costs of the NI Water elements of the Plan increasing by over 50% within 2 years of publication, the department conducted an affordability review.The review concluded that the need for the Belfast Plan still exists, but delivery will take longer than the 12 years originally estimated. Delivery of the projects in the Belfast Plan will now be taken forward by NI Water and my Department at a pace and scale achievable within allocated budgets. The Living With Water in Derry Plan, which is currently open for consultation, proposes a similar delivery approach.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any steps his Department is taking to ensure all wastewater treatment plants are using electronic data monitors.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) regulates discharges to the water environment under the Water Order (as amended) 1999. These Consents require dischargers to treat sewage to a level that can be sustained by the receiving environment and to maintain their treatments to avoid pollution. The NIEA sets numeric discharge quality limits in consented wastewater treatment works serving a population equivalent (PE) of 250 and above. These consents require compliance of the flow conditions as stipulated in the water order consent by the operators. The NIEA is currently developing regulatory operational guidance on assessing flow compliance at wastewater treatment works which includes guidance on flow monitoring instrumentation and quality control systems. Northern Ireland Water is taking forward a pilot exercise on the implementation of flow monitoring compliance at selected wastewater treatment works that will take account of new and existing electronic data monitors, the results of which will be reported to NIEA for consideration.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 18925/22-27.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the (i) review of Community Transport; and (ii) Phase 1 report will be made publicly available.
answer_text: The Phase 1 report evaluation report on Community Transport schemes, funded by my Department, was shared with relevant stakeholders on 10 March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps his Department is taking to improve media literacy education in schools, including to protect children from harmful online content and misinformation.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Curriculum already includes elements of digital literacy and online safety, particularly within the cross-curricular skill of Using ICT, the Personal Development and Mutual Understanding area of learning at primary level and the Learning for Life and Work area of learning at post-primary level.My officials are working with the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) and the Safer Schools Northern Ireland app to ensure that emerging risks, including misinformation and deep fakes, are appropriately reflected within digital literacy education.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the projects that will be impacted by the failure to fund the £8.2 million bid for Railway Capital in the October Monitoring Round.
answer_text: My Department did not receive an allocation for railway capital in October Monitoring Round. As a result of the bid not being funded, some projects to renew or refurbish railway assets have been deferred or delayed to future financial years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on her engagement with law enforcement to address the risks associated with online radicalisation.
answer_text: Online radicalisation is a dangerous and worrying trend that can lead to the cultivation of extremist beliefs and behaviours, and the incitement of hatred. Online activity can be used to express and spread hate, which, in extreme circumstances, can manifest into violent or even terrorist attacks.    Terrorism and telecommunications are both reserved matters and decisions regarding their legislation, policy and enforcement are outside my competence and controlled by the UK Government. My Department did however liaise with the UK Government on online harms in the lead up to the Online Safety Act 2023 (the Act) which is a new set of laws that protects children and adults online. The Act imposes new duties on social media companies and search services to make them more responsible for their users’ safety on their platforms. It also modernises false and threatening communication offences in Northern Ireland to address online harms.  The kinds of illegal content and activity that platforms need to protect users from are set out in the Act.  This includes content relating to racially or religiously aggravated public order offences and inciting violence.I have expressed previously that I believe the legislation should have gone further in preventing and addressing online harms, including ensuring that individuals cannot act completely anonymously online which, in my opinion, only further serves to encourage individuals to behave in a way that they would not in the “real” world. In relation to those issues that fall within the devolved space, and therefore within my competence as Minister of Justice, I have regular opportunities to meet law enforcement agencies and discuss measures which are crucial to ensure the security and safety of citizens.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps his Department is taking to raise awareness of online scams targeting pensioners, especially those involving fake winter fuel allowance claims.
answer_text: My Department is actively engaged in ensuring that those in vulnerable groups, including pensioners, are aware of preventative measures to take to avoid online scams. This includes ensuring they can detect potential scams, what action to take and who to contact. Benefit information on nidirect sets out how my Department will liaise with or contact customers. Within many of the online information pages, my Department also signposts to the public sector’s Scamwise campaign which provides advice on how to recognise and report a scam. In addition, Universal Credit claimants have access to a guide within their online account which provides information on how to stay safe online and keep their Universal Credit account secure. My Department regularly uses social media messaging to promote scam awareness. Those messages make it clear that my Department will never ask for details by text, email or a web link and encourage customers to report anything suspicious to Action Fraud or their local Jobs and Benefits Office.Specifically in relation to the fake winter fuel allowance scams, my Department promoted social media posts to publicise scam awareness. These were scheduled once per week throughout March 2025 to coincide with the issuing of the Pension Age Fuel Support Payment. Digital and IT courses are run regularly across the library network through the Go On NI programme, to help people develop their digital skills and to raise awareness, and this includes courses on topics such as common internet scams and how to avoid them. Libraries NI also links up with partners to deliver activities as part of national initiatives such as Cyber Week and Safer Internet Day.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will review the current policy around pothole repair, such as recording and prioritising areas which have had three or more temporary repairs in the last 12 months.
answer_text: My Department’s defect recording system does not automatically record the number of temporary or recurring pothole defects.  Temporary repairs are not regularly utilised and are limited to where permanent repairs cannot be undertaken in a timely fashion. In such circumstances, my Department seeks to deliver a follow up permanent repair as resources permit.My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government and as such the level of repairs that can be carried out in future financial years will depend on the 2025-26 final budget determination. Until such times as sufficient funding is made available, it will not be possible to change the current level of service. My officials are working on a new Road Maintenance Strategy that I will be considering in detail in the coming weeks. We will focus on delivering higher quality repairs instead of spreading resources too thinly, ensuring a more reliable and safer road network where we intervene.We will use modern technology to direct our investments where they are needed most, ensuring smarter maintenance decisions and will future proof our roads to meet the changing needs of our society including active travel, reducing the carbon footprint of our operations, and ensuring that the network is resilient after adverse weather events.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the establishment of a working group with the Department for Infrastructure to address issues with wastewater connections impacting on the delivery of housing.
answer_text: As part of the implementation of the Housing Supply Strategy, a cross-Executive Project Board has been established to begin development of the first action plan focussed on addressing the whole system issues impacting on the delivery of housing. This includes the issue of wastewater infrastructure constraints.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to provide a time frame for the delivery of the baby loss certificate scheme.
answer_text: I am keen to see this initiative continue to be taken forward as quickly as possible to give bereaved families the opportunity to have formal recognition of their loss. I want all parents and families who have suffered the devastating loss of a baby to feel that their grief is recognised. Primary legislation has been drafted which will be the start of the process to establish a scheme here. I hope to introduce the Deaths, Still-Births and Baby Loss Bill into the Assembly in the next few weeks. The introduction of the Bill is the first step in establishing the scheme here. Work will follow – involving officials from the Department of Health – to consult with stakeholders on the detail of the scheme, to draft the required secondary legislation and to operationalise these processes. If these matters progress smoothly, I anticipate that the scheme may be operational by the end of the year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the establishment of a working group with the Department for Communities to address issues with wastewater connections impacting on the delivery of housing.
answer_text: The Department for Communities (DfC) has reconstituted the Housing Supply Strategy Project Board, and my Department will be represented on that group.    In addition, my Department has facilitated workshops with the Northern Ireland Environment Agency, DfC, the Northern Ireland Housing Executive and NI Water on the issue of wastewater capacity to seek to identify areas where those Departments could work together and share information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide a time frame for the delivery of the baby loss certificate scheme.
answer_text: I am fully supportive of the Finance Minister's plans to bring forward legislation that will enable the General Register Office to take forward a Baby Loss Certificate Scheme for Northern Ireland. As this work will be taken forward by the Department of Finance and is subject to the passage of legislation in the Assembly, I am unable to provide a time frame for the availability of the scheme; however, my Department will continue working closely with the Department of Finance to support the necessary planning as required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the total number of pensioners missing out on the Winter Fuel Payment who have to date received the one-off fuel support payment of £100.
answer_text: To date 248,500 pension age fuel support payments have been made.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 21473/22-27, to detail any steps that will be taken to address prolonged delays around the issuing of the National Joint Council annual pay awards.
answer_text: The Department makes every effort to process approvals for the Housing Executive’s pay remits in a timely manner and without undue delay.  Once the Housing Executive submits its pay remits to the Department for approval, these must receive appropriate scrutiny in order that the Department, as the sponsor body for the Housing Executive, can be satisfied that these can be approved. This scrutiny sometimes requires seeking further information or clarification from the Housing Executive. I can assure you that we work closely with the Housing Executive to prioritise this work and avoid undue delay.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the delivery of the Anti-Poverty Strategy.
answer_text: I can confirm I have now sent the draft Anti-Poverty Strategy to the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether consideration will be given to creating access to the Strathfoyle Greenway, Derry, via Westlake.
answer_text: The Strathfoyle Greenway is council owned, and they are primarily responsible for the facility, although my Department was pleased to be able to contribute towards its initial construction.My Department has concluded that there may be merit in providing access from the Westlake residential area to the greenway via Butlers Wharf, however at this time the development remains unadopted.  My officials will give further consideration to the creation of this access once Butlers Wharf has gone through the necessary processes for adoption onto the road network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) to provide clarity on whether the 100,000 homes outlined in the Housing Supply Strategy is a firm target; and (ii) to detail any specific measures in place to ensure that the one third social homes is delivered within the strategy's timeframe.
answer_text: (i) The delivery of ‘at least 100,000 homes’ across all tenures is a long-term ambition over the next 15 years (2024 – 2039), representing the additional housing required to meet our housing need and demand. It is intended that the Executive Strategy will be reviewed during its lifetime to ensure it can respond to any changing need and demand.  Meeting this ambition will be dependent on the Executive working collectively to deliver on its commitment to the Housing Supply Strategy.(ii) Regarding the delivery of social homes, my ambition is to increase delivery and optimise the use of grant/subsidy, but this will be reliant on sufficient budget being made available. The outworkings of the Strategic Insight Lab, which took place in June 2024, will inform how issues and pressures associated with the existing delivery model might be addressed to maximise delivery, particularly in the current challenging budget environment.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they will increase financial contributions towards the DNA Museum at Ebrington to ensure the project can go ahead.
answer_text: The Executive Office is currently contributing £3.3m to the new regional visitor attraction, the Derry~Londonderry North Atlantic Museum (DNA) due to open at Ebrington in 2026.  Like other Departments, TEO are faced with a very challenging budgetary position; however, officials have requested a meeting with representatives of DCSDC to better understand the revised ask.The opening of the DNA Museum will strengthen the economic, social, tourism and cultural impact and value of the Ebrington site to the city and the North West.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when her Department will publish an application and assessment process for residents' parking schemes.
answer_text: I have asked my officials to expedite the development of an application and assessment process for residents’ parking schemes to ensure that any requests can be appropriately considered. This process will take into account the findings and recommendations of the report and will provide a streamlined approach for considering, assessing and prioritising requests going forward.  It is my intention to make an announcement over the coming months as to how future schemes will be assessed by my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 6642/22-27, for his assessment of the feasibility of the delivery of 6,450 new social homes over the next three years within the current budget.
answer_text: In my response to AQW 6642/22-27 which was answered on 20 February 2024, I advised that subject to sufficient budget being made available the following targets had been set of 2,000 starts for 2024/25, increasing to 2,150 units for 2025/26 and 2,300 units for 2026/27.Due to insufficient budget the target for starts for 2024/25 has been revised down to 1,400 units. As outlined in the Programme for Government by the end of this mandate, work on at least 5,850 new build social homes will have commenced.While mindful of the financial pressures faced by my Department, I am committed to the housing targets outlined in both the Executive’s Housing Supply Strategy and Programme for Government.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of individuals in temporary or emergency housing placements who are subject to bail conditions in the last 12 months, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: I do not hold this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide a breakdown of the total allocated funding for (i) Minor Work; and (ii) Major Work projects for schools in the Foyle constituency, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: As funding is not allocated to individual schools, nor by constituency, capital expenditure detail has been provided. In line with record retention policies, details of the amount spent on (i) minor works and (ii) major works for schools in the Foyle constituency is only available for each of the last seven financial years and is set out in a table which I have deposited in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she plans to review The Street Works (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 to introduce measures to ensure utility companies resurface roads to the best possible standard after street works to prevent potholes.
answer_text: The Street Works (Northern Ireland) Order 1995, forms the legislative framework for street works by statutory undertakers. The Order requires the undertaker, when reinstating a street, to comply with such requirements as may be prescribed in relation to the specification of materials to be used and the standards of workmanship to be observed. These requirements have been prescribed in the Specification for the Reinstatement of Openings in Roads (2nd Edition), a code of practice approved by the Department under Article 31 of the Order. Northern Ireland Road Authority and Utilities Committee (NIRAUC) - Code of Practice - Specification for the reinstatement of openings in roads - 2nd Edition (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk)The Department has powers to carry out investigatory works to check whether or not a utility company has complied with its duty to reinstate the street. These are detailed in the Code of Practice for Inspections.Northern Ireland Road Authority and Utilities Committee (NIRAUC) - Code of Practice for Inspections (2nd Edition) (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk)Where a utility company fails to reinstate to an acceptable standard, the reinstatement defects are notified to the utility company responsible for the repair or replacement. Where necessary, additional inspections are carried out by my officials, at the utility company’s expense, to ensure the defects have been corrected.I have no plans to introduce further legislation in relation to these powers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has any plans to increase the availability of defibrillators.
answer_text: Currently there are approximately 3708 permanently fixed community defibrillators registered with NIAS on the ‘The Circuit’.  Whilst I have currently no plans to increase the availability of defibrillators, local Councils provide opportunities for community and sporting organisations to apply for funding towards defibrillators and there are several charities who will fully or part fund defibrillators.  These charities include but are not limited to The British Heart Foundation and Bravehearts NI. The Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) leads on the implementation of the Community Resuscitation Strategy and is funded to provide a supporting role in advising organisations, businesses, and individuals regarding siting of defibrillators and best practice for defibrillators. NIAS has worked with the British Heart Foundation to develop ‘The Circuit’ (https://www.thecircuit.uk/) which is a national online defibrillator network linking to every ambulance service in the UK.  NIAS can readily provide information on AEDs registered on ‘The Circuit’ to 999 callers, should they require access. Further information on the location of defibrillators can be found at www.defibfinder.uk or by contacting the NIAS Community Resuscitation Team at resus.admin@nias.hscni.net or telephone 028 9040 5215

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline what measures his Department is taking in the 2024-25 academic year to deliver investment for new accommodation for the Bunscoil Cholmcille.
answer_text: A business case for a Multi-Purpose Hall, with servery and pupil toilets and an administration block was approved by my Department in April 2024, at a total cost of £1.8m including VAT, fees and inflation. This investment will greatly improve facilities for both pupils and teachers at the school.The project is in its very early stages, with a design team recently appointed. Approval has recently been given for an acoustics survey to be undertaken to inform the design and planning.It is too early to indicate when the project will reach construction stage, as it will be subject to detailed design, statutory approvals and budget availability. Officials in my Estate Operations Team will continue to keep the school updated as the scheme progresses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he intends to increase the household income threshold criterion for entitlement to free school meals and uniform grants ahead of the 2025-26 academic year.
answer_text: Ahead of the conclusion of the review of the eligibility criteria for free school meals and uniform grants, I announced in June 2024 that I was introducing an interim rise in the income threshold that applies to those in receipt of Universal Credit from £14,000 per annum to £15,000. This rise took effect from the start of the application period for the 2024/25 academic year.On 14 February 2025, a 12-week public consultation period closed on the options considered by my Department as part of this review. Analysis of the responses is currently underway and this will help inform my decision on whether any change to the eligibility criteria should be taken forward, including any further changes to the income threshold.  In addition to the views expressed through the consultation, my decision will also have to take full account of the severe budgetary pressures currently being experienced by my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide a detailed breakdown of the costs incurred by her Department in response to Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: My Department estimated the costs incurred in response to Storm Éowyn as detailed in the table below.Spending AreaResource£mCapital£mNI Water (overtime, repairs, alternative water supply)3.41.8Translink (lost income, repairs and remedial action)23Driver and Vehicle Agency (repairs and vehicle/driving test cancellations)0.2-Waterways Ireland (repairs and remedial action)0.1-Department (overtime, repairs, remedial action, lost income)3.53.2Total9.28

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will consider increasing funding for the Rates Support Grant, particularly for less well-off local council areas.
answer_text: The NI Executive’s draft budget for 2025/26 has been issued and it is presently out for consultation prior to finalisation.  The funding position for my Department remains constrained, as was the case in 2024/25. While the RSG is a statutory grant, the amount allocated is discretionary and is not ring-fenced. I will need to consider the RSG allocation in the context of my Department’s overall budget position, once confirmed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans to improve the support available for women suffering pregnancy loss in Emergency Departments, including access to dedicated facilities and specialist staff.
answer_text: My Department provides information and support for women suffering pregnancy loss in Emergency Departments in Northern Ireland. Early Pregnancy Clinics are operational in each of the five Trusts across Northern Ireland. These clinics are equipped with telephone triage and self-referral triage systems that help to divert those experiencing pregnancy loss away from Emergency Departments.However, it is acknowledged that it is not currently possible to provide 24/7 Early Pregnancy Clinics, which means that not all patients can avail of this service when needed. In situations where patients are admitted to a gynaecology ward, staff are highly sensitive to the needs of mothers and families during the time of a miscarriage or loss of a baby. Efforts are made to admit women into quieter side wards during these difficult times.While there are efforts to provide dedicated facilities and specialist staff, the availability of such resources can vary, and there may be occasions when a mother may have to be placed in a ward where there are babies nearby, due to the availability of rooms. However, staff do their utmost to provide a private room and the necessary support during these times .In Northern Ireland, Health and Social Care Trusts have established ambulatory care models with a focus on gynaecology and early pregnancy services, which can reduce presentations at Emergency Departments and referrals to gynae waiting lists. Through regional planning structures, it is envisaged that further work to develop appropriate service models and enhance existing services across NI Trusts will be progressed through 2025, subject to available staff capacity and resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of claimants receiving Discretionary Housing Payments in each of the last five years, broken down by (i) payment brackets from £5 up to £20 per week; and (ii) local council area.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW/23364/22-27 the information has been placed in the assembly library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of Tax Credit claimants who incurred an overpayment due to migration to Universal Credit, including the total cost of these overpayments, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: As Tax Credits are administered by HMRC, my Department does not hold a breakdown of how Tax Credits overpayments have occurred.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of initial applications to Personal Independence Payment that have been rejected; and (ii) the number of these rejected applications were subsequently successful upon appeal, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The most recent Personal Independence Payment statistics were published on 26 February 2025 and cover the period September 2024 to 30 November 2024. (i)The total number of initial applications to Personal Independence Payment that were disallowed in the last five years is shown in the table below:YearsPIP claims disallowed2019/202016,6102020/202111,2302021/202213,9102022/202312,1002023/202417,930April 2024 to November 202410,680		(ii) My Department does not hold appeals information in the format requested.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement he has had with Housing Associations in relation to rent increases at their properties and the shortfall of current housing benefit rates.
answer_text: I have not engaged directly with Registered Housing Associations (RHAs) in relation to these issues. It is a matter for individual Housing Association Boards to determine appropriate rents levels and to consider and approve any rent increases. 		The rents set are accepted as eligible rents for Housing Benefit and Universal Credit Housing Costs purposes unless a restriction applies due to the application of the Social Sector Size Criteria.  		For those Housing Benefits recipients who are impacted by a Social Sector Size Criteria percentage reduction (commonly known as the bedroom tax), a mitigation payment equal to the amount of the deduction is automatically made.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the details of Transitional Protection payments for Universal Credit claimants, including the duration of this support.
answer_text: Move to Universal Credit (managed migration) claimants who make a claim to Universal Credit within one month of the deadline date outlined on their migration notice are eligible to be considered for Transitional Protection.Transitional protection is made up of a transitional capital disregard and a transitional element. The transitional capital disregard enables a Move to Universal Credit claimant moving from tax credits to claim Universal Credit while having over £16,000 in capital for 12 assessment periods. If their capital or savings remains above £16,000 after the 12 assessment periods have passed, they will no longer be eligible for Universal Credit. The transitional element is calculated by comparing the total amount of all legacy benefit(s) the claimant had been awarded with the total amount of Universal Credit they would be entitled to. Where the Universal Credit amount is lower, the transitional element will be paid to make up the difference and will be paid as part of the Universal Credit award.An increase in earnings will not erode a claimant’s transitional element. While the transitional element entitlement remains the same the award of Universal Credit may be lower due to increased earnings.The transitional element is not time limited and is not increased by the annual uprating of benefits. However, it is eroded by the uprating of all other Universal Credit allowances and additional amounts in the award with the exception of the additional amount for childcare. When this occurs, the transitional element will erode penny for penny in the assessment period that the increase is paid.Transitional Protection stops in the following circumstances:couples forming;couples separating;when an increase in Universal Credit is greater than the amount of Transitional Protection in payment;the claimant has earnings equal to or above the Administrative Earnings Threshold (AET) in their first assessment period and the claimant’s earnings then drop below the AET for more than 3 consecutive assessments periods at any time during the Universal Credit claim;the Universal Credit award is terminated.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) for his assessment of the cost of home insurance in the Foyle constituency, specifically the BT47 and BT48 postcode areas; and (ii) whether any comparative analysis is available for insurance increases in each constituency area.
answer_text: ANSWERI am deeply concerned about the cost of home insurance for our citizens right across the North, adding further financial pressure to households. I met with the Association of British Insurers (ABI) on 13 February 2025 and pressed for the industry to do all it can to control the cost of insurance, including home, motor and business insurance. I also pressed the ABI on the need for local data in this regard. However, the ABI have advised that data on the cost of home insurance is not currently available at constituency or postcode level to be able to make this assessment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the total number of applications to the Affordable Warmth Scheme; (ii) the number of successful applications; and (iii) the waiting times from approval to payment, for each local council area in the last five years.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 23146/22-27, the information has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to ensure that care homes have appropriate staffing levels at night in order to guarantee the safety and well-being of residents.
answer_text: When a person moves to a care home it remains their home in their community. A key focus is therefore to ensure a secure environment where the quality of care provided to all residents is safe and effective. A crucial element in delivering safe and effective care is ensuring appropriate staffing levels.The Care Standards for Nursing Homes (December 2022) aim to improve the quality and consistency of care for people living in nursing homes. They provide the criteria for registration and inspection set out in the Nursing Homes Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2005. Standards are used by providers to set a benchmark of quality care and also by the RQIA in registering and inspecting nursing home services. Safe and effective staffing in care homes is set out in “Standard 41” of the Care Standards for Nursing Homes. This Standard stipulates that the number and ratio of staff on duty at all times meet the care needs of residents. There are a range of evidence-based staffing tools which providers currently use to inform nursing and healthcare capacity in conjunction with the Minimum Standards and which are approved by the regulator and NICE (National Institute for Clinical Excellence).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the reasons for the delay in implementing the Housing Executive planned cavity wall insulation for properties at Westway, Derry; and (ii) the time frame for the commencement of these works.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The even numbered Housing Executive-owned dwellings in Westway have been included as one of 4 schemes in a planned major improvement programme that goes beyond the replacement of cavity wall insulation.The first of these 4 schemes is Creggan Heights which is currently out to tender and subject to a successful tender exercise will go on site in 2025/26.  The delivery of the required works in this scheme will provide a model for the other three schemes.The works and costs involved in these schemes is very considerable, and the Housing Executive does not have sufficient funding with which to deliver the other 3 schemes at or close to the same time as Creggan Heights.  In addition, there are other capital improvement schemes elsewhere in our stock against which this programme must be prioritised.  The timing of these schemes will be considered via our annual programme review due to commence shortly.Consequently, it is not possible at this time to definitively advise when the scheme for Westway will start.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any engagement he has had with Executive colleagues in relation to securing appropriate funding for the Core Grant Scheme 2025-26.
answer_text: In my department’s response to the DoF Budget 2025-28 Information Gathering exercise we made it clear additional funding of over £1 billion would be required to maintain existing commitments and allow for a modest 2025-26 pay award.We highlighted that if funding to cover our inescapable pressures was not provided in our budget allocation this would require the implementation of a significant number of savings measures that will be contrary to the interests of health and social care, requiring us to actively shrink the HSC and resulting in more pressures on services and staff and yet more gaps in provision at a time when the clamour is understandably to invest further.All of Health’s funding requirements were shared with Executive colleagues by DoF to aid our discussions on agreeing the Draft Budget. However, the outcome for my department has resulted in a budget allocation significantly less than required to maintain existing services and we are facing a funding gap of some £400 million.Difficult choices will need to be made in the face of the financial position, and my priority will be to protect and maintain existing services where possible. While I would have liked to have been able to direct additional funding towards the Core Grant Scheme in 2025-26, at this time my focus will be to maintain the Core Grant at 2024-25 levels and thereby continue to provide much needed support to the community and voluntary sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will engage with the Department for Work and Pensions to find a solution for Health and Social Care staff and education staff on Universal Credit to ensure they are not adversely affected by backdated or one-off pay awards.
answer_text: The amount of Universal Credit a person receives is likely to be reduced if their earnings increase, including the receipt of backpay or one-off pay awards.However, based on individual circumstances, a person may be eligible for a work allowance enabling them to earn a certain amount before their Universal Credit payments are reduced. Claimants will also keep £0.45 of every £1.00 they earn over the work allowance.Further information on how Universal Credit treats earnings can be found at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/universal-credit-if-youre-employedThe rules on the treatment of backdated or one-off pay awards, apply equally throughout the United Kingdom to all Universal Credit claimants who receive earned income. The Department has ongoing engagement on the design and delivery of benefits, including Universal Credit.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether ongoing investment by Derry City Football Club in the Brandywell Stadium can be considered as Partnership Funding for the Northern Ireland Football Fund, as opposed to previous Capital Improvements through own investment.
answer_text: Previous investment in a performance club ground cannot be included as partnership funding for projects proposed under The Northern Ireland Football Fund. Project business cases, setting out total project costs, will be required to underpin final approvals. Business cases are prepared to inform expenditure decisions about which decisions can still be made. Expenditures that have already been incurred cannot be included in proposed project costs within business cases.I recognise that some organisations were compelled to proceed with investment in their facilities during the intervening period before I progressed The Northern Ireland Football Fund. They were unable to hold funding which might otherwise have been contributed as partnership funding. The Performance Programme’s application therefore provides applicants with an opportunity to score points for previous investment to deliver capital improvements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the rationale for setting the Partnership Funding criterion for the Northern Ireland Football Fund at 40 per cent for council-owned grounds and 5 per cent for other eligible applicants.
answer_text: Partnership funding is required under The Northern Ireland Football Fund’s Performance Programme to ensure that government is a funder of last resort. The minimum percentage requirement was determined following engagement with stakeholders, financial scoping to consider available information for potential applicants and benchmarking work to consider other similar schemes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 22849/22-27, if the Education Authority does not hold information that would identify employees on benefits, whether they can implement a phased payment approach to ensure fairness for all staff.
answer_text: The Pay & Grading agreement reached by my Department, Management Side and Trade Unions Side of the JNC, included a commitment to making a non-consolidated payment to staff in April 2025.  Details of this agreement can be found at: https://www.education-ni.gov.uk/publications/pay-and-grading-review-agreement The agreement was subsequently endorsed by Northern Ireland support staff.  I therefore intend to ensure that payments are made in line with the commitments given.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, following the recent contamination of Glenmornan River, to detail any measures he is taking to prevent the contamination of waterways.
answer_text: Water resources are managed through an integrated catchment approach as outlined in the draft 3rd cycle River Basin Management Plan for Northern Ireland.  The Plan includes the Programme of Measures to protect and improve our waterways through regulation, education and policy initiatives like the innovative Soil Nutrient Health Scheme. 	The draft River Basin Management Plan has been prepared, consulted on and reviewed in light of emerging information on water quality.  This includes the Office of Environmental Protection’s review of the implementation of the Water Framework Directive Regulations and River Basin Management Planning in Northern Ireland, the report from the Northern Ireland Audit Office on water quality in Northern Ireland’s rivers and lakes, and the Lough Neagh Report.  I have approved the third cycle River Basin Management Plan and subject to Executive approval, I will publish the plan in the near future.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients under the age of 18 who have required a mental health bed in each of the last five years, broken broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of mental health beds available for patients under the age of 18 in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Belfast Health and Social Care Trust is responsible for the management and provision of the two Regional Adolescent Mental Health Inpatient units named Beechcroft and Iveagh. Beechcroft is a 29 bedded unit providing 25 general adolescent inpatient beds and 4 Psychiatric Intensive Care Unit (PICU) beds. It has the capacity to treat 25 young people at any given time. This is due to criteria that states when a young person is being treated within PICU they need access to a general inpatient bed as PICU is for short stay treatment only.Iveagh is a 6 bedded unit for young people with a learning disability and additional mental health problems.  As Regional beds, places are not allocated per Trust but upon assessed clinical need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people diagnosed with an eating disorder between 2022 and 2025 had to travel outside Northern Ireland to access specialist hospital care.
answer_text: Belfast Health and Social Care Trust is responsible for the management and provision of the two Regional Adolescent Mental Health Inpatient units named Beechcroft and Iveagh. Beechcroft is a 29 bedded unit providing 25 general adolescent inpatient beds and 4 Psychiatric Intensive Care Unit (PICU) beds. It has the capacity to treat 25 young people at any given time. This is due to criteria that states when a young person is being treated within PICU they need access to a general inpatient bed as PICU is for short stay treatment only.Iveagh is a 6 bedded unit for young people with a learning disability and additional mental health problems.  As Regional beds, places are not allocated per Trust but upon assessed clinical need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of patients referred to Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service eating disorder services; and (ii) how many of these patients also have a diagnosis of ASD in each of the last three years.
answer_text: Child & Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS) are for young people up to 18 years, however there may be some who are over 18 years who are in the process of transitioning to Adult Services. Belfast Trust is responsible for the provision of the CAMHS waiting lists for South Eastern and Belfast Trust residents. As part of the Encompass roll-out, the South Eastern, Belfast and Northern Trusts have now gone live with a new electronic patient record system. As these Trusts continue to transition to completely digitised records, their data is undergoing extensive validation. Until this process is complete, there may be gaps in the data they are able to submit for reporting purposes.  Currently, Mental Health Waiting Times reports are under development in Encompass. answer_text: (i) Average waiting times are not collected by the Department. The figures below are active waits at a snapshot point in time across each Trust. Numbers below 5 (<5) have been suppressed for non-disclosure purposes. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 13868/22-27,  why it is not possible to arrange for payments to be staggered.
answer_text: The Pay & Grading settlement is a collective agreement between my Department, Management Side and the Trade Unions and I am committed to ensuring that payments are made in line with this agreement.  Officials in the Department and the Education Authority (EA) have investigated the impact that backdated or one-off payments may have on employees who claim means-tested benefits.  However, the EA does not hold the information that would identify the employees affected and benefits such as Universal Credit apply in different ways to different individuals. Therefore, there is no way to know what adjustment to pay would be required for anyone affected by this.  Trying to tailor payments on an individual basis would significantly complicate the process and lead to capacity issues for those administrating the pay roll.The UK NJC National Employers examined this issue as part of their last pay settlement and concluded that it would not be feasible for employers to resolve this issue through phased payments. Their advice is that any employees affected by this should speak to their Universal Credit Work Coach via their online account at Universal Credit Service Centre | nidirect.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the allocated funding for Occupational Therapy services in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether his Department will work to establish a local connection requirement for homeless individuals seeking emergency housing to ensure they can more readily access support services.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“There are no plans to establish a local connection requirement. The Housing Executive provides housing and homelessness assistance on a Northern Ireland wide basis.” For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 11746/22-27, for an update on funding for the four discretionary Home Improvement Grants.
answer_text: The Housing Executive received a budget allocation of £14.751m for Home Improvement Grants for the current financial year (2024/25).  This funding covers two statutory grants, the Disabled Facilities Grant (DFG) and Repair Grant, as well as the discretionary grants. The discretionary grants administered by the Housing Executive are currently only awarded in exceptional circumstances and are subject to budget availability within the overall pot of home improvement grant funding provided to the Housing Executive (which also covers statutory grants). The Housing Executive estimate that £13.91m of this budget will be spent on funding Disabled Facilities Grants this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 19678/22-27, for an update on the recommendations outlined in the Speech and Language Workforce Review 2019-2029, specifically future workforce requirements.

To ask the Minister for Communities, following media reports relating to a support scheme for traders in the Sandy Row area, whether he will look at the potential for a similar scheme to support businesses in Foyle Street, Derry, impacted by prolonged closure of the street to facilitate upgrades to water and sewerage infrastructure by NI Water.
answer_text: My Department presently has no plans for a Public Realm or a Revitalisation scheme in the Foyle Street area of Londonderry.  However Departmental officials will continue to engage with key stakeholders to include Derry City and Strabane District Council in identifying and agreeing future regeneration priorities across the city and the wider Council area going forward.		Revitalisation schemes normally follow on from the delivery of Public Realm schemes and are a means, for example, to deliver improved building frontages, carry out additional cleansing, and promote the area as a shopping destination.   Revitalisation schemes are not compensation schemes arising from the impacts of wider infrastructure works.  		In the case of the Sandy Row area of Belfast, there was a significant justification to support a Revitalisation scheme in advance of the planned Public Realm works due to the unprecedented levels of traffic disruption associated with the redevelopment of Grand Central Station.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 21150/22-27, whether she will consider the Marlborough area, Derry, for a residents' parking scheme.
answer_text: Following the publication of the Residents’ Parking Review report on 29 November 2024, Minister O’Dowd previously committed to continue to develop the schemes already commenced such as in the Bogside in Derry and Iveagh area of Belfast and will give consideration to schemes in the vicinity of the new Ulster University campus in Belfast and the Magee campus.During the development of the report, and following publication, there has been a significant level of interest for the establishment of residents’ parking zones across the north.Given this level of demand, and ongoing budgetary and resource constraints, I have asked my officials to expedite the development of an application and assessment process for residents’ parking schemes to ensure that any requests can be appropriately considered. This process will take into account the findings and recommendations of the report and will provide a streamlined approach for considering, assessing and prioritising requests going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there are any plans to introduce a positron emission tomography scanner at the North West Cancer Centre.
answer_text: The Regional Medical Imaging Board (RMIB) submitted a proposal for a third PET CT Scanner for Northern Ireland, to be located in the Western HSC Trust, at the North West Cancer Centre.My ability to deliver this PET CT Scanner is dependent on the budget allocations my Department receives.  The proposed capital allocation for my Department in 2025/26 is £391 million and does not provide for new capital investment, unless the final budget settlement provides an increased allocation.The Trust will be invited to prepare a business case, once budget availability is confirmed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of non-standard placements in temporary accommodation in each of the last five financial years, including the 2024-25 year to date, broken down by (i) local council area; and (ii) household type.
answer_text: The biannual Northern Ireland Homelessness Bulletin April – September 2024, published on 12 December 2024, contains information on a range of areas relating to homelessness and is available at https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-homelessness-bulletin-april-september-2024The accompanying tables in section 3 contain details on temporary accommodation placements by type of accommodation and by household type; however, I am unable to provide a breakdown in the format you have requested.

answer_text: The biannual Northern Ireland Homelessness Bulletin April – September 2024, published on 12 December 2024, contains information on a range of areas relating to homelessness and is available at https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-homelessness-bulletin-april-september-2024The accompanying tables at Section 3 contain details on temporary accommodation placements; however, I am unable to provide a breakdown in the format you have requested.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 19771/22-27, for an update on the provision of Givinostat for children with Duchenne muscular dystrophy.
To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will consider an uplift in the £23,000 annual gross income cap for the Affordable Warmth Scheme to reflect an increase in the National Minimum Wage.
answer_text: The Department is currently developing a new scheme, which will replace the existing Affordable Warmth Scheme, providing continued support to low-income households whilst also reducing emissions and increasing efficiency. Pre-consultation engagement events were held between April-June 2024, and the feedback gathered from stakeholders has formed part of the Fuel Poverty Strategy public consultation. Key aspects of the new scheme, including increased income eligibility and grant thresholds to better reflect average household income levels, are being consulted on as part of the current public consultation which closes on 6 March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 21581/22-27, to detail the total costs, inclusive of rates, in each of the last three years.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any measures in place to monitor staffing ratios in day opportunities to ensure that service users receive their full care entitlement.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) are responsible for the planning and delivery of Day Opportunities aligned to assessed needs. Assessed need is determined by a Named Worker and together with the service user and carer, a support plan will be put in place outlining care entitlement. It is the role of the Named Worker to monitor that each service user is in receipt of their care entitlement. This is completed in line with Care Management processes, usually by completion of an annual Care Management review. An assessment of need will normally not specify for someone to have a specific staffing ratio. This will only happen in exceptional circumstances, for example if Deprivation of Liberty Safeguards are in place and the service user requires close supervision, in keeping with the Mental Capacity (NI) Act 2016. This is monitored by the Named Worker in keeping with processes and timeframes outlined within the Mental Capacity (NI) Act 2016 and is not applicable to Day Opportunities. Another circumstance where an individual may require specified individual staffing ratios is when it is determined that they required regulated service provision. The monitoring of regulated services is the role of the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA), who determine ratio of staff to service users within these facilities. For Learning Disability Adult Centres staffing ratios are in keeping with RQIA Day Care Settings Minimum Standards, 2021. This is not applicable to Day Opportunities.Individuals who access non-regulated services such as Day opportunities do not normally have a requirement for individual staffing ratios. Day opportunities cater for a range of needs and therefore operate with  varying levels of staffing based on the service that they provide. For example the staffing required to deliver Social Farming will be different from the level required to deliver Performing Arts. The staffing levels are monitored through contractual review arrangements and will be in keeping with the specified level that will have been outlined by the Trust in the service specification, during the procurement process. Named workers will support service users to determine which opportunity best meets their needs and will monitor this through Care Management processes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline her plans to ease financial pressures on local businesses.
answer_text: Question 11 AQO 1595/22-27The local business community has expressed serious concerns about the impact of the British Government’s Autumn Statement. While the Statement brought welcome benefits for workers with increases in the National Living Wage and National Minimum Wage; rather than offsetting this increase in pay with a decrease in taxes, the Budget also increased employer National Insurance contributions. I am also aware of business concerns around the issues of rates and VAT. These will be examined as part of the Cost of Doing Business research which I commissioned as Finance Minister.  This evidence will be used to strengthen our lobbying efforts in London and to inform any potential actions on behalf of the Executive.Businesses are now reporting that financial pressures are mainly driven by rising labour costs, with the rate of input cost inflation in January accelerating at the fastest pace in almost two years.DfE and its agencies provide a wide range of support to local companies to generate growth. These include dedicated financial offers of support from Invest NI, upskilling the workforce through FE and HE provision and Tourism NI and Tourism Ireland encouraging visitors to visit and spend here, whilst supporting industry development.Making businesses more sustainable also makes economic sense. My department lead on the delivery of the Energy Strategy and last year saw the launch of an Energy & Resource Efficiency Programme through Invest NI which provides essential support for businesses to reduce operating costs whilst also creating economic opportunities in the green economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Mobuoy Remediation Project.
answer_text: A draft remediation strategy has been developed, in line with an industry standard process, for the long-term remediation of the Mobuoy site. The next step will be a public consultation.I have instructed  my officials to move forward with that consultation which at present is intended to issue in early spring. However, I am mindful that the criminal case proceedings, remain live. A plea and sentencing hearing is scheduled by the court for the 7 March 2025. The size and complexity of the Mobuoy site means that its remediation will not be a quick fix and will be subject to affordability considerations in the context of the constraints of public sector finances. There is no agreed preferred option, and there will not be until a consultation has been completed and responses carefully considered. It is vitally important step, and I encourage all those interested to consider and respond to the consultation.Any subsequent implementation plan and timetable for remediation remains subject to the outcome the consultation and will be considered in the context of the Department’s responsibility to protect water quality and the extremely challenging constraints of public finances. In the meantime, the NIEA continues to implement a comprehensive Environmental Monitoring Programme (EMP) at the site. This includes site and riverbank inspections, surface water and groundwater monitoring, together with daily laboratory testing of water quality at the NI Water drinking water abstraction point.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice when will the assessment into the required number of police officers and PSNI civilian staff be concluded.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 15 - AQO 1614/22-27ANSWERThe assessment of police officers and staff is an operational matter for the Chief constable who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  That said, I can confirm through discussions with the Board and the Chief Constable, it has been agreed that the Chief Constable’s initial assessment of the required numbers of police officers and staff would not be achievable within this mandate, given the current funding issues and the capacity issues of the Police College.  Therefore, focus has turned to what is achievable.The focus is now on recovering workforce levels to 7,000 police officers and 2,572 police staff over the next three years. Discussions will be ongoing as will further assessments and reviews. This was discussed at the tripartite meeting I had with the Chief Constable and the Chair of the Northern Ireland Policing Board which took place on 20 February.  The recovery of police numbers is a priority for all tripartite members.I can confirm that my officials have been working closely with the PSNI to develop a robust business case for the funding required to recover workforce numbers over the course of the current mandate and I am pleased to confirm that this business case has been submitted to the Department of Finance (DoF) for its consideration.  I should stress that affordability remains a key issue in terms of business case approval.  Subject to the DoF approval, I will be making a case to the Executive for the additional funding required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail what steps he is taking to address the impact of private renting on poverty levels.
answer_text: I recognise that rents and housing costs are generally a household’s largest single expense. 		As mandated by the Assembly, I have instructed my officials to commence the power to restrict the frequency of rent increases to once in any 12-month period, with a 3-month written notice. Commencing this legislation will assist low-income tenants by providing an assurance that they will not face multiple or unexpected rent increases over a short period of time.	In parallel, I will continue to advocate for the Local Housing Allowance (LHA) rate to keep pace with market rents, as I am acutely aware of the impact that the last freeze had on affordability for lower income tenants.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any measures his Department is taking to ensure the effective regulation of Houses in Multiple Occupation, including any issues related to their impact on local communities.
answer_text: Houses in Multiple Occupation (HMOs) are an important part of our housing supply and provide homes for many people who may otherwise struggle to find suitable accommodation.  The Houses in Multiple Occupation Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 transferred the operational responsibility for Houses in Multiple Occupation (HMOs) to councils and introduced a licensing scheme. 	Under the NI Houses in Multiple Occupation Act, landlords or agents must have good management policies and procedures in place to make sure:	physical standards are maintained	occupiers' rights are respected, and	any problems which arise during the period of the licence are effectively addressed.	They must also be able to manage issues which may concern neighbours effectively (such as building maintenance, cleaning, noise or disturbance and suitability of the applicant or agent).		Councils consider a landlord’s potential to manage these issues (or past performance) when deciding whether to grant a licence.		Councils must also consider planning permission for Houses in Multiple Occupation and whether the granting of an HMO licence would result in overprovision of this type of accommodation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether consideration has been given to ensure cancer patients, who are immunosuppressed, are fast-tracked for treatment when presenting at Emergency Departments.
answer_text: It is recognised that one of the many potential side effects of cancer and its treatments is a suppressed immune system, or immunosuppression.  Oncology and surgical teams who manage cancer patients strive to minimise the need for emergency department visits.  Every hospital trust in Northern Ireland has a 24-hour Systemic Anti-Cancer Therapy (SACT) telephone advice service.   Patients are encouraged to use this service to contact their treating unit if they feel unwell during, or within, six weeks of completing SACT treatment (SACT includes chemotherapy and immunotherapies), or within 12 months of completing immunotherapy treatment.   Similarly, cancer patients who have undergone recent surgical treatment receive verbal and written discharge advice, which includes instructions on how to access the surgical team and under what circumstances they may need to seek emergency care.  When a cancer patient presents at an Emergency Department (ED), they will be triaged, stabilised and assessed by the ED team based on clinical priority within the physical and staffing constraints of the ED.   If necessary, the ED team will contact the relevant oncology or surgical team to assess and admit the patient.  In addition, each Trust has an Acute Oncology Service (AOS) that aims to provide a coordinated approach to the care of cancer patients suspected of having a first diagnosis in the emergency setting or those admitted acutely ill due to the disease itself or treatment complications. Currently, this service operates on a five-day, 9am – 5pm model across all Trusts.  Extending the acute oncology service to seven-day working is a recommendation from the Northern Ireland Cancer Strategy; however, it has not been progressed due to budgetary constraints.  Work is, however, still progressing to assess demand and develop appropriate pathways, with costed proposals for extending the service to be considered based on available funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in light of ongoing postal delays, what steps are being taken to ensure patients are notified of hospital appointments via telephone, text message, or email.
answer_text: Each Trust has mitigating actions in place to ensure patients are advised of their outpatient appointments in a timely manner.In the Western Trust, all Departments within Unscheduled Care and Medicine, Cancer and Diagnostics are fully aware of the current delays within the Post office and are making alternative arrangements such as text alerts and telephone contact.The Belfast Trust has been holding ongoing and regular meetings in place with the provider to monitor the service provision, address any issues which may arise and mitigate against service failures.As part of the Integrated Elective Access Policy (IEAP) that applies to all Trusts, Northern Trust patients are invited to call the Trust to agree an outpatient appointment and this is followed up by an appointment letter confirming the date and time of the appointment.  An interim process has been put in place to contact all patients, by telephone, receiving an appointment with less than two weeks’ notice.  The South Eastern Trust acute specialties issue text reminders about appointments. While, Mental Health Outpatients do not routinely issue reminders. Contact by telephone occurs for those people known to services who benefit from encouragement to attend. It is envisaged that MyCare will be used to keep people updated regarding appointments.In the Southern Trust when patients are being booked for an 'acute' hospital out-patient appointment they will either be directly booked or partially booked.  Through the partial booking process a patient will receive one letter which gives them 2-weeks to respond.  If they have not responded they will get a 2nd letter which give them 1-week to respond.  If they fail to respond they will be discharged and a letter will be sent to the patient and their GP.  The patient or GP, on the patient's behalf, can request to be added back on to the waiting list if they make contact within a 4-week time period.  For those booked for out-patient appointments, from an 'acute' perspective, text message appointment reminders are sent to patients coming for an out-patient appointment 7-days prior to the appointment date and 1-day prior to the appointment date.This is currently only funded to be used within the 'acute' out-patient setting but could be rolled out across all operational Directorates should funding be made available.When patients are being booked for a community AHP appointment they will be partially booked.  Through the partial booking process a patient will receive one letter which gives them 3 weeks to respond.  If a new patient for assessment does not respond they will be discharged and a letter will be sent to the patient and their referrer and/or GP.  If a patient awaiting a review appointment does not respond to the partial booking letter, advice will be taken from a clinician as to how to proceed. The patient may be offered another appointment, remain on the waiting list or discharged.  If they are discharged a letter will be sent to the patient an

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the review and guidance on Discretionary Housing Payments.
answer_text: There have been no significant changes to the Discretionary Financial Assistance Regulations (NI) 2001 for several years. Therefore, the grounds on which an award of Discretionary Housing Payment may be made, remain unchanged. There is currently no review of Discretionary Housing Payments.  The Discretionary Housing Payment guide has been updated by the Housing Executive and is currently with the Department to provide feedback on some minor changes to the guide.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he plans to conduct a review into the Education Maintenance Allowance.
answer_text: The Education Maintenance Allowance (EMA) scheme is a joint policy for which responsibility is shared between my Department and the Department for the Economy (DfE) and any future plans on this cross-cutting issue would be agreed between both Departments. The Minister for the Economy and I do not currently have plans in place to conduct a joint review of the EMA policy. I do, however, recognise the valuable support that EMA provides to enable young people to remain in education or training and my officials will continue to discuss support for these young people with DfE officials.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will implement a phased payment approach as part of the backdated Health and Social Care staff payment 2024-25 to accommodate those in receipt of Universal Credit.
answer_text: The processing of any pay award is complex, with many different calculations required to ensure that all salary-based allowances are paid at the correct rate and that staff will receive their correct payment of arrears. The HSC has over 60,000 WTE employees, and phasing the payments due to staff as a result of the 2024/25 pay deal would require significant time for Business Services Organisation to process and would delay the pay award to staff for a period of months.Any additional income may have unwelcome implications for any individual in receipt of income assessed benefits. Individuals in receipt of Universal Credit should seek advice from their work coach or contact the service centre on 0800 0121 331, as neither my Department, nor individual employers can offer advice on benefit entitlements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the Medicines Regulatory Group, to detail the total number of (i) investigations; (ii) prosecutions; and (iii) pending prosecutions around the unlawful possession, importation, advertisement or supply of unlicensed prescription medicinal products for use in the non-surgical cosmetic sector, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: My Department remains committed in its efforts to combat unlawful promotion, supply or misuse of medicines, including those identified for use in the non-surgical cosmetic sector.Where evidence or information suggests unlawful practice is occurring, my Department’s Medicines Regulatory Group (MRG) will consider a range of enforcement options proportionate to the circumstances to achieve compliance, including for example through the issuing of warning or advice, referral to professional bodies, or prosecution.My Department cannot disclose all information in respect of its investigative work. However, I can confirm during the period 2022 - 2024 inclusive, MRG has conducted 102 investigations focusing on the unlawful possession, advertisement and/or supply of counterfeit or unlicensed medicinal products for use within the non-surgical cosmetic sector. During this period, eight persons have been successfully prosecuted and a further six prosecutions are pending.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQO 1353/22-27, for an update on his Department's collaboration with the Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime, specifically work relating to illegal money lending.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for taking forward two strands of work that directly contribute to the Executive’s Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime (EPPOC).  These are the Fresh Start in the Community Projects and the Developing Women in the Community Programme.In relation to illegal money lending, my Department’s engagement with EPPOC has involved highlighting and sharing information on the independent advice services funded by my Department. This collaboration ensured that the EPPOC-funded “Ending the Harm” public campaign to raise awareness on the impact of illegal money lending also highlighted the support available to those affected.  The dedicated website at www.endingtheharm.com provides links to a number of organisations including the Independent Advice Network and the Advice NI helpline which can support victims by offering confidential, free, independent debt advice.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of state pension overpayment requests issued to bereaved relatives following the death of a claimant; (ii) the total cost of these overpayments; and (iii) the amount recouped, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Department is unable to provide the information in the format requested.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) why the Housing Executive does not use self-contained accommodation for emergency usage; and (ii) whether there is an official Housing Executive policy which precludes their use.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive regularly avails of self-contained accommodation through the provision of Single Lets with the most recent published figures noting a total of 2,957 households having been placed in Single Lets as of November 2024. The Housing Executive is keen to avail of any opportunity to maintain access to, and to increase, its portfolio of Single Lets, as evidenced by a number of recent social media campaigns asking providers to get in contact if they were interested in providing Single Lets for use by the Housing Executive. However, where accommodation is being offered on arrangements similar to short-term holiday let accommodation, this is categorised as non-standard temporary accommodation. In such circumstances it is subject to the same considerations in terms of being used in the absence of all other options and for as short a duration as possible. While there is not an official Housing Executive policy which precludes their use, any decision to use short-term holiday let accommodation will consider a range of factors including costs and the impact on our Single Let portfolio.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.
