question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the enforcement powers available to her Department in relation to overgrown trees or vegetation on private land that obstruct public roads and footpaths.
answer_text: My Department does not have a statutory duty regarding the condition of roadside trees or hedges, however it does have a statutory duty to maintain the public road network, which includes ensuring that boundary trees or hedges do not overgrow the roadway and footway, thereby impeding road users and pedestrians.Most roadside trees and hedges are on lands adjacent to public roads and it is the responsibility of property owners or occupiers of those lands to ensure that such trees and hedges/shrubs do not endanger or obstruct road users including pedestrians. From time to time, my Department will place notices in the local press and in farming journals reminding owners/occupiers of this responsibility. If a tree or hedge is suspected of being a hazard, owners / occupiers are identified and are requested to co-operate in removing or cutting the trees or hedges to address any concerns in relation to public safety. If that approach does not achieve the desired outcome, my Department has powers under Article 50 of the Roads (Northern Ireland) Order 1993 to serve a notice on the owner/occupier requiring him to remove or cut back the tree or hedge so as to remove the danger or obstruction. Where an owner / occupier does not comply with the requirements of the notice, my Department may carry out the necessary works and seek to recover costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 17816/22-27, for an update on the publication of condition insight reports.
answer_text: It is expected that Capita will publish the Condition Insight Reports (CIRs) by 21st March 2025. The PIP CIRs will be published on Capita’s Health Assessment Advisory Service website. Updated CIRs are in use within the current Functional Assessment Services contract. Capita do not have consent to publish these as this information is intellectual property of the Department for Work & Pensions (DWP) . However, a copy of the CIRs can be requested from DWP under a Freedom of Information request.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will give consideration to renaming the Contingency Fund to improve accessibility for claimants.
answer_text: Plans to change the Universal Credit Contingency Fund name are currently being taken forward by officials within my Department. It is expected that the change in name will be implemented by the end of this financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 16780/22-27, for an update on the District Valuer wider review.
answer_text: To date only three applications requesting a review of the Net Annual Value (NAV) by the District Valuer have been received from businesses located in the area. Land & Property Services (LPS) wrote to the three ratepayers on 26th November 2024 asking for additional information to support their applications. To date only one ratepayer has responded, reminders will be issued to the others shortly. The District Valuer is also gathering information such as footfall figures on the affected street. This supplementary data will form part of the review of the current applications and whether a wider review of properties on Foyle Street is required.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide an update on any progress to establish an Infrastructure Commission, following the creation of a cross-departmental working group in early 2022.
To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 17008/22-27, to provide an indicative time frame for the completion of the temporary accommodation standards framework for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The development of a temporary accommodation standards framework is a commitment within the Strategic Action Plan for Temporary Accommodation which covers the period from April 2022 through to March 2027. While work has commenced on this framework, in order to ensure appropriate engagement with a range of stakeholders which includes customers and providers, it is anticipated that work on this will conclude in line with the conclusion of the overall action plan in March 2027.” For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has engaged with student accommodation providers in Belfast to explore the possibility of offering low-cost accommodation during off-peak months for parents of sick children who must travel for treatment.
To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 20551/22-27, for a breakdown of the total number of journeys made, categorised by their final destination within each local council area.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive business systems do not record the destination of the homeless taxi journeys undertaken, nor is information on the number of individual journeys undertaken readily accessible.Accordingly, the Housing Executive is unable to provide the information requested.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the steps his Department is taking to support social housing tenants struggling with rent arrears.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Support for Housing Executive tenants experiencing rent arrears is primarily delivered under the remit of the Housing Executive’s Financial Inclusion Strategy 2023-2028 and associated action plans. The aim of this strategy is to both: prevent rent arrears and enable tenants to get back on track should they fall into an arrear. The first point of contact for a tenant experiencing rent arrears is the Patch Manager. Rent arrears are flagged via the housing management system and function as a trigger point for the delivery of the Housing Executive’s Making Your Money Work service.  The service includes providing benefit and budget calculations, referrals to third parties for debt advice or floating support, and referrals to the in-house Financial Inclusion Team. New tenants will also be referred to the Financial Inclusion Team as necessary; the current referral triggers are:New tenants (arrears prevention) – All new tenants are offered the chance to avail of the Housing Executive Making Your Money Work service either before they sign for a new tenancy, during the sign-up process, or at their early tenancy visit (tenant choice). Tenants with rent arrears – all tenants reaching circa four weeks’ rent arrears are offered the Making Your Money Work Service which includes the provision of information about the Financial Inclusion Team and an offer of referral.All tenants who may be at risk of legal action to recover properties are referred to the Financial Inclusion Team.Tenants who have engaged with an external debt advice provider and been awarded a Debt Relief Order may be referred to the Financial Inclusion team to set them up for success going forward.Tenants without housing cost assistance are prioritised for Making Your Money Work referrals; 36.3% of all referrals made in 2023/24 were for tenants without housing cost assistance.Further to the above mandated trigger points for a financial inclusion intervention, frontline staff will also outline the Financial Inclusion Team’s work to customers who have particularly complex financial circumstances (e.g. disability/health status, foreign nationals, student, or self-employed), or when the tenant is asking multiple benefit related questions which may not be easily remedied by frontline staff. The service is also advertised to tenants in receipt of a housing cost benefit with dependent children in their property, tenants who have lost a housing cost benefit entitlement, tenants who have seen a reduction in their housing cost benefit entitlement, and those who have had a significant change in their circumstances which affects their financial wellbeing.The Financial Inclusion Team help tenants by:Providing benefits checks to make sure they are receiving everything they are entitled to;Performing ‘Better off’ calculations to help tenants make informed

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on any engagement he has had with Executive colleagues in relation to funding the Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme.
answer_text: I continue to engage and work extensively and collaboratively with Executive colleagues, the Health Committee and other Assembly members in all areas of health, including reduction of hospital waiting lists and the additional investment needed to tackle our unacceptable waits.	The Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme (RoIRS) was introduced on 30 June 2021 as part of the Elective Care Framework to help reduce waiting lists in Northern Ireland (NI). The scheme was a limited 12-month administrative version of the Cross-Border Healthcare Directive which ceased to apply to NI following the UK’s exit from the EU. A bid for funding for a replacement scheme was submitted during the 2024/25 budget setting process as part of a wider bid for £135million of additional funding to tackle waiting lists. It was intended that a new reimbursement scheme could have treated over 4,000 patients this financial year. 	Unfortunately, under the 2024/25 Budget, no additional funding was provided for tackling our waiting lists nor does the draft budget for 2025/26 provide any additional funding for waiting list reduction initiatives and the focus has to be on supporting those on cancer and time critical waiting lists.	As such, there is no funding available at present to reinstate a cross-border reimbursement scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on plans for Phase 3 of the Derry to Coleraine rail upgrade.
answer_text: I fully recognise the need to address regional imbalance and better connect our communities including those within the Northwest. In the 2024/25 budget, my predecessor ring fenced funding of £4.7m, which has allowed Translink to progress this project as planned. Work has continued including the production of the detailed design of the required track improvements and preparation for the procurement phase of the project. It has also included the manufacture of railway sleepers in advance of the main works.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the proposed plans for the widening of the A2 Buncrana Road, Derry.
answer_text: Major Road schemes are an important part of the work that is delivered by the Department as we seek to reduce journey times, increase reliability and improve road safety.The A2 Buncrana Road scheme will provide a key link in the North West and County Donegal. On 05 April 2023 work on the scheme was paused through a decision taken under the NI (Executive Formation etc) Act 2022, to allow appropriate alignment with the emerging transport plans. The A2 Buncrana Road scheme will be considered as part of the North West Transport Plan which will be subject to a public consultation process. Views from the public were sought to help shape the vision and objectives for the Transport Plan, as well as identifying local transport concerns in the district through public engagement which commenced on 27th August and concluded on 5th November 2024. The draft NWTP is expected to be published for public consultation in December 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for a breakdown of applications made to the Contingency Fund by those affected by the five-week wait for Universal Credit, detailing (i) the number of grants awarded; (ii) the number of loans issued; and (iii) total expenditure in each of the last five years.
answer_text: I refer the Member to my previous answer on this topic in AQW 14159-27 which provides the number of applications to the UCCF scheme and the number of grant awards made in each of the last 5 years.UCCF awards are made as non-repayable grants and are not awarded as loans.  The Department publishes an annual report which details Discretionary Support performance and expenditure during each financial year, including expenditure relating to the Universal Credit Contingency Fund. The annual reports can be accessed via the following website www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/welfare-supplementary-payments-discretionary-support-standards-advice-assistance-and-sanctions

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will engage with the Minister for Communities in relation to the delays in accessing occupational therapy services and its impact on applicants to the Disabled Facilities Grant.
answer_text: Partnership working between the Department of Health (DoH) and the Department of Communities (DfC) is essential for the provision of suitable support for disabled people in need of occupational therapy services and accessible homes.  The Disabled Facilities Grant (DFG) is funded by the DfC and administered by the Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE).  DoH is involved through HSC Trust occupational therapy services, which are responsible for establishing individuals’ needs and making recommendations for interventions via the grant process.A review of the DFG is currently underway, led by DfC with input from DoH, and includes a task group focusing on occupational therapy involvement in the process, including waiting times and workforce.  Once the review is complete later this year, recommendations will be made to both Ministers to improve access to and administration of the Grant.Recognising the importance of cross departmental working in these matters, we have created the post of Housing Adaptation Liaison Officer, split on a 50/50 basis between DoH and NIHE. The post-holder, an occupational therapist, provides strategic oversight and operational linkage of such matters and reports to the DoH Chief Allied Health Professions Officer and the NIHE Assistant Director Private Sector Investment & Sustainable Development respectively.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has conducted any research into discrimination in the private rented sector and refusals of tenancies for prospective tenants in receipt of housing benefits.
answer_text: My Department has not conducted research into discrimination in the private rented sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement he has had with the Minister of Health during this mandate in relation to improved access to supported living accommodation for vulnerable adults with disabilities.
answer_text: My Department funds the Supporting People Programme which provides housing support to vulnerable people including adults with disabilities. There has been ongoing engagement between my Department and the Department of Health on the issue of access to supported living accommodation for vulnerable adults and the interface with the Supporting People Programme.  My Department leads a Cross Departmental Policy Forum (with Department of Health, Department of Justice and the NI Housing Executive) that aims to enhance and support co-operation and engagement between the organisations involved in supported living services.  My Department also collaborates with Department of Health through its work in a Social Care Collaborative Forum to identify and undertake actions to address the needs and challenges related to supported living for individuals with diverse abilities and support requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any engagement he has had with the Minister for Communities during this mandate in relation to improved access to supported living accommodation for vulnerable adults with disabilities.
answer_text: There has been ongoing engagement between Health and the Department for Communities (DfC) on the issue of access to supported living accommodation for vulnerable adults and the interface with the Supporting People Programme.Through a Joint Review Project Board, my officials have alongside officials from DfC been reviewing the Supporting People programme, to better understand the financial planning arrangements to enable the development of joint funding arrangements for domiciliary care/supported housing/supported living services.My department is also leading work through the Social Care Collaborative Forum in respect of the future strategic direction of the provision of adult care and support services, including the redesign of the system that funds and delivers supported living through collaborate co-design with key stakeholders including DfC.The overarching aim of the Supported Living workstream of the Social Care Collaborative Forum is to identify and undertake actions to address the needs and challenges related to Supported Living for individuals with diverse abilities and support requirements. It will aim to develop and enhance supported living services by •	Exploring the issues and challenges of delivering supported living.•	Understanding the reach of supported living and the difference it makes in Northern Ireland.•	Considering best practice and alternative delivery models to enable vulnerable adults to live independently. •	Maximising opportunities to better collaborate in the delivery of jointly commissioned services.•	Developing proposals and recommendations to reshape how services are commissioned, regulated and delivered.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on work carried out by his Department on the future service provision for (i) childhood; and (ii) adult attention deficit hyperactivity disorder.
answer_text: (i) My Department has published the Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework for public consultation in May 2025. This Framework will guide future development of services. The Framework embraces a move towards supporting ADHD services within a broader inclusive neurodevelopmental approach that will incorporate regionally consistent single points of referral and diagnostic assessment pathways.Officials have met with all Trusts individually to map current paediatric children’s ADHD referral, assessment and intervention pathways. Department officials are working with colleagues from the Public Health Agency and Child Health Partnership to develop a regionally consistent and defined neurodevelopmental pathway that complies with NICE guidelines. The pathway will take into consideration, best practice, emerging evidence and the recognition that workforce planning is critical to meeting the needs of children and young people in a timely manner. A consistent pathway will inform future investment across all HSC Trusts to provide equitable and effective pathways, and reduce waiting times. This work is being overseen by the Joint Children’s Planning and Performance Team chaired by two Deputy Directors, one from my Department and one from the PHA. (ii) Regarding provision of adult services for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD), demand for assessment and diagnosis has grown across all Trusts.All Trusts are committed to working closely and collaboratively with commissioners to develop innovative, cost effective, co-produced, quality and evidence based services that can meet the needs of adults with ADHD within Trust areas. This work would include the development of clinical pathways.The Department has commissioned a research exercise into the current and projected demand with a view to bringing forward recommendations to inform a commissioned ADHD service, subject to future budget availability. This research has commenced, with an anticipated completion date of end June 2025.The exercise will seek to establish the prevalence of ADHD (for both children and adults) in Northern Ireland, examine best practice from other jurisdictions and identify challenges and barriers in relation to ADHD diagnosis and treatment. Importantly, it will be expected to make recommendations on what any future commissioned service might look like in terms of, for example, costs, staffing profiles and recommended care pathways.Any future commissioned service will be subject to both budget availability and evidenced demand, but the research, once completed, will help provide a firm evidence base to inform a future model.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to (i) re-band porter staff pay scales in the Western Health and Social Care Trust to Band 3 in light of their increased responsibilities; and (ii) address staff shortages within this service.
answer_text: Officials from my Department have been in contact with colleagues in HR in the Western trust, who have advised that The Western Health & Social Care Trust has a robust job evaluation process in place which facilitates any changed job to be assessed in line with the NHS Job Evaluation Handbook. The trust has confirmed that the Porter post has been assessed via the job evaluation process within the last 18 months. Following this process, Porters remain at Band 2. Managers, Trade Unions and the Trust job evaluation team are available to provide advice and support to any employee in relation to this outcome. The Trust does have a Charge Hand Porters role which is a Band 3; these are the Portering Supervisor roles.  The Trust is currently processing job evaluation requests from portering supervisors currently employed at Band 3.With respect to staff shortages, the Trust advises that it has undertaken a number of recruitment campaigns in the last 12 months to fill vacancies in portering teams Trustwide and there has been good progress made in many areas.  A number of new porters took up post in January 2025 on the Altnagelvin and Omagh Hospital & Primary Care Complex site and are now being supported through a period of induction and training.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 20456/22-27, for his assessment of the reduction in the uptake of HPV vaccinations.
answer_text: Northern Ireland has experienced a drop in HPV vaccination uptake rates in recent years, mainly as a result of the disruption to school vaccination services during the pandemic, although uptake rates have started to improve. The Public Health Agency (PHA) has established a multi-disciplinary working group to identify and implement how to improve vaccination uptake rates and my department is represented on this group.  The PHA is also a member of the ‘UK forum to facilitate discussion on declining coverage and inequalities’, which is chaired by the UK Health Security Agency. This groups brings together relevant stakeholders from across the UK nations and is exploring the likely contributing factors to declining coverage across the routine immunisation programmes, to identify common issues, discuss potential actions to address the issues, and facilitate the sharing of experience and learning across the UK nations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the street lighting columns at Greenhaw Road and Shantallow Avenue, Derry, can be retained as part of the current upgrade works in the area, in keeping with commitments to uphold community safety.
answer_text: My Department is currently replacing the street lights at Shantallow Avenue, Derry as the old installation is nearing the end of its serviceable life.When undertaking street lighting renewal schemes, my officials will carry out an assessment of the area to determine if it meets the current policy for the provision of street lighting. Replacement lighting will generally be designed and installed for those areas which meet this policy. I am conscious that street lighting can contribute significantly to feelings of personal security and safety, particularly for women travelling at night. This is an important consideration when officials are developing proposals to improve pedestrian and cycling facilities to key destinations with the aim of encouraging modal shift, such as routes to Park & Ride sites near towns.However, the desire for lighting has to be balanced against environmental considerations, our desire to reduce the carbon footprint and electricity costs associated with our infrastructure, and limited budgets. Decisions on provision or removal of lighting are made taking account of these considerations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the proposed restrictions for new drivers in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: GDL is a major resource-intensive undertaking, constituting fundamental reform of current driver training, testing and post-testing arrangements. Implementation will require substantial dedicated funding, significant operational planning activity and detailed supporting secondary legislation.My predecessor and I have committed to providing the required funding and my officials have resumed work on operational planning and the required Regulations. Once this work has progressed I will be able to provide a date for introduction.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the provisions in place to support prospective social tenants who have limited capacity to sign a tenancy agreement.
answer_text: In cases such as this, the relevant statutory authorities work collaboratively to identify solutions appropriate to each individual case. Tenancy agreements are legal contracts. Supported housing is provided for people with housing support needs who cannot live independently. This may include people whose support needs are such that they do not have capacity to enter into a legal contract.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps he is taking to provide sufficient funding for the Supporting People Programme, in light of inflation and the cost of the employers' National Insurance increase.
answer_text: The substantial increase to Employers’ Allowance included in the Chancellor’s recent Budget Announcement is expected to offset some of the increase to Employers’ National Insurance Contributions and my Department encourages all eligible organisations to claim this allowance.The issue of rising costs will be considered against the Department’s current and future priorities and the challenging financial climate. Work has begun within the Department to align budget allocations with priorities and Programme for Government objectives.  Final decisions will depend on the outcome of the budget consultation which ends in March.I am very aware of the value and impact of the Supporting People Programme, which enables people with housing support needs to live independently in the community. I have already demonstrated my commitment to funding this programme and I welcome the inclusion of the three year strategy within the draft Programme for Government.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the immediate steps that will be taken to deliver essential capital works at St Joseph’s Boys’ School, Derry, including urgent upgrades to toilet facilities.
answer_text: The school submitted an Unavoidable Minor Works application in February 2020 for refurbishment/upgrade of current toilet facilities. The application was not progressed at that time as it was not considered priority 1 i.e. a project that meets inescapable statutory requirements, such as health and safety, fire protection and statutory obligations under the Disability Discrimination Act. Following a site visit in June 2022, it was agreed that the most urgent works should be progressed, and emergency works to upgrade first floor toilets at a cost of £31k were subsequently carried out in summer 2023.The Education Authority’s Maintenance Service will continue to action critical health and safety issues which are logged with the maintenance helpdesk, including repairs to cubicles and locks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she has received an update from the Registrar General in relation to the examination of changes to the Human Fertilisation and Embryology Act 2008, to address problems facing couples who receive IVF treatment outside the UK.
answer_text: The Registrar General has contacted his counterparts in England, Wales and Scotland to ascertain the breadth and scale of this problem and establish if legislative change is required to the Human Fertilisation and Embryology Act 2008 to address birth registration problems facing couples who receive IVF treatment outside the UK.Officials in the other jurisdictions have advised that they have encountered no issues or complaints with regards the application of Section 43 of Human Fertilisation and Embryology Act 2008 and have advised they are not aware of any imminent planned changes in this Westminster legislation.If work to make changes to the legislation is required, discussion will be required between the various jurisdictions and will take time to progress. General Register Office’s immediate focus is on the Bill for the permanent replacement of the temporary provisions in the Coronavirus Act 2020 for the registration of deaths and still-births and the establishment of a Baby Loss Certificate Scheme. We will return to this work when resources permit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will work with the Education Authority to establish a consistent, region-specific approach for schools in response to weather warnings.
answer_text: The Education Authority has a regional approach in place for schools in response to weather warnings.  In the recent case of Storm Éowyn, when the RED weather warning was put in place by the Met Office, all schools were communicated to at the same time as the warning covered all areas of Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the total number of patients awaiting cataract surgery; and (ii) the average waiting times, in the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The current regional cataract surgical waiting list position is laid out in the Table below:Time-bandsDec 2024 Total613Over 13 weeks196Over 26 weeks88Over 30 weeks74Over 1 year29Over 2 years7Over 3 years2Over 4 years 0Note: waiting list figures for South Eastern Trust are only available up to October 2023, for Belfast Trust up to May 2024, and for Northern Trust up to November 2024.In order to address waiting lists and times, a network of three Cataract Day Procedure Centres was established in 2018/19 to act as a resource for the region, working across Trust boundaries, to equalise and reduce regional waits, and to avoid clinic and theatre short-notice cancellations in more acute hospital settings due to competition with unscheduled care, beds, and anaesthetic cover.The Day Procedure Centre cataract service has had a positive impact on waiting times. At the 31 March 2024 there were approximately 3,000 patients waiting on regional surgical waiting lists for a cataract procedure, of whom 1,325 were waiting over 13 weeks. This is an improved position when compared to March 2022 when there was a total of 4,600 patients waiting for this procedure, of which, approximately 3,250 were waiting over 13 weeks. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps his Department is taking to (i) support social housing tenants experiencing rent arrears; and (ii) provide security of tenure within the sector.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive, and I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me that: “(i) to support social housing tenants experiencing rent arrears;Support for Housing Executive tenants experiencing rent arrears is primarily delivered under the remit of the Financial Inclusion Strategy 2023-2028 and associated action plans. The aim of this strategy is to both: prevent rent arrears and enable tenants to get back on track should they fall into an arrear. The first point of contact for a tenant experiencing rent arrears is the Patch Manager. Rent arrears are flagged via the housing management system and function as a trigger point for the delivery of the Housing Executive’s Making Your Money Work service.  The service includes providing benefit and budget calculations, referrals to third parties for debt advice or floating support, and referrals to the in-house Financial Inclusion Team. New tenants will also be referred to the Financial Inclusion Team as necessary; the current referral triggers are:New tenants (arrears prevention) – All new tenants are offered the chance to avail of the Housing Executive Making Your Money Work service either before they sign for a new tenancy, during the sign-up process, or at their early tenancy visit (tenant choice). Tenants with rent arrears – all tenants reaching circa four weeks’ rent arrears are offered the Making Your Money Work Service which includes the provision of information about the Financial Inclusion Team and an offer of referral.All tenants who may be at risk of legal action to recover properties are referred to the Financial Inclusion Team.Tenants who have engaged with an external debt advice provider and been awarded a Debt Relief Order may be referred to the Financial Inclusion team to set them up for success going forward.Tenants without housing cost assistance are prioritised for Making Your Money Work referrals; 36.3% of all referrals made in 2023/24 were for tenants without housing cost assistance.Further to the above mandated trigger points for a financial inclusion intervention, frontline staff will also outline the Financial Inclusion Team’s work to customers who have particularly complex financial circumstances (e.g. disability/health status, foreign nationals, student, or self-employed), or when the tenant is asking multiple benefit related questions which may not be easily remedied by frontline staff. The service is also advertised to tenants in receipt of a housing cost benefit with dependent children in their property, tenants who have lost a housing cost benefit entitlement, tenants who have seen a reduction in their housing cost benefit entitlement, and those who have had a significant change in their circumstances which affects their financial wellbeing.The Financial Inclusion Team help tenants by:Providing benefits checks to make sure they are receiving everything they are entitled to;Performing ‘Better off

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in addition to the removal of Rule 23 of the Housing Selection Scheme, to detail any actions being taken to address and prevent paramilitary involvement within the housing sector.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I sought the requested information from the Housing Executive’s Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:	“The Housing Executive invests large amounts of time working with partners and discussing this area of work. NIHE is a member of the Executive Programme for Tackling Paramilitarism and Organised Crime (EPPOC) programme board. We work with the Community Safety Network, Communities in Transition Project Board, Community Response Groups etc. along with a large number of statutory organisations sharing knowledge, discussing funded projects and the EPPOC objectives, implications for organisations and customers.		The Housing Executive works collaboratively with organisations from across the statutory, voluntary and community sectors to address issues of Anti-social behaviour (ASB) where it occurs in our estates. We continue to work closely with police and criminal justice partners as well as a broad range of agencies to deal with ASB when it occurs.		Our Good Relations Officers work collaboratively with local community groups to promote positive relations within and between diverse ethnic communities and the wider community. This includes supporting new groups in hotspot areas to foster a community-led approach.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) when the National Joint Council pay award 2024-25 will be paid; and (ii) any steps that will be taken to address prolonged delays around the issuing of these pay increases.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer to AQW 21575/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps being taken to ensure that all temporary special educational needs staff in schools have completed Access NI checks and meet essential role criteria to guarantee the safety and quality of care for pupils.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) employs the majority of classroom assistants working in schools across Northern Ireland. A range of actions are taken by the EA to ensure that appropriate checks are in place and that essential role criteria are met for temporary staff working in special educational needs roles in schools. When a school engages a temporary staff member, they are required to follow the guidance set out in DE Circular 2024/10 ‘Guidance on Pre-Employment Vetting’, which relates to pre-employment and Access Northern Ireland requirements and safer resourcing practices for staff working in school settings. These requirements, which include the need for all teaching and support staff to undergo an Access Northern Ireland check before taking up their post, are strictly applied to all permanent and temporary staff appointed through a recruitment process.In the case of an emergency cover appointment, it is not always possible to finalise the Access Northern Ireland check prior to engagement.  In such situations, the school principal is required to complete a risk assessment and take all necessary steps to ensure the safeguarding of children and young people. This includes arranging for staff awaiting the outcome of an Access Northern Ireland check to be supervised at all times by someone who is vetted to work in regulated activity.  The EA also has processes in place to follow-up and confirm that checks that were not able to be completed prior to engagement have subsequently been finalised.The management of staffing in schools, including the delivery of recruitment processes to fill vacancies, is delegated to the relevant Board of Governors. It is therefore the responsibility of the Board of Governors of each school to ensure that all staff meet the essential criteria for the post. The EA supports this process through the provision of advice and guidance across the end-to-end recruitment process, including template job descriptions and person specifications setting out the essential role criteria.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the review of Primary Social Needs points within the Housing Selection Scheme will remove the current cap of 40 points for victims of abuse.
answer_text: I have asked the Housing Executive to carry out a review of Primary Social Needs criteria and points for all victims of violence, abuse and trauma. This will require careful consideration, engagement with stakeholders as well as modelling and analysis. The Housing Executive will bring forward findings and recommendations for my consideration in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the review of the Northern Ireland Childcare Scheme, including whether it will be extended beyond March 2025.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme has high levels of uptake and is successfully reducing the costs of childcare for thousands of working families across all parts of Northern Ireland.  Within the draft Budget which is out for public consultation, I have been able to secure the funding necessary to continue the Scheme into the next financial year.  The funding to do so will be finalised when we agree it as an Executive.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on Meenan Square development, including an indicative time frame for the beginning of construction works.
answer_text: A positive public consultation allowing the community to have their say on proposals for the redevelopment of Meenan Square has recently ended. The process included a public information event and webinar where members of the project team were on hand to answer questions.A planning application for the project was submitted on 29th January 2025.Subject to a successful outcome in relation to planning approval it is hoped that the project works will commence on site in Spring 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 17082/22-27, to detail (i) whether there have been any discussions in relation to delivering a Complex Lives Model for Derry; and (ii) any engagement his Department has had with Derry City and Strabane District Council in relation to this initiative.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me that:“As outlined in the response to AQW 17082 any expansion of Complex Lives would be for local partners, including the Council, to take forward. At this stage there have been no further discussions in relation to delivering a Complex Lives Model for Londonderry. The Housing Executive is happy to be part of any discussion as and when they are initiated. However, any delivery of a Complex Lives Model in Londonderry would be reliant on appropriate funding becoming available.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether her Department will explore tougher sentencing on breaches of non-molestation orders.
answer_text: Sentencing for breaches of non-molestation orders is a matter for the judiciary. Sentencing guidelines are applicable to the offence of breaching a non-molestation order, and judges will take into account aggravating factors, together with any mitigating factors in determining the appropriate sentence. The Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy 2024-2031, which was jointly published by the Department of Justice and Department of Health sets out a series of priorities and objectives that are intended to enhance protections for victims. My officials liaise frequently with colleagues in those Departments and will continue to work with them and others in relation to issues relating to non-molestation orders.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the progress of the Deposit Return Scheme.
answer_text: I am very pleased to report that The Deposit Scheme for Drinks Containers (England and Northern Ireland) Regulations 2025 were made on 23rd January 2025.  With Scotland's own regulations also progressing, this marks a major step forward for the introduction of the scheme across the three nations. This follows on from the decision by Welsh Government to withdraw from the scheme in November 2024. However, the three-nation DRS remains on track to be introduced in October 2027, with Polyethylene terephthalate (PET) plastic bottles plus aluminium & steel cans from 150ml to 3 litres included in the scheme. This matches the volumes and materials in-scope of the Republic of Ireland DRS already in operation, reflecting the desire for the schemes to be interoperable where possible. The introduction of the new parliamentary regulations is a significant milestone and further signals our commitment to move forward together to deliver our goals to protect our climate, drive green growth and reduce unnecessary waste, whilst tackling the scourge of litter in our communities.The application process to appoint a Deposit Management Organisation (DMO), which will be an independent, not for profit and a private body responsible for the administration and day-to-day running of the scheme, is ongoing and is due to close on 3rd February 2025, with an appointment expected by end of April 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an indicative time frame for the next steps to address inconsiderate pavement parking.
answer_text: My Department sought the public’s views on options to tackle inconsiderate pavement parking and a Findings Report was published on 6 December 2023. Officials are currently developing the next steps for my consideration and a formal response will issue when I have determined the best way forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any measures being considered to improve waste management in schools in order to make it more environmentally friendly; and (ii) whether he will consider implementing a standardised system across all schools.
answer_text: Schools can avail of either the Education Authority (EA) centrally managed private sector waste contract or local council collection services, all of which provide a dry-mixed recycling service and food waste recycling. The EA currently has a contract with RiverRidge Ltd for the provision of waste management services. As part of this contract, RiverRidge Ltd are required to implement a sustainable structure that encourages continued value for money, efficiencies and continuous improvement, whilst integrating environmental considerations through the performance of the contract. RiverRidge regularly undertake efficiency audits of waste management data to ensure schools are not only maximising their value for money but also ensuring every opportunity is taken to reduce the amount of general waste that is being produced and that all waste is disposed of in the most sustainable manner, following the waste hierarchy.(ii)	In accordance with Local Management of Schools delegation, schools can appoint their own waste provider and therefore are not obliged to use the EA centrally managed contract. Although schools are not contractually obliged to use this contract, EA strongly encourages the use of RiverRidge. The Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 has set a target of at least 70% of waste recycled by 2030. The Department would encourage all schools to consider the impact of climate change and choose options which balance sustainability and value for money.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to (i) improve access to; and (ii) increase the number of, support services for male victims of domestic violence.
answer_text: The Departments of Health and Justice jointly launched the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy (2024-2025) on 25 September 2024. The Strategy recognises that anyone can be a victim of domestic and/or sexual abuse regardless of age, ethnicity, religion, gender, gender identity, sexual orientation, or any form of disability. The Strategy also recognises that while anyone can experience domestic and /or sexual abuse, some people may be more at risk or may face additional barriers in accessing services and will therefore require specialist support and intervention. For example, many men may feel reluctant to disclose their experiences due to societal expectations of masculinity, fear of not being believed, or concerns about being stigmatised. The Strategy acknowledges the importance of identifying and responding to the intersectional needs of victims, to both prevent abuse from happening and to provide appropriate support in the event that it does occur.A three-year action plan was published alongside the Strategy, detailing actions under each pillar to support all victims, regardless of gender. As part of that action plan, the Commissioner for Victims of Crime Office intends to carry out a Mapping of Services for Domestic and/or Sexual Abuse Victims exercise. The first phase of this exercise will include services available for male victims.The findings of the IRIS pilot evaluation will also be considered under the action plan. IRIS is a specialist domestic and sexual abuse training, support and referral programme for primary care. The core elements include training and supporting general practice to identify and refer victims of domestic and sexual abuse, including men, to appropriate support services. Any future roll-out of IRIS will be subject to funding.In November 2024, the Department of Health supported the launch of research commissioned by the Commissioner Designate for Victims of Crime into the experience of males who suffer intimate partner violence in Northern Ireland. The launch of this research was a crucial step in understanding the specific experiences of male victims of intimate partner violence. It provides the insights needed to help determine whether the services and support systems provided are good enough or whether they need to be tailored to better meet the spectrum of needs. An integral aspect of the Strategy is to increase the opportunities for the voices of all victims to be heard – including men and boys.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the steps being taken to increase access to emergency accommodation for male victims of domestic violence.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Staff within Housing Solutions and Support Teams will seek to identify the most appropriate temporary accommodation placement from options which include voluntary sector hostels, Housing Executive hostels, Single Lets, and options such as non-standard (B&Bs/Hotels).Whilst there is currently no specific provision for this group funded through the Supporting People (SP) programme, there are a number of homeless services for males where staff undertake key work, engaging with clients through needs assessment, risk assessment and support planning in their provision of housing related support. This includes signposting clients to other agencies, where the support provider does not have the specific expertise or ability to support a particular element of need. SP funded accommodation based services for ‘Single Homeless with Support Needs’ are detailed below:Number of ServicesNumber of UnitsMale only6244Mixed gender23614Furthermore, an action from the Housing Executive's Supporting People Strategic Plan and Covid Recovery Plan 2022-2025 is aligned to Domestic Violence and aims to develop new services and enhanced delivery for people, male and female, at risk of domestic abuse but remains dependent on funding.”  For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly. Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will consider the inclusion of dementia-friendly measures for improvements to hospital Emergency Departments.
answer_text: The department is committed to including dementia friendly measures for improvement to hospital emergency departments.  Each of the five HSC Trusts have appointed Acute Dementia Service Improvement Leads to lead local service improvement initiatives across our acute settings, this includes work to make Emergency Departments more dementia friendly.  These staff are also contributing to the regional improvement plans being developed through the dementia project board.   Some examples of improvements made, to date, include:Trials of practical changes within Emergency Departments such as soft close bins; reduction to the noise / lighting, where possible, at night to promote normal sleep / wake routines; clocks that the time, date, day and year are within eye level for the patients, to help promote orientation and the placement of dementia friendly, pictorial signage to assist with wayfinding.The introduction of Cognitive Champions within the core staffing team which focuses on educating and empowering hospital staff to provide the highest standard of care for patients attending hospital with cognitive impairment including dementia.  Dementia Companions are in place in some EDs, these additional staff offer reassurance, support and cognitive stimulation to patients who are attending our acute wards / departments with cognitive impairment relating to dementia and delirium. The Dementia Companions also link with carers / family members to ensure our staff deliver person centred care and therapeutic interventions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the agency staff spend in the last two years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Trust expenditure on agency staff has been incurred to ensure that safe and effective services are sustained and contributes importantly to maintaining service provision to patients and clients. HSC Trusts employ agency staff for several reasons, for example, cover for sickness and maternity/paternity leave; cover for existing vacancies; and when demand increases over the winter months.With costs increasing substantially over the last number of years I have been concerned of the impact they are having on the HSC budget and recognise that these costs are not sustainable, particularly at a time of serious financial pressures right across the public sector. My Department has therefore undertaken significant work to try and alleviate the workforce pressures across HSC, which includes addressing the issues of rising agency and locum expenditure. Work has been ongoing regionally across HSC, under the Regional Agency Reduction Implementation Group (ARIG) with significant progress already made towards the cessation of ‘Off-Framework’ agency use in Nursing and Midwifery and Social Workers across NI’s health service. For these two staff groups, the progress made is projected to yield a reduction in agency expenditure in 2024/25 compared to the base year (2022/23), of £48m.Alongside this, work has also been focused on Recruitment, Retention and Training of staff, in addition to a dedicated workstream examining the reform of the Bank system. Further work in ongoing to reduce ‘Off-Framework’ agency for Medical and Dental, and other staff groups. Total agency expenditure by Trust, (which excludes directly employed locums) for the last two financial years, alongside the first two quarters of 2024/25, is set out in the table below. question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the rationale for their Department’s decision to remove security from the Ebrington site.
To ask the Minister of Health for a breakdown of bed capacity at each hospital in the last five years.
answer_text: The Department of Health produces statistics on the average number of available beds in each hospital, broken down by quarter. This data can be found at the link: Hospital statistics: inpatient and day case activity 2023/24 | Department of Health. This data goes back beyond the last five years. Available beds are measured at midnight, only in wards that are open overnight.On 9th November 2023, the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT) launched ‘encompass’ - a new electronic patient record system.  The encompass system is being rolled out across the other Trusts on a phased basis throughout 2024/25 and 2025/26. Bed data on or after 9th November 2023 in South Eastern HSCT is still being developed following the Trust’s transition to these completely digitised health records. Therefore, only South Eastern HSCT data until 8th November 2023 has been included in this file.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the business case for upgraded facilities at Altnagelvin Area Hospital Emergency Department.
answer_text: The Western Health and Social Care Trust is refreshing the original outline business case (OBC) to ensure the case presented for approval is robust and up to date. This is an essential requirement to secure departmental support and approval. The Trust is aiming to have the OBC completed by Spring/Summer 2025.  The OBC will be subject to Trust internal approval before submission to the Department of Health and Department of Finance for approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the newspapers and media outlets used by Belfast City Council to make the public aware of House in Multiple Occupation licences and applications in their local council area.
answer_text: The Houses in Multiple Occupation (Notice of Applications) Regulations 2019 state that the applicant for a licence for a house in multiple occupation (HMO), must publish a notice in one or more newspaper circulating in the locality of the HMO, and supply a copy of the notice to the council. As such, it is for the applicant to decide the paper in which to place the advert.On receipt of a copy of the notice, the council will publish the notice on its website for a period of 28 days.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will answer AQW 20296/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 20296/22-27 was answered on 20 March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when his Department will begin a public consultation exercise on the review of the Taxis Act (Northern Ireland) 2008.
answer_text: I have asked my officials to provide me with options for reviewing the Taxis Act (Northern Ireland) 2008. Following a review any proposed legislative changes will be subject to a public consultation within the current Assembly mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will commit to considering a residents' parking scheme within the periphery of the Ulster University's Magee campus, Derry.
answer_text: Following the publication of the Residents’ Parking Review report on 29 November 2024, I committed that my Department would give consideration to a residents' parking scheme to address the emerging pressures with on-street parking in the vicinity of the Ulster University Magee campus.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the total number of staff employed by providers of temporary and emergency accommodation; and (ii) how many of these staff have been asked to complete an Access NI check in the last 12 months, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive does not record the information requested.While Access NI checks are undertaken for temporary accommodation funded through the Supporting People (SP) programme, the vast majority of temporary accommodation is not provided by SP, and therefore not covered by Access NI checks.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly. Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether Strand Road, Derry, will be prioritised for improvement works as part of the upcoming resurfacing programme for 2024-25.
answer_text: The last structural survey, undertaken in July 2021, outlined that the residual life and skid resistance of the existing carriageway was 13 years. Further structural survey information is expected in the next few months and my officials will consider this information when it becomes available. In the interim, routine inspections of the Strand Road will continue in line with my Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 17662/22-27, whether he will introduce a requirement for all staff employed for the purposes of temporary and emergency accommodation to complete an Access NI check.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive uses a range of temporary accommodation options to meet the statutory duties outlined in the Housing (NI) Order 1988. This includes a range of options that are both within and outside the management of the Housing Executive. The Housing Executive avails of temporary accommodation managed by Supporting People (SP)and those services which are funded through the SP programme must provide an assurance that Access NI checks are undertaken. This is part of the SP accreditation process and arrangements are managed by Housing Executive Regional Services.The Housing Executive also avails of a range of other options not directly managed by the Housing Executive such as Single Lets and Non-Standard Accommodation (Hotels and B&Bs). It would not be feasible to introduce a requirement that all staff in accommodation, used for temporary and emergency accommodation complete an Access NI check. In respect of single lets, it should be noted that these are independent housing units with no support staff on site. The regulatory framework for such options sits outside the Housing Executive. Implementing a requirement for all staff to complete an Access NI check may inadvertently reduce our temporary accommodation options if providers choose not to go beyond standards set by the relevant regulatory framework. In such circumstances, there would be an associated risk that the Housing Executive would not be able to meet our statutory duties as per the Housing (NI) Order 1988.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) to provide clarity on existing legislation relating to ground rent; and (ii) whether there is a legal requirement to pay ground rent.
answer_text: ANSWERGround rents have been an established part of our system of land law since the Landlord and Tenant Law Amendment (Ireland) Act 1860 (“Deasy’s Act) and the provisions set out in the Conveyancing Act 1881 were enacted. Recent legislative reforms have sought to shift the emphasis from leasehold to freehold tenure. The Property (NI) Order 1997 restricted the creation of new ground rents with the effect that since its implementation over 25 years ago, it has not been possible to create a new long lease on a dwelling house, and ground rents for all such houses built since then have been free from ground rent.The 1997 Order also created a scheme for the redemption of existing ground rents. That scheme was revised and simplified by the Ground Rents Act (NI) 2001. Anyone wishing to redeem their ground rent can do so by using the process set out in the attached link - Buying out your ground rent | nidirectThere is a legal requirement to pay ground rent (if demanded) if provision for such payment is included in the terms of the lease. In those circumstances, where a rent due has not been paid or demanded for 6 years or more, Article 30 of the Limitation (NI) Order 1989 applies, and the right to arrears of rent for that period is extinguished. There are instances, particularly where the ground rent is very small, where the ground rent is not demanded.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 16313/22-27, for an estimated time frame for a decision on the introduction of a levy on gambling operators.
answer_text: I am not in a position to provide an estimated timeframe at this stage. My officials are currently engaging on a cross departmental and cross jurisdictional basis to gather data in relation to this issue. Analysis of this and the results of a Gambling Prevalence Survey, expected in March, will contribute to my decision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any efforts being made to ensure the full utilisation of bed capacity at the South West Acute Hospital.
answer_text: The Western Health & Social Care Trust has shown that South West Acute Hospital has been, and continues to operate at full commissioned capacity, utilising full commissioned bed capacity for a considerable period of time. This includes having been over 100% bed capacity for the majority of 2024/25. The Trust has also now returned to and is ongoing with the implementation of the fully commissioned 15.3 sessions per week (over 50 weeks) of Elective Surgery.

To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any actions his Department is taking to ensure that the online application system for a provisional driving licence, via NI Direct, can accept an Irish passport as a valid form of identification.
answer_text: The online application system for a provisional driving licence, via nidirect, accepts an Irish passport as a valid form of identification.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the effectiveness of the Healthy Happy Minds Programme.
answer_text: Whilst the overall outcome of the post project evaluation was positive, there is no centrally funded service for primary school counselling service.It is important that any future provision is considered in conjunction with health colleagues and wider health services, as primary providers of mental health services and therapies.The challenging financial context across the education system means that budgets and investments are severely constrained.  As with all services that are not statutory, investment is unfortunately limited to budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of (i) the effectiveness of the Healthy Happy Minds Programme Therapeutic Counselling Programme; and (ii) how this aligns with objectives in the Mental Health Strategy.
answer_text: Whilst the overall outcome of the Healthy Happy Minds post project evaluation was positive, there is no centrally funded service for primary school counselling, although schools have the autonomy to procure counselling or other therapeutic supports from within existing budgetsHowever, one of the key areas of joint working between my Department and the Department of Education relates to the delivery of the Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework.Services and projects that have been established under the Framework are available across primary and post-primary schools in Northern Ireland. These services very much contribute to our focus on Early Intervention and Prevention (Actions 1 and 2 of the Mental Health Strategy), and it is our aim, through these interventions, to reduce the number of children and young people requiring more specialist services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans to introduce measures to improve traffic flow on Northland Road, Derry, given the impending opening of Ardnashee School and College.
answer_text: As Ardnashee School and College is being built on the site previously occupied by Foyle College until 2018, no additional measures to improve traffic flow are planned at this time.As a statutory consultee, my Department assessed the development's transport impact during the processing of the planning application. The assessment concluded that measures should be put in place to improve road safety, rather than capacity. These road safety measures have been included in the planning approval and should help to protect our most vulnerable road users.Once the school opens and traffic patterns have had sufficient time to bed in, a review of possible traffic interventions will be considered if the need arises.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps being taken to improve (i) diagnostic times; and (ii) treatment for people with inflammatory bowel disease.
answer_text: Each of the Trusts has in recent years taken a number of steps to improve (i) diagnostic times and (ii) treatment for patients with Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD) as briefly described below.Belfast HSC Trust(i)	Within Belfast Health and Social Care Trust there are dedicated IBD clinics including urgent slots for new patients and those experiencing a flare.  Rapid access endoscopy slots each week are dedicated to IBD patients for rapid assessment and diagnosis. Patients are also seen on an urgent basis if necessary following contact with the IBD Nurse led helpline.(ii)	Within the Belfast Trust, the gastroenterology service currently offers patients with inflammatory bowel disease:A full weekly IBD multi-disciplinary team meeting attended by Physicians, Surgeons, Radiology, specialist nurses and trainees. This is supported by regular IBD service meetings with the IBD lead, specialist nurses and pharmacy to discuss issues, service improvements and areas for development.In addition to this, IBD Clinics are held with Specialist IBD Nurses that can be either telephone/virtual clinics. Two nurse led clinics are in place aiming to improve access to specialist advice to IBD patients.  For patients who experience a flare, an IBD Helpline aims to respond to patients within 24 hours of them contacting it either by phone or email.  There are rapid access clinic slots for new diagnosis, suspected diagnosis or patients that are unwell.Patients have links with Healthcare at Home to initiate and supply biologic drugs directly to their homes to avoid them having to attend hospital.The Trust is currently in the process of securing funding for a further IBD Consultant to help with transitioning children with IBD to the Adult service and improve waiting times for IBD patients. Plans are also underway to employ a further adult IBD Nurse Specialist (there are over 1000 patients on biologic drugs which is one of the largest centres in the UK) as well as an additional pharmacist to support advanced treatments and patient monitoring.  The aim is to introduce a small molecule clinic for flaring patients to allow rapid medication switches.The Belfast team is also in contact with other UK teams who have developed the IBD Registry and a patient portal so that one may be developed for the HSC.The Trust has access to a Significant Research Group giving patients access to the latest International Drug studies being used in IBD. South Eastern HSC Trust(i)	The South Eastern Trust has taken a number of steps to help improve the diagnostic times and treatment for patients with inflammatory bowel disease (IBD).A 5-day consultant led rapid access gastroenterology hub is well established within the Trust. This allows rapid assessment/treatment of patients presenting with symptoms of IBD or patients with confirmed IBD experiencing a flare. GPs, the Emergency Department and medical wards can refer to the service electronically allowing assessment usually within 24-48 hours. A phone ref

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the steps his Department is taking to meet the Net Zero emissions target by 2050 for electric vehicles (EV); and (ii) the EV uptake in each of the last five years.
answer_text: I have made clear my commitment to delivering greener, cleaner, sustainable transport. Modelling undertaken by my Department has shown that a focus on removing car emissions through the transition to zero emission vehicles will have the most significant impact on reducing carbon emissions from the transport sector.  At present, 63% percent of our overall transport emissions are from the private car. The transition to zero emission vehicles has a significant role to play if we are to meet our collective emission reduction targets by 2050. Switching fuels to low or zero emission alternatives is a key tenet of our approach to transport decarbonisation.  I believe that the introduction of the Vehicle Emissions Trading Schemes (VETS) legislation in the North is an important and positive step to encourage those that depend on vehicles for transport to switch away from fossil fuels.  Supporting this transition by further accelerating the number of zero emission vehicles entering the new car market will build stock to enter the second-hand market. The introduction of VETS here will give confidence to the private sector led charging infrastructure providers that the number of Electric Vehicles (EVs) in the North will continue to increase. Charge Point Operators’ investments in EV infrastructure and the subsequent expansion of the charging network will play a key part in providing the services that EV owners will require to seamlessly move away from petrol and diesel.My officials are also undertaking a range of actions and wider policy initiatives to support the transition to net zero vehicles and help meet targets set out in the Climate Change Act (NI) 2022. My officials will continue to engage with industry stakeholders and officials from both the British Government’s Office for Zero Emission Vehicles (OZEV), and the South’s Zero Emission Vehicles Ireland (ZEVI) to consider how new technologies and alternative fuel solutions can be adopted to meet the challenge of decarbonising the transport Sector.My Department does not produce Electric Vehicle sales Statistics. The British Government publishes periodic updates which you may find helpful, these can be found in the link below: Vehicles statistics - GOV.UK

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether there is a requirement to notify local residents of a person convicted of sexual offences against children living in their area.
answer_text: Management of individuals convicted of sexual offences is an operational matter for PSNI.  Offenders who are subject to notification requirements under the Sexual Offences Act 2003, commonly referred to as the ‘sex offenders register’, must notify police of certain personal details and information, including (but not limited to) their name, address, date of birth and national insurance number and when they leave the jurisdiction.    Where offenders are also made the subject of a civil prevention order to further manage risk from sexual harm, such as the Sexual Offences Prevention Order, further conditions will be placed on them.  This can include only residing at an address that is approved by the police or prohibiting them from going to certain places such as to schools or playparks.   This framework assists PSNI, in conjunction with other risk management partners, to manage these offenders in the community. There is no requirement for the authorities to notify local residents of a person convicted of sexual offences against children living in their area.  However, disclosures may be made where it is necessary to protect a particular child or particular children from serious harm caused by such offenders.  In addition, members of the public may make an application for disclosure to the police.  Further information may be found at: https://www.psni.police.uk/request/child-protection-disclosure#no-back   Where the PSNI holds conviction data relating to sexual offences, it will - in cooperation with other agencies where appropriate - decide whether it is necessary to disclose that information to the person who has responsibility for the child and who is best placed to safeguard them.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the estimated resource cost required to review the Taxis Act (Northern Ireland) 2008.
answer_text: I have asked my officials to provide me with options for reviewing the Taxis Act (Northern Ireland) 2008, taking into account the resources required. Only after I have agreed the scope of this review will I be in a position to detail the estimated resources required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of times a vehicle encountered difficulty and became stuck at Fountain Hill, Derry, in the last five years; and (ii) the measures that will be taken to address this issue.
answer_text: My department does not keep a record of the occasions when vehicles encounter difficulty negotiating Fountain Hill. A review of signage was carried out in 2024 which resulted in an additional advance sign being provided on the Irish Street approach to Fountain Hill. There are now five signs from Altnagelvin Roundabout to Fountain Hill informing vehicles of a 3-tonne maximum gross weight limit. Three of the five signs also state Fountain Hill is unsuitable for long vehicles and buses. My officials will consider providing high visibility backing to these signs to further highlight the restrictions on Fountain Hill.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Northern Ireland Football Fund.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Football Fund will provide investment for football at all levels. It is a key priority for me, and I am committed to moving forward at pace.I want to roll out the funding as quickly as possible, however, it is important that funding is disbursed in a fair and transparent manner. The Northern Ireland Football Fund will prioritise performance clubs first, but I am also moving forward with proposals to invest at grassroots level and in creation of a National Football Centre.I approved the Business Case for the Performance Programme in December 2024 and in the coming weeks I expect to open a call for applications to the Fund from performance clubs.  This will begin a rolling program of awards and project delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) when the first Housing Supply Strategy Action Plan will be released; (ii) the organisations that will be involved in its development; and (iii) how it will be informed by lived experience of homelessness.
answer_text: Governance structures to develop the action plan are in the process of being established and it is intended that the first Housing Supply Strategy action will be published in the coming months. The development process will involve all relevant Executive Departments, alongside a range of housing stakeholders. Specific stakeholders are to be finalised, however it is intended that organisations representing those who are homeless will be involved.
