question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the total number of patients on waiting lists for cervical screening results; and (ii) the average wait time in the last 12 months, broken down Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: As of 4 February 2025, there are 18,522 cervical screening samples awaiting authorisation on the laboratory information system. These are samples which have been received by the laboratory and are at various stages of the testing process but a final report/result has not yet been issued to the GP.  Average wait times are not available, however, laboratory turnaround times, which indicate the percentage of samples reported within 14 days, 28 days or greater than 28 days are available.  The table below shows comparative data for 2023/24 and quarters 1 and 2 of 2024/25. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in relation to the agreed 5 per cent pay uplift for NI Water staff, to detail (i) when an agreement is expected to be reached with NIPSA and non-trade union staff; and (ii) whether non-frontline staff will be awarded a 5 per cent increase to their pay.
answer_text: The agreement of the pay offer is an operational matter for NI Water; however I have requested an update on the current status of the pay agreement. NI Water has advised that:(i) The company has presented the pay offer to staff. NIPSA are currently surveying members to determine whether or not to issue a ballot for the offer. NI Water is dependent on NIPSA deciding if it intends to hold a ballot on the offer, and the outcome of the ballot, before it can confirm agreement of the offer with members and non-members in the non-frontline unit.(ii) If agreed, non-frontline staff will be awarded a pay increase ranging from 4% to 8%, with an average individual pay increase of 6%. This offer includes the consolidated uplift and the performance related pay element as part of the individual pay scales.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for his assessment of the rising number of empty homes, which currently stands at 21,081; and (ii) whether his Department will commit to conducting an analysis of the underlying causes of these increases in each local council area.
answer_text: There is a very small increase in the recorded number of empty homes of 0.58%.  A comparative analysis using figures from previous years demonstrates a constantly shifting position with an annual turnover of around 20% of properties recorded as vacant on the LPS rating system, which is likely to cause some fluctuations in the figures from year to year.Given the small extent of this change, that comparative analysis makes it clear that many homes are recently vacated and will soon be occupied again, and that while for others no intervention available to the Department will bring them back into use (e.g. ongoing court proceedings) I do not plan to conduct any further analysis at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) when the gritting schedule was last reviewed; and (ii) whether he will commit to maintaining a list of roads which are added to the schedule each year.
answer_text: At the start of each winter season a review is undertaken and additional roads added to the salted network if they meet the current policy. Updates to the gritting schedule are managed at a local level, with changes made to the publicly available map for the gritted network.  Given the availability of this information on a live basis, a list of roads added to the schedule each year has not been held.My officials are currently considering the delivery of a centralised winter service route optimisation solution for the next winter service season.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) why the individual vehicle approval height requirement for Class B drivers in Northern Ireland is set at 50mm higher than in England; and (ii) whether there are plans to review this standard to align with other regions.
answer_text: The Taxi Accessibility Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015 require the wheelchair space in an accessible (Class B) taxi to be at least 1350 millimetres in height. These regulations were developed after the Department engaged Integrated Transport Planning (ITP) to conduct research into the taxi industry in the North and building on this developed a new specification for wheelchair accessible taxis, which was consulted on in 2014.In Britain there are around 300 individual taxi licensing authorities that apply a variety of differing taxi construction requirements, including height requirements, meaning any alignment of standards is not feasible.I currently have no plans to revise the Taxi Accessibility Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether there are plans to remove security from the Ebrington site, Derry.
answer_text: Guard security was put in place at Ebrington when the site was largely vacant and under development.  Today, Ebrington is thriving and much like the city centre, is a hub of activity with a wide range of both day and night-time businesses on the site who have their own security provision in place.  From April 2025, security guard provision at the Ebrington site will cease.  The site will continue to be monitored 24 hours a day via the existing extensive CCTV network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will consider an indefinite extension of the Benefit Cap.
answer_text: As announced on 5 December 2024, I secured Executive agreement to extend the welfare mitigation payments including the Benefit Cap, which were due to end on 31 March 2025 for a further three years until 31 March 2028.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department will ensure the views of the community, within the immediate vicinity of the Ulster University Magee campus, will be sought and taken into account to minimise any disruption arising from the University’s expansion.
To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the Happy Healthy Minds project.

To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the work of the cross-departmental Coordinated Action Group established by the Mental Health Champion to consider crisis services and suicide prevention in Derry.
answer_text: This Group was not established by this Department, however, officials represent the Department. It would be appropriate for an update on progress to be provided by the Mental Health Champion as Chair of the Group.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether there are any plans to review the Sunday Trading (Northern Ireland) Order 1997 to allow larger shops to open before 1:00pm.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer to question AQW 6469/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 19424/22-27, to provide clarity on the difference between this figure of vacant properties and the 52,300 vacant households recorded in Census 2021.
answer_text: The Land & Property Services (LPS) rating system does not maintain a register of vacant properties. Since the introduction of the Rating of Empty Homes legislation on 1st October 2011, rate liability for all domestic properties has been assessed at 100% whether occupied or not. Information on the status of occupation of a property is subject to ratepayers informing LPS that the property is unoccupied and not used for storage. Therefore, the number of vacant properties within the rating system would not be directly comparable to information recorded in Census 2021.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the steps his Department is taking to improve the timely refreshing of road markings.
answer_text: My officials undertake regular routine maintenance inspections of all roads during which all defects, including defective signs and road markings, are noted.Maintenance of traffic signs and road markings are generally concentrated on regulatory signs and markings that are necessary for continued road safety. Additional road markings are considered for renewal subject to the availability of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the implementation of the Law Commission recommendations on Land Law Reform and Ground Rents.
answer_text: ANSWERI refer the member to the answer provided to AQW 17252/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of pensioners that have received the one-off fuel support payment of £100 to date.
answer_text: Whilst the payment has not yet issued, my officials are working at pace to put in place the arrangements for making this payment via existing payment channels before the end of March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of deaths by suicide in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: NISRA collates data on death registrations as collected by the General Register Office.  Data on the numbers of deaths from suicide up to 2022, broken down by parliamentary constituency can be found in table 9 of the publication ‘NI Suicide Statistics 2002-2022’. Overall totals of suicide deaths registered in 2023 are available in table 6.1 of the Registrar General Annual Report 2023.  The Suicide statistics 2023 detailed report and tables (including breakdown by parliamentary constituency) are scheduled to be published on Wednesday 5 February 2024.Deaths are coded and reported according to World Health Organisation (WHO) International Classification of Diseases (ICD tenth revision).There are some situations where the registration of the death can be delayed, specifically where the death has been accidental, unexpected, or suspicious, and must be referred to the Coroners Service, only to be registered after completion of the investigation. It should be noted that suicides registered during a particular time will therefore not be reflective of suicides that occurred in that period.  NISRA recommends that users of suicide statistics refer to the subset of ‘self-inflicted injury’ (ICD code X60-X84 &Y87.0) as the most accurate count in relation to longer term trends.In 2019 NISRA and the Coroners Service commenced a review of suicide statistics following the identification of a classification issue in published statistics which concluded in 2022. This marked a major revision to this statistical series with a break in the time series being introduced from 2015. Comparisons with data prior to 2015 should therefore be made with caution.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will consider establishing a dedicated suicide prevention taskforce for the North West region.
answer_text: Given current pressures on budgets and staff resources it is important we do not duplicate existing structures.  Establishment of a task force would replicate the broad range of suicide prevention services that are already provided to those living in the North West area. These include:  Northern and Western Protect Life Implementation Groups; self-harm intervention programmes; the mental health crisis care team; counselling services; bereavement support; community response plans and training for community gatekeepers.The governance of the Strategy includes local Protect Life Implementation Groups (PLIGs) based within each HSC Trust area. These groups help ensure effective implementation of the strategy at a local level in a way that takes account of local needs and assets. They develop local action plans based on the Protect Life 2 action plan, and oversee delivery of these plans. In October 2023, the NI Mental Health Champion, Professor Siobhan O’Neill, established the Cross Departmental Co-ordinated Action Group to Consider Crisis Services and Suicide Prevention in Derry/Londonderry. The Action Group is chaired by the Mental Health Champion and includes representatives from Government Departments, Derry and Strabane District Council, Derry City Centre Initiative, PHA, PSNI, and the Western Health and Social Care Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps being taken to address concerns in relation to the use of LED lights on vehicles, particularly in terms of the potential dangers they pose to oncoming drivers, including glare and reduced visibility.
answer_text: The responsibility for certifying a new vehicle for use on our roads lies with the Vehicle Certification Agency (VCA), which is an Executive Agency of the Department for Transport (DfT). VCA work to the regulations that are ultimately agreed to by DfT. You may wish to note that as part of the annual roadworthiness test, the Driver & Vehicle Agency checks the headlight alignment on vehicles, however, light intensity or glare are not currently testable items. DfT officials have advised that all vehicle headlamps are designed and tested to comply with internationally recognised standards. This is to ensure that they balance the need to be sufficiently bright to illuminate the road ahead against the risk of affecting the vision of other road users. The standards define the beam pattern and include maximum and minimum light intensities. In light of concerns raised by the public, DfT officials raised the issue at the United Nations international expert group on vehicle lighting. Proposals to amend headlamp aiming criteria were subsequently agreed in April 2023, together with requirements for mandatory automatic headlamp levelling. The implementation of the changes agreed will take some time as vehicle manufacturers have been allowed the time needed to redesign their products and adapt the manufacturing process. DfT expect that the tighter tolerances will come into effect in September 2027. Once implemented, these tougher requirements for new vehicle types will in due course help alleviate the number of cases where road users are dazzled.In addition, DfT have recently awarded Transport Research Laboratory (TRL) a research contract to investigate the factors that cause glare from the headlights of oncoming vehicles, and how to address this. TRL are due to report their findings in Spring of 2025. My officials will monitor the progress of this research and will advise of any implications or advice for road users here once the findings have been published.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the measures he introduced to reduce the risk of attack by XL Bully dogs.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what assessment his Department has made of the impact of illegal money lending on communities and households in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Problem debt can have a devastating impact on individuals and families. The stigma associated with debt can be particularly acute when money is borrowed from illegal lenders.Tackling illegal money lending spans the remit of several departments and requires a co-ordinated approach.My Department is responsible for financial wellbeing policy in Northern Ireland and provides funding to Advice NI and its affiliated local advice centres.  These debt advisers can support victims of illegal money lending by offering free, independent debt advice and signposting to relevant organisations that can provide help.  Research has shown that individuals who have received illegal loans are reluctant to admit it, and do not feel that debt advice would be of benefit in their circumstances.  My officials continue to work with colleagues in The Executive Programme on Paramilitarism & Organised Crime to discuss findings and inform a collective approach to supporting victims of this type of lending.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of Occupational Therapists currently employed, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 16481/22-27, to detail any steps his Department will take to secure additional resources for the Western Health and Social Care Trust, given the waiting time for Occupational Therapy.
answer_text: PHA continue to work with SPPG and HSC Trusts to ensure Allied Health Profession (AHP) services including occupational therapy deliver a range of support that reflects the needs of the population of Northern Ireland and is aligned to evidence based practice. Work is underway between SPPG and PHA to separate data on waiting times for under and over 18-year olds across elective AHP waiting times including occupational therapy.  This will help provide a better understanding of the specific waiting times across CYP and adult elective AHP services to help identify demands and trends across each of the HSC Trusts.The Western Health and Social Care Trust’s occupational therapy service is one of the lowest funded occupational therapy services in Northern Ireland.As of 30th November 2024, there were 3937 patients awaiting occupational therapy assessment across all the different specialties of the service which include children’s occupational therapy, rehabilitation, community adult, rheumatology, adult mental health, neurology and wheelchair services. Referrals to the service are managed in accordance with clinical risk and clients who fall into this high-risk category will be seen as urgently as capacity allows. The occupational therapy service is doing all it can to deliver the shortest waiting time possible within the funded resources available.The Western Health and Social Care Trust acknowledges the current pressures within occupational therapy services and accepts waiting times for therapy assessment are longer than we would like our clients to have to wait.  This is due to the growing level of demand, complexity of clients referred for these services, recovery from the Covid-19 pandemic as well as workforce challenges.  The Western Health and Social Care Trust would welcome additional investment in occupational therapy services to address the significant service pressures.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has explored solutions to resolve issues experienced by claimants whose wages are paid on a four-weekly rather than monthly basis, impacting their assessment periods for Universal Credit.
answer_text: My Department has not carried out any exploratory work on cases where Universal Credit claimants are paid by their employer on a four-weekly basis rather than a monthly basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 19002/22-27, whether his Department advocated for the inclusion of suicide prevention at any stage during the drafting of the Programme for Government.
answer_text: The draft Programme for Government (PfG) sets out our proposed collective priorities of The Executive. The PfG does not include everything that every department is working to deliver. Work continues on suicide prevention and the development of the new Protect Life 2 Action Plan is being progressed as a priority.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will consider establishing designated parking bays for wheelchair accessible vehicles that would afford the additional necessary space required for a ramp.
answer_text: The Department’s Director of Engineering Memorandum DEM 187/21 ‘Accessible Parking Bays for Longer Vehicles’ provides guidance on ways to accommodate wheelchair accessible vehicles within on-street accessible parking bays. The document can be viewed using the following link: Accessible Parking Bays for Longer Vehicles DEM 187/21v1

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide a breakdown of the 5,378 children living in temporary accommodation by local council area.
answer_text: The biannual Northern Ireland Homelessness Bulletin April – September 2024, published on 12 December 2024, contains information on a range of areas relating to homelessness and is available at https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-homelessness-bulletin-april-september-2024Section 3 from the accompanying tables contains details on children living in temporary accommodation, however I am unable to provide a breakdown in the format you have requested.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any plans his Department has to address the 5-week wait for Universal Credit; and (ii) whether research has been conducted into links between the 5-week wait and debt, poverty and exploitation by illegal money lenders.
answer_text: Please see previous responses to AQW6086/22-27 and AQW19402/22-27.  The Department has not conducted research into links between the 5-week wait and debt, poverty and exploitation by illegal money lenders.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities why addressing the number of empty homes has not been included as a feature of the Housing Supply Strategy.
answer_text: My Department published a new Housing Supply Strategy on 9th December to address the wider challenges facing housing supply. It recognises the need to consider actions around the use of existing buildings to support housing supply. The detail of specific actions required will be considered as part of the action planning process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on proposals for traffic calming measures on Temple Road, Strathfoyle.
answer_text: A public consultation to provide traffic calming on the Temple Road, Strathfoyle closed in October this year. There were no objections to the scheme proposals and it is anticipated that the traffic calming scheme will be delivered in the new financial year, subject to available resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will seek to secure specific accommodation for parents of premature babies in neonatal wards, particularly for mothers who have been discharged and are required to travel to the Maternity Hospital in Belfast to visit their child.
answer_text: At the date of handover there were no concerns raised by my Department in relation to the handover of the new Maternity Hospital from the contractor. The contractor had achieved the water quality acceptance criteria required under the contract and this was substantiated by laboratory tests of the water samples taken.The detection of Pseudomonas Aeruginosa within the domestic water systems of the new Regional Maternity Hospital was first reported to the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust and Health Estates by the contractor in January 2023.Under the terms of the contract, the contractor was responsible for remediating the water systems contamination.Following detection of Pseudomonas Aeruginosa, the following remediations actions were implemented by the contractor prior to handover; Specialist chemical treatment sub- contractors were engaged to analyse and treat the pipework contamination.An additional chemical treatment system was installed on the pipework system.Sections of pipework were replaced.Valves and pipeline components were replaced, installed and chemically cleaned.Pipework circuits were re-balanced.Water flushing, water circulation, sampling and laboratory analysis procedures were employed to address the contamination.The contractors procedures were independently reviewed by the Trust appointed independent water specialist.Reporting in relation to the handover of the new Maternity Hospital included management of the design team, regular Project Board Meetings attended by officials from my Department and direct reporting from the Belfast Trust to my Department.My Department is providing oversight, professional and technical support to ensure that the matter is resolved at the earliest opportunity and that the new Hospital will be safely operational soon as possible.I can also advise that there have been regular discussions between the Department of Health Permanent Secretary and Belfast Health and Social Care Trust senior management in relation to the new regional Maternity Hospital project since May 2022. These meetings were to discuss progress on the project and to allow Belfast Trust to raise any issues.  They were not decision meetings and were in addition to the normal governance arrangements for a project of this nature.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will consider seeking access to the early access programme for Givinostat for children with Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy.
answer_text: Responsibility for participation in the early access programme for givinostat lies with Health and Social Care Trusts. Applications to the scheme are made by clinicians on an individual case-by-case basis. However, I can confirm that the management team and clinical staff in the Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children in the Belfast Trust are working with commissioning colleagues in my Department’s Strategic Performance and Planning Group to determine whether it is viable for the Trust to support the provision of this treatment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 18368/22-27, whether the working group examined the potential for developer contributions, including (i) how long this would take to implement; and (ii) the impact it would have on the overall aim of resolving funding shortfalls and addressing regional inequalities.
answer_text: I can confirm that the working group considered the potential for developer contributions. (i) My officials are currently examining all options for developers providing financial contributions to NI Water to improve wastewater infrastructure capacity. (ii) The level of income anticipated at that time through such a change was predicted to be modest.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of times he has met with the Minister for Infrastructure to discuss wastewater issues and the impact on the delivery of housing since the beginning of the 2022-2027 mandate.
answer_text: Since taking up office in February 2024, I have met formally with the Minister for Infrastructure on one occasion to discuss the draft Housing Supply Strategy and the impact of wastewater issues.I have also raised the issue of wastewater with the Minister for Infrastructure and other Executive colleagues at Executive meetings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will consider establishing a platform similar to the Scottish Empty Homes Partnership.
answer_text: My Department published a new Housing Supply Strategy on 9th December to address the wider challenges facing housing supply. It recognises the need to consider actions around the use of existing buildings to support housing supply. The detail of specific actions required will be considered as part of the action planning process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of times he has met with the Minister for Communities to discuss wastewater issues and the impact on the delivery of housing since the beginning of the 2022-2027 mandate.
answer_text: Since the beginning of the 2022-2027 mandate, I have met with the Minister for Communities on one occasion, to discuss wastewater issues and the impact on the delivery of housing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of households in the private rented sector that are in receipt of Local Housing Allowance, through (i) universal credit housing costs; and (ii) housing benefit.
answer_text: (i) In July 2024, there were 37,820 households in the private rented sector in receipt of Local Housing Allowance (LHA), through Universal Credit housing element.(ii) In July 2024, there were 20,990 claimants in the private rented sector in receipt of LHA, through Housing Benefit.Data Source: NIHE Housing Benefit Data and Universal Credit Full Service System (UCFS) – July 2024Note that due to retrospection of UCFS data UC figures are subject to change.All claimant / household figures are rounded to the nearest ten.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has evaluated the specialist workforce required to meet the needs of babies, children and young people with speech, language, and communication needs, including Auditory Verbal therapists.
answer_text: The Speech and Language Workforce Review 2019-2029 evaluated the speech and language therapy workforce required to deliver services to the whole demographic of Northern Ireland. It is widely accepted that since this review and the recommendations made therein that demands on the speech and language service have increased. The Advisory Committee of Allied Health Professions was reconvened in March 2024. My Chief Allied Health Professions Officer has commissioned the workforce subcommittee of my Advisory Committee of Allied Health Professions to consider all workforce issues for Allied Health Professions.  As part of this work, the committee will critically review the recommendations in the Speech and Language Workforce Review 2019-2029 in order to inform the future workforce requirements.  I anticipate receiving their advice in coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department plans to support the work already being done to ensure all deaf children have the opportunity to access Auditory Verbal therapy through local speech and language therapists.
answer_text: There are around 1,700 deaf children in Northern Ireland. Northern Ireland has one of the best New-born Hearing Screening Programmes in the world and this state-of-the-art hearing technology (mainly hearing aids and cochlear implants) is available to babies and young children across NI. Audio Verbal Therapy (AVT) is not a currently commissioned service within Northern Ireland.Audio Verbal UK (AVUK) offers specialised training for the HSC and other professionals. AVUK state that approximately 80% of children who attend their Auditory Verbal Programme for at least two years achieve the same level of spoken language as their hearing peers, rising to 97% of children without additional needs. My Department has reached out to clinical colleagues for some initial input on Auditory Verbal Therapy (AVT) for non-hearing children and while they have suggested that there is a lack of well-controlled studies addressing the use of AVT, they have also suggested that this would not necessarily suggest AVT may be an ineffective intervention.  Therefore, more information is required about the intervention and this will be reviewed/agreed by clinicians in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has made an assessment of the need for early and effective support, including Auditory Verbal therapy, to develop language and communication for deaf children.
answer_text: Early and effective support for deaf children and their families is essential.  The Education Authority’s (EA) Sensory Service provides support services to deaf children, delivered by qualified Teachers of the Deaf (ToD), from birth via referrals from the newborn hearing screening programme through all stages of their education.For all deaf children, early exposure to a rich fluent language model is critically important for all later language learning.  It is important that families have access to a range of evidencebased interventions.  This includes options for communication such as sign language, Sign Supported English and approaches such as Auditory Verbal Therapy (AVT).  The EA is engaging with Auditory Verbal UK (AVUK) and six TODs within the Sensory Service are being funded to complete AVT training which will make this intervention more widely available in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department plans to support the work already being done to ensure all deaf children have the opportunity to access Auditory Verbal therapy through local teachers of the deaf.
answer_text: I would refer the member to the answer to question AQW 19676/22-27, which was published week ending 13 December 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the rationale for requiring an unlawful assembly of three or more people to meet the eligibility criteria for compensation for criminal damage; (ii) whether there are plans to review this criterion; and (iii) any alternative routes of compensation that are available for victims of criminal damage caused by one or two individuals.
answer_text: Where damage has been unlawfully, maliciously or wantonly caused to any property, The Criminal Damage (Compensation) (Northern Ireland) Order 1977 provides for compensation to be payable where it can be shown that three or more persons unlawfully, riotously, or tumultuously assembled together have caused the damage.  The legislation was introduced when Northern Ireland was subject to a serious and sustained campaign of bombing and arson.  It is understood that at the time, the rationale for requiring three or more being involved was to satisfy the common law requirements of an unlawful assembly and in doing so to attempt to balance the individual’s responsibility (and that of private insurance companies) with that of the state.Preparatory work has commenced to review the Criminal Damage Compensation Scheme in response to the progress that has been made towards Northern Ireland becoming a more peaceful, prosperous, and safer society. It is not possible to advise on compensation that might be available to victims of criminal damage caused by one or two individuals.  An individual whose property has been damaged would, of course, have recourse to any insurance policies in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for a breakdown of how the £47.3 million allocated for a welfare mitigations package in 2025-26 will be spent.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the total cost of managing the environmental risk at the Mobuoy site in the last five years.
answer_text: From 1 April 2019 to November 2024, DAERA’s total cost of managing the environmental risk from the Mobuoy site, through the Mobuoy Remediation Project is £6.27m. This figure includes, but is not limited to, costs for the ongoing water quality monitoring under the comprehensive Environmental Monitoring Programme, extensive site investigations, monitoring infrastructure, completion of a Detailed Quantitative Risk Assessment (DQRA) and the development of the draft remediation strategy for the site.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the dualling of the A2 Buncrana Road.
answer_text: The A2 Buncrana Road scheme will be considered as part of the North West Transport Plan which will be subject to a public consultation process. Views from the public were sought to help shape the vision and objectives for the Transport Plan, as well as identifying local transport concerns in the district through public engagement which commenced on 27th August 2024 and concluded on 5th November.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the loopholes that will be closed as part of the recently announced welfare mitigations extension; and (ii) which claimants will benefit from these changes.
answer_text: The initial design of the Benefit Cap and Social Sector Size Criteria Welfare Supplementary Payment schemes included provisions that could lead to mitigation payments being reduced or stopped completely. This included the amount of a Benefit Cap Welfare Supplementary Payment being restricted to the amount when a person first had their benefit capped (i.e. the mitigation payment could never increase). In addition a Social Sector Size Criteria Welfare Supplementary Payment could cease if someone chose to move property and continued to under-occupy by at least the same number of bedrooms.On 8 February 2022 the Assembly approved the Welfare Supplementary Payment (Amendment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2022 to remove these provisions from the mitigation schemes until 31 March 2025.  Following completion of the statutory review into the operation of the existing mitigation schemes I have decided that these statutory protections should be continued. This will guarantee that all households in Northern Ireland affected by the Social Sector Size Criteria will receive mitigation payments equivalent to the reduction in their benefit. All families with children will also continue to be protected, in full, from the Benefit Cap.I will introduce legislation in early 2025 to provide for this continued protection.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on funding for community transport.
answer_text: I value the contribution that community transport in all its forms makes to the lives of those who use it.  Having access to suitable transport assists many people to remain connected to their communities and get to places they need to go.Earlier this year, I increased annual funding to those providing community transport by 4.2%. This increase was not as much as I would have liked to provide.  I hope the increase will help alleviate some of the cost pressures faced by service providers. I have also indicated my intention to maintain this level of funding into 2025/26, subject to allocation of funds to my Department.You will be aware that public finances and budgets remain pressurised.  I would have liked to have done more but my department, like other departments, is operating in a challenging financial environment due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. This has led to a situation where even maintaining existing funding for services is challenging.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 17988/22-27, for a breakdown of where (i) the allocation for structural maintenance of roads and street lighting; and (ii) the £19.5 million to release wastewater constraints, will be spent, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Further to AQW 17988/22-27, the breakdown of where (i) the allocation for structural maintenance of roads and street lighting, and (ii) the £19.5m to release wastewater constraints, will be spent is detailed in the tables below.Structural Maintenance & Street LightingRoad Division North£’mSouth£’mEast £’mWest£’mTotal£mStructural Maintenance4.623.021.83.0612.5Street Lighting0.60.50.450.452Note: Road maintenance and street lighting budget is allocated to road divisions rather than constituency.Wastewater ConstraintsCouncil Area £mBelfast City Council 7.0Derry City & Strabane District Council 2.1Antrim & Newtownabbey Borough Council 0.5Lisburn & Castlereagh City Council 7.4Antrim & Newtownabbey Borough Council 0.5Mid Ulster District Council2.8Note: NI Water systems do not record capital projects by constituency, but rather by Council area.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of additional, publicly-accessible, electric vehicle charge points and rapid charge points since April 2023, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: My Department does not hold records of the number of publicly available Electric Vehicle (EV) charge points in the North. The British Government publishes periodic updates by local council area and Parliamentary constituency which you may find helpful. These can be found in the link below. https://www.gov.uk/government/statistics/electric-vehicle-public-charging-infrastructure-statistics-july-2024Latest statistics show that there are 651 publicly accessible EV charge points in the North. This represents a growth of 67% in the total number of available since April 2023 and the number of rapid charge points, which provide significantly faster opportunity to charge, has grown by over 300% in the same period.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of (i) construction; and (ii) commercial projects that have been lost due to issues with wastewater infrastructure in each of the last five years.
answer_text: NI Water has advised that it does not hold any information on the number of construction or commercial projects that might have been lost due to issues with wastewater infrastructure

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the estimated number of vacant homes in each of the last three years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The Land & Property Services (LPS) rating system does not hold information at a constituency level and does not maintain a register of 'empty homes'. For the purposes of this response, 'empty homes' is defined as properties included in the housing stock and recorded as vacant, on the LPS rating system. Please note, information in the rating system on domestic properties recorded as vacant may not be current or complete. Since the introduction of the Rating of Empty Homes legislation on 1st October 2011, the rate liability for vacant domestic properties has been assessed at 100%. There is currently no requirement for ratepayers to inform Land & Property Services that their property is vacant, nor is there any financial advantage in doing so.Figures have been provided by District Council (April 2022 to April 2024).Number of properties included in Housing stock, recorded as vacant, by District Council (April 2022 - April 2024)District Council2022 2023 2024 Antrim and Newtownabbey        985      1,016    1,131 Ards and North Down     1,410      1,477    1,541 Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon     2,132      2,176    2,192 Belfast     3,291      3,355    3,292 Causeway Coast and Glens     1,825      1,886    1,906 Derry City and Strabane     1,406      1,454    1,524 Fermanagh and Omagh     2,482      2,497    2,341 Lisburn and Castlereagh     1,113      1,138    1,095 Mid and East Antrim     1,287      1,342    1,389 Mid Ulster     2,110      2,184    2,235 Newry, Mourne and Down     2,384      2,427    2,435 Northern Ireland   20,425    20,952  21,081

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 17081/22-27, to detail the (i) topics discussed at; and (ii) duration of, each of the three meetings of the Homelessness Strategy Steering Group in 2024.
answer_text: Topics discussed during 2024 Homelessness Strategy Steering Group meetings included:The Interdepartmental Homelessness Action Plan The Homelessness Strategy operational update Emerging issuesWhilst the length of these meetings is not formally recorded, they last on average between one and a half to two hours.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 6197/22-27, for an update on any further engagement with Ronald McDonald House Charities in relation to the establishment of accommodation for sick children and their families.
answer_text: Further to Minister Swann’s answer on this some months ago my officials have spoken at length with senior officers in Belfast Health and Social Services (BHSCT) on the matter.BHSCT have informed my officials that they had a number of engagements with Ronald McDonald House Charity (RMDHC) about potentially opening a house in proximity to the Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children (RBHSC) over the past decade.  During the first round of discussions the Trust felt that these discussions had been productive with the charity. There was significant support from a local convent that was felt to be an ideal location for a house but the charity didn’t support the proposal at that time.BHSCT then re-engaged with RMDHC in 2019 and again held a number of meetings with the charity.  The Trust felt that these discussions again had been productive.  The Trust agreed to put significant resources into refurbishment of a suitable building in the area.  However, at this point the charity stopped engagement with the Trust.  BHSCT have explored a number of options with the local community following this but the opportunity that was there for use of a suitable site in close proximity to the RBHSC has now passed.The Trust is still keen to ensure that a suitable site for families is found but are cognisant that an opportunity was lost a number of years ago despite significant work and the promise of financial support from the Trust and the support of the local community.They will continue to examine means to open a property in the vicinity of the RBHSC but they are not convinced that the RMDHC business model which requires a new build site to be used would suit the landscape in and around the RBHSC where demand for new social housing is the main priority.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will consider establishing a model whereby women who undergo a dilation and curettage procedure following a miscarriage, or who experience a stillbirth, are provided access to a separate birthing suite or placed in a separate ward to avoid the added trauma of being near newborns and new mothers.
answer_text: Early Pregnancy Clinics are available in most Maternity/Gynaecology units across Northern Ireland.  These clinics will have telephone triage and self-referral triage in place that successfully help to divert those experiencing pregnancy loss away from ED units, however it is not possible at this time to provide 24/7 Early Pregnancy Clinics and this can mean that not all patients avail of this service in their time of need. If a patient is admitted into a gynaecology ward, staff will be very aware of the sensitivities for mothers and families around the time of a miscarriage or loss of a baby and if at all possible the women will be admitted into a quieter side ward at this sad time.Maternity wards and midwifery service staff are also very aware of the sensitivities for mothers and families around the time of a loss of a baby and how important it is to provide a safe environment to allow a family to grieve without being exposed to others celebrating. All Trusts endeavour to provide a room in a quieter ward e.g. Early Pregnancy Clinic Area, a side toom in a gynaecology ward, or a Bereavement Suite / room in a maternity area where there is less likelihood of babies in the vicinity. However, there may be some occasions, when a mother may have to be placed in a ward bed were there are babies nearby, due to availability of rooms. However, staff do their utmost to provide a private room as well as all other psychological and physical supports available at this sad time.The infrastructure varies across Trusts. Trust’s responses are summarised below:South Eastern HSC TrustWithin the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust miscarriages are managed within the Early Pregnancy Problem Clinic (EPPC).  This clinic is based from Ward 4E which is our Gynaecology ward.  Ward 4E is located in a separate building from the Maternity Hospital.In the event a woman has a still birth she is cared for in our Bereavement Suite which is located in a specific area of Labour Ward which is designed specifically for this purpose.Northern HSC TrustThe Northern Trust cares for women who have a stillbirth in the ‘Willow Suite’ which is a bereavement room opened in 2023,  outside of the birthing suite.  This room was specifically commissioned and designed to provide compassionate and sensitive bereavement care to women and their families during the sad loss of their baby.  The woman will remain in the room after the birth until she is discharged home. Regrettably if there is more than one woman, they may be placed in a side room in the maternity ward. Midwives will try to minimise exposure to other patients or families who are celebrating a birth.  Women who sadly experience a miscarriage are cared for in a single room in the gynaecology ward for the duration of their care so are also not placed in wards where other women or families are celebrating a birth.Belfast HSC TrustThe Belfast Trust Maternity Service staff are very aware of the sensitivities for mothers and families aro

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the average; and (ii) the longest wait for first consultant-led neurology appointments in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Information on the average waiting times by Health and Social Care Trust by speciality across Northern Ireland is available on My Waiting Times NI.
