question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients on neurology waiting lists in each of the last three years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Information on the number of patients waiting for a first consultant-led outpatient appointment in Northern Ireland is published on a quarterly basis in the ‘Northern Ireland Outpatient Waiting Time Statistics’ bulletin. The most recent issue of this bulletin has a reporting date of 30 September 2024 and is available to view at:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-waiting-time-statistics-outpatient-waiting-times-september-2024 The number of patients waiting for a first consultant-led outpatient appointment under the Neurology specialty, by Health and Social Care Trust, is included in the associated ‘Outpatient Waiting Times Statistics by HSC Trust and Specialty September 2024’ Excel file, which is available for download at the same location. Information on the number of patients waiting for admission for inpatient or day case treatment in Northern Ireland is published on a quarterly basis in the ‘Northern Ireland Inpatient and Day Case Waiting Time Statistics’ bulletin. The most recent issue of this bulletin has a reporting date of 30 September 2024 and is available to view at:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-waiting-time-statistics-inpatient-and-day-case-waiting-times-september-2024 The number of patients waiting for admission for inpatient or day case treatment under the Neurology specialty, by Health and Social Care Trust, is included in the associated ‘Statistics on Inpatient & Day Case Waiting Times by HSC Trust September 2024’ Excel file, which is available for download at the same location. The next issues of these bulletins will have a reporting date of 30 December 2024 and will be published on 27 February 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to the Registrar General Annual Report 2023, for his assessment of the statistic that suicide rates are three times higher in areas of high deprivation.
answer_text: The Protect Life 2 Strategy recognises that suicide is a major inequality where those living in more deprived areas are disproportionately affected by suicide. For this reason, the Strategy aims to ensure that suicide prevention services and support are delivered appropriately in deprived areas where suicide and self-harm rates are highest. The Public Health Agency also fund a small grant programme for community and voluntary organisations to promote positive mental health and emotional wellbeing and tackle the contributing factors of self-harm and suicide through the Take 5 Steps to Wellbeing. The aim of this grant programme is to reduce health inequalities by increasing the delivery of resources, programmes and/or services to the top 20% most disadvantaged neighbourhoods. This programme issues each year and in the 2024 – 2025 financial year there have been 491 grants awarded for an investment of around £1.6 million.The Health Inequalities Annual Report 2024 shows consistent decreases in self-harm presentations in deprived areas over recent years. The Self Harm Intervention Programme has recently been commissioned across all HSC Trust areas to continue to provide support for those who self harm and their families and carers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether his Department has assessed the effectiveness of the Protect Life 2 - Suicide Prevention Strategy in its aim to prevent suicide; and (ii) whether the strategy will be reviewed.
answer_text: An independent review of the Protect Life 2 Action Plan was commissioned by the Department of Health to help identify areas where additional priority or new actions should be considered. The Review Report was published on 27 September 2024 and is available at: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/doh-pl2-action-plan-report-recommendations.pdf. The review specifically considered whether the Strategy actions support the progress of objectives within the Strategy. The Review showed that over 80% of respondents felt that the actions were effective in progressing against objectives. The Review has made a number of recommendations for enhancing the action plan. A revised action plan and implementation plan has been drafted and the process of stakeholder engagement has commenced. Considerable focus is being given to enhancing the measurement and efficacy of the Strategy going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether data relating to the number of attempted suicide-related presentations to emergency departments is being collated and monitored by his Department.
answer_text: Northern Ireland is one of the few countries to have a comprehensive country-wide self-harm monitoring system. The Self Harm Registry collects data regarding attendances to all eleven hospital Emergency Departments with self-harming behaviour. In addition it also uniquely collects data in relation to attendances with ideation (thoughts of self-harm and suicide where no act has taken place).An Annual Report is produced by the Registry each year and enhances understanding of the issue and is used by those involved in policy, planning and delivery of services for people who experience these difficult issues. The figures for the most recent year’s report, 2021/2022, showed that 61% of self-harm presentations were for drug overdose; 28% were for self-cutting; 1% were for self-poisoning; 7% were attempted hanging, and 5% were for attempted drowning.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will publish a revised reduction target for the Protect Life 2 - Suicide Prevention Strategy.
answer_text: A new baseline will be set as part of the new Protect Life 2 Action Plan which is currently in development. The existing 10% reduction target will then apply to that revised baseline. I would stress that sustained reductions in suicide will only be possible with full implementation and delivery of all Protect Life 2 actions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for a breakdown of how the £6.7 million announced for homelessness prevention services will be spent, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive will be utilising £3.7m of the funding allocated to homelessness through the October Monitoring Round on key community prevention and support initiatives. These activities are delivered within communities by 3rd party providers, many of which provide services across Northern Ireland. Additionally, £3m of funding was also received to support Housing Executive service delivery across a range of activities (including homelessness) across Northern Ireland.Consequently, it is not possible to readily identify how this funding will be utilised across local council areas.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 18284/22-27, to clarify which dates his Department has defined as the Christmas period.
answer_text: In line with the Department for Work and Pensions, my Department did not issue Migration Notices from 24 September 2024 to 2 October 2024 which would result in no deadline dates from 25 December 2024 to 3 January 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she has been made aware of ongoing audio and video issues with the civil service application process, following the latest recruitment exercise.
answer_text: A number of candidates have reported some technical issues during the pre-recorded interview stage of the recent recruitment competition for NICS Administrative Officers (IRC303169).Any issues raised by candidates have been assessed on a case by case basis and in conjunction with the interview platform provider.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has had any engagement with the Department of Health in relation to the Help with Health Costs Scheme, including review of the current threshold criteria.
answer_text: My officials are in regular contact with the relevant Department of Health (DoH) officials in regard to the Help with Health Costs Scheme for Universal Credit claimants. However, as the Health Costs scheme is owned by the Department of Health, any review of the current threshold criteria would be within that Department’s remit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) an estimate of the amount of publicly-owned land across Northern Ireland; and (ii) a breakdown of the main agencies or Departments that hold ownership.
answer_text: The Government Land and Property Register (GLPR) project, that sits within Land & Property Services, has been developing a centrally held database of all government owned and/or occupied land and property here.The table below outlines the breakdown of the area of land that is owned and/or occupied by each of the departments and their ALB’s as of March 2024. The capture of assets is part of an ongoing mapping project and should not be regarded as complete.DepartmentTotal Area of Land (Hectares) – Owned and/or Occupied by Departments & their ALBsDAERA	108,108DE	5,014DfC	2,134DfE	1,812DoH	1,042TEO	418DoF	315DoJ	176DfI	737Total	119,756 HectaresAs part of the ongoing work for GLPR, the team are working with internal colleagues to identify the breakdown of ownership and review legacy folios where assets fall within old department names.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) his Department's threshold for the resurfacing of trunk roads; and (ii) whether this threshold applies across all four divisional offices.
answer_text: Under Article 8 of the Roads (Northern Ireland) Order 1993, my Department has a statutory duty to maintain all public roads in a safe and serviceable condition and an annual programme of resurfacing and reconstruction works is undertaken, the extent of which is dictated by the availability of funding.On trunk roads, information is routinely gathered and analysed from a variety of specialist machine surveys and visual inspections, and this information is used in order to help inform decisions regarding annual maintenance programmes. Programmes are subject to continual review, taking into account a wide range of factors, for example, worsening condition of other roads, planned works by utilities, additional in year funding, contractor capacity etc. There is no set threshold that a trunk road has to meet to be considered for resurfacing. Rather, in the absence of a fully funded allocation for the structural maintenance programme, my Department aims to prioritise decisions and interventions on the basis of the indicators outlined above as well as on the professional engineering judgements of our local engineers. Those schemes not included in a particular year’s programme as result of funding constraints will routinely be considered in future years’ programmes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will work with the Housing Executive to ensure that homeless families and individuals are not displaced from temporary accommodation in Derry during December and throughout the festive period.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive seeks to minimise disruption to households in temporary accommodation during December and the festive period. In the vast majority of cases, it is anticipated that there will be no impact on temporary accommodation arrangements. The Housing Executive has begun contacting providers to confirm arrangements over December and the festive period. In any cases where opening times over December and the festive period result in a requirement for households to be moved, this will be facilitated with a view to minimising the impact on households affected.” For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 18601/22-27, whether he has any indication when agreement between the UK and Irish Governments will be reached.
answer_text: As previously stated in response to AQW 18601/22-27, discussions between the UK and Irish Governments on this issue are a reserved matter and not within the competence of my Department or the Northern Ireland Assembly. As my Department is not involved in the discussions, a timeline for an agreement is not known at this time.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the introduction of a Baby Loss Certificate Scheme.
answer_text: I remain fully supportive of the introduction of a Baby Loss Certificate Scheme in Northern Ireland for parents who have suffered the loss of a child before 24 weeks gestation. In a joint announcement on 9 October, the Finance Minister outlined her Department’s intention to bring forward legislation to enable a scheme to be developed in Northern Ireland. My Department will continue to work closely with the Department of Finance on the necessary scoping work. I have also asked my officials to continue to engage with the Department of Health and Social Care so that all options for introducing a scheme can be considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 15219/22-27, whether his Department has reviewed the report from the developer on their traffic assumptions and modelling updates in relation to the signalisation of the Caw roundabout.
answer_text: The upgrade and signalisation of Caw roundabout is included in the planning conditions for the adjacent H30 housing development and responsibility for its delivery rests with the developer.My officials have completed a review of the information provided on the 20 September and met with the developer on 23 October to discuss their proposals, and the timing for delivery of their works to Caw Roundabout. Further information was submitted by the developer on 15 November to detail their proposals and timeframes, which will inform a decision on the proposed mitigation strategy. My officials are currently considering this further information. In the interim, the developer has agreed to progress the detailed design of Caw Roundabout.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the introduction of a Baby Loss Certificate Scheme.
answer_text: I am keen to see this scheme taken forward as quickly as possible to give bereaved families the opportunity to have formal recognition of their loss. I want all parents and families who have suffered the devastating loss of a baby to feel that their grief is recognised.My Department is currently working with the Office of the Legislative Counsel to draft the Deaths, Still-Births and Baby Loss Bill to enable a Baby Loss Certificate Scheme. This primary legislation will be the start of the process to establish a scheme here and I hope to introduce the Bill into the Assembly in early 2025. We will continue to work with the Department of Health to specify the detail of this important initiative, to make sure that it meets the needs and expectations of the bereaved families, and to scope delivery options. My officials continue to progress this work at pace: A more detailed schedule will be made available when this legislation has been considered by the Assembly, including the secondary regulations which will set out the detail of the scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on Action 7.2 of the Protect Life 2 Suicide Prevention Strategy to train 50 per cent of frontline health and social care staff in suicide prevention; and (ii) to detail the number of staff that have been trained since the implementation of the strategy, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: This target has not been possible to monitor as the information is not held centrally and suicide prevention training data held by HSC Trusts cannot be broken down by profession. Information is only available in relation to numbers of staff trained. A revised action relating to training will be included within the new Protect Life 2 Action Plan that can be appropriately monitored.HSC Trusts have responsibility around staff training and how they collect that data. GPs are largely responsible for directing their own Continuing Professional Development which should cover the whole of their practice and is reviewed annually as part of their annual appraisal.This table details the number of staff who have completed training in suicide prevention, broken down by Health & Social Care Trust.TrainingNumber of Trust staff who completed BHSCT Number of Trust staff who completed SEHSCTNumber of Trust staff who completed SHSCTNumber of Trust staff who completed NHSCTNumber of Trust staff who completed WHSCTLevel 1 zero suicide prevention online awareness training completed by all Trust staff 18001776412860001326Storm suicide prevention and self-harm mitigation 2 day course42403513495Safety planning information session attended by trust mental health practitioners across the 5 HSC Trusts.460735604250350Suicide prevention care pathway (SPCP) training (Case & Pisani)  21538211103127Suicide prevention and emotional health and wellbeing training will continue to be widely promoted and staff encouraged to undertake training.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) whether his Department has conducted research into links between increased poverty rates and the prevalence of mental health issues; and (ii) any engagement he has had with the Minister of Health in relation to the higher suicide rates in areas of high deprivation, as part of the Anti-Poverty Strategy.
answer_text: Poverty is a complex and multi-faceted issue and addressing these issues at their root cause is a key priority for me.Whilst my Department has not carried out any specific research on the relationship between poverty and mental health, my officials continue to engage with their colleagues in the other Executive Departments, including the Department of Health, in developing the Anti-poverty Strategy. I would expect that this will include their consideration of a range of health-related issues which are linked to socio-economic disadvantage.Ultimately, the content of the Anti-Poverty Strategy will be a matter for the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will commit to ensuring lighting provision is installed at Coshquin, Buncrana Road, to improve safety for both road users and the wider community.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he has given further consideration to introduce full free public transport travel for individuals with disabilities, similar to provisions in other UK regions.
answer_text: In June 2024, following a public consultation on the NI Concessionary Fares Scheme, I announced that, due to the financial pressures facing the Department, I was not in a position to extend the half-fare concession to free travel for people with disabilities, but would reconsider introduction of the option in the future if the financial position allows.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) why suicide prevention was not included in the Programme for Government; and (ii) for his assessment of whether suicide is an urgent public health crisis.
answer_text: The Executive Office was responsible for the development and drafting of the Programme for Government.I am acutely aware of the devastating impact of each suicide on families, communities and indeed further across society. Whilst every suicide is one suicide too many, it is also worth noting that there can be fluctuations in the suicide rate from year to year, and calculations on a 3 year rolling average provide a more accurate representation of trends. The 3 year rolling average of suicide deaths has remained relatively stable since 2017 at around 11.6 deaths per 100,000.The development of the new Protect Life 2 Action Plan and implementation plan, which will shortly be circulated to stakeholders for comment and further refinement, will contain a wide range of actions to reduce the rate of suicide in Northern Ireland. The Lifeline 24/7 helpline remains available for anyone in distress or despair on 0808 808 8000. The Self harm Intervention programme has recently been recommissioned and is being provided on a regional basis.I would stress that sustained reductions in suicide will only be possible with full implementation and delivery of all Protect Life 2 actions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure why the commitments made in 2019 to heighten parapets at (i) Divis Street and Clifton Street, Belfast; and (ii) the Foyle Bridge, Derry, have not been actioned.
answer_text: I fully appreciate the significance of these works to the Divis and Clifton Street Bridges over Westlink in Belfast and the Foyle Bridge in Derry. I have recently had discussions with the Mental Health Champion, Professor Siobhan O'Neill, and a number of local MLAs regarding the Westlink barriers proposals. Depending on the availability of funding and successful completion of the detailed design, contract documents and business case, officials hope to be in a position to erect the barriers at Divis and Clifton Street bridges over Westlink in the new financial year.In respect to the Foyle Bridge, please may I refer you to my previous answer dated 5th March 2024 (AQW 7886/22-27).  I confirm that the information contained in this response remains an accurate reflection of the Department’s position on this matter.I continue to support the need for these works, but with the very challenging budgetary position within my Department, the indicative cost of £10 million is unaffordable at this time. I do however want to assure you that my officials will continue to work with the Mental Health Champion and the other groups involved in the provision of crisis services, to seek to identify funding to carry out the bridge assessment and the installation of suicide prevention measures.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of damp-related reports received by the Housing Executive; and (ii) the number of these reports that relate to temporary accommodation, in each of the last three years, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“ (i) 	The following table details the number of damp-related reports received by the Housing Executive in each of the last three years, broken down by local council area. The following table figures represent:Jobs with Mould/Damp/Condensation within the Job Description.Reported Date between 01/04/21 – 31/03/24 (inclusive of all Job Statuses) Council AreaReported financial yearGrand total2021/20222022/20232023/2024Antrim and Newtownabbey8601,0881,2423,190Ards and North Down7909241,0782,792Armagh City Banbridge and Craigavon8941,1321,6523,678Belfast2,7293,4774,77310,979Causeway Coast and Glens7909641,3073,061Derry City and Strabane1,1391,4791,5504,168Fermanagh and Omagh3675006791,546Lisburn and Castlereagh5587031,2242,485Mid and East Antrim8051,2421,7913,838Mid Ulster3684284841,280Newry Mourne and Down7759801,4173,172Grand total10,07512,91717,19740,189(ii)The Housing Executive does not hold a central database detailing the number of damp related reports for temporary accommodation. In order to extract this information, the Housing Executive would need to review each file where a household has been placed in temporary accommodation and identify the overall number. The Housing Executive is exploring ways in which this information can be collated centrally for properties used as Single Lets.” For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any engagement his Department has had with the Department for Communities in relation to potential changes to the Help with Healthcare Costs scheme.
answer_text: As you are aware, work on the proposed changes to the Travelling Expenses and Remission of Charges Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2004 is at an advanced stage. This is a complex area, it is not the case that all Universal Credit recipients in GB are automatically eligible for help with health care costs including free eye tests. In GB, an earnings threshold is applied alongside Universal Credit to determine eligibility. This work is being finalised and my Department is making final preparations to launch a short public consultation in January, with the associated legislative process commencing quickly thereafter.Ongoing engagement with other NI Departments, the Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC) in England and other Devolved Administrations has been crucial throughout this process. Officials continue to have regular and ongoing engagement with the Department for Communities (DfC) across all aspects of Help with Health Costs (HwHC) including the legislative change that is required for the future delivery of the HwHC Scheme under Universal Credit. While DfC has a significant role in terms of processing applications to the Low Income Scheme, my Department, through BSO, conducts considerable administrative processing in terms of reimbursement to opticians and dentists.While ongoing engagement with DfC will continue to be key for the future delivery of HwHC, ultimately the Department of Health has policy responsibility for the HwHC Scheme. Therefore, responsibility for the forthcoming legislative amendment and implementation of the changes to current operations once the legislation is amended rests with my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the current reliance on non-standard accommodation, including its effectiveness at meeting the needs of homeless individuals experiencing mental health and addiction issues.
answer_text: The Housing Executive will only use non-standard temporary accommodation in the absence of all other options and for as short a duration as possible. The most recently published figures note that there was a total of 465 households in non-standard temporary accommodation as of 5th November 2024.  This reflects the challenges in accessing other types of temporary accommodation such as single lets and voluntary sector hostels.I have previously acknowledged that being placed in non-standard temporary accommodation is not good for people and can have an additional impact on those already suffering from mental health or addiction issues. However, the Housing Executive will signpost any individuals placed in non-standard accommodation to floating support services to assist with those issues.  The Complex Lives programme facilitated by Belfast City Council, and led by a Multi-Disciplinary Team, takes a person-centred approach and addresses issues like mental health and addiction.The Departments Supporting People programme also funds the provision of short-term accommodation-based support for those in housing need, short-term floating support to maintain people’s independence in their own home and support on an ongoing peripatetic basis for service users with more enduring or complex needs. In recognition that not all issues are accommodation based, my Department leads on the Interdepartmental Homelessness Action Plan which focuses on addressing gaps in non-accommodation services, like mental health and addiction, for those who are homeless or at risk of becoming homeless.  Both the Department of Health and Justice are responsible owners of actions within the IDHAP.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will consider a Move-On Accommodation Scheme, to include ongoing support from trained resettlement advisors.
answer_text: I announced The Loan to Acquire Move-on Accommodation program, on 9 December 2024, to enable homelessness charities in Northern Ireland to access funding and purchase properties to offer safe, secure and affordable rental accommodation for people who are currently homeless and living in hostels. This program will support a wide range of clients and it will be at the discretion of the provider making use of the loan to determine whether a resettlement advisor is appropriate or required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will consider mandatory dementia training for adult social care staff.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the implementation of actions in the (i) Blue Carbon Action Plan; (ii) Marine Protected Areas Strategy Review; and (iii) Seabird Conservation Strategy.
answer_text: At the request of stakeholders, I extended the public consultation period for both the Blue Carbon Action Plan and the reviewed Marine Protected Areas Strategy, and the consultations closed on 9th August 2024.  My officials are currently analysing the responses.My Department also recently launched a public consultation on the Seabird Conservation Strategy and Action Plan for Northern Ireland. This consultation closes on the 16th December 2024, after which my officials will review the responses.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any engagement he has had with the Minister for Communities in relation to (i) addressing high rates of mental health issues and addiction among the homeless population; and (ii) ensuring access to crisis support in order to prevent an escalation in needs.
answer_text: Mental health and addressing the harm related to substance use is a key priority for my department. While I have not had any direct engagement with the Minister for Communities in respect of these issues, my officials continue to work collaboratively with Department for Communities’ officials, and cross-departmentally, in the development and progression of the Inter-Departmental Homelessness Action Plan, which aims to improves the lives of those who are or at risk of becoming homeless. One action for my department within this plan includes taking forward programmes of work in relation to early intervention and prevention for those disproportionately affected by mental ill health, including those who are homeless.A further action relates to the development and delivery of a new outcomes focused strategic plan and the securing of an additional resource to lead on a piece of work to understand the services, population profile, models of service delivery, and training needs to support those with co-occurring mental health and substance use issues.The Mental Health Strategy and Substance Use Strategy aim to improve mental health and prevent harm and empower recovery for all and those most at risk, including those who are homeless. A newly established co-occurring Mental Health and Substance Use Task and Finish Group has been established, which will look at how we develop an integrated approach to co-occurring mental health and substance use, delivered in both specialist mental health and drug and alcohol services. Implementing a new regional crisis service is a commitment in the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031, under actions 12 and 27. The Strategic Planning and Performance Group and the Public Health Agency are working in partnership to deliver on both of these actions, to develop a Regional Mental Health Crisis Service for all citizens of Northern Ireland. Any individual, regardless of their resident status, who self presents or comes to the attention of emergency services will be able to access crisis support via current services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on the progress of the revised strategic planning policy for renewable and low carbon energy; and (ii) when he intends to publish the policy.
answer_text: I circulated a draft Executive Paper to my Executive colleagues seeking their agreement to the final policy in September. The revised policy will be published as soon as possible following Executive Committee agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given the 8 per cent rise in death by suicide as reported in the Annual Report of the Registrar General for 2023, to detail any immediate steps being taken to address this increase.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the devastating impact of each suicide on families, communities and indeed further across society. Whilst every suicide is one suicide too many, it is also worth noting that there can be fluctuations in the suicide rate from year to year, and calculations on a 3 year rolling average provide a more accurate representation of trends. The 3 year rolling average of suicide deaths has remained relatively stable since 2017 at around 11.6 deaths per 100,000.The development of the new Protect Life 2 Action Plan and implementation plan which will shortly be circulated to stakeholders for comment and further refinement will contain a wide range of actions to reduce the rate of suicide in Northern Ireland. The Lifeline 24/7 helpline remains available for anyone in distress or despair on 0808 808 8000. The Self harm Intervention programme has recently been recommissioned and is being provided on a regional basis.I would stress that sustained reductions in suicide will only be possible with full implementation and delivery of all Protect Life 2 actions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 16853/22-27, to detail (i) the steps he is taking to reduce planning timelines for regionally significant renewable energy projects: and (ii) the additional resource is being committed to process the increased number of such applications that will be required to meet the 80 per cent renewable electricity by 2030 target, as set out in the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: I am committed to ensuring our planning system operates in an efficient and effective way and recognise we all have an important role to play to meet the targets set out in the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022. My Department continues to work closely with stakeholders and partners to improve current processes and the performance and delivery of our planning system. This includes all planning applications, some of which involve projects for renewable energy.  Within my Department, steps have already been taken to increase staffing, mitigate and address performance of consultees. I am pleased during 2023/24 the proportion of statutory consultations responded to on-time (within 21 calendar days or longer agreed timeframe) was 74%, this rate improved from the 72% recorded on-time in 2022/23. But there is more to do and I am delighted the ‘Transforming Planning’ bid to the Interim Public Sector Transformation Board has made it to Stage 2 of the process. This bid includes proposals to support and enhance the role of my Department’s Statutory Consultees and to appoint persons to undertake hear and report work, some of which is for regionally significant renewable energy projects, that the PAC has been unable to progress. I am hopeful that this bid will ultimately be successful.I want to continue to build on positive work that has been taken forward  through the Planning Improvement Programme and within my own Department, will focus on identifying challenges, improving performance,  recognising success and promoting key learning across the system.My Department is also looking to fill a range of vacancies in the Planning Group, in line with my Department’s priorities including renewable energy planning applications, and which will be subject to the availability of the necessary funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has considered the use of Artificial Intelligence technologies across Health and Social Trusts to assist in enhancing services, including to improve digital communications and personalise treatment plans.
answer_text: Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Generative AI technologies offers opportunities to improve both productivity of the existing HSC workforce and the quality-of-care services delivered, with benefits including the freeing up of capacity to enhance services through improved communications and tailored treatment plans.The latest initiative utilises smart technology for waiting list validation. The HSC is now sending text reminders and placing QR codes on patient letters taking them to surveys which help the relevant clinician determine whether the patient needs to remain on the waiting list. Future plans include testing AI algorithms to assist with referral/ decision making, scheduling and acquisition, reporting and triage of reporting, and results acknowledgement/alerts.However, the potential capabilities of this technology must be set against the potential risks – for instance in the areas of clinical efficacy and information governance. In these areas the Department of Health and the Health and Social Care (HSC) system needs to ensure that the tools provided are trustworthy and do not breach the privacy of patients or service users.Additionally, the wide adoption of advanced technologies like AI/GenAI require a number of underlying enablers – including good quality, codified data that describes a service users needs and historic episodes and treatments (for training purposes), the clinical safety and ethical governance structures that are required to oversee the safe introduction of these AI tools, and skills in cloud services engineering and security (recognising that the compute power required to run AI algorithms is often best provided from cloud technologies). The Department of Health and its partners in the HSC are establishing structures to develop these enablers and explore the capabilities of providers and products, with a particular focus in the first instance on medical image analysis for diagnostic purposes, an area with growing evidence to support clinical effectiveness.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the high number of anonymity orders granted to suspects of drug-related crime in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: Open justice is a key principle of the justice system and of society, however a court may, in the interests of justice, deem it necessary to depart from this principle.An anonymity order may be granted for defendants, victims or witnesses and normally prohibits the naming of an individual but does not prohibit the reporting of the proceedings.The media may make representations to the court when it is considering making such an order or may challenge such decisions judicially either by way of appeal if the order is made in the Crown Court or by way of judicial review proceedings if it was made in the magistrates’ court.The longstanding principle of judicial independence precludes me from commenting on judicial decisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any discussions he has had with his Executive colleagues in relation to capital budget commitments that could be hampered by ongoing water and wastewater infrastructure issues.
answer_text: In terms of my engagement with the Executive, I am taking a three-pronged approach to address this issue of capital investment which is being affected by the historic underfunding of our water and wastewater system.Specifically, I am: (i) continuing to work with my Executive colleagues to try and increase wastewater investment; (ii) reviewing the policy and legislation around developer contributions; and (iii) I have secured Executive agreement for the Water, Flooding and Sustainable Drainage Bill to be introduced to the Assembly. This legislation will provide powers to encourage increased use of sustainable drainage systems which use nature to separate and release storm water more slowly into the system so that it does not overrun wastewater treatment works.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, following his comments made on BBC's The View on 21 November 2024, (i) to provide further detail on the 18,500 potential homes that could be connected to water and wastewater infrastructure through developer contributions; and (ii) for a breakdown of these homes for each local council area.
answer_text: (i)	The estimated 18,500 properties, to which I referred on BBC’s The View, are properties that could be connected if developers design, fund and construct alternative solutions, such as stormwater offsetting.  No legislative amendment is required to permit this type of developer contribution.    It should be noted that these numbers are estimated figures that NI Water has obtained   through discussion with developers and they are subject to a degree of change, as developer’s plans change with time and other market pressures. 	(ii) 	The table below details the number of properties, by council area, that could be connected if developers design, fund and construct alternative solutions such as stormwater offsetting.Council AreaTotal UnitsAntrim & Newtownabbey 866 Ards & North Down 3257 Armagh, Banbridge & Craigavon 448 Belfast 2405 Causeway Coast & Glens 413 Derry City & Strabane 5268 Fermanagh & Omagh 1589 Lisburn & Castlereagh 1712 Mid & East Antrim 157 Mid Ulster 1210 Newry, Mourne & Down 1334     Total 18659

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement he has had with (i) developers; and (ii) the Minister for Communities in relation to new build housing developments delayed by water and wastewater infrastructure.
answer_text: I have discussed wastewater issues with a range of stakeholders representing developers’ interests including the Construction Employers Federation, Confederation of British Industry, Federation of Master Builders and the Royal Institution of Chartered Surveyors as well as the Northern Ireland Housing Executive in respect of delivering social housing and outlined the measures being taken to help alleviate the impact of wastewater issues; andI have also discussed wastewater issues with Minister Lyons in respect of its impact on the delivery of the Housing Supply Strategy and advised of the actions that my Department is taking to help address wastewater issues.   You will be aware that I am taking a three-pronged approach to help address wastewater capacity challenges. Firstly, I continue to work with my Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment for NI Water, secondly, I am bringing forward new legislation in relation to sustainable drainage systems (SuDS), which will help to store stormwater so that it releases slower into the wastewater network and thirdly, I have asked my officials to examine the legislative change required to bring forward developer contributions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has assessed the current levels of (i) inter-tidal hand-gathering of shellfish species; and (ii) bait-digging within Foyle Lough.
answer_text: The Department carried out a 12-week Consultation and Call for Evidence on the Intertidal Hand-gathering of Shellfish within Northern Ireland in 2022. Of the 34 responses received, no evidence was provided of intertidal hand-gathering of shellfish within Lough Foyle.My Department does not carry out targeted monitoring of bait digging. Staff will however record any occurrences of the activity observed during the course of routine monitoring in the inter-tidal region.  With specific reference to Lough Foyle, one incident of bait digging has been recorded in the past 5 years.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any action his Department is taking to address the number of drug-related deaths involving Class C drugs; and (ii) any engagement he has had with the Minister of Justice in relation to setting tougher guidelines for Class C drugs.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the draft Nature Recovery Strategy.
answer_text: My Department plans to consult on the draft Nature Recovery Strategy in early 2025 after which it will be prepared for publication later that year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the status of the Major Capital Works Programme.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any action he is taking to ensure the cessation of all unlicensed aquaculture activities; and (ii) any engagement he has had with the Loughs Agency in relation to delivering effective enforcement, particularly in relation to Foyle Lough.
answer_text: The unlicensed aquaculture in Lough Foyle is inextricably linked to the jurisdictional issue, a reserved matter, which is not within the competence of my Department, or the Northern Ireland Assembly. While this is a matter for the UK and Irish Governments, my Department continues to engage with all stakeholders, including the UK Government, to make progress to enable the regulation of aquaculture in Lough Foyle.  Constructive engagement with the UK Government took place as recently as October 2024.  My Department, in conjunction with the Loughs Agency, has also been proactive, as far as possible, in addressing further expansion.  Enforcement action was taken by my Department to remove unregulated oyster trestles located on the Northern Irish shoreline in 2018, and again in 2023, which resulted in unregulated oyster trestles being moved from the environmentally sensitive Lough Foyle Area of Special Scientific Interest. There is regular engagement between my Department and the Agency on enforcement matters, including the Agency providing an update to Ministers for the North South Ministerial Council meeting in June 2024 and engagement between my officials and Agency staff who are continuing to monitor aquaculture activities in Lough Foyle.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has assessed aquaculture activity at Foyle Lough to ensure that it is being managed sustainably.
answer_text: There has never been a formal agreement between the UK and Irish Governments on the territorial water boundary in Lough Foyle and this has created difficulties in creating an aquaculture licensing system by the Loughs Agency, in accordance with the North/South Co-operation (Implementation Bodies) (Northern Ireland) Order 1999.  Therefore, aquaculture activity in Lough Foyle is currently unregulated.  While this unregulated activity is inextricably linked to the jurisdictional issue, a reserved matter, which is not within the competence of my Department, or the Northern Ireland Assembly, my Department continues to engage with all stakeholders, including the UK Government, to make progress to enable the regulation of aquaculture in Lough Foyle.  The unregulated activity is regularly monitored by my officials and the Loughs Agency.  In addition, through the cross-border INTERREG SWELL Project, the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute, Loughs Agency, and other partners, have enhanced the ecosystem modelling framework to enable the carrying capacity of Lough Foyle to be assessed for shellfish aquaculture, the native oyster fishery and wider ecosystem interactions.  This will provide an evidence base to support policy decisions for the regulation and sustainable management of aquaculture in Lough Foyle, when an agreement between the UK and Irish Governments has been reached.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will consider the implementation of street lighting along Buncrana Road, near the Coshquin Estate, Foyle, to improve safety in the area.
answer_text: My Department’s policy for the provision of street lighting in rural areas, which was established in 1993, is detailed in RSPPG E072 and can be accessed via the following link. Street lighting RSPPG E072 | Department for Infrastructure (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk)The policy uses two main criteria when considering the provision of street lighting in rural areas:		the density of housing within a community, including public buildings with significant night-time use; and 		road safety, where street lighting would contribute to a reduction in the number of night-time collisions.The policy is subject to continual informal review and ensures that the limited resources available for the provision of street lighting are directed to those areas where they can provide the maximum benefit to the public, particularly where road or infrastructure safety is concerned.It also serves to balance the demand for more rural lighting against the unwelcome effects of increased urbanisation of the countryside and the environmental impact of night sky light pollution on people, wildlife and fauna.At present the section of the Buncrana Road near Coshquin does not meet the criteria under my departmental policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) whether his Department has considered establishing a centralised and publicly-accessible list of all registered and licensed dog breeders; and (ii) to detail any engagement his Department has had with local councils in relation to this matter.
answer_text: There is currently no legal basis to collate and publish this information on a Northern Ireland wide basis.  However, I am aware there may be scope, under the current legislation, for councils to modify the way they record and process licence applications from dog breeders and publish this information on a council-by-council basis.  It is for councils to determine if this is feasible in the first instance.I am mindful that dog breeding continues to be a subject of interest.  I am actively considering a programme of animal welfare reforms for the remainder of this Assembly mandate and will be discussing the matter of dog breeding reform with key stakeholders, including local councils, in the time ahead.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department will take steps to ensure adequate staff cover at the Pilot's Row Community Centre to allow its canteen facilities to remain open over the Christmas period to support vulnerable service users who depend on this service.
answer_text: Pilot’s Row Youth & Community centre is jointly managed through a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) between Derry City & Strabane District Council and the Education Authority (EA), with the EA currently responsible for all staff currently working at the venue, including staff in the community café.  Whilst it is normal for Youth Services to close for a two-week period over Christmas (20 December 2024 – 06 January 2025) the EA is aware that the community café would only close on public and statutory holidays over the same period. Unfortunately, following resignations of existing community café staff there are no canteen staff at the centre.  The EA have already undertaken two unsuccessful recruitment exercises as well as failing to identify staff from within EA’s Catering Team who are willing to cover the canteen at Pilot’s Row.The EA is continuing to seek for an appropriate solution to the issue from both within EA and the local community.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the proposed grant scheme for reservoir managers, including a timeline for implementation.
answer_text: I intend to introduce a grant scheme, for those who need it, to assist reservoir managers to meet their obligations under the Act.    My Department is currently progressing the required business cases and consultation documents and aims to issue the consultation relating to the grant scheme by the end of March.  It should be noted however that the grant cannot be established until such times as the legislation giving the Department to powers to establish the grant scheme is in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has explored the potential for a long-term winter fuel support scheme for vulnerable households, including pensioners affected by the Winter Fuel Payment cuts.
answer_text: My department is currently developing a Fuel Poverty Strategy that will provide a long-term framework for reducing fuel poverty, recognising that this will have positive impacts on both mental and physical health, especially for the most vulnerable in society. The draft strategy will propose an ambitious vision that “a warm, healthy home for everyone” underpinned by guiding principles of long-term sustainable solutions, a needs-based approach and collaborative and participative working.  It is anticipated that a public consultation on the Fuel Poverty Strategy will be launched in December 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 17987/22-27, whether he can clarify the basis for the 1,400 figure referenced in the October Monitoring Round announcement.
answer_text: The figure of up to 1,400 referenced in the October Monitoring Round was contained in a Department of Finance statement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of times he has (i) met with; and (ii) written to the Secretary of State for the Department of Work and Pensions in relation to the cuts to the Winter Fuel Payment.
answer_text: I, along with all Executive Ministers, wrote to the Prime Minister, Sir Keir Starmer, to convey our collective concern at the decision by the Labour Party (sister party of the SDLP) to change the Winter Fuel Payment Scheme.I raised the matter of the winter fuel payment scheme changes during my video conference call meeting with the Secretary of State for Work and Pensions, Liz Kendall MP, on 13 August.I also raised this matter directly with Sir Stephen Timms MP, Minister of State for Social Security and Disability, when I met with him on 3 September.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will introduce a men's health policy similar to the Healthy Ireland - National Men's Health Action Plan.
answer_text: Improving the health of both men and women is a ministerial priority for my Department. Evidence suggests that men are disproportionately affected by certain conditions, such as ischaemic heart disease, are more likely to smoke or engage in other health harming behaviours and are more likely to die by suicide.There is no plan to develop a Northern Ireland men’s health strategy or action plan with existing resource constraints, however, the Department ensures that, in the development of health-promoting strategies and plans, relevant actions targeting men are included where there is a difference in behaviours or outcomes. A wide range of programmes and initiatives are also in place in the statutory and voluntary/community sectors which address specific health risks for men.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) how the 1,400 new social home figure, referenced in the October Monitoring Round announcement, was determined; and (ii) which Department calculated this figure.
answer_text: The figure of 1,400 new social home starts referred to reflects discussions that took place between DoF and DfC officials as part of the October Monitoring process. It was always clear that this is an assessment of the final total that could potentially be achieved given the new allocation in October Monitoring.The actual figure of new social home starts will not be available until the year has finished and relies on the delivery of projects on the ground, including land purchases, planning and construction which may be subject to delays or cost increases.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department has explored the possibility of introducing an expanded Warm Homes Discount Scheme for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Thank you for your correspondence dated 20 November 2024 asking if my Department has explored the possibility of introducing an expanded Warm Homes Discount Scheme for the North of Ireland.You will be aware of the British Warm Home Discount scheme, which is paid for by a charge levied on all domestic customers in Britain.  The British and North of Ireland Energy markets differ in several ways, most notably size.  The costs of implementing and administering a scheme in the North of Ireland equivalent to the British Scheme would be disproportionately high given our small market size and would lead to higher bills for many customers, including, potentially, those in, or close to, fuel poverty.  This would also introduce additional administrative costs for suppliers which could have unintended consequences for supplier tariffs and competition at retail level. We do already have the Northern Ireland Sustainable Energy Programme (NISEP), which receives £8m of funding raised through electricity charges.  In September 2024 I announced a further £2.5m funding package for NISEP which will allow energy efficiency measures to be installed in more low-income households.You will be aware that the Department for Communities has lead responsibility for fuel poverty and any mitigations which may be put in place to assist with rising fuel costs. The Department for the Economy assists in tackling fuel poverty through setting energy efficiency policy that supports the reduction in household energy use and as a consequence household energy bills.My officials are in regular dialogue with their colleagues in the Department for Communities to identify further interventions that may assist customers facing increases in energy costs.  The Department for Communities will lead the Executive’s response, particularly in relation to vulnerable consumers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will launch an independent review of NI Water's current funding and governance arrangements, as recommended by the Northern Ireland Audit Office.
answer_text: I have noted the Comptroller & Auditor General’s report on the funding of water infrastructure that was published earlier this year. The  Audit Office encouraged my Department and NI Water to complete a comprehensive review of alternative funding and governance arrangements, led by suitably qualified experts.My Department has previously carried out considerable work on the funding and governance options for NI Water, and officials continue to engage with the company within my policy framework.  Alongside this work, I am progressing a three-pronged approach in (i) continuing to work with Executive colleagues to try and increase wastewater investment; (ii) reviewing the policy and legislation around developer contributions; and (iii) in securing Executive agreement for the Water, Flooding and Sustainable Drainage Bill to be introduced to the Assembly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 15800/22-27, to detail the research his Department has conducted on the possible mutualisation of NI Water in the last 12 months.
answer_text: In 2019/ 2020 the Department formed a working group with NI Water, the Utility Regulator, DoF, SIB and DfI Finance, to undertake research and analysis into different funding/revenue sources and governance models for NI Water.  This work concluded that to achieve mutualisation would require charges to be paid by domestic water customers. This work still remains relevant.  As already set out in a Private Members’ Motion in the Assembly in April, I will not introduce domestic water charges and therefore have no plans to mutualise NI Water or carry out any further research.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 8864/22-27 (i) whether he has had an opportunity to consider possible changes to the Social Sector Size Criteria mitigation, including the tapering of this benefit based on household income; and (ii) for an update on his Department's report on existing mitigations schemes.
answer_text: My Department has a statutory obligation to produce a report on the operation of the existing Welfare Supplementary Payment schemes before 31 March 2025.  This report will include proposals for the future delivery of the welfare mitigation schemes, including the Social Sector Size Criteria.I am committed to laying this report before the Assembly by the end of 2024, this will allow time for the introduction of any legislation that may be required ahead of 31 March 2025.It will be for the Executive to consider and agree any future mitigation package to ensure that the limited resources available are used in the most effective way possible to provide support to those in need.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether a winter fuel support scheme will be established within the November 2024 to January 2025 period, to coincide with the Winter Fuel Payment time frame.
answer_text: I refer to my statement in the Assembly on 19 November 2024 in relation to a one-off fuel payment to eligible households.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the proportion of applicants on the social housing waiting list identified as having (i) mental health needs; and (ii) complex needs, broken down by (a) sex; and (b) local council area.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 18371/22-27, the information has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether consideration will be given to expanding the Dental Access Scheme to include a practice in Derry, given the significant need for services for unregistered patients in the area.
answer_text: A Northern Ireland-wide Dental Access Scheme (DAS) commenced on 1st August 2024.  This new scheme aims to improve access (during the working week) for unregistered patients experiencing an emergency, urgent, or pressing dental condition.  Practices were selected primarily on the basis of capacity to treat additional unregistered patients without displacement of normal GDS work.There are currently 29 practices participating in the scheme.  Details of the scheme (including participating practices) can be viewed at:  https://online.hscni.net/dental-access-scheme/ There are currently 2 practices who are delivering the DAS in the Western area, these are: Catherine Street Dental Care, LimavadyStrabane Family Dental Care, StrabaneEarly indications are that the scheme is helping to address the dental needs of unregistered patients

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will work with his Department's Universal Credit team to delay sending migration letters to ensure legacy benefit claimants are not required to transition to Universal Credit over the Christmas period.
answer_text: Anyone that receives a migration notice will have 3 months to make their claim to Universal Credit (UC).  My Department took the decision earlier in the year not to issue migration notices which would result in a deadline date over the Christmas period. Further details on all of the help and support available can be found at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/campaigns/move-universal-credit.Staff are also available to provide support on our dedicated Move to UC phone line, or face to face in any of our network of 35 Jobs and Benefits offices.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 17085/22-27, to provide information on where the live piloting of this solution will take place, or if this will happen on a region-wide basis.
answer_text: A digital solution to allow contractors to use a mobile device to locate and update cleaning action on gully assets is planned to commence subject to successful testing, and the award of the new Environmental Maintenance Contracts.  A contractor and area to undertake an initial pilot of the new software will be agreed after the successful candidates for the contracts are in place, and training is provided, early in the new year.  Wider implementation of the solution to other areas will then be rolled out taking onboard any lessons learnt from the pilot together with any changes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 13758/22-27, for an update on his meeting with the Minister of Health in relation to securing a more cost effective travel solution for patients.
answer_text: I recently met with Minister Nesbitt and Minister Muir on 9 September 2024 to explore what options are there to best meet the needs of users and the potential for community transport to support the work and objectives of other departments.  My officials are continuing to engage with colleagues in DoH and DAERA to explore potential.  This work will consider the needs of all stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 17008/22-27,  to detail the relevant checks and accreditation required by the Housing Executive for premises to operate as short-term accommodation.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive avails of several different temporary accommodation options. In the acquisition of Single Lets the Housing Executive will inspect the property and ensure that the following criteria is met: Proof of ownership and Public Liability InsuranceElectric meter – meter card typeNICEIC certificate for wired smoke alarms Gas Safe or Oil Boiler test certificate and carbon monoxide alarmsConfirmation that provider is registered on the Landlord Registration scheme and with Land & Property ServicesProvision of fire blanket or extinguisher in the kitchenIf any of the above criteria is not met, or the inspection at the point of acquisition is unsatisfactory, the Housing Executive will not use the property. For placements in non-standard accommodation (B&B/Hotels), the Housing Executive secures the accommodation in the same manner as any other customer group, whereby the respective Hotel/B&B is subject to the relevant checks and accreditation via Tourism NI to operate as a short-term accommodation. The Housing Executive is not responsible for such inspections. The Housing Executive also avails of a range of temporary accommodation options provided by the voluntary sector. These services are funded and managed by Supporting People to ensure quality support is delivered and that the temporary accommodation is appropriate for households who are homeless or threatened with homelessness. As part of the Strategic Action Plan for Temporary Accommodation the Housing Executive intends to commission work to agree a temporary accommodation standards framework for Northern Ireland. This should include physical standards, suitability standards and service standards for all types of temporary accommodation.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 16704/22-27, for an update on funding for the Affordable Warmth Scheme.
answer_text: I allocated an opening budget of £7.175m for the Affordable Warmth Scheme to cover £5.2m of approvals carried forward and £1.975m for new approvals.  As part of the October Monitoring Round, I allocated an additional £0.7m to the Affordable Warmth Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how much of the October Monitoring Round allocation to his Department will be directed towards road maintenance.
answer_text: Almost £19m of the additional funding received at October Monitoring will be used towards essential and structural road maintenance and street lighting work. This includes £2m for Street lighting, £4.2m for critical essential maintenance and £12.5m for investment in structural maintenance to progress existing and new schemes. In addition, I have also funded a £1m targeted intervention for gully emptying given the potential impact of severe weather and resultant risk of flooding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any assessment that has been made on the potential impact of staff shortages within the Water and Departmental Delivery Group, particularly in relation to wastewater management and potential sewage overspills.
answer_text: Wastewater management and sewage overspills are operational matters for NI Water, whereas  the Water and Departmental Delivery Group’s focus regarding NI Water is primarily policy related. Therefore, the current vacancies within this group would have no direct impact on operational matters such as wastewater management and sewage overspills.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com. Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps he is taking to restore the diversity of Northern Ireland’s habitats and species.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the implementation of the Right Care, Right Person model, specifically in relation to individuals in mental health crisis attending emergency departments accompanied by police officers in the absence of approved social workers; and (ii) to detail any discussions that have taken place with the Minister of Justice on this matter.
answer_text: Implementation of the Right Care Right Person model in Northern Ireland has not yet commenced.   I am aware that currently police officers often accompany individuals in mental health crisis to emergency departments and can spend prolonged periods of time there.  This is an issue that Right Care Right Person will aim to resolve. In September 2024 the Minister of Justice agreed to the establishment of the RCRP Strategic Oversight Group chaired jointly by DoH and DoJ to ensure that the approach to RCRP implementation is planned and developed through cross-agency partnerships.  The focus of the Oversight Group is to identify and address the required operational change, while recognising and respecting the operational independence of PSNI, and ensuring effective future partnership working between all relevant statutory partners in the delivery of health and social care, including mental health care.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 17293/22-27, (i) whether this number includes both with technical arrears and without technical arrears; and (ii) to detail the total estimated costs of these rental arrears, as of the end of September 2024.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 18216/22-27, the information has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department is coordinating with the Department for Work and Pensions to ensure legacy benefit claimants migrating to Universal Credit are not disadvantaged by overlapping or benefit payment run-ons.
answer_text: My officials continue to work closely with counterparts in DWP to ensure the smooth transition of legacy benefit claimants to Universal Credit. Anyone who moves directly from Housing Benefit, income-based Jobseeker’s Allowance, Income Support or income-related Employment and Support Allowance onto Universal Credit will be entitled to an additional two-week payment of their legacy benefit, known as a run-on.The run-on payment is disregarded as income for the purposes of entitlement to Universal Credit and the claimant will not be asked to refund it.  The payment does not impact entitlement to transitional protection for claimants migrating to Universal Credit.This run-on of the payment of the legacy benefit and Universal Credit serves to mitigate for the different payment schedules between legacy benefits (fortnightly) and UC (monthly) and is aimed at ensuring that claimants are not negatively impacted by the migration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide clarity on the decision to reconstitute the Living With Water Programme Board.
answer_text: As previously reported to Executive colleagues and the Committee for Infrastructure, due to the increase in the estimated costs to deliver the Living With Water in Belfast Plan, my Department carried out a review of the affordability of the Plan. The recommendations from the review, which I have accepted, are that the Belfast Plan still needs to be delivered but will take longer than the 12 years originally estimated.Given both the scale of the increased costs and the level of budget available, delivery of the Belfast Plan schemes will therefore no longer be taken forward through a formal programme but instead by individual partners as normal business and delivered at a scale and pace achievable within available budgets.  The governance structure will be aligned to the new delivery approach with the Living With Water Programme Board replaced by a stakeholder oversight group to monitor delivery of the Belfast Plan. I remain committed to achieving the objectives of the Belfast Plan and collaborative working between my Department and partners to develop and implement integrated drainage solutions including blue / green infrastructure, will continue.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department consulted with the Housing Executive before setting the target of 1,400 new social homes, as referenced in the October Monitoring Round statement.
answer_text: My department has not yet set a target for new social homes based on the recently announced increased allocation.The target will be set by my department following engagement with the Housing Executive. As the commissioning body for the Social Housing Development Programme, the Housing Executive will assess how the additional budget secured in October monitoring round may best be used to meet identified housing need before any final starts target in 2024/25 can be confirmed. I am advised that work on this is underway.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an update on the Intermediate Rent Scheme; and (ii) to detail the total number of successful applications, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: The Intermediate Rent scheme will, in this initial phase, award funding to a single Intermediate Rent Operator.The competition for funding assessment process has identified a highest scoring applicant, and initial due diligence is underway. I expect to announce the outcome in due course. The first Intermediate Rent homes, under this scheme, are expected to be available from 2026, and the Operator will invite applications from eligible tenants as properties come available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he can provide (i) an estimate of the amount of publicly-owned land across Northern Ireland; and (ii) a breakdown of the main agencies or departments that hold ownership.
answer_text: My Department does not have responsibility for recording publicly owned land across all departments, I can, however, provide details of land and property owned by my Department.Across my department, land and property are retained for operational purposes and planned road schemes.   The Department has a dedicated Property Centre, which is responsible for managing land and processing the disposal of all such land once it has become surplus to my Department’s requirements.
