question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 16562/22-27, when changes to the Housing Selection Scheme to allow the allocation of intimidation points for victims of domestic and gender-based violence will be implemented.
answer_text: My officials are working alongside the Housing Executive to consider independently commissioned research on intimidation points. This will be used to inform any changes I may make to the Housing Selection Scheme. I expect to make a decision on this matter soon.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide a time frame for the publication of the Housing Supply Strategy.
answer_text: The Housing Supply Strategy received Executive approval on the 5th of December 2024 and was published thereafter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the National Insurance increases for employers and its impact on the homelessness sector.
answer_text: The decision by the Labour Government to increase National Insurance Contributions will have a significant impact for all employers in organisations with 20+ employees. This increase will be challenging for most organisations across the voluntary and community sector, including those in the homeless sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when the Independent Review of Rates Support Grant will be published.
answer_text: It is anticipated that the report on the Review of the Rates Support Grant will be received from Business Consultancy Services in early December 2024.I will consider publication in due course once I have reviewed the report and its recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department will introduce a fit and proper persons requirement for providers of standard and non-standard temporary accommodation, similar to the fitness of premises standards applied to temporary accommodation.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive does not currently plan to introduce any further measures.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a breakdown of how the October Monitoring Round allocation of £39.6 million to his Department will be spent.
answer_text: The £39.6 million allocated to my Department through the October Monitoring Round has provided additional funding towards the structural maintenance of our roads and the street lighting network. NI Water is also receiving an extra £19.5m in capital funding for targeted action to release wastewater constraints, which is almost half of the total capital allocation received. Translink will also receive an additional capital allocation of £5m.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for a breakdown of the planned expenditure for the £17 million allocated to Winter Fuel Support.
answer_text: I refer the member to my statement on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to provide clarity on the Resource to Capital DEL switch for the NI Road Safety Partnership.
answer_text: The Resource to Capital DEL reclassification of £30,000 for the NI Road Safety Partnership (NIRSP), relates to NIRSP resource fee income which is being used to fund a NIRSP capital pressure, as the scheme is designed to be self-funding.The relevant governing provision is contained in Section 89 (2) of the Police, Crime, Sentencing and Courts Act 2022 (“the 2022 Act”) which was commenced on 26th October 2022. As a result, Part 4B of the Road Traffic Offenders (NI) Order 1996 (“the 1996 Order”) came into force. Under Article 91G (1) of Part 4B, the Chief Constable may charge a fee for enrolment on an approved course offered as an alternative to prosecution for a fixed penalty offence. In accordance with subsection (2), any excess in fees must be used for the purpose of promoting road safety.The reclassification request was submitted by the Department of Justice to the Department of Finance as part of October Monitoring and was approved by the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will consider introducing tobacco-style warning labels to help reduce the rate of alcohol consumption and abuse.
answer_text: Warning labels are not an identified action within the substance use strategy, Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery. The World Health Organisation, and other experts, have noted that pricing policies are among the most effective and cost-effective alcohol control measures. My current focus is therefore on addressing the harmful impacts and health inequalities caused by alcohol use through minimum unit pricing (MUP). Subject to Executive agreement, it is my intention to bring legislation to the Assembly on this matter within the current mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) how the target of 1,400 new social homes was determined; (ii) any engagement with the Housing Executive prior to setting this figure; and (iii) whether potential costs for water and wastewater connections have been considered as part of this calculation.
answer_text: My department has not yet set a target for new social homes based on the recently announced increased allocation.The target will be set by my department following engagement with the Housing Executive. As the commissioning body for the Social Housing Development Programme, the Housing Executive will assess how the additional budget secured in October monitoring round may best be used to meet identified housing need before any final starts target in 2024/25 can be confirmed. I am advised that work on this is underway.Costs that Registered Housing Associations can claim for are clearly defined in the Housing Association Guide. Any infrastructure costs (including sewers, connections or sustainable drainage systems (SUDs) which are within the boundaries of the Social Housing development will be deemed as qualifying scheme costs and therefore eligible for grant.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps his Department has taken to address delays experienced by agency health staff who have not received their backdated pay award for 2023-24, including the one-off non-consolidated payment of £1,500, particularly those in the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The relevant pay awards for 2023/24 for staff employed under Agenda for Change terms and conditions have already been paid to staff directly employed within the HSC. For workers supplied by an agency, it remains the responsibility of that agency to pay the appropriate rates of pay, including any arrears, and to then invoice the Business Services Organisation for the correct amount.For such individuals, issues relating to pay, pay rises, etc., are for their employer to consider. Any queries on payments that agency staff have should therefore be addressed to the employing agency directly.The additional non-consolidated pay award of up to £1,505, which formed part of the 2023/24 HSCNI Agenda for Change pay award, applies solely to directly employed HSC staff for whom the Department has direct responsibility for rates of pay. This reflects the position in NHS England, whereby the additional elements negotiated with trades unions were applicable only to those directly employed within the service. This was a negotiated supplementary settlement, not a recognition payment.It remains at the discretion of an employing agency if they wish to replicate any non-consolidated elements within the payments due to them under their contract framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 15318/22-27, to detail the steps being taken to enhance accessibility for all passengers, including training for taxi drivers to assist (i) passengers with dementia; (ii) deaf passengers; and (iii) visually impaired passengers with guide dogs.
answer_text: Taxi Driver Periodic Training (TDPT) is a mandatory training requirement for taxi drivers here, introduced in 2014 through powers in the Taxis Act (Northern Ireland) 2008 and the Taxi Drivers’ Licences Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2014.  The purpose of TDPT is to raise the standards of the taxi industry and to develop the current skills of taxi drivers.  Its benefits include improvements to road safety, a better understanding of the environmentally friendly driving techniques and to complement and enhance a taxi driver’s professionalism.All taxi drivers are required to complete 35 hours of TDPT during the validity of their five-year taxi driver licence, before they can renew their taxi driver licence.  The training must be delivered by a training provider authorised by the Department for this purpose, and at least 3.5 hours of TDPT must be Disability Awareness training.  The Driver and Vehicle Agency has developed a “Disability Equality Training Course” and a “Making Your Taxi Service Inclusive Course” as resources for training providers who deliver Disability Awareness training.   Their scope includes a range of topics related to understanding disability and the law and providing an inclusive and accessible service to people with disabilities.  The resources also suggest discussion points which can be supplemented by training providers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) to detail any engagement she has had with the Minister for Communities in relation to restoring the Rates Support Grant; and (ii) for her assessment of ring-fencing this funding.
answer_text: The Rates Support Grant provides financial support, on an annual basis, to those councils whose needs exceed their wealth relative to other councils in NI. The Rates Support Grant is a statutory grant; however, the level of funding is not set in legislation.As a result, allocations to this grant are the responsibility of the Minister for Communities and he must decide what can be afforded from his department’s budget and if the allocation can be ring-fenced.I understand the Department for Communities is also currently independently reviewing the Rates Support Grant, and it is hoped that this will assist the Department to understand how this grant funding is used and/or considered by Councils within their overall funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will consider exploring a park with us in mind style initiative across local councils areas, including Derry City and Strabane District Council, to address pavement parking.
answer_text: My Department will consider, as capacity allows, any request made by the local councils seeking the support of parking enforcement with any initiative similar to the ‘park with us in mind’ campaign led by Belfast City Council in collaboration with my Department and the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the engagement he has had with the taxi industry in relation to improving passenger safety, especially for women in light of growing safety concerns.
answer_text: I have recently met with the taxi industry to understand how we can best meet the needs of passengers, drivers and operators. Improving passenger safety,  current taxi legislation provides for the safety of all passengers. Everyone providing a taxi service in the North must either be the holder of a taxi operator’s licence or work as an affiliated driver for a licensed operator.  In addition, a taxi operator must only use properly licensed drivers and vehicles and must retain booking records which identifies the specific driver and vehicle dispatched to pick up passengers for each booking.  These are statutory requirements set out in the Taxis Act (NI) 2008.Licensed taxis that are permitted to pick up customers from the street, without a pre-booking must display a roof sign with taxi plates showing their licence number.  In addition, a licensed taxi driver must wear an ID badge showing their photograph and badge number.  Customers should satisfy themselves that they are travelling in a bona fide taxi and in the event of concerns regarding the taxi service capture as much information as possible to support any investigation that may ensue.Legislation also requires my Department to be satisfied that an applicant for a taxi driver’s licence is a ‘fit and proper person to hold a taxi driver’s licence’ before a licence will be granted. The same standard applies during the currency of a licence and my Department has the powers to suspend or revoke a licence if required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the steps his Department is taking to ensure that income used to calculate Rate Rebate as part of the Universal Credit application excludes Holiday Pay and Pay in Lieu of Notice.
answer_text: Rate Rebate is not part of Universal Credit (UC). It is a tax relief for domestic ratepayers. An applicant’s earnings for a Rate Rebate assessment flow from their UC entitlement. Details of holiday pay and pay in lieu of notice are not differentiated in the UC information currently provided in the data transferred to my Department, and therefore this information must come from claimants directly. Currently, the Rate Rebate award notification advises the applicant that their award has not removed any holiday pay and pay in lieu of notice that may have been included in their UC assessed earnings. The notification offers opportunity for reconsideration of the award. As part of the reconsideration process, applicants can provide evidence such as payslips or employers letters via their online Rate Rebate account. There are opportunities for continuous improvement in this evolving support. Following recent engagement with representative organisations, LPS are undertaking feasibility research to redesign the online application form. This is to enable an applicant to provide supporting evidence prior to an assessment being made. These changes require updates to the online application, as well as the core IT system and my officials are actively working with IT suppliers to bring about that change as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he has engaged with NI Water in relation to the possibility of borrowing capital, based on revenue generated through non-domestic charges.
answer_text: NI Water receives the majority of its revenue from the public purse and, due to the level of public money provided to the company, it is subsequently classified as Non Departmental Public Body (NDPB) in Central Government in regard to Public Expenditure accounting and budgeting treatment. Revenue received from non-domestic customers forms part of NI Water’s resource DEL budget requirement. As an NDPB, all of NI Water’s expenditure, including expenditure of the income from non-domestic customers, must be within the overall resource DEL and capital DEL budget allocations. In terms of borrowing, my Department provides NI Water with a loan facility to finance the company’s capital investment programme and all expenditure of debt finance also scores in NI Water’s capital DEL budget. Therefore, given NI Water’s status in Central Government, external borrowing from commercial lenders would not represent additional capital budget for the company. Additionally, securing external finance will likely result in an unfavourable consequence for the public purse in terms of an increased cost of debt, additional risks in terms of liabilities and guarantees, and longer-term uncertainty in relation to servicing and redemption of the debt.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide an update on efforts to ensure timely street lighting repairs.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on progress in establishing an Infrastructure Commission to advise on major capital projects, following the creation of a cross-departmental working group in early 2022.
answer_text: An inter-departmental working group was established in 2021 to bring forward advice on the options for an Infrastructure Commission.The working group produced a draft paper in early 2022 which set out initial options for consideration, as a precursor to more detailed analysis and development of a business case. However, the paper had not been cleared by previous Ministers before the end of the term.Officials are reviewing the options to take account of developments in the interim, including: the NIAO follow up report on major capital programmes and the subsequent PAC hearing; the SIB report on root causes of delay; progress with the Investment Strategy; and the work of the ISNI Committee of the NICS Board.We are currently considering the report from the recent SIB Review. The recommendations from the review will also inform a revised paper on options for an Infrastructure Commission.Decisions on the way forward will be a matter for the Executive in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps his Department is taking (i) to ensure Capita publish publicly-accessible Condition Insight Reports; and (ii) to facilitate access to these publications on the new Health Assessment Advisory Service website.
answer_text: On 8 October 2024, my department requested, through the contract change control process, that Capita publish the Condition Insight Reports used by their Healthcare Professionals in completing functional assessments for Personal Independence Payment (PIP). My officials are currently working through the detail of this request with Capita, and it is expected to be completed by early 2025.It is intended that PIP Condition Insight Reports will be published on Capita’s Health Assessment Advisory Service website.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in relation to the pilot scheme to permit Class A and Class C taxis to use bus lanes in Belfast, to detail any consideration that has been given to the impact on cyclists.
answer_text: The proposal to permit Class A and Class C taxis to use certain bus lanes in the vicinity of the new Belfast Grand Central Station will be undertaken as an Experimental Traffic Control Scheme so that the benefits of the new arrangements can be assessed and this will help inform future policy decisions in this area During the scheme we intend to seek the views of all users, including cyclists, on how the arrangement is working.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the Chief Constable's ambition to bring police officer numbers to 7,000 within the next three years.
To ask the Minister for Communities whether a date has been set for the migration of Employment and Support Allowance claimants to Universal Credit.
answer_text: My Department’s current priority is the migration of those households in receipt of Tax Credits to Universal Credit by April 2025.Announcements on the Department’s plans for its completion of Move to UC for those remaining in receipt of income-based legacy benefits, including Employment and Support Allowance, will be made in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether consideration has been given to establishing a dedicated Housing Ombudsman for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland, the Housing Executive and all Registered Housing Associations have complaints procedures which should be followed in the first instance.  Should a customer remain unhappy with the outcome of their complaint following completion of the internal investigation, the customer can refer their complaint to Northern Ireland Public Services Ombudsman.The responsibility of establishing a dedicated Housing Ombudsman in Northern Ireland would rest with the Executive, however this is not currently being considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will consider introducing legislation to regulate private parking firms operating in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Whilst my Department is responsible for on-street parking enforcement across the north, it has no responsibility for the operation and management of private car parks nor the enforcement companies that operate within them.There is no legislation which specifically applies to privately owned car parks and my Department currently has no plans to introduce legislation in this area. However, any organisation that operates parking on private land and requests keeper details from the DVLA must be approved by an Accredited Trade Association such as the Approved Operator Scheme (AOS) managed by the British Parking Association (BPA). Members of this scheme must adhere to the BPA AOS Code of Practice for the control and enforcement of parking on private land and unregulated public car parks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on (i) the implementation of National Institute for Health and Care Excellence TA 943; and (ii) the integration of advanced continuous glucose monitoring technologies into diabetes care.
answer_text: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence TA943, hybrid closed loop systems for managing blood glucose levels in Type 1 diabetes, has been endorsed as applicable in Northern Ireland. Subsequently, my Department has requested work to be undertaken to understand the implications of this decision.Overall provision; priority groups; system costs; workforce and communication requirements to both enable and support, are being considered. Given the scale of investment needed, financial requirements are also being considered, as funding is required for devices as well as resources to upskill and recruit the workforce needed to offer Hybrid Closed Loop safely, effectively and equitably across the region.Final decision-making and commissioning arrangements for NI will be made, supported by an implementation plan. This will support the integration continuous glucose monitoring technologies into diabetes care and will be communicated to all stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether his Department holds any information on the amount of lead piping currently in use in each local council area; and (ii) to detail the steps being taken to assist property owners with replacing this infrastructure.
answer_text: My Department does not hold any information on the amount of lead piping in use in each council area.  The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water.  I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised that NI Water is responsible for the portion of the service pipe from the watermain to the house/property boundary with the remainder of the pipe, within private property, being the responsibility of the property owner.  However, NI Water provides advice and guidance for customers to check for lead pipework within their home if it was built before 1970 and currently has a 25-year programme to identify and replace lead pipes within its infrastructure. If NI Water takes a water sample from a property and the sample fails, the lead standard then NI Water informs the customer and replaces the pipe up to the property boundary.  If a customer informs NI Water that they are replacing the lead supply pipe in their property, then NI Water will also actively replace the service pipe up to the property boundary.  In addition, through its Water Mains Rehabilitation Programme, NI Water has focused on removing all lead pipes from the public supply system. As part of this programme, customers are informed when lead pipes to the property boundary have been replaced and are encouraged to replace any lead supply pipes within their private property. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline how the £270 million allocation, announced for infrastructure within the Autumn Budget 2024, will be spent.
answer_text: The £270 million capital announced within the Autumn Budget 2024 refers to the Barnett-based funding allocation provide by the  Government to the Northern Ireland Executive.  Barnett consequentials are unhypothecated and it is for the Executive to decide how these are used as part of the 2025-26 budget setting process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) whether his Department has raised the need for changes to the Universal Credit application process that would allow vulnerable claimants, specifically Care Leavers, to apply before turning 18 years of age to circumvent the five-week wait for payment; and (ii) to backdate claims.
answer_text: Care leavers can prepare their claim to Universal Credit up to 28 days before their 18th birthday, however, the claim must not be submitted until their 18th birthday. This helps to ensure that support is in place as early as possible. The Universal Credit Contingency Fund is available to eligible claimants while they await their first payment. This is a non-repayable grant which is only available in Northern Ireland.An advance payment of up to 100% of Universal Credit entitlement is also available should the claimant require further financial help during this period. The claimant is required to repay this advance through deductions from their Universal Credit payments.  Advances must usually be paid back within 24 months, although this can be delayed in certain circumstances.A Universal Credit claim can be backdated in limited circumstances to protect the most vulnerable claimants who may be delayed in claiming through no fault of their own, such as an illness or disability which prevented them making an earlier claim. The maximum period a claim can be backdated is one calendar month from the date a claim is made.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the funding of the Housing Executive's out of hours homelessness prevention services.
answer_text: Following the October Monitoring Round, I am providing another £6.7m for the Housing Executive to prevent the risk of homelessness service closures and ensure that its statutory obligations can continue to be met.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether providers of temporary and emergency accommodation and their staff are required to carry out Access NI checks.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive uses a range of temporary accommodation options in order to meet the statutory duties outlined in the Housing (NI) Order 1988. While there is no requirement for Access NI checks to be carried out by temporary accommodation providers, those funded through the Supporting People (SP) programme must provide an assurance that Access NI checks are undertaken. This is part of the SP accreditation process.” For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when payment of the uplift for health service staff, which was due from April 2024, will be paid.
answer_text: The NHS Pay Review Body in its 37th report, has called for a consolidated 5.5% pay increase with effect from 1 April 2024 for all Agenda for Change staff, including those in Northern Ireland. I am already on record as being supportive, in principle, of the recommendations set out in the report. However, given the severe financial constraints placed upon my Department, it is not possible, at the current time, to make a final decision on the acceptance of the review body recommendations for this year.As I have stated publicly, our health service would be nothing without our hard-working workforce and I am committed to doing everything I can do to support them.  I will continue to make the case, to Executive colleagues, for additional financial allocations that would allow me to implement a pay award in line with the recommendations of the NHSPRB for 24/25.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 17188/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 17188/22-27 was answered on 29 November 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, following the recent attacks on women in Derry over the last week, whether additional measures are being considered to protect women and girls.
answer_text: I wholeheartedly condemn the shocking attacks of women in Londonderry/Derry in recent weeks.I am clear that women in Northern Ireland should not be in fear of attack, and that there needs to be a collective effort from across government and all of society to address these issues. Some additional measures have already been put in place.I understand that the Policing and Community partnerships (PCSPs) in Derry City & Strabane District Council, through their Community Safety Warden scheme, has provided support to community groups including delivering personal safety talks and has provided a range of personal safety equipment such as personal alarms and high visibility equipment, where requested.  Community patrols by the PCSP Community Safety Wardens Scheme have been increased throughout the city and district parks, both at nighttime and during daylight hours in response to the current attacks. The PCSP Community Safety Wardens also attended the rally for Women’s Safety which took place at the Guildhall Square on Friday 7 November 2024, where they engaged with attendees to provide information on how to access their services and to provide some personal alarms to women.   I understand that PSNI responded quickly to both incidents. I also understand that PSNI have increased high visibility patrols and police presence in the area to reassure women in the community.My Department also has a clear role to play in tackling violence against women and girls. I introduced a significant body of legislation in the last mandate around domestic abuse, serious sexual offences and stalking, all of which are helping to make women and girls safer. My Department is building on this progress and there is now further ongoing work underway to reduce risk and to contribute to an end to violence against women and girls.  This includes work under the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy, which is led by my Department and the Department of Health, and which was launched by the Health Minister and myself on 25th September. My Department and its partners are taking forward work against five priority areas under this strategy, focused on:Partnership - how we collaborate across government and wider organisations;Prevention - through early intervention and challenging harmful behaviours’;Children & Young people - so we can prevent victimisation and support affected youth;Support - how we can best provide tailored services for victim recovery; and Justice - so that we can ensure swift accountability for abusers and support victims through criminal justice processes.There are strong interdependencies between this Strategy and the Strategic Framework on Ending Violence Against Women and Girls, given that women and girls are disproportionately affected by domestic and sexual abuse.  Therefore the work that we are progressing under this strategy  domestic and sexual will also directly contribute to the broader goals of ending violence against women and girls. However, ending vio

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he would consider delaying the partial closure of the A5 on 9th and 10th November 2024, in anticipation of the significant increase in road usage of the A5, with people travelling from Derry to Dublin for the Football Association of Ireland Cup Final.
answer_text: A £260,000 road improvement scheme commenced on Tuesday 5 November 2024 as part of a £1 million package of measures to improve safety on the existing A5 Victoria Road, Strabane.  As part of the implementation of this improvement scheme, a full road closure was planned for Saturday 9th and Sunday 10th November between 8am and 6pm.However, in response to concerns raised, and the potential for a large volume of Derry City FC supporters travelling to Dublin on Sunday morning, my Department has engaged with the contractor and arranged to deploy additional resources to substantially complete the scheme by Saturday evening.  The road will be open to traffic on Sunday.Further work may be required on the following Saturday to complete any outstanding works on the scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) how much blue bin recycling can be processed; and (ii) how much of the blue bin recycling is redirected back to general waste, in each of the last five years, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: In both the answers below the phrase blue bin recycling has been interpreted as referring to local authority collected municipal waste (LACMW) which is collected at the kerbside and consists of co-mingled or mixed non-compostable waste streams primarily intended to be sent for recycling. These are also sometimes known as LAC kerbside Mixed Dry Recyclables (MDR). My department does not hold this information.In this answer the phrase ‘general waste’ has been interpreted as meaning LAC kerbside residual waste. department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department is considering additional measures to tackle holiday hunger over the Christmas period, given access to foodbanks is limited during this time.
answer_text: My Department currently has no interventions directly targeting the issue of Holiday Hunger however support is available on an ongoing basis for households struggling with the cost of living. My Department’s Discretionary Support service is unique to Northern Ireland and provides a fast and responsive means of delivering short term financial support.  An award may be made via an interest free loan or non-repayable grant to assist with day to day living expenses such as food costs or essential household items if a person is experiencing financial difficulties, provided they meet the eligibility criteria. More information about the financial support available through the Discretionary Support scheme can be found at www.nidirect.gov.uk/campaigns/finance-support.Information on further help and support can also be found at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/campaigns/cost-livingThe Department also supports households facing food insecurity through a Social Supermarket programme for which funding was increased to £2.75m for 2024/25 in recognition of the increased levels of need in our communities. I have also recently announced £1.3m for an Integrated Advice Partnership Fund which will promote a community-focused approach to debt advice provision so we can ensure that those facing financial difficulties will be able to access the support they need.Holiday hunger is part of the multi-faceted issue of poverty and I remain committed to developing effective and, just as importantly, sustainable solutions to tackle poverty in all its forms for our communities right across Northern Ireland, through the implementation of a robust Anti-poverty Strategy. I intend this work to be taken forward at pace, presenting a paper to the Executive early in 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 10719/22-27, (i) for an update on the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority Interim Inquiry Policy; and (ii) to detail the number of cases that have been referred to this pilot to date.
answer_text: (i) the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA) policy for reviewing the care and treatment of people living with a mental disorder remains an interim policy.  RQIA are developing it to provide clarity on how they will fulfil their role to “undertake inquiry” in any setting under the Mental Health Order Article 86.; and(ii) From 1 March 2024 to 31 August 2024, RQIA have reported there have been 100 stage 1 patient inquiries. Stage 1 inquiries may include where a concern about care and treatment is raised with RQIA, relating to a patient living with mental disorder in any setting. RQIA may make contact with the HSC Trust, with a provider organisation or others to establish the nature of the circumstances.  Such preliminary actions may lead on to further steps including seeking written information or holding a meeting with a provider organisation.  In some circumstances these actions will not be sufficient to resolve the matter. The interim policy seeks to clarify the circumstances where a ‘structured inquiry’ is considered necessary to drill into the detail of the care and treatment arrangements. This may be considered a second phase or stage 2 of ‘making inquiry’.  RQIA have advised that to date, two stage 2 inquiries have been completed for one patient, and a stage 2 inquiry is ongoing for 2 patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the establishment of an emergency dental clinic for the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: An Emergency Dental Clinic (EDC) has not yet been established in the Western Health and Social Care Trust area.In 2021, the Region Health and Social Care Board (the predecessor organisation of the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG)) set up a working group to review the existing out of hours (OOH) arrangements and assess future dental OOH requirements for Northern Ireland.  A detailed report was produced and in the Western area the working group recommended that the standing local OOH rotas operated by dental practices should be maintained while plans for a new EDC were developed.SPPG have had several meetings with the Western Local Dental Committee (LDC) and representatives from the Local Commissioning Group (LCG) to seek their views on options for future dental OOH provision. SPPG have now produced an initial scoping plan on the development of a Western area EDC. Work is ongoing to explore the acceptability of this proposal to key stakeholders and the costs involved.At present OOH emergency dental care continues to be available at weekends and bank holidays to patients in the Western Area via the local OOH rotas. Following additional investment from my Department, the Dental Access Scheme (DAS) began on 1 August 2024.  This scheme provides weekday access to General Dental Services for unregistered patients with an urgent or pressing dental need. There are two participating practices in the Western Trust Area and between them they have seen more than 370 patients under the scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on changes to the Homeowner Flood Protection Grant Scheme.
answer_text: My Department’s Homeowner Flood Protection Grant Scheme (HFPGS) remains open to new applications. The grant scheme covers 90% of the costs, up to a maximum of £10,000, of the total survey and estimated installation costs.   You will be aware that I instructed my officials to review the process and I am pleased to confirm that following a review of the eligibility criteria for the scheme, the application process will be significantly reduced for the homeowner. This has taken into account the need for a more considerate approach for homeowners and to address the significant emotional and financial distress they face following a flood event. In addition, properties which are likely to benefit from a government flood alleviation solution in the medium term will not automatically be precluded from the scheme. Furthermore, homeowners must now provide evidence of only one flooding event in the past 25 years, as opposed to the original criteria of two events in 25 years.Whilst the financial contributions as part of the scheme remain the same, it is anticipated that the changes to the eligibility criteria will reduce the application process by up to 6 weeks. These changes are effective now and my officials will be updating the website in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 10618/22-27, for an update on support for cancer patients who experience the late effects of treatment.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on progress relating to the remediation of the Mobuoy site.
answer_text: A draft remediation strategy has been developed, in line with an industry standard process, for the long-term remediation of the Mobuoy site. The next step will be a public consultation. I intend to announce a launch date for this consultation in the coming weeks. Any implementation plan and timetable for remediation remains subject to the outcome of the upcoming public consultation and will be considered in the context of the Department’s responsibility to protect water quality and the extremely challenging constraints of public finances.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will roll-out the Alleyways Transformation Scheme to other local council areas.
answer_text: No Departmental funding was allocated to any Council for greening of alleyways as part of the previous Blue Green Infrastructure Fund. The Blue Green Infrastructure fund ended in 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will consider the establishment of an environmental protection agency.
answer_text: I am keen to bring forward proposals for strengthening environment governance, including options for an Independent Environmental Protection Agency (IEPA) for consideration by the Executive, as I indicated in response to your previous AQW 16626/22-27. I want to explore models that would deliver improved and more independent environmental governance. I have asked officials to take forward some work in that regard and will be announcing next steps shortly.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the establishment of a Regional Obesity Management Service for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: In November last year, my Department launched a public consultation on the proposed introduction of a Regional Obesity Management Service (ROMS), which would provide a means for people living with obesity in NI to access treatment and specialist support which are not currently available to them.  The consultation closed on 1 March 2024, and I am currently considering the analysis of the responses received.  Decisions regarding future development of this service will be based on consideration of the evidence presented, availability of resources, and competing priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline his plans to provide financial support to businesses, in light of current economic challenges and rising operational costs.
answer_text: Question 14 – AQO 1122/22-27I recognise the current challenges faced by businesses, many of which are driven by international factors, and I have set out an ambitious economic plan which will help to improve resilience within the local economy.  Examples of delivery to date include Invest NI introducing interventions to make efficiency savings, address supply chain risks and, depending on business circumstances, provide financial assistance towards supply chain development.  Support also exists to assist companies to upskill and reskill staff, develop new business models and enhance leadership capability.To support delivery of my decarbonisation priority, I have also committed £40m to the Energy and Resource Efficiency Programme over the next five years, providing essential support for businesses to decarbonise and reduce costs, whilst creating economic opportunities in the green economy.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide an update on any progress to establish an Infrastructure Commission, following the creation of a cross-departmental working group in early 2022.
answer_text: An inter-departmental working group was established in 2021 to bring forward advice on the options for an Infrastructure Commission.  The working group produced a draft paper in early 2022 which set out initial options for consideration, as a precursor to more detailed analysis and development of a business case.   Officials are reviewing this assessment to take account of developments, including: the NIAO follow up report on major capital programmes and the subsequent PAC hearing; the SIB report on root causes of delay; progress with the Investment Strategy; and the work of the ISNI Committee of the NICS Board.We are currently considering the report from the recent SIB Review. The recommendations from the review will also inform that work.Decisions on the way forward will be a matter for the Executive in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he has engaged with Translink in relation to the introduction of the Nightmovers service over the Christmas period.
answer_text: My Department is engaging with Translink in relation to the introduction of the Nightmovers service for the forthcoming festive period.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of tooth extractions carried out on patients under the age of 18; and (ii) the number of these extractions that were conducted due to decay, in the last 12 months, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Children, young people and adults receive dental care across a range of settings in Northern Ireland: General Dental Services (GDS), Community Dental Services (CDS), and the Hospital Dental Services (HDS). The majority of children receive all their dental care, including extractions, in the GDS. Extractions in the GDS are carried out under Local Anaesthetic (LA).The CDS in each of the Health and Social Care Trusts in Northern Ireland provide a range of dental services for children and young people with additional needs, this includes extractions under General Anaesthetic (GA).GA is only used in dentistry when other options are not feasible. All dental GAs require justification and the majority of children on GA waiting lists have experienced infection or pain due to decayed teeth.The information requested is outlined below. While the reasons for the extractions will be detailed in the patient’s records, they are not included in the monitoring returns.Service which undertook extractionsExtractions by Trust Area over 12 month periodBelfast Northern South EasternSouthern WesternNI TotalGDS5,0166,8585,5806,2974,50028,269CDS1,5264,0324,9283,1462,38616,018Total6,54210,89010,5089,4436,88644,287NotesThe most recent annual GDS data is for 2023-24 and the most recent CDS data is for 2022-23.There were 18 teeth extracted in the GDS for which it was not possible to determine the Trust area.There were a small number of children and young people aged 13-18 years with highly complex needs in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT) for which the numbers of teeth extracted were not included in the monitoring returns.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 13649/22-27, for an update on the introduction of a waiver process for Welfare Support Payment overpayments, in particular for Social Sector Size Criteria and the Benefit Cap.
answer_text: The new waiver process for Welfare Supplementary Payment overpayments was introduced by my Department on 30 September 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he intends to introduce a scheme similar to the Households Support Fund.
answer_text: The Discretionary Support Scheme is the Northern Ireland equivalent of the Household Support Fund.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Strategic Drainage Infrastructure Plan, including the Vision for Living With Water in Derry/Londonderry.
answer_text: My Department is leading on the development of a Living With Water in Derry Plan, to help protect against flooding, enhance the environment and support economic growth. My officials have been engaging with key stakeholders during the development of the draft Plan, including Derry City & Strabane District Council, Northern Ireland Housing Executive, and the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs. In May 2023 the Department published ‘A Vision for Derry / Londonderry’ which outlines the need for a Strategic Drainage Infrastructure Plan for Derry and the objectives of the Living With Water approach. The draft Living With Water in Derry Plan is due to be published for consultation later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the flood alleviation schemes for the (i) Drumahoe; and (ii) Eglinton areas, Derry.
answer_text: Development of potential flood alleviation proposals for Drumahoe is complex and remains ongoing. An economically viable scheme has been identified at this stage and consultants have been instructed to progress the detailed design phase of this project. It is intended that the detailed design phase will be complete by early 2026. Following this, progress to procurement and construction will be dependent on proposals remaining viable and availability of capital funding.Development of flood alleviation proposals for Eglinton are being progressed to determine if an economically viable solution can be found. Whilst a potential solution that would mitigate the risk of flooding from the river has been identified, surface water flooding would still affect properties. It is hoped that a wider-ranging review, which will look beyond traditional hard engineered solutions will be completed by Spring 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 10426/22-27, (i) for an update on the review of the regulation for both non-surgical cosmetic procedures and tattoo and piercing industry; and (ii) to detail any engagement he has had with the Department for Communities on this matter.
answer_text: The position remains the same as set out by former Health Minister Swann’s response to AQW (10426/22-27), there is no further update at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an update on the ban on conversion therapy; and (ii) whether he intends to implement a ban in the 2022-2027 mandate.
answer_text: This is a complex issue, not least due to difficulties around how we define what constitutes these practices therefore policy proposals must be robust, appropriate and provide protection of human rights, such as freedom of religion and freedom of speech. As I have stated previously, I cannot commit to a specific timescale for this work; it is essential to take the appropriate amount of time to engage with all those who have an interest and to carefully formulate policy on this issue. I have asked my officials to scope out options for further engagement.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the establishment of an Infrastructure Commission.
answer_text: We are currently considering the report from the SIB Review. The recommendations from the review will help to inform a revised paper on options for an Infrastructure Commission.  Decisions on the way forward will be a matter for the Executive in due course.

answer_text: I refer to my statement in the Assembly on 19 November 2024 in relation to a one-off fuel payment to eligible households.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 11208/22-27, for an update on the digital mobile working solution for gully maintenance.
answer_text: The testing of a planned IT development solution for the display of gully locations on a tablet device to allow contractors to update actions during routine cleaning operations, is to take place later this year, followed by live piloting of the final solution at the beginning of next year.  This will be completed in conjunction with the release of new Environmental Maintenance Contracts, that will include gully cleaning activities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 12078/22-27, for an update on the introduction of a Better Start Grant, including a pregnancy and baby payment.
answer_text: My officials have recently completed work to update the estimated costs for all of the recommendations from the Independent Advisory Panel Report into the future of welfare mitigations, including the introduction of a Better Start Grant. I will share these details with Executive colleagues and the Committee for Communities in due course.Any future mitigation package will need to be agreed by the Executive and assessed in the climate of financial constraint and the requirement to ensure that budgets remain sustainable over the coming years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an update on the Complex Lives Project; and (ii) when the project will be implemented in the Derry and Strabane area for individuals experiencing chronic homelessness.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me that:“Within Belfast the Multidisciplinary Team continues to meet weekly and has offered support to more than 140 people, showing positive outcomes for the majority people and high engagements rates. Partners have come together to recruit six Intensive Support Workers across different agencies and are offering intensive support to those most in need.Work is underway is to further develop this model and closely align it to the scaling up of the Housing First model, to ensure there are effective pathways to suitable accommodation solutions with the right wrap around support tailored to individual needs.  In the longer term it would be the intention to introduce this approach to other geographical areas. This would be based on need, stakeholder willingness and commitment to collaborate both strategically and operationally, to undertake work on the necessary system changes to allow for joint commissioning, information sharing and to secure long-term funding arrangements. However, it would be for local partners, including the Council to take this forward.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

answer_text: The total cost of benefit overpayments for these 87,505 claimants is £245.2m.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 15234/22-27, when the short public consultation on the Travelling Expenses and Remission of Charges Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2004 will open.
answer_text: I want to assure you that work on the proposed changes to the Travelling Expenses and Remission of Charges Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2004 is a key priority for my Department and that I am committed to ensuring that this work is completed without undue delay. This is a complex area, as it is not the case that in GB all Universal Credit (UC) recipients are automatically eligible for help with health costs. When UC was added as an automatic threshold elsewhere in GB, earnings thresholds were introduced to ensure that there was broadly the same number of people eligible for help with health costs once migration to UC had completed. Work is well advanced to develop policy options that navigate this complexity and strike an appropriate balance between providing help to patients who need support in accessing services, while protecting the public purse in the context of an extremely difficult financial environment. As previously outlined (AQW 15234/22-27 refers), my intention is that a short public consultation will be conducted in the coming months, with the associated legislative process commencing thereafter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she will reconsider VAT exemptions for homeowners affected by defective blocks who require remediation works.
answer_text: I recognise the difficulties faced by those in homes with defective mica and pyrite blocks, and the ongoing challenges in remedying this.However, the administration of VAT collection and payments, including VAT exemptions, is an excepted matter and responsibility sits with HMRC. Therefore, I do not have the scope to provide VAT exemptions for homeowners affected by defective blocks who require remediation works. My officials have previously been in contact with HMRC, on behalf of affected homeowners, who have advised anyone affected by these problems and seeking assistance with VAT payments to get in touch with HMRC directly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to clarify the speed limit on the A2 Clooney Road dual carriageway, Derry.
answer_text: I can confirm a 50mph speed limit is in place for eastbound traffic (country bound)  and a 70mph speed limit is in place for westbound, (city bound) traffic. The lower limit on the eastbound carriageway is due to the road being constructed as a bi-directional single lane carriageway and as such fails to meet current design standards for a dual carriageway. The westbound carriageway was constructed to a more recent design standard.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any engagement he has had in relation to the safety of residents living in non-standard accommodation and the wider community; and (ii) whether his Department has considered any additional safeguards to address potential risks.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive acquires the use of accommodation in the same manner as other customer groups who avail of Hotels/B&Bs, whereby the respective Hotel/B&B is subject to the relevant checks and accreditation to operate as a short-term accommodation. Designated Officers actively case manage their customers living in non-standard accommodation by maintaining regular contact which allows an opportunity for any concerns in respect of the accommodation to be raised.The Housing Executive’s long-term plans for temporary accommodation are guided by the Strategic Action Plan for Temporary Accommodation. As part of this action plan the Housing Executive intends to commission work with customers and providers to agree a temporary accommodation standards framework for Northern Ireland, including physical standards, suitability standards, and service standards for all types of temporary accommodation.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly. question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether all the recommendations of A review of waste disposal at the Mobuoy site and the lessons learnt for the future regulation of the waste industry in Northern Ireland, published in 2013 by Christopher Mills, have been implemented.
answer_text: The environmental crime that was committed at Mobuoy is simply appalling and my department is committed to pursuing the perpetrators of this crime through ongoing proceedings. This includes ensuring that the polluter pays through confiscation under the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002.My department is also continuing to progress work in relation to monitoring the site and determining the best approach for remediation through an industry standard process. A draft remediation strategy has been developed in relation to the long-term remediation of the Mobuoy site and the next step is the launch of a consultation with the public, to seek their views on the remediation of the site. I intend to announce a launch date for this consultation in the coming weeks. The Mills Report highlighted systemic failures in the management and regulation of the Waste Industry in Northern Ireland including significant criminal infiltration. There were 14 recommendations in the Mills Report, all of which were accepted by the department. A Management Action Plan, containing 25 actions, was developed in order to implement the recommendations.A final Internal Audit review completed in March 2018, confimed that of the 25 actions in the Mills Report, 23 were implemented effectively and 2 could not be implemented as their introduction would contravene European Law. These two actions related to changing policy to prevent retrospective planning permission for sand and gravel workings and became the responsibility of DfI in April 2015 when it assumed responsibility for planning policy.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) why the Western Health and Social Care Trust applied a different threshold than other Health and Social Care Trusts when categorising cervical cancer audits with an unsatisfactory outcome; and (ii) the potential impact this differentiation may have had on the number of patients affected.
answer_text: An audit of the previous screening pathway(s) in patients diagnosed with invasive cervical cancer in Northern Ireland was established by the NI Cervical Screening Programme for the purpose of learning and improvement. The process for undertaking this audit is set out in the 2014 “NI Protocol for the audit of Invasive cervical Cancers,” guidance document. When a case of invasive cervical cancer is diagnosed the audit process commences, with the diagnosing Trust leading a review of all elements of the women’s screening history, where one exists. The Public Health Agency (PHA) introduced the ‘Framework for the Audit of Invasive Cervical Cancers and Disclosure of Findings,’ in December 2019.  The purpose of this framework was to standardise approaches in Northern Ireland in terms of reporting and reviewing invasive cervical cancers; categorisation of audit outcomes and informing women of audit activities and audit findings. Where an audit of a case of invasive cervical cancer is assigned a category 3 outcome (unsatisfactory) following discussion by the Trust’s Multidisciplinary Team, a process to review and identify potential learning should be taken forward by the respective Trust under the Trust’s Serious Adverse Incident (SAI) review process. It is the responsibility of the diagnosing Trust’s Multidisciplinary Team to determine the most appropriate outcome for each case of invasive cervical cancer that is audited. Between 2019-2023, the Western Health and Social Care Trust (WHSCT) reported an audit outcome in 12 cases as a category 3 (unsatisfactory) outcome. Review and assessment of these cases was then taken forward under the Trust’s SAI process. This number is higher than the number of category 3 audit outcomes identified by other HSC Trusts over the similar time period. The WHSCT have advised that at that time the regional guidance did not include guidance on the standard to be applied in the review process. The WHSCT sought advice from their legal team in September 2020 which concluded that the WHSCT could use the “reasonableness method”.  As a result, a different threshold was applied in WHSCT than in the other HSC Trusts which resulted in a higher number of category 3 outcomes identified in WHSCT. The impact was that a higher proportion of patients with cervical cancer in WHSCT had their screening pathway notified as a serious adverse incident, compared to other Trusts.Additional guidance was then issued by the PHA, in July 2024, using a more recent toolkit published in England, to promote a more standardised approach by all Trusts in Northern Ireland and WHSCT have confirmed this has since been implemented.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Equality Impact Assessment consultation on the reduction in Rates Support Grant.

To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the external review of the cytology slides reported as Serious Adverse Incidents secured by the Public Health Agency, in conjunction with the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: As outlined in my Written Statement of 25 July 2024, Public Health Wales (PHW) was commissioned by WHSCT, in conjunction with the Public Health Agency (PHA), to provide a clinical opinion on the small number of cytology slides associated with the 12 cases identified with a category 3 audit outcome. On receipt of the clinical opinions from PHW, information in relation to the 12 cases (with a category 3 audit outcome from the Trust’s invasive cervical cancer audit process and notified as SAIs) was triangulated and assessed by the WHSCT and the PHA. This assessment concluded that the number of category 3 outcomes notified by WHSCT, occurred as a result of the Trust applying a different threshold when categorising cervical cancer audits with an ‘unsatisfactory’ outcome. I have been advised that the WHSCT did not deviate from the 2019 ‘Framework for the Audit of Invasive Cervical Cancers and Disclosure of Findings,’ but instead applied a more rigorous interpretation to the guidance of when an audit outcome is considered to be ‘unsatisfactory’.Additional guidance has now been issued by the PHA, using a more recent toolkit published in England, to promote a more standardised approach by all Trusts in NI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how the 10 per cent active travel spend, required by the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, will be spent in the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: Since the introduction of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, my Department has been developing plans to support increased investment in active travel and carrying out analysis of overall expenditure relevant to Section 22 of the Act. Ramping up an ambitious programme of investment of this nature will takes years. and therefore, the 10% spend will not be achieved in the current financial year. I am pleased to say that my officials have taken a number of significant steps since the introduction of the Act to increase the future delivery capacity as we strive to meet our ambitions for active travel in the future. In the current financial year, £20.7m has been directly allocated to the Active Travel capital and resource budgets. This represents an increase of approximately £10m compared to recorded spend last year and demonstrates my commitment to increasing investment in active travel.  In addition to this direct allocation, my Department routinely undertake many activities for the benefit of pedestrians and cyclists that are not included within these figures. Work is currently ongoing within my Department to finalise those areas of spend for future reporting.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 16702/22-27, to detail the number of hostels that provide accommodation to support the management of offenders, including individuals being supervised by the Probation Board, in each of the last two years, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: The provision of stable accommodation upon release from prison can be one of the critical success factors in desistance from reoffending. Statutory responsibility for the allocation of accommodation sits solely with the Department for Communities and the Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE).  My Department does not therefore hold details of where individuals are housed post release. Many Probation Board (PBNI) service users experience homelessness and avail of support from direct access hostels through NIHE.  PBNI have advised that the number of individuals being supervised by PBNI in hostel accommodation, other than Approved Premises, is not information held by them.  Neither does it have a statutory role in relation to where its service users reside.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) to detail any engagement he has had with the Department for Work and Pensions in the last 12 months in relation to freezing the Local Housing Allowance rates from April 2025; and (ii) for his assessment on the potential impact on private renters.
answer_text: Responsibility for reviewing Local Housing Allowance lies with the Secretary of State for Work and Pensions who has committed to an annual review of Local Housing Allowance.I wrote to the Secretary of State for Work and Pensions on 5 March 2024 requesting that, going forward, Local Housing Allowance rates are considered with a view to maintaining pace with the increases in private sector rents.Rents are generally a household’s largest single expense. The increase in Local Housing Allowance (LHA) rate from April 2024 therefore assisted many renters with affordability of rent costs and with cost-of-living challenges more generally. Indeed, some households saw an increase in housing support of over 40 percent, which illustrates the scale of the impact that the 2020 freeze had on those living in the private rented sector. If the LHA rate is frozen again from 1 April 2025, the burden will once again be felt acutely by low-income households who will face a growing shortfall between housing support and housing costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the implementation of the Cancer Strategy for Northern Ireland 2022-2032.
answer_text: Despite a challenging budget, significant progress has been made to deliver a wide range of key cancer strategy actions, which include:Two Rapid Diagnosis Centres (RDC) delivering a vague symptoms pathway for people across the region, with vague but worrying symptoms which may be cancer.Haematology pathway reviews have now been completed. Officials are currently implementing the recommendations to help improve service delivery and sustainability.To ensure that the services are better staffed, we have invested in oncology and haematology stabilisation plans and Clinical Nurse Specialists.A review of cancer support services for Adolescents and Young Adults has also now been completed, with work progressing to implement the recommendations.A directory of cancer support services has been developed to help signpost those impacted by a cancer diagnosis to support services in their area. Northern Ireland was the first part of the UK to commence work to implement an optimal care pathway for pancreatic cancer patients. On 25 September 2024, I announced my plans for regional Breast Cancer Services, with a regional waiting list for breast assessment to be established in the upcoming months. Work to develop a Cancer Research Strategy is progressing, with plans to publish during the current financial year. Undoubtedly, there is more to be done, and additional founding is required, however, the cancer strategy is already starting to deliver benefits for cancer patients across Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of unadopted housing developments, broken down by local council area; and (ii) how many of these remain unadopted due to issues around water and wastewater infrastructure over the last 12 months.
answer_text: The number of unadopted housing developments recorded by my Department is provided in the table below.  These figures include housing developments started as at 24/10/2024 and those that have remained unadopted for a significant period.DivisionDistrict Council AreaSites unadoptedNorthernMid & East Antrim131 Antrim & Newtownabbey130 Causeway Coast & Glens160     EasternBelfast Council Area148 Lisburn & Castlereagh Council Area163     WesternFODC146 Strabane L/Derry147 Mid Ulster273   SouthernABC Council 249 Ards and North Down151 Newry Mourne & Down   170TOTAL1,868The department is unable to provide information on the number unadopted due to issues around water and wastewater infrastructure over the last 12 months as this information is not readily held by NI Water and to attempt to provide details would require an extensive exercise to be conducted across the department which might only be expected to produce data with a limited degree of exactitude, and this exercise itself would very likely involve disproportionate costs..For any future queries relating to water/wastewater operational matters, please contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on (i) the revaluation of businesses on Foyle Street, Derry; and (ii) whether she has considered offering any support, including rate reductions, for business owners who face a significant decline in footfall due to ongoing NI Water works.
answer_text: The degree to which public works may affect the Net Annual Value (NAV) of premises in a particular locality is based on the duration of the works and severity of impact on the businesses affected.  LPS is currently gathering information on how the businesses have been affected by the works. The District Valuer will then consider if a temporary allowance is warranted and decide if a wider review is required of similarly affected property on Foyle Street. I am not considering an additional relief for public works as the rating and valuation system contains an appropriate mechanism to consider the impact of such works on businesses affected. That is determined by established case law and a long-standing statutory framework that supports the stability of a tax base used to fund central and local government services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 14949/22-27, (i) for an indicative time frame for the publication of this report; and (ii) for his assessment of the value for money of temporary accommodation value as costs continue to escalate.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The report is currently in draft format and the Housing Executive continues to work closely with the Centre for Homelessness Impact in the development of a final report.  The Housing Executive is not in a position to confirm an indicative time frame for publication at this stage. The unprecedented increase in the demand for temporary accommodation has led to higher usage of costly non-standard (Hotel/B&B) accommodation to meet the Housing Executive’s statutory duties. The Housing Executive has significantly expanded its portfolio of more cost effective, standard temporary accommodation and work is ongoing in this regard.  This includes efforts to secure additional single lets and HMOs, as well as identification of suitable void Housing Executive and housing association stock for use as temporary accommodation.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, given that the current operator agreement for Crumlin Road Gaol Visitor and Conference Centre ends in November 2024, whether (i) the facilities will remain open after this date if the procurement is not completed; and (ii) staff jobs at the Gaol will be secure.
answer_text: The Crumlin Road Gaol is an important attraction, providing employment for, and attracting tourists to an area of Belfast which has experienced deprivation. Presently, there are three parties with an interest in the Gaol, namely, the Department, the operator of the visitor and conference centre, and the operator of the distillery. The Gaol Visitor Attraction and Conference Centre (GVACC) is an important component in the Crumlin Road Gaol regeneration project. Management of GVACC is under an Operator Agreement with Belfast Tours Limited (BTL), the term of which ends in November 2024. The appointment of a new operator is managed by Construction and Procurement Delivery (CPD/DoF), through an open and transparent procurement process, in line with best practice. There has been significant investment in the scheme out of the public purse, it is important to ensure best value for money. To provide for a new contract to be in place by November 2024, the procurement process commenced in January 2024 with a pre-market engagement exercise. The intention was to publicly advertise the tender around April 2024. However, the current operator raised a dispute with my Department on 29 March 2024, and this has resulted in the pausing of the advertising of the procurement. Since then, a further legal issue has been pursued by the operator and there are presently legal proceedings before the Lands Tribunal. I do not wish to prejudice these proceedings by providing any further information at this time. Importantly, however, steps are being taken to agree and introduce arrangements which will ensure that the Gaol facilities remain open until such times as a new contract is awarded for the visitor and conference centre. Whilst staffing matters are for the Operator, Officials have been, and will continue, to work hard to ensure that jobs are secure not least as it brings much needed employment to the area and this will be a key factor of any future tender process.The Department, as owners of the site, continues to recognise that the Crumlin Road Gaol is a public asset and the key objective remains to promote the Gaol as a location of international significance and encourage it’s regeneration as a force for positive change in North Belfast.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on his Department's Optimum Remediation Strategy and programme for remediation for the site at Mobuoy.
answer_text: A draft Optimum Remediation Strategy has been developed, in line with an  industry standard process, for the long-term remediation of the Mobuoy site. The next step will be a public consultation.The consultation is currently going through internal departmental clearance processes, with the aim to release the consultation document early in the new year. Any implementation plan and timetable for remediation remain subject to the outcome of the upcoming public consultation and will be considered in the context of the Department’s responsibility to protect water quality and the extremely challenging constraints of public finances.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs
