answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for NIHE.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The table below shows the volume of approvals for grant-aid, to enable the installation of energy efficiency measures through the Affordable Warmth Scheme, by Council area for the previous five financial years.”Council areaFinancial Year2019/ 20202020/ 20212021/ 20222022/ 20232023/ 2024ANTRIM AND NEWTOWNABBEY225189245295209ARDS AND NORTH DOWN260243232295224ARMAGH CITY BANBRIDGE AND CRAIGAVON217138332305271BELFAST262224283324310CAUSEWAY COAST AND GLENS247237295308277DERRY CITY AND STRABANE235317290298252FERMANAGH AND OMAGH225171253300230LISBURN AND CASTLEREAGH280205267292166MID AND EAST ANTRIM215281278299202MID ULSTER230159232300253NEWRY MOURNE AND DOWN225126345291288Annual Total26212290305233072682

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 15721/22-27, to detail the date on which he offered to meet with the Construction Employers Federation.
answer_text: My office issued correspondence on 5th March 2024 to the Head of Public Affairs at the Construction Employers Federation accepting an invite to meet with the body and requested that they liaise with my office to make the necessary arrangements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of approved premises per local council area, for each of the last five years.
answer_text: Approved premises act as a transition or half-way house between prison and settlement in the community for high risk offenders leaving custody.  They have two main roles: to help rehabilitate and resettle some of the most serious offenders leaving custody; and to make sure the public are protected in the offenders’ early months in the community.All residents are supervised by the Probation Board for Northern Ireland (PBNI) and managed in accordance with PBNI practice standards.  There are seven approved premises across Northern Ireland that currently provide 91 places.The table below details the breakdown of approved premises in Northern Ireland per local council area between 2020 and 2024:Local Council AreaNumber of Approved PremisesBelfast City Council4Derry and Strabane District Council1Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council1Mid Ulster District Council1There are other hostels across Northern Ireland which provide accommodation to support the management of offenders, including individuals being supervised by the PBNI, but what distinguishes them from approved premises is that they primarily address a homelessness rather than a risk management need.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of successful applications to the Affordable Warmth Scheme still awaiting completion; and (ii) whether there are plans to increase funding for the scheme to ensure that all awarded applications can be fulfilled.
answer_text: Part one of this question is an operational matter for NIHE.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“As of 16 October 2024, the Housing Executive confirmed that of those approvals issued up to end June 2024, 175 applicants are still awaiting works to be completed.Statistical information for the Affordable Warmth Scheme is classed as an official/national statistic. This is currently officially released through DfC’s annual Housing Statistics Report which is the over-arching document classed as an official/national statistic and its quarterly Housing Bulletin (at Housing statistics | Department for Communities (communities-ni.gov.uk)) to allow a more timely release. The Code of Practice for the publication of national/official statistics means the Housing Executive cannot share information on approvals issued for Q2 (July – Sept) 2024-25 until it has been published in the quarterly Housing Bulletin.”(ii) My Department has undertaken an equality impact assessment on its Budget.  Informed by responses received and any further Budget allocations received in October Monitoring, the final report is expected to be published in November.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any consideration he has given to the establishment of an independent environmental protection agency for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I am keen to bring forward proposals for an Independent Environment Protection Agency (IEPA) for consideration by the Executive. I want to explore models that would deliver improved and more independent environmental governance. I have asked officials to take forward some work in that regard and will be announcing next steps shortly.The last consultation on an Independent Environmental Protection Agency (IEPA) was in 2015 when NIEA was part of the old Department for the Environment and much will have changed since then, not least public opinion on environmental degradation and the introduction of the Environment Act 2021. It is important that we take account of this and ensure the best possible outcomes and public confidence going forward.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) to detail any engagement he has had with the Minister for Communities in relation to holiday hunger; and (ii) whether he has given further consideration to the establishment of a School Holiday Food Grant ahead of the Christmas period.
answer_text: I met with the Minister for Communities on 9 September to discuss the issue of child food poverty including how this can impact families both during and outside of school term time. We agreed that it was appropriate for this issue to be considered holistically as part of the development of the Executive’s Anti-Poverty Strategy. While the Department for Communities leads on the development of the Anti-Poverty Strategy, my Department will also contribute to its development, and will work to ensure that the needs of children in food poverty, including those experienced by children during school holidays, are fully considered. In light of this, there are no current plans for my Department to reintroduce a School Holiday Food Grant scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of Pension Credit applications received by his Department in each of the last 12 months; and (ii) how many of these have been successful.
answer_text: The Department has received a total of 8712 applications for the period 1st October 2023 to 31st September 2024. The monthly breakdown is detailed in the table below.MonthReceivedOctober 2023694November 2023672December 2023421January 2024699February 2024805March 2024752April 2024707May 2024598June 2024502July 2024663August 2024889September 20241310Total8712The Department is unable to provide a definitive figure for the number of successful applications during this period, due to the reporting limitations of the computer systems used. However, locally held management information indicates around 65% of applications are successful.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 15799/22-27, to detail (i) the number of women that have reached the age threshold of eligibility for IVF treatment due to delays in accessing fertility services in the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) the steps his Department is taking to reduce waiting times for an initial consultation with a fertility specialist, particularly in the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: (i) As outlined in my response to AQW 15799/22-27, IVF treatment is commissioned and provided by the Regional Fertility Centre in Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.  Belfast Trust recording and reporting systems do not provide data on the number of women that have reached the age threshold of eligibility for IVF treatment due to delays in accessing fertility services in the last five years. The Trust have attempted to gather this information through manual checks of patient records but are unable to manage this resource/staff pressure and the time required to supply this information in a timely manner.   The Trust have also tried to identify other ways of obtaining this information but the results would not answer the particular question being asked.  As previously indicated in my response to AQW 15799/22-27 the Northern Health and Social Care Trust do not provide dedicated fertility clinics and therefore do not hold detail on the number of women that have reached the age threshold of eligibility for IVF treatment due to delays in accessing fertility services in the last five years. South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust also do not hold information of this nature.  If a woman is referred to the South Eastern Trust who required treatment in the Regional Fertility Centre the Trust would inform the GP and advise direct referral to the Regional Fertility Centre to prevent any delays.   If women are referred to the South Eastern Trust over the age of 37, the referral is graded as urgent to ensure that they are not waiting an excessive amount of time to be seen. As at June 2024 the current waiting time within the Western Health and Social Care Trust is 75 weeks (approx. 1.5 years) for first appointment at a fertility/reproductive outpatient clinic.  Further detail is provided in Tables 1 and 2 below: Table 1: WHSCT as at June 2023 Age at referral Number on the waiting list >38.5 years 66 Table 2: WHSCT as at June 2024 Age at referral Number on the waiting list >38.5 years 82 It should be noted that not all women on the Fertility waiting list will require or meet the eligibility criteria for referral for IVF to the Regional Fertility Centre.Prior to 2023 patients were seen within Western Trust general Gynaecology clinics and were not specifically coded as fertility, hence data prior to 2023 cannot be provided.  Direct referrals to the Western Trust’s fertility clinic are triaged by a consultant who has a specialist interest in infertility/ reproduction and are actioned according to clinical priority.  The patient’s age forms part of this triage process for direct onward referral to Belfast Trust’s Regional Fertility Centre if all other essential criteria have been met.  Southern Health and Social Care Trust do not centrally collate information of this nature.  The Trust has reported that the cost of retrieving the information would be above the ‘appropriate limit’, and have estimated that it would take 18 hours or more of input from one st

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 14758/22-27, (i) whether he has outlined the next steps of the Anti-Poverty Strategy development to Executive colleagues; and (ii) for an indicative time frame for the implementation of the strategy.
answer_text: I have recently written to my Ministerial colleagues to set out the next steps in relation to the development of the Strategy. My officials will be engaging with officials from other Departments over the coming weeks to discuss the development of a range of keystone actions which will help mitigate impacts, reduce the risk of falling into poverty and support people in exiting poverty.These discussions will aim to finalise the drafting process for the Anti-Poverty Strategy and to ensure that work proceeds in the most effective and expeditious way possible. It is anticipated that a paper on the Strategy will be provided to the Executive in early 2025. Final decisions on the content and implementation of the Anti-Poverty Strategy will be subject to Executive agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the total funding allocation provided by his Department to the City of Derry Airport in each of the last five years; and (ii) whether he intends to allocate any additional funding to the airport in the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: Over the last 5 years my Department has facilitated payments totalling £4,957,824 to Derry City & Strabane District Council to assist the City of Derry Airport (CoDA). The payments broken down by year, are detailed below:YearPayment2019/2020£02020/2021£1,850,6102021/2022£107,2142022/2023£3,000,0002023/2024£0Total£4,957,824A funding package of up to £3m has been agreed for CoDA for the 2024/25 financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the review of the Housing Selection Scheme, specifically the allocation of intimidation points for victims of domestic and gender-based violence.
answer_text: Work on the implementation phase of the Fundamental Review of Allocations is ongoing. This is now progressing to Stage Three. I have received independent research that was commissioned by the Housing Executive which specifically looks at recognition for all victims of violence within the Housing Selection Scheme. I am keen to address this issue and am treating this as high priority.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for his assessment of the issues at Creggan Reservoir and the impact on Glenowen Fisheries; and (ii) to detail any action his Department is taking to help resolve them.
answer_text: My Department does not own or manage the Creggan Reservoirs and has no direct involvement in the identification of potential issues or any remedial options as may be required. My Officials encourage all reservoir managers to manage their reservoirs in a responsible manner and follow recommendations as may be directed by a reservoir engineer. In this case, my Officials have met with Derry City and Strabane District Council officers but have not been a party to their negotiations with any tenants on the reservoirs.I intend to introduce five pieces of secondary legislations that will further commence and implement the Reservoirs Act (NI) 2015. Once commenced, these will enable my officials to monitor compliance with the reservoir safety regime that provides for the safety of controlled reservoirs.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 14594/22-27, when insights of the community workshops in relation to Ebrington will be shared.
answer_text: Three community workshops took place in June to explore better ways of working together that will realise the full potential of Ebrington.   A report including feedback and insights from recent community and stakeholder engagement in relation to Ebrington is currently being finalised.  The feedback highlights ambitions for the site and enthusiasm for community led approaches and a diverse range of creative and vibrant possibilities. The report will be shared directly with those who took part in the engagement process and also published online on the Your Ebrington website as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 14667/22-27, to detail the amount of (i) Belfast Council costs for Block Booking Non-Standard; (ii) Block Booked Singled Lets; (iii) Miscellaneous; and (iv) Centralised Costs, has been spent in the Derry and Strabane area in the 2023-24 financial year.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive and been advised that:“i) I can confirm that all Block Booking Non-Standard costs included within Table 2 of AQW 14667/22-27 are exclusive to Belfast, and that no such arrangements exist in Derry/Strabane at present. ii) As outlined in our response to AQW 14667/22-27, it is not possible to provide a breakdown for Block Booked Single Lets (Table 3) per Local Council area as this level of detail for this form of temporary accommodation is not captured on NIHE systems at present. iii) As outlined in our response to AQW 14667/22-27, it is not possible to provide a breakdown for Miscellaneous Expenditure (Table 3) per Local Council area as this level of detail for this form of temporary accommodation is not captured on NIHE systems at present. iv) For clarification, the Centralised cost heading in Table 3, represents the sum total of Block Booked Single Let and Miscellaneous costs (refer above).”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 12488/22-27, (i) when his Department last conducted an assessment of the (a) Westway; and (b) Beechwood areas for road and footpath defects; and (ii) whether safety features, including pedestrian crossings and dropped kerbs, have been evaluated to ensure the safety of elderly residents.
answer_text: The last inspection of defects of the Westway and Beechwood areas for road and footway defects was carried out on 4th October 2024 with the next inspection scheduled for 5th November 2024.Officials have also confirmed that requests for the provision of safety features such as dropped kerbs or pedestrian crossings have not been received for the Westway and Beechwood areas to allow assessments to be undertaken.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any engagement he has had with the Minister of Health in relation to homeless individuals seeking refuge at hospital emergency departments; and (ii) any steps he is taking to address this issue to support the homeless population and ensure emergency departments are used for their intended purpose.
answer_text: I have had no engagement on this matter with the Minister of Health.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of patients waiting to access occupational therapy services; and (ii) the average waiting times in the last 12 months, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Number of Patients waiting to access Occupational Therapy Services by HSC Trust:HSC TrustNumber of Patients WaitingBHSCT3257NHSCT5695SEHSCT2884SHSCT3644WHSCT3917The average waiting times in the last 12 monthsHSC TrustAverage Waiting TimesBHSCTAdult 8.5 Months (Assessment)Paediatric 7.5 Months (Assessment)NHSCT195 (Days)SEHSCT135 (Days)SHSCT95 (Days)WHSCTAdult 128 Weeks (Assessment)Paediatric 177 Weeks (Assessment)The BHSCT and WHSCT do not hold data on average waiting times in the last 12 months, however they have provided the average waiting time for a routine assessment.PHA continue to work with SPPG and Trusts to ensure Allied Health Profession (AHP) services including Occupational Therapy deliver a range of support that reflects the needs of the population of NI and is aligned to evidence based practice.Work is underway between SPPG and PHA to extract Children and Young People (CYP) statistics regarding elective AHP waiting times including Occupational Therapy to provide a better understanding of the specific waiting times across CYP elective AHP services which is hoped to be operational from October 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will commit to conducting a traffic survey for the Creggan Heights area, Derry.
answer_text: I am advised by officials, following development in the area, that traffic levels on Creggan Heights have increased in recent years, however no specific road safety measures have been identified as being required. My Department has no plans to conduct a traffic survey for the Creggan Heights area.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 10045/22-27, to detail any engagement he has had with Executive colleagues in relation to securing a sustainable solution to the ongoing problem of illegally erected flags and emblems.
answer_text: Finding a sustainable solution to the ongoing problem of illegally erected flags and emblems requires leadership and a united approach. I can assure you that I am committed to working with my Executive colleagues to deal with this issue and remain ready to engage with them going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether Translink will facilitate football supporters travelling from Derry to Dublin for the FAI Cup Final on 10 November 2024 with the provision of special rail and bus services.
answer_text: I would firstly like to apologise for the delay in returning this answer. This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Translink facilitated football supporters by bus travelling from Derry to Dublin for the FAI Cup Final on 10th November 2024.  There were ticket sales for three buses. Translink also had a rail special express for fans travelling from Derry~Londonderry, to the FAI Cup Final. The rail special operated between the North West Transport Hub and Dublin Connolly Train Station, with all tickets sold out. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an update on the changes to eligibility criteria for the Winter Fuel Payment, including the qualifying benefits; and (ii) when eligible claimants should expect their payment.
answer_text: The revised eligibility criteria for Winter Fuel Payments came into operation on 16 September 2024. The main change is a restriction of entitlement to people who are in receipt of Pension Credit or other means tested benefits. The Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) administers the Winter Fuel Payment on behalf of the Department for Communities. DWP will begin to issue payments in November 2024. All payments will be made by the end of January 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the provision of Palforzia in Northern Ireland; and (ii) to detail a timeline for its availability to patients.
answer_text: Following previous communications in the summer on the subject a regional Task and Finish Group is currently being convened to consider the range of options that are available to develop a service model for the delivery of this novel peanut allergy treatment in NI.   A clinical chair has been appointed and paediatricians and specialist allergy nurses across all Trusts as well as service planners will be members of the group.  It is expected the group will commence its considerations in the coming weeks. Given as this is a novel treatment that has not been commissioned previously in NI and the complexities involved it will be essential that the clinicians consider all the options to ensure that a safe, high quality service is developed.  It is hoped that this will be completed as quickly as possible but it is more important that clinicians are content that the agreed model is safe.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 14851/22-27, for an update on his Department's assessment of the impact of the changes to the Winter Fuel Payment on pensioners.
answer_text: My Department is progressing plans for an Equality Impact Assessment consultation on the changes to the Winter Fuel Payment eligibility criteria. Further details will be announced in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 779/22-27, for an update on efforts to provide a one-off hardship payment to those in receipt of state pension who will miss out on the Winter Fuel Payment this winter.
answer_text: My Department is currently exploring options on how to mitigate the loss of Winter Fuel Payments to pensioners who will not be receiving the Winter Fuel Payment. This is dependent on Executive agreement to the provision of appropriate funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any consideration his Department has given to a cross-border approach to resolve issues in relation to Coshquin Pumping Station and the consequent impediment to the delivery of housing.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and been advised that NI Water has not pursued cross-border funding for Coshquin Pumping Station.  Given the current capacity challenges, my Department is exploring developer led contributions, working to progress sustainable drainage solutions and engaging with Executive colleagues about additional funding . For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.  Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an update on the Intermediate Rent Housing Scheme; and (ii) whether the allocation process is based on cost or assessed need.
answer_text: The competition for funding closed on 23rd August 2024, attracting interest from a range of organisations. Officials are now assessing the applications received and the outcome will be made public once that process completes. An intensive period of diligence and legal agreements will follow, and the development of a Full Business Case will secure the necessary budget.There is no allocation process for Intermediate Rent homes. The Intermediate Rent policy sets out the application process alongside the eligibility criteria and is available at the following link: https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/articles/intermediate-rent.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will introduce a levy on gambling operator profits, which could fund research and treatment for problem gambling.
answer_text: The Department is currently gathering data to ensure that an evidence base is available to inform any future policy decisions, inclusive of the possible introduction of a levy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he has considered establishing a dedicated unit within the planning system, similar to the Scottish Government's Energy Consents Unit.
answer_text: Regionally significant energy proposals are dealt with by my officials in the Regional Planning Policy and Casework team.   I do not consider the establishment of a separate unit to be necessary.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the steps he is taking to protect marine life in our waters; and (ii) his Department's plans to enhance marine biodiversity conservation in line with current environmental commitments.
answer_text: Since April my Department has launched four public consultations on marine recovery related strategies which are targets in the recently adopted Environmental Improvement Plan: Blue Carbon Action Plan; Marine Protected Area Strategy Review; Seabird Conservation Strategy; and Elasmobranch Conservation Strategy. These strategies and action plans aim to address biodiversity loss while improving resilience and making steps towards recovery. These are also linked closely with the development of my Department’s draft Nature Recovery Strategy.The draft Marine Plan for Northern Ireland is being finalised to facilitate the sustainable development of the marine area. Marine spatial planning provides a mechanism to ensure that the conservation objectives of the Marine Protected Area network are not adversely impacted by wider human activities.Officials are engaging with stakeholders to discuss a range of marine environment issues, including Fisheries Managements Plans which will set out measures to ensure the long-term sustainability of our fish stocks for current and future generations, whilst protecting the marine environment.My Department’s marine scientists and fisheries officers continue to maintain their programme of monitoring, assessment, regulation, inspection and enforcement, ensuring that current management measures are providing effective protection for marine ecosystems.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the measures his Department will take to address long-term pupil absences due to mental health issues; and (ii) how schools are being supported to ensure early intervention and resources for pupils.
answer_text: My department recognises that attitudes towards regular attendance at school have changed since the pandemic. I also acknowledge that some pupils with unique challenges are finding attending school difficult for various reasons, one of which is poorer mental health.The joint Department of Education and Department of Health ‘Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework’ supports several projects that work to develop resilience and promote positive emotional wellbeing in our children and young people across primary and post-primary ages.  There are also wider health services, in both statutory, voluntary and community services available to support young people with mental health issues.Officials have also been developing a new attendance strategy, to reflect these new challenges and will be consulting on that document in the coming months.A strong pastoral culture and ethos in a school will support pupil attendance and where children are too unwell to attend school. The EA has services to support pupils to maintain engagement with education and reintegrate back into school when deemed medically fit to do so.  For those children who, by virtue of their mental health, are unable to attend school, alternative education provision is available both in the short and longer term.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of pupils on long-term absence from school; and (ii) the number that have been referred to the Education Welfare Service, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My department uses the term “persistent absence” to describe circumstances where pupils have 10% or more absence, equating to 19 days absent in any academic year. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients on waiting lists for elective surgery in each of the last three years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The requested information is available on the Departmental website (www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/inpatient-waiting-times). Reducing waiting times for elective care remains a key priority.  Within the constraints of existing budgets, I’m committed to taking whatever actions I can to tackle waiting times and ensure that patients are treated as quickly as possible to ensure best possible outcomes. This demands a long-term collective effort requiring sustained and substantial recurrent investment through multi-year budgets, workforce development and system wide transformation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of potholes reported in the last 12 months, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: Data showing potholes reported by the public for the past several years is regularly published and available for public viewing through the OpenDataNI portal at https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/dataset/pothole-enquiries which can be downloaded and filtered by date, geographical area, road name, local district council, and status.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will reintroduce the Healthy Happy Minds school counselling support programme.
answer_text: The former Healthy Happy Minds pilot concluded on 31 March 2023. My department is not, at present, developing a programme for counselling services in primary schools. The challenging financial context across the education system means that budgets and investments are severely constrained.  As with all services that are not statutory, investment is limited to budget availability.    Schools continue to have flexibility to use their individual budgets to best meet the needs of their pupils.  In some circumstances this can include employing the services of a counsellor or therapist from existing funds.  Any immediate and appropriately assessed need for counselling or therapy support for primary age children continues to be available through health services, including CAMHS and associated pathways and support services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on his Department's response to the Department for Work and Pensions' Modernising Support for Independent Living Green Paper and proposed changes to Personal Independence Payments, including the cessation of cash payments to claimants.
answer_text: The consultation on the Modernising Support Green Paper, published under the previous government, closed on Monday 22 July.  The Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) has informed my Department that over 16,000 responses were received.  DWP have reviewed these responses, and the new Government will consider their approach to social security in due course.There is no further update at this stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the level of funding that will be allocated to the City of Derry Airport; and (ii) the timeline for when his Department will assume responsibility for funding from Derry City and Strabane District Council.
answer_text: My Department has agreed to assume responsibility for providing financial assistance to Derry City and Strabane District Council (DCSDC) to support the City of Derry Airport.  This to safeguard air connectivity and the associated economic benefits to the North West Region. In answer to your questions;This will be an initial c£3m for 2024/25 which will be administered via DfI. It is anticipated that my Department will assume funding responsibility from 1 April 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the additional funding his Department will contribute to the A5 Western Transport Corridor beyond the €600 million committed by the Irish Government.
answer_text: The emerging estimate for the whole scheme is in the region of £2.1 billion and for the part of the scheme being authorised, the emerging cost is around £1.2 billion.The Irish Government has committed €600million of this.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will allocate funding to Shopmobility as part of the Transport Programme for People with Disabilities to ensure it can sustain its services.
answer_text: I have recently awarded an additional 4.2% funding in 2024/25 to Shopmobility schemes providing front-line services above the levels provided in 2023/24 from the Transport Programme for People with Disabilities fund.I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to protect and support front line transport services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how many bids from the Minister of Health have been progressed by the Interim Public Sector Transformation Board.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of claimants in receipt of Personal Independence Payment in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The total number of claimants in receipt of Personal Independence Payment in each of the last 5 years is shown in the table below.20202021202220232024144,140152,760168,480185,330205,840Further statistics in relation to PIP can be found using the link below.Personal Independence Payment Statistics - May 2024 | Department for Communities (communities-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 14592/22-27, to detail (i) the timeline for when final decisions on funding for homelessness services will be made; and (ii) how the Department plans to ensure that vital services are protected given potential budget constraints.
answer_text: The Equality Impact Assessment (EQIA) consultation on Budget 2024-25 closed on 3 September 2024. Responses are currently being reviewed and will be used to draft a Consultation Outcome Report for my attention. Any further mitigation measures raised will be considered and recommendations put forward for my decision. A final decision report will then be drafted for publication. I have committed to holding Homelessness funding at the 2023-24 Final Budget position. Officials are actively working with the Housing Executive to ensure that they can prioritise the delivery of frontline services within a challenging budget position. I will continue to raise with Executive colleagues the serious and long-term detrimental impact on the services delivered to people in our communities and my intention to bid for additional funding for the housing and homelessness budget through the in-year monitoring round process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 15128/22-27, whether he will consider (i) a ban on no-fault evictions; and (ii) tighter rent controls, including the restriction of rent increases to once in a 12-month period, as part of phase 2 of this programme of reforms.
answer_text: I have no plans to ban no fault evictions, as my Department is already taking forward work, as mandated by the Assembly, to increase security of tenure through the introduction of much longer notice to quit periods. I am currently considering what further actions I can take in the next phase of my private rented sector reform programme. My considerations include whether the frequency of rent increases should be limited to once in a 12-month period, with a 3 month notice period.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 15387/22-27, whether his Department plans to provide financial support to assist the Magee campus to expand affordable student accommodation at Duncreggan Student Village.
answer_text: My Department has not received a request for additional finance for the expansion of the Duncreggan Student Village.  However, my officials will continue to work with Ulster University and other partners to support the development of affordable student accommodation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she has had any engagement with the Minister of Health in relation to funding the Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme.
answer_text: No.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when his Department's formal response to the consultation on inconsiderate pavement parking will be issued.
answer_text: The Findings Report on responses received to the consultation exercise on inconsiderate pavement parking is available on the Departmental website:  https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/inconsiderate-pavement-parking-findings-report. A formal response will be made public when I have had the time to determine the way forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an update on the Review of the Disabled Facilities Grant Scheme; and (ii) to detail any engagement he has had with the Minister of Health around access to Occupational Therapists as part of this process.
answer_text: Please refer to AQW 15494/22-27.    To date I have not engaged with the Minister of Health around access to Occupational Therapists as part of the review of the Disabled Facilities Grant Scheme. My officials have regular engagement with officials from the Department of Health as part of the Review of (NIHE-administered) Private Sector Grants, which includes the Disabled Facilities Grant.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of people on a waiting list for adaptations to their homes under the Disabled Facilities Grant; and (ii) the length of time they have been waiting, broken down by council area, for each of the last three years.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 16027/ 22-27, the information has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure why primary legislation is necessary to grant his Department the authority to conduct planning validation checks, whereby it is not necessary for local councils.
answer_text: Subsequent to the public consultation on the original policy proposals, the Department obtained legal advice which indicated that to provide the Department with equivalent legislative powers to prepare and publish validation checklists (for applications where it has jurisdiction) and with the associated dispute mechanism, would require changes to section 58 of the Planning Act (NI) 2011. This is because section 58, which is relied upon to provide the means for appealing a decision by the council not to validate a planning application, only applies to councils, and not the Department.  Given that a right to appeal is a fundamental part of the process there would be no acceptance of, or confidence in, a process lacking an appeal mechanism.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 15459/22-27, for a timeline for when this strategy will be presented to the Executive for approval.
answer_text: As previously stated, I have been meeting with Executive colleagues who have a key strategic remit around housing supply, to garner support for future implementation of the Housing Supply Strategy.This engagement has now concluded, and I am finalising the strategy document to bring to the Executive very shortly.Publication of a final strategy will be dependent on Executive approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 15233/22-27, whether he will consider a moratorium on, rather than an end to, the Housing Executive's Right to Buy Scheme.
answer_text: I have no plans to consider a moratorium on the Housing Executive’s House Sales Scheme.

answer_text: I refer the Member to the response to AQW 15026/22-27 which issued on 24/09/24.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will give further consideration to extending the 50 per cent discount period for a Penalty Charge Notice to help alleviate the financial burden facing individuals from low-income households.
answer_text: The Traffic Management Order (Northern Ireland) 2005 prescribes a reduction to the amount of a Penalty Charge Notice (PCN) if payment is received within 14-days.  Accordingly, the guidance issued to PCN recipients is that the £90 PCN charge will be discounted by 50% if paid within 14 days. Should a recipient wish to challenge a PCN within 14 days of it being issued, the 50% discount will not expire while the challenge is being considered.Further to my undertaking at the Infrastructure Committee meeting on 25 September 2024, I have asked my officials to examine whether there is capacity within my Department to consider extending the discount period on the basis of other competing priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 14668/22-27, to detail the timeframe for when the final report will be published.
answer_text: The review of residents’ parking schemes is complete, and I have had the opportunity to consider the draft review report and plan to issue the final review report soon.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the provision of a Better Start Larger Families Payment, as recommended by the Welfare Mitigations Review's Independent Advisory Panel.
answer_text: Officials have recently completed work to update the estimated costs for all of the recommendations coming out of the Independent Advisory Panel Report into the future of welfare mitigations, including the provision of a Better Start Larger Families Payment.In due course these details will be shared with the Executive and the Committee for Communities.Any future mitigation package will need to be considered and agreed by the Executive and assessed in the climate of financial constraint with the requirement to ensure that budgets remain sustainable over the coming years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he has considered introducing free school meal provision for children in primary one through to primary five, similar to the Scottish model.
answer_text: My Department is continuing to progress a review of the eligibility criteria for free school meals (FSM) and uniform grants. I plan to launch a consultation on the options considered by this review in the coming weeks. The options include the provision of universal FSM to some/all year groups.  The consultation documents are currently being finalised and they will outline the modelling undertaken as part of the review, including the number of additional pupils who would be able to access a free school meal and the estimated associated costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the Department for Infrastructure-related concerns that are delaying the discharge of planning conditions for the Arbour Housing site at Buncrana Road, Derry.
answer_text: As part of the pre-commencement conditions in the planning permission for this site, the developer was required to submit a detailed drainage and geotechnical design for approval prior to commencement of any work on site.  In this case, the developer chose to proceed at risk and submitted a Discharge of Condition application in February 2024, after the construction was well underway.My Officials and our partner consultants have been liaising with the developer’s consultant for a number of months to agree the discharge of planning conditions related to drainage infrastructure and ground stabilisation works. A detailed response was provided to the developer on Friday 21st June 2024 setting out the position and advising of the information required to progress the matter.The developer’s consultant has since proposed an engineering solution which requires the area in question to be surcharged and monitored for a period of 12 months, but has commenced these works without the necessary approvals from my department. As such, the conditions of the application cannot be discharged until this matter is resolved.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of applicants that presented to the Housing Executive as homeless, citing loss of private rented accommodation, in the last three years, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: The biannual Northern Ireland Homelessness Bulletin October 2023 – March 2024 contains information on a range of areas relating to homelessness and is available at https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-homelessness-bulletin-october-2023-march-2024   	Section 1 provides details on homelessness presentations, however as some of the information is not published in the format you have requested, I am unable to provide a fuller response.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any engagement he has had with Northern Ireland Companion Animal Welfare Group in relation to the development of legislation for the third-party sale of puppies and kittens; and (ii) the proposed timeline for implementation.
answer_text: My Department met with members of the Northern Ireland Companion Animal Welfare Group (NICAWG) in June of this year, and provided an update on the introduction of Lucy’s Law in Northern Ireland.I am planning to meet with key stakeholders in the months ahead, including the NICAWG, to help shape the policy for appropriately regulating third-party sales of puppies and kittens. Any proposal I am minded to introduce will of course be subject to public consultation.It remains my intention to deliver Lucy’s Law in Northern Ireland within the lifetime of this Assembly mandate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will consider establishing a centralised and publicly-accessible list of all registered and licensed breeders and sellers, in partnership with local councils.
answer_text: At present, there is no legal basis to collate and publish this information on a Northern Ireland wide basis. However, there may be scope, under the current legislation, for councils to modify the way they record and process licence applications from dog breeders and publish this information on a council-by-council basis. It is for councils to determine if this was feasible in the first instance.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they will commit to publishing their Department’s full response to the recommendations of the Commission on Flags, Identity, Culture and Tradition’s Final Report, as part their review of Together: Building A United Community.
answer_text: We are progressing the recommendations of the FICT report within the current review of the T:BUC Strategy.Officials are currently finalising a draft of the Review for our consideration and this will include an assessment of the recommendations made in the FICT Report.		Progressing the report through the T:BUC review will ensure the cross cutting and wide reaching impacts of the issues covered within FICT inform broader thinking on Good Relations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any further consideration that has been given to the mutualisation of NI Water; and (ii) whether his Department has assessed the potential benefits and challenges of implementing such a model.
answer_text: No further consideration has been given to the mutualisation of NI Water.  My Department has carried out considerable work on this issue.  Mutualisation leads to domestic water charges and would require the relinquishment of Executive control over the company.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Electric Vehicle Infrastructure Taskforce six point Action Plan.
answer_text: The Electric Vehicle (EV) Infrastructure Action Plan developed by the EV Infrastructure Task Force sets out six key actions to be delivered by members representing government, councils and key stakeholders:Future Proofing Electrical Capacity at key strategic sites along Key Transport Corridors – led by DfE.Review of Connection Costs – led by the Utility Regulator.The establishment of oversight group to ensure co-ordinated approach to EV infrastructure – led by DfI.Paying for Charge – led by Electricity Supply Board.Consumer Priorities – led by the Consumer Council NI; andEV Charging at Park and Ride Sites – led by DfI.The role of the Task-force is to consider EV infrastructure requirements with a focus on delivering a fit for purpose, modern EV charging network. The EV infrastructure landscape has changed considerably since the formation of the Task-Force, four of the six actions are now complete or being implemented. The remaining actions associated with the Park and Ride and future proofing electrical capacity actions have been amalgamated and the Task- Force will be considering how this should be developed taking account of future need in the private car and freight sectors and the impacts of a rapidly improving EV charging landscape.Thes actions have resulted in a 60% increase in the number of EV charge points in the North since the introduction of pay for charge and has created the commercial platform for expansion. The Vehicle Emissions Trading Schemes to be introduced, subject to Assembly approval, will provide further confidence for market stakeholders. Over the next few months, DfE and the Utility Regulator will be consulting on the socialisation of connection costs which subject to implementation, may also assist in meeting infrastructure costs associated with new charging infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement he has had with (i) the Minister for Infrastructure; and (ii) the Construction Employers Federation in the last two years in relation to the impact of NI Water capacity issues on social housing development.
answer_text: Below I have detailed my engagement with the Minister for Infrastructure and the Construction Employers Federation (CEF) in relation to the impact of NI Water capacity issues on social housing development since taking office in February 2024.I met with the Minister of Infrastructure on August 12th, 2024, when we discussed the issue of wastewater, including the budget shortfall as well as potential options around NI Water working in tandem with Housing Associations/developers to identify where capacity exists. On 5th March 2024 I accepted a request from the CEF to meet with them, they have yet to follow up on my acceptance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement he has had with the Construction Employers Federation in the last two years in relation to the impact of NI Water capacity issues on housing development.
answer_text: I met with the Construction Employers Federation (CEF) in April 2024 to discuss the impact of NI Water capacity issues on housing development.  I outlined the measures being taken to help alleviate the impact of wastewater issues and the Planning Improvement Programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 14438/22-27, whether his Department will consider an independent, expert-led review into the funding of NI Water.
answer_text: My Department had already carried out considerable work on this issue in 2019 and 2020 which I have asked my officials to update in collaboration with NI Water.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how his Department is integrating climate change considerations into its planning for future flood risks.
answer_text: My Department integrates climate change considerations into its planning for future flood risks by implementing a range of measures. These include the publication of flood maps which highlight the areas throughout the North that are prone to flooding for both the Present Day and Climate Change scenarios. These maps are available at this link: Flood Maps (NI) (arcgis.com).  Through my Department’s role as a Statutory Consultee in the planning process, advice is provided to Planning Authorities, for both present day and climate change flood risk scenarios, in line with Planning Policy. This advice, which is a material consideration for the planning authorities in their decision making process, is to ensure that new development, where possible, is kept out of flood risk areas and does not contribute to an increase in flood risk elsewhere.  In addition, any new flood alleviation schemes are designed in accordance with my Departments ‘Technical Flood Risk Guidance in relation to Allowances for Climate Change’. This ensures that there is an appropriate consideration of Climate Change in the design of infrastructure and future proofs public assets for the management of flood risk. Presently the Department is updating its climate change technical guidance in relation to flood risk. This may cause flood plain outlines to increase, with impacts on development land availability and the scale of future flood alleviation projects. As the Lead Government Department for the strategic co-ordination of the emergency response to flooding, emergency plans and processes are developed in conjunction with our multi-agency partners so that the response to flooding incidents, which may increase as a result of climate change, are as effective as they can be.  Through the work of the Regional Community Resilience Group (RCRG), my Department and other partners continue to provide support to communities at risk of flooding to help them adapt, prepare for, and respond to weather related emergencies which may occur more frequently due to climate change impacts

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of Suicide Liaison Officers employed in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The following provisions are provided by Trusts.BHSCTBelfast Trust has one Band 7 Bereavement Support officer. This post is responsible for the coordination of delivery of postvention services for family/individuals bereaved by suicide within the Belfast locality.NHSCTNorthern Trust have 2 bereaved by suicide support staff.SEHSCTThe South Eastern Trust is not commissioned to provide Bereaved by Suicide Family Liaison Officers.  The PHA contracts Action Mental Health to provide bereavement support in the SEHSCT area. This service is available to family and friends of the person who died by suicide.  They can also offer support to the person who found the person who has died if it is e.g. a member of the public.  The people who are offered support are identified through the SD1 form that the police complete. The Trust currently has a pilot project in place whereby families involved in the Serious Adverse Incident (SAI) have a member of staff not involved in the SAI available to them to provide support, help them identify questions they have about the SAI.  This is currently unfunded and an additional duty for two staff while the pilot is in place and being evaluated.SHSCTThe SHSCT employs 1 Band 7 Protect Life Co-ordinator in the Adult Community Services Directorate.The role is to ensure the effective and efficient co-ordination and implementation of evidence-based suicide prevention services and initiatives to support the implementation of the Protect Life 2 Regional Strategy for Suicide Prevention across the Southern area as well as Making Life Better, the strategic framework for public health. The post holder also provides postvention family support to those bereaved by suicide.WHSCTThere is one full time, Suicide Liaison Officer in post covering Derry City, Limavady & Strabane Council areas.  The other full time Suicide Liaison Officer covers the Omagh and Fermanagh Council areas.  This post is vacant since the beginning of September 2024, following the departure of the staff member.  However, the recruitment process is underway and the post will be advertised in the coming weeks.  Cover for the vacant post is currently in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for a funding update for the four discretionary Housing Improvement Grants administered by the Housing Executive.
answer_text: The discretionary grants administered by the Housing Executive are currently only awarded in exceptional circumstances and are subject to budget availability within the overall pot of home improvement grant funding provided to the Housing Executive (which also covers statutory grants).The indicative budget allocation for the 2024-25 financial year is £14.751m.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on (i) the funding; and (ii) timeframes for the delivery of phase 2 and phase 3 of the Fundamental Review of Allocations.
answer_text: The Housing Executive received a capital allocation of £137k from the Department for the Fundamental Review of Allocations within its opening 2024/25 budgets and the Housing Executive is currently progressing ‘at risk.’  Work has been ongoing. I gave my approval to Stage Two in June 2024 and my officials are now working with the Housing Executive on the early stages of Stage Three.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide a timeline for the appointment of an Irish Language Commissioner.
answer_text: The appointment of the Irish Language Commissioner, along with the Commissioner for the Ulster Scots and the Ulster British tradition, and the Members of the Office of Identity and Cultural Expression are essential to implement the provisions of the Identity and Language (NI) Act 2022, for which TEO is responsible.We are keen that these appointments are made as soon as possible. As previously advised, we have instructed our officials to prepare additional advice to enable the competitions to be launched. The competitions will be run in line with the Commissioner for Public Appointments guidelines.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the initiatives in place to support those who find themselves homeless upon their release from prison; and (ii) the engagement she has had with the Department for Communities and other stakeholders in relation to securing a more comprehensive support package to aid the reintegration of former prisoners into society.
answer_text: The Department for Communities and the Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE) have responsibility for assessing and meeting housing needs and working with communities to develop programmes to meet those needs.  However, in recognition of the particular needs of people leaving prison custody, the Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS) has put in place a number of measures to mitigate against the issue of homelessness for those leaving prison.  These include:an assessment of prisoner needs upon committal, which includes their housing arrangements, and enables staff to put suitable interventions in place, such as support from Housing Rights advocacy service; the introduction of NIHE Housing Advisors into Maghaberry Prison. This provides early intervention on housing needs and allows decisions on accommodation to take cognisance of other resettlement factors including health and employability; the use of ‘Approved Premises’ to enable appropriate support and supervision arrangements to be put in place for high-risk offenders who are subject to Public Protection Arrangements Northern Ireland (PPANI) requirements; andthe introduction of a partnership project between Hydebank Wood College and Belfast City Council Complex Lives, looking at how better information sharing at committal and release could support females most at risk of chronic homelessness.As I have regularly advised my Executive colleagues and members of the NI Assembly, reducing re-offending and supporting rehabilitation cannot be the responsibility of one agency alone and relies upon cross-executive collaboration and support. A specific example is the work of my Department with the Department for Communities in the delivery of the Inter-Departmental Homelessness Action Plan. The introduction of NIHE Housing Advisors and Department for Communities Work Coaches into the prison setting to enhance release planning arrangements are actions within this plan. My Department also works in partnership with a range of both statutory and voluntary and community Sector organisations to provide a comprehensive range of services and interventions to address offending behaviour and prepare those leaving prison for their effective transition and resettlement into the community. This is kept under review to ensure that services delivered support the diverse resettlement needs of the prison population.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether she will take steps to extend the Freedom of Information Act 2000 to cover housing associations that provide social housing.
answer_text: I currently have no plans to seek to amend the Freedom of Information Act 2000 to include Housing Associations in the list of named Public Authorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of children and young people on waiting lists for mental health services in the last 12 months, broken down by (i) average waiting time; and (ii) Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Children and Adolescent Mental Health Service (CAMHS) waiting time information is published on the Department’s website, broken down by time waiting at the end of each quarter and Health and Social Care Trust and can be accessed at-Children and adolescent mental health service (CAMHS) statistics | Department of Health (health-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) this year's budget allocation for retrofitting existing Housing Executive stock; (ii) future funding plans for the decarbonisation of Housing Executive properties; and (iii) how his Department will ensure that the Housing Executive meets its targets for reducing carbon emissions.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 15553/22-27, the information has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will explore the potential of reclassifying the possession of non-prescription Pregabalin with intent to supply, as a Class A offence.
answer_text: The policy and legal frameworks relating to the misuse of drugs, including the Misuse of Drugs Act 1971 and the Psychoactive Substances Act 2016, are reserved to the UK Government and any change to the current classification of controlled drugs would be for the Home Office and the Advisory Council on the Misuse of Drugs (ACMD) to consider.Pregabalin is a Class C drug.  The maximum sentence for possession of Pregabalin without a prescription is 2 years imprisonment, or a fine, or both (conviction on indictment), or 3 months imprisonment, or a fine, or both (summary conviction). The maximum sentence for intent to supply Pregabalin is 14 years imprisonment, or a fine, or both (conviction on indictment), or 3 months imprisonment, or a fine, or both (summary conviction). I have not been made aware of any plans within the Home Office to review the classification of drugs. Any change to the status quo would require the engagement of all four nations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will consider introducing Operation Snap as part of the Road Safety Strategy, which would allow members of the public to submit video evidence of potential driving offences to the PSNI.
answer_text: As part of the Road Safety Strategy Action Plan 2023/24 my Department made two Statutory Rules in April 2023 to enable careless driving offences to be disposed by way of a Fixed Penalty Notice.  Introducing a scheme such as Operation Snap would be an operational matter for the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the steps his Department will take to improve safety at the Coshquin estate and Buncrana Road junction, Derry.
answer_text: The busy Buncrana Road provides an important transport link in the city and the wider north west area to Donegal and I am aware that this can cause a challenge to road users particularly at junctions.I can confirm that my officials have inspected the junction of Buncrana Road and Benview Road and plan to renew the junction road makings and install new advance junction warning signs at this location.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the steps he has taken to seek approval from Treasury to give the Housing Executive greater ability to borrow and invest in housing stock.
answer_text: Engagement has taken place with HMT via Department of Finance on treatment of NIHE borrowing, focused on how existing budgeting rules might be applied to enable NIHE to access borrowing which does not score in Public Expenditure terms.  I have also met with a number of UK Ministers and asked for this issue to be prioritised.  I am actively pressing for a solution that does not require the reclassification or significant reform of NIHE, that will move it onto a sustainable footing to increase routine and planned investment, to deal with maintenance backlogs and enable a program of retrofit to improve energy efficiency in homes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department will (i) review the efficacy of the Empty Homes Strategy; and (ii) update this strategy.
answer_text: The Empty Homes Strategy and Action Plan was operational from 2013 to 2018. A review of the Strategy was completed and published on my Department’s website in 2021. The Review can be accessed at https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-empty-homes-strategy-and-action-plan-2013-%E2%80%93-2018I have no plans to review it a second time.While the Review recognised that the Strategy had limited success in addressing housing stress, as a result of this work we are now much better informed about the complexities of this issue and the reasons why so many properties appear to be vacant.My Department has developed a new Housing Supply Strategy which will include consideration of actions around housing led regeneration and the reuse of existing buildings to generate additional supply. I am currently finalising this Strategy and I intend to bring it to the Executive for its consideration shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the provision of adult ADHD services.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the total number of Housing Executive properties sold via the Right to Buy Scheme; (ii) the total revenue generated through these sales; and (iii) the amount of this revenue that was reinvested in Housing Executive housing stock, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Director of Finance, Audit and Assurance on behalf of the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Please see the tables below which outline (i) the total number of Housing Executive properties sold via the Right to Buy Scheme; (ii) the total revenue generated through these sales; and (iii) the amount of this revenue that was reinvested in Housing Executive housing stock, in the last five financial years by the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. 2019/202020/212021/222022/232023/24(i) Number of Properties sold under Right to Buy448239444554356  2019/202020/212021/222022/232023/24 £k's£k's£k's£k's£k's(ii) Net Revenue generated through the Right to Buy Scheme (Net of Discount and Expenses)(21,094)(12,000)(24,097)(31,778)(20,582) (iii) Total Amount reinvested into NIHE stock18,00712,00024,09721,84420,541”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly. Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the actions his Department is taking to address biodiversity loss.
answer_text: The draft Nature Recovery Strategy is intended to identify key actions and objectives required to meet local and national commitments under the UN’s Convention on Biological Diversity. It contains a number of new proposed policies which aim to address the many challenges affecting the natural environment.The strategy is cross cutting and will require Executive approval. It is hoped, subject to Ministers’ views, to have the draft strategy ready for public consultation later in the year.DAERA’s current Environmental Farming Scheme delivers a range of measures to support biodiversity such as creating field and pollinator margins, creation of new hedgerows, hedge laying, a range of tree planting measures including establishment of native woodland less than 5 hectares, woodland regeneration, planting native tree corridors and various riparian buffer options.  After six tranches of EFS there are some 76,300 hectares of land under agreement which are contributing to improved biodiversity.My officials are engaging closely with key farming and environmental organisations in the co-design of the Farming with Nature Package of my Department’s new Farm Support and Development Programme to facilitate ecosystem restoration and address biodiversity loss.The recently published Lough Neagh Report and Action Plan also outlines measures to reduce pollution and improve water quality. Pollution is a major driver of biodiversity loss in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any plans he has to address the shortage of affordable student accommodation, particularly in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: My officials continue to work closely with higher education institutions, student representatives and other departments on a range of key issues, including action to address the cost of living pressures facing students.  In relation to the Foyle constituency, the independent Magee Expansion Taskforce Interim Report, published on 11 September 2024, highlighted that expansion of the Magee Campus is dependent on provision of affordable student accommodation. The Report estimates that c.£275m, primarily from private investment, will be necessary to provide sufficient student accommodation. By setting up the Taskforce and providing funding for additional student places and capital works I have sent a clear signal about my intent to deliver on the commitment to 10,000 students at the Magee campus.  This certainty is crucial in generating much needed private sector investment.  Since the report of the Taskforce was published a planning application has been submitted for construction of a purpose-built student accomodation on the waterfront, conveniently located near to the campus. The Report notes that Ulster University has increased total available student bedspaces from 632 in 2022 to 831 in 2024.  Future plans from the University include expanding provision in Duncreggan Student Village.  My Department will continue to work with Ulster University and other partners to support the development of affordable student accommodation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline his engagement with NI Water and the Minister for Infrastructure regarding the discharge of one million tonnes of untreated sewage into the River Foyle.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline what action he is taking to improve the electric vehicle charging network.
answer_text: In the commercially led Electric Vehicle infrastructure market, my officials continue to engage with EV owners and provide advice to Charge Point Operators on operational matters. My Department is also leading an EV Infrastructure Task Force, bringing together representatives from government, consumers, energy providers, industry, and EV drivers to consider future charging needs including grid capacity and future commercial need. There are now more than 20 Charge Point Operators operating in the North. Latest statistics show a 60.5% growth in the number of publicly accessible charge points since April 2023 and the number of rapid charge points, which provide significantly faster opportunity to charge, has grown by over 300% in that same period.Local Councils have been able to avail of funding for the installation of charge points under the Office for Zero Emissions Vehicles (OZEV) ‘On Street Residential Charge Point Scheme’, which my Department has match funded. This will provide 127 charge points across those Council areas.My Department continues to work with colleagues in OZEV to promote the provision of a range of grants made available to support the rollout of additional EV charging infrastructure such as Workplace Charging Scheme for State Funded Education Institutions and with the Irish Government who are providing funding to Sports Clubs for the installation of charge points at their grounds, through the Shared Island Fund.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) when Agenda for Change staff will receive a pay rise for 2024-25; and (ii) whether his Department will consider a reduction of the working week in the health service to 36 hours.
answer_text: (i) The NHS Pay Review Body in its 37th report, has called for a consolidated 5.5% pay increase with effect from 1 April 2024 for all Agenda for Change staff, including those in Northern Ireland. I am already on record as being supportive, in principle, of the recommendations set out in the report. However, given the severe financial constraints placed upon my Department, it is not possible, at the current time, to make a final decision on the acceptance of the review body recommendations for this year.As I have stated publicly, our health service would be nothing without our hard-working workforce and I am committed to doing everything I can do to support them.  I will continue to make the case, to Executive colleagues, for additional financial allocations that would allow me to implement a pay award in line with the recommendations of the NHSPRB for 24/25.(ii) As part of the framework agreement reached to bring the most recent pay dispute to a close, the Department and Agenda for Change Trade Unions have committed to work together to explore the viability of a reduction of the working week to 36 hours without any detriment to pay.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the working targets for bodies of water in the Water Environment (Water Framework Directive) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2017; and (ii) when the latest River Basin Management Plan will be published.
answer_text: The primary target set by the Regulations is to achieve “Good ecological status” and “Good chemical status” for all surface water bodies (such as rivers, lakes and coastal waters). The targets for groundwater bodies are “Good Chemical Status” and “Good Quantitative Status”.Northern Ireland’s third cycle River Basin Management Plan has been prepared and consulted on; however it is still in draft form. I will need time to review it in the context of emerging information on water quality, including reports from: the Northern Ireland Audit Office; the Office for Environmental Protection; and the Lough Neagh Report.  While I am not yet in a position to provide a date for the publication of the final River Basin Management Plan, I am aware of the urgency of this work and aim to publish the final Plan as soon as possible.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he is aware of any social housing developments that have been delayed by ongoing issues with NI Water infrastructure in the Derry and Strabane area.
answer_text: The potential implications for the development of social housing due to infrastructural issues has been of concern, particularly given the increasing levels of housing stress and need and the risks to the achievement of target delivery in future years. Capacity restrictions of water/sewerage infrastructure are routinely highlighted to my Department, as an issue impacting on the delivery of social housing particularly in areas of high housing need.Whilst I haven’t been made aware of any site specific issues, I understand from the Housing Executive that 258 new social homes planned to start on the Springtown Road, by Apex Housing as part of the 24/25 SHDP (subject to budget availability and planning approval on the reserved matters) are subject to an ongoing Wastewater Impact Assessment to ascertain if a suitable solution can be agreed to address NI Water capacity issues. I understand that engagement is also on-going between NI Water and Apex Housing regarding plans to develop c1,500 new social homes over the next 3-5 years – c800 new homes on H2 lands (Buncrana Road) and c700 new homes on H1B lands (Upper Galliagh/Skeoge/Beragh Hill). Apex is already in contract (since March 2023) with a developer to deliver the first phase of 421 new homes on the H2 lands and construction is underway.
