question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of staff currently employed by the Education Authority who are in receipt of Universal Credit.
answer_text: I can confirm that neither the Department nor the Education Authority (EA) hold this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, following an additional funding allocation for social housing, for an estimate of how many social homes can be built with £20 million.
answer_text: The delivery of social housing remains a priority for me and this additional £20 million will be used to deliver much needed social housing. It will be for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE), as the commissioning body for the Social Housing Development Programme to determine how this additional budget may be best used to meet identified housing need and upon confirmation and consideration of budget will be able to determine how many additional homes can be started.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of staff currently employed in Health and Social Care who are in receipt of Universal Credit, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Universal Credit claimants are not required to tell their employer that they are on such benefits, therefore neither Health & Social Care employers nor the Department are able to provide this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he has engaged with Translink or local councils in relation to the installation of bee buzz stops, by planting bus shelters and other flat rooftops with pollinator-friendly plants.
answer_text: Translink’s recently published their Biodiversity Strategy and Action Plan which sets out an approach to deliver nature-positive operations across the public transport network by 2030. It is vital that we all continue to actively protect, conserve and enhance our natural assets by integrating biodiversity considerations into our decision making, whilst maintaining a focus on safety and performance. I therefore fully support Translink’s intention to enhance biodiversity assets and increase sustainability by integrating Green Infrastructure such as green roofs across the bus and rail network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 12817/22-27, to detail the total cost of this recent road safety advertising campaign.
answer_text: The total amount of sponsorship provided by the Northern Ireland Road Safety Partnership for road safety campaigns was £301,000.00.  This sponsorship was to address careless / inattentive driving and excess speeding for the roads or the conditions. The sponsorship was paid in the 2023/24 financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 13360/22-27, to detail why works have been delayed until 2027.
answer_text: The delay to the start of the delivery of the Coleraine to Derry phase 3 project came about by decisions made before I came into office.However, I fully recognise the need to address regional imbalance and when I assumed office in February 2024, I have prioritised this vital project and have ring-fenced £4·7m in my budget to ensure that the Coleraine to Derry phase 3 project proceeds as quickly as possible. Work is continuing to produce the 55,000 railway sleepers needed for the project, as well as the procurement of the technical and engineering teams. This will enable the main construction works to start in 2027.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an indicative timeframe for the introduction of a Baby Loss Certificate Scheme for parents who have lost a baby during pregnancy before 24 weeks; and (ii) to detail the engagement he has had with the Department of Finance on this scheme.
answer_text: My officials have been engaging with their counterparts in the Department of Health and Social Care in England as the new system is embedded there, with a view to understanding whether it might be possible to implement such a scheme in Northern Ireland, including any associated costs. Following a debate in the Assembly on 15 April regarding baby loss certificates and the paediatric pathology service, it has been agreed that my officials will work with their counterparts in the Department of Finance’s General Register Office to consider the introduction of a scheme in Northern Ireland.Discussions are taking place regularly between my Department and the General Register Office officials, as to how a baby loss certificate scheme may be developed. It is not possible to provide an indicative timeframe for the introduction of such a scheme; however, the ongoing engagement will provide my Department with potential pathways and lessons as to how a scheme could be developed for Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for a breakdown of the number of successful free school meals and uniform grants applicants who are (i) self-employed; and (ii) single parents, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that parents/carers are not asked to provide information about their employment status or whether they are a single parent as part of the application process for free school meals and uniform grants. The information requested is therefore not able to be provided.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 13062/22-27, to detail (i) the number of individuals who (a) are currently facing prosecution; (b) have been prosecuted for purchasing counterfeit weight loss drugs in the last three years; and (ii) whether his Department has considered utilising social media as a tool to raise awareness of the dangers around this issue.
answer_text: My Department is committed to taking all possible steps to stop illegal promotion, supply or misuse of medicines and, when appropriate, to alert the public about the dangers of using unauthorised and counterfeit medicines outside the regulated supply chain. Where evidence or information suggests unlawful practice is occurring or a breach of the Human Medicines Regulations is identified, my Department’s Medicines Regulatory Group (MRG) will consider a range of enforcement options proportionate to the circumstances to achieve compliance, including for example through the issuing of warning or advice, referral to professional bodies, or prosecution. My Department cannot disclose all information in respect of its live investigative work. However, I can confirm that during the period 2022 -2024 inclusive, MRG has conducted 18 investigations focusing on the unlawful possession, advertisement and/or supply of counterfeit or unlicensed weight-loss medicinal products. To date, one person has been successfully prosecuted and a further four prosecutions are pending.MRG has also issued 10 formal Advice & Warning letters, seized over 340 weight-loss medicinal products and reported two persons to their professional regulatory bodies.My Department has taken appropriate steps to alert the public about the dangers of using unauthorised and counterfeit medicines outside the regulated supply chain, including for example through the issue of statements and press releases. This includes the use of my Department’s official social media platforms.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the total cost of road marking and lining works, including refreshing existing provision, for each of the last ten years.
answer_text: Expenditure on road markings and lining works including refreshing existing provision has been provided in the table below from the inception of the Department in 2016-17.   Road Markings£’0002016-171,6432017-189122018-191,3982019-201,5842020-211,4852021-222,4742022-232,346 2023-24*1,949* 2023-24 figures represent provisional outturn, which is subject to final audit.The table above does not reflect expenditure on road markings in relation to capital schemes as such expenditure is part of overall scheme costs and cannot be separately identified.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 12747-22/27, how many of these overpayments were caused due to excess or a fluctuation in earnings, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The table below shows the total number of recoverable Carer's Allowance overpayments that were due to breaches of the weekly earnings limit, in each of the last five years.YearCarer’s Allowance Overpayments due to breaches in the weekly earnings limitValue £2019/208431,014,2272020/21521424,9652021/221,6652,552,0842022/231,5522,318,2642023/241,5452,947,827

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will consider introducing a financial support scheme for parents whose child has received a terminal diagnosis.
answer_text: I recognise that the unexpected costs for parents who have a sick child can add additional financial pressure on a family at an already difficult time.		There are existing schemes in place to help support children and their families in Northern Ireland who need access to healthcare. The Help with Healthcare Costs Scheme provides individuals and their dependants, primarily on low incomes, with financial help towards the cost of dental treatment, eyesight tests, glasses or contact lens, and travel costs for health and social care treatment on referral by a doctor or dentist.  The eligibility criteria are set out in legislation and certain benefits automatically passport on to the scheme.  In addition, should a child be sent outside of Northern Ireland to access specialist care and treatment, costs will be met for parents to travel with the child. 		Development of any additional support scheme would be dependent on additional recurrent funding being made available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether consideration has been given to increasing the earnings threshold criterion for Discretionary Support to ensure individuals, in emergency circumstances, can access financial support.
answer_text: The Discretionary Support income threshold is automatically increased annually in accordance with the annual uplift to the National Living Wage.It was increased by £2,386.80 to £26,769.60 from 1 April 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the total number of Employment and Support Allowance claimants who have been subjected to a six-week penalty for late payment of National Insurance contributions, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department does not hold the information requested.The decision to treat National Insurance contributions as late paid is made by HMRC and my Department has no authority to change this decision. The late payment decision can be appealed directly with HMRC and the 42 day waiting time reversed if the appeal is successful.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether further consideration has been given to restoring and expanding the timeframe for Discretionary Housing Payments.
answer_text: Currently there are no plans to remove the reinstated maximum payment period for Discretionary Housing Payments, which are intended to provide temporary assistance. The two-year maximum payment period for Discretionary Housing Payments was introduced to manage the budget and ensure the temporary assistance remains available to as many of those who need it. Any claimant whose DHP was stopped has a right to appeal this decision to the Housing Executive.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 7669/22-27, (i) whether the assessment of high level options has been reviewed; and (ii) for an update on the establishment of an Infrastructure Commission.
answer_text: The assessment of high-level options has not yet been reviewed.  Some further work is required on the ongoing SIB Review, the output from which will help to inform a revised paper on options for an Infrastructure Commission.  Decisions on the next steps will be a matter for the Executive in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 13360/22-27, to detail how the ring-fenced funding of £4.7 million in 2024-25 will be spent.
answer_text: The £4.7m will be spent progressing the project including site surveys, detailed design, and project management costs.  This will also include the purchase of railway sleepers in advance of the main construction work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in relation to the pay offer to school support staff, whether his Department plans to establish a phased payment, rather than the provision of a lump sum, to ensure staff in receipt of Universal Credit can benefit from any earned backdated payment.
answer_text: The final agreement between my Department, Management Side and the Trade Unions is a two-stage deal on which all the support staff unions have agreed to formally consult their members.  Subject to a positive collective response, Stage 1 of the proposed agreement, which involves staff being moved onto new pay scales with effect from 1 April 2024, should be paid by late autumn 2024. Stage 2 involves support staff receiving a gross non-consolidated payment planned for April 2025. Full details of the proposed agreement can be found at Pay related issues | Department of Education (education-ni.gov.uk).I am committed to ensuring that all money owed to staff as a result of the proposed agreement is paid as soon as possible. While I am sympathetic towards those staff in receipt of Universal Credit whose payments may be affected, unfortunately, as is normally the case with government pay settlements, it is not possible to arrange for payments to be staggered. If anyone on Universal Credit is affected, they should contact the Universal Credit Service Centre at Universal Credit Service Centre | nidirect

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps he will take to restore training for personal assistants and carers employed via Direct Payments, to carry out nursing and delegable tasks.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the standard response time for the Health and Safety Executive to inspect and assess sites after being notified of an imminent health and safety risk.
answer_text: HSENI is established under the Health and Safety at Work (NI) Order 1978 [The Order]. The Order is silent in respect of response times for the Health and Safety Executive (HSENI) to inspect and assess sites after being notified of an imminent or perceived health and safety risk.HSENI deploys a number of different approaches when making inquiries following reports of accidents or dangerous occurrences. These include telephone enquiries, gathering evidence by email, site visits, witness statements, interviews after caution etc. The decision on how to approach any situation will take account of a number of factors such as an assessment of the level of risk (e.g. where a fatality or very serious injury is likely this will be prioritised over issues which may result in minor injuries), the need for a site visit or whether the issue can be resolved by agreement. We also have to take into account the available resources etc.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for a breakdown of (i) the number of Direct Payment applications awaiting assessment; and (ii) the duration of waiting times, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Direct Payments are local Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT) payments for people who have been assessed as needing help from Social Services and would like to arrange their own care and support services instead of receiving them directly from the local Trust. Direct Payments may be implemented to support individuals following an assessment of need.  Northern and Southern Health and Social Care Trusts advise that currently there are no Direct Payment applications awaiting assessment and therefore no associated waiting times across all Programmes of Care.The Northern Trust advise the Trust do not undertake a specific Direct Payment assessment, however all assessments undertaken consider areas of need. Direct Payment may be an option considered to meet the assessed need of the service user, providing some flexibility and choice for the care required.  The Western Health and Social Care Trust advise that currently within Adult Learning Disability, Mental Health, Physical Disability and Older People Programmes of Care; there are no Direct Payment applications awaiting assessment and therefore no associated waiting times.In respect of Children’s Services, the Western Trust advise there are currently 246 families awaiting a Social Work assessment of need, which may lead to a Direct Payment application. Only upon completion of a Social Work assessment can the requirement for a Direct Payment be determined. As this process is in progress there cannot be a confirmed number identified for a Direct Payment of the 246 families currently being assessed. The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust advise that within Adult Mental Health, Learning Disability, Older People’s and Physical Disability Services, there are currently no Direct Payment applications awaiting assessment or service and therefore no associated waiting time.. The South Eastern Trust note however that on occasion, there can be waiting times associated with Direct Payments, relating to the timescale for set up and commencement of the Direct Payment, and this can vary from approximately 1 month to 6 months.  This can be, for example, due to waiting time for Access Northern Ireland checks, for set up of the bank account, payroll, insurance or the time taken to employ a Personal Assistant.In respect of Children’s Services, the South Eastern Trust note that Direct Payments are awarded on the basis of an assessment of need, which in Children’s Services is in line with the UNOCINI Assessment Process.  Currently South Eastern Trust advise there are 92 families awaiting a UNOCINI Initial Assessment, some of whom may meet the criteria for Direct Payments.  Should the assessment highlight the need for the provision of a Direct Payment, this will initiate a referral to the Direct Payments Panel which meets on a monthly basis and prompt payment will be made thereafter; within the parameters of the extent of the award.The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust advise that currently within Adult Mental Hea

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of (i) successful; and (ii) unsuccessful applications for Free School Meals and Uniform Grants for 2024-25, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: The Education Authority’s application portal for free school meals (FSM) and uniform grants (UG) opened for the 2024/25 school year on 18 June 2024.  The table below shows the successful applications for FSM and UG broken down by council area as at 5 July 2024.Council AreaNo. of ApplicationsFSMUGAntrim and Newtownabbey408405Ards and North Down385384Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon408397Belfast1,3861,347Causeway Coast and Glens314310Derry City and Strabane953909Fermanagh and Omagh177172Lisburn and Castlereagh249246Mid and East Antrim272267Mid Ulster210206Newry, Mourne and Down309307Total5,0714,950The number of unsuccessful applications is 784.  At this stage in the process, the Education Authority is not in a position to categorise the unsuccessful applications by local council areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) whether his Department assessed the impact of a reduced earnings threshold under Universal Credit on Free School Meal and Uniform Grant eligibility, prior to the migration process; and (ii) whether he will consider restoring the earnings threshold to £16,000 for Universal Credit claimants.
answer_text: The free school meals and uniform grant eligibility criteria were amended in September 2018 to introduce a new criterion that a pupil would be entitled to free school meals where the pupil or parents is in receipt of Universal Credit and has annual net household earnings not exceeding £14,000.  This criterion remained unchanged until my announcement of an interim rise in the threshold to £15,000 on 7 June 2024. This criterion is in addition to the criterion that continues to allow pupils to receive free school meals where a parent receives Child Tax Credit or Working Tax Credit and has an annual taxable income less than £16,190. The ongoing review of the eligibility criteria for free school meals and uniform grants has only sought to examine options to increase the numbers able to access these supports, including options that raise the net earnings threshold. As a result, no modelling has been undertaken on options that would reduce the net earnings threshold that applies to applications made by those in receipt of Universal Credit.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will engage with (i) impacted homeowners; and (ii) the Northern Ireland Federation of Housing Associations on the issue of defective blocks.
answer_text: I can advise that my department has (i) engaged with the only known private homeowner.(ii) My department continues to engage with the Northern Ireland Federation of Housing Associations (NIFHA) on a range of matters. However, in relation to defective blocks no engagement has taken place to date as there is no current evidence to suggest that defective blocks are a systemic issue across Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he has considered the introduction of a free breakfast club programme for primary schools.
answer_text: My Department provides the Extended Schools programme which helps schools serving our most disadvantaged communities to offer a range of provision for children and young people.  Each of the participating schools decide how to use their programme resources in line with their pupils’ needs.  Many choose to provide breakfast clubs.Schools that are not participating in the Extended Schools programme may also provide breakfast clubs which may be funded in full or in part through parental contributions.Given the challenging budget position for my Department in 2024/25, I cannot commit to additional expenditure on breakfast clubs at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will engage with community transport providers and the Department of Health to help secure more cost-effective travel solutions for patients.
answer_text: I have set up a cross departmental working group including representation from the Department of Health which will consider how community transport groups, the Departments, and other partners can work together to improve transport for those in our society who cannot access or use public transport, particularly the elderly, those with a disability and people who are at risk of social isolation.  I am requesting a meeting with the Minister for Health to explore how this work can be taken forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the planning application for the Arc21 incinerator in Mallusk.
answer_text: The necessary administrative processes are currently being undertaken including assessing consultation advice from the necessary interested bodies and public authorities.When all statutory processes are satisfactorily completed, a recommendation will be brought forward by my officials.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will commit to extending the 50 per cent discount period for a Penalty Charge Notice to help alleviate the financial burden facing individuals from low-income households.
answer_text: The Traffic Management Order (Northern Ireland) 2005 allows a Penalty Charge Notice (PCN) to be reduced if payment is received within 14 days. My Department has no plans to change the discount period.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans his Department has to increase the current rate of pay for (i) adult learning disability day care; and (ii) day opportunities providers.
answer_text: Buildings-based day care for people with learning disabilities are typically delivered by HSC Trusts with staff under Agenda for Change Terms. Community-based Day Opportunities are typically provided by the independent sector; therefore, my Department does not set the rate of pay.The current rate of pay in Adult Learning Disability Day Centres, depends on the band of the staff member. Most Health & Social Care HSC posts are ‘banded’ as part of Agenda of Change, depending on the tasks required and level of responsibility across a number of core and specific dimensions. Health and Social Care (HSC) members in Northern Ireland voted to accept a pay offer from the Department of Health for 2023/24. Payment of the 2023/24 pay award for all Agenda for Change HSC Staff was split with staff receiving the non-consolidated pay award of 5% in June. Payment of the non-consolidated award of £1505 for 2023/24 will follow in August.  Community-based Day Opportunities are funded by Trusts as ‘community & voluntary sector’ contracts. In March 2024, my Department announced a 3.11% uplift to these contracts to account for changes in the National Living Wage.  I recently approved a further uplift of 0.96% which effectively increased the 2024/25 interim uplift to be applied to 2023/24 contract rates, to a revised interim uplift of 4.07% in respect of these contracts from 1 April 2024.  It should be noted that this interim uplift for 2024/25 may need to be revisited in line with the element of the uplift which tracks the HSC’s Agenda for Change pay awards..My Department and HSC Trusts remain committed to expanding community-based Day Opportunities to provide people with learning disabilities with more meaningful day activities and greater choice and control throughout their lives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps he will take to ensure the Direct Payments process can provide for non-delegable tasks to allow for overnight respite care within Adult Learning Disability.
answer_text: Regionally, Direct Payments are offered by Health and Social Care Trusts in lieu of a social care package to enable a more personalised method of care provision and to allow people to live as independently as possible at home. Carers may also use Direct Payments to avail of overnight respite care to support them in their caring role.Policy on Direct Payments is specifically designed to be person centred. The needs of service users and carers are individually assessed and services or Direct Payments in lieu of direct service provision, provided in accordance with assessed need.Individual needs may change over time and these needs will also be different to those of others receiving a Direct Payment.In specific individual circumstances, if there is a particular complex need to be met and or a non-delegable task which falls outside of the routine scope of a social care service, then a formal delegation process and/or alternative service provision may need to be considered, by individual Trusts.My Department will continue to ensure that Direct Payments enable choice, control and flexibility for service users and carers in terms of how care and support is arranged and provided, to meet assessed need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the rationale for undertaking a public consultation before reinstating automatic passporting to the Help with Health Care scheme for Universal Credit claimants.
answer_text: The Travelling Expenses and Remission of Charges Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2004 do not make provision for the automatic passporting to the Help with Health Costs Scheme for Universal Credit claimants. Automatic passporting to the Help with Health Costs Scheme for eligible Universal Credit claimants will therefore require legislative changes to the aforementioned Regulations. The overall policy aim is to ensure that broadly the same number of people are eligible for help under the new arrangements as under the legacy benefits. However, due to the structural differences between Universal Credit and legacy benefits there may be some who will lose eligibility for help.As a result, it would not be acceptable to proceed to update the legislation without a short consultation that will outline the potential options that the Department is considering.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how much funding has been allocated to Phase 3 of the railway line between Coleraine and the North West.
To ask the Minister of Finance to outline what she is doing to support local businesses struggling with rising rates bills.
answer_text: This year alone my Department and the Executive has renewed the legislation for Small Business Rate Relief which provides £21.5 million of support to 29,000 businesses for 2024/25, providing automatic bill reductions of between 20% to 50% depending on a property’s valuation level, held the annual regional rate increase for businesses at the rate of inflation, and  made and laid legislation for Assembly debate on the restoration of the Back in Business and Rural ATM schemes to further support businesses.We also have our own generous support schemes for other sectors of the economy that are not available in England, Scotland or Wales such as Industrial Derating which in 2024/25 provides £75M of support to over 4,400 local manufacturers.In total in the last rating year over £247M of support was provided to non-domestic ratepayers here, with over 75% getting some form of support.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what consideration he has given to reviewing the welfare mitigation overpayment recovery.
answer_text: The recovery of overpayments of Welfare Supplementary Payments is currently operating in line with the relevant Regulations.  Work is currently ongoing to introduce a waiver process for Welfare Supplementary Payment overpayments, particularly those in respect of Social Sector Size Criteria and Benefit Cap mitigations where overpayments are often recovered directly from landlords. The recovery of overpayments is expected to be considered as part of the Statutory Review of Welfare Supplementary Payment schemes that is being taken forward by my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 12747/22-27, and in relation to these overpayments, to detail (i) how much money has been recouped; (ii) how many mandatory reconsiderations were requested; and (iii) how many mandatory reconsideration requests were successful, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The table below shows the total amount of Carers Allowance overpayment debt recovered in each of the last five years.YearTotal Value of Recovered Carers Allowance Debt (£)*2019/201,257,189.512020/21841,736.062021/221,493,545.042022/231,581,719.282023/241,590,926.13	*To note that this amount shows all Carers Allowance overpayments recovered in each year and includes overpayments identified prior to 2019/2020.(ii) and (iii)	My Department does not hold this information in the format requested.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will review the accessible parking bay application process, including reducing the length of the legislative process.
answer_text: While a new on-line application process for an accessible parking bay was introduced in March 2023 to make the application process less burdensome, I am keen to make this even quicker and have already asked my officials to consider if this is possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on his Department's work to extend the Welfare Supplementary Payment (Extension) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2022 to protect the mitigation beyond March 2025.
answer_text: My Department has a statutory obligation to produce a report on the operation of the existing Welfare Supplementary Payment schemes before 31 March 2025.The purpose of this report will be to assess the operation of each of the current mitigation payment schemes.  The report will include evidence of the effect of the schemes and an assessment of the scheduled cessation of some payments on 31 March 2025.The final report must include proposals, if there are any, to amend, revoke or introduce new legislation in respect of the schemes.  My officials are currently progressing work on this report.It will be for the Executive to consider and agree any future mitigation package to ensure that the limited resources available are used in the most effective way possible to provide support to those in need.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 11914/22-27, to detail (i) the works carried out in May 2022; (ii) the project expenditure to date; and (iii) the engagement his Department has had with NI Water in relation to this issue and the sewer capacity in the area.
answer_text: I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:(i) that the works carried out in May 2022 included:• Trial holes to identify services,• The excavation, laying/installation, and reinstatement of approximately- 78 m of 375 mm diameter Concrete combined sewer including three 1350 mm manholes- 26 m of 250 mm diameter twin uPVC combined sewers, • Connection to the existing combined sewer on Aberfoyle Crescent and Aberfoyle Crescent South,• Reinstatement works, and• All necessary testing of sewers, materials, etc.(ii) The final project cost for was £162k, which includes early contractor involvement, the main construction works and Consultants costs.(iii) My Department has engaged with NI Water on a number of occasions throughout the work at Aberfoyle Crescent.  Discussions between NI Water and DfI Roads in late 22/early 23 led to NI Water providing its survey information to DfI Roads to assist with managing the overland storm flow in the area.  DfI Roads has recently commenced work to connect replacement gullies to the new sewer which should reduce the likelihood of flooding at Aberfoyle Crescent.During the flooding incident in July 2022 the sewer was operating as designed and was not at full capacity during that storm.  For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the introduction of the Better Start Larger Families payment to offset the impact of the two-child limit, as recommended by the Welfare Mitigation Review published in 2022.
answer_text: Officials are currently finalising work to update the estimated costs for all of the recommendations coming out of the Independent Advisory Panel Report into the future of welfare mitigations, including mitigating the Universal Credit two-child limit.Once this work to refresh the estimated costs is complete, I will share further details with the Executive and the Committee for Communities.Any future mitigation package will need to be considered and agreed by the Executive and assessed in the climate of financial constraint with the requirement to ensure that budgets remain sustainable over the coming years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether parents from a low-income household whose child has received a terminal diagnosis can avail of the Hospital Travel Costs Scheme.
answer_text: The eligibility criteria for the Hospital Travel Costs Scheme is published on the NI Direct website:https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/help-health-costs A person is either automatically entitled to a refund help with the costs of travel for treatment on referral by a doctor or dentist if they are in receipt of certain benefits or they have to apply through the Low Income Scheme.  If a child under age 16 is the patient, it is the parent’s income that is taken into account when assessing entitlement to a refund. The Hospital Travel Costs Scheme does not make provision for help with costs incurred when visiting an inpatient.The Department for Communities (DfC) are responsible for the provisions within the Discretionary Support Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2016, which may provide for discretionary support being awarded in certain circumstances. It is not within my Department’s remit to provide further information on the provisions within these Regulations. Any further requests for information regarding Discretionary Support should be directed at DfC for response.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 13007-22/27 (i) whether she will request this data to establish how much money the Executive receives from revenue generated by the Soft Drinks Industry Levy; and (ii) why this information is not readily available to her Department.
answer_text: As set out in the Statement of Funding Policy, changes to the Executive’s Block Grant are generally provided through Barnett consequentials on changes to Whitehall department’s budgets. As such, the source of funding is not specified.As Barnett consequentials are unhypothecated it is for the Executive to determine how funding is to be spent, in line with local priorities. Therefore, there is no need to seek details of funding sources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department intends to increase the amount charged for Penalty Charge Notices.
answer_text: My Department currently has no plans to increase the Penalty Charge Notice Fee.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of patients who have availed of Direct Payments; and (ii) the total cost for this provision in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: This information is available in our latest Direct Payments Publication which can be found on the Department of Health website: Quarterly direct payments statistics March 2024 | Department of Health (health-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any engagement she has had with the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs in relation to implementing tougher sentences for perpetrators of animal cruelty.
answer_text: Policy responsibility for the welfare of animals sits with the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA). Any decision to increase the penalties available for animal cruelty offences under the Welfare of Animals (Northern Ireland) Act 2011 is, therefore, a matter for DAERA. However, I understand the DAERA Minister is undertaking stakeholder engagement on this issue and I am open to discussing with the DAERA Minister how best my Department can support his, if a policy decision is made by his Department to increase the penalties and fines attached to animal welfare offences.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the establishment of a baby loss certificate scheme for parents who have lost a baby during pregnancy before 24 weeks.
answer_text: I am keen to see this scheme taken forward as quickly as possible to give bereaved families the opportunity to have formal recognition of their loss.Discussions are taking place regularly between the Department of Health and General Register Office officials as to how the baby loss scheme work may be developed. My officials and those from the Department of Health have met with managers from the Department of Health and Social Care who deliver the Baby Loss Certificate scheme in England. A meeting has also taken place with National Records of Scotland officials to investigate how the Scottish scheme operates.This ongoing engagement will provide important lessons for the development of a scheme here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the establishment of a Task and Finish group to evaluate the Direct Payments system; (ii) to detail the steps being taken to assist families in navigating this scheme; and (iii) for his assessment of its accessibility for clients.
answer_text: (i) My Department is finalising a 10-year Strategic Plan and an initial 3-year Delivery Plan to reform adult social care.  The Plans are built around six strategic priorities, make a number of strategic commitments and include an ambitious range of actions through to 2028.  They include a commitment to embed and mainstream Self-Directed Support options in line with the recommendations of the Power to People Report published in 2017.  As part of that process of reform, a Managed Budgets Pilot will take place across three HSC Trusts, with two Trusts piloting other elements of Self-Directed Support. The pilot is intended to run for six months. (ii) HSC Trusts continue to promote and support the use and uptake of Direct Payment as a means of meeting an individual’s needs in a more flexible and innovative manner, with the aim of giving individuals greater choice over the care they receive.  Guidance covering the provision and receipt of Direct Payments for all HSC Trusts can be accessed via: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/direct-payments-guidance. The Centre for Independent Living (CILNI) can also provide advice to individuals on Direct Payments, including navigating the system.  CILNI’s contact details can be found at: https://cilni.org/contact-us/. (iii) In relation to the accessibility of Direct Payments, if an individual requests a Direct Payment and the HSC Trust is content that their assessed eligible needs can be met via the provision of a Direct Payment, the payment is approved and issued as soon as all conditions of the agreed arrangement are met.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 12490/22-27, to detail (i) the total number of short break admissions to Rosebud respite facility; and (ii) the number of patients who have availed of this service, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: (i) My department officials have made direct contact with the Western Health and Social Care Trust, and have been advised as follows;Rosebud Cottage (23a Racecourse Road) opened for its first Short Break admissions at the end of June 2021. However, in November 2021 Rosebud Cottage had to temporarily close as the buildings Statement of Purpose and name changed to Heather House.The WHSCT provided ‘Outreach Support’ and also Avalon House Short Break Unit were available to families at times when there was no access to Rosebud Cottage Short Breaks.Rosebud reopened and commenced providing Short Breaks in August 2022.Numbers of short breaks admissions to Rosebud Cottage during the previous 5-year period are as follows:Dates Numbers of admissions29th June 2020 -30th June 20211421st July 2021 – 30th November 202110311th August 2022- 30th June 20232091st July 2023 – 21st June 2024242(ii) Number of Children and Young People who have availed of a service from Rosebud Short Break Unit during the previous 5-year period is outlined below:Dates Numbers of children and young people29th June 2020 -30th June 2021311st July 2021 – 30th November 20213611th August 2022- 30th June 2023571st July 2023 – 21st June 202457

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what additional legislative or policy actions are being considered to improve protections for public transport staff, including taxi drivers.
answer_text: With regard to legislation to protect public transport staff, a number of offences against the person already exist, with maximum sentences ranging from 6 months at the lower end of the scale up to a potential life sentence for the offence of causing grievous bodily harm with intent.These offences apply to all victims, regardless of their occupation. However, I have been shocked by reports of aggression being displayed against people who serve the public in a range of occupations, including transport workers and taxi drivers.To recognise this increased risk the Department’s proposed Sentencing Bill will include a new offence of assaulting a person who is engaged in providing a public service, performing a public duty or providing services to the public.  The new offence will have an increased maximum sentence of 12 months’ imprisonment in the magistrates’ courts and a two-year maximum if tried on indictment.  In more serious cases the fact that the victim was such a worker will become a statutory aggravating factor.   Policy in respect of public transport and transport workers is a matter for the Department for Infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the steps his Department is taking to strengthen statutory protections for transport workers; and (ii) the engagement he has had with the Minister of Justice on this matter.
answer_text: The safety of our public transport workers and passengers is vitally important. Our public transport workers deserve to feel safe doing their job and should not be subjected to attacks, either verbal or physical. Translink continues to engage on a regular basis with the PSNI at a senior level to ensure the continued safety for all their customers, staff and passenger services.  General workers' rights legislation is a matter for the Department for Economy.In Autumn 2023, Translink’s Chief Executive wrote to both the Director of Public Prosecutions (PPS) and the Lady Chief Justice highlighting the significant impact of assaults on Translink workers. The letters urged that the PPS and judiciary fully consider the impact of assaults on Translink staff and their family/colleagues when assessing the appropriate treatment of perpetrators.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 3645/22-27, for an update on the expansion of the Hospital Travel Costs Scheme to include provision for parents of children regularly travelling for hospital-based treatments.
answer_text: My Department has commenced a review of the Help with Health Costs and the Hospital Travel Costs schemes.  This review is primarily to take forward the necessary legislative amendments due the migration of means-tested benefits to Universal Credit by the Department of Communities (DfC).  The focus of the review is to reinstate automatic passporting for those eligible to receive financial help towards the cost of dental treatment, eyesight tests, glasses or contact lens, and travel costs for HSC treatment on referral by a doctor or dentist.  The commencement of a wider review of the provisions within these Regulations and associated HwHC scheme will be considered once this urgent work is completed and in line with available resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of children currently residing in temporary and emergency accommodation, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: The biannual Northern Ireland Homelessness Bulletin October 2023 – March 2024, published on 13 June, contains information on a range of areas relating to homelessness and is available at https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-homelessness-bulletin-october-2023-march-2024. Please refer to Section 3 in the accompanying tables for details on Children in temporary accommodation. Table 3.3 details children in temporary accommodation by type of accommodation, however we do not hold information in the format you have requested.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) whether his Department has carried out scoping work to determine the impact of the increased Administrative Earnings Threshold for Universal Credit on claimants in Northern Ireland; and (ii) what engagement he has had with the Minister for the Economy on its wider impact on the local economy.
answer_text: My Department assessed the impact of the increased Administrative Earnings Threshold as part of the section 75 equality screening exercise for the Universal Credit (Administrative Earnings Threshold) (Amendment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2024. I have not engaged with the Minister for the Economy on the impact of this specific policy on the wider local economy. However, it is a key principle of Universal Credit that claimants who are able to work should be supported into work and if in work supported to increase their earnings. The increase in the Administrative Earnings Threshold will enhance this support, while maintaining the overall approach of Universal Credit Work Coaches tailoring requirements to the specific circumstances of individual claimants.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when will he answer AQW 12490/22-27.

To ask the Minister of Health whether Health and Social Care Agenda for Change staff who are due to receive a 5 per cent pay rise and a one-off non-consolidated payment, and are also in receipt of Universal Credit, can request a phased payment similar to the provision made for the special recognition payment.
answer_text: My Department asked Regional Payroll within the Business Services Organisation (BSO) to work with Trust HR colleagues to formulate an implementation plan for all outstanding pay awards. Following their discussions over what was feasible, the resultant solution of splitting consolidated and non-consolidated payments was acknowledged as the best way of ensuring all staff would at least receive their substantive pay award in the June pay run. Non-consolidated payments will follow in August. The alternative to this would have been to delay implementation of all elements of the awards, for all staff, until September.

answer_text: Information on the number of cancelled elective surgeries is not held centrally. Each of the five Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) was, therefore, asked to provide a response.South Eastern HSCT’s response is provided overleaf. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to provide further details on the £97 million allocation made by his Department to Translink to progress the Coleraine to Derry Phase 3 project; and (ii) to detail how the funding (a) has been spent; and (b) will be spent.
answer_text: The draft budget includes ring fenced funding of £4.7m in 2024/25, which will allow Translink to progress the project as planned and seeks to ensure investment in public transport cross the entire region. My Department approved the business case for Phase 3 of the Derry to Coleraine project in November 2022 and issued a letter of offer for £97.7m to take forward this important project for the Northwest.Some £5.3m has been spent to progress the project to this stage. This has included the production of the required feasibility studies, business case, design of the track improvements and preparation for the procurement phase of the project. It has also included the construction of railway sleepers in advance of the main works.  Construction work is scheduled to be carried out in two phases:February – May 2027 for physical intervention work on 6 miles of track.April – June 2028 to facilitate ballast cleaning of 14 miles of track.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what his Department is doing to address vaping in schools.
answer_text: The issue of vaping is primarily a public health matter and in certain circumstances, a criminal justice matter. As such, my Department will follow appropriate guidance from the Department of Health / Public Health Agency (PHA) and Department of Justice/PSNI on this issue.    All schools are required to have a drugs policy and the Council for Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment has published Drugs Guidance for Schools in Northern Ireland.  The guidance was developed in consultation with the PHA and South-Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.Education will continue to address issues such as this as part of the statutory curriculum.  At post primary level (Key Stage 3), schools are required to provide learning opportunities for pupils to investigate the effects on the body of legal and illegal substances and the risks and consequences of their misuse. Schools have the flexibility to work directly with the PHA, PSNI and other organisations to provide learning opportunities in the delivery of these aspects of the curriculum.Furthermore, there is a statutory duty on schools to both promote positive behaviour and have relevant policies that set out the positive behaviour requirements of pupils.  A school’s safeguarding and child protection policy should also link to their drugs policy and include the requirement to notify the PSNI in relation to an incidents relating to illegal substances.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission for an update on efforts to reduce the carbon footprint of Parliament Buildings.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they discussed funding for Casement Park at the inaugural meeting of the East West Council, in March 2024.
To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will engage with the Minister of Health to introduce specific dental hygienist training courses.
answer_text: The Department works with a wide range of employers and stakeholders across sectors to inform the development and curriculum of apprenticeships and associated vocational education provision. This includes a Health and Social Care Sectoral Partnership. The group has been focusing on a new Health and Social Care Framework and in the new academic year will consider a Level 3 Dental Nursing Framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 5809/22-27, for an update on increasing funding for the Rates Support Grant, particularly for less well-off local council areas.
answer_text: The funding available for my Department was set within the context of a constrained financial position for 2024/25 and considered the overall Departmental budget position against competing priorities. The Rates Support Grant opening budget for 2024/25 has been set at £3.124m, which is a reduction of £1.8m on the previous financial year. I have launched an Equality Impact Assessment consultation on my initial decisions on my Department’s 2024-25 budget. The consultation will run from 11 June until 3 September 2024, and I have asked for views on the allocation of funds for the financial year 2024-25.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for a breakdown of (i) the number of social security appeals lodged; and (ii) the number of those appeals that were successful, in each year since 2017.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 12081/22-27, to detail (i) the total budget allocation for this project for 2024-25; and (ii) the total projected cost for the Coleraine to Derry Phase 3 project.
answer_text: The draft budget includes ring fenced funding of £4.7m in 2024/25, which will allow Translink to progress the project as planned and seeks to ensure investment in public transport cross the entire region. My Department approved the business case for Phase 3 of the Derry to Coleraine project in November 2022 and issued a letter of offer for £97.7m to take forward this important project for the Northwest.Some £5.3m has been spent to progress the project to this stage. This has included the production of the required feasibility studies, business case, design of the track improvements and preparation for the procurement phase of the project. It has also included the construction of railway sleepers in advance of the main works.  Construction work is scheduled to be carried out in two phases:February – May 2027 for physical intervention work on 6 miles of track.April – June 2028 to facilitate ballast cleaning of 14 miles of track.
