question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 12489/22-27, (i) whether a survey took place in autumn 2023; (ii) to detail the dates of that survey; and (iii) to detail which time periods this survey was conducted.
answer_text: Automatic 24 hour traffic counts were carried out on the B118 between Wednesday 8 and Tuesday 14 November 2023 inclusive.A junction turning count was carried out at the B118 Tamnaherin Road / Brockagh Road junction on Wednesday 8 November 2023 between 7am and 7 pm.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, in relation to the recent recruitment competition for Administrative Officer in the Northern Ireland Civil Service, to detail the number of (i) positions available; (ii) positions that have been filled; (iii) individuals that were interviewed; and (iv) complaints lodged in relation to the interview process.
answer_text: There are currently 1060 Alternative Working Pattern Administrative Officer vacancies declared within the Department for Communities to be filled from this competition. As a result of the recent AO competition 1207 candidates were successful and letters have been issued.  On a phased basis, successful candidates will receive confirmation of start dates with the first cohort commencing positions in June / July 2024. Remaining candidates have been placed on a merit list and will be offered posts over the coming months.3315 applicants were invited to complete the prerecorded interview. Of these, 810 did not attend. The interviews were completed during a 1 week period (23 February – 1 March 2024) using modern technology that allowed candidates to complete their interview at a time of their choice during the interview period. 140 complaints have been received relating to the prerecorded interview which NICS HR have been asked to investigate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an update on the Housing Executive Cavity Wall Insulation Action Plan; and (ii) to detail the number of properties that have benefited from the scheme in each of the last five years, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Due to the timescale required to put in place both the new extraction/installation process and a procurement process to secure suitable contracts to deliver the works, the planned CWI remediation/replacement programme has been slightly delayed but will begin this year with four schemes totalling some 300 homes and will be followed by 3,000 homes per annum in each of the next three years. Delivery of a full remediation/replacement programme across all NIHE cavity wall constructed stock will be subject to the necessary funding becoming available.(ii)  However, although this standalone CWI Programme arising out of the CWI Action Plan is only commencing this year, NIHE has already been addressing CWI in a small number of properties that were in multi-element improvement, thermal improvement or structural improvement schemes and where action on CWI was required as part of the package of necessary works.  The table below sets out the number of properties by year and Council area in the last five years.	CouncilDwellingsYearFermanagh & Omagh102021/22Belfast City362022/23Fermanagh & Omagh132022/23Causeway Coast & Glens52022/23Causeway Coast & Glens202023/24Mid & East Antrim1192023/24This has continued with a number of similar schemes involving some 900 homes on site or planned to start this year that include an element of Cavity Wall Insulation remediation or replacement.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans in place to (i) establish free and confidential drug testing services for dependent users of drugs, including heroin; and (ii) provide drugs amnesty bins.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they will include a commitment in the Programme for Government to meet social housing new build targets of 2,000 homes in 2024-25, including a sufficient budget allocation to meet this commitment.
answer_text: The Programme for Government is currently under development. The finalised Programme requires agreement from Ministers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) whether she is aware of concerns raised around the recent interview process for an Administrative Officer position in the Northern Ireland Civil Service; (ii) to detail any divergence from previous Administrative Officer processes; and (iii) to detail the steps being taken to rectify this situation.
answer_text: The previous recruitment process for Administrative Officers has been aptitude tests and interviews. For this competition the prerecorded interview format was used as utilising modern technology allows candidates to complete their interview at a time of their choice during the interview period. It also speeds up the interview process and reduces costs. The platform has a high level of accessibility to allow for reasonable adjustments making the interview process more accessible to a wider range of people with disabilities.A number of candidates have reported some concerns regarding the prerecorded interview process for an Administrative Officer competition being run for the Department for Communities for their Department of Work and Pensions (DWP) expansion programme.  Any issues raised by candidates have been investigated by NICS HR throughout the recruitment process. Going forward, NICS HR are working closely with the interview platform provider to examine where improvements can be made throughout the prerecorded interview stage to enhance guidance and make advice to candidates more explicit to reduce issues being reported during future competitions.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the timeframe for the completion of a Programme for Government, inclusive of a stand-alone outcome for housing.
answer_text: Work on the Programme for Government continues at pace. We have previously spoken about a number of priorities including: Childcare, Reducing Hospital Waiting Lists, Tackling Violence against women and girls, Special Educational Needs, Lough Neagh, Housing, and Developing a globally competitive economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 6642/22-27, whether the target of 2,000 new build starts can be achieved within his Department's current capital budget allocation.
answer_text: The delivery of social housing remains a priority for me.  My Department will continue to spend most of our capital budget on the delivery of new social homes.In my response to AQW 66422/22-27 I advised that subject to an appropriate budget being made available a target of 2,000 new starts would be set for 2024/25.The funding available will severely constrain the number of new starts possible this year. NIHE, as the commissioning body for the Social Housing Development Programme, will now consider how the budget may best be used to meet identified housing need and will need to consider the programme in more detail before any figure for starts in 2024/25 can be confirmed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the Department for Work and Pensions proposed changes to Personal Independence Payments, including the cessation of cash payments to claimants.
answer_text: My Department is currently engaging with the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) on its Green Paper consultation exercise which is looking at whether the UK Government should make fundamental changes to how it supports disabled people and people with health conditions, and whether the system is delivering the right support to people most in need. Northern Ireland’s social security policy and legislation, including that for Personal Independence Payment (PIP), generally operates in parity with that in place under DWP in Great Britain. It is important to note that at this stage options are being consulted on and no decisions have yet been taken in relation to any future changes to PIP.  In Northern Ireland, any reform of the benefit will be subject, as necessary, to the appropriate impact assessment considerations, as well as the relevant Ministerial, Executive and Assembly approvals.Should local stakeholders wish to give their views on the high level proposals, they can do so via  this link: https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/consultation-modernising-support.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an update on the Housing Supply Strategy; and (ii) whether the Department has re-evaluated its aim to deliver 100,000 homes within 15 years.
answer_text: i) I am currently reviewing the draft Housing Supply Strategy to ensure that it remains accurate and aligns with current policy. This needs to be an Executive Strategy so I have written to Ministerial colleagues in the Departments of Finance, Health, Infrastructure and the Economy who have key strategic remits on issues affecting housing, to seek their commitment to the Strategy approach. Following this, I intend to bring a final strategy to the Executive for its consideration as soon as possible.ii) The indicative baseline of ‘100,000 homes’ outlined in the draft Strategy is intended as a long-term ambition over a 15-year period to meet our housing needs and demands. It is intended that the final strategy will be regularly reviewed throughout its lifetime to ensure it can respond to any changing need and demand. Ultimately, meeting any ambition will require the Executive to prioritise housing over the long-term and ensure this is matched with sufficient funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of (i) the current levels of child poverty; and (ii) whether the delay in enacting an Anti-Poverty Strategy has contributed to these levels.
answer_text: The Poverty and Income Inequality report published by my Department in March 2024 shows a rise across some indicators relating to the number of children living in poverty in 2022/23. This is a matter of concern and, although my Department is leading on the development of the Executive’s Anti-Poverty Strategy, in order to deal with this effectively it will be necessary for the Executive to act together. An Anti-Poverty Strategy which will also address Child poverty is likely to have a lifespan of ten years. Given that this will impact on a generation of our population, we need to make sure that it includes the right priorities, the right actions and the right interventions. Given the many competing priorities we are facing, it is essential that we focus our efforts on the areas that will yield the greatest benefits.While there is a range of work that my Department and others are currently taking forward to supporting the most vulnerable in our society, the Anti-Poverty Strategy will aim to address the root causes of poverty and the impacts of socio-economic disadvantage and inequality and set out the NI Executive’s commitment to a joined up, long term approach to addressing poverty.Final decisions on the content and implementation of the Anti-Poverty Strategy will be subject to Executive agreement as it is an Executive Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the provision of (a) Tier 2; and (b) Tier 3 obesity services; and (ii) whether his Department has explored cross-border collaboration to ensure patients can access specialist support in this area.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency invests in a number of obesity prevention programmes and continues to work with a range of statutory and non-statutory partners to promote activities that will encourage healthy lifestyles that contribute to obesity prevention.  Examples of these include the Early Years Obesity Prevention Programme (targeting children aged 0-5 and their families), the Physical Activity Referral Scheme (12-week structured programme for people with a BMI between 25 and 40), Physical activity training programmes (commissioned from Health and Social Care Trusts), and Weigh to a Healthy Pregnancy (for pregnant women with a BMI over 38).A public consultation was launched on 24 November 2023 seeking views on the potential establishment of a prototype Regional Obesity Management Service (ROMS) in Northern Ireland. Such a service would provide a means for people living with obesity in NI to access tier 3 and 4 obesity specialist treatment and support which is not currently available to them.  The consultation closed on 1 March 2024, and I am currently considering the analysis of the responses received.  Decisions regarding future development of this service will need to be made on the basis of the evidence presented, will be subject to availability of resources and in line with competing priorities.  Alongside this, my Department will continue to consider opportunities for cross border collaboration in this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps he is taking to address the crisis in dental care, including (i) ensuring all patients can access subsidised treatment under the health service arrangement; and (ii) ensuring NHS patients can register with a dentist.
answer_text: In March 2024, a 6% pay uplift for General Dental Services for 2023/24, in line with recommendations contained in the 51st Doctors and Dentists Review Board report, was announced. This uplift comes with a 7.3% uplift to non-staff expenses for 2023/24, with a 3% recurrent award in future years.In March 2024 the Department also announced a £9.2m investment package for General Dental Services to include:£1m for newly registered child patients through the reestablishment of the Enhanced Child Examination Scheme. This scheme provides children aged 0-10 who have not been registered with a dentist with an examination, individualised oral health advice and age-specific fluoride application to teeth to assist with preventing dental decay;£4.3m to fund a 30% enhancement to fees paid to dentists for health service fillings, extractions and root canal treatment for 2024/25, to support public access to priority treatments; and£3.9m for the treatment of high priority unregistered patients through a Dental Access Scheme.Until the Dental Access Scheme is implemented, a pilot scheme will continue to run to provide appointments to unregistered patients who require emergency dental treatment during the working week. If a patient is not registered with a dentist, requires emergency dental treatment, and has been unable to access an appointment, they can contact their local Strategic Planning and Performance Group office for advice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps his Department has taken to (i) address the purchase of counterfeit Ozempic weight loss drugs; and (ii) raise awareness around the risk these counterfeit drugs pose.
answer_text: The risk to public safety posed by the unlawful distribution and misuse of unauthorised medicinal products in Northern Ireland is an issue which is taken very seriously by my Department.Where such issues are identified my Department’s Medicines Regulatory Group (MRG) will investigate the matter for suspected breaches of medicines legislation. MRG strives to ensure compliance with applicable laws and guidance and where evidence or information suggests unlawful practice is occurring or a breach of the Regulations is identified, robust enforcement actions are taken. MRG will consider a range of enforcement options proportionate to the circumstances to achieve compliance including for example through the issuing of warning or advice, referral to professional bodies, or prosecution. My Department is committed to taking all possible steps to stop the illegal promotion, supply or misuse of medicines, and when appropriate, has taken steps to alert the public about the dangers of using unauthorised and counterfeit medicines outside the regulated supply chain for example through the issue of statements and press releases. MRG officials remain vigilant and continue to work with key partner agencies to monitor the illicit marketplace and to take effective action where this is necessary to ensure public safety in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any measures being taken to ensure effective pain management is offered to patients undergoing invasive gynecological procedures; and (ii) whether there are plans to review current pain relief protocols in this area.
answer_text: There are a range of Gynaecological procedures that women may require depending on the symptoms that she is experiencing.The pain management provided following a procedure will depend on the symptoms being experienced, the procedure performed and a woman’s own request for pain relief and how she would like the pain medication administered ( tablet solid or dissolving, injection, infusion, patch).  Every woman will have a different pain threshold and this is considered when undergoing a procedure and when requesting pain relief.Any change in pain relief protocols will be considered within the recently established Regional Gynaecology Forum which has been established by the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) following the publication of Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT). The Regional Gynaecology Forum will have representatives from Trusts, PHA and GPs and has been established to oversee ongoing service improvement in gynaecology services and the implementation of same at a local and regional level.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 10878/22-27, to detail the number of non-standard accommodation placements made to (i) Belfast City Council area; and (ii) Derry City and Strabane District Council area, in each year since 2020.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive and I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“As per AQW 10878, the Housing Executive does not routinely report placements by the area to which they are made. The addresses for such placements are in ‘free text’ fields which contain the address of each respective placement and to facilitate this request the Housing Executive would have to allocate each of the placements from 2020/21, 2021/22 and 2022/23 to a council area with a review of the ‘free text’ fields for each non-standard placement. AQW 11649 details the number of placements in each respective financial year by the area from which the placement was made as per the Housing Executive’s routine reporting of non-standard placements by council area.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly. Making direct contact with the Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 12619/22-27, to detail the number of (i) temporary and agency staff employed; and (ii) staff vacancies, in each of the last five years, broken down by Land and Property Service office.
answer_text: The total number of temporary and agency staff employed; and staff vacancies in each LPS office for the past five years (2019/20 to 2023/24) are provided below.  31/03/202031/03/202131/03/202231/03/202331/03/2024BuildingVacanciesTemporary and agency staffVacanciesTemporary and agency staffVacanciesTemporary and agency staffVacanciesTemporary and agency staffVacanciesTemporary and agency staffAcademy House, Ballymena2101727080Boaz House, Omagh62209273122Lanyon Plaza, Belfast177351824916111816811117786Marlborough House, Craigavon62511325060Waterside House, Derry71331148160Total198411925420112819511520988The number of vacancies provided in this response as at 31/03/2024, is 209. NICS HR recently submitted AQW 12618/22-27 where the response stated NICS HR holds a caseload of 125 vacancies for LPS as at 30/4/24. That response explained that NICSHR only hold details on those vacancies where a request is referred to NICSHR to initiate the process to fill the post. However, 209 is the total number of vacancies held by LPS which includes those notified to NICS HR and those which have not yet been referred.LPS reviews the vacancies on a regular basis to seek to prioritise recruitment considering priority activities and allocated financial resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement he has had with the Irish government in relation to jointly funding the A2 Buncrana Road dualling scheme.
answer_text: Major Road schemes are an important part of the work that is delivered by my Department as we seek to reduce journey times, increase reliability, and improve road safety. The A2 Buncrana Road scheme is critical to providing a key link in the North West and to County Donegal.Work on the A2 Buncrana Road scheme was paused through a decision taken under the NI (Executive Formation etc) Act 2022, to allow appropriate alignment with the emerging transport plans.The A2 Buncrana Road scheme will be considered as part of the North West Transport Plan, which will be subject to a public consultation process.I am fully supportive of increased cross-border collaboration and will continue to engage with the Irish Government to ensure that transport linkages are strengthened. My Department will play its part in taking forward the many projects that are planned and in doing so we will contribute to the development of the region.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 5523/22-27, for an update on the H30 upgrade to the Caw Roundabout.
answer_text: Officials in my department continue to engage with the Developer, South Bank Square on this issue and most recently met them on 15th May.  At this meeting, proposals for the infrastructure mitigations necessary as part of the site’s planning permission were discussed, including the upgrade of Caw Roundabout.The department received additional transport data from the developer on 29 May which is currently under consideration.A further meeting is planned with South Bank Square in mid-June.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the amount of money received by way of Barnett consequentials from revenue generated by the Soft Drinks Industry Levy in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Executive does not receive a specific allocation from the Soft Drinks Industry Levy. Rather it receives a Barnett consequential on any changes in funding provided to Whitehall departments. While some of these changes may be funded by the Levy, my department is not informed of how much the Executive receives as a result of the Levy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 10222/22-27, to detail the estimated total cost of the 41,001 rent arrears.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows.“Please see the below table, which details the current Tenant Rent/Rates Arrears as at 31st March 2024:Financial Year EndTotal Number in ArrearTotal Value of ArrearsNumbers with True Arrear*Value of True ArrearsNumber in arrears due to technical payments**Value of Technical Arrears2023/2441,001£16,675,01024,920£14,024,83716,081£2,650,173* This is the number of tenants without technical arrears (i.e. those who owe true arrears).** This is the number of tenants with technical arrears. Technical arrears are Welfare Reform related payments which are paid 4 weekly or monthly in arrears (i.e. Universal Credit, Rate Rebate, and Social Sector Size Criteria Mitigation) that were due to the Housing Executive but had not been received. Had these payments been received the tenant would not be in arrears.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly. Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the cost of taxi provision for applicants in need of temporary accommodation for the last two years, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. As such I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive of the Northern Ireland Housing Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The table below details the costs to the Northern Ireland Housing Executive for taxi provision for applicants in need of temporary accommodation for the last two financial years by council area:Council Area Total Taxi Spend (£)2022/232023/24Antrim & Newtownabbey10,90619,320Ards & North Down10,15710,044Armagh, Banbridge & Craigavon11,18416,472Belfast75,884142,193Causeway Coast & Glens20,77717,883Derry & Strabane11,75011,098Fermanagh & Omagh7,3606,967Lisburn & Castlereagh11,14010,794Mid & East Antrim2,2211,348Mid Ulster19,91916,684Newry, Mourne & Down9,16412,338Miscellaneous*2,602-2,602Grand Total 193,064262,540Please note: 2023/24 figures are currently draft pending completion of the annual audit process, therefore remain subject to change.*Miscellaneous costs reflect an accounting adjustment between financial years.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with the Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on Action 17 of the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031.
answer_text: The Department recognises that there is a need to prioritise the integration of the Community & Voluntary (C&V) sector into statutory mental health service delivery.In delivering on Action 17 of the Mental Health Strategy (MHS) 2021-2031, “Fully integrate community and voluntary sector in mental health service delivery with a lifespan approach including the development of a protocol to make maximum use of the sector’s expertise”, the Department commissioned a review in 2022/23, to consider the most effective mechanism and structure by which the C&V sector could collectively use their experience, knowledge and skills to shape the implementation of the MHS and development of mental health services across Northern Ireland. The Department now intends to consider the findings of this review as part of a wider study to assess the capacity and scope within the C&V sector to inform the required steps for integration of the sector within the delivery of mental health services, including attendant structures of the Regional Mental Health Service. It is anticipated that this work will be completed by later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 11745/22-27, (i) for clarification on the change in the pattern of usage; and (ii) whether these monthly updates will be (a) shared with the Committee for Communities; and (b) made publicly available.
answer_text: Since 2020 a higher proportion of people with Full Duty Applicant status are staying in Temporary Accommodation.The number of Temporary Accommodation placements provided annually has increased from 4,527 placements in 2019/20 to 10,253 in 2022/23.  The increased demand combined with a reduced rate of social housing allocations (which has fallen from around 9,000 a year to 7,000) means people are staying much longer in Temporary Accommodation.  More people have taken up the offer of temporary accommodation; as numbers in the system have increased and exceeded fixed capacity accommodation like hostels, costs have risen, and expensive Hotel and B&B use has spiked sharply. Furthermore, within the last few months, since the Home Office prioritised work to increase the rate of decision making, the number of asylum seekers being granted leave to remain, and consequently access to housing support, has risen.  As of 9 May there were 311 households who had transitioned out of the asylum seeker system.While management information from the Housing Executive cannot routinely be made publicly available, the Department regularly publishes a range of official statistics relating to housing and homelessness, which are available at www.communities-ni.gov.uk/topics/housing-statistics.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 9293/22-27, how many of the 3,809 non-standard placements were provided in the Derry City & Strabane District Council area.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive and I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive does not maintain a list of non-standard placements (Hotel/B&B) by the area to which households are placed. Provision of the requested data would involve a review of all 3,809 placements to identify the number of premises referred to in the free text fields which are located within the Derry City & Strabane District Council area. The Housing Executive is therefore unable to provide this information.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly. Making direct contact with the Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 10879/22-27, (i) whether these Single Let properties are routinely inspected beyond the initial acquisition; (ii) to detail the outcome of the November 2023 process; and (iii) to detail the percentage of properties which were deemed reasonable as part of this exercise.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“(i) There are no routine inspections carried out beyond the inspection at the point of acquisition. While there are no routine physical inspections carried out by the Housing Executive there is an obligation for providers to provide evidence of the renewal of all certificates noted in AQW 10879/22-27 on an annual basis. Additionally, should there be any issues that require an inspection beyond the initial point of acquisition, the Housing Executive will carry these out these as and when required. (ii) & (iii) The exercise carried out in November 2023 did not identify any issues in terms of occupancy or quality of non-standard accommodation and therefore 100% of properties were deemed reasonable.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the steps his Department has taken to improve communication to (i) elected representatives; and (ii) the wider public in relation to upcoming roadworks in their area.
answer_text: My Department processes approximately 22,000 road or lane closure applications annually with individual approved roadwork applications manually added to the TrafficWatchNI website system, where resources permit.  Consequently, not all roadworks are displayed on the TrafficWatchNI website. However, priority is given to those roadworks that affect the public the most.Most of the closures prepared by my Department are on behalf of third parties, such as utilities  companies.  Given the volume of closures, and in line with the Limited Service principles being implemented by my Department due to funding pressures, communication with road users including elected representatives in relation to upcoming roadworks is focused on the busiest routes, primarily the 'A' Class network.  Communications used include use of signs on the impacted route in advance of the works, as well as notification on TrafficWatchNI. This notification is extended to busier lower tier roads as resources permit. However, I have asked my officials to review the process to ensure that a consistent approach is being taken, one that maximises the benefit of the website to the public.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has engaged with the Northern Ireland Road Safety Partnership in relation to how it could reinvest revenue from fixed penalties into road safety initiatives.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Road Safety Partnership includes the Department for Infrastructure as a partner organisation. I can confirm that my officials attend the quarterly Partnership Board meetings and contribute to decisions made in respect of how revenue is reinvested.Through this collaboration the Partnership kindly sponsored two of the Department’s road safety advertising campaigns at the end of financial year 2023/24.Officials will continue to work closely with the other board members, though any final decision on how revenue is reinvested is a matter for the Partnership.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of housing association tenants currently facing rent arrears; and (ii) the number of these cases that have escalated to court proceedings.
answer_text: My Department does not request information on tenants facing rent arrears as part of the Regulatory Framework for Registered Housing Associations in Northern Ireland. The requested information in relation to the number of housing associations facing rent arrears and cases that have escalated to court proceedings is not held by the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any engagement his Department has had with (a) Derry City and Strabane District Council; and (b) the Irish government in relation to confirmed cases of Mica blocks and traces of Reinforced Autoclaved Aerated Concrete in Northern Ireland; and (ii) whether his Department is conducting scoping work to identify defective premises among social housing stock.
answer_text: I can advise that my department has (i) engaged with (a) Derry City and Strabane District Council; and (b) no such engagement has taken place with the Irish Government.  (ii)  The Housing Executive (NIHE) is not aware of any Mica block construction in its housing stock following internal consultation with a number of long serving technical staff who are familiar with NIHE housing stock. In addition, mica construction has not been identified in surveys undertaken by NIHE consultants and its in-house survey team.  The NIHE Sponsor Team are working on gathering information in relation to Reinforced Autoclaved Aerated Concrete (RAAC) in the NIHE housing stock.There was a previous question on mica (AQW 4655/22-27) in relation to the Registered Housing Association stock. There is no further information in relation to the RHA stock since then.In relation to RAAC in Housing Association stock, my Department has not engaged with the Northern Ireland Federation of Housing Associations (NIFHA) on the matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 10222/22-27, to detail the number of Housing Executive tenants in rent arrears for (i) 2022-23; (ii) 2021-22; (iii) 2020-21; and (iv) 2019-20.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows.“The number of Housing Executive tenants in rent arrears for (i) 2022-23; (ii) 2021-22; (iii) 2020-21; and (iv) 2019-20 are detailed below:Financial Year-EndTotal Number in Arrears*Number with Technical Arrears**Number without Technical Arrears2022/23 40,09616,38223,7142021/2238,59716,21622,3812020/2137,47415,86021,6142019/2036,77212,75624,016*This is the number of tenants with technical arrears. Technical arrears are Welfare Reform related payments which are paid 4 weekly or monthly in arrears (i.e. Universal Credit, Rate Rebate, and Social Sector Size Criteria Mitigation) that were due to the Housing Executive but had not been received. Had these payments been received the tenant would not be in arrears. **This is the number of tenants without technical arrears (i.e. those who owe true arrears).”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly. Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement he has had with counterparts in Westminster in relation to (i) removing the cap on Tax Free Childcare; and (ii) increasing it to reflect inflation.
answer_text: Tax Free Childcare is the responsibility of HM Revenue & Customs. My Department has not had any engagement with the UK Government on the design or operation of this scheme, as engagement with HM Treasury is a matter for the Department of Finance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will engage with the Minister for the Economy to introduce specific dental hygienist training courses.
answer_text: The Department is currently considering how best to address future workforce requirements across the full range of dental professions.  The inadequate funding made available to my Department in the 2024/25 budget highlights the need for collective sharing across the Executive of the financial burden of ensuring the future supply of professionals to deliver health and social care to our population.  The Department of the Economy will be an important partner in this endeavour.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will engage with the Minister of Finance to ring-fence funding, generated by the Soft Drinks Industry Levy, to address health-related conditions including obesity and type 2 diabetes.
answer_text: The Soft Drinks Industry Levy is a UK-wide tariff added to drinks that contain added sugar. It seeks to contribute to strategies to reduce obesity by reducing or removing added sugar from soft drinks by encouraging producers and importers to reformulate their products to reduce the sugar content; and/or reduce portion sizes for added sugar drinks.Given the nature of the market and the fact that the levy applies to producers and importers, this means these businesses are reporting once for all receipts raised across the UK. Therefore, His HMRC does not report receipts below the UK wide level. However, in 22/23, the levy raised £355m across the UK. Using population measure, and assuming production of soft drinks is representative across the UK, it could be estimated that £10.6m was raised in Northern Ireland in 22/23. The levy is not formally linked by the UK Government to any specific spending and therefore it is not known how much the Executive receives as a result of the Levy.My Department previously explored the potential to use funds equivalent to those likely to have been raised through the Levy in Northern Ireland for tackling obesity and other public health related issues, but given there was no budget line, this did not prove to be possible. It would still be my preference that the revenue raised through the Levy would be targeted at obesity related purposes. My Department recently consulted on a new, co-produced obesity strategic framework, ‘Healthy Futures’. The public consultation commenced on 24 November 2023 and closed on 1 March 2024. A consultation summary report is being developed, and the Department will give further consideration for the potential to use resources, such as the Soft Drinks Industry Levy, and the mechanisms to do so, as work on the new strategic framework progresses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will introduce a compensation scheme for women injured as a result of pelvic mesh implants.
answer_text: The Hughes Report by the Patient Safety Commissioner in England, published on 7 February 2024, made recommendations for the government in England to consider in relation to redress. There have been no final decisions on redress taken by the government in England and no set timeframe for such decisions. My officials continue to engage in discussions with counterparts in England, and in Scotland and Wales, to understand the emerging considerations there. Options regarding next steps for Northern Ireland will also take account of these discussions and of related timeframes which remain under consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of Carer's Allowance overpayments; and (ii) the total cost of these overpayments, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The table below shows the total number of recoverable Carer's Allowance overpayments and the total cost of these overpayments, in each of the last five years.YearCarers Allowance OverpaymentsValue £2019/201,6391,543,1962020/21912637,4702021/222,2782,919,6952022/232,1692,940,1612023/242,0673,349,067

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department will consider offering a financial contribution to deliver an alternative community event to a bonfire in the Galliagh area.
answer_text: Unfortunately, my Department is facing a very difficult financial position this year, and given these pressures, it is very unlikely that my Department will be in a position to contribute. I understand that an approach has been made to the NIHE (which is an arms length body of my Department) and other statutory bodies /organisations regarding funding, and that monies have been secured at this time, which will help to deliver upon an event programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will consider introducing oral immunotherapy trials for patients with allergies to peanuts and other foods.
answer_text: Officers within the Department and its Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) remain committed to the introduction of an oral immunotherapy service for patients with allergies to nuts and other foods.   Work is ongoing to agree the most appropriate service delivery model for Northern Ireland.   The Child Health Programme Board which provides strategic direction regarding child health issues across NI is meeting in June   to consider a number of key issues including the Palforzia service model.  It is intended that the meeting will prioritise the establishment of a working group to develop and recommend the NI service model with its report to be available by late summer 2024. In the interim the regime is available on case by case basis for eligible patients in line with the Department’s endorsed guidance.Paediatricians and specialist allergy nurses across all Trusts work closely together to ensure that children and young people get the best care possible for treatment for their allergy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 11103/22-27, to detail the funding allocation for the Social Housing Development Programme for 2024-25.
answer_text: As part of my initial decisions on the Departments 2024/25 budget, I have allocated £116.3m for the Social Housing Development Programme (including Shared Housing) for 2024-25.The vast majority of the Capital budget has been invested in social housing.  However, the funding available will severely constrain the number of new starts possible this year.I will launch an Equality Impact Assessment consultation on the initial decisions on the Department for Communities 2024-25 budget. The consultation will launch on 11 June and will end on 3 September 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department will consider the development of a gambling strategy, to include a gambling regulator and age verification for online gambling sites.
answer_text: I have concluded that primary legislation of the magnitude and complexity required to address issues such as age verification for online gambling sites and a gambling regulator could not be drafted and make its way through all Assembly scrutiny and legislative processes within the current mandate. For that reason, I have decided not to proceed with a gambling Bill in this mandate but am actively considering what can be delivered in the interim.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has considered introducing an all-island register of banned animal cruelty offenders.
answer_text: An all-Ireland register of animal welfare offenders can only be created if such a register was considered feasible in both jurisdictions.  No register exists in the Republic of Ireland nor are there any plans to create one there.		My Department has considered this matter extensively in the past and concluded that the creation of a register is untenable.  The situation remains that a clear evidential need has not been identified, the costs of establishing and maintaining a register would be significant and the data protection implications involved are prohibitive. 		However, if Assembly colleagues or stakeholders advocating for a register can provide additional evidence to support the establishment and operation of a register, which could include where any such register has worked effectively elsewhere, my officials will consider any such detail received. 		I am conscious that an objective of any such register would be to ensure, as much as possible, that those banned from keeping animals do not obtain animals again. My Department remains available to discuss with stakeholders other ways of working together to minimise the possibility of these instances occurring.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the actions his Department has taken to improve dementia diagnosis.
answer_text: The Department recognises the urgency with which individuals awaiting assessment will wish to be seen in order to understand their condition and to commence treatment.  The Department has issued a 9-week target which sets out that people should have a first assessment following referral into Health and Social Care Trust memory services within that timescale. All Trusts have advised that they employ a rigorous triaging regime on receipt of dementia referrals to ensure that those individuals at greatest risk are seen first.My officials continue to engage with the Regional Trust Dementia Leads in order to scrutinize performance against this 9-week target and the Regional Service Delivery Plan which monitors the numbers of new and review clinic appointments which have been achieved. In relation to waiting times, as of 30th April 2024, there were 58.3% breaches of the 9-week target. Therefore, 2,663 people were awaiting first assessment, with 1,553 people waiting for more than 9 weeks.   There is a commitment across Health and Social Care in Northern Ireland to improving timely access to accurate diagnosis and treatment for people living with Dementia.  My Department recognises the importance of timely diagnosis of early Alzheimer’s disease in the event that the new disease modifying treatments for this condition become licenced for use in the United Kingdom My officials have held the inaugural meeting of a Regional Dementia Project Board, this took place on 24th May 2024. The project board aims to support the delivery of existing dementia services, as well as consider future service delivery models. Key priorities for the Regional Dementia Project Board will be to decrease waiting times for assessment and diagnosis, to consider steps to improve efficiency in systems and processes across the region, to consider regional approaches to training, to improve the experience of diagnosis for people with dementia and their relatives/carers and to formulate a clear regional vision of services into the future. The Regional Dementia Project Board will additionally focus on the Regional Dementia Care Pathway. The roll out of the Regional Dementia Care Pathway will significantly contribute to raising the standards across all trusts, including addressing delays in dementia diagnosis.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will engage with (i) other Ministers; and (ii) stakeholders to improve post-19 special educational needs provision.
answer_text: I want every young person in the north to have the opportunity to access opportunities and fulfil their potential. I have tasked my officials with scoping the existing provision and providing me with options to improve access and outcomes for our young people with special educational needs. I have asked for this initial phase to be carried out at pace, and to include an assessment of how best to engage other departments and partners, as well as those with lived experience, with a view to improving the existing provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the current number of staff vacancies in each Land and Property Service office.
answer_text: Details of the total vacancies in each business area is not held centrally within the Department of Finance. Each business area is responsible for managing its resources – both financial and staff. Each business area determines its individual workforce needs and is responsible for ensuring that any additional staffing resources are affordable. When a business area within the Department of Finance identifies a vacancy, the request is referred to NICSHR to initiate the process to fill the post. NICSHR liaises with all business areas on an ongoing basis to seek to prioritise recruitment activity within allocated financial and staff resources. As at the end of April 2024, NICSHR was managing a caseload of 125 vacancies in Land and Property Services. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the actions his Department has taken to improve tailored day care and respite opportunities for young adults with autism and learning disabilities.
answer_text: HSCTs continue to provide day care for young adults with learning disabilities, including those with autism, through a range of regulated building-based day centres specifically designed to promote choice and independence. HSCTs also offer support to service users/carers in the form of Cash Grants (one off payments), cash ‘Direct payments’ in lieu of statutory services directly provided, Domiciliary Short Breaks and access to nursing and residential home facilities to improve access to respite/short break services. DoH officials are working with HSCTs to develop and progress the Shared Lives Model, helping to enhance access to day support and short break services.DoH officials are also working to finalise ‘The Learning Disability Service Model’ which will set out a new model for the future of Learning Disability Services. Public Consultation on the model will take place Summer 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Bicycle Strategy.
answer_text: Since the publication of The Bicycle Strategy in 2015, my Department is developing a suite of comprehensive network plans to deliver active travel infrastructure and supporting measures for cyclists. This work includes the development of an Active Travel Network Delivery Plan for the North, a full update of the design guidance and proposed active travel network plans for larger towns and cities. When complete, the plan will complement the Belfast Cycling Network Delivery Plan (2022) and the Strategic Plan for Greenways (2016) to provide a firm basis for the future prioritisation of active travel infrastructure investment and delivery.We plan to consult on the draft Active Travel Delivery Plan later in 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will consider allocating funds to Easilink Community Transport's Dial-a-Lift service in order to (i) bolster services; and (ii) address operational deficits.
answer_text: I recognise and value the contribution of all organisation’s that provide community transport services, and the impact that they have on some of the most vulnerable groups in society.I have made the decision to provide funding to community transport for 2024/25 at the total levels awarded for 2023/24, which reverses in part some of the reduction in funding allocation from August 2023. This is subject to the planned Equality Impact Assessment consultation on the budget and so this allocation is indicative at this stage. I do, however, expect to be able to confirm opening budget allocations, once I have taken final decisions following consideration of responses to the Equality Impact Assessment after an initial four-week period. My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. The value of our infrastructure cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when claimants in receipt of Employment and Support Allowance will migrate to Universal Credit.
answer_text: My Department is working closely with the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) on the timeline for migrating Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) claimants to Universal Credit, following the Prime Minister’s announcement on 19 April 2024.I will provide an update when further details are known, and decisions have been taken in respect of ESA claimants in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of (i) frontline Universal Credit staff; and (ii) vacant frontline posts in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The number of UC frontline staff and vacancies over the last five years were as follows:  31 March 2020 – 1246 staff, no vacancies 31 March 2021 –1261 staff, vacancy information not available.31 March 2022 –1466 staff, vacancy information not available. 31 March 2023 –1466 staff, 261 vacancies31 March 2024 –1381 staff, 251 vacanciesThe vacancy positions at 31March 2021 and 2022 have not been provided as Universal Credit front line staff were redirected to back office processing due to unprecedented levels of new claims and our priority to protect payments at that time. It is important to emphasise that staffing levels are impacted by the implications of budget restrictions, the pause on recruitment, and the availability of supply to fill vacancies when making comparisons between years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the rationale for offering Temporary Exemption Certificates for five and seven year old vehicles, but not for six year old vehicles.
answer_text: From 1 June 2024, private cars first registered between 1 June 2019 and 31 May 2020 or between 1 June 2017 and 31 May 2018, which have a valid MOT issued by the Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) will have a one-year Temporary Exemption Certificate (TEC) automatically applied from the date their current MOT certificate expires. Historically, the youngest vehicles presenting for test have had the highest pass rates. As legislation requires that private cars must have an initial roadworthiness test when they are four years old these cars are ineligible to receive a TEC.  I have therefore limited the eligibility criteria to younger cars which have a valid MOT certificate in place.  Vehicle licensing information shows that around 115,000 five and seven year old cars may be eligible for a TEC, which should make a notable improvement to vehicle testing waiting times. I appreciate that external factors can negatively impact the DVA’s plans for service delivery.  Therefore, a 12-month gap between the registration dates of the two eligible groups of cars will provide flexibility needed should it be necessary to extend the use of TECs in the future.I am satisfied that the selected dates ranges for TECs strikes the right balance between managing vehicle testing waiting times and the impact on road safety.As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. Vehicle owners also have a legal responsibility for maintaining their vehicles in a roadworthy condition, regardless of MOT status.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on a heavy goods vehicle traffic survey for (i) Eglington Village, Derry; and (ii) the B118 Tamnaherin Road.
answer_text: A detailed traffic survey for traffic passing through Eglinton village and using the B118 Tamnaherin Road was carried out in autumn 2024. The data indicated that the proportion of HGVs passing through the Main Street of Eglinton on a weekday is around 3% of all traffic.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the steps his Department is taking to (i) refresh road markings; (ii) resurface roads in the Creggan area of Derry, specifically Westway and Beechwood.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. As a result my Department has had to introduce a limited maintenance policy meaning that only the highest priority defects are repaired. In terms of road markings, my Department’s limited resources are directed towards the maintenance of regulatory markings and junctions with other road markings, including longitudinal lines renewed when budgets permit. The value of our infrastructure cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made.Resurfacing of Westway and Beechwood Avenue is not currently included within the Department’s Structural Maintenance programme for the Derry City and Strabane District Council area.  Officials will continue to inspect these areas and address any defects which meet the current threshold for repair in accordance with my Department’s policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what engagement his officials have had with NI Water to prioritise and identify funding to progress the adoption of Burnside Manor, Derry.
answer_text: NI Water has no responsibility for unadopted private sewerage infrastructure. . However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water on this development and have been advised as follows:The site was inspected in May 2023 and a range of defects was identified which did not meet the current NI Water standards. Although DfI Roads holds a bond for this site, it is estimated that repairs to bring the development to an adoptable standard will cost well in excess of the amount in the bond.  The developer of the site is no longer operating and NI Water is not funded to carry out required repairs to an unadopted site. The responsibility for bringing the site up to an adoptable standard therefore falls to the residents. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

answer_text: The capital DEL funding my Department has received since its inception, and the amount of capital funding my Department has allocated to public transport in the same financial years is detailed in the table below.For 2024-25, my Department has been allocated an opening capital budget of £820. I am carefully considering how this funding is to be allocated across the functions of my Department. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the temporary closure of Rosebud respite facility, Derry; and (ii) for his assessment of the impact this will have on service users and their families.
answer_text: (i) It is recognised that regionally availability of short breaks / respite for children with disability is a pressure across the HSC Trusts.My department officials have made direct contact with the Western Health and Social Care Trust, in respect of the concerns raised regarding the temporary closure of Rosebud Short Break Unit.The Western Health and Social Care Trust has informed my Department that Rosebud Short Break Unit remains open at this time, however, there is limited short breaks being provided, due to 2 children requiring full time care placements within the unit.As a consequence of the current limited capacity for short breaks within Rosebud - the Western Health and Social Care Trust cannot guarantee planned respite admissions at this time. Short Break admissions to Rosebud are currently being prioritised by the Western HSC Trust dependant on assessed need.(ii) It is acknowledged that the current restrictions on short break / respite care provision within Rosebud will be creating significant pressures for children and their families. The Western Health and Social Care Trust has informed my department that they are aware of the stresses and anxieties for families that the limited short break provision will be creating. The Trust has advised my officials that all parents can continue to liaise with the allocated Trust Social Worker or Duty Social Worker to explore potential alternative supports options.Furthermore, I understand that the Western HSC Trust’s Children With Disability Head of Service has previously met with parents to inform them of current pressures and availability. The Trust has advised my officials that a further face to face meeting with parents will be arranged as soon as possible, however, in the interim the Western Health and Social Care Trust are intending to provide an update letter to all parents eligible for short breaks to ensure families remain updated.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in light of rising rates of food insecurity, what consideration he has given to introducing further Holiday Hunger Payment Schemes.
answer_text: Ringfenced funding for School Holiday Food Grant payments ceased in 2023 and the payments were therefore discontinued. Food poverty is an issue for many low-income families that affects all age groups across every day of the year. While tackling “holiday hunger” is an important aspect of this, I believe it is for the Executive to seek to address food poverty across all our society through a wider and more holistic approach within the context of the Executive’s Anti-Poverty Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 7884/22-27, for an update on (i) the Healthy Happy Minds Programme; and (ii) his Department's long-term approach to support the emotional health and well-being of pupils.
answer_text: Healthy Happy Minds was a pilot which ended in March 2023.  The overall outcome of the post project evaluation was positive, however, there is no central funded service for primary school counselling services.  The challenging financial context across the education system means that budgets and investments are severely constrained.  As with all services that are not statutory, investment is unfortunately limited to budget availability.The joint Department of Education and Department of Health ‘Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework’ provides investment into a number of projects that work to develop resilience in our children and young people across primary and post primary ages to promote positive emotional wellbeing as well as early support and enhanced support pathways where needs are identified in schools.  My department will continue to invest in the joint Framework as far as budgets will allow, and I hope the Health Minister will also be in the position to do so to further embed the Framework as a whole.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 9184/22-27 (i) whether there are plans to collate data on the number of pupils on long-term absence experiencing mental health problems; and (ii) what measures will be put in place to support children, parents and staff.
answer_text: My officials are working with the Education Authority to ensure that the new school administration system, “Education Information Solutions” (EdiS), is able to facilitate the recording of absences at a more granular level. That work is continuing.My Department, the Education Authority and schools have a range of measures in place to support pupils. These include DE’s “Miss School. Miss Out Strategy”, “The Challenge of Improving Pupil Attendance at School - Case Studies paper” and the Absence Tiers Guidance. The Education and Training Inspectorate also published a series of case studies in 2016 which schools can continue to draw upon.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the implementation of the Private Tenancies Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, including regulations to restrict rent increases to a maximum of once every 12 months.
answer_text: Sections 1- 6: were commenced on 1 April 2023. These sections specify the need to provide certain information to tenants and the requirement for receipts for cash payments. They also limit the amount of deposit to no more than one month’s rent and increase the time to protect these deposits;Section 7: A report on the impact of a rent freeze/cut was laid and published on 28 October 2022, in line with the statutory deadline. The power to cut or freeze rents expired on 28 October 2023. Controls on the frequency of rent increases have not been commenced. I am aware of the potential unintended consequences of these types of measures, so I will consider this action in this mandate, along with other proposals for further reform of the Private Rented Sector; Sections 8 & 10: Regulations on Fire and Electrical Safety are well advanced, and I anticipate they will be introduced over the next couple of months; Section 9:  Initial scoping suggests work to set Minimum Energy Efficiency standards for Private Rented Sector may take some time to complete, as it involves complex considerations which cut across the remits of several Departments; Section 11: A robust Equality Impact Assessment (EQIA) is in train to inform regulations on the circumstances when longer notice periods will not apply, and I anticipate that my Department will consult on this and the draft regulations in late summer; andSection 12: As required, my Department consulted, laid, and published a report on payment options for tenants by 28 October 2023.  The report findings did not indicate a pressing need to legislate in relation to this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether he is aware of some insurance providers refusing to offer roadside recovery for vehicles without a valid MOT certificate, despite having a Temporary Exemption Certificate or MOT booking in place; and (ii) what engagement he has had with relevant stakeholders to ensure motorists are not penalised.
answer_text: From 1 June 2024, private cars first registered between 1 June 2019 and 31 May 2020 or between 1 June 2017 and 31 May 2018, which have a valid MOT issued by the Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) will have a one-year Temporary Exemption Certificate (TEC) automatically applied.Once TECs have been applied this will extend the MOT certificate by 12 months from the expiry of the current MOT. This will allow motorists to continue to drive their vehicle legally, providing it is kept in a roadworthy condition, is taxed and insured. The DVA has consulted with the PSNI and the Association of British Insurers (ABI) who both understand the need to introduce measures to reduce waiting times.  Both organisations have also confirmed they will continue to apply the current mitigations that are in place for customers who cannot secure an MOT appointment before their current certificate expires.The view of the ABI is that a lack of a valid MOT certificate due to delays in the MOT testing regime here would not necessarily invalidate insurance but customers who find themselves in that situation are advised to check their policy documents or speak to their insurer.My officials contacted the Automobile Association (AA) who confirmed that they will no longer apply a surcharge of £60 to customers in the North who require breakdown recovery if their vehicle has no MOT. They recognise that MOT waiting times are creating unforeseen problems and, as a result, they have decided to provide roadside service to their members here who can show the vehicle is properly insured; they are waiting for their booked MOT appointment; and their vehicle is in a roadworthy and safe condition.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department will increase the number of places at oversubscribed post-primary schools, with specific reference to (i) St. Cecelia’s College, Derry; and (ii) St. Brigid’s College, Derry.
answer_text: My Department allocates additional places to schools where oversubscription arises during the competitive admissions process in order to ensure children can access a school place in their area and in their parents’ preferred sector.  For the purposes of school admissions my Department recognises four school sectors, namely denominational; non-denominational; Integrated and Irish-medium.  No child can be guaranteed admission to a particular school.To date my Department has approved almost 700 additional places at Year 8 for September 2024 admission across Northern Ireland and will continue to allocate places where the need arises.As sufficient capacity exists in the denominational sector in the Foyle constituency for both boys and girls there are currently no plans to increase capacity at either St Cecilia’s College or St Brigid’s College.

answer_text: The number of children who applied for a post-primary school place through the competitive admissions process and who are yet to secure a place, broken down by council area, is set out in the following table.
