question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what action his Department is taking to address the contamination of the Four Mile Burn which has led to the death of more than 1000 brown trout.
answer_text: Following the identification of the source of the major fish kill and the collection of statutory samples in respect of its Water (NI) Order 1999 investigation, NIEA staff engaged with the owner of the premises involved to identify the cause of the discharge and to seek the prevention of further pollution to the river. The owner of the premises cooperated with the investigation and carried out a number of actions to remediate the issue identified on site which were confirmed by the NIEA inspectors following a follow up inspection on Tuesday 21 May 2024.In respect of the contamination of the Four Mile Burn itself, a significant impact on the fishery was recorded. As the investigation and gathering of evidence continues, I am unable to make further comment at this time as NIEA must treat all aspects of a live investigation as if it were ‘sub judice’.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what his Department is doing to prevent further contamination of rivers and lakes.
answer_text: The required actions are summarised in the draft third River Basin Management Plan (RBMP) for Northern Ireland, which DAERA publicly consulted on in 2021. In light of the challenges posed by the occurrence of blue-green algal blooms in Lough Neagh, I am considering next steps for the RMBP and plan to bring it to the Executive for consideration in due course.My department has already commenced actions aimed at reducing impacts on the ecological status of rivers and lakes from excess nutrients originating from agricultural land use and sewage infrastructure. The measures include the Soil Nutrient Health Scheme and review of the Nutrient Actions Programme. Other actions are delivered by our partners like upgrades of wastewater treatment works and infrastructure as set out in the price control period PC21 (2021-2027) by Northern Ireland Water.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what action his Department is taking to address the lack of places in care homes in Derry.
answer_text: There are 468 registered Care Homes in Northern Ireland, providing a total of 15,836 beds. Nursing Home beds make up approximately two thirds of these beds, with one third being within Residential Care Homes. Independent sector Care Homes account for 93% of the total Care Home beds registered in Northern Ireland.The Western and Southern Trust areas have the least number of registered care home beds, in comparison to the other Trusts. The Western Health and Social Care Trust has 31 nursing homes and 36 residential care homes with a total of 2079 care home beds. (Census of Bed Availability in Registered Care Homes in Northern Ireland on 27 September 2023 (Dec 2023)).My colleagues within the Department of Health have been working closely with Trust care home leads and other key stakeholders to understand the pertinent issues facing the sector.Western Health and Social Care Trust representatives have advised that the majority of beds in their nursing and residential care homes in their trust areas are provided by the independent sector. There are currently no plans to increase bed provision within the Statutory Sector within the Western Trust. In order to explore increasing independent care home bed capacity the Western Trust scoped availability and published market engagement notices on “Find a Tender” in May 2023. These market engagement notices sought to explore increasing of bed stock, particularly within dementia care beds. As a result, two independent care home providers came forward expressing interest in increasing bed capacity. The Western Trust have advised should other providers within the independent sector wish to develop a proposal to increase bed capacity the Trust would engage with them and consider the proposal in line with contractual requirements and tariff. The Trust additionally advised that similar market engagement would be carried out in this financial year.The Department of Health acknowledges the need to increase bed availability and to make better use of existing vacant capacity across the region. Workstream 1 of the Social Care Collaborative Forum is taking forward work to progress a regional digital solution for bed capacity and availability. It is hoped that this digital solution will improve communication regarding location of available beds and provide an approach for data gathering that supports better identification, individual care planning and provision of services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his plans to increase the number of respite facilities available in the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Respite care/short breaks provide valuable opportunities for children and adults with disability to spend time away from carers and family, whilst also providing a break from caring responsibilities. Trusts provide a range of services to enable families/carers to get a break from caring responsibilities, including residential short breaks, fostering, Cash Grants (one off payments), cash ‘Direct payments’ and Domiciliary Short Breaks. This diversity of service ensures that carers can access flexible options for support, dependent on assessed need. The Western Health and Social Care Trust (WHSCT) advised my officials within the Strategic Planning and Performance Group that all available resource in the Trust area continues to be maximised to provide respite/short breaks, including bed capacity in the independent sector; as appropriate. However, in the absence of any newly aligned additional funding for respite/short breaks, the WHSCT advise as a Trust they are not in a position to increase the number of respite/short break facilities.In terms of Children with Disabilities, work is ongoing to finalise the Children with Disabilities Framework, which will better enable Trusts and independent sector providers to respond to the pressure points across Health and Social Care, specifically, increasing demand for early and community-based support; increasing demand for respite care/short breaks; increasing demand for more bespoke residential placements; and better planning and management of transitions from children to adult services.While I fully recognise the need to develop regional solutions, residential short breaks beds are commissioned by Trusts on a localised basis. My officials within the Strategic Planning and Performance Group have developed a monitoring framework to capture monthly data from individual Trusts to gain a better understanding of residential short break provision across the region. In addition, officials regularly engage with Trust colleagues to identify pressures and allocate additional funding to increase capacity. There has been additional funding provided for services for Children with a Disability in recent years. Since 2020, including additional funding for 2023/24, specific recurrent funding of approximately £7.5m has been provided for services and supports for Children with a Disability, which includes over £1m specifically in relation to short breaks (respite) and family support enhancement services.In terms of Adult Learning Disability services, I am aware that the demand for short breaks has exceeded supply for a number of years across the region, due to a growth in population size but more importantly increased complexity of need. These pressures have been raised as one of a number of commissioning priorities within the Learning Disability programme of care. My officials are currently finalising a draft service model for learning disability, which includes several outcomes and actions to expand respite/short breaks and d

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 10127/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 10127 22-27 was answered on 8th November 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will consider additional support for individuals with severe food allergies, specifically the provision of free-from food on prescription.
answer_text: Some foods have characteristics of drugs necessary for the management of specific conditions. The Advisory Committee on Borderline Substances (ACBS) advises as to the circumstances in which such substances may be regarded as drugs. The Northern Ireland Drug Tariff outlines products recommended by the ACBS for specific conditions which are available on HSC prescription, including provision of gluten-free staple foods for patients with confirmed gluten-sensitive enteropathies such as coeliac disease. Clinicians should still satisfy themselves that ACBS recommended products can be safely prescribed, that patients are adequately monitored and that, where necessary, expert hospital supervision is available.As we deal with the consequences of COVID-19 my clear priority and focus is to rebuild and transform the Health and Social Care (HSC) system. My Department's resources, and those of the entire HSC, are fully committed to supporting that critical work, which includes work on cancer services, elective care services, unscheduled care services, mental health services, social care services, and children's services. In this context, my Department has no plans at this time to initiate work on an allergy strategy. People can continue to be referred to allergy services, as appropriate, by their GP.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his Department's plans to support those aged over 19 years old with special educational needs in their transition into further education.
answer_text: The need for provision of clear and appropriate pathways for young people transitioning from education into further education or employment is a key theme within the Department of Education’s (DE) End to End (E2E Review of Special Educational Needs (SEN) and associated Transitions workstream.  A ‘Transitions Working Group’, jointly chaired by an official from my department and the Department of Health (DoH), has been established and is supported by representatives from the Education Authority (EA), the Public Health Authority (PHA) and officials from the Departments for the Communities (DfC) and the Economy (DfE). The aim of the working group is to evaluate current transitions processes and practices, with the aim of identifying improvements to create better ways of working and sharing of information to ensure that there are clear and appropriate pathways to post-19 provisions for those aged over 19 years old with SEN or disability across further and higher education or employment. The working group recently conducted a survey of practitioners and parents to better understand their experience of the current process and to seek views on potential solutions for the future.  The results of this survey are being considered. My department is continuing to engage directly with practitioners, parents and young people who are going through – or have gone through – the transitions process to help inform recommendations on how we can improve pathways for young people aged over 19 years old with SEN or disability into further education or employment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 8101/22-27, whether his Department has considered a mitigation payment for Universal Claimants in receipt of Maternity Allowance.
answer_text: I have noted the recommendations relating to a Better Start Grant that were made in the Independent Advisory Panel’s Report into the future of welfare mitigations that was published in October 2022, including the introduction of a pregnancy and baby payment. Officials are currently finalising work to update the estimated costs for all of the recommendations coming out of the Independent Advisory Panel Report, to help inform decisions on this matter.Any future mitigation package will need to be agreed by the Executive and assessed in the climate of financial constraint and the requirement to ensure that budgets remain sustainable over the coming years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has explored further cross-border healthcare opportunities beyond elective care via the Shared Island Initiative.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 6767/22-27 and in light of the Budget 2024-25, for an update on Phase 3 of the Derry to Coleraine project.
answer_text: I am committed to improving transport connectivity for the benefit of our economy and communities across the North, including the Northwest. However, My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. In this context, I have recently agreed an indictive 2024/25 budget which will be subject to an equality consultation. This includes ring fencing funding for Coleraine - Derry Phase 3 project, which will allow the project to proceed as planned.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what steps he will take to uphold transitional protections for claimants (i) on maternity or sick leave; and (ii) in receipt of a reduced income in the first three months of a migration to Universal Credit.
answer_text: Transitional Protection is available to all claimants who migrate from a ‘legacy’ benefit to Universal Credit (UC). If their UC entitlement is less than their entitlement on the legacy benefit, the difference is paid as a Transitional Protection Element (TPE) so that they are not worse off financially at the point of migration.		(i)         As noted above, Transitional Protection is only available to claimants who migrate to UC from a legacy benefit following receipt of a migration notice. It is not available to a person on maternity leave or sick leave unless they are in receipt of a legacy benefit and claim Universal Credit following receipt of a migration notice. 		(ii)      Transitional Protection will stop if a claimant’s earnings falls below a certain threshold for three consecutive months (currently £892 a month for a single claimant and £1,437 a month for joint claimants), and at the time they migrated to Universal Credit their earnings were above this level.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of (i) respite beds; and (ii) day centre places available for patients with dementia, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The department do not hold this information centrally and as such the Trusts were contacted to provide their responses.  (i) This is the Trusts response in relation to respite beds for patients with dementia;Belfast Health and Social Care TrustBelfast Health and Social Care Trust have no dedicated respite beds but key workers will arrange respite for service users with dementia in a care home suitable to their assessed need and availability of a care home bed.Northern Health and Social Care TrustWe do not hold respite beds and day centre places specifically based on POC or dementia.South Eastern Health and Social Care TrustThe South Eastern Trust does not provide formal respite bed provision or day centre places for people with a dementia. Southern Health and Social Care TrustThe Mental Health Directorate within SHSCT contract two respite beds within care homes. Further bed based respite is sourced as required but is dependent on bed availability.Respite beds for service users in the Adult Community Directorate with Dementia are spot purchased as required across the Independent Sector and the four Trust Statutory Residential homes.Western Health and Social Care TrustThe Western Trust has 3 dedicated respite beds in one of its statutory residential care homes for people with dementia.   The Western Trust’s contractual arrangements with the Independent Sector care homes does not stipulate dedicated dementia respite beds.  However, if an individual with dementia requires respite care, a bed will be sourced for them in the Independent Sector(ii) This is the Trusts response in relation to Day Centre places available for patients with Dementia;Belfast Health and Social Care TrustBelfast Trust currently have 3 Day Centres dedicated to providing day care placements for those living with dementia: Knockbracken Day Centre, Edgcumbe Day Centre and Carlisle Day Centre and there are 171 service users registered to attend.The Trust has a further 7 Day Centres registered for service users over 65 years of age.  These Day Centres can also afford placements to those with a diagnosis of dementia.Northern Health and Social Care TrustWe do not hold respite beds and day centre places specifically based on POC or dementia.South Eastern Health and Social Care TrustThe South Eastern Trust does not provide formal respite bed provision or day centre places for people with a dementia. The Trust may commission day care from external sources based on assessed need and availability of resource.Southern Health and Social Care TrustAcross the Southern Trust there are 8 statutory daycare facilities registered to provide day-care for older people (over the age of 65) including people with dementia offering 783 daycare places per week. Only one of these day centres is dedicated to dementia day care services and this service offers 20 places daily.  Additional spaces can be accessed as required. There are also 18 places commissioned by Adult Community Directorate for service users

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there has been underspend in allocated funding for domiciliary care in any Health and Social Care Trust in each of the last three years.
answer_text: Belfast, South Eastern, Southern and Western Health and Social Care Trusts have advised my department that there has been no underspend within the area of domiciliary care in each of the last three years.Northern Health and Social Care Trust has advised that they did not have an underspend in 2021/22 but did underspend against budget in 2022/23 and 2023/24 years.  The reason for this has been due to a large number of vacancies in their in-house domiciliary care workforce.  All Trusts are finding it hard to recruit new staff into this workforce.  The Trust would be overspent in other parts of the system supporting people who are awaiting a package of care to return home.Northern Ireland Ambulance Service Health and Social Care Trust has no domiciliary care expenditure.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients currently (i) in a hospital setting; and (ii) staying in a care home, who are unable to return home due to the absence of a domiciliary care package, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his plans to reduce reliance on non-standard accommodation.
answer_text: I recognise we have an immediate issue with the use of non-standard accommodation; it is not good for families, they need the security of their own homes, and it is prohibitively expensive. I have asked the Housing Executive to focus on this work and to provide me with monthly updates on the use of all Temporary Accommodation and, in doing so, the Chief Executive of the Housing Executive has advised me that it is: “…committed to a range of actions that seek to reduce use of non-standard accommodation.  In addressing specific challenges on use of non-standard usage the Housing Executive has initiated a Housing Solutions Task and Finish Group which is making progress looking at options for alternative and more financially sustainable options for temporary accommodation.”.Alongside this, my officials are continuing to work on the financial transaction capital Loan to Acquire Move-on Accommodation.  This will support tenancy ready clients into affordable secure accommodation, who would otherwise be unable to access the private rented sector. Additionally, I recently launched a scheme to fund delivery of new more affordable Intermediate Rent homes which may, in time, help ensure that more households can access a safer, more secure, affordable home.My aim is to support a shift to prevention, I recognise this will take time to solve and so my priority will be to focus on those currently in crises.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the efficacy of allocating funds towards non-standard accommodation in comparison to investment in homelessness prevention.
answer_text: While the funding of temporary accommodation is a statutory duty outlined in the Housing (NI) Order 1988, I am keen to prioritise homelessness prevention.  The use of non-standard temporary accommodation, which is used in the absence of other alternatives, should be for as short a duration as possible.The Housing Executive has experienced sustained and increasing demand for temporary accommodation since the outbreak of the pandemic providing 4,527 placements in 2019/20, increasing to 10,253 in 2022/23. In recognition of the challenges this presents the Chief Executive of the Housing Executive has advised me that it is: “…committed to a range of actions that seek to reduce use of non-standard accommodation.  In addressing specific challenges on use of non-standard usage the Housing Executive has initiated a Housing Solutions Task and Finish Group which is making progress looking at options for alternative and more financially sustainable options for temporary accommodation.”It is my intention to make homelessness rare, brief, and non-recurrent and my aim is to support a shift to prevention. This will take time and my short-term focus will continue to be supporting those currently in crisis.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any action that has been taken to address flooding at Aberfoyle Road, Derry; (ii) the cost of that action; and (iii) his assessment of its effectiveness.
answer_text: I am aware that the Member clarified the AQW to now be about Aberfoyle Crescent. This issue is an operational matter for NI Water.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows: (i) In 2020/2021, NI Water developed the Culmore Drainage Area Plan (DAP) model locally to enable appraisal of flooding events around the Aberfoyle Crescent area, with the technical report available in September 2021. Construction commenced in May 2022 to install a high-level relief sewer along Aberfoyle Crescent and was completed in early July 2022.  I can also confirm that the road gullies in Aberfoyle Crescent were cleaned in May 2023 and March 2024 although due to parked cars, not all gullies were able to be cleaned each time. (ii) Total project expenditure to date is £162k.   (iii) Following a severe weather flooding event in July 2022 NI Water undertook further investigation into the operation of the newly installed relief sewer.  In storm events beyond the design capacity of the sewer, a volume of overland flow will remain. NI Water increased the frequency of inspection and sewer cleaning along Aberfoyle Crescent due to inappropriate materials being found in the sewer during investigations and the construction phase.   For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly.  Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the total number of emergency care attendances at each Emergency Department in the last three months.
answer_text: Information on the number of new and unplanned review attendances at each Emergency Department (ED) is published on a quarterly basis in the ‘Urgent and Emergency Care Waiting Time Statistics for Northern Ireland’ report. The most recent version of this report covers the period January to March 2024, with the total number of ED attendances at each ED detailed in table 11B on page 37 of the report, which is available to view or download at: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/hs-niwts-ecwt-q4-23-24.pdf Please note that urgent and emergency care data for clinical care episodes completed on or after 9th November 2023 in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (SEHSCT) are still being developed following the Trust’s transition to completely digitised health records. Therefore, SEHSCT data has been excluded from all analysis in this report to allow for comparisons to be made with the four remaining Health and Social Care Trusts at previous time points. As such, all Department Type and Northern Ireland level figures presented throughout this report only include those for Belfast, Northern, Southern and Western Health and Social Care Trusts.In the interim, some provisional figures have been produced for SEHSCT, covering only the current quarter January - March 2024. These are presented in Tables A-E on pages 5 to 6 of the report and are considered to be ‘official statistics in development’. Caution must be exercised when using these figures as they may be subject to change and should not be used to make comparisons over time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans to address access to healthcare and support services for homeless individuals and families staying in non-standard accommodation.
answer_text: The provision of healthcare support is a matter for the Minister of Health.  Health care and support service requirements will vary and are dependent on individual circumstances.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the regeneration plan for the Fort George site, including the progress of the health hub.
answer_text: My Department continues to work with the Western Health and Social Care Trust, the Council’s Planning team, and other key statutory agencies in preparing the planning application for the development of the Fort George site, which includes the health hub. While we are still in the Pre-Application Discussion period, it is expected that this will lead to a live planning application.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department plans to engage with communities and stakeholders to allow their input on policies related to the proliferation of non-standard accommodation in their area.
answer_text: No, my Department does not have any plans in this regard.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will consider introducing a Baby Box initiative, similar to what is provided in Scotland.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) what plans his Department has to address flooding on Foyle Road, Derry; and (ii) for an update on the Strategic Drainage Infrastructure Area Plan for this area.
answer_text: NI Water has advised that the Culmore Drainage Area Plan (DAP) has now been completed and has not identified a requirement for any capital interventions to address out of sewer flooding from NI Water infrastructure in this area. In addition, NI Water has also recently undertaken some storm separation work around the Lone Moor Road area, this has involved removing storm water from the combined sewer network. Further investigations are ongoing in the area to identify other potential opportunities for storm separation.My Department is continuing to lead the development of a Strategic Drainage Infrastructure Plan for Derry, similar to the Living With Water in Belfast Plan,  in collaboration with key stakeholders. As part of this development my Department published ‘A Vision for Living With Water in Derry’ in May 2023, and I intend to publish the draft Living With Water in Derry Plan for consultation later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when his Department will publish an Environmental Improvement Plan for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer I gave to AQW 10962/22-27.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the engagement his Department has had with the Housing Executive, in each of the last four years, to ensure the strategic allocation of temporary accommodation to optimise spend and reduce costs.
answer_text: The increasing use of Temporary Accommodation is due to an unprecedented demand, arising not from an increase in homelessness presentation or acceptance, but from a change in the pattern of usage.  This has resulted in more people availing of the Housing Executive’s offer to provide them with temporary accommodation.  Over the last four years my Department has engaged on the issue of temporary accommodation via multiple quarterly and monthly meetings and more recently I have requested that the Housing Executive provide me with a specific monthly update on the issue of Temporary Accommodation, including progress to address the current position. This will allow me to be fully satisfied that the Housing Executive is doing all it can to manage the use of temporary accommodation, and the resultant cost.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department intends to legislate to relax the current legislation in relation to the use of e-scooters on public roads.
answer_text: Currently there are no regulations here associated with the use of e-scooters. The position remains that it is illegal to use e-scooters on public roads and public spaces here. You may be aware that the Department for Transport announced its intention to create regulations which will legalise the use of electric scooters in England. The introduction of regulations will only apply in England. However, currently there is no detail on timings for the introduction of this legislation. My officials will continue to monitor developments there and in due course I will consider the way forward for here.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) what steps her Department has taken to improve broadband connectivity in rural and under-served areas; and (ii) for an update on the contract for Project Gigabit.
answer_text: My Department is managing Project Stratum, a broadband investment project which is delivering gigabit-capable broadband to over 80,000 premises which were unable to access to a service of 30 Megabit per second; the Project Stratum intervention area is more than 97% rural.  To date, over 74,000 eligible premises have been addressed under the project by the contractor, Fibrus Networks Ltd. Prior to Project Stratum, my Department had invested £78 million in various projects to improve broadband provision here.In relation to Project Gigabit, my Department has carried out an Open Market Review and public consultation to determine the current and planned availability of gigabit-capable broadband availability here, and is carrying out Pre-Procurement Market Engagement with interested suppliers in expectation of carrying out a formal procurement exercise for the project later this year. My Department expects to award a contract to for Project Gigabit by March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has considered allocating funding to allow the return of the Housing Executive renovation grant.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has responsibility for administering a range of grants including four discretionary Home Improvement Grants. •	Renovation Grant, •	Replacement Grant, •	Home Repair Assistance Grant and •	Common Parts Grant. Due to budget pressures these discretionary grants are currently only made available in exceptional circumstances. The Executive agreed a 2024-25 budget on 25 April 2024.  The funding envelope awarded to DfC for 2024-25 is heavily constrained, and the level of both Resource and Capital requirements identified by DfC far exceeds the funding allocation.I am currently considering options on how my Department could live within its 2024-25 Budget allocation and difficult decisions will be required in order to manage pressures and deliver a balanced budget for 2024-25.Once I have made decisions on the budget, NIHE will be provided with a final 2024-25 budget allocation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the cost of taxi provision for temporary accommodation applicants travelling to the Derry City and Strabane District Council area; and (ii) the number of journeys made, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive and I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive is unable to provide the information requested. We do not record the destination of the journeys made and taxi costs are recorded based on the area office in which the applicant presents.  Given that the destination is not recorded we are also unable to confirm the number of journeys made in each of the last five years to Derry City and Strabane District Council area.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with the Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the tailored support available for patients suffering from chronic fatigue syndrome.
answer_text: Patients who have symptoms of ME/CFS and have contacted their GP should be offered treatment for their symptoms in line with NICE guidelines.  The GP may refer the patient to specialists to exclude other potential underlying causes and may refer the patient for therapeutic interventions.Although the current arrangements for the provision of ME/CFS services in Health and Social Care Trusts varies in each trust; support includes occupational therapy led ME/CFS programme, self-management programmes and support and symptom management through core services that best meet the individual’s needs.My officials continue to explore opportunities to build on the current support offered for patients suffering from chronic fatigue syndrome.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of how many police officers are required to enable the PSNI to discharge its duties effectively.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the total agency spend in the 2023-24 financial year, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Trust expenditure on agency staff has been incurred to ensure that safe and effective services are sustained and contributes importantly to maintaining service provision to patients and clients. HSC Trusts employ agency staff for several reasons, for example, cover for sickness and maternity/paternity leave; cover for existing vacancies; and when demand increases over the winter months.With costs increasing substantially over the last number of years I have been concerned of the impact they are having on the HSC budget and recognise that these costs are not sustainable, particularly at a time of serious financial pressures right across the public sector. My Department has therefore undertaken significant work to try and alleviate the workforce pressures across HSC, which includes addressing the issues of rising agency and locum expenditure. Work has been ongoing regionally across HSC, under the Regional Agency Reduction Implementation Group (ARIG) with significant progress already made towards the cessation of ‘Off-Framework’ agency use in Nursing and Midwifery and Social Workers across NI’s health service. The Nursing and Social Work staff groups focussed on by ARIG has shown a projected reduction of approximately £40m.Alongside this, work has also been focused on Recruitment, Retention and Training of staff, in addition to a dedicated workstream examining the reform of the Bank system. Further work in ongoing to reduce ‘Off-Framework’ agency for Medical and Dental, and other staff groups. Total agency expenditure by Trust, (which excludes directly employed locums) in 2023-24 is available for April 2023 – December 2023, and is set out in the table below. TrustApr '23 - Dec '23£'000Belfast HSC Trust92,816Northern HSC Trust59,334Northern Ireland Ambulance Service1,507South Eastern HSC Trust38,936Southern HSC Trust41,667Western HSC Trust45,595Total279,855

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of (a) bed and breakfasts; (b) guesthouses; and (c) hotels being used for temporary accommodation by the Housing Executive; and (ii) the number of beds being occupied, in each of the last five years, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive and I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“In relation to (i) (a), (b) and (c) the Housing Executive would refer to AQW 9293 which noted the following:‘The Housing Executive does not maintain a list of non-standard premises (Hotel/B&B) by individual properties which have been used over the past 5 years. The Housing Executive records data on non-standard placements using a free text field on our Housing Management System and therefore, there is no readily identifiable option to extract placements in non-standard by number of individual premises.’Provision of the requested data would involve a review of all non-standard placements from 1st April 2018 to 31st March 2023 to identify the number of premises referred to in the free text fields. In relation to part (ii), it should be noted that the Housing Executive does not record non-standard placements by the number of beds being occupied.”     For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with the Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the advantages of community transport in (i) reducing isolation; and (ii) connecting rural communities.
answer_text: I fully recognise the many direct benefits community transport brings to its users, including a reduction in isolation for some of the most vulnerable in our society, particularly the elderly and people with a disability.  Community transport facilitates a reduction in isolation by providing increased independence and social connections to other passengers, the driver, friends, family and local communities.  It also enables access to essential goods and services as well as to education, training, volunteering, and employment opportunities. Community transport significantly enhances daily lives and improves the physical and mental health and well-being of users.  This in turn has positive impacts on the health and social care sector.The benefits of community transport are far reaching and go beyond the direct benefits to the user. The wider societal and economic benefits include the ability to connect rural communities by providing transport to local community groups, events and health services such as GPs and dentists. Community transport also helps connect rural communities by offering employment and volunteering opportunities and by facilitating spend in local business and services.  In addition, community transport makes an important contribution in connecting rural communities to the wider public transport network. These factors help rural communities to survive and thrive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he intends to remove the Right to Buy for Housing Executive tenants.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer to the previous question AQW 7910/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has considered the introduction of specialist care centres for patients with Niemann-Pick disease.
answer_text: Niemann-Pick diseases are a group of rare inherited lysosomal storage disorders that can affect both children and adults. There are 8 specialist care centres within the UK that provide comprehensive, integrated multi-disciplinary care for adults and children, with lysosomal storage disorders, which includes Neimann-Pick disease. The number of patients within Northern Ireland with Niemann-Pick disease is extremely low. Whilst our aim is to offer as much specialist care as possible within Northern Ireland where it is safe and sustainable to do so, the networked approach currently in place allows access to, and services to be provided to our Niemann-Pick population from specialist UK centres. This networked approach with GB specialist centres ensures that the multi-disciplinary team that manages Niemann-Pick patients will see sufficient numbers to maintain expertise and with such patients treated efficiently, and specialist therapies used most effectively.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement his Department has had with the Department for Work and Pensions in relation to Universal Credit online identity verification issues for customers with a Northern Ireland-issued driving licence or Irish passport.
answer_text: The Universal Credit online Identity Verification system currently used by the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) does not have the capability to recognise non-UK passports or Northern Irish driving licences. I can confirm that this has been raised with DWP on a number of occasions, before and after implementation of Universal Credit in Northern Ireland. DWP are reviewing identity verification functionality and have been advised of the Northern Ireland specific requirements.	In the meantime, ID verification can be completed using non-UK passports and Northern Ireland driving licences, as well as many other acceptable forms of documentation, at a face to face appointment in a Jobs & Benefits office.  ID can also be verified via biographical questions or through a third party.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will provide additional funding for community transport for 2024-25.
answer_text: I fully recognise the importance of community transport and how both rural Dial-a-Lift (DAL) and urban Disability Action Transport Scheme (DATS) services continue to help improve access to local health appointments, shops and facilities, voluntary groups, friends and family. These services enable people to be independent and remain connected to their local communities.While funding for community transport is not part of my Department’s statutory defined spend, in the absence of an agreed budget at the beginning of the financial year, I approved interim funding for both DAL and DATS for April to June 2024 based on 2023/24 funding levels to support continued provision of community transport. The overall budget allocation for 2024/25 is currently being considered.My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.  The value of our infrastructure cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 10801/22-27, whether a comparative analysis has been conducted in relation to the number of domestic properties per capita in Northern Ireland and other regions across the UK.
answer_text: The Department does not hold information in relation to comparative analysis on properties per population.Information on housing stock per 1,000 population for here, Britain and the Republic of Ireland is published by the Department for Communities in its annual Housing Statistics publication.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in order to alleviate pressures on the wider health system, whether he has considered a framework for a wider range of pharmacy-led clinical services in the community, including conducting medication reviews.
answer_text: The HSC in Northern Ireland benefits from having pharmacists involved in the care of patients in all hospitals, general practices and community pharmacies. Strategically we are seeking to make the best use of the skills and expertise of this cross-sector workforce to support the optimal and safe use and supply of medicines. My Department has recently launched a new Community Pharmacy Strategic Plan 2030 which aims to fully realise the potential for community pharmacy services to support better health outcomes from medicines and to prevent illness. Building on the trusted role that community pharmacies already have in supplying medicines, the plan outlines a vision where community pharmacies can offer more clinical services that utilise the skills of their teams to offer the public safe, convenient, and faster access to care. Implementation of the Strategic Plan will be progressed through four major reform programmes for services, legislation, workforce, and digital enablers that together will transform community pharmacy’s role within the HSC. Full implementation will require targeted investment to implement reforms, including new digital systems and service developments, and is subject to available funding. My Department will continue to collaborate with Community Pharmacy NI and the pharmacy profession to progress a phased approach to implementation over the coming years.Following an initial five year rollout from 2016, every GP practice in Northern Ireland now has a pharmacist working as an integral part of their clinical team, using their skills and experience to improve patient outcomes, improve the safety of prescribing through medication reviews and reconciliation, reduce the level of medication errors and reduce waste through management of prescribing systems. Pharmacists also release much needed capacity within the general practice team so that GPs can focus more of their time on the care and management of patients with complex medical needs.Informed by a positive evaluation which demonstrated significant improvements in the safety, efficiency, and consistency of prescribing in general practice, my Department has recently published a new strategy for this sector titled, GPP NI 2030 – A strategy for General Practice in Northern Ireland. This strategy outlines ambitious plans for the future development of the general practice pharmacy sector, including how pharmacists will contribute fully to optimising the health outcomes of our population as the clinical leads for medicines within the general practice team. My Department is committed to working to progress implementation of the strategy in the coming years, however realizing the full potential of the pharmacy workforce will require investment to support service capacity and capability, and so full implementation is dependent on securing the additional resources required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will confirm his Department's funding allocation for health-related courses at Ulster University's Magee Campus.
answer_text: On 7 May 2024, my officials wrote to Ulster University confirming the numbers of pre-registration training places being commissioned by my Department on healthcare programmes commencing during the 2024/25 academic year.  This included the pre-registration Nursing and Allied Health programmes provided at Ulster University’s Magee Campus. Over the coming months, my officials will confirm the intake, effective from January 2025, onto the University’s Physician Associate programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 10618/22-27, whether this work will include the implementation of Action 35 through to Action 38 of the Cancer Strategy 2022-2032.
answer_text: Although unfunded, work to implement Actions 35 to 38 of the Cancer Strategy is underway.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has specific benchmarks in place to monitor and improve the availability of domestic properties relative to the population.
answer_text: The annual Northern Ireland Housing Statistics report provides information covering: supply; energy; social renting demand; private renting demand; owner occupier demand and household characteristics. The report is publicly available at:https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/topics/housing-statistics

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department will introduce a ban on single-use mini shampoo bottles in hotels.
answer_text: There has been recent publicity about proposals to end the practice of providing single use packaging for cosmetics, hygiene, and toiletry products for use in the accommodation sector. 		The proposal for restricting such products is contained within a new EU Regulation on Packaging and Packaging Waste.			The proposal for a new Regulation on Packaging and Packaging Waste has received provisional agreement at EU level, potentially coming into effect at the end of 2024 or the beginning of 2025. 		Its application would begin with a transition period of 18 months.	The precise application of the new Packaging and Packaging Waste Regulation in NI under the Windsor Framework will be subject to final negotiations between the UK Government and the EU.Separately, transposition of EU’s Directive on single-use plastics into domestic law will address cotton bud sticks, cutlery, plates, straws and stirrers, sticks for balloons, food containers, cups for beverages, beverage containers, cigarette butts, plus wet wipes and sanitary items.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 5809/22-27, whether his Department will reconsider increasing the Rate Support Grant for the poorest local council areas.
answer_text: The funding available for my Department was set within the context of a constrained financial position for 2024/25 and considered the overall Departmental budget position against competing priorities. The Rates Support Grant opening budget for 2024/25 has been set at £3.124m, which is a reduction of £1.8m on the previous financial year. I have launched an Equality Impact Assessment consultation on my initial decisions on my Departments 2024-25 budget. The consultation will run from 11 June until 3 September 2024, and I have asked for views on the allocation of funds for the financial year 2024-25.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 8864/22-27, whether changes to the Social Sector Size Criteria have been assessed following confirmation of the Budget 2024-25.
answer_text: In the Budget for 2024-25 my Department was allocated the required funding allocation for Social Sector Size Criteria mitigation payments.  Those people affected by the Social Sector Size Criteria will continue to receive mitigation in full and there will be no change to that position in this financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will update the Autism Strategy 2023-2028 to include a commitment to ensure (i) tailored mental health services for adults and children with autism; and (ii) training for mental health professionals, in line with Clause 4D of the Autism (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: It is accepted that autistic people are at higher risk of having negative mental health outcomes. While it is vital that such individuals have good and equitable access to mental health services, and that those services are able to cope with their specific needs, it is not considered appropriate to develop dedicated mental health services designed to treat those individuals.All HSC Trusts offer training and support to all staff in the effective support of those patients with communication challenges and barriers, including autism, when accessing services, whilst also supporting staff to be more aware.  HSC Trusts do take a service-wide approach to addressing the needs of those with Autism. This includes training for all staff, either run by individual Trusts, or commissioned from external organisations, as well as self-directed learning for those with a particular identified training need.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will consider a wildflower planting scheme for road verges to promote biodiversity and reduce maintenance costs.
answer_text: My Department’s approach to verge management was recently changed to increase the focus on environmental benefit, without compromising on roads safety or our duties to deal with noxious weeds on Departmental land. This change occurred because I recognise the environmental benefits of road verges and our approach seeks to encourage the establishment of wildflowers in these areas. Wildflowers, and in particular native species, promote biodiversity, provide habitats for pollinators, and can enhance the aesthetic value of an area. The planting and maintenance of verges to create wildflower areas involves a level of additional investment and is not suitable for all areas. However, my Department has supported wildflower planting on verges through a pilot scheme in partnership with Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council. Using an arrangement such as this, it would be possible to increase the number of areas where wildflower planting schemes could be developed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will work with other Departments to secure funding for the Foyle Cup 2024.
answer_text: I understand that Tourism NI (TNI) has offered to meet the organisers of the Foyle Cup to discuss the development potential of The Foyle Cup, and how TNI may support it to progress as a tourism experience that motivates international visitors to travel to attend.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 10723/22-27, (i) why these technical barriers did not apply to previous welfare mitigations; and (ii) whether the Department has considered establishing a welfare supplementary payment that would effectively remove the Two Child Limit.
answer_text: The option of amending the DWP benefit systems to deliver the existing welfare mitigations was examined but it was determined that functionality could not be delivered to support the introduction of welfare supplementary payments by May 2016.  A decision was taken, therefore, to develop a bespoke standalone IT system to support mitigation payments in Northern Ireland.Officials are currently finalising work to update the estimated costs for all of the recommendations coming out of the Independent Advisory Panel Report, including mitigating the Universal Credit two child limit, to help inform decisions on this matter.Any future mitigation package will need to be agreed by the Executive and assessed in the climate of financial constraint and the requirement to ensure that budgets remain sustainable over the coming years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for a breakdown of (i) the organisations whose bids were successful to the International Tourism Events Fund 2024-25; and; (ii) the total funding allocated to each organisation.
answer_text: Twenty one (21) applications were received to the scheme and have now progressed through a competitive scoring and assessment process. Notifications of the outcome were sent to 13 successful and 8 unsuccessful applicants on 1 May 2024.Funding requests were received totalling £1,430,000, against a projected scheme budget of £650,000.Tourism NI will not be in a position to confirm awards until all Letters of Offer to successful applicants have been accepted, signed, and returned as per the usual process. Once all Letters of Offer have been finalised, a full list of successful organisations and the funding allocated to each will be published on www.tourismni.com/events.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department will consider funding the Foyle Cup 2024.
answer_text: My Department is facing significant pressures and is dealing with an extremely challenging budget outlook for the year ahead.  For this reason regrettably my Department does not have the budget available to fund the Foyle Cup 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the frequency of DfI Roads' routine gully maintenance in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. As a result, my Department has only been able to deliver a limited service across a range of functions including gully emptying. The value of our infrastructure cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made.The level of service in place since 2014-15 is summarised below;2014-15 – Initially, all gullies in urban areas were to be cleaned twice annually and gullies in rural areas once annually. In August 2014, urban gully cleaning was reduced from twice annually to once annually.2015-16 – Initially, responsive gully cleaning only was possible across all areas plus known flooding hotspots. From 1 July 2015, this increased to one full cycle of gully cleaning in all areas.2016-17 – Initially, one full cycle of gully cleaning in all areas. From 20 June 2016, a second gully clean in urban areas was permitted.2017-18 to date - Currently my Department’s target is to clean all gullies once annually.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the new housing development at the former Tillie and Henderson site, Derry, will include social tenants.
answer_text: I can confirm that the scheme at the former Tillie and Henderson site, will include social housing tenants.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether he will consider introducing legislation to control the sale of vapes; and (ii) what engagement he has had with the Minister of Education around raising awareness of the dangers of vaping and spice.
answer_text: In 2022, regulations prohibiting the sale of nicotine inhaling products to under 18s came into force in NI. Subject to Assembly legislative consent, the UK Tobacco and Vapes Bill will provide further powers to help us tackle the growing problem of youth vaping. The consultation on these proposals was shared with the Department of Education and distributed to schools. The Public Health Agency (PHA) works directly with schools and the education sector to raise awareness of issues around vaping, including the risks of vaping synthetic substances such as ‘spice’. A pilot school-based stop smoking and vaping service is underway in the Belfast and South Eastern areas. A recent PHA task and finish group on vaping also included representation from CCEA. Resources produced by the group have been shared with post primary schools, SEN schools and regional colleges and can be accessed online at: https://www.publichealth.hscni.net/vaping.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the barriers that prevents the introduction of biennial MOT testing under existing legislation.
answer_text: I can advise that MOT testing is conducted in compliance with The Road Traffic (Northern Ireland) Order 1995, the Motor Vehicle Testing Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2003 and the Goods Vehicle Testing Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2003. The existing legislation stipulates that private cars and motorcycles are first MOT tested at 4 years old and light goods vehicles under 3,500kg are first tested at 3 years old. Under the aforementioned legislation each of those vehicle categories are then tested annually thereafter by the Department. The existing legislative framework does not provide scope for biennial MOT testing.  A move to biennial MOT testing would involve significant policy development, engagement with a range of stakeholders and be subject to public consultation and then potentially amendment to primary legislation. As you are aware vehicle testing is a devolved matter, and so any move to biennial MOT testing would entail an Act of the NI Assembly. You will also be aware that this process could take a number of years to reach a conclusion.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has considered the introduction of an online mapping system to allow DfI Roads staff to record instances when they have been unable to clean gullies due to parked vehicles, ensuring they are revisited at a later date.
answer_text: My Department is currently developing a digital mobile working solution that will display gully locations on a tablet device to allow contractors to update actions during routine cleaning operations.  This will include exception reporting, such as being unable to clean gullies due to parked vehicles.  It is envisaged that the testing of this software solution will begin later this year.As you will appreciate, in built-up urban locations, were there is extensive road side parking, it can be difficult to gain access to clean all gullies, even after several visits.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 6085/22-27, to detail (i) the work carried out by his Department; and (ii) when a ban on conversion therapy will be implemented.
answer_text: Work has been taken forward within my Department to inform policy proposals on a ban on conversion practices and to identify options for the next steps in development, taking into consideration all of the elements involved. Officials have also worked closely with colleagues from other jurisdictions to learn from their experience. I cannot commit to a specific timescale for bringing forward policy proposals. It is important to take the appropriate amount of time to ensure that they are robust and fit for purpose.  Any legislation will be published only following extensive engagement with all those who have an interest, careful formulation of policy and full public consultation on the issues involved.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the (i) minimum unit cost; (ii) mean unit cost; and (iii) maximum unit cost of each unit of social housing completed in the 2023-24 financial year.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“We are unable to give the minimum unit cost or the maximum unit cost of social housing units completed in 2023/24 because we do not hold this information on individual units.We can, however, provide the mean cost because we do hold such information at a scheme level.The mean cost of social housing units completed (1,403) in 2023/24 was £166k.The mean grant paid for social housing units completed in 2023/24 was £94k”.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly at  Public.Affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the failure to record the number of patients under the care of community health services at the time of their death.

To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 10722/22-27, whether consideration will be given to include a marker to indicate whether individuals were under the care of community health services at the time of their death, as part of official statistics.
answer_text: NISRA is committed to the ongoing review and improvement of all official statistical outputs. The collection of this information via the death registration process would be difficult for a number of reasons: it would require the informant of the death to have an understanding of the definition and coverage of community health services, the informant being aware of the deceased’s interaction with such services; and it would also rely on the informant being prepared to offer that information. An alternative route to acquire this information could be to link administrative information on community health service customers directly to death registrations. Separate investigation of the existing data systems, and the appropriate legal gateways for this linkage would be required, as well as work to establish clear user need and priority.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will implement a mortgage rescue scheme to protect both homeowners and private renters at risk of losing their home.
answer_text: The Executive Office is committed to the delivery of financial savings through efficiency measures and ensuring that value for money is secured through all activities and plans.  2023-24 Spending Plans are published on the TEO website. As we develop financial plans for 2024-25, especially in the continuing constrained budget environment, we are committed to delivering savings through efficiencies and by identifying and delivering opportunities for transformation.  This will include reviewing our activities, plans and structures in order to ensure that all opportunities for efficiencies are identified to ensure best value is achieved.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 10430/22-27, to detail a timeframe as to when his Department intends to assess these findings.

To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department will consider proposals put forward by the Department for Work and Pensions to reform the disability benefits system amid the rising number of claimants with a mental health condition.
answer_text: My officials and I are currently engaging with officials in the Department for Work and Pensions to understand the proposed changes to disability benefits and will assess the impact on Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail plans to address the growing number of social housing applications from people who are homeless due to the loss of private rented accommodation.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has statutory responsibility for responding to homelessness, including provision of homelessness accommodation, as such I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive’s approach to supporting households who are homeless or threatened with homelessness, including those who are impacted by loss of private rented accommodation, is outlined in the Homelessness Strategy 2022-27 which can be found via: https://www.nihe.gov.uk/getattachment/73313718-aa0e-4aae-b122-6573dcab88c7/Ending-Homelessness-Together-Homelessness-Strategy-2022-27.pdf”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on progressing the necessary funding required by the City of Derry Airport, given that it is recognised that the 2024-25 financial year will be a critical year for the airport in achieving continued growth.
answer_text: As Minister for Infrastructure, my powers relating to our three main airports are limited to the Airports (NI) Order 1994.  These are powers to control noise, control land in the interests of the safe and efficient use of airports, make byelaws, provide for airport constabularies and airport consultative committees and to give grants to assist capital expenditure.In April 2021 Derry City and Strabane District Council (DC&SDC) submitted the original Business Case requesting support for City of Derry Airport.  My officials had requested updated information in the form of an addendum to the original Business Case which was received by my Department on 21 March 2024.  This information was considered and a request for clarification relating to parts of the addendum was sent to DC&SDC on 22 April 2024.  DC&SDC provided further information and clarification on 3 May 2024 and this is now being considered by my officials.I remain committed to working with Executive colleagues to listen to the case brought forward by Derry City and Strabane District Council for support for City of Derry Airport, and to working together with the rest of the Executive to identify ways to support our airports.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, following his statement in April 2024 in relation to the beneficial impact the A6 has had on air quality in Dungiven, whether action has been taken to measure the impact of air quality in Drumahoe.
answer_text: An Environmental Assessment of the A6 Dualling scheme, which included the section in the vicinity of Drumahoe, was undertaken in 2011. This assessment determined that properties along the existing route, particularly between Caw and Drumahoe, where the preferred route is offline, may experience a marginal improvement in air quality, due to the reassignment of some strategic traffic.My Department has not specifically measured the impact of air quality in Drumahoe as Air Quality Monitoring Stations are the responsibility of  DAERA, with  others managed by district councils. Drumahoe is not in an Air Quality Management Area and does not have an Air Quality Monitoring Station.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his plan to deliver the Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for Soccer.
answer_text: The Sub-Regional Programme is a key priority for me and I hope to be in the position to make an announcement shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the work planned for the A6 in the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: There are currently no planned works on the A6 in the 2024-25 financial year, beyond routine maintenance.  My officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the NHS pay settlement will be awarded to staff in June 2024.
answer_text: Following the issue of formal pay circulars on 10 April 2024, the Regional Payroll team within the Business Services Organisation have been working with colleagues in Trusts to agree a plan to implement the pay award. Substantive pay awards for AfC and for doctors and dentists in hospital settings are due to be processed in the June pay run. The non-consolidated award for AfC staff will follow in August.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 6355/22-27, when he will assess the findings of the evaluation.
answer_text: I refer Mr Durkan to the answer I gave to AQW 7884/22-27 on 15 March, also in pursuant of AQW 6355/22-27, which I believe answers his question.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what action he is taking to reduce the number of staff in his Department with temporary promotions lasting over a three year period.
answer_text: NICS has a long standing policy on the use of temporary promotions which allows Departments to temporarily fill vacant posts where there is a clear business need and no immediate alternative available.  For example, when there is no supply of staff at particular grades / disciplines, or a particular skillset is required for a time bound period.  It also makes clear, however, that, by their nature, these are temporary arrangements.  My officials continue to work with NICS HR in the Department of Finance – who provide a centralised HR function for the NI Civil Service – to fill all affordable vacancies as quickly as possible.  As always, these appointments will on the basis of merit through fair and open competition.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) whether there is a fitness standard framework for temporary accommodation; and (ii) whether the Housing Executive routinely assesses temporary accommodation properties.
answer_text: The Fitness Standard applies to all houses, irrespective of tenure.The Housing Executive has statutory responsibility for responding to homelessness, including provision of homelessness accommodation, as such I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“In the acquisition of Single Lets the Housing Executive the Housing Executive will inspect the property and ensure a number of criteria are met. If any of the criteria are not met, and/or the inspection at the point of acquisition is unsatisfactory, the Housing Executive will not sure the property.  The Housing Executive will also ensure the required certificates are renewed on an annual basis as part of the continued use of any Single Let property. The Housing Executive also avails of a range of temporary accommodation options provided by the voluntary sector.  These services are funded and managed by Supporting People to ensure quality support is delivered and that the temporary accommodation is appropriate for households who are homeless or threatened with homelessness. For placements in non-standard accommodation (B&B/Hotels), the Housing Executive is acquiring the use of accommodation in the same manner of other customer groups who avail of Hotels/B&Bs whereby the respective Hotel/B&B is subject to the relevant checks and accreditation in order to operate as a short-term accommodation.  The Housing Executive is not responsible for such inspections.  While the Housing Executive is not responsible for inspections relevant to accreditation, an exercise took place on November 2023 whereby non-standard properties were subject to a process which involved visits from Housing Executive staff and a repeat exercise is currently being planned.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 9751/22-27, to detail the number of non-standard accommodation placements made to each council area, rather than from which the placement has been made.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The table below details placements in non-standard temporary accommodation, as of 31st December 2023, by the area to which the placement was made. Council Area to which placement was madeTotal Number of PlacementsAntrim & Newtownabbey25Ards and North Down3Armagh, Banbridge and Craigavon9Belfast77Causeway41Derry City & Strabane222Fermanagh and Omagh13Lisburn1Mid and East Antrim5Mid Ulster1Total397It should be noted that in order to provide the detail required a live database was used and therefore, a further 2 placements have been added when compared to the 395 placements outlined in AQW 9751. These additional placements are a result of 2 cases having been keyed after the information for AQW 9751 was extracted. The Housing Executive does not routinely report placements by the area to which they are made and to facilitate this request, a one-off exercise was carried out which involved a line by line review of ‘free text’ fields which contain the address of each respective placement as of 31st December 2023.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of gastroenterologists specialising in Pelvic Radiation Disease; and (ii) the services available for patients suffering from this condition, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Pelvic Radiation Disease (PRD) can affect patients who have undergone radiotherapy treatment for abdominal or pelvic cancers.  This includes cervical, uterine, bowel and prostate cancers.  The condition affects non-cancerous body tissue.  It causes difficulties as well as pain and/or discomfort in the area of the body that has received radiotherapy.The following information has been received from HSC Trusts on the services offered for PRD.Belfast Trust(i)	 There is no specific gastroenterologist that patients are referred to in the Belfast Trust, and no specific clinic for patients to attend. (ii) 	These patients are all followed up by consultant clinical oncologists and supporting teams, including clinical nurse specialists.  They can also be referred on to other appropriate specialties when required. Patients may for example, see a gastroenterologist for symptoms such as rectal bleeding, or a gynaecologist for sexual dysfunction.(iii) If a patient’s condition results in severe nutritional difficulties and/or needing extensive surgery for their PRD they will be managed by the Regional Intestinal Failure Service in Belfast Trust, as is the case with all Gastrointestinal Disease. There are currently a small number of patients with PRD (less than 10) under the care of the Regional Intestinal Failure Team Northern Trust(i) 	In relation to Gastroenterology referrals for radiation proctitis there are 2 Gastroenterologists and 1 Nurse Endoscopist who specialise in this treatment in the Northern Trust.(ii) 	The treatment offered by Gastroenterologists for radiation proctitis is intra-rectal formalin treatmentSouth Eastern Trust(i)	Patients referred to the South Eastern HSC Trust are seen by a consultant member of the gastroenterology team.  Like any condition, where the level of clinical need is beyond the scope of the expertise of a clinician, they will seek advice, or refer to a specialist centre. (ii)	There are various treatment plans and options available to people with this condition, including outpatient review, with availability for a rapid review when clinically required.  Following this, patients have access to appropriate clinical management, including endoscopic treatment, dietetic support and a specialist gastroenterology pharmacist for treatments such as sucralfate enemas for patients with radiation proctopathyThere is no specialist centre in Northern Ireland for this condition although referral would be made to the intestinal failure team for specific nutritional management.Southern Trust(i) 	In the Southern Trust there are no Gastroenterology Consultants who specialise in Pelvic Radiation Disease.Western Trust(i)	There are no Gastroenterologists at present in the Western Trust that specialise in Pelvic Radiation Disease.(ii)	There are no services available for Pelvic Radiation Disease in the Western Trust post treatment side effects.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will launch a public inquiry into missed abnormalities in cervical screening.
answer_text: I will await the outcome of the Cervical Cytology Review looking at cases in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust before considering the findings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any engagement she has had with the Minister of Education in relation to funding a pay and grading review for education support staff.
answer_text: I have engaged with the Education Minister regarding the funding to implement the outcome of the pay and regrading review of non-teaching support staff both in correspondence, and when we have met to discuss the Executive’s Budget 2024-25.In agreeing the Budget 2024-25 on 25 April, the Executive also agreed that I will write to the Chief Secretary to the Treasury to seek approval from the Treasury to reprofile some of the repurposed funds provided in the financial package for this purpose once costs are confirmed.
