question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of pupils deemed chronic absentees or with long term absences in (i) primary; and (ii) post-primary settings in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The department does not hold information on the duration of absences just the total absence sessions recorded against each pupil attending primary and post primary schools.  The table below shows the number and percentage of pupils with “persistent” absence, defined as 10 per cent or more half day sessions recorded as absent, for the five most recent years that data is available.Primary schoolsPost-Primary schoolsNumber%Number%2017/1822,4031320,042202018/1920,3541223,345212019/2026,9561526,769232020/2119,9811122,289182021/2248,3342846,34638Source: Annual School CensusNote: Primary includes years 1 to 7; Post-Primary includes years 8 to 12In-year management data for 2023/24 (which is unverified) suggests that the level of “persistent” absence has reduced compared with 2021/22. Data for 2023/24 (by Free School Meal Entitlement Band) is published monthly and can be found at: Pupil Absence Data by Free School Meal Entitlement (FSME) Bands | Department of Education (education-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the total number of domestic properties per capita.
answer_text: The total number of domestic properties in the Valuation List at 31st March 2024 is 843,482.  Based on the most recent mid-year population estimates derived from Census 2021, the total number of domestic properties per capita is 0.44.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will establish a national registry for perinatal and neonatal stroke to provide support for impacted babies and their parents.
answer_text: The Health and Social Care system in Northern Ireland follows a national clinical guideline for diagnosis, management and rehabilitation of Stroke in Childhood, which was developed by the Royal College of Paediatrics and Child Health in 2017 and updated in 2021. While my Department does not have plans to establish a registry at this time, the need to ensure critical support is provided to babies affected by stroke is recognised. To that end, the Child Health Partnership is considering the need for the development of a Paediatric Stroke Pathway in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide clarity on the IT infrastructure which would be required to remove the Two Child Limit.
answer_text: The Universal Credit IT system is owned and developed by the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) for the delivery of Universal Credit on a UK wide basis. DWP would therefore have to deploy developers to complete a comprehensive assessment of the scope and functionality required to support the removal of the Two Child Limit for Northern Ireland only.As there are both legislative and technical barriers to making this change, it would take considerable time and cost to resolve and could not be scoped further without agreed funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of patients that died while under the care of community mental health services, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: NISRA produces official statistics on death registration information as collected by the General Register Office. The information requested is not collected as part of the death registration process. While place of death data is available and can be used to indicate, for example, deaths at home by underlying cause, there is no marker to indicate if individuals were under the care of community health services at the time of death.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she intends to introduce legislation to make it an offence to abuse, threaten or assault a public-facing retail worker.
answer_text: I condemn all abuse and violence directed at any frontline workers. No-one should face mistreatment whilst doing their job and serving the public and such attacks must not be tolerated. Assault of a retail worker can be prosecuted under existing laws designed to protect any member of the public from harm.  However, I intend to introduce an offence of assaulting a frontline worker in my proposed Sentencing Bill and will give further consideration to protections for these workers in that context.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the regulation and inspection of community-based mental health services.
answer_text: Unlike services that are required to register with RQIA under The Health and Personal Social Services (Quality, Improvement and Regulation) (Northern Ireland) Order 2003 (“the 2003 Order”) (for example, Nursing and Residential Care Homes), HSC Trust statutory services such as Community Mental Health Services are not required in law to register with RQIA in order to operate and are not subject to a specified frequency of inspection set out in Statutory Regulations. However, RQIA does have the power in accordance with Article 35 of 2003 Order to investigate either the management, provision or quality of the health and social care service for which a HSC Trust is responsible, including provision of mental health services in hospital or in the community.  Further, RQIA also has the power (Article 39 of the 2003 Order) to issue Improvement Notices on a particular HSC Trust where failings in minimum standards related to the provision of a service has been found; or can report its view or findings to the Department and include recommendations for improvement of the service.In addition to the 2003 Order legislation, RQIA has duties under the Mental Health Order (Northern Ireland) 1986 to keep under review the care and treatment of patients who are detained in hospital and those cared for in community settings. Following a Judicial Declaration in May 2023, RQIA reflected that, while there is a well-developed process for engaging with patients within Mental Health and Learning Disability hospital settings, there was a need to develop a protocol that would enable RQIA to inquire into issues of concern about possible detriment in care and treatment for patients living in the community, should concerns be raised directly with RQIA or based on other intelligence it receives. RQIA has developed an interim Inquiry Policy to enable an inquiry to be conducted into potential detriment of care and treatment for individual patients living with mental disorder in the community.  This interim Inquiry Policy involves engagement with family members or carers, service providers, and others, including legal representatives.  RQIA is currently piloting this in relation to a number of individual cases.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 7777/22-27, when automatic passporting for Help with Healthcare will be reinstated.
answer_text: As stated in my answer to AQW 5522/22-27, preparatory work has recently commenced in advance of updating the Travelling Expenses and Remission of Charges Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2004. The focus of work is to reinstate automatic passporting for those eligible to receive free sight tests and free Health Service dental treatment through the Help with Health Care (HwHC) scheme following the migration of their benefits to Universal Credit by the Department for Communities (DfC).The Department, in consultation with DfC, has implemented an interim solution to help ensure that people do not lose out on their entitlement. However, this is no longer an automatic system. It is paper based, and individuals must apply for an annual certificate. This has obvious implications for some of our most vulnerable people. While the Department is currently liaising with officials in DfC to explore options for streamline this process, it is clear that there is an urgent need to update our legislation to reinstate automatic passporting to those eligible.The Department will seek to maintain, as closely as possible, the caseload of recipients who are eligible for HwHC, balancing the continued protection of the health of members of society who need additional support without adding pressures on HSC budgets. My Department is currently liaising with other NI departments and colleagues in GB. This will inform the decision on setting the income threshold for UC becoming an automated passport benefit. A short public consultation will be conducted in the coming months on proposed changes to our legislation in relation to UC, with the associated legislative process commencing in due course thereafter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will implement a scheme similar to the Agri-Climate Rural Environment Scheme in the Republic of Ireland, to compensate farmers for loss of earnings due to large numbers of migratory birds leaving the land unusable.
answer_text: My Department’s current Farm Support and Development Programme is planned to contain similar elements the to the Agri-Climate Rural Environment Scheme (ACRES) that is being introduced in Ireland.  I am always keen to learn from others about interventions that can support both farmers and our rich biodiversity and have asked my officials to liaise with their Irish counterparts as this scheme progresses so that any key learning can be captured and applied as we move forward our farm support programmes.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the plans he has to improve follow-up support for cancer patients who experience late effects of treatment.
answer_text: Work has commenced to scope and benchmark the current provision of late effects services across Northern Ireland. It is anticipated that this will be completed in the current financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) when a review of the waiting list initiatives will be concluded; and (ii) whether a return of the Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme will be considered as part of this process.
To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will consider establishing another Spend Local Scheme to boost local economic activity and support small businesses.
answer_text: The High Street scheme which gave people £100 to spend in local businesses was established during covid at a cost of £136.5m. The resources are not available to re-introduce the scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether the Western Health and Social Care Trust audit process into abnormal smear test results has been concluded; and (ii) how many patients have been contacted to date as part of this process.
answer_text: As the Western Health and Social Care Trust (WHSCT) reported a higher than expected number of ‘unsatisfactory’ outcomes for invasive cervical cancer audits undertaken since 2019, the Public Health Agency (PHA) requested that any cervical screening cytology slides associated with these cases be sent to Public Health Wales for an external opinion. The reports from PH Wales have now been received and considered by WHSCT and shared with PHA. PHA is currently reviewing all information held relating to these cases and will bring forward any findings to the Department shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 8762/22-27, whether he will consider engaging with the Minister of Health to jointly deliver a public consultation on improved regulations in both non-surgical cosmetic procedures and the tattoo and body piercing industry.
answer_text: Once the Department of Health is in a position to consult on a new draft regulatory framework, my Department can engage and provide input on the areas relating to cosmetic procedures for which it has policy responsibility.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when he will provide an update on the review of the eligibility criteria for free school meals and uniform grants.
answer_text: The review of the eligibility criteria for free school meals and uniform grants is examining a range of options for increasing the numbers able to access free school meals and uniform grants. The options being considered include raising the income thresholds that apply to some of the means tested criteria, the introduction of universal free school meals provision to pupils in some school years and those that are a combination of the two. My officials are currently updating the modelling of the options to take into account the managed migration of those in receipt of some benefits to Universal Credit that is being led by the Department for Communities. I plan to launch a public consultation on the options for changing the eligibility criteria in the autumn of this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 8762/22-27, whether he will consider engaging with the Minister for Communities to jointly deliver a public consultation on improved regulations in both non-surgical cosmetic procedures and the tattoo and body piercing industry.
answer_text: As set out in my response to AQW 8762/22-27, my Department is currently operating within a constrained budgetary context and is required to make decisions in relation to the work that can be delivered within current resources. In that context, work on the review of the regulation is currently paused to allow for other priority projects to progress. When Departmental resources permit the review of regulation to restart it will provide a platform to consider identified improvements in the regulation and monitoring of relevant services. As part of the review, my officials will engage with all relevant stakeholders including the Department for Communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail what support schools provide to (i) staff; (ii) parents; and (iii) pupils for children diagnosed with (a) dyslexia; and (b) dyscalculia.
answer_text: Support for DyslexiaThe Education Authority Literacy Service (EALS) provides a broad range of training, information and suggested activities and resources to help parents and schools to support literacy development https://send.eani.org.uk/support-services/literacy.The Special Educational Needs (SEN) Code of Practice is needs led and not dependent on a child/young person having a diagnosis of dyslexia.  Children and young people experiencing literacy difficulties should be recorded on the school SEN register under the category Cognition and Learning.  Where a parent/carer has a concern in relation to their child’s literacy they should, in the first instance, discuss this with their child’s school.  All schools have access to a designated EA Educational Psychologist (EP) to consult with on these matters.  Currently, if following assessment by an EP a request is made to the EALS for support, this may be in the form of advice and guidance, small group support or individual support.  This will depend on the level of the child/young person’s need.Supports and resources available from all EA SEN/Disability (SEND) services to support schools in the implementation of the SEN Code of Practice are set out on the EA SEND Plan of Arrangements.  Further details can be viewed here: https://send.eani.org.uk/ea-plan-arrangements If any parent/carer or school has a specific query, they can contact the literacy service at any time by e-mailing literacyservice@eani.org.uk  or phoning 028 9448 2228.DyscalculiaSchools play a key role in addressing any difficulties a pupil may be facing in relation to numeracy and it is important that any concerns regarding difficulties in the area of mathematics should be brought to the attention of the class teacher or the school SEN Co-ordinator (SENCo), in the first instance. SENCos, classroom teachers and classroom assistants have had training on meeting the needs of children with a wide range of SEN, including literacy and numeracy.  They will be able to advise a parent/carer on how best they can support a child with this presenting need.The challenges the pupil is experiencing in relation to maths should be clearly identified within their Individual Education Plan (IEP), which should set out agreed actions for development and support, reflecting the needs of the individual child/young person.  Progress should then be monitored and reviewed in consultation with the child/young person and their parent/carer in line with the Code of Practice of the Identification and Assessment of SEN (1998).  It is important at these early stages that schools can demonstrate relevant and purposeful measures, as well as reasonable adjustments having been put in place to help support a child/young person.Further support is available via the EA Pupil Support Services (PSS) which can be accessed at https://send.eani.org.uk/support-services.My department has made additional resources available to schools to support pupils with dyscalculia, eg through

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 8331/22-27, to detail the number of patients waiting for access to endocrinology services, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: The number of patients waiting for inpatient or day case admission are published quarterly, broken down by HSC Trust and specialty. These publications can be found at the following link: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/inpatient-waiting-times. The accompanying csv files provide this data quarterly going back beyond the past three years and include figures for endocrinology.The number of patients waiting for a first consultant-led outpatient appointment are published quarterly, broken down by HSC Trust and specialty.  These publications can be found at the following link: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/outpatient-waiting-times. The accompanying csv files provide this data quarterly going back beyond the past three years. accompanying csv files provide this data quarterly going back beyond the past three years and include figures for endocrinology.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will implement the recommendations of the Independent Review of Children's Social Care Services.
answer_text: The report of the Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services was published in June 2023 and a public consultation on the Review recommendations ran for 12 weeks between September and December 2023. My officials are in the process of developing advice based on analysis of the responses to the public consultation. Subject to the final outcome to consultation and other factors, including the availability of budget and the views of other Ministers where relevant, the intention will be to implement recommendations as quickly as possible.  A number of the recommendations will be taken forward through the Children's Social Care Services Reform Programme as appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will consider a feasibility study to enhance the future sustainability of the Northwest Teachers' Centre.
answer_text: The Education Authority is retaining the Northwest Teachers’ Centre for future Special Educational Needs provision.  As such, the sustainability of the centre will be maintained and a feasibility study is not required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on his Department's proposed capital investment for (i) Strathfoyle Youth Centre; and (ii) the Northwest Teachers' Centre.
answer_text: Strathfoyle Youth Centre has been identified as a potential capital project under the Education Authority youth service refurbishment review.  Given the demands on the Department of Education’s capital budget, unfortunately, difficult decisions have had to be made regarding the prioritisation of capital spend and no funding was available to progress any youth capital schemes to site in 2023/24.Given that the budget allocation for 2024/25 will be required to meet the Department’s inescapable capital needs and many difficult decisions will again need to be taken over coming months, it is unlikely that work on Strathfoyle Youth Centre will progress this year. I will continue to bid for additional funding throughout the year. The EA is retaining the Northwest Teachers Centre for future Special Educational Needs provision.  Currently, there is no proposal for capital investment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the total number of speech and language therapists currently employed; and (ii) how many are based in stroke units, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Information provided by Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts on the number of speech and language therapists employed at 31 March 2024, and how many are based in stroke units, is detailed in the table below.HSC TrustSpeech and language therapists employed at 31 March 2024Of which: numbers based in stroke unitsHeadcountWTEHeadcountWTEBelfast192164.9787.8Northern164133.402*1.3*South Eastern11897.82  2   0.8  Southern133101.0072.26Western9586.193*3.0**These staff provide a service to stroke patients but are not based at the stroke unit itself.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of Housing Executive tenants currently in rent arrears; and (ii) the number of cases which have escalated to court proceedings.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows.“The Housing Executive can advise:(i) The number of Housing Executive tenants in rent arrears at 31st March 2024 was 41,001.(ii) Please see table below detailing the number of cases which have escalated to court proceedings.Financial YearCases escalated to Court Proceedings 2023/243302024/25 YTD14”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of patients waiting for orthotists services; and (ii) the average length of time they have been waiting, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 8862/22-27 and AQW 14342/17-22, how these improvements in waiting times were achieved, particularly in the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Trusts are experiencing significant pressures as a consequence of increased demands upon services, including increased numbers of new referrals, increased complexity and acuity of people presenting to the service and significant workforce pressures as the result of increased staff vacancy levels.The improved position within the Western Trust Adult Mental Health (AMH) services has been due to the Primary Care Liaison Service undertaking a service review to improve systems and processes to optimise capacity within the multi-disciplinary team and the implementation of standardised robust triage arrangements.  This improved position has also been due to successful recruitment to vacant posts which increased clinical capacity.  The AMH Primary Care Liaison Service has no patients waiting over 9 weeks for a first appointment (as at 31/03/24).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 9632/22-27, to detail the cost to remove wooden pallets at this site prior to 15 August 2023, for each of the last three years.
answer_text: My Department engaged an external contractor to remove materials in advance of the 15 August 2023 bonfire. Release of the cost information associated with this removal would breach a confidential commercial agreement and could impact on the Department’s ability to undertake a similar removal exercise should the need arise again. Therefore, with the public interest in mind, the cost information requested for 2023 is not being released.  There were no costs incurred by my Department for removal of materials at the bonfire site in advance of the 15 August bonfire in both 2021 and 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will establish a support package for small businesses struggling to meet increased cost pressures, including energy bills and the rise in minimum wage.
answer_text: The large fluctuations in energy prices over the past number of years, driven by our continued reliance on importing fossil fuels, highlights the importance of our local Energy Strategy ‘The Path to Net Zero Energy’.   One of the key objectives of the strategy is to ensure that all businesses have access to essential and affordable energy to enable an adequate standard of competitiveness. The focus is now on priorities for delivery to ensure that we maximise the benefits for our society and economy as soon as possible.The Energy team is working with Invest NI to deliver a new phase of its Energy Resource Efficiency (ERE) Programme for Businesses. The Programme will be open to all eligible NI Businesses.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will explore a residents' parking scheme for college and university areas, in particular the residential areas in the vicinity of the Magee Campus of Ulster University.
answer_text: I am aware of concerns about parking in residential areas in the vicinity of colleges and universities such as Magee campus of Ulster University.The review of residents’ parking schemes, which includes lessons learned from the Rugby Road scheme in the vicinity of Queen’s University Belfast, is complete. The report will be published when I have considered the findings, which I expect will be in the coming months. Until such time, I am not in a position to comment on specific requests for further schemes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) whether she plans to review the business rates system; and (ii) for her assessment of the impact increased business rates are having on small businesses.
answer_text: (i) The rates system and rate reliefs have been subject to public consultation, review, reform and revision in 2007, 2012, 2016, 2017, 2019 and most recently this year. A number of changes have already been implemented from these reviews including more frequent revaluations which helps ensure stability and fairness in the tax system, and a 12.5% reduction in the 2020/21 regional rate poundage.I recently advised the Assembly that I want to strategically align the rating system here with the Executive’s priorities for Government. Rates are our only devolved tax, contributing £1.5bn to public services, and so it makes sense for it to align with Executive strategy. As a devolved tax it is currently our only substantive revenue raising lever raising vital funds for public services. (ii) In terms of the impact of rates on small businesses, my Department renewed the legislation for Small Business Rate Relief which will provide £21.5 million of support to 29,000 businesses for 2024/25, providing automatic bill reductions of between 20% to 50% depending on a property’s valuation level. The Executive has also held its annual rate increase for businesses at the rate of inflation and my Department has made and laid legislation for Assembly debate on the restoration of the Back in Business and Rural ATM schemes to further support businesses. While my Department has no control over district council poundages, I have approved sponsorship of research by the Ulster University Economic Policy Centre on the economic impacts of differential rate poundages set by district councils here, which was initiated following concerns that high district rate poundages in some areas may risk displacement or other adverse impacts. I also know from speaking to businesses and trade bodies here that there are many wider problems facing them including energy costs, food price inflation, VAT, skills shortages, squeezes on household budgets.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will work to restore funding to the Waterside Theatre, which is at imminent risk of closure.
answer_text: Funding to arts organisations is delivered on my Department’s behalf by the Arts Council of Northern Ireland and decisions in relation to the terms of its programmes, assessment processes and the allocation of funding are a matter for the Board of the Arts Council.I understand that the Arts Council remains open to discussing the possibility of support for the Waterside Theatre, but decisions on its viability and future are ultimately a matter for the management board of the organisation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) how many government and public procurement contracts have been awarded to businesses based in Northern Ireland; (ii) how many have been outsourced; and (iii) what steps are being taken to ensure fair representation of local businesses.
answer_text: The Department of Finance has awarded 86 contracts over the last three years, 38 of these contracts are with businesses registered on eTendersNI as based in the North. There have been no outsourced services through these contracts.The awarding of public contracts is carried out in strict accordance with the Executive’s Public Procurement Policy and the Public Contracts Regulations 2015 (as amended) which enshrine the key principles of equality of opportunity, non-discrimination, transparency and competitive supply. This regulatory and policy framework ensures that local businesses have equality of opportunity and are treated fairly. This includes the Procurement Control Limits Policy (PPN 02/21) which was designed to ensure that public procurement contributes to a strong, competitive, regionally balanced economy by stimulating effective competition. For example, by openly advertising all opportunities over £30,000 on a central procurement portal, eTendersNI.The Supply Chain Resilience Policy (PPN 03/21) addresses sustainability of local supply chains, ensuring specifications do not exclude locally manufactured products and guidance to Departments to carry out pre-market engagement exercises to seek feedback from local manufacturers on what products are available locally and how contracts should be structured to accommodate small/niche suppliers (e.g. dividing contracts into lots).In addition, my Department publishes a pipeline of planned and future procurements to ensure local businesses are aware of upcoming requirements and opportunities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) his Department's plans has to address unauthorised flags, emblems and signage attached to street furniture; and (ii) the engagement he has had with other (a) Departments; and (b) relevant stakeholders in relation to this issue.
answer_text: My Department does not permit the attachment of flags to its property.  Article 87 of the Roads (NI) Order 1993 makes it an offence to attach unauthorised signs or advertisements to structures such as lamp posts. One of my Department’s primary considerations is the safety of the public and where unauthorised flags or attachments pose a risk to road users, my Department will seek to remove that hazard. Where there is no such danger, my Department may liaise with other key stakeholders and seek to provide a solution. However, given the Department’s ongoing resource limitations, situations where there is no clear road safety risk are considered a low priority.I note the commitments to respect for different cultures and identities contained in the New Decade New Approach. The Commission on Flags, Identity, Culture and Tradition, which was established as part of the Fresh Start Agreement, was tasked with exploring and finding consensus on a range of issues, including the flying of flags. This group has submitted its report; however it has not provided a solution to the issue. The report can be accessed at the following link:https://www.executiveoffice-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/execoffice/commission-on-fict-final-report.pdfFinding a sustainable solution to the ongoing problem of illegally erected flags and emblems requires leadership and a united approach. I can assure you I am committed to working with Executive colleagues and all stakeholders to deal with this issue comprehensively.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what engagement he has had with the Minister of Finance around ring-fencing funds generated by the Soft Drinks Industry Levy to address health conditions such as obesity and Type 2 diabetes.

To ask the Minister of Education (i) how many reports the Education Authority has received of classroom assistants being removed from their class, or their assigned pupil with special educational needs, for the purpose of invigilating exams, in the last three years; and (ii) for his assessment of the situation.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that it does not hold this level of information as the attendance of classroom assistants and the allocation of daily duties rests at a school level.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) when he will review proposed changes to the Taxi Operators Licensing Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2012; and (ii) what steps he will take to mitigate negative impacts on the taxi industry.
answer_text: There is no timetable for the proposed legislative change to the taxi regulations. I can advise that my officials are seeking further legal advice on this matter, and I will then consider next steps.You will be aware that my Department is responsible for the regulation of the taxi industry, in relation to taxi driver, taxi operator and vehicle licencing.  Other business related matters for the taxi industry such as VAT are outside the remit of my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 9087/22-27, whether he will consider changes that would allow those turning 65 years of age to access all-Ireland travel via a 65+ SmartPass at the beginning of their birth month, rather than at the end.
answer_text: There is currently no plans to change the 65+ SmartPass application process to issue SmartPasses at the beginning of the applicant’s birth month.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans he has to (i) amend; and (ii) close the loopholes in Class C taxi licensing in order to protect passengers.
answer_text: As you are aware Class C vehicles can only be used on a pre-booked basis and booked at an operating centre.  They cannot be hailed and cannot be used on taxi ranks.  Examples of Class C taxis are vehicles used for Chauffeur services, weddings, funeral, and courtesy transport.  Uber taxis also fall within the Class C classification.I currently have no plans in place to amend the definition of Class C taxis or the existing taxi licensing process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will conduct a full review of the Taxis Act (Northern Ireland) 2008.
answer_text: I do not have any plans at this time to update or conduct a full review of the Taxis Act (Northern Ireland) 2008.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the clinical waste budget allocation in primary care in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The clinical waste budget allocated to practices in Primary care in the last 5 years is as follows:YearTotal Budget2019/20£488,0002020/21£429,0002021/22£1,136,0002022/23£768,0002023/24£756,000

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of occasions his Department has exercised its powers to remove or serve notices regarding unauthorised (a) markings; (b) signs; and (c) advertisements on roads; and (ii) the total costs recovered, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The illegal erection of advertising signs along the public road is an offence under The Roads (Northern Ireland) Order 1993. Where signs of any kind are erected illegally on DfI property or unlawfully affixed to the surface of a road, action may be taken by my Department under Article 87 of the Roads (NI) Order 1993, to try and remove them. However during recent years my Department has faced significant budget shortfall and must carefully consider how best to use its limited resources. In order to ensure that the highest priority, safety related maintenance can be undertaken, my Department targets the limited resources available towards road safety related matters. The removal of advertising signage is considered low priority and therefore will only be carried out as resources permit / where there is a road safety implication.My Department does not maintain records of the number of signs, markings or advertisements removed and has not sought to recover costs in recent years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the future of (i) gynaecology; (ii) dermatology; and (iii) surgical services in primary care.
answer_text: I am fully committed to ensuring that patients will be able to access high quality, sustainable primary care services now and in the future.   I also recognise the valuable role played by those providing primary elective care services. Five specialty pathways are provided across the Primary Care Elective model each providing an assessment, treatment and follow up service.  The pathways are: dermatology, minor surgery, gynae, musculoskeletal and vasectomy.This service has been sustained through non-recurrent funding since its establishment in 2018.  Regrettably, given the wider budgetary pressures that are impacting on the delivery of many health and social care services have meant that it was not possible to continue the additional levels of Primary Care Elective Care services in 2023/24. My Department has worked with GPs to enable continuation of the service within the limits of the funding available.  This approach has ensured that while the vasectomy service was suspended in September 2023, a reduced service for dermatology, musculoskeletal, gynae and primary care surgery has been maintained.I was delighted to announce that vasectomy services in Northern Ireland will be transferred from secondary care to primary care.  This will take effect from April 2024, with a transition phase throughout 2024/25.  From 2025/26 onwards, primary care will be the sole provider of health service vasectomy services.  This will ensure a more localised service for patients and is a positive example of services being relocated for the benefit of patients – and with lower costs per procedure.While a budget for 2024/25 has not yet been set it is expected that the overall financial position for all NI departments will remain very challenging.  My expectation therefore is that the Department will not be able to do all that we want or need to do and the main focus will be on preserving and protecting existing services, with all the limitations that they currently have.  The Department is currently facing a significant number of competing funding challenges across Health and Social Care and will be in a clearer position to address specific funding pressures when the budget settlement is agreed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of non-standard accommodation placements in each council area.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me that the table below details the number of non-standard accommodation placements by council area as of 31 December 2023:It should be noted that this table refers to the area from which the placement has been made and not the area to which the placement has been made. Council AreaNon-Standard Placements as of 31 December 2023Antrim & Newtownabbey2Ards & North Down2Armagh, Banbridge & Craigavon5Belfast190Causeway Coast & Glens4Derry & Strabane167Fermanagh & Omagh6Lisburn & Castlereagh8Mid & East Antrim3Mid Ulster1Newry, Mourne & Down7Total395For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with the Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients diagnosed with borderline personality disorder, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Information on diagnosis of borderline personality disorder is not routinely collected or available within HSC Trusts.When Encompass is implemented across all HSC Trusts, this will provide the digital platform for diagnostic information to be recorded and should allow for statistical information to be retrieved and reported on.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide an update on the Taxi Action Plan.
answer_text: I do not have any plans at this time to update or conduct a full review of the Taxis Act (Northern Ireland) 2008.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the measures he will take to prevent lorries becoming lodged at the lower deck of the Craigavon Bridge, Derry; and (ii) how many such incidences have occurred in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The lower deck of Craigavon Bridge has a height restriction in place which bans high sided vehicles in excess of the specified heights from using the lower deck of the bridge.  Warning signage is provided on all approaches to the bridge in accordance with the Traffic Signs Manual.My Department does not keep a record of the number of occasions when lorries have become lodged in the lower deck of Craigavon Bridge.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the full implementation and roll-out of Palforzia for peanut allergies.
answer_text: Officers within the Department and its SPPG remain committed to the introduction of a local service to provide Palforzia to patients within Northern Ireland.  Work is ongoing to agree the most appropriate service delivery model for Northern Ireland.  In the interim, Palforzia is available on a case by case basis for eligible patients in line with the Department’s endorsed guidance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 6487/22-27, and under the current taxi legislation, whether his Department can support the (i) purchase; (ii) conversion; and (iii) running costs for wheelchair taxis under Part 5 of the Transport Act (Northern Ireland) 2011.
answer_text: Pursuant to AQW 6487/22-27 I would advise that there is currently no policy or budget in place to provide financial support under Part 5 of the Transport Act (NI) 2011 towards the purchase, conversion or running costs for wheelchair taxis.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the costs associated with clean-up of the 15 August bonfire at Galliagh Linear Park in the Foyle constituency, for each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department provides annual funding to Derry City and Strabane District Council for additional cleansing, maintenance and remedial works which includes funding for emergency clean ups after bonfires at Galliagh Linear Park.  In the past five years the following amounts have been allocated for this purpose: 2019/20 - £2,6012020/21 - £2,6792021/22 - £2,7592022/23 - £2,8422023/24 - £2,927

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether artificial pancreas technology will be made available to patients with Type 1 diabetes.
answer_text: Artificial pancreas technology, also known as closed loop system for diabetes management, continues to evolve and develop.  In December 2023, the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence published final guidance on Hybrid closed loop systems for managing blood glucose levels in Type 1 diabetes.  My Department has formally endorsed the guidance and work is ongoing to understand the implications of this decision in terms of overall provision; priority groups; system costs; workforce and communication requirements to both enable and support. This will inform final decision-making and commissioning arrangements for NI and will be communicated to all stakeholders. It is worth noting that NHS England has developed a phased implementation strategy over a 5-year period in response to the guidance, owing to the scale and scope of this recommendation. Significant additional investment will be required to support provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an update on the Housing Supply Strategy; and (ii) what engagement his Department has had with the Department for Infrastructure in relation to wastewater infrastructure.
answer_text: The housing supply strategy is currently being refreshed, given the passage of time since the draft was published. I intend to meet with Ministerial colleagues, including the Infrastructure Minister, as a priority in seeking their commitment to the strategy, ahead of bringing a final version to the Executive for its consideration. The issue of water infrastructure constraints is regularly highlighted in discussions between my Department and stakeholders regarding housing delivery. My Department has been engaged with the Department for Infrastructure at official level throughout the strategy development process and continues to do so through representation on the Project Board overseeing the strategy work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of children and adults who have been referred to a long COVID clinic; and (ii) the available provision for patients under the age of 18, in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: (i) There have been 4,427 referrals to post-COVID syndrome (PCS) multi-disciplinary assessment clinics from May 2022 to December 2023.  This includes patients aged 16-17 years.(ii)  In line with clinical advice from paediatric specialists in Northern Ireland, children who have not reached their 16th birthday and who have persistent symptoms after a diagnosis of COVID, and who require more specialist care, can be referred to Trust paediatric services in the usual way to receive appropriate assessment and treatment in line with their individual clinical needs. The following services to support and assist adults who are living with this condition have been established in all 5 HSC Trusts:Strand 1 - Post-COVID-19 syndrome (PCS) patients referred by primary or secondary care to a one-stop-shop MDT assessment servicePatients can be referred to the MDT assessment clinic from primary and secondary care.  Patients receive an holistic assessment to include:identifying patients with breathlessness who may benefit from pulmonary rehabilitation; and, those who may need onward referral to secondary or community services and/or signposting to self-management resources.Strand 2: - Bespoke pulmonary rehabilitation / dysfunctional breathing service for patients with significant respiratory symptoms post-COVID-19The purpose of this strand is to put in place timely, effective arrangements for outpatient rehabilitation following COVID-19 resulting in significant respiratory symptoms.  Strand 3 - Patients discharged from critical care (both COVID-19 and non-COVID-19)The purpose of this strand is to put in place timely, effective  arrangements for the follow-up and assessment of people discharged from critical care (both COVID-19 and non-COVID-19), reflecting the known risk of long-term sequelae following a long stay in critical care.Strand 4 - Strengthening psychology support to all TrustsThe psychological impact of COVID-19 (depression, anxiety disorders, PTSD, traumatic bereavement, psychosis, risk to self and/or others) is recognised in extant national guidance.  Each Trust has been supported to recruit an additional senior psychologist, with their main role supporting strand 3, but also providing advice to staff working in strands 1 and 2.Strand 5 - Regional MDT clinical physiology, SLT and dietetic support Selected patients may need diagnostic tests by respiratory clinical physiologists, onward referral for dietetic and speech and language therapy (SLT) from the one-stop-shop MDT assessment service, the critical care follow-up and pulmonary rehabilitation strands.  Strand 6 - Signposting and access to self-management resourcesPatients in NI can access self-management resources which include the NHS App Your Covid Recovery (Managing The Effects | Your COVID Recovery)

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 5805/22-27, to detail (i) when this Equality Impact Assessment will be completed; (ii) how many claimants have lost their Discretionary Housing Payment since August 2023; and (iii) how many of these were also in receipt of a disability benefit.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:        The EQIA process is currently ongoing.The Housing Executive is unable to confirm the number of customers who have lost their DHP from August 2023, due to the reporting periods that the Housing Benefit system generates; however, between April 2023 & February 2024, 5,697 DHP awards have ended. The Housing Executive has advised that DHPs will end for the following reasons:When a person changes address, a new DHP application will be required in all cases.When the Housing Executive becomes aware of a change in the customer’s financial / personal circumstances and is satisfied that this change results in the customer no longer being in financial / medical stress. When the Housing Executive is satisfied that the customer misrepresented or failed to disclose a material fact (fraudulently or otherwise) and had the true information been available at the time of the decision, no DHP award would have been made.When a DHP award has been made as the result of an error.When a person no longer satisfies the conditions of entitlement e.g. their Housing Benefit or Universal Credit housing costs end.When a customer’s shortfall between their eligible rent charge and the rent charged by their landlord is less than £5 per week.When an award has been in payment for two years.In answer to the final question the Housing Executive is unable to confirm the number of customers in receipt of a disability benefit who have had their DHP award ended. A decision to award a DHP is made and recorded manually as the software used does not record the grounds for the award being made, and takes account of several factors including disability, domestic abuse, risk of homelessness and vulnerability.  In some cases, more than one of these factors may be present.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly. Making direct contact with the Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what plans he has to ban no fault evictions and strengthen security of tenure in the Private Rented Sector.
answer_text: I have no plans at present to bring forward legislation to ban no fault evictions. As mandated by the Assembly in the Private Tenancies Amendment (NI) Act, 2022, I am already taking forward work under Phase 1 of my Department’s reform of the Private Rented Sector to introduce much longer notice to quit periods. These changes will greatly improve security of tenure for individuals and families in the Private Rent Sector. My future consideration of what needs to happen in Phase 2 of the reform programme will be focused on what further measures I can take to improve the safety, security and quality of this sector. Taking note of the recent Assembly debate, my officials have already started to re-engage with key stakeholders and to review what is happening in other jurisdictions to inform this work.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 5646/22-27, whether he intends to introduce legislation similar to Awaab's Law.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the consultation on Awaab’s law and officials are monitoring developments to consider the most appropriate approach here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 5807/22-27, (i) to provide further detail on the pilot scheme for dental appointments; and (ii) whether this scheme applies across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The scheme referred to in AQW 5807/22-27 is the Pilot for Unregistered Patients and Asylums Seekers (PUPAS) which was established to address the access problem for patients experiencing an urgent or emergency dental condition on weekdays. Practices participating in the scheme receive a fee of £70 for each unregistered patient treated and £120 per asylum seeker, in addition to the fees for treatment provided.Patients who are experiencing an urgent dental condition and are unable to access an appointment themselves are advised to contact their local SPPG office who will provide them with the appropriate information including the contact details of the pilot practices.There are 11 practices participating in the PUPAS Scheme which see approximately 70 patients per week. No geographic restrictions are built into the scheme, however, no practices applied to join from the Western Local Commissioning Group.On 27 March 2024, I announced a further £3.9m investment in an enhanced Dental Access Scheme (DAS) which is intended to replace PUPAS.  The DAS will operate a similar model to the current scheme but provide access to a second, wider, category of unregistered patients with a pressing (but not urgent) need for dental treatment. A new, enhanced fee structure will also be introduced which is intended to encourage more practices from across the region to participate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail actions to improve access to talking therapies, particularly in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: The Western Health and Social care Trust currently has one Primary Care Talking Therapy Hub which is situated in Oldbridge House, Londonderry, within the Foyle constituency. The Direction as mentioned in Action 5 of the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 is that Primary Care Talking Therapies will be managed in Hubs at the Primary Care MDT population level. The expansion of Primary Care Talking Therapies hubs will be available to people of all ages with a referral criteria and pathway for those under and over 18 years of age. The planned locations will align with the current GP federation localities which should support with the variance of service across the Western Health and Social Care Trust. There is a consistent regional review to allow this to happen; and the Western Health and Social Care Trust have representation on this review from adult mental health services to ensure the new model of service delivery regarding Primary Care Talking Therapy Hubs aligns with the other relevant actions in the Mental Health Strategy and as part of a regional Stepped Care Model.The Public Health Agency currently fund interventions within the Foyle areas for Crisis Support, Self-harm and for individuals who identify as lesbian, gay and bi-sexual. Lifeline Lifeline is Northern Ireland’s crisis response helpline for people experiencing distress or despair or who are suicidal. It is available 24 hours a day, 7 days a week, every day of the year and provides compassionate, trauma-informed support. Lifeline can also support people by contacting them by telephone, as agreed, to offer more support, which may include: Safety check-in: a call to an individual to continue developing a collaborative safety plan.Follow-up: a call within 24 hours to a person that has agreed to a referral by someone they know. Support call: a routine call to provide information and education about Lifeline and to talk about options for ongoing support.Where suitable, Lifeline also provides a course of short-term counselling (either remote or in-person).Self Harm Intervention Project (SHIP)Provides psychological interventions for individuals who have self-harmed to prevent repetition of self-harm and suicide in line with National Institute of Health and Care Excellence (NICE) guidance. On average clients will receive approximately 5 sessions with flexibility for those who require additional support, up to approximately 10 sessions. Rainbow Project The Rainbow Project provides support for the mental and emotional well-being of lesbian, gay and bisexual people in Northern Ireland. The Rainbow Project offers health and wellbeing assessments to those who are referred or self-refer to the service. Services provided include health and wellbeing interventions, counselling and delivery of awareness sessions to improve understanding of gender identity and sexual orientation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the total number of women born in the 1950s who are impacted by the increase in State Pension age; and (ii) what engagement he has had with counterparts in Westminster in relation to a compensation scheme to support those adversely affected in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The number of women in Northern Ireland born in the 1950s affected by the changes in State pension age is estimated to be 100,700. The Parliamentary and Health Service Ombudsman report into how changes to women’s State pension age were communicated by the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) was published on 21 March 2024. I await the Westminster Government’s response to that report.In the meantime, my officials will continue to engage with DWP on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he plans to extend the timeframe for an exchange of Ukrainian driving licences from one year to three years, to align with the current policy in the UK.
answer_text: A public consultation on extending the validity period of Ukrainian driving licences in Northern Ireland from 12 months to 36 months ran from 6 September 2023 to 3 October 2023. A total of 137 responses were received and overall highlighted strong levels of support for the proposal. A synopsis of results was published on the DfI website on 23 January 2024. The associated amending legislation, the Motor Vehicles (Driving Licences) (Amendment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2024 (S.R. 2024 No. 22), was made on 27th February 2024 and laid before the NI Assembly on 29 February 2024. It came into force on 25th March 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 5806/22-27 (i) how many of those impacted are single parent households; and (ii) whether his Department has carried out any assessments to determine whether the two-child rule disproportionately affects women.
answer_text: In November 2023, 8,430 households receiving Universal Credit were impacted by the two-child policy, of which 5,660 were single parent households.The legislation on the two-child policy is contained in the Welfare Reform and Work (Northern Ireland) Order 2016. This Order ensured that the welfare reforms enabled by the Welfare Reform and Work Act 2016 in Great Britain are delivered in Northern Ireland. The Order brought Northern Ireland social security into parity with the rest of the UK, as agreed in the Fresh Start Agreement in 2015.Prior to the introduction of the Great Britain Welfare Reform and Work Bill in the House of Commons, HM Treasury and the Department for Work and Pensions completed an Impact Assessment of Tax Credits and Universal Credit, changes to Child Element and Family Element including the introduction of the two-child policy. It was acknowledged that on an individual basis women may be more likely to be affected than men as more women are lone parents.My Department has not carried any assessment to determine whether the two-child policy disproportionately affects women.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will consider establishing a dedicated outreach service to improve the uptake of benefits for people of State Pension age.
answer_text: My Department currently provides support to people of State Pension age to assist them to access the benefits and services to which they are entitled, including supporting those who require a home visit to make an application. The most recent performance data for my Department’s Make the Call service, published in September 2023, shows that Make the Call assisted 4,819 people of pension age to receive just under £20 million in additional annualised Attendance Allowance and Pension Credit payments.In addition, the Department, through partnerships with community-based organisations and other government departments promotes the uptake of services for State Pension age customers by facilitating local advice clinics; providing practical assistance to access benefits and supports; and holding regular stakeholder engagement events.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the total number of overpayments incurred by welfare claimants as a result of official errors; (ii) the total cost of these overpayments; and (iii) the total number of successful waivers, in the last three years.
answer_text: The table below details the total number of official error overpayments, the value of these overpayments and the total number of successful official error waivers for each of the last three years. The data contained is internal management information, which is not subject to the same level of quality assurance as accredited statistics. Official Error Overpayments Financial year Total Numbers*Value £mNumber of debts  waived2021-229,1596.1122022-239,5975.9362023-24**10,3047.642*some customers have more than one social security benefit debt** period April 23 to February 24The Department publishes annual estimates of fraud and error in the Departmental Annual Report & Accounts. The latest published figures for official error (2022) estimate overpayments due to official error at 0.3% of benefit expenditure, equal to the Department for Work & Pensions figure.  My Department is committed to the reduction of official error and has a robust range of control mechanisms built into benefit administration to ensure high levels of financial accuracy including extensive staff training and the measurement and quarterly reporting on accuracy performance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the average waiting time for smear test results; (ii) the total number of patients waiting; and (iii) the length of the longest wait, for each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: As information on smear tests results is not available centrally in the Department, data have been provided by HSC Trusts and can be found in table 1.Table 1.
