question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 6489/22-27, to detail (i) the number of women identified as part of the Western Health and Social Care Trust audit; (ii) how many of these patients subsequently underwent a radical hysterectomy; and (iii) how many were offered postmenopausal support or treatment.
answer_text: (i)The number of women identified as part of the Western Health and Social Care Trust audit who were diagnosed with invasive cervical cancer was 94 (2018 to 2023 inclusive); (ii) of these 94 women, the number who underwent radical hysterectomy is 18 ; and (iii) of these 94 women, the number offered postmenopausal support or treatment is 30.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 6898/22-27, what steps are being taken by his Department to ensure Action 6 of the strategy is taken forward.
answer_text: Action 6 of the Empty Homes Strategy and Action Plan 2013 - 2018 was to meet with lenders where repossessed property is empty and develop protocols. Responsibility for taking this action forward sat with the NIHE. As is noted in the review of the Strategy, early investigations into this work meant this action could be discounted as lenders were actively trying to sell properties in their possession to minimise potential loss.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any underspend in allocated funding for autism services, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust, in the last three years.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of Housing Executive approved bed and breakfast and guesthouses for non-standard accommodation purposes in each local council area, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive does not maintain a list of non-standard premises (Hotel/B&B) by individual properties which have been used over the past five years.  The Housing Executive records data on non-standard placements using a free text field on our Housing Management System and therefore, there is no readily identifiable option to extract placements in non-standard by number of individual premises.From 1 April 2022 to 31 March 2023 there was a total of 3,809 non-standard placements provided by the Housing Executive.  Provision of the requested data would involve a review of all 3,809 placements to identify the number of premises referred to in the free text fields.  This exercise would need to be replicated for each of the previous four years in order to answer this query.”The biannual Northern Ireland Homelessness Bulletin July - December 2023 contains information on the numbers classified as experiencing homelessness and is available via:https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-homelessness-bulletin-july-december-2023 For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will introduce legislative change to ensure full-time students from low-income households can access welfare support to include Discretionary Support and childcare costs under the Adviser Discretion Fund.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of the Discretionary Support Scheme in assisting vulnerable people in a crisis situation. Although full-time students cannot normally access Discretionary Support, exceptions are made to enable full-time third-level education students to apply for Discretionary Support in the event of a disaster. There are no plans to amend the current Discretionary Support policy with regard to students’ eligibility criteria. The Adviser Discretion Fund is available (subject to eligibility criteria) to benefit claimants to assist them in overcoming financial barriers to starting, increasing or returning to work. It is therefore unlikely to be an appropriate source of funding for full-time students. There are no plans to amend the Adviser Discretion Fund eligibility criteria. In terms of other welfare support, it is the general rule that full-time students are not entitled to Universal Credit. However, there are exceptions (for example, where the claimant is responsible for a child or qualifying young person). Where a student satisfies one of these exceptions and is entitled to Universal Credit, they may also be entitled to receive the childcare costs element as part of their award.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) how many applicants to the Disabled Facilites Grant have requested a recalculation of their grant award; and (ii) how many applicants have received grant aid in excess of £35,000 in the last year, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: (i) how many applicants to the Disabled Facilities Grant have requested a recalculation of their grant award; A request from an applicant to recalculate the amount of grant-aid approved is not formally captured by the Housing Executive’s Grant Management System.  However, during the normal lifetime of a claim the Housing Executive will recalculate the grant-aid approved, and associated values, on multiple occasions and for a number of different reasons. At the end of each case the Housing Executive will complete a recalculation by way of a final re-cost to ensure amounts of all eligible works are accounted for.(ii) how many applicants have received grant aid in excess of £35,000 in the last year, broken down by local council area;The Housing Executive has provided a breakdown of the total number of applicants, by council area, whose approval of grant-aid was in excess of £35,000 during the period 01 March 2023 to 29 February 2024. See table below: Council AreaGrant Aid  >£35,000Antrim & Newtownabbey6Ards & North Down22Armagh City Banbridge & Craigavon16Belfast17Causeway Coast & Glens14Derry City & Strabane38Fermanagh & Omagh14Lisburn & Castlereagh6Mid & East Antrim10Mid Ulster11Newry Mourne & Down22Total176I hope this information is useful.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the roads in the Foyle constituency that have had a temporary pothole repair on three or more occasions in the last year.
answer_text: Unfortunately, my Department’s defect recording system does not automatically record the number of temporary or recurring pothole defects.  Instructed repairs remain on-going until confirmation is received that a permanent repair is complete while other defects identified through the routine inspection process are recorded as new items.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how many jobs have been lost as a direct result of the removal of European Social Funding.
answer_text: My department does not hold this information. Most of the key policy areas that the ESF Programme addressed fell to other NI Departments, in particular Health, Communities and Justice.Upon conclusion of ESF, newly formed consortia emerged to bid for SPF and some organisations strayed beyond their historic ESF remit in bidding, rendering impossible any direct comparison with previous ESF funding. The majority of previous ESF supported organisations/projects were part of other successful group bids to the SPF.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of pupils on long-term absence for ill mental health in (i) primary; and (ii) post-primary settings in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Department does not hold information on pupils who are absent from school for mental health reasons.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he intends to co-fund the Active School Travel Programme in the next financial year.
answer_text: I refer the member to my response to AQW 5456/22-27 of 15 February 2024. My officials continue to liaise with the Public Health Agency who co-fund the Active School Travel Programme with my Department and await the outcome of these discussions.  Any decision on this programme in the next financial year will be subject to the availability of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) whether she will review the personal injury discount rate; (ii) whether her Department has assessed the extent the personal injury discount rate impacts on the cost of insurance policies; and (iii) whether a comparative study into the position in Northern Ireland against Great Britain will be conducted.
answer_text: The Damages (Return on Investment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 amended the Damages Act 1996 to assign the task of reviewing and setting the personal injury discount rate to the Government Actuary, provide a statutory methodology for calculating the rate, and a timeframe for regular review of the rate. Under the legislation, the Government Actuary must commence a review of the rate on 1st July 2024, and conclude it within ninety days. In anticipation of that, my Department is presently considering whether the statutory parameters by which the rate is to be set require modification and if required, will bring forward draft regulations for approval by the Assembly.The purpose of the discount rate is to give effect to the legal principle that claimants should be fully compensated for their losses, but no more and no less. It would not be appropriate, therefore, for my Department to assess the extent to which the discount rate impacts on the cost of insurance policies. Any such impact arises from the liabilities of insurers for personal injury damages.There are no plans for the Department to conduct a comparative study into the position in Northern Ireland against Great Britain.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will take action to allow the 60+ SmartPass to remain active until holders receive their Senior 65+ SmartPass, in order to ensure a smooth transition.
answer_text: 60+ SmartPasses are active until the end of the calendar month in which a person turns 65, rather than the actual date they turn 65.  SmartPass holders are written to 3 months prior to the expiry of their SmartPass to inform them of the steps to take to apply for a 65+ SmartPass. 60+ SmartPass holders who renew their pass will receive their SmartPass towards the end of their birth month. This ensures continuous and uninterrupted eligibility for public transport discounts.There are currently no plans to change this process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what steps she will take to increase transparency, including media presence, in family court proceedings.
answer_text: I am keen to look at ways to increase the transparency of family courts, to promote a better understanding of, and enhance confidence in, our legal system. However, I am also very mindful that family courts deal with the most sensitive and personal matters, so any move towards greater openness needs to be carefully considered to ensure the privacy of the families and children involved can be protected.My Department will be undertaking policy scoping work in the current mandate but owing to the limited time remaining, any potential legislative change would have to be progressed in the longer term. Nevertheless, I believe that it is important to start the process as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 7186/22-27, where the 47 staff will be located, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Regional work has been undertaken by Department of Health to focus on the provision of specific training to upskill 47 suitably qualified staff located across all trust areas, who can lawfully undertake the Approved Social Worker (ASW) statutory functions of waiting under Direction 1 (2023) of the MH (NI) Order. Currently these posts are being finalised in terms of the associated human resources processes. The breakdown of these staff trained per trust is as follows: -Trust / RESWSNo.RESWS12BHSCT9WHSCT16SET7NHSCT3SHSCT0Total47These staff would continue to be based in their own Trust, noting the 47 staff are already Trust staff who have been trained to a specialist level and will continue with their usual substantive employment and also work on the out of hours regional bank as part of RESWS. It will depend upon where the need for the out of hours service is and where available staff are located, ideally within their own Trust or local /home area.It is anticipated that when Human Resources processes are completed, the addition of these staff will ease the pressures for Approved Social Workers in completing their statutory functions of waiting under Direction 1 (2023) of the MH (NI) Order.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will engage with the Minister for the Economy to establish an Independent Review into Education, with a view to develop a single department for all educational stages, including further education.
answer_text: The Department is currently considering the report produced by the Independent Review of Education Panel and carrying out internal consultation to inform a way forward. This process includes discussion with key education stakeholders including other relevant Departments and Arm’s-Length Bodies.    Following this consultation, officials will produce a paper for my consideration setting out advice on the various recommendations in the report including those on structures and a single system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the practical driving test pass rate at each DVA testing centre in each of the last five years.
answer_text: DVA Statistics produce a National Statistics quarterly publication, ‘DfI Driver, Vehicle, Operator, and Enforcement Statistics’ which details practical driving test pass rates by test centre.  Quarter four reports provide the position at the financial year end, with Table 4.7b detailing annual driving test pass rates for each test centre.  This same table is detailed in the quarter four reports for each financial year from 2018/19 to 2022/23.The relevant quarterly publications can be found at: Driver & Vehicle Agency Activity Statistics | Department for Infrastructure (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on discussions with Trade Union representatives in relation to enhancing the mileage rate for domiciliary care staff.
answer_text: As part of pay negotiations with Agenda for Change (AfC) Trade Unions, which completed on 22 February, Trade Unions, Employers and the Department have committed to work together on a number of non-pay issues, including an agreement on new AfC mileage rates.TUS have asked that the issue of mileage be dealt with under usual business at Joint Negotiation meetings to provide them the opportunity to examine any proposed options.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what engagement his Department has had with (i) Derry City and Strabane District Council in relation to defective Mica blocks; and (ii) the Irish government in relation to establishing a redress scheme for homes impacted in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: (i) On 6th January 2023, the Council’s Director of Environment and Regeneration wrote to the Department’s Permanent Secretary regarding defective Mica blocks.  Officials responded on 19th January 2023 to advise that the issue pertaining to defective Mica blocks is a Reserved Matter that is not within the responsibility of my Department.  There has been no further correspondence with Derry City and Strabane District Council on this issue since the launch of their Mica register.(ii)   My Department has not engaged with the Irish government in relation to establishing a redress scheme for homes impacted in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of unsuccessful Personal Independence Payment claim decisions which were deemed successful at (i) mandatory reconsideration stage; and (ii) appeal stage, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: The most recent Personal Independence Payment (PIP) statistics were published in February 2024 and covered the period up to 30 November 2023. (i) The table below shows the number of PIP applications that have been initially unsuccessful, but subsequently awarded at mandatory reconsideration stage in each of the last 3 years.202120222023Total1,3701,2601,0203,650		(ii) My Department does not hold appeals information in the format requested.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what engagement he has had with the Minister of Education around the establishment of an independent review of education, with a view to develop a single department for all educational stages, including further education.
answer_text: Further to the publication of the Independent Review of Education’s final report on 13 December 2023, I will be considering the recommendations which relate to my Department. partnership working and collaboration between the Department of Education and my Department will be critical to support all our learners, and the Minister and I will be engaging on areas of mutual responsibility and interest.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the review of the Further Education Delivery Model, specifically the delivery of pay parity for further education lecturers.
answer_text: A thriving, modern Further Education sector will be a key enabler for delivery of my economic vision, and I have asked my officials for an update on the work already progressed in respect the Review of Further Education Delivery Model and for options available going forward. 	Further Education lecturers’ pay, as well as the terms and conditions of employment, are matters for the Further Education colleges as employers. I am however committed to improving pay for Further Education lecturers. 		I am giving full attention to the concerns raised by Further Education lecturers following the announcement of the school teachers’ pay agreement. I am pleased to have approved a revised budget envelope which allows the Further Education Colleges, as employers, to make a revised pay offer which provides an increase to lecturers’ starting salaries to £30,000, and a 8.4% increase plus a consolidated £1,000 on all other pay points as a first step towards parity with teachers.  It is hoped this revised pay offer will be welcomed by Further Education lecturers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department is considering changes to the Social Sector Size Criteria-Bedroom Tax mitigation, including tapering the provision depending on household income.
answer_text: My Department has a statutory obligation to produce a report on the operation of the existing mitigation schemes before 31 March 2025. This report will include proposals for the future delivery of the welfare mitigation schemes, including the Social Sector Size Criteria (Bedroom Tax).This matter along with other recommendations that have been made for a future mitigation package in Northern Ireland will need to be assessed in the climate of financial constraint and the requirement to ensure that budgets remain sustainable over the coming years.

answer_text: Information is not available in the format requested, however the number waiting longer than 9 weeks, and the total number waiting, for access to Adult (Table 1) and Child and Adolescent (Table 2) Mental Health Services is shown below.Table 1: Adult mental health waits over 9 weeks, and total waits, by HSC Trust, as at 31 January 2024HSC TrustWaits over 9 weeksTotal waitsBelfast1,0681,934Northern19744South Eastern8161,748Southern5451,265Western271656Northern Ireland2,7196,347Source: SPPG Mental Health Breaches submissionTable 2: CAMHS waits over 9 weeks, and total waits, by HSC Trust, as at 31 January 2024HSC TrustWaits over 9 weeksTotal waitsBelfast315566Northern439674South Eastern*Southern107440Western271389Northern Ireland1,1322,069Source: SPPG Mental Health Breaches submission* South Eastern HSC Trust CAMHS services are provided by Belfast HSC Trust

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide a timeframe for the implementation of the Zero Emission Vehicle mandate.
answer_text: The Zero Emissions Vehicle Mandate was laid by the British Government, Scottish Government, and Welsh Senedd as the Vehicle Emissions Trading Schemes Order (VETS) in England, Scotland, and Wales in October 2023 and came into force in those jurisdictions from 03 January 2024. Unfortunately, due to the absence of the Assembly, we were not able to join at that time. I am working with my officials to consider the best way forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the Department intends to implement the Road Traffic (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2016.
answer_text: The Road Traffic (Amendment) Act 2016 provides the statutory framework for a revised drink driving regime, as well as for the reform of driver testing, training and licensing arrangements. Full implementation requires a significant package of subordinate legislation. I have asked my officials for an update on the work that has been completed to date, including any barriers to satisfactory completion. I will then make a decision on the way forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will consider issuing a public consultation into mandatory licensing for non-surgical cosmetic procedures.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the wider, changing health and social care service delivery environment, including in relation to cosmetic treatments. Prior to the COVID-19 pandemic, the Department had developed a new draft regulatory policy framework however further development work is required, including consultation on the draft policy.  The Department is currently operating within a constrained budget and is required to make decisions in relation to the work that can be delivered within current resources. In that context, work on the review of the regulation is currently paused to allow for other priority projects to progress. My Department has no current plans to issue a public consultation into mandatory licensing for non-surgical cosmetic procedures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Clean Air Strategy for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Officials have been progressing this work in the absence of an Executive. Now that the Executive has returned, I will work with my officials and Ministerial colleagues to finalise this important cross-cutting Strategy.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will consider removing the English drug tariff and system for Community Pharmacy, in favour of a model similar to that in Scotland.
answer_text: The English Drug Tariff is not applicable to Northern Ireland. Prices of medicines are set on a UK basis and NI Drug Tariff as with other Tariffs source prices from the English Drug Tariff. I can assure the member that I have committed that further work will be taken forward in the coming months with Community Pharmacy NI, the representative body for community pharmacy contractors in Northern Ireland, to review the current financial framework within current affordability and to consider the Scottish model of reimbursement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for his assessment of the increasing numbers of children living in poverty; and (ii) for an update on the Child Poverty Strategy.
answer_text: My Department publishes official poverty statistics in the Northern Ireland Poverty and Income Inequality Report on an annual basis. The most recent report includes data up to 2022/23. The number of children estimated to be living in Relative Poverty Before Housing Costs (BHC) in 2022/23 was 109,000; compared to 83,000 in 2021/22, and the number of children living in Relative Poverty After Housing Costs (AHC) was 114,000; compared to 97,000 in 2021/22. There is no discernible trend in either Relative Poverty measures for children over the last decade with both measures fluctuating.		The number of children estimated to be living in Absolute Poverty Before Housing Costs (BHC) in 2022/23 was 86,000; compared to 68,000 in 2021/22, and the number of children living in Absolute Poverty After Housing Costs (AHC) was 93,000; compared to 72,000 in 2021/22. The number of children in absolute poverty generally showed a decreasing trend between 2012/13 and 2017/18 before beginning to fluctuate in more recent years.		An individual is considered to be in Relative Poverty if they are living in a household with an income below 60% of UK median income in the year in question. An individual is considered to be in Absolute Poverty if they are living in a household with an income below 60% of the inflation adjusted 2010/11 UK median income.	Addressing poverty in all its forms is a key priority for me.  I am committed to delivering long-term, sustainable solutions to poverty for all in our communities, including our young people, right across Northern Ireland.I am currently considering the next steps to be taken in relation to an Executive Anti-Poverty Strategy and addressing the issues faced by children and young people remains a key focus of this ongoing work. Final decisions on the content and implementation of the Anti-Poverty Strategy will be subject to Executive agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what engagement his Department has had with stakeholders, including (i) trade unions; (ii) the PSNI; (iii) the automotive industry; and (iv) the insurance industry, regarding the introduction of biennial MOT testing.
answer_text: I can advise that my Department engaged with stakeholders including those listed above as part of the biennial MOT testing Call for Evidence process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, following the introduction of the Climate Change (Northern Ireland) Act 2022, to detail the number of times his Department has met with the Department for Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to establish sectoral plans.
answer_text: Given the timeframe for the publication of plans as outlined in the Act, my officials have been concentrating first on the development of policies and proposals for inclusion in the Climate Action Plan.  They have been engaging with DAERA officials on a continuous basis throughout this process and this work will help inform development of the sectoral plans that are required to support the Climate Action Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of electrical vehicle charging points, broken down by local council area, for each of the last five years.
answer_text: The provision and operation of publicly accessible electric vehicle charge points is a commercial operation. Responsibility for the maintenance and installation of any commercially operated charge point rests solely with the charge point operator. Current providers operating in the North include ESB, Weev, Maxol, BP Pulse and Easygo. The market is also open to other commercial operators who would wish to provide charging infrastructure.My Department does not hold information detailing electrical vehicle charging points or their locations.  Relevant up to date electric vehicle charge point locations can be found on online platforms, such as https://www.zapmap.com.  The relevant charge point operators are best placed to advise you of their plans for the installation of charge points in your constituency.   Local Councils have also been able to avail of funding for the installation of charge points under the Office for Zero Emissions’, On Street Residential Charge Point Scheme which my Department match funded. Derry City and Strabane District Council are leading a consortium on this work having been successful in securing funding to install 124 charge points across nine Council areas and may be in a position to outline their plans should you wish to contact them. It may also be of interest that The British Government provide statistical updates quarterly on the number of publicly available charging points available in the UK.  The latest update published in January 2024 can be found here: https://www.gov.uk/government/statistics/electric-vehicle-charging-device-statistics-january-2024

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail his plan to improve the electric vehicle infrastructure in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: The provision and operation of publicly accessible electric vehicle charge points is a commercial operation in the North of Ireland. Responsibility for the maintenance and installation of any commercially operated charge point rests solely with the charge point operator. Current providers operating in the North include ESB, Weev, Maxol, BP Pulse and Easygo. The market is also open to other commercial operators who would wish to provide charging infrastructure.The relevant charge point operators are best placed to advise you of their plans for the installation of charge points in your constituency.Funding opportunities for the installation of charge point infrastructure are also provided by the Office for Zero Emission Vehicles (OZEV). These schemes are open to Councils who provide charging infrastructure for EV owners, or potential owners, who do not have driveway access (On Street Residential Charge Point Scheme – ORCS), and for residential and commercial landlords. My Department has made match-funding available to assist local councils making an application to ORCS.Derry City and Strabane District Council made an application under the 2021 -22 round of funding on behalf of nine Local Councils. This application was successful, and the consortium received a Grant Offer Letter from OZEV in July 2022 for funding to install 124 charge points across these council areas. Derry City and Strabane District Council would be best placed to provide you with an update on their plans for the Foyle Constituency.My Department has also set up an EV Infrastructure Task-Force, bringing together representatives from government, consumers, energy providers, industry and EV drivers to consider the barriers to enhancing our EV charging infrastructure requirements.  An Action Plan to deliver a commercially led, fit for purpose, modern EV charging network was agreed in autumn 2022. The Action Plan can be found here:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/electric-vehicle-ev-infrastructure-action-plan .

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will review the existing pothole repair policy to deliver a sustainable and more cost-effective approach.
answer_text: My Department’s Resource budget, which is used to deliver day-to-day maintenance activities such as patching and grass cutting, has been under pressure for a number of years. In response, my Department has had to reduce its routine road maintenance activities to a limited service, which prioritises delivery of essential services for maintaining public safety and protecting the transport network. Consequently, only the highest priority defects across the network are currently being repaired.The level of repairs that can be carried out in future financial years will depend on the 2024-25 final budget determination for my Department. Until such times as sufficient funding is made available, it will not be possible to change the current level of service. The importance of a well maintained road network cannot be understated but this requires investment. I will continue to seek the support of Executive colleagues to enable this to happen.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on the A2 Buncrana Road dualling scheme; and (ii) a date for the commencement of the North West Transport Plan public consultation.
answer_text: Major Road schemes are an important part of the work that is delivered by my Department as we seek to reduce journey times, increase reliability and improve road safety.Work on the A2 Buncrana Road scheme was paused through a decision taken under the NI (Executive Formation etc) Act 2022, to allow appropriate alignment with the emerging transport strategy and plans.Public engagement on the vision and objectives of the North West Transport Plan is scheduled for May 2024, with further full public consultation on the plan proposed after its publication, in winter 2024/25.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when road safety improvements at the Whitehouse and Woodbrook junction, Derry, will be delivered.
answer_text: I can confirm that all the junction markings at the Whitehouse and Woodbrook junction will be refreshed in April. The local Network Development team are also considering if additional signage to improve safety at this busy junction on the Buncrana Road is required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether he will provide immediate financial support to community pharmacies; and (ii) what plans he has to deliver sustainable funding to address the crisis facing the Community Pharmacy sector.
answer_text: I can assure the member that I am committed to working with the Community Pharmacy sector via their representative body, Community Pharmacy NI (CPNI) to deliver fair, sustainable and affordable funding for community pharmacy. Following an urgent meeting I held with representatives of CPNI on 21st February 2024, I have committed to an inflationary uplift of £6.1m for pharmacy services in 2023/24. Furthermore, an additional £4m was provided in recognition of service pressures for the 2023/24 year. Following the commitments in relation to £10.1m, arrangements were made with The Business Services Organisation to invest 10/12th (£8.4m) within the February payment. Arrangements have been put in place to make the remainder available with the March and April payments. Further work will be taken forward to review community pharmacy financial arrangements in the coming months with CPNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the impact of the two child benefit cap on Universal Credit households.
answer_text: This policy was introduced by the United Kingdom Government and under the principle of parity, the same measures apply in Northern Ireland.It is estimated that 8,430 Universal Credit households were impacted by the two-child limit at November 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Drumahoe flood alleviation scheme.
answer_text: Development of potential flood alleviation proposals for Drumahoe remains ongoing. An economically viable scheme has been identified and consultants have been instructed to commence the design phase of this project. Progress to procurement will be dependent on proposals remaining viable and availability of capital funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will increase the annual emergency medicine trainee doctor numbers to bring the workforce to safe levels, as recommended by the Royal College of Emergency Medicine.
answer_text: I am aware of the recommendation in the Royal College of Emergency Medicine’s Northern Ireland Emergency Medicine Workforce Census 2024 to increase the annual number of trainees recruited into this specialty training programme by an additional six for a minimum of six years.My Department hopes to have a new strategic workforce review for emergency medicine completed by December 2024.  While this will provide robust evidence to inform the commissioning of future postgraduate training places, implementation of any increase will depend upon the availability of recurrent funding. A budget for Northern Ireland Departments has not yet been set for 2024/25, although I expect that the settlement will be extremely challenging.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on shortages of Hormone Replacement Therapy products; and (ii) what action is being taken to ensure individuals experiencing menopause are fully supported.
answer_text: There are over 70 Hormone Replacement Therapy (HRT) products authorised for supply in the UK. There were issues with the supply of these products, primarily due to a large increase in demand. However, following intensive engagement between industry and the Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC), who leads on the maintenance of medicine supplies to the UK on behalf of all devolved administrations, there has been considerable improvement in the supply position of HRT products over the last year. DHSC has also produced prescribing advice for healthcare professionals on the availability of HRT products on their Specialist Pharmacy Service (SPS)  website: https://www.sps.nhs.uk/articles/prescribing-available-hrt-products/. In addition, there are currently no active Serious Shortage Protocols in place which further demonstrates the improved supply position of HRT products at present. My Department continues to monitor the supply position locally, while DHSC are continuing to meet with relevant stakeholders, including suppliers and wholesalers to understand current supply positions so appropriate action can be taken should any potential disruptions arise.My Department has a formal arrangement with the National Institute for Health and Clinical Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Clinical Guidelines are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability in Northern Ireland. Where found to be appliable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. In January 2016, my Department endorsed NICE NG23- Menopause: diagnosis and management, meaning these guidelines are applicable in NI within HSC organisations for individuals experiencing menopause.Whilst NICE NG23 is currently endorsed and there are many examples of good practice in terms of menopause care within our system, individuals’ care experiences are not consistent and that means that care is not equitable. My officials have been engaging with primary and secondary care clinicians to benchmark what is available in each Trust and to consider how to standardise best practice care across the region.To help address these concerns, I want to prioritise implementation of the recently published Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) Gynaecology report. My Department has already written to all Trusts about the report and will engage with relevant stakeholders to progress implementation as far as possible within available resources.In addition, menopause care will form part of the Women’s Health Action Plan that my Department is developing that builds on ongoing work and identifies the priority actions across women’s health services that can be taken forward within current budget, as well as those actions for which additional investment will be required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will consider legislation to ensure carers receiving the State Pension can access the full amount of Carer's Allowance.
answer_text: t is a basic principle of the social security system that only one benefit at a time can be paid for the same purpose.  Even though the circumstances which give rise to entitlement to Carer’s Allowance and State Pension are different, they are both designed to provide a degree of replacement for lost or foregone income. Carer’s Allowance provides a measure of financial support for people who forgo the opportunity of full time employment due to caring for a severely disabled person.  State Pension provides a degree of income replacement in retirement. It is possible that an individual over State Pension age can have entitlement to Carer’s Allowance and State Pension at the same time, however the Social Security (Overlapping Benefits) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1979 provide that they cannot be paid in full together.  The payment of State Pension takes precedence, either reducing or extinguishing the amount of Carer’s Allowance payable.Where Carer’s Allowance entitlement is established but cannot be paid due to overlapping benefit rules, the person will keep an underlying entitlement which may give them access to extra payments with other benefits. For example, a carer’s premium in Housing Benefit or the equivalent additional amount for caring in State Pension Credit if they qualify for these income-related benefits.There are no plans for changes to the overlapping benefit rules.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail his plans to (i) address backlogs in MOT testing; and (ii) introduce diesel emissions testing for vehicles.
answer_text: In 2022-2023, the Driver & Vehicle Agency (DVA) conducted over 1.15 million vehicle tests which is the highest number ever recorded in a year. This increase in capacity has been achieved through a range of measures including the recruitment of additional vehicle examiners, the use of overtime to provide cover for leave and offering vehicle test appointments on Sundays and bank holidays, when testing is not normally available.  In 2023, there were only seven days in the year when DVA did not offer MOT appointments.  A further vehicle examiner recruitment competition also launched on 19 February and will be used to fill staff vacancies across the DVA’s network of test centres. Disappointingly, the number of vehicles that failed to attend a booked appointment in 2022-2023 was also the highest on record at 50,772.The DVA appreciates that, in some cases, customers may not be able to have their vehicles tested before their current MOT certificate expires and has taken steps to ensure that motorists are still able to use their vehicles when they are unable to secure an MOT appointment before their current MOT expires.  The latest information on MOTs is available on NI Direct at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/guide-booking-your-mot-vehicle-test and is also included as an advice note with every MOT reminder letter issued to customers.In addition, vehicle test appointments are released on a daily basis, when resources become available, so customers are encouraged to regularly check the booking system as they may be able to change their existing appointment to a different test centre and time if a slot becomes available.  Information on how a customer can change, cancel or view their MOT appointment online is available on NI Direct at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/services/change-cancel-or-view-your-mot-vehicle-test-appointment-online.To further increase vehicle testing capacity, the DVA is investing in new test centres.  The construction of a new test centre at Hydebank, in Belfast, is nearing completion and will open later this year.  The construction contract for a second new test centre at Mallusk has also been awarded and this new test centre is expected to open in 2025.  Once fully operational, these two new centres will provide the capacity to test over 200,000 additional vehicles per annum, which will help the DVA meet the future demand for this service.In regard to the introduction of diesel emission testing, the DVA introduced a diesel smoke test for cars, light and heavy goods vehicles and buses in 2006, in compliance with the roadworthiness directive.  However, for health and safety reasons related to the build-up of fumes in the test halls and in consultation with the Health and Safety Executive, the diesel smoke test for cars and light goods vehicles was suspended in June 2006.The DVA currently conducts a fully compliant diesel smoke test on all heavy goods vehicles, buses and vans over 3,500kgs, and a partial diesel emissions test for

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there are plans to invest in a new MOT vehicle test centre to improve capacity in the North West region.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) is developing proposals for two further new test centres at Campsie and Enniskillen, which includes completing detailed designs, business case approval, submitting planning applications, and preparing tender documentation to enable a procurement process to commence.  It is estimated this work will take approximately six months to complete and my approval to proceed with these proposals will be subject to further consideration and funding availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his plans to improve (i) endocrinology services; and (ii) post-hysterectomy care for patients.
answer_text: The Department of Health acknowledges that endocrinology services waiting lists are unacceptably high.Covid, and general overarching pressures within the HSC have delayed the development of  endocrinology services.As you may be aware, my Department recently launched the Women’s Health Action Plan for Northern Ireland – this signals our commitment to working towards improving services for women requiring endocrinology services and post-hysterectomy care.As Minister, I will continue to do the best I can to deliver for the public with the resources I have available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of no shows for MOT appointments, broken down per test centre, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: DVA’s National Statistics publication ‘DfI Driver, Vehicle, Operator and Enforcement Statistics’ details quarterly figures for the number of vehicles which failed to attend a booked vehicle test in Tables 1.8 (full tests), 1.9 (re-tests) and 1.10 (total tests). These tables can be found at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/driver-vehicle-agency-activity-statisticsThe table below provides this information broken down by test centre, for each of the last five financial years.Number of vehicles which failed to attend a booked vehicle test:2018-192019-202020-212021-222022-: B&RSmall differences in table totals relative to published figures are due to post-publication administrative system changes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what steps he will take to ensure newly-recruited driving examiners are posted to DVA test centres with the greatest demand.
answer_text: Since the resumption of driving test services following the pandemic the Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) has steadily increased their driving test capacity by adopting a range of measures including the recruitment of additional driving examiners, use of overtime to rota off-shift dual role examiners to provide further capacity and cover for leave, they also offer driving test appointments on Sundays and bank holidays, where it is suitable to do so without compromising the integrity of the test. In 2022-2023, the DVA conducted 68,540 driving tests, which is the highest number of driving tests conducted since 2009.  Almost 82% (56,132) of all driving tests in 2022-2023 were Category B (private car) tests.  Disappointingly, 2,812 people failed to attend their test, which is the highest number on record. The DVA is in the process of recruiting new full time driving examiners who on successful completion of their training will be allocated to test centres from late March 2024 onwards, with priority being given to filling vacant posts.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether he will retain free bus and rail travel for those aged over 60; and (ii) when he will provide an update on the consultation for changes to the Concessionary Fares Scheme.
answer_text: The Concessionary Fares Consultation ran from 1st June 2023 to 24th August 2024 and generated over 20,000 responses. Work has been ongoing to analyse those responses and develop a consultation report and accompanying impact assessments. I am currently considering the outcome of the consultation and will provide an update in due course.While my intention is to retain the concession for the 60-64 age group, this must be affordable within the budget made available to my Department and will require the support of my Executive colleagues.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when he will commence a public consultation into biennial MOT testing.
answer_text: I have agreed to explore with my officials the potential for biennial vehicle testing in the North and in due course this may result in a public consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) whether he will increase funding for the Supporting People Programme; and (ii) his plans to improve workforce retention and recruitment.
answer_text: On 6th March 2024 I announced an additional £3 million in funding for the Supporting People Programme. The Executive will agree and set the 2024-25 Budget and once the outcome is known I will consider how best to allocate the Department’s budget against key priorities. The Housing Executive is not the direct employer for Supporting People staff and therefore, from a contractual perspective, has no role in workforce recruitment and retention.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the total number of cases which have been referred to his Department's debt management services in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The table below details the total number of cases referred to my Department’s Debt Management Branch in each of the last five years. Financial YearVolume of CasesReferred*2019/20153,2152020/21184,4392021/22200,7282022/23183,3632023/24**158,764								*the number of customers will be lower than the number of cases referred as a person may have more than one social security benefit debt** period April 23 to January 24

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what plans are in place to ensure the Housing Executive has the capacity to deliver Group 2 changes of the Fundamental Review of Allocations Implementation Project.
answer_text: Work is ongoing to implement proposals within the second phase of the Fundamental Review of Allocations project.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of applicants on the Disabled Facility Grant waiting list for each of the last five years.
answer_text: The number of applicants currently awaiting grant approval in respect of a Disabled Facilities Grant is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. You should therefore direct your question to the Northern Ireland Housing Executive at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he intends to roll-out 20mph speed limits outside schools.
answer_text: There have been two tranches of part time 20mph speed limits delivered which introduced these measures at 215 schools, an investment of about £4m. Schools were assessed in accordance with the criteria in the ‘Road Safety at Schools’ document which can be viewed at:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/road-safety-schools-rsppg-e070A review is underway to determine if a further tranche is justified.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will review the current rate of pay for NHS interpreters, which has not been updated in eighteen years.
answer_text: Face-to-face spoken language interpreters are independent self-employed contractors who are not employed by the Health and Social Care (HSC) service but are paid for their service through the Business Services Organisation (BSO) at an agreed rate.HSC Interpreters must be registered with BSO and adhere to the HSC Terms of Engagement.  This stipulates an hourly rate of pay as well as a preparation payment.  The Department is aware that pay has not increased for a number of years and the impact this is having on the valuable service that interpreters provide. You will, however, be aware of the significant financial constraints within the wider Health Service.  I have nevertheless asked the Strategic Planning and Performance Group to review this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will implement a 20mph speed limit outside Eglinton Primary School.
answer_text: Part-time 20mph limits near schools are implemented based on assessments considering collision history, speed, traffic volume, infrastructure, and site constraints.  Schools have been assessed in accordance with DfI's policy ‘Road Safety at Schools’ which can be viewed at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/road-safety-schools-rsppg-e070The programme to date has been rolled out in tranches, with the most recent completed in November 2022.  Over the coming weeks, I will be considering my priorities, including the potential for a further tranche of the 20mph schools programme, in the context of the funding that is currently available to my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will introduce legislative change to permit Maternity Allowance to be treated as earnings similar to the policy applied to Statutory Maternity Pay, for Universal Credit claimants.
answer_text: Universal Credit is a means tested system of support. Therefore, when an individual claims Universal Credit, their award will be adjusted to take account of any other financial support that they already receive – including earnings, income and benefits.Maternity Allowance is a benefit which may be payable to pregnant women who cannot receive Statutory Maternity Pay and is intended to help meet daily living costs. As any unearned income provided to meet daily living costs is taken fully into account in determining entitlement to Universal Credit, this means that Maternity Allowance is deducted pound for pound from the total value of the award. This principle also applies to other benefits, such as new style Jobseeker’s Allowance and new style Employment and Support Allowance.Statutory Maternity Pay is treated as a form of earnings in common with other statutory payments paid by employers. As a result, when adjusting the Universal Credit award, as with other earnings, Statutory Maternity Pay is subject to the work allowance and tapering rules in Universal Credit. This means that claimants retain 45 pence for every pound of earnings (or more if they are entitled to a work allowance).There are currently no plans to change the way that Maternity Allowance is treated in Universal Credit.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 6765/22-27, to detail (i) a timeline for implementation; and (ii) for his assessment of the impact this strategy will have in improving life chances, educational outcomes and addressing health inequalities in disadvantaged areas.
answer_text: Anti-Poverty is a priority issue for me and I want to ensure that the actions in the Strategy are deliverable and can make a lasting difference. The Executive Anti-Poverty Strategy should aim to help people here get the best possible start in life, giving them the opportunity to live in safe, stable homes, to have a good education and a good job.I am currently considering the next steps to be taken in relation to the Anti-Poverty Strategy and final decisions on the content and timeline for implementation will be subject to Executive agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what engagement his Department has had with local councils and the voluntary and community sector in relation to the increased level of needs presenting in the community.
answer_text: My officials engage with local councils and voluntary and community sector partners on an ongoing basis, to deliver a host of policies and programmes to meet the level of needs presenting in communities, that aim to lift people out of poverty.Examples include the refresh of the policy and funding frameworks which underpin our strategic support for the voluntary and community sector, the enhanced delivery of Neighbourhood Renewal programmes and the implementation of local Social Supermarket models. My Department also engages specifically with councils on an ongoing basis through a variety of fora, including the PSG/SOLACE Engagement Forum and Community Planning partnerships.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what action he will take to address the growing number of children living in relative poverty, particularly in the Derry and Strabane area.
answer_text: My Department provides funding to organisations in the Derry City & Strabane District Council Area through the Neighbourhood Renewal Investment Fund to deliver community-based interventions to tackle the impact of poverty and deprivation in the area.In the broader context, I remain committed to delivering long-term, sustainable solutions to poverty for children in all areas of Northern Ireland, including the Derry and Strabane area.  I am currently considering the next steps to be taken in relation to the Anti-Poverty Strategy. Final content of the Strategy will be subject to Executive agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what schemes he will bring forward for the Foyle constituency as part of the £8.1 million funding announced for structural road maintenance.
answer_text: The additional £8.1m of funding that I provided for my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme is to deliver additional resurfacing schemes and continue to address the poor condition of our road network. This funding has now been allocated across all four of my Department’s road divisions.Work has started on the planning and delivery of new schemes for delivery this financial year. However, as development and delivery of the programmes is on-going, a full breakdown of the schemes is not yet available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for his assessment of the increased number of people using foodbanks; and (ii) to detail his plans to address food poverty in our communities.
answer_text: My Department does not directly fund foodbanks and does not compile statistics on the number of people accessing them.  However, engagement with organisations and people in local communities would indicate a significant increase in the number of people using foodbanks as a result of the increased Cost of Living.The Trussell Trust operates the largest network of foodbanks here and publishes 6 monthly statistics which support this assessment.  The statistics can be viewed at: https://www.trusselltrust.org/news-and-blog/latest-stats/end-year-stats/ My Department has been working with local Councils on a co-design basis to roll out Social Supermarket models across Northern Ireland as an alternative to foodbanks. The aim of Social Supermarkets is to offer a longer term, more sustainable, response to food poverty by providing wraparound services that seek to address the underlying reasons why someone requires assistance alongside access to food.  In the broader context, I remain committed to delivering long-term, sustainable solutions to poverty, including food poverty, in all its forms for our communities right across Northern Ireland. I am currently considering the next steps to be taken in relation to the development of an Anti-Poverty Strategy, which will be subject to Executive agreement.	In the short term details of government support for those struggling with the Cost of Living are available at: https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/campaigns/cost-living

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) what engagement he has had with the Our Future Foyle project; and (ii) whether he will consider the installation of suicide prevention infrastructure along the Foyle Bridge.
answer_text: My Officials had significant involvement with the ‘Our Future Foyle’ team during the development of that project. and continue to liaise with various groups in the area on the provision of suicide prevention services, including the potential for the installation of higher parapet railings on the Foyle Bridge. You will be pleased to hear that I have recently met with Professor Siobhan O’Neill, the Mental Health Champion, and we discussed the important role that infrastructure plays in support of wellbeing, and the promotion of positive mental health. During this meeting it was explained that before any work can be carried out on the bridge, a detailed structural model will need to be developed to ensure that any changes to the railings do not compromise the structural integrity of the bridge. The model is estimated to cost around £800k, and the subsequent works to install a barrier may cost in the region of £10m.While I am very supportive of the need for these works, I am not in a position to fund them due to the very challenging budgetary position within my Department. I do however want to assure you that my officials will continue to work with the Mental Health Champion, and the other groups involved in the provision of crisis services, to seek to identify funding to carry out the bridge assessment and the installation of suicide prevention measures on Foyle Bridge.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the future of the Our Future Foyle project.
answer_text: The Health element of Future Foyle, the awareness raising project called Comkit, has been delivered and is being widely used and promoted. The considerable cost of other elements, combined with challenging budgetary positions, technical complexity and other Departmental priorities, set against the COVID-19 background, have meant that other elements of the initiative were unable to be progressed.A Regional Mental Health Crisis Service implementation plan was published in November 2021.  The Department and the Public Health Agency (PHA) are leading on delivery of the new crisis service, with HSC Trusts responsible for the implementation and delivery of regionally consistent mental health crisis services. It is anticipated that the Foyle area will benefit from this implementation.A Cross Departmental Coordinated Action Group was established in October 2023 by the Mental Health Champion to consider crisis services and suicide prevention in Derry/Londonderry. The purpose of the Group is to develop actions to address suicide prevention, along with other relevant issues, at the river and bridges in Derry/Londonderry.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 6355/22-27, (i) when he will assess the findings of the evaluation; and (ii) for his assessment of the effectiveness of early intervention programmes such as the Healthy Happy Minds Therapeutic and Counselling Programme.
answer_text: I appreciate there is significant interest in the pilot and post project evaluation, however, it is important that I take the time to consider this along with wider research/feedback from stakeholders in developing the Department's longer-term approach.The benefits of the right early intervention at the right time, in the right place and by the right people, are widely accepted and can improve outcomes for children and young people.  The effectiveness of any early intervention programme would be dependent on specific assessment of the impact and outcomes.  Early intervention is not limited to the early years of children’s lives, but also to intervening early to address problems that are emerging for children and young people at any point in their lives.  Nor is early intervention limited to education services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) what steps have been taken to address the links between Autism Spectrum Disorder and eating disorders; and (ii) his plans to improve access to treatment within children's services for patients with these co-occurring conditions.
answer_text: The links between Autism Spectrum Disorder and eating problems are well known and evidenced in research. These problems can be linked to sensory differences, food presentation, environmental factors and illness. National Autistic Society notes they do not know how common eating disorders are for autistic people. Some research suggests between 4% to 23% of people with an eating disorder are also autistic. Some research suggests anorexia is the most common eating disorder amongst autistic peopleCare pathways across children’s health services exist for patients with these co-occurring conditions. Children and young people who present with atypical eating disorders would present to primary care and non-mental health settings such as GP, neurodevelopmental and paediatric clinics. If young people with ASD have been assessed as having Bulimia Nervosa or Anorexia Nervosa they would be seen within CAMHS Eating Disorder services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many under-18s have received treatment for eating disorders, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department does not collect information on the number of under 18's who received eating disorder treatment. The figures provided in the table below are the numbers of New Contacts seen by Eating Disorder Services for Children, for the financial years 2019/20 to 2023/24 year to date (January 2024).  Table 1. Number of new contacts seen by the CAMHS Eating Disorder Service between 2019/20 to January 2024, by HSC TrustHSC Trust2019/202020/212021/222022/23April 2023 to January 2024Belfast4566757456Northern7769787154South EasternSouth Eastern HSC Trust provision is via Belfast HSC Trust.Southern4761886255Western6858616862Source: SPPGNote: The information provided is in relation to New Contacts, not 'people'.  It is not possible to determine whether there are multiple new contacts for individuals as the information submitted by HSC Trusts is in an aggregated format and cannot be disaggregated.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the rationale for referring Members seeking a ministerial response via an Assembly Written Question to the Public Affairs Unit at the Housing Executive.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the quantum of funding allocated by the Arts Council to (i) organisations; and (ii) individuals in 2022-23, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: The distribution of funding is an operational matter for the Arts Council of Northern Ireland. You should, therefore, direct your question to the Communications Manager of the Arts Council of Northern Ireland at info@artscouncil-ni.org.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 5522/22-27, whether the Travelling Expenses and Remission of Charges Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2004 update will make provision for parents with sick children.
answer_text: The focus of the update is to reinstate automatic passporting for those eligible to receive free sight tests and free Health Service dental treatment through the Help with Health Care (HwHC) scheme following the migration of their benefits to Universal Credit by the Department of Communities (DfC).The commencement of a wider review of the provisions within these Regulations and associated HwHC scheme will be considered once this urgent work is completed and in line with available resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the Empty Homes Strategy funding allocations in each of the last 10 years.
answer_text: The Department provided funding for the Empty Homes Strategy totalling £9,282,593 million in the last 10 years. Funding was allocated as outlined in the table below:  DateOrganisation FundedAmountMarch 2014 Clanmil Housing Association: Empty Homes Loan Fund £3,700,000March 2015Clanmil Housing Association: Empty Homes Loan Fund £5,500,000March 2017 South Regional Development Office Neighbourhood Renewal Scheme: Mount Zion House£43,356.82May 2017South Regional Development Office Neighbourhood Renewal Scheme: Mount Zion House£37,171.40November 2018South Regional Development Office Neighbourhood Renewal Scheme: Mount Zion House£2064.83The Department does not hold information regarding the Housing Executive allocation and spend under the Empty Homes Strategy. You should, therefore, direct your question to the Housing Executive at its dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how much of the £8.1 million funding announced for structural road maintenance schemes will be allocated to each DfI Roads division.
answer_text: The additional £8.1m funding for my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme is to deliver additional resurfacing schemes and continue to address the poor condition of our road network. This funding has now been allocated across all four of my Department’s road divisions.My Department will prioritise decisions and interventions on the basis of a number of data factors including road usage and general surface condition surveys as well as information from maintenance inspections, public enquiries and public liability claims, as well as on the professional engineering judgements of local engineers. The additional £8.1m for structural maintenance activities is to be allocated as follows:Northern Division 	£2265kSouthern Division 	£2050kEastern Division	£915kWestern Division	£2870k

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will consider establishing a strategic oversight group to improve cooperation between DfI Roads and utility companies on future street and road improvement schemes.
answer_text: The Street Works (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 (“the Order”) forms the legislative framework for street works activities. The Department has issued a number of codes of practice under the Order which were prepared jointly by the Northern Ireland Road Authority and Utilities Committee (NIRAUC) and the Department.The Order places a duty on my Department to use best endeavours in the co-ordination of street works with a parallel duty on the undertakers of these works to co-operate with the department.The Code of Practice for the Co-ordination of Street Works and Works for Road Purposes and Related Matters defines the co-ordination function, with the purpose of minimising inconvenience and to protect the street structure and the apparatus within it.The Northern Ireland Street Works Register and Notification System (NISRANS) is a vital tool in the co-ordination and communication of all such street works.There are already a number of groups set up for street works activities including NIRAUC and four Divisional Road and Utility Committees, DRAUC. Officials in my Department are also in daily contact with utility providers to co-ordinate works and balance the potentially conflicting interests of road users and utility customers.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the progress on establishing an Infrastructure Commission to advise on major capital projects following an Audit Office analysis that major infrastructure projects have increased by £2.5 billion.
answer_text: A cross-departmental working group was established to bring forward advice on the options for an Infrastructure Commission. The group produced an initial assessment of high-level options in early 2022.  Officials will review this assessment to take account of developments including the NIAO report, the forthcoming SIB report on root causes of delay, output from the ongoing SIB Review, and the work of the recently established Infrastructure Sub-Group of the NICS Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department will include safeguarding measures for young families and vulnerable individuals in temporary and emergency accommodation placements as part of Year 3 of the Strategic Action Plan for Temporary Accommodation.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has statutory responsibility for responding to homelessness, including provision of homelessness accommodation, and has provided information for this reply.While the Housing Executive does prioritise safeguarding of all individuals in temporary accommodation, any consideration of specific measures in Year 3 of the Strategic Action Plan for Temporary Accommodation, will be in the context of wider safeguarding measures which are already in place with the Housing Executive’s Safeguarding Policy & Procedures available viahttps://www.nihe.gov.uk/getattachment/0fe862bf-c44f-403f-b95a-ed7191e2c7b3/Safeguarding-Policy-and-Procedures.pdfThe Year 3 Action Plan is still being developed and is subject to engagement with internal and external stakeholders such as the Central Homelessness Forum.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) for an update on the non-consolidated payment for Northern Ireland Civil Service (NICS) agency staff; and (ii) what plans she has to offer permanent positions to existing agency staff to address the workforce shortfall within NICS.
answer_text: (i) The non-consolidated, non-pensionable payment is an element of the formal 2023 NICS Pay Award offer that has been made to Trade Unions. As recruitment agency workers have parity of pay with substantive NICS staff, they will be entitled to the payment if they meet the eligibility criteria. My department is working closely with the relevant recruitment agencies to prepare for delivery of the 2023 pay award, following the conclusion of Trade Union Side consultation.(ii) The NICS cannot offer recruitment agency workers permanent positions as to do this would be contrary to our statutory obligation to adhere to the Merit Principle, that is, the requirement that selection for appointment to posts in the NICS should be on merit, on the basis of fair and open competition. In December 2023 the NICS launched an external Administrative Officer competition to fill permanent posts in the Department for Communities. There are also plans for an external Administrative Officer competition to fill permanent posts across the NICS. These are external competitions and recruitment agency workers currently on assignment to the NICS can apply for these permanent positions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will introduce a mandatory licensing scheme and protections for the tattoo and body piercing industry.
answer_text: Part V of the Local Government (Miscellaneous Provisions) Northern Ireland Order 1985 provides for a registration scheme for persons carrying on the practices of tattooing, ear piercing, acupuncture, cosmetic piercing, semi-permanent skin colouring and electrolysis.  It gives district councils powers to require anyone providing these services in their areas to register and to comply with any associated byelaws. There are currently no plans to amend this scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 5299/22-27, and in light of the reduction in successful applications for Free School Meals in 2023-24, whether he will expand the provision to include all Universal Credit claimants.
answer_text: My answer to AQW 5299/22-27 referenced that the figures for 2023/24 were as at 8 February 2024. Applications will continue to be received and processed by the Education Authority until the end of the 2023/24 academic year resulting in the number of children entitled to both free school meals and uniform grants rising from the figure provided as at 8 February 2024. My Department is continuing to progress a review of the eligibility criteria for free school meals and uniform grants. The options being considered as part of this review include those that raise the income threshold that applies to households applying on the basis of entitlement to Universal Credit. I will be considering the next steps for this review in the time ahead.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what action he has taken to secure funding for a pay and grading review for Education Authority support staff.
answer_text: Following the agreement of the financial package from HM Treasury, the Finance Minister allocated additional Resource DEL funding of £296.8m to the Department of Education.  However, this did not include any funding in respect of the Pay & Grading review for education sector support staff.  The outstanding queries in relation to the Pay & Grading review business case are currently being addressed and the Finance Minister has indicated that funding for the review can be revisited as part of the Budget 2024-25 process. My Department will be submitting a number of bids as part of this process, and this will include the estimated cost of the Pay & Grading review to the end of 2024-25.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his plans to increase capacity at the Regional Fertility Centre, to ensure patients can access IVF treatment in a timely manner.
answer_text: On 15 February, I announced that publicly funded In Vitro Fertilisation (IVF) treatment will increase to one full cycle for eligible women. A woman will be eligible if she meets the published access criteria for IVF and has been placed on the waiting list for publicly funded IVF/ICSI treatment on or after 1 October 2022. It should be noted that staff at the Regional Fertility Centre have worked extremely hard to improve treatment waiting times following the COVID-19 pandemic and to create the capacity to increase to one full cycle within the current limited budget.  Whilst I would support further expansion of the service, it will require recurrent funding assurances, which have not been provided at this time. In addition, the RFC will need to increase its physical footprint and ensure the appropriate number of specialist staff are available to deliver any further increase in provision.A copy of the press release can be accessed at https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/news/increase-funded-ivf-provision

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what engagement his Department has had with the Department for Communities in relation to the retention of an automatic entitlement to assistance with healthcare costs for Universal Credit claimants.
answer_text: My Department has acknowledged the concerns raised in relation to Universal Credit not being an automatically passporting benefit in Northern Ireland and is committed to taking forward the necessary legislation to include Universal Credit recipients with incomes below specified thresholds. This work, along with a review of the interim measure (HC1 Form), has commenced, and as part of this process there is ongoing engagement between my Department and the Department of Communities.  To date the main engagement has been around; potential options to streamline the interim measure; the availability of statistical information to evidence the impact of making Universal Credit recipients, with incomes below a specified threshold, an automatically passporting benefit within this scheme; and around raising awareness within society of the need to apply via the interim measure for financial assistance with health costs for individuals who have migrated from a legacy benefit onto Universal Credit.As part of this process, wider engagement has also taken place between my officials and colleagues in other GB Jurisdictions and NI Departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will consider reinstating the School Holiday Food Grant scheme.
answer_text: Following a decision by the Executive, the Department of Education, made, on an interim basis, School Holiday Food Grant payments from July 2020 to March 2023.  The ringfenced funding for this ceased in 2023 and the payments were therefore discontinued. I will be considering the way forward on this issue in the time ahead.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will reverse funding cuts to (i) the Bookstart Baby programme; and (ii) the Engage Programme.
