answer_text: Due to significant budgetary pressures, the Department made the difficult decision not to continue funding the Bookstart Baby Programme in 2023/24.  Any decision regarding budget allocations in 2024/25 will be considered in the context of my Department’s 2024/25 allocation and wider financial pressures. I recognise the importance of encouraging parents to read with their children from birth.  I also acknowledge the positive impact the programme had in promoting an early love of reading, developing early communication skills, and supporting attachment between children and their parents.   The importance of reading to young children continues to be emphasised through the ‘Big Bedtime Read’ within the Getting Ready to Learn Programme. The Engage programme was initially funded, during 2020-21 and 2021-22, using specific Covid-19 funding provided by the Department of Finance.  As further Covid funding was not available, the third year of the programme was funded by DE and began in September 2022 under Ministerial Direction, which approved funding up to March 2023.  The Engage programme ended in March 2023 as further funding was not available. There are currently no plans for future Engage funding. Feedback from schools has shown that children and young people benefitted from the support that the programme provided, in particular at early years and key transition points. We will learn from the excellent practice that has been established as a result of the programme and use this to inform our policies going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has considered the development of a separate strategy to deliver comprehensive mental health statistics data for Northern Ireland, as recommended by the UK Office for Statistics Regulation in its review of mental health statistics in 2021.
answer_text: The Department recognises the importance of having robust data systems in place to ensure that data is accessible, usable and can act as a strategic asset for decision making, policy development and to support the delivery of services.  Reflecting this need, in 2022 the Department published a Data Strategy for Health and Social Care Northern Ireland (HSCNI) 2022-2030, which outlines the vision, mission and objectives for data and information for health and social care up to 2030.  A copy of the Strategy is available on the publications section of the DoH website.  Implementation of this Data Strategy, which is subject to resource availability, will help ensure development of robust datasets across health and social care, including for mental health services, and is aligned to the Office for Statistics Regulation’s recommendation.  Action 34 of the Mental Health Strategy commits to the development of a Regional Outcomes Framework in collaboration with service users and professionals, to underpin and drive service development and delivery.  The Framework was finalised in October 2022 and implementation is currently underway, aligned to the delivery of the Encompass project.  Once fully implemented, the Outcomes Framework will help ensure that the collection of robust data to enable outcomes measurement is firmly embedded in the delivery of mental health services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what engagement his Department has had with the landlord of Altnagelvin Driving Test Centre in relation to conducting essential road surface repairs.
answer_text: Road surface repairs of the premises at Altnagelvin Driving Test Centre were completed by the landlord on 29 February 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the total number of approved social workers for each Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) what steps his Department has taken to improve the Regional Emergency Social Work Service.
answer_text: detail the total number of approved social workers for each Health and Social Care Trust. Number of Approved Social Workers at year end(who have fulfilled Requirements consistent with quality standards)TRUST ruTRUSTs202320222021202020192018BHSCT 292936386132NHSCT636155625757SEHSCT484143494230SHSCT403629253233WHSCT292632384037Source: Directed Functions 9.7b(ii) What steps his Department has taken to improve the Regional Emergency Social Work Service.The Regional Emergency Social Work Service (RESWS) was commissioned in 2013 by HSCB in response to a number of key policy drivers which highlighted the need to review existing emergency and out of hours social work arrangements, with a view to the creation of a comprehensive, modernised emergency and out of hours social work service for Northern Ireland.The RESWS continues to provide an emergency out of hour’s social work response across all five Trusts and across all of social care, including Family and Child Care, Learning Disability, Mental Health, Physical Health and Disability and Older Peoples Services. The service operates from 5pm-9am Monday to Friday and on a 24-hour basis at weekends and public holidays. Since the RESW service became operational in 2013, there have been a number of significant changes in Northern Ireland which have impacted on the RESW Service including; population need, policy and legislation; HSC service configuration and workforce challenges. In response to these challenges and in an attempt to resolve some of the service pressures being experienced currently, SPPG has commissioned a review of this service commencing in Mid-March 2024 to understand;What service configuration is required to meet the demands for emergency and out of hours social work provision across the Region to fulfil statutory requirements; Whether the existing RESW service model is fit for purpose within the HSC System context in 2023; Whether there are existing NI generated proposals which may enhance existing RESW Service provision; Whether there are any existing models of good practice out with Northern Ireland which may be useful in the NI context; Whether there are any resource or funding implications linked to the above.Whether there are any workforce implications linked to the above.Additionally, work has been undertaken by Department of Health to focus on the provision of specific training to upskill 47 suitably qualified staff located across all trust areas, who can lawfully undertake the Approved Social Worker (ASW) statutory functions of waiting under Direction 1 (2023) of the MH (NI) Order. Currently these posts are being finalised in terms of the associated human resources processes. The purpose of these staff will be to relieve ASWs once a mental health assessment has been completed, which indicates the need for detention. These staff will be suitably qualified to wait with the person until they can be safely admitted to a bed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his plans to publish mental health data sets including waiting times for (i) psychological therapies; (ii) adult community mental health services; (iii) primary care talking therapy hubs; and (iv) adult in-patient mental health services.
answer_text: Currently the Department is not in the position to publish mental health data sets including waiting times for these specific mental health services as data is not currently available or of sufficient quality.  The Mental Health Strategy recognises that, in order to ensure we have the right services to meet the needs of the population, we must have data to measure outcomes.  Action 34 of the Strategy commits to “Develop a Regional Outcomes Framework in collaboration with service users and professionals, to underpin and drive service development and delivery.”  The Outcomes Framework will collect data on outcomes relating to service Quantity (‘How much did we do?’), Quality (‘How well did we do it?’) and Effect (‘Is anyone better off?’).   A comprehensive set of basic metrics, including data for eg number of referrals, waiting time to first appointment etc, has been identified for the Outcomes Framework, to be collected and reported upon across mental health services.  The Outcomes Framework is being implemented as part of Encompass and once it is fully embedded, consideration will then be given to the publication of mental health data.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will consider increasing the rate of the Winter Fuel Payment.
answer_text: Social security benefits, pensions and allowances, including Winter Fuel Payments, in Northern Ireland (NI) are generally maintained in parity with Great Britain. As Communities Minister, I continue to consider any additional ways to help vulnerable people.  However, any deviation from parity which increases costs in Northern Ireland would need to be paid from the NI Block Grant and require Executive approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 5644/22-27, whether he will increase funding for the provision of Home Starter Packs.
answer_text: Pursuant to AQW 5644/22-27, the Executive will agree and set the 2024-25 Budget and once the outcome is known I will consider how best to allocate the Department’s allocations against key priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients awaiting an autism assessment, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust, for the each of the last five years.
answer_text: Please find information detailed below. Table 1. Number of children and adults waiting for an autism assessment, by HSC Trust, as at 31 December 2019-2023. BelfastNorthern*South EasternSouthernWesternTotalDecember 20191,3891,0622212879983,957December 20201,9941,8822833051,2235,687December 20212,6142,4693994131,4117,306December 20223,2963,6135567211,7629,948December 20234,2625,3897711,4702,23814,130Source: SPPGNote: Adult Northern HSC Trust autism assessment waiting time information from March 2020 to March 2023 is not available due to unavailability of information arising from system migration issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) what engagement he has had with the Minister of Finance to deliver fair pay for public transport staff; and (ii) to provide a timeline for the implementation of a financial package to secure a pay deal.
answer_text: The Executive agreed at its meeting on 15 February 2024 to allocate my Department additional resource budget of £87.8m. Consequently, I have been able to provide an additional budget allocation to Translink to enable it to negotiate a 2023-24 pay award with trade unions. I welcome the positive development on these negotiations and the unions agreement to suspend the forthcoming strikes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the total funding allocated by his Department for homelessness prevention in the last five years; and (ii) whether he intends to increase homelessness prevention funding for the next financial year.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Housing Executive provided the table below which details funding provided to support a range of projects as part of the Homelessness Prevention Fund over the past five years. It should be noted that these projects are directly supported to deliver homelessness prevention and the Housing Executive also supports a range of outside agencies which are not included in the figures below as they deliver services which include but are not limited to homelessness prevention. Year Homelessness Prevention Fund2019/20£336,217.002020/21£749.800.422021/22£1,287,751.15*2022/23£898,068.792023/24£0***In 2021/22 there was £400k of COVID-19 related funding allocated to the Homelessness Prevention Fund.**Funding challenges in 2023/24 resulted in the Housing Executive being unable to allocate any expenditure towards the delivery of a Homelessness Prevention Fund. In relation to part two of your question, the Executive will agree and set the 2024-25 Budget and once the outcome is known I will consider how best to allocate the Departments budget against key priorities.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what steps they will take to address waiting times for processing applications to the Victims' Payment Scheme.
answer_text: Almost £38 million has been paid to victims since the Victims’ Payments Scheme opened for applications on 31 August 2021.	This is a novel scheme and each application is unique with its own complexities.  Many applications may also include multiple related relevant incidents (as defined in the Victims’ Payments Regulations 2020).  The Executive Office has committed to funding a range of support services to enhance the application process to ensure a victim centred approach.To date, funding of almost £5 million has been provided to support victims’ and survivors’ representative groups via the Victims and Survivors Service (VSS).  This has facilitated the recruitment of welfare officers and admin support staff to assist applicants with the application process and additional funding to provide health and wellbeing support. These groups have been working closely with the Victims’ Payments Board and VSS on improving the process for applicants, and feedback from those discussions will be considered at the Sectoral Engagement Group which is chaired by TEO.		TEO also continues to provide additional resources to evidence retrieval partners to assist in the retrieval of evidence on behalf of applicants and the Victims’ Payments Board.  TEO officials engage regularly with colleagues in the Department of Justice and the Victims’ Payments Board to consider scheme process and communications issues.  In addition, the Victims’ Payments Board are currently engaged with the Department of Finance’s Business Consultancy Service to conduct a high-level review of the scheme administration processes to help enhance the delivery of the scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will engage with the Minister of Finance to ring-fence funds garnered through the Soft Drinks Industry Levy to address health conditions such as obesity and Type 2 diabetes.
answer_text: The Soft Drink Industry Levy (SDIL) came into effect across the UK from 6 April 2018. It is important to note that the levy was never formally hypothecated for any specific policy purpose at the UK level. This means that there is no specific line in the NI budget that identifies resources from the SDIL. Northern Ireland may however have received Barnett consequentials from any increased spending in policy areas in England because of the revenue from the SDIL.	My Department previously explored the potential to use funds equivalent to those likely to have been raised through the SDIL in Northern Ireland for tackling obesity and other public health related issues, but given there was no budget line this did not prove to be possible. 	However, my Department is currently consulting on a new, co-produced obesity strategic framework, ‘Healthy Futures’. The public consultation commenced on 24 November 2023 and closes on 1 March 2024. My Department will give further consideration for the potential to use resources such as the Soft Drinks Industry Levy, and the mechanisms to do so, as work on the new strategic framework progresses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will address the difficulty patients are facing in terms of access to emergency dentalcare, particularly in the Foyle constituency.
To ask the Minister of Finance whether she will ring-fence funds generated via the Soft Drink Industry Levy for obesity prevention and treatment.
answer_text: ANSWERFunds that are generated by the Soft Drinks Industry Levy are not formally linked by the British Government to any specific spending. Rather, spending in Whitehall departments for children’s food and to promote children’s health is allocated through Spending Reviews. The Executive receives a Barnett consequential on any changes in funding provided to Whitehall departments. For Spending Reviews this is provided at a departmental level rather than by specific programme. My department is not informed of how much the Executive receives as a result of the Levy. The Minister of Health has primary policy responsibility for health-related issues such as obesity prevention and treatment; and has the ability to prioritise his departmental budget in line with Ministerial priorities. As Finance Minister, I am willing to consider proposals which will help to address this issue. It would be for the Executive to agree to the ring-fencing of such funds and the quantum of funding to be allocated.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister (i) for an update on the Programme for Government; and (ii) whether this will include a specific, standalone housing outcome.
answer_text: Work to develop a Programme for Government is moving forward quickly. The Executive is agreed on priorities, including: childcare; reducing hospital waiting lists; tackling violence against women and girls; Special Educational Needs; housing; developing a globally competitive economy; and Reform and Transformation of public services. We will move at pace to agree a programme and bring it before the Assembly for scrutiny and approval. We will keep Members updated as progress continues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his plans to (i) improve emergency support for people experiencing mental health crisis; and (ii) increase resources within the Regional Emergency Social Work Service.
answer_text: (i) improve emergency support for people experiencing mental health crisis;Implementing the new regional crisis service is a commitment in the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031, under actions 12 and 27.  The Strategic Planning and Performance Group and the Public Health Agency are working in partnership to deliver on both of these actions, to develop a Regional Mental Health Crisis Service for Northern Ireland. The components of the proposed regional mental health crisis service   are:  accessing mental health crisis services, primary care and interagency partnership, community crisis service, mental health liaison service and crisis resolution home treatment. A mapping report of current crisis services including community and voluntary sector is currently been finalised. Phase 2 future state work has commenced including scoping what a good service would look like. A Regional Mental Health Crisis Service workshop is taking place in March 2024 with all relevant stakeholders to progress this work, to include pathways and access to emergency mental health crisis. In line with the usual process for commissioning of services, any decision to commission mental health crisis services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and within the context of future budget availability.(ii) increase resources within the Regional Emergency Social Work Service. The Regional Emergency Social Work Service (RESWS) was commissioned in 2013 by HSCB in response to a number of key policy drivers which highlighted the need to review existing emergency and out of hours social work arrangements, with a view to the creation of a comprehensive, modernised emergency and out of hours social work service for Northern Ireland.The RESWS continues to provide an emergency out of hour’s social work response across all five Trusts and across all of social care, including Family and Child Care, Learning Disability, Mental Health, Physical Health and Disability and Older Peoples Services. The service operates from 5pm-9am Monday to Friday and on a 24-hour basis at weekends and public holidays. Since the RESW service became operational in 2013, there have been a number of significant changes in Northern Ireland which have impacted on the RESW Service including; population need, policy and legislation; HSC service configuration and workforce challenges. In response to these challenges and in an attempt to resolve some of the service pressures being experienced currently, The Department has commissioned a review of this service commencing in Mid-March 2024 to understand;•	What service configuration is required to meet the demands for emergency and out of hours social work provision across the Region to fulfil statutory requirements; •	Whether the existing RESW service model is fit for purpose within the HSC System context in 2023; •	Whether there are existing NI generated proposals which may enhance existing RESW Service provision; •	Whether there are

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail his Department's assessment of the costs associated with the appointment of independent persons to hold a Public Inquiry or Planning Appeal, in order to relieve pressures faced by the Planning Appeals Commission.
answer_text: I recognise the resourcing challenges of the Planning Appeals Commission and acknowledge that, under the Planning Act (NI) 2011, the Department for Infrastructure has powers to appoint independent persons to conduct certain matters such as hearings or examinations instead of the Commission. However, this is not necessarily a simple undertaking and I will be considering this issue with my officials in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) what engagement his Department has had with the Planning Appeals Commission regarding delays in processing cases; and (ii) what actions are being undertaken to address these delays, particularly with respect to green infrastructure.
answer_text: The Planning Appeals Commission is a wholly independent Non-Departmental Public Body and is sponsored by the Department for Justice. My officials have been engaging with them to encourage the best use of existing resources and procedures and overall, I want to work with all partners to ensure that everyone is playing their part to support and deliver an efficient and effective planning system, although that is subject to all parts of the planning system being properly funded and resourced, including the Planning Appeals Commission.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the funding position of nutrition and dietetic services for patients with an Autism Spectrum Disorder diagnosis, in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: HSC Trusts offer Nutrition and Dietetic services in accordance with the needs of the population. These services are provided based on the individual's presenting need and clinical priority. It is important to note that there is currently no dedicated service or funding specifically for patients diagnosed with ASD. The provision of Nutrition and Dietetic services for individuals with an Autistic Diagnosis is not systematically recorded.		It is therefore difficult to accurately determine the amount of level of funding allocated for individuals diagnosed with ASD who access support for their nutrition and dietetic needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline any discussions he has had with UK Ministers on the impact that a ban on overseas care workers from bringing dependents will have on the provision of local health care services.
answer_text: In December 2023, the Home Secretary announced changes to the immigration system that are to be implemented from Spring 2024.  One of these measures is a ban on overseas care workers from bringing dependants.  The social care sector in Northern Ireland relies heavily on an overseas workforce, particularly in the private nursing and residential home sector. In addition, there are significant waiting lists and high levels of unmet need for social care provision because of the difficulties in recruiting and retaining staff in low wage posts.  Therefore, officials in this Department have been engaging with care providers and other Departmental officials here and across the UK to consider the impact that banning overseas care workers from bringing dependents will have in Northern Ireland.  Officials will continue to engage and monitor the impact that this will have on our services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 5806/22-27, for an estimate of the potential number of households which may be impacted by the two child benefit cap when the migration of Tax Credit claimants to Universal Credit is completed.
answer_text: In August 2023, 7,975 households receiving Universal Credit and 5,220 household claims on Tax Credits were affected by the policy. All households currently impacted may potentially continue to be impacted by the policy when the migration to Universal Credit is complete.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his plan to reduce pressures on hospital Emergency Departments, with particular regard to Altnagelvin Hospital.
answer_text: It is recognised that pressures on hospital Emergency Departments are symptomatic of broader pressures on the urgent and emergency care system and to address these delays, a wider-system approach is required. My Department has taken a number of steps to reduce pressures on hospital Emergency Departments under the umbrella of the Review of Urgent and Emergency Care.  This review set out 3 Strategic Priorities for the transformation of our urgent and emergency care system. They are interdependent and all are essential for the delivery of an improved urgent and emergency care system:Creating and integrated Urgent and Emergency Care ServiceCapacity, Co-ordination and Performance ManagementIntermediate CareThe key actions associated with the Urgent and Emergency Care Strategic Priority One – known as the No More Silos programme are: -	Introduction of Urgent Care Centres-	Implementation of Rapid Access Clinics-	Introduction of Telephone Clinical Triage – A Regional Phone First ServiceThe NMS Programme has planned and commissioned a number of services with the Western Trust to support the key actions above, to support keeping Emergency Departments for emergencies only, by moving urgent demand away from EDs and scheduling unscheduled care to these urgent care services via Phone First & GP referrals.Western Trust investments include;1. Altnagelvin Hospital – Establishment of an Enhanced Minor Injuries Unit which will have capacity to see 17,000 patients per year2. Altnagelvin Hospital – Establishment of Respiratory Rapid Access Services 3. Altnagelvin Hospital – Expansion of Ambulatory Care Unit Rapid Access Services 4. South West Acute Hospital – Establishment of Same Day Emergency Care Rapid Access Services

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what action his Department has taken under the Empty Homes Strategy to engage with banks regarding an increase in property repossessions.
answer_text: The Empty Homes Strategy and Action Plan was operational from 2013 to 2018 with a review of the Strategy completed and published on my Department’s website in 2021. The Review can be accessed at https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-empty-homes-strategy-and-action-plan-2013-%E2%80%93-2018Action 6 of this Strategy was to meet with lenders where repossessed property is empty and develop protocols. Responsibility for taking this action forward sat with the NIHE. The review noted that early investigations into this work meant this action could be discounted as lenders were actively trying to sell properties in their possession to minimise potential loss.  My Department consulted on a new Housing Supply Strategy in 2021/22. It is intended that this Strategy will continue work to make the best use of existing assets and include consideration of actions around housing led regeneration and adaptive reuse of existing buildings to generate additional supply. I am currently reviewing this Strategy and considering options for progressing it.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for his assessment of the efficacy of the existing Empty Home Strategy and Action Plan; and (ii) when this strategy will be updated.
answer_text: The Empty Homes Strategy and Action Plan was operational from 2013 to 2018. A review of the Strategy was completed and published on my Department’s website in 2021. The Review can be accessed at https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-empty-homes-strategy-and-action-plan-2013-%E2%80%93-2018While the Review recognised that the Strategy had limited success in addressing housing stress, as a result of this work we are now much better informed about the complexities of this issue and the reasons why so many properties appear to be vacant.My Department consulted on a new Housing Supply Strategy in 2021/22. It is intended that this Strategy will continue work to make the best use of existing assets and include consideration of actions around housing led regeneration and use of existing buildings to generate additional supply. I am currently reviewing this Strategy and considering options for progressing it.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of psychiatrists employed in each Health and Social Care Trust, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Information provided by the Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts on the number of consultant psychiatrists employed at 31 December in each of the last five years is detailed in the table below.Consultant Psychiatrists Employed, by HSC TrustDateNumber / WTEHSC TrustBelfastNorthernSouth EasternSouthernWestern31-Dec-19Number  7339203019WTE61.0935.618.2525.917.1731-Dec-20Number  6839223519WTE57.2535.6419.9530.9116.5131-Dec-21Number  7340253120WTE62.1636.0921.4728.1418.0731-Dec-22Number  6943273018WTE58.6337.7924.0326.8316.1531-Dec-23Number  7644302817WTE63.9139.2626.2525.6115.66

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail his plan to improve patient access to GPs; and (ii) whether he will implement a GP indemnity scheme.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of access to GP services for patients.  GP services have been experiencing sustained, high levels of demand since before the COVID-19 pandemic.  Practices have therefore continued to adapt how services are delivered in response to increasing pressures. In response to increasing patient demand GPs are utilising a mixed consultation model with telephone consultations, video and face to face consultations as a means of managing the rising demand on services within the existing workforce while retaining responsiveness to those patients who need the most urgent attention and care. Additional measures have been put in place to support primary care to adapt flexibly to increasing demand.  These include focusing on increasing capacity to meet the demand from patients through, for example, reinforcing the GP workforce and increasing the range of healthcare professionals working in primary care, investment in general practice and the implementation of new and innovative ways of working, including making greater use of technology and telephony.  As part of supporting work in this area, my Department established a GP Access Working Group, which includes GP and service-user representation, to explore issues around accessing services and to oversee work to improve the experience for patients.  It is essential that we continue to use of all the tools at our disposal to ensure that we can help our services to work as effectively as possible to meet the need for access to primary care support and treatment.  Turning to GP indemnity, I am aware that arrangements for clinical negligence indemnity are a matter of concern for GPs locally and I am committed to identifying solutions to address this issue for Northern Ireland GPs.  Recognising this, my Department recently reallocated funding from other areas of the GP budget to boost capacity in the service and mitigate demand and capacity pressures; this funding included support for additional costs of indemnity to enhance the capacity of the system to manage demand and was on a non recurrent basis. My Department is continuing to take forward work to look at the future model of GP indemnity provision in Northern Ireland. A number of possible options have been identified as part of this work, ranging from the current model to state intervention that includes a number of state backed model options. Departmental officials are continuing to take forward options appraisal through the business case process which will conclude with the identification of a proposed preferred way forward for indemnity arrangements in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for (i) an update of the feasibility study for Phase 3 of Derry to Coleraine rail line upgrade; and (ii) whether he has considered the inclusion of halts at (a) Strathfoyle; (b) City of Derry Airport; and (c) Ballykelly.
answer_text: (i) My Department approved the business case for Phase 3 of the Derry to Coleraine project in November 2022 and issued a letter of offer for £97.7m to take forward this important project for the Northwest. Translink are progressing the project and subject to the availability of funding, construction is scheduled to commence in 2026.(ii) A separate feasibility study is examining the introduction of a half hourly service between Belfast and Derry which will consider the possibility of additional rail halts on the Coleraine to Derry line. This study is currently ongoing and subject to the availability of funding, is planned to be completed later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients awaiting a domiciliary care package, in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Information on the number of patients awaiting a domiciliary care package has been provided by each Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust for the position on 19th February 2024 and is detailed in the Table 1 overleaf.Table 1: Number of patients in each Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust who are awaiting a domiciliary care package, for the position on 19th February 2024 HSC TrustNumber Waiting in Hospital SettingNumber Waiting in Community SettingBelfast 20793Northern51,307South Eastern26296Southern40625Western26657Northern Ireland1173,678 Source: Health and Social Care TrustsDomiciliary care services continue to experience significant pressures across the region, and there are a range of factors currently impacting upon delivery of this service; this includes significant retention issues in regard to domiciliary care staff.Where delivery of care packages has been negatively impacted due to challenges regarding capacity, Trusts have in place mitigation measures to try to ensure services continue to be provided.My Department remains committed to the provision of a high quality domiciliary care service, and will continue to work alongside Trusts and independent sector providers to maintain and support service provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on work to progress the Anti-Poverty Strategy.
answer_text: I am committed to delivering long-term, sustainable solutions to poverty in all its forms for our communities right across Northern Ireland.I am currently considering the next steps to be taken in relation to an Executive Anti-Poverty Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 5525/22-27, whether the redevelopment of the Ryan McBride Brandywell Stadium will be included in final proposals.
answer_text: My officials have been working with a range of stakeholders to develop proposals. I have asked for these to be presented to me as a matter of priority. I expect funding to help modernise performance club grounds to be disbursed through a fair, transparent and open competition process. All successful applicants will be expected to demonstrate need.The working definition of performance clubs is all clubs with senior/ top level men’s and women’s teams whose home ground is located in Northern Ireland. Derry City, Derry City FC Women and Institute FC, who all play at the Ryan McBride Brandywell Stadium, would meet the department’s definition of performance clubs. Once final proposals are confirmed and approvals are secured, I will be able to provide a timeframe for the process to apply for funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many new social houses have been built in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The information you have requested regarding new social home completions over the next five years, is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. You should, therefore, direct your question to Northern Ireland Housing Executive at  Public.Affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) how many households are currently experiencing rate arrears; and (ii) how many businesses are in rate arrears, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: As at 11th February 2024, 39,360 domestic and 10,937 non-domestic occupancies have a current balance greater than £0 and also have rate arrears remaining from previous rating years.  The table below illustrates these values by District Council area.District CouncilDomesticNon-DomesticAntrim and Newtownabbey2,050541 Ards and North Down2,826 726 Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon4,622 1,072 Belfast8,257 3,321 Causeway Coast and Glens2,755 717 Derry City and Strabane3,654 944 Fermanagh and Omagh2,807 641 Lisburn and Castlereagh2,201 672 Mid and East Antrim2,107 590 Mid Ulster3,251 745 Newry, Mourne and Down4,830 968 Total39,360 10,937

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) whether he will increase funding for new build social housing; and (ii) to detail his plan to increase the rates of social housing build.
answer_text: The Department has not yet received its capital budget allocation for 2024/25 and beyond. As in prior years, once capital budget allocations are known, projects will have to be prioritised across the Department.The Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) for the 3-year period 2024/25 to 2026/27 has recently been approved. Subject to appropriate budget being made available, the SHDP new build starts would increase each year. A target of 2,000 starts for 2024/25 has been set, increasing to 2,150 units for 2025/26 and 2,300 units for 2026/27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she intends to extend the Small Business Rate Relief scheme beyond this financial year.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery - Substance Use Strategy.
answer_text: I launched Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery in September 2021 as Northern Ireland's new 10-year Substance Use Strategy. As part of the delivery of the strategy’s outcomes, I tasked the Public Health Agency and the Strategic Planning & Performance Group to co-produce a new outcomes-focused strategic service delivery plan to replace the current Alcohol & Drug Services Commissioning Framework, covering all tiers of service provision. This new Substance Use Strategic Commissioning & Implementation Plan, which is currently being finalised after a public consultation, will: •	ensure that the population of NI have access to a continuum of service with clear pathways and step up/step down provision;•	ensure equal access to community detoxification for alcohol and other drugs across NI;•	ensure that all services are delivered in line with the UK-wide “Drug Misuse and Dependence: Guidelines on Clinical Management” and NICE Guidelines; •	provide support to address the wider physical, mental health, and wellbeing needs of those in treatment, including housing, education, employment, personal finance, healthcare e.g. should be supported to stop smoking and address other physical health conditions; and•	recognise the importance of co-production and strengthen joint working between the community/voluntary sector, service users and peers, and the Health & Social Care Sector.To date an additional £750,000 has been invested recurrently in the Strategy’s implementation. Two other priority pieces of work are also due to report shortly – a Population Needs-Based Assessment of Substance Use and related services in the Western Health & Social Care Trust area, and an Independent Review of Inpatient Tier 4 Detoxification & Residential Rehabilitation Services across Northern Ireland. The recommendations from both reports will help inform the final Strategic Commissioning & Implementation Plan, and it is the intention that all future funding decisions around substance use related services will be based on this new commissioning framework. The full delivery of the strategy will be dependent on additional funding being made available going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline his plans to reduce waiting times for occupational therapy services.
answer_text: The Occupational Therapy (OT) Service is doing all it can to deliver the shortest waiting time possible within the funded resources available.There has been previous investment in elective services, with approximately 7 (wte) additional OT staff funded through the allocation of transformational and winter pressures funding and Public Health Agency (PHA) funding for children with special needs. In addition, waiting list initiatives have been completed within children's services and funded overtime initiatives across services to improve the position.  This additional capacity does not however address the overall demand capacity gaps that exist within the service. The service strives to retain staff, but it is recognised there is however a national shortage of OTs.Within the limitations of the current available resources, the OT service is actively working to address waiting lists through the following measures:  an effective triage process in line with regional guidance, to give priority to those with most urgent need;pro-active waiting list management by implementation of a partial booking process to offer a choice of appointments in advance and reduce the numbers of appointments that are not attended;regular waiting list validation and review of individual circumstances should the service become aware of someone’s changing needs;dynamic use of all available resources including timely and efficient recruitment processes to maintain funded staffing levels, deployment of staff to areas of greatest need, and engagement of additional temporary staff, as available, to address service gaps; and,the provision of domiciliary assessment visits and clinic appointments as appropriate to the individual service user’s needs to ensure the most efficient use of service capacity.My Department will continue to work with the Public Health Agency to determine the requirement of OT support across the region. Any increase in staffing levels will be subject to budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will commit to a independent review into missed abnormal smear tests for each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: There are no plans to request an independent review into missed abnormal smear tests for each Health and Social Care Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what pathway of aftercare and support will be provided to patients impacted by missed abnormal cervical screening tests in the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: All cases of diagnosed cervical cancer are audited in line with the regional framework document and disclosure undertaken as appropriate to the audit outcome. Any case which is deemed to be a Serious Adverse Incident (SAI), is managed in accordance with Trust policy and protocols for all SAIs, to include patient involvement and provision of support.Disclosure meetings will be offered to the individual impacted and there will be a multi-disciplinary approach to who attends.  If the issues relate to laboratory/screening part of the pathway the Consultant Cytopathologist is usually the one to lead the meeting and a Consultant Colposcopist and Clinical nurse specialist nurse will be in attendance. The meetings are multidisciplinary to ensure that there are professionals available with the appropriate knowledge base to provide advice and guidance regarding next steps.It is important to note that no screening programme is 100% effective and false negative results are a recognised feature of any screening programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the provision of primary care vasectomy service.
answer_text: The Primary Care Elective Service model has historically been sustained through non-recurrent funding. I recognise the value of the Primary Care Elective Services but due to a significant non-recurrent funding gap and budgetary pressures in 2023/24 it has not been possible to sustain the primary care vasectomy service beyond September 2023.Despite a reduction in the primary care service, GPs are still able to refer patients to Secondary Care, however I recognise that hospital-based service waiting times are long and addressing lengthy waiting times is a priority.I remain committed to the delivery of GP Elective Services across Northern Ireland, and I am working with my Executive colleagues to secure the funding needed to tackle our length waiting lists.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail his plans to improve the provision of wheelchair accessible taxis.
answer_text: Under current taxi legislation there is no requirement for taxi operators to have a minimum number of wheelchair accessible vehicles, and the option to include these in their fleet is at the discretion of the operators and drivers.  Neither does my Department under current taxi legislation have the powers to deliver financial support for the purchase or operation of a Wheelchair Accessible Taxi.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will introduce regulations for non-surgical cosmetic procedures.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the wider, changing health and social care service delivery environment including in relation to cosmetic treatments. Prior to the COVID-19 pandemic, the Department had developed a new draft regulatory policy framework however further development work is required, including consultation on the draft policy.  The Department is currently operating within a constrained budget and is required to make decisions in relation to the work that can be delivered within current resources. In that context, work on the review of the regulation is currently paused to allow for other priority projects to progress. As such, there are no active plans to bring forward legislation in this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he will remove the rates cap for homes valued at over £400,000.
answer_text: ANSWERMy Department has just concluded a consultation on Non-domestic and Domestic rating measures including the rates cap for homes valued at over £400,000. I will be assessing the findings of the consultation, alongside a number of other strategic areas that I wish to consider within the rating system. It will be for the Executive as a whole to consider this issue in the full context of wider budgetary discussions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what plans he has to protect and support private renters impacted by rising rental costs.
answer_text: I am aware of an increase in rents in the private rented sector. Research carried out by my Department concluded that the best ways of relieving pressure on affordability for renters was by increasing housing supply, and ensuring the benefit system properly takes into account the cost of housing.One of my key priorities is to ensure that people have access to safe, warm and affordable homes. To help achieve this, I will continue to prioritise the building of new social homes and utilise new funding sources to support the delivery of intermediate housing, including a new intermediate rent product.  I am also currently reviewing a draft Housing Supply Strategy, aimed at unlocking supply across all tenures, and considering options on the way forward.In the shorter term, the increase in the Local Housing Allowance rate from April 2024 to the 30th percentile of local market rents, should go some way to assist many renters with affordability of rent costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will consider providing additional funding to sustain border region Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: The DOH’s budget and the challenging financial pressures and demands it faces are significant. However, the DOH is committed to equity of access for our whole population, wherever this is best delivered. This also requires all of our Trusts to be as productive as possible, ensuring maximum value for the funds invested.The commitment to equity has meant, in particular additional funding being skewed in recent years towards our Southern locality, however going forward given the financial outlook this may mean a redistribution of resources or changes in access and referrals, rather than significant additional funding to any particular Trust.All of our Trusts are reporting significant financial deficits which will require careful management within the Budget available to the DOH, this will include our ‘Border Region Health and Social Care Trusts’.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will consider the introduction of pregnancy loss certificates for parents who experience pregnancy loss before 24 weeks.
answer_text: My Department recognises that there is a need for further improvement in relation to services for women and their partners following pregnancy and baby loss. The introduction of a pre-24-week pregnancy loss certificate is a recommendation of the Independent Pregnancy Loss Review commissioned by the Westminster Government and relates to services in England.My officials will continue to engage with their counterparts in the Department of Health and Social Care in England as the system is developed there, with a view to understanding whether, and how, it might be possible to implement such a scheme in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will reopen the Happy Healthy Minds Therapeutic and Counselling Programme for both primary and post-primary school children.
answer_text: The Healthy Happy Minds Pilot concluded on 31 March 2023.  An evaluation of the pilot has been carried out and I will assess its findings as I seek to better support the emotional health and wellbeing of learners.  The Independent Counselling Service for Schools (ICSS) remains available to post-primary age pupils.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Deposit Return Scheme.
answer_text: DAERA committed back in February 2020 to Northern Ireland’s continued participation in the development of UK-wide proposals to reform the Packaging Producer Responsibility system and the introduction of a Deposit Return Scheme. My Department published a Government Response to the 2021 Joint consultation with England and Wales in January 2023 with 83% respondents in support of introducing a DRS. The Government Response proposed to include in the regulations a commencement date for DRS of 1st October 2025 as a stretching target date. To ensure due consideration is given to all parties involved, following publication of the Government response to the last formal consultation in January 2023, officials across the UK have been working together to ensure compatibility between different DRS’s throughout the UK. Alongside this work officials have been working with their counterparts in Ireland to prevent any unintended consequences in the two schemes and consider lessons they have learned in launching their scheme which went live on February 1st, 2024. I aim to share the outcome of this engagement in a joint policy statement with the other UK nations in the coming months.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will commission Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder services for adults in the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Although there are currently no commissioned services for Adult ADHD, provision has grown organically across Trusts in response to population demand and expertise within HSC teams. While this has ensured a level of provision is available, the Department is aware that this has also led to inconsistencies between Trusts including pathways and gaps between what can be provided by Trusts in terms of demand and the level of prevalence of adult ADHD, leading to individuals waiting longer than intended for assessment and support.The Health and Social Care Trusts are committed to work closely and collaboratively with commissioners to develop innovative, cost effective, co-produced, quality, and evidenced based services that can meet the needs of patients with ADHD within Trust areas. This work would include the development of clinical pathways.SPPG alongside Policy colleagues in the Department are keeping the situation regarding the commissioning of Adult ADHD services under review, recognising that the demand for ADHD diagnosis has risen in recent years.  In line with the usual process for commissioning of services, any decision to commission Adult ADHD services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and in the context of future budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will increase the number of Occupational Therapists in the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: My Department will continue to work with the Public Health Agency and to determine the requirement of Occupational Therapy support across the region including the Western Health & Social Care Trust. Any increase in staffing levels will be subject to budget availability which has yet to be confirmed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans he has to improve road safety to address the growing number of deaths on our roads.
answer_text: As Minister for Infrastructure part of my responsibility is promoting and improving road safety. This is a responsibility I take very seriously - every death or. serious injury is one too many.In October 2022 I published the draft Road Safety Strategy for Northern Ireland to 2030. The current draft Road Safety Strategy is supported by an action plan for 2023/24, containing 52 action measures. As this is a cross-cutting matter I aim to present an updated Road Safety Strategy and Action Plan to the Executive in the coming weeks for their consideration.I am mindful that the 71 deaths on our roads during 2023 was an eight-year high and very aware that more work needs to be done. My officials are currently working with other stakeholders including PSNI, Fire & Rescue Service, and the Ambulance Service to reduce deaths and serious injuries on our roads.For your ease of reference, the draft Road Safety Strategy and associated action plan can be found at the following webpagehttps://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/consultation-outcome-report-new-road-safety-strategy-northern-ireland-2030

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what engagement his Department has had with Ronald McDonald House Charities regarding the establishment of accommodation for sick children and their families who travel for hospital appointments.
answer_text: I am very aware that the provision of accommodation for parents and families with an acutely ill child in hospital is extremely important in the overall provision of inpatient hospital care for children.  Officials within this department are fully supportive of such arrangements and we have engaged with Trust colleagues to discuss their considerations on how best to facilitate this.It must be noted that responsibility for securing such arrangements resides with HSC Trusts.  In this regard they must be satisfied that all governance and other safety and quality requirements are fully explored and comply with Trust standards as appropriate.Use of Ronald McDonald House Charity (RMDHC) is a well-established arrangement across the wider NHS and we, in principle, are supportive of an approach to fully explore the potential of this arrangement in Northern Ireland, subject to Trust governance responsibilities as outlined above.Colleagues in the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) of the DOH have had initial discussions with colleagues in the HSC Trusts to understand the current position and to seek to support the Trust in resolving constraints with uptake of the Ronald McDonald House Charity if appropriate.In the event that issues cannot be resolved, SPPG will ask the Trust to undertake a review of current arrangements and their adequacy, together with proposals for improvement, if appropriate.We will ensure to keep you informed of developments on this proposal.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on his Department's engagement with the Irish government in relation to the All-Island Strategic Rail Review.
answer_text: My officials have been engaging closely with their colleagues in the Department of Transport to progress the All-Island Strategic Rail Review (AISRR) since the Review was announced in 2021. The draft AISRR report was released for the purposes of Strategic Environmental Assessment from 25 July to 29 September 2023 and recent engagement has focussed on analysing the responses to the consultation and working to finalise the draft Report. This engagement will continue at Ministerial and official level  over the coming months as both Departments work to address the issues raised through the consultation and present the final report for agreement by the governments in both jurisdictions. Once the Review is published, joint efforts will turn to prioritising the recommendations within the Review, developing an implementation plan, and looking to explore avenues of funding to progress the interventions. This close engagement between the two departments has been an excellent example of joint working on cross-border priorities and will be key to the successful delivery of the AISRR and realising the many benefits it can deliver for our communities, our people, and our economies right across the island.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for (i) an update on the progress of the Meenan Square redevelopment, Derry; and (ii) an estimated date for commencement works on site.
answer_text: The project, delivered by The Executive Office’s Urban Villages Initiative in conjunction with Apex Housing Association and Meenan Square Developments Ltd, will see the construction of a mixed-use development comprising housing, community hub, and essential local amenities.Apex Housing Association, funded by The Executive Office, purchased the majority of the site from the private sector landowner Westco Developments in March 2023.Funding for the wider capital development is being provided primarily by The Executive Office, supported by Apex Housing Association and the Department for Communities. The project is now subject to a formal planning application, including public consultation giving the local community an opportunity to share their views on the planning designs.Work is anticipated to commence on site by Spring 2025, subject to planning approval. The Executive Office remains absolutely committed to this important transformational project and will work with all statutory and community partners to deliver this exciting project for the community.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when his Department will bring forward welfare legislation to (i) scrap the two child benefit limit; and (ii) address the 5-week wait for Universal Credit.
answer_text: There are currently no plans to bring forward legislation to change entitlement to Universal Credit for people who are affected by the two child benefit limit or to amend the period after which the initial payment of Universal Credit is made. People who make a new claim to Universal Credit may be eligible for additional financial support through the Universal Credit Contingency Fund. This is in a non-repayable grant that is only available in Northern Ireland. Information about Universal Credit and the support available can be found at: https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/campaigns/universal-creditIn addition, when a claimant moves from a legacy benefit to Universal Credit they may continue to receive the legacy benefit for a further two weeks after making their Universal Credit claim, subject to eligibility. This payment is also non-repayable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his next steps to implement a ban on conversion therapy.
answer_text: I will be engaging with my officials over the coming weeks to understand work completed to date and to consider the next steps to be taken regarding a ban on conversion therapy practices in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on his Department's work to extend the benefit cap mitigations.
answer_text: Legislation approved by the Assembly in 2022 ensures that all families with children who are receiving Housing Benefit are entitled to a Welfare Supplementary Payment to mitigate the impact of the Benefit Cap. Equivalent payments are currently provided to eligible families who are receiving Universal Credit. These payments will continue until 31 March 2025.My Department has a statutory obligation to produce a report on the operation of the ongoing mitigation schemes by the 31 March 2025.  This report will include proposals for the future delivery of the welfare mitigation schemes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will engage with the Department of Health to retain an automatic entitlement to assistance with healthcare costs for Universal Credit claimants.
answer_text: My officials are in regular contact with the relevant Department of Health (DoH) officials on the changes required to the Help with Health Costs Scheme for Universal Credit claimants.  In the meantime, Universal Credit claimants should continue to apply for Help with Health Costs by completing a HC1 application form and forwarding it to the Department for Communities.  My Department will issue a health service certificate on behalf of DoH which will enable eligible Universal Credit

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many housing association properties have been sold under the House Sales Scheme for each year between 2012 and 2022, broken down by Council area.
answer_text: Unfortunately, this information is not available in the format requested, details are not recorded by Council area. Please see figures below listing the number of Housing Association properties sold under the House Sales Scheme for the requested years.YEARHOUSE SALES BY HOUSING ASSOCIATIONS2012/2013222013/2014302014/2015332015/2016302016/2017412017/2018642018/2019442019/2020972020/2021892021/20221602022/2023Not yet available

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide an update on the investigation into why WhatsApp messages relevant to the Covid-19 Inquiry were deleted from Ministers' phones.

To ask the Minister of Health (i) what provision is in place for the post-operative monitoring of patients who access bariatric surgery outside the NHS; and (ii) for an update on the Regional Obesity Management Service.
answer_text: Regarding provision of post operative monitoring of patients who access bariatric surgery outside of the NHS, the Department’s extant policy is that, where an individual avails of health care in a private capacity, it is important for them to ensure that all pre and post operative treatment costs are included in the overall package of care procured from the private healthcare provider. In any such instances, an individual will not be entitled to related pre or post treatment support from primary or secondary care services in Northern Ireland. Individuals should therefore discuss the full pre and post operative support available with the private healthcare provider, to understand what packages would be available. In 2019 the Department of Health began work to develop a prototype Regional Obesity Management Service (ROMS). While this had to be paused due to Covid-19 pressures, I am pleased to advise that development work was restarted in September 2023, and on 24 November 2023 the Department launched a public consultation to seek opinion on a proposed way forward for specialist obesity management services in Northern Ireland.  The proposed ROMS would provide a means for people living with obesity in NI to access treatment and specialist support that is not currently available to them, including weight loss medication and/or weight loss (bariatric) surgery, in line with clinical assessment of need.  The consultation, which will close at 5pm on 1 March 2024, is available on the Department’s website at https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/consultations/proposed-regional-obesity-management-service-roms-northern-ireland.  While this is an important first step in the development of this new service, it is important to note that final decisions would be subject to Ministerial approval and relevant funding being available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for a date for the resumption of the stocking programme for the Public Angling Estate (PAE); and (ii) whether he will consider reducing the permit fee for anglers until stocking resumes at all 27 PAE fisheries.
answer_text: I cannot provide a date for the resumption of stocking at this stage due to the continued presence of Zebra Mussels at Movanagher Fish Farm. My Department is working with the Agri-Foods and Biosciences Institute (AFBI) to put in place a project to develop and assess Zebra Mussel treatment protocols for stocking that will comply with the Wildlife (Northern Ireland) Order 1985. I have also no plans to reduce permit fees as purchasing a PAE permit does not guarantee any level of stocking within the PAE as it only provides the anglers with the permission to fish in those waters. In addition, only 27 of the Department’s 89 fisheries are stocked so a wide range of fishing opportunities still exist for PAE permit holders. Information on the Zebra Mussels issue and its impacts on the stocking of the PAE are available on the DAERA website: https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/news/zebra-mussels-and-impact-fish-stocking-public-angling-estate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will seek the support of the Executive to safeguard the future of Derry City Airport.
answer_text: As Minister for Infrastructure, my powers relating to NI’s three main airports are limited to the Airports (NI) Order 1994.  These are powers to control noise, control land in the interests of the safe and efficient use of airports, make byelaws, provide for airport constabularies and airport consultative committees and to give grants to assist capital expenditure.I remain committed to working with Executive colleagues to listen to the case brought forward by Derry City and Strabane District Council for support for City of Derry Airport, and to working together with the rest of the Executive to identify ways to support our airports.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what plans his Department has to (i) provide financial assistance to local councils; and (ii) increase the Rates Support Grant for less well-off local council areas.
answer_text: I will consider the funding for councils, including Rates Support Grant, in the context of my Department’s overall budget priorities, if any further funding becomes available for the current financial year.My Department’s budget for 2024/25 has not been agreed yet by the Executive and I am therefore unable to confirm any funding at this stage for councils for the next financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on (i) the Equality Impact Assessment into cuts to Discretionary Housing Payments; and (ii) whether he will consider reinstating the provision for tenants whose payments were stopped in 2023.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has advised that its Equality Impact Assessment (EQIA) in respect of its decision taken in August 2023 to reintroduce a maximum payment period of two years for Discretionary Housing Payments (DHP) is ongoing.Currently there are no plans within the Housing Executive or the Department to remove the maximum payment period. The reinstated two-year maximum payment period for DHPs remains a necessary mechanism to manage the limited DHP budget and ensure it remains available to as many of those who need it.Any claimant whose DHP was stopped has a right to appeal this decision. Whilst it is the case that anyone whose DHP was stopped this year is unable to reapply in the same financial year they can apply to the scheme from the beginning of the next financial year, and their case will be assessed by the Housing Executive on its own merits.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an estimate of the total number of households which will be impacted by the two child benefit cap.
answer_text: In August 2023, there were 7,975 households receiving Universal Credit impacted by the two-child policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps he will take to restore urgently bed capacity at Northern Ireland Children's Hospice.
answer_text: I recognise and applaud the vital work of the NICH and all hospices in Northern Ireland.As requested by me, DoH officials have engaged in detailed discussions last week with NI Hospice.It is clear to me that the financial pressures facing NICH go much deeper than any reduction of Departmental support.  This reduction involved the ending last summer of an additional but temporary annual payment of £170k. This decision was notified to the NICH in June 2023 and took effect from September 2023 which resulted in £85K reduction in this financial year.  I took the decision on Friday 09/02/2022 to reinstate that funding in full for this year.This payment is in addition to the core annual funding of £1.6m which my Department is providing directly to NICH this year. This core funding included a £420,000 component which had not been recurrent, meaning it would have to be subject to confirmation each year. I can confirm that, going forward, this will be made recurrent, giving the Hospice greater certainty.I am keenly aware that the financial challenges facing NICH go much deeper than this or next year. Like all health care providers, it has encountered significantly rising running costs, reflecting inflationary pressures.Regrettably, the NICH has advised my Department that the restoration of the full £170k for this year will not lead to the reversal of the service reduction made public this week.The NICH and NI Hospice parent body will need to develop longer-term solutions, to which I am sure Government will want to be supportive.Given the current absence of a budget for next year, and the well-documented financial uncertainties and challenges for Government, my Department is at the limit of what it can do right now.I am also very conscious that other hospice organisations, and many other health and care providers, are also grappling with growing budget pressures and will be seeking further statutory finance.I am grateful for the support and personal interest of the First Minister and deputy First Minister regarding NICH’s position. I am also aware of the fact that other NI Departments have provided financial support to NICH in the past.I believe the long-term need for financial stability across the hospice sector would benefit from cross-Departmental input, helping to assist organisations in identifying sustainable ways forward.Consequently, I wrote on Friday 09/02/2002 to the Finance Minister to seek further discussions between the charities and our two Departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans his Department has to address issues within dentistry in order to improve access for NHS patients.
answer_text: A key part of the access issues facing patients are the rates being paid to our dentists for treatment.  The absence of Executive Ministers has meant that it has not been possible to progress the recommended pay uplifts for staffing groups for 2023/24.  This is now my immediate priority, and I am meeting with Trade Unions in early course to take this forward. Officials have already been making arrangements in anticipation of funding being made available to ensure that uplifts can be implemented as quickly as possible.I know that an additional £95m has been invested in General Dental Services via the Financial Support Scheme and Rebuilding Support Scheme since April 2020. This came to an end in July 2023 due to the severe financial pressures arising from the Department’s 2023/24 budget settlement.A pilot scheme is currently running to provide appointments to unregistered patients who require emergency dental treatment during the working week. If a patient is not registered with a dentist, requires emergency dental treatment, and has been unable to access an appointment, they can contact their local Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) office for advice.I am also conscious of the progress that has been made in the rest of the UK on dental reform and I will be discussing these issues with officials as soon as possible, to ensure that the sector here in NI is supported in delivering important care to patients. More immediately, officials have been developing investment proposals to further support General Dental Services and I will consider them in early course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average waiting times for (i) hip replacement surgery; (ii) knee replacement surgery; and (iii) shoulder surgery, in each Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients in each Health and Social Care Trust awaiting a first consultant-led appointment for (i) hip replacement surgery; (ii) knee replacement surgery; and (iii) shoulder surgery.
answer_text: Reason for referral, or confirmed diagnosis, is not collected for patients waiting for a first consultant-led outpatient appointment. Therefore, it is not possible to separately identify patients waiting for a first appointment who need a hip replacement, knee replacement or shoulder surgery within the Trauma & Orthopedic specialty.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will introduce legal requirements for social housing landlords to address damp and mould within their properties.
answer_text: The requirement for homes to be free of dampness prejudicial to the health of occupants already forms part of the Housing Fitness Standard as detailed in Article 46 of the Housing (Northern Ireland) Order 1981.My officials are also in the process of initiating a review of the current Decent Homes Standard which is an administrative standard applicable to social housing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline plans to protect vulnerable families and individuals currently in emergency accommodation placements.
answer_text: Families and individuals currently in emergency accommodation is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  You should therefore direct your question to the Housing Executive’s dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the costs of Home Starter Pack provision for (i) the Northern Ireland Housing Executive; and (ii) Housing Associations, for each of the last five years.
answer_text: Home Starter Pack provision is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  You should therefore direct your question to the Housing Executive’s dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide (i) an update on Phase 2 of the A6 project and (ii) a timeframe for completion.
answer_text: Major Road schemes are an important part of the work that is delivered by my Department as we seek to reduce journey times, increase reliability and improve road safety.Part of the road alignment for Phase 2 of the A6 encroaches on the Mobuoy waste site. Once plans for remediation of that site are in place, a delivery strategy for Phase 2 including timelines will be finalised.Details of the current status of Phase 2 of the A6 project will continue to be available on the project webpage which can be viewed at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/a6-londonderry-dungiven-overview

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when funding allocated for the Sub-Regional Stadia Programme will be released.
answer_text: I recognise and share the deep frustration of many that this programme has not been delivered. Work is progressing to develop proposals for the Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for Football and I have asked my officials to present me with proposals and options for consideration as a matter of priority. Once final proposals are confirmed and approvals are secured, I will be able to provide a timeframe for the process to apply for funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the commitment to review the Hospital Travel Costs Scheme and provision for parents with sick children.
answer_text: Preparatory work has recently commenced in advance of updating the Travelling Expenses and Remission of Charges Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2004.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he intends to reopen the Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme.
answer_text: Reducing waiting times for elective access is a key priority for me. Current waiting times are unacceptable, no one should have to wait longer than necessary for the surgery they need.  While good progress has been made in tackling waiting times in some areas, there remains much more to be done. Within the constraints of existing budgets, I’m committed to taking whatever actions I can to tackle these waiting times and ensure that patients are treated as quickly as possible to ensure best possible outcomes.As you will be aware, I introduced the Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme (RoIRS) on 30 June 2021 as part of the Elective Care Framework to help reduce NI waiting lists. The scheme was a limited 12-month administrative version of the Cross-Border Healthcare Directive (CBHD) which ceased to apply to NI following the UK’s exit from the EU. In June 2022, I announced that the RoIRS would be extended beyond the planned 30 June 2022 deadline, until an additional £5m of new funding had been committed. The RoIRS closed to new applications on Wednesday 21 September 2022 when the additional £5m funding had been exhausted. Given the pressing demands for funding across many areas of health and social care no further funding could be assigned to this scheme.Any future funding allocation to a similar scheme to help reduce waiting lists will now need to be considered as part of a broader review of resource investment in Waiting List Initiatives.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide an update on the work to progress the signalisation of Caw Roundabout in the Foyle constituency.
