question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the current level of access to learning disability day centres and respite services.
answer_text: In January 2022, I approved the Pathway to remobilise services for adult social care. In the following months, my officials worked with the Public Health Agency to adjust public health guidance and infection controls to better enable Trusts to restore adult day services and short breaks to pre-pandemic capacity. Trusts Trusts have been provided a framework to dynamically manage Covid-19 risk while providing day services. The PHA have clearly stated that infection prevention and control guidance should not be considered an absolute barrier to individuals accessing appropriate health and social care. As of May 2022, the Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) receive fortnightly updates from each Trust on progress towards the full remobilisation of adult day care, respite/short breaks and transport. Data across all relevant Programmes of Care is collated by SPPG to assist with the development of a regional consistent approach. Data is baselined against service provision in February 2020, which is defined as pre-pandemic capacity. Data across all Programmes of Care is analysed with the data for Learning Disability currently being published monthly on the Department of Health website as noted below:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/remobilisation-learning-disability-servicesThere is a continued focus within Trusts to remobilise day care services, however there is a cohort of service users still not receiving adult day care or receiving a partial service.  It is regretable that services have not yet been fully restored to pre pandemic levels and I sincerely apologise to those individuals, families and carers. Service remobilisation will continue to be an area of focus.A percentage of service users and carers have chosen not to return to day care for a variety of reasons. Some families/service users have found alternative arrangements via direct payments and community-based day opportunities. Trusts continue to engage with those  service users who have chosen not to do this to ensure their needs continue to be met through alternative service pathways, such as day opportunities whilst advising that the option of day care remains available to them. Aligned to this process, SPPG engage in performance meetings with Trusts to discuss any noted barriers to full day care, respite/short break service remobilisation and to agree mitigating actions to support continued progress towards full remobilisation. In addition, my officials are engaging with both All Party Groups for Carers and Learning Disability to provide clear updates on progress and outstanding challenges.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the work to introduce a deposit return scheme.
answer_text: My Officials have been working closely with Defra and Welsh Government colleagues to develop options for a Deposit Return Scheme (DRS). I launched a second public consultation on options for the DRS in Northern Ireland on 24 March 2021, which closed on 04 June 2021. 		The responses to the DRS consultation have now been analysed and a government response will be published in due course.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of neonatal deaths within the last three years; and (ii) of these, how many bereaved parents received a follow up visit with a consultant within 4-6 weeks of their loss, for each Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of the approval of Planning Application LA11/2022/0144/RM for 252 social housing units at Ballyoan, what steps are being taken by his Department to implement the required improvements at Caw Roundabout and Gransha Roundabout.
answer_text: The conditions attached to planning permission for housing and other development in this area require developers to implement signalisation and upgrade works to both the Caw and Gransha Roundabouts. Upgrade works to Gransha Roundabout, conditioned as part of the planning permission, are to be delivered by the developer. Separately, my Department has also been considering the upgrade of the Caw Roundabout given the existing safety and traffic progression issues. My officials continue to liaise with developers to explore options to jointly fund the works and have recently engaged a team of engineering consultants to further develop the design of the scheme and to better quantify the associated construction costs. This work will inform the appropriate apportionment of costs between my Department and the developer.Should you have any further questions on this please contact DfI Roads Western Division at dfiroads.western@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has considered the establishment of a specialist inpatient Mother and Baby Unit in Northern Ireland for women suffering from mental illness and their babies.
answer_text: Implementation of specialist perinatal mental health services is currently being progressed on a phased basis, with phase one focusing on the roll out of community perinatal mental health teams. Both the Belfast Trust and the South Eastern Trust are operational and offering a full consultancy service while in the remaining Trusts, recruitment is advancing with the aim of commencing full service delivery as soon as possible.  In parallel, preliminary scoping work has also commenced on the establishment of a Mother and Baby Unit for the region, including engagement with Health and Social Care Trusts.The Mental Health Strategy 2021-31 sets out a clear commitment to continue the rollout of perinatal mental health services and the Year One Delivery Plan, which was published on my Department’s website on 18 July 2022, sets out a summary of priority activities for 2022/23.  In relation to perinatal mental health, this includes progressing scoping work for a Mother and Baby Unit and completion of a Strategic Outline Case by 31 March 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 3982/22-27, why statistics on breast cancer diagnosis are not available for 2020, 2021 or 2022.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Cancer Registry (NICR) is responsible for the production of annual Official Statistics on cancer incidence, prevalence and survival in Northern Ireland. The NICR have advised that cancer registrations are usually completed within two years of diagnosis but, in a small proportion of cases, the process can take longer.  Cancer registration involves rigorous data validation processes undertaken by a team of experienced cancer intelligence officers and collation and linkage of data from a large number of sources.Due to the delays caused by the COVID-19 pandemic and additional work undertaken by NICR to show COVID-19 impact on cancer diagnosis this year they have moved to a site by site basis for 2020 data release. Breast data for 2020 will be available in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the HGV survey planned for the B118 Woodvale Road, Eglinton.
answer_text: I can confirm that a survey is planned for the B118 Woodvale Road / Tamnaherin Road once the current restrictions associated with the construction of the dual carriageway between Dungiven and Drumahoe are removed and the new dual carriageway becomes operational. This will allow traffic patterns to become used to the new road layout and will more accurately reflect the position going forward.Should you have any further questions on this please contact DfI Roads Western Division at dfiroads.western@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the installation of A6 signage will be completed.
answer_text: Signage on the A6 Drumahoe to Dungiven project is currently programmed to be installed by the end of next month.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the current aftercare support provision in Northern Ireland for parents who experience stillbirth or neonatal death.
answer_text: A wide range of support services for bereaved parents are available across each Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust and delivered in partnership between clinicians, bereavement coordinators and the community and voluntary sector. In cases of pregnancy loss, stillbirth or early neonatal death, all Trusts in Northern Ireland have a bereavement midwife who provides support and guidance for bereaved women, partners and their families and ensures that parents receive care that is clinically skilled, emotionally intelligent, consistent and authentically caring.  The midwife also works to ensure that all staff within the relevant health settings are educated, supported and enabled to provide sensitive, safe, effective, compassionate, kind and respectful care for families who experience such a loss. Each Trust has also had a Trust Bereavement Coordinator in place since 2006, when the HSC Bereavement Network was established to provide information, guidance and resources for those who care for and support people around the time of death and bereavement, as well as a Bereavement Forum which meets regularly with a wide membership comprising staff and service users. The HSC Bereavement Network was expanded in April 2021 to become the Northern Ireland Bereavement Network, comprising representation from across Departmental and community organisations and agencies, including two representatives from each HSC Trust, as well as third sector organisations such as SANDS, Cruse, NI Children’s Hospice and NICVA.  While the new Network’s website is under development, information on bereavement support continues to be available on the HSC  Bereavement Network website at: https://hscbereavementnetwork.hscni.net/ and on individual Trust websites, which provide guidance and support on a range of issues and contact information for a number of organisations which specialise in supporting bereaved parents.The vast majority of parents who experience stillbirth or neonatal death will experience normal grief and adjustment difficulties.  They are most appropriately helped by psychological input provided by primary care services, or counselling and other supports located in the community and voluntary sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline what steps her Department has taken to identify Universal Credit claimants who receive Carer's Allowance but have not had the carer element applied to their award.
answer_text: In May 2023, the Universal Credit system was updated to prompt claimants who indicated they were in receipt of Carer’s Allowance to report that they were caring for a person for over 35 hours per week. My Department completed an exercise to identify claimants in receipt of Carer’s Allowance who had not reported caring responsibilities prior to May 2023 to ensure they were claiming their full entitlement. This resulted in further payments totalling more than £1.2m being issued to claimants.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the impact that the absence of an Executive is having on the delivery of the New Decade, New Approach commitment to provide three funded IVF cycles treatment.

To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline how the £400 Energy Bills Support Scheme will be issued to energy customers in Northern Ireland; and when this information will be communicated to all MLAs.
answer_text: The UK Government (UKG) announced the £400 Energy Bills Support Scheme following a statement delivered on 26 May 2022 by the Chancellor in the House of Commons in which he said, “we will legislate to deliver this support on the same terms in every part of the United Kingdom, including Northern Ireland”.		UKG’s current plan, which is not yet finalised, is for the payment of £400 to be provided by electricity suppliers directly to domestic electricity consumers in Northern Ireland and the Prime Minister has suggested that it will be available in November.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of individuals in receipt of Carer's Allowance, for each of the last five years.
answer_text: The information requested is included in the table below:Date Figures Published Carer’s Allowance RecipientsNovember 201948,500November 202049,870November 202148,600November 202248,320November 202348,270Please note that figures are rounded to the nearest ten and that the totals may not sum due to rounding.More information on Carer’s Allowance statistics and can be found using the link below:	https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/articles/benefits-statistics-summary

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what steps she has taken to (i) increase the rate of the Carer's Allowance; and (ii) secure a Cost of Living Payment for claimants in receipt of Carer's Allowance.
answer_text: As part of the annual benefits up-rating exercise the weekly rate of Carer’s Allowance increased by 6.7% to £81.90 in April 2024.  The UK government has not announced any plans to extend the Cost of Living Payments scheme.  The previous additional support packages, which were paid in 2022/23 and 2023/24 financial years did not include a specific payment for recipients of Carer’s Allowance.  However, depending on individual circumstances carers in receipt of certain income-related or disability benefits may have qualified for an appropriate Cost of Living Payment at the time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide an update on the Reform of Adult Social Care.
answer_text: The Department of Health has recently concluded a public consultation on its proposals to Reform Adult Social Care in Northern Ireland.  The consultation which was launched on 26th January 2022 ended on 01st July 2022.Now that the consultation has ended the Department is undertaking a full evaluation and analysis of consultation responses which will inform a summary and evaluation report.  When finalised, the Department will publish a post consultation report and evaluation detailing how it will take forward the strategic reforms of Adult Social Care.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide a breakdown of the estimated amount of unclaimed means tested benefits, for each of last three years.
answer_text: The latest iteration of the Pension Credit: Estimates of Benefit Take-Up report, providing estimates for the 2019/20 financial year, was released on 01 September 2022 and can be found at: Pension Credit: Estimates of Benefit Take-up - 2019/20 | Department for Communities (communities-ni.gov.uk).(https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/pension-credit-estimates-benefit-take-201920)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether plans by the UK government to review anti-obesity legislation will impact on revenue raised from the Soft Drinks Industry Levy.
answer_text: Revenue raised from the Soft Drinks Industry Levy is a matter for HMRC.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide an update on a renewed Oral Health Strategy.
answer_text: Prior to the Covid 19 pandemic my Department decided that the two sections of the Northern Ireland population that would benefit most from updated oral disease prevention programs where children and the older population. Unfortunately, the pandemic delayed the establishment of the two planned working groups, but significant progress has been made by two oral health groups - one dedicated to improving the oral health of children and the other specifically looking at improving the oral health of older people.The challenges facing the HSC and DoH today mean that, unfortunately, the significant resources required to develop a new wide-ranging OHS are not available at this time. However, the importance of analysing the oral health status of the population and recommending evidence-based approaches to health improvement is recognised. In addition, my Department has funded a dental health survey of five year old children this financial year, and a survey of twelve year old children is planned for 2022/23.  These surveys will provide key information for future planning of oral health improvement in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what policy is in place in Emergency Departments to reduce the risk of infection for immunocompromised individuals.
answer_text: All Health and Social care settings in Northern Ireland are required to implement policies and procedures to prevent, control and mitigate the risk of transmission of infection.  As part of this, a clinical risk assessment is required for those individuals considered to be at high risk and appropriate action taken.  The risk of transmitting infection can be minimised through robust compliance with and continued adherence to all appropriate policies and measures in place - including but not limited to the appropriate use of Personal Protective Equipment (PPE), good hand hygiene, and other robust infection prevention and control measures.The “Pathway to Enhanced Visiting” document published in May 2021 set out the approach to limiting footfall in all healthcare settings.  This approach has been a key strategic component of managing the pandemic and was introduced to protect patients, their families, and staff by reducing the risk of infection transmission.All healthcare environments can be a source of virus spread, including among healthcare workers, patients, and visitors.  In terms of access to Emergency Departments, the risk of spread of infection in such facilities can be mitigated by:Normally allowing one person only to accompany each patient; Recommending the use of:appropriate personal protective equipment (PPE), good hand hygiene; and good respiratory hygiene Maintaining physical distancing.While most pandemic related restrictions have been lifted across society, the risk of transmission of the virus remains, and those in hospital are especially vulnerable so a degree of risk-assessed management remains appropriate. Guidance for testing in relation to COVID-19 is in place to support clinical pathways across a range of hospital settings. Guidance stipulates that all patients who require emergency or unplanned admission through Emergency Departments are to be tested for COVID-19.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the impact that dental surgeries unable to accept registrations from NHS patients will have on children's oral health.
answer_text: Prior to the arrival of COVID-19, General Dental Services (GDS) in Northern Ireland faced many challenges, however access to treatment was good and the general oral health of the population was improving.The COVID-19 pandemic continues to have a significant impact on capacity within GDS.  Efforts to address the shortage of registration places have focussed on increasing capacity within practices. The Rebuilding Support Scheme (RSS) has seen an increase in activity levels of around 16.5% over the first 5 months of 2022/23, and will continue, offering enhanced payments in return for increasing levels of activity, until the end of the financial year.  A condition of the RSS is that patients – including children – are treated on the basis of clinical need, rather than registration status.  This helps to ensure that those in most need of treatment are prioritised.To provide treatment for patients with urgent dental need, Emergency Dental Clinics are also available at weekends and Bank Holidays where the patient will be triaged remotely, and appropriate advice given or treatment arranged.In addition, my Department commenced a scheme on 3 October 2022 throughout Northern Ireland to provide access to non-registered patients who are experiencing an emergency or urgent dental condition.However, I do not underestimate the impact that the last two years will have had on children’s oral health nor the repercussions this will have over the coming years. My Department is committed to rebuilding GDS in order to mitigate these repercussions.The Chief Dental Officer has established an Oral Health Options Group to allow for the consideration and development of further interventions for children, focussing on reducing early experiences of dental disease.  In addition, a Northern Ireland dental survey of five-year-old children has been commissioned, with the data collection exercise recently completed. The results of this will used to help inform the oral health need and plan appropriate interventions moving forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) to outline the reason for the delay in issuing free school meal and uniform grants for this academic year; and (ii) what steps will be taken to streamline this process in future.
answer_text: The online portal for free school meal (FSM) applications operated by the Education Authority (EA) opened on 9 June 2022 for the 22/23 academic year. In recognising the importance of pupils’ entitlement being confirmed before the start of the academic year, the EA has undertaken extensive communications explaining to families that in order to ensure they were able to access these supports from the start of the academic year, they should apply before 31 July. This was communicated via social media, the EA website and through letters issued to applicants who received support last year encouraging them to apply again for the current school year.  While many parents did apply early enough to ensure entitlement was in place for the start of the academic year, unfortunately this was not the case for all applications. The EA continues to make every effort to process applications as quickly as possible and has consistently met its target of processing 95% of free school meals and uniform grant applications within four weeks. As at 14 October, the EA was processing applications received on 16th September.In order to support parents in their applications, the EA provides extensive guidance materials and technical support. While the majority of parents are able to apply online without assistance, there is a dedicated EA helpdesk service where parents can ask for help if they have difficulty in uploading the required information or need advice on how to progress their application. Any parents who are unable to apply after such support have been provided with paper applications by the EA. When an application is submitted without the appropriate documentation, all such applicants are sent a tailored email response explaining the correct documentation that they are required to provide. It is necessary to verify applicants’ eligibility in order to ensure that public funds are used correctly.The Department continues to work with the EA to help improve the current system wherever possible. As part of this process, the EA is currently gathering information for a post project review of the current year’s application process and any lessons learned will be considered in determining how support could be improved for the next application cycle.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the (i) reform of adult social care; and (ii) steps to increase the domiciliary care rate for Direct Payment clients.
answer_text: (i) The Department of Health has recently concluded a public consultation on its proposals to Reform Adult Social Care in Northern Ireland.  The consultation which was launched on 26th January 2022 ended on 01st July 2022.Now that the consultation has ended the Department is undertaking a full evaluation and analysis of consultation responses which will inform a summary and evaluation report.  When finalised, the Department will publish a post consultation report and evaluation detailing how it will take forward the strategic reforms of Adult Social Care.(ii)  I was pleased to announce an uplift of £7.1m for 2022-23 for Direct Payments on 6 October. In real terms, this should see Personal Assistants receiving an uplift of around 12% in their hourly rate.The funding will allow individuals who employ personal assistant carers via Direct Payments to offer improved rates of pay and in turn support the continued provision of care in the context of an increasingly competitive employment market, including in the social care sector.Whilst we continue to operate in a very challenging environment, I am confident that this funding package will support the continued provision of care through direct payments. It further underlines my commitment to supporting a person-centred approach, whilst ensuring that people’s assessed care needs are fulfilled.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the number of people under 18 years of age who have had operations to remove rotting teeth for each of the last five years.
answer_text: The number of people under 18 years of age who have had operations to remove rotting teeth for each of the last five years is not available.  The HSC Business Services Organisation (BSO) holds information on treatments that take place in primary care i.e. high street dental practices employing a dentist who has made an arrangement with the Department of Health’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) to provide health service dentistry.  BSO does not hold any information in relation to wholly private treatments that are carried out by dental practices. The number of children’s teeth extracted, and the number of children treated for an extraction, under health service dentistry in primary care for each of the last five complete financial years, are shown in Table 1.  Extractions are not always performed on decayed teeth, particularly in preparation for orthodontic treatments where healthy teeth will be extracted.  BSO does not hold information on the reason for an extraction, therefore the numbers in Table 1 represent more than just the extraction of decayed teeth.  In addition, children who have extractions carried out in hospital under general anaesthetic are not included in these figures nor are those who may have had extractions in Urgent Dental Care Centres which were set up during the Covid-19 pandemic.  Table 1: Number of teeth extracted from children and the number of unique children1 treated for an extraction2 under health service dentistry in primary care by Financial Year3.Financial Year3Teeth Extracted from ChildrenUnique Children Treated12017-1831,80119,2422018-1930,87919,1262019-2030,86918,6382020-21414,6269,2482021-2221,90313,408Source: Family Practitioner Services, HSC Business Services Organisation.1 Age is calculated at the start of treatment. Children are those under 18. 2 Treatment codes for extractions are all 21, 22 and 52 codes except 2121 and 5206.  (See SDR for more details http://www.hscbusiness.hscni.net/2069.htm). Based on paid treatments and information supplied by dentist making the claim. Excludes all private work. Excludes work carried out in hospitals. May include small number of duplicates. 3 Financial year relates to when claim was paid, this is not necessarily the same year as when the treatment took place.4 On 23rd March 2020, general dental practitioners (GDPs) were advised to continue to see patients who did not meet the COVID-19 case definition and who required urgent dental care which could be provided using non-aerosol generating procedures (AGPs) however all routine dental treatment was to be postponed. In addition, 5 Trust-based Urgent Dental Care Centres were opened at the beginning of April 2020. From 29th June 2020, non-urgent dental care was provided under Phase 2 but AGPs were still prohibited. It was decided to offer a fast-track option to move to Phase 3 from 1 July 2020 onwards for those practices that are able to demonstrate that they meet all the necessary standards to safely offer AGP

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps have been taken to tackle dental care inequalities for adults and children in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Oral health inequalities remain a significant public health issue in Northern Ireland. The impact of poor oral health disproportionately affects socially disadvantaged groups of society. “Making Life Better” is the overarching strategic framework for public health in Northern Ireland. I am committed to creating the conditions for individuals, families, and communities to take greater control over their lives and be enabled and supported to lead healthy lives. As independent contractors, General Dental Practitioners (GDPs) are free to decide where they wish to set up dental practices, and the number of health service patients they wish to treat.  In the most recent data published by the Business Services organisation, Northern Ireland has 60 dentists per 100,000 of the population.  At Northern Ireland level, 94% of the population live within five miles of a dental practice.67% of the overall population are registered with a Health Service dentist, with 72% of children registered with a GDP.Despite this, I am aware of the continued high levels of dental disease in Northern Ireland.  At the present time, the Chief Dental Officer (CDO) has established “Oral Health Options Groups” to allow for the consideration and development of further interventions for the two most urgent groups at present; namely, children and older people. Work in these groups is at an advanced stage and recommendations will be focussed on reducing dental decay experience for young children to help them reach adulthood with reduced experience of dental disease, and supporting older people and their carers to maintain and promote good oral health and reduce the burden of disease. My departments framework for universal child health promotion, “Healthy Child Healthy Future (HCHF)”, is currently being reviewed.  Oral health is included in this important piece of work, and, crucially, raises the profile of oral health among wider healthcare professionals who engage with families at a young age. To support oral health in early years, my department continues to fund the “Happy Smiles” programme, which is aimed at improving the dental health of pre-school children.  The initiative strives to support childcare providers in oral health promotion and to educate parents regarding goodoral health practices in the home environment. Work has restarted on reform of the General Dental Services contract.  Part of this work will consider how prevention can be embedded in the contract for delivery of general dental services to our patient population. All future plans will have the prevention of oral disease and promotion of oral health at their centre.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what consideration has been given to putting on additional trains during Derry's Halloween festival.
answer_text: The scheduling and provision of additional train services during Derry's Halloween festival is an operational matter for Translink. You should, therefore, direct your question to Translink at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of individuals diagnosed with breast cancer, broken down by the stage at diagnosis, for each of the last three years.
answer_text: Statistics on breast cancer diagnoses in Northern Ireland (NI) are currently only available from the NI Cancer Registry (NICR) up to the end of 2019.Information provided by the NICR on the number of breast cancer diagnoses from 2017 to 2019, broken down by stage of diagnosis is provided in Table 1 overleaf.Table 1: Number of Breast Cancer cases by Stage of Diagnosis and Year of Diagnosis for Patients Diagnosed in Northern Ireland 2017-2019.Stage at Diagnosis201720182019TotalStage I6186416251,884Stage II5995835781,760Stage III172179147498Stage IV738369225Unknown415160152Total1,5031,5371,4794,519Source: Northern Ireland Cancer Registry (NICR)

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the impact of the public realm works along Bishop Street on traffic flow in Derry city centre.
answer_text: I am aware that the temporary pilot of outdoor seating areas on Bishop Street, while generally well received by the public, has resulted in some traffic congestion.My Department intends to address this issue by introducing double yellow line restrictions.  As this process can take time, interim measures are being considered to discourage parking adjacent to the outdoor seating areas.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Western Division at: dfiroads.western@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what engagement he has had with his UK counterparts in relation to a ban on fracking.
answer_text: There has been no engagement with UK government counterparts on this issue as the decision on fracking applies to England only. It will be up to a Northern Ireland Executive Committee to determine local onshore petroleum licensing policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on plans to move towards a zero emission rail network.
answer_text: Translink’s ‘Climate Positive Strategy’ places a key priority on greener vehicles and recognises their role in helping attain its objectives of a 50% reduction in current emissions by 2030 and Net Zero by 2040 on all trains as well as buses and buildings.With the support of my Department, Translink is undertaking extensive engagement with the industry and its supply chain to determine the optimum traction solution to decarbonise the rail network. Several desktop studies are ongoing and traction options under consideration include full electric and battery. An Initial Technical Study was completed in March 2022 which assessed the implications of electrifying the rail network.  Further detailed feasibility studies are planned to commence in early 2023. Moving to a zero emission rail network is a significant undertaking and will require time and investment to implement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what steps have been taken to improve signalling and maintenance on the Derry to Belfast rail line.
answer_text: The improvement of signalling and maintenance on our rail network is the responsibility of Translink. You should, therefore, direct your question to Translink at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Narrow Bridge Water Project.
answer_text: My officials are working closely with the Irish government, Louth County Council and other stakeholders to deliver the Narrow Water Bridge project as quickly as possible. The required Bridge Orders were made by my Department on 24 February 2022 and became operational on 26 March 2022. Louth County Council has recently commenced the initial stage of a procurement process to appoint a contractor to construct the bridge. Subject to necessary Irish Government approvals, it is currently envisaged that the tender document will issue later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 1119/22-27, when her Department's report on barriers to pension credit uptake will be published.
answer_text: The research is currently being finalised and is due to report in Autumn 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on enhancing cross border connectivity.
answer_text: I am ambitious for our public transport networks, and have a vision to enhance connectivity to better connect our communities while delivering cleaner, greener, more sustainable transport within the budgetary envelope that is available. As you will be aware, the Public Consultation phase of the All Island Strategic Rail Review closed on 21st January 2022. Work on the review has been progressing and I recently met with Minister Ryan and ARUP in advance of the Review’s anticipated completion in the final quarter of 2022. The Review is being undertaken to allow us to consider our rail network across this island with a view to how it can be improved for everyone including how our existing connections could be improved and where new rail links would be best placed.  In addition, Translink has recently commenced a joint project with Iarnród Eireann to replace the existing Enterprise Train Sets with modern rolling stock. This project aims to see the introduction of an hourly service between Belfast and Dublin with a sub 2 hour journey time. In addition as passenger numbers grow and resources are available, Translink will continue to enhance the cross border bus timetables.Officials from my Department’s Active Travel Branch continue to meet with their counterparts in Department of Transport (Dublin) on greenway development in general and, more recently, in relation to the development of the proposed Enniskillen – Collooney Greenway in Co. Sligo.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 3337/22-27, how many of the 200 individual providers which were assessed by the Department's Medicines Regulatory Group were (i) determined to be in breach of advertising legislation; and (ii) given formal referrals to professional bodies.
answer_text: The Medicines Regulatory Group (MRG) enforcement and compliance programme into the supply, promotion and advertising of Botulinum Toxin Type A, generally referred to as Botox, had focused operational objectives to investigate and establish the scale of breaches of relevant medicines legislation. The principal UK legislation regulating the advertising, supply and use of POM’s is the Human Medicines Regulations 2012 (‘the Regulations’). A breach of any of the relevant provisions of the Regulations is a criminal offence. Where MRG identify that there has been a breach of the Regulations it will always consider a range of enforcement options or disposal methods as appropriate and proportionate to the circumstances. This includes securing compliance with the Regulations through the issuing of warning or advice, referral to professional bodies or prosecution. During a 6-month programme investigations and assessments were conducted into over 200 providers of Botox and where shortcomings in prescribing processes were identified, 4 formal referrals were made to professional bodies, including the General Medical Council (2); General Pharmaceutical Council (1) and the Medicines and Healthcare Products Regulatory Agency (1).It was also established that many businesses were in breach of the Human Medicines Regulations 2012, in advertising or promoting the use of a prescription only medicine. Those with advertisements in contravention of the regulations (110) received warning and advice notices requesting that offending advertising be removed and brought into immediate compliance. Where compliance with law and procedures was sought it was confirmed in 96% of cases.In relation to any ongoing unlawful advertising or supply of prescription medicines, MRG continues to ensure compliance with applicable laws. Routine inspections are not undertaken, but where evidence or information suggests unlawful practice is occurring, enforcement visits and investigations are conducted to ensure public safety in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of Universal Credit claimants subject to benefit sanctions, for each of the last three years.
answer_text: Details on Social Security Sanctions for each of the last three years can be found in the Welfare Supplementary Payments, Discretionary Support, Standards of Advice & Assistance and Sanctions Annual Reports: https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/welfare-supplementary-payments-discretionary-support-standards-advice-assistance-and-sanctionsThese published statistics can be accessed on the DfC website; https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/articles/universal-credit-statistics

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether a Letter of Offer has been issued for the Phase 3 upgrade to the Coleraine to Derry railway line.
answer_text: My Department is currently working with the Department of Finance in consideration of the Outline Business Case for the Phase 3 track renewal between Derry and Coleraine following completion of the feasibility study of the project. As consideration of the Outline Business Case is continuing a letter of offer has not been issued for this project.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the total number of social housing new build completions since 2012.

To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the number of successful Right to Buy applications that were received in (i) the Northern Ireland Housing Executive; and (ii) housing associations, since 2012.
answer_text: While the Department does not hold the data requested in relation to Housing Associations, the Housing Executive has provided the table below which details the number of completed tenant purchases under its House Sales scheme since 2012.Completions by year - Housing Exec House Sales (including Flats and Maisonettes)Financial YearBelfastNorthSouthTotal2011/126679912362012/1374119962892013/141591941965492014/151191801724712015/16971271633872016/171161521344022017/181351391624362018/191301391804492019/201301541644482020/219382642392021/222071221154442022/23 YTD6811189268Total1394159816264618

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether she will consider steps to change Support for Mortgage Interest from a loan to a benefit, in light of increased interest rates.
answer_text: Support for Mortgage Interest changed from a benefit to an interest-bearing loan as part of Welfare Reform in April 2018. Claimants continue to receive the same amount of support and the same level of protection as they did under the previous Support for Mortgage Interest scheme.The loan is only recoverable from any equity in the sale or transfer of ownership of the property or when the recipient dies.  If there is insufficient equity to repay the total of the loan, the Department will write off any amount that cannot be repaid.There are no current plans to change Support for Mortgage Interest from a loan to a benefit.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline what engagement her Department has had with housing organisations and relevant stakeholders in relation to mortgage support provision.
answer_text: My Department has not had any recent engagement with housing organisations and relevant stakeholders in relation to mortgage support provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) whether she has considered a mortgage rescue scheme, similar to the model of the Irish Icare Scheme; and (ii) has her Department carried out any analysis on the merit of such a provision.
answer_text: I am aware of mortgage rescue schemes that are available across other jurisdictions. Evidence from research commissioned by my Department and conducted by the Ulster University Economic Policy Centre, suggests that mortgage rescue schemes are expensive to operate and not particularly effective.  The research outlined other interventions have been shown to have a greater impact, for example mortgage holidays, payment deferrals and timely advice services.There is currently not sufficient evidence to warrant the establishment of such a scheme in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide an update on the implementation of the Agenda for Change Pay Award.
answer_text: I recently publicly acknowledged the need to ensure all staff are appropriately paid, when highlighting the financial pressures and budgetary uncertainty facing our health service. Unlike my colleagues in other parts of the United Kingdom, I am unable to announce the immediate implementation of pay awards locally as Northern Ireland does not have an agreed Executive Budget for 2022/23. I must stress that our pay award decisions can also only be taken in the context of a wider public sector pay policy set by the Department of Finance. Any pay increase will also require to be fully funded by the Executive, given the financial outlook facing my Department in 2022/23.The current cost of living crisis impacts on us all, yet Departments cannot address all issues in isolation – we need a functioning Executive to deliver appropriate support across our society.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities the number of Northern Ireland Housing Executive planned maintenance and improvement schemes which have been postponed in the Foyle constituency, in the last two years.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has advised me that there were 15 schemes from mid-2020 in the Foyle Constituency over the past 2 years that were delayed due to a revision of programme dates. These consisted of two kitchen schemes, one bathroom scheme, two External Cyclical Maintenance, four Double Glazing schemes and six Heating schemes. Every effort has been made to try and reduce this delay to the tenants with the schemes delayed now either on site or completed successfully on site.More recently, issues with one major contract which is to deliver Bathrooms, Kitchens, Double Glazing and Doors have postponed current work on five schemes consisting of two Kitchens, two Bathroom/Kitchen/Rewire (BKR) and one Bathroom.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what plans she has to reconfigure the Northern Ireland Housing Executive offices based in Derry.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has advised that it has been considering options in relation to the possible reconfiguration of its accommodation portfolio in Derry.No final decisions have been taken and a business case is being developed by the Housing Executive. Any proposed changes will be subject to Section 75 consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the expansion of the Hospital Travel Costs Scheme to include parents of sick children.
answer_text: My Department intends to review both the Help with Health Costs and the Hospital Travel Costs schemes.  This review will be primarily to take forward the necessary legislative amendments due to the introduction of Universal Credit.  Part of the review will involve comparisons with other GB jurisdictions and will give us the opportunity to consider if the schemes require further amendments to support people on low incomes with their families healthcare costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans he has to signalise the Caw Roundabout in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: An initial study to investigate the feasibility of introducing signals and a revised layout on the Caw Roundabout has been undertaken. My Department has recently engaged a team of engineering consultants to further develop the design of the scheme and to better quantify the associated construction costs. Part of the conditions of planning permission granted for housing and other development in the area also requires developers to implement signalisation and upgrade works to Caw Roundabout to mitigate the impacts of increased vehicular and pedestrian traffic generated by their developments.  Given the benefits that signalisation of Caw Roundabout will have on safety and traffic progression in this area, my officials are continuing to liaise with developers to explore options to jointly fund the upgrade works.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he plans to relax current regulations around the use of e-scooters on public roads.
answer_text: The position remains that it is illegal to use e-scooters on public roads and public spaces in the North. You may be aware that the Department for Transport has announced its intention to create regulations which will legalise the use of electric scooters in England. The proposed change in regulations in England will not apply here, but my officials will monitor developments there.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how many applicants have received treatment as part of the ROI Reimbursement Scheme; and (ii) whether he will extend this provision.
answer_text: The Republic of Ireland (RoI) Reimbursement Scheme was put in place on a temporary basis for a period of 12 months from 1 July 2021 to help reduce Northern Ireland waiting lists and to provide an opportunity for some patients to have their treatment much earlier.  A further £5m was allocated to continue to operate the scheme beyond the planned 30 June 2022 deadline.  This further funding is exhausted and the scheme closed on 21 September 2022 to new applicants.The Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG), who administrate the temporary RoI cross border reimbursement scheme, do not record when patients have their treatment but rather when patients submit claims.  At 30 September the SPPG had processed 1605 patient claims and received a further 494 claims for payment.  It is therefore estimated that approximately 2099 patients had benefitted from the reimbursement scheme in the period July 2021 – Sept 2022. I am very conscious of pressing demands for extra funding across many areas of health and social care. As Minister, I have continued to do the best I can to deliver for the public with the resources I have available. Amid ongoing budget uncertainty, I am on record as warning that funding pressures in health may be significant by the second half of 2022/23. The financial situation will undoubtedly be constrained whatever the final budget settlement. However, because of these pressures, I regret that it has not been possible to assign funding to further extend the RoIRS.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Feasibility Study for Phase 3 of the Derry to Coleraine railway line.
answer_text: I can confirm that my Department is currently working with the Department of Finance in consideration of the Outline Business Case for the Phase 3 track renewal between Derry and Coleraine following completion of the feasibility study of the project.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will consider extending the timeframe an individual can benefit from the Penalty Charge Notice 50 per cent discount from 14 days to six weeks.
answer_text: The Traffic Management Order (Northern Ireland) 2005 allows a Penalty Charge Notice (PCN) to be reduced if payment is received within 14 days. As PCNs are intended to discourage illegal parking, my Department has no plans to change the discount period.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) what consideration she have given to increasing the threshold eligibility criteria for Free School Meals and Uniform grants in line with minimum wage increases; and (ii) when this threshold was last reviewed.
answer_text: The Department is continuing to progress a review of Free School Meals and Uniform Grant eligibility criteria. The review is considering a wide range of options that include the provision of a universal free school meal for some/all year groups as well as raising the current income thresholds that apply to the eligibility criteria for applicants in receipt of Universal Credit, Working Tax Credit or Child Tax Credit. It is planned that a public consultation will commence later this year. The current criteria for eligibility for Free School Meals and Uniform Grant were last updated in September 2017 in order to introduce a criterion for those in receipt of Universal Credit.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in instances when the Road Service is unable to clean gullies due to parked vehicles, is this recorded and revisited at a later date or are they left until the next annual maintenance schedule.
answer_text: My officials advise that if vehicles are parked and gullies cannot be cleaned during a cyclical clean, these are generally noted by the contractor and further attempts to clean them are made at least once soon after.  If further attempts to clean the gullies are still unsuccessful then, depending on the circumstances, plans may be made to enter the area at weekends or during the evenings, as resources permit, especially in areas with flooding problems. Alternatively, in the case of individual gullies, they may be left to the next cyclical clean unless there is a specific complaint or request for cleaning made.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the upgrade of wastewater treatment networks.
answer_text: The status of work at wastewater infrastructure across the North is an operational matter for NI Water. You should therefore, direct your question to NI Water at waterline@niwater.com.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he intends to commission a new Palliative and End of Life Care Strategy for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of quality palliative care in supporting people with life-limiting conditions to live well and achieve the best quality of life as their illness progresses.  The Palliative Care in Partnership programme continues to oversee implementation of the Department’s ‘Living Matters: Dying Matters palliative and end of life care strategy for adults’ and the development and improvement of palliative and end of life care across the region.In terms of any new palliative care strategy, it would be appropriate to wait for the Programme for Government process to be progressed, as this will provide the strategic driver for the future development of palliative and end of life care.  In the meantime, a number of initiatives are currently being progressed which I believe will further improve services and support in relation to both palliative care and bereavement.  These include my Department’s new Cancer Strategy, the work being taken forward to develop the Advance Care Planning policy for adults and the work of the new regional Bereavement Network to enhance bereavement support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what steps are being taken to address the 60 per cent increase in the number of individuals waiting for a domiciliary care package.
answer_text: Covid-19 related and non Covid-19 related absences throughout the pandemic have been a challenge for all domiciliary care providers.  The demand for domiciliary care has increased as well as the complexity of need of service users.  Recently fuel cost increases have put additional pressures on the domiciliary care sector.  Providers operate rolling recruitment exercises to fill workforce vacancies. The Health Trusts have processes in place to risk assess and prioritise those awaiting a package of care.  The local HSC Trusts are promoting Direct Payments as an alternative to traditional packages of care and we would encourage as many people as possible in the community to avail of this option.  You will be aware that on 24 November 2021 I announced a support package of up to £23m for the independent domiciliary and wider social care sector. This additional funding has been provided with the expectation that providers will not only maintain current service levels, but increase capacity and be in a position to reach out particularly into rural areas.  £25 million has been released for 2022/2023 period.Care hours delivered up to year ending March 2022 have increased regionally compared to figures at year end 2017.Colleagues within the Department of Health have been collating unmet needs figures from the five HSC Trusts since mid-November 2021 to date. The Department of Health has met with the five Health and Social Care Trusts to formalise this data collation and provide a ‘deeper dive’ on the information regarding ‘length of wait’ and ‘number of hours service users are waiting on broken down into bands’.    This data set collation is being piloted from the beginning of October 2022.I will continue to support the domiciliary care sector in their attempts to meet the current demand for packages of care in the community.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline what engagement she has had with the British government to remove or amend the two child limit policy for Child Tax Credit and Universal Credit claimants.
answer_text: I appointed an independent panel to complete a comprehensive review of existing welfare mitigation measures and to consider a future mitigation package. As part of this work the independent panel were specifically asked to consider the impact of welfare reforms on household income, including on children.The independent panel has provided its final report to my Department and this will be published at the earliest opportunity. The implementation of any new payment schemes will require Executive agreement on the allocation of necessary funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the total number of households in Northern Ireland impacted by the two child limit, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: The total number of Universal Credit households impacted by the two child limit in each of the last three years is as follows.              2019 -  1,6702020 -  4,0932021 -  6,049 It should be noted that there will be households in receipt of Tax Credit who may also be impacted by the two child limit, however, this data will need to be obtained from Revenue and Customs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of (i) individuals awaiting a domiciliary care package; and (ii) domiciliary care staff within the Western Trust.
To ask the Minister of Education whether Free School Meals applicants who experienced delays in approval will receive backdated support.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the administration of Free School Meal (FSM) applications. The EA has reported that its staff are currently processing applications within the four week timeframe as set out in its service level agreement.  The EA has undertaken extensive communications regarding FSM and Uniform Grants explaining to families that in order to ensure they were able access these supports from the start of the school year, they should apply before 31 July. This was communicated via social media, the EA website and through letters issued to applicants who received support last year encouraging them to apply again for the current school year.  Unfortunately, many parents have applied late or provided incorrect supporting documentation and have been required to reapply.  All such applicants are sent a tailored e-mail response advising them to resubmit their application and confirming the correct documentation they are required to provide.  However, it is important that public funds are only committed after an appropriate verification process is completed.FSM is a benefit in kind and families will only be able to access this support when applications have been processed and approved. There is therefore no facility for providing backdated payments in respect of FSM.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he intends to consult on the regulatory framework of non-surgical cosmetic procedures for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: It is my intention that a Review of Regulation will be taken forward during the current mandate of the Assembly.  This Review of Regulation will include an assessment of non-surgical cosmetic procedures in order to consider whether any further assurances are required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what consideration has been given to classifying all dermal fillers as medical devices, in line with protections provided under European Medical Device Regulations, to improve the safety and quality of these products.
answer_text: Under the terms of the Northern Ireland Protocol, manufacturers of medical devices supplying the Northern Ireland market must meet the requirements of the EU Medical Device Regulations (MDR) 2017. These regulations came fully into force on 26th May 2021 and cover certain aesthetic products, including dermal fillers, that are marketed as having no intended medical purpose. The responsibility for complying with the regulations resides with the manufacturer or their EU-based authorised representative if the manufacturer is located outside the EU. If the manufacturer is located outside the EU, the importers of the product, who may be the final customer, have certain obligations under the MDR. These obligations include checking that the product has the necessary product certification that confirms compliance with the MDR.  If an importer considers that a device is not compliant with the MDR, they must not out put the device on the market. The importer must then inform the manufacturer or their authorised representative, as well as the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA), which is responsible for enforcing compliance with the EU MDR in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the estimated number of non-surgical cosmetic procedures including botox and dermal filler, that have been carried out on people under 18 years old in Northern Ireland within the last three years.
answer_text: The information requested is not collected centrally in the Department. Each of the five Health and Social Care Trusts has been asked previously for input on similar queries relating to botox and dermal filler procedures, and indicated that they do not deliver non-surgical cosmetic procedures.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the total amount of private financial initiative contracts taken out by his Department; (ii) the amount still owed to the private sector; and (iii) how many of these contracts are subject to financial penalties for failing to meet contractual obligations.
answer_text: The Department for the Economy has taken out eight Private Financial Initiative contracts with a total value of £715.7 million. The amount still owed to the private sector is £212.5 million. Where there is a failure to meet contractual obligations, financial penalty clauses are contained in each of the eight contracts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has any plans to legislate for the introduction of a regulatory framework on who can perform non-surgical cosmetic procedure in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Under the Independent Health Care Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2005, the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA) regulate clinics providing certain laser or intense pulsed light cosmetic treatments and these providers are required to undergo the appropriate training etc. However, those carrying out non-surgical cosmetic procedures/treatments such as Botox or dermal fillers are not required to register with RQIA.  There are no qualifications legally required to administer Botox or dermal fillers.My Department’s Medicines Regulatory Group (MRG) carried out an enforcement and compliance programme into the provision of Botox over a 6 month period. Initial investigations and assessments have been conducted into over 200 individual providers of Botulinum Toxin Type A.  Shortcomings in prescribing processes and breaches of advertising legislation have been identified.  Those in breach of legislation have been issued formal advice and warning notices where appropriate and have moved into legal compliance in the vast majority of cases investigated.  In addition, formal referrals have been made to professional bodies, e.g. GMC where appropriate.  The MRG continues to monitor the situation.My Department is aware of the changing health and social care environment, notably around cosmetic treatments, and has carried out a fundamental review of health and social care regulatory framework.  My officials have developed a new draft regulatory policy that includes the principles of regulation, along with the broad scope of services to be regulated and the proposal that the regulator should have wider powers of enforcement etc.   This work has been the first phase of the process before moving on to phase 2. As part of my Department’s response to the pandemic the launch of the consultation on phase 1 was delayed.  Before a draft policy is consulted upon it will be subject to further review taking into account any additional lessons from the pandemic and any other regulatory and quality issues that have emerged over the past few years.It is my intention that a Review of Regulation will be taken forward during the current mandate of the Assembly.  This Review of Regulation will also include an assessment of current standards in order to consider whether any further assurances are required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the total amount of private financial initiative contracts taken out by his department; (ii) the amounts still owed to the private sector; and (iii) to outline how many of these contracts are subject to financial penalties for failing to meet contractual obligations.
answer_text: The Department does not have any commitments under Private Finance Initiative contracts.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what steps have been taken to to improve road safety at the Tamnaherin and Brockagh Road junction in the Foyle constituency.
answer_text: I can confirm that my officials are undertaking a feasibility study into the need for a junction improvement scheme at the B118 Tamnaherin Road / U1145 Brockagh Road junction.  Upon completion of the feasibility study a decision will then be made whether to progress a scheme to design stage.  Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Western Division at: dfiroads.western@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 2311/22-27, to provide an update on the work of the independent panel appointed to review existing welfare mitigation measures, including the impact of welfare reforms on household incomes.
answer_text: The implementation of any new payment schemes recommended by the independent advisory panel on welfare mitigations will require Executive agreement on the allocation of necessary funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the total amount of private financial initiative contracts taken out by his Department; (ii) the amounts still owed to the private sector; and (iii) how many of these contracts are subject to financial penalties for failing to meet contractual obligations.
answer_text: The Department for Infrastructure (Roads) has entered into two PPP contracts for the Design, Build, Finance and Operations (DBFO) of sections of the Road Network. NI Water currently has three operational PPP/PFI contracts.		Kinnegar WwTW 	(Wastewater Treatment PFI Contract) 		Duration: 	1999-2024		Total Contract Value: 	£52.49M		Project Alpha 	(Drinking Water PPP Contract)		Duration: 	2006-2031		Total Contract Value: 	£516.56M		Project Omega 	(Wastewater & Sludge Disposal Contract)		Duration: 	2007-2032		Total Contract Value: 	£663.08MFor the DfI Roads Package 1 and Package 2 contracts, the outstanding amounts to repay as at 01/04/22 are £302.4m and £768.6m respectively.For NI Water contracts as at March 22, the estimated remaining future payments are as follows:		Kinnegar WWTW 	£4.75M		Project Alpha 	£264.51M		Project Omega 	£335.83MThe roads contracts are based on a lane availability payment mechanism. Where lanes are unavailable appropriate deductions are made from the payments. Failure to comply with contract obligations may lead to the Department issuing Remedial Notices and/or award of Penalty Points all in accordance with the contract provisions.The NI Water contracts allow NI Water to apply financial performance deductions when performance is below contracted standards. The NI Water contracts also allow NI Water to terminate for material breach of contract terms.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many driving examiners are employed by the Driving and Vehicle Agency in each test centre.
answer_text: In line with the Driver & Vehicle Agency’s (DVA) obligations to comply with the code of practice for official statistics and other data protection legislation, only regional figures will be provided in respect of driving examiners and dual role examiners.  This is to protect the identity of individual driving examiners working within each testing location, given the small numbers at each site. As at 21 September 2022, the DVA has 94 full time and dual role staff capable of conducting driving tests across its network of test centres, and is in the process of completing an external recruitment competition for 16 new full time driving examiners.  To date 12 new driving examiners have been appointed from this competition and training will continue until all new driving examiner posts are filled.  In addition to the recruitment of additional examiners, the DVA continue to offer driving tests on a Saturday and at certain centres on Sundays and bank holidays, where it is suitable to do so without compromising the integrity of the test.  Overtime is also being used to rota off-shift dual role driving examiners to provide further capacity and to provide cover for scheduled driving tests, where due to a variety of unforeseen reasons such as sick absence or the requirement to self-isolate, driving examiners are unable to attend work. The DVA has, and continues, to work hard to increase its capacity to help meet the high demand for driving tests for all customers.  However, all driving test services across these islands are experiencing high demand with longer than usual waiting times and I would ask customers for their continued patience.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the All-Island Strategic Rail Review.
answer_text: As you will be aware, the Public Consultation phase of the All Island Strategic Rail Review closed on 21st January 2022. Work on the review has been progressing and I recently met with Minister Ryan and ARUP in advance of the Review’s anticipated completion in the final quarter of 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline (i) what engagement she has had with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive in relation to the termination of £455 million of maintenance contracts; (ii) the potential impact this will have on tenants; and (iii) what steps have been taken to resolve the situation.
answer_text: (i)	The Housing Executive has kept my Department updated as to the ongoing negotiations with the contractors as they are still in contract.(ii)	The current economic environment has put pressure on the contractor’s tendered rates for works submitted prior to the significant rise in inflation. Discussions are ongoing with the aim of finding a resolution as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on his Department's review into existing parking restrictions on Duncreggan Road; and (ii) whether consideration has been given to implementing double yellow lines in the area.
answer_text: My officials plan to carry out a review of parking activity in the coming weeks following the return of students and commencement of the academic year. Should the review identify significant parking issues that are adversely impacting traffic progression along the Duncreggan Road, access to private properties or general road safety, then appropriate restrictions will be considered.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Western Division at: dfiroads.western@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what impact the absence of an Executive is having on the rollout of the Protect Life 2 Suicide Prevention Strategy.
answer_text: Significant work continues across Departments to support suicide prevention and emotional health and wellbeing. This includes the rollout of Protect Life 2. Full funding of Protect Life 2 will however be required to ensure the Strategy can be implemented fully.In the absence of an Executive it has unfortunately not been possible to agree a final 2022-25 Budget. I have stated before the destabilising impact the current financial certainty is having across a wide range of health and social care services. Without a confirmed budget position the Department will not be able to plan effectively to develop longer term plans to deliver services within the funding envelope provided and to sustain services going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what prioritisation is given to areas at greatest risk of flooding in his Department's gully maintenance schedule.
answer_text: Historically my Department aimed to inspect and, where necessary, clean all gullies in urban areas twice each year and gullies in rural areas once per year however, due to budgetary constraints, in recent years my Department aims to inspect and, where necessary, clean all gullies once annually.My officials advise that when severe weather warnings are received, gully emptiers are placed on standby, stocks of sandbags, pumps and signs are checked and replenished as necessary and gullies and grilles at known flooding hotspots are inspected and cleaned, when required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what engagement his Department has had with Derry City and Strabane District Council in relation to remediation requirements and safety improvements at Creggan reservoir.
answer_text: My Department encourages all reservoir managers to manage their reservoirs in a responsible manner. My officials have, as part of ongoing engagement, recently met with officers from the Council to discuss how, they as reservoir owners and manager, can take forward safety measures at their Creggan reservoirs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on pigeon roosting prevention measures at the Craigavon Bridge in Foyle; and (ii) what other steps will be taken to ensure that bird droppings does not pose a public health risk.
answer_text: My Department implemented measures to prevent pigeons roosting and nesting in Craigavon Bridge on 25 August 2022.Initial assessments indicate the measures put in place are sufficient to ensure that bird droppings no longer pose a public health risk to users of the bridge and I am pleased that positive feedback on the work has also been received from members of the public who regularly use the footway on Craigavon Bridge. My officials will continue to monitor the effectiveness of these works for the foreseeable future.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Western Division at: dfiroads.western@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 180/22-27, to outline whether a meeting with counterpart Ministers has been secured.
answer_text: I can confirm that a meeting has been arranged with the Scottish Cabinet Secretary for Social Justice, Housing and Local Government and the Welsh Minister for Social Justice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the total expenditure on locum and agency staff for each of the last three years, broken down by each Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to detail the average wait time for an MOT appointment; and (ii) whether consideration has been given to the reintroduction of a temporary exemption certificate provision.
answer_text: For the latest information on MOTs I would ask you to encourage your constituents to visit NI Direct at Book, view, change or cancel an MOT/ vehicle test | nidirect. This information is also included as an advice note with every MOT Reminder Letter issued to DVA customers.  The advice confirms if a customer gets to within five days of the expiry date of their vehicle tax and they have been unable to secure an MOT appointment, or their vehicle tax has expired, they should contact the DVA Customer Services team at dva.customerservices@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk, and they will do their best to get an urgent appointment for the vehicle.  To date, all customers who have contacted the DVA in these circumstances have been offered an earlier appointment slot.The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) has steadily increased its vehicle testing capacity to pre-Covid levels by adopting a range of measures including the recruitment of additional vehicle examiners, the use of overtime to provide cover for leave and by offering vehicle test appointments on a Sunday and bank holidays, when testing is not normally available.  The DVA is monitoring the number of applications received and tests carried out and expects waiting times to reduce over time through the actions they have taken to increase vehicle testing capacity across the network.  However, the DVA does not currently hold information to enable it to accurately report on the average waiting times for vehicle tests, given that this position changes on a regular basis.The circumstances that provided for the DVA to issue Temporary Exemption Certificates (TECs) have significantly changed, along with the return of normal testing services.  As the DVA is now providing testing capacity at pre-Covid levels it is important, from a road safety perspective, that all vehicles are tested at the earliest opportunity.  As such, there are no plans to issue further TECs at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the total number of approved uniform grant applications which have yet to be paid.
answer_text: As of 7 September, the total number of approved uniform grant applications that have not yet been paid is 1,766 and the Education Authority has confirmed that these payments are due to be made during week commencing 12 September 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average waiting times for assessment by an occupational therapist in each Health and Social Care Trust, for each of the last three years.
answer_text: Average waiting time is not available, but information on the number of people waiting for an Occupational Therapist assessment as at 31st March for each of the last three years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust is collated in time bands and shown in the tables below.
