question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what interim arrangements are in place for motorists whose MOT certificate has expired and who are unable to tax their vehicle and cannot access an MOT appointment.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) has steadily increased its vehicle testing capacity to pre-Covid levels by adopting a range of measures including the recruitment of additional vehicle examiners, the use of overtime to provide cover for leave and by offering vehicle test appointments on a Sunday and bank holidays, when testing is not normally available.The DVA continues to experience significant demand for vehicle testing services and in some cases customers may not be able to have their vehicles tested before their current MOT certificate expires.  In such cases, customers must book the earliest available test appointment for their vehicle, which may mean travelling to another test centre instead of their preferred location.  The DVA releases vehicle test appointments on a daily basis and customers are encouraged to regularly check the booking system as they may be able to change their existing appointment to a different test centre and time if a slot becomes available.The DVA’s advice to vehicle owners who require an MOT to tax their vehicle and cannot secure a date before their MOT expires is to:book the earliest available appointment; andkeep checking the booking system for an earlier appointment.If they get to within five days of the expiry date of their vehicle tax and they have been unable to secure an MOT appointment, or their vehicle tax has expired, they should contact the DVA Customer Services team at dva.customerservices@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk, and they will do their best to get an urgent appointment for the vehicle.  To date, all customers who have contacted the DVA in these circumstances have been offered an earlier appointment slot.For the latest information on MOTs I would ask you to encourage your constituents to visit NI Direct at Book, view, change or cancel an MOT/ vehicle test | nidirect.  This information is also included as an advice note with every MOT Reminder Letter issued to DVA customers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether she has considered expanding access to the Accessible Housing Register database to relevant organisations which are providing housing support to clients.
answer_text: The AHR contains sensitive information therefore, it is recommended that staff from housing support organisations liaise with the Housing Solutions Team in the relevant Area Office for queries of this nature in order to ensure the most up to date position is being reported.

answer_text: In the last 5 years (2017/18 – 2021/22) there have been 233 new accessible social housing completions across each of the parliamentary constituencies. The table 1 below shows the breakdown constituency. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of social housing applicants with a disability in need of accessible housing, in each constituency, for each of the last five years.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 2642/22-27, the information has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what consideration his Department has given to raising the height of safety barriers at the Foyle Bridge.
answer_text: My Department is preparing a strategic outline case on options to improve the Foyle Bridge. This includes considering how the bridge could be modified to encourage people to undertake more active travel journeys, increase connectivity and better aid a green recovery, which will have benefits for the mental health and wellbeing of people in the area. It will also investigate how the design of the parapets may be altered.My Department is working closely with stakeholders, including the Mental Health Champion, Professor Siobhan O’Neill, to develop proposals which will have the widest benefits for the people of Derry.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what consideration she has given to the creation of a scheme to fund the training of accredited road marshals, which would enable sporting clubs, organisations, charities and community groups to run events and reduce costs.
answer_text: Safety at sports events and any associated costs is the responsibility of the event organiser.  I have asked Sport NI to explore this matter further with the appropriate sports governing bodies to establish what is required and options may be available for event organisers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he has considered implementing a dual naming approach, seeking the addition of Derry alongside Londonderry, on signage for the A6.
answer_text: I can advise that my officials have been asked to review the current approach with a view to moving to one that accommodates dual naming on traffic signs across the network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline (i) the total number of private tenants in receipt of Local Housing Allowance who are being penalised for having underused bedrooms; and (ii) for an update on her Department's work to introduce new mitigations, including support for people impacted by the Local Housing Allowance.
answer_text: (i)  At the end of March 2022 there were 18,002 private tenants in receipt of Universal Credit capped at the Local Housing Allowance rate and living in houses with ‘underused’ bedrooms. Housing Benefit is also subject to Local Housing Allowance Rules. However, the Housing Executive do not collect any data in relation to under-occupancy for those in the private rental sector who are subject to Local Housing Allowance Rules (ii)  The independent review of welfare mitigations has been completed and a final report has been submitted to the Department. This will be published at the earliest opportunity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the total amount of money requested by her Department from benefit claimants for overpayments due to official errors; (ii) the amount of money recouped for overpayments; and (iii) the amount written off upon appeal, for 2021-22.
answer_text: The Department requested £6.1m from benefit claimants for overpayments due to official error during 2021 -22.The total amount of money recovered by the Department for overpayments during 2021 – 22 was £21.8m of which £3.4m was in relation to official error.Where the outcome of an appeal replaces the original decision and is favourable, the overpayment will be either be deemed non-recoverable or will be reduced.  The total value of overpayments reclassified as non-recoverable on appeal during 2021-22 was £1.2m.This figure also includes overpayments made non–recoverable or reduced following Mandatory Reconsideration, which is part of the disputes process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of staff employed to provide Autism Spectrum Disorder assessments in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The table below indicates the total of whole time equivalent (WTE) staff in each Trust that provide autism assessment of need for intervention and assessment for diagnosis. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the total number of private tenants in receipt of Local Housing Allowance; (ii) the number of claimants currently facing a shortfall between their Local Housing Allowance payment and actual rent costs; and (iii) the average shortfall cost, for each of the last three years.
answer_text: The Housing Executive advises that they are only able to confirm the number of Housing Benefit claimants assessed under Local Housing Allowance (LHA) and cannot confirm how many did not have their rent fully covered.They can confirm that as at 31st May 2022 there were 27,096 Housing Benefit claimants who had Housing Benefit assessed under LHA. In relation to the Private Rented Sector receiving Housing Costs via Universal Credit the figures are outlined below.As at the end of March 2022:(i) There were 32,673 households in the Private rented sector in receipt of Universal Credit(ii) Of these, 26,797 were in receipt of an LHA-capped housing payment that was lower than their declared rent(iii) The average shortfall between rent and LHA was £119.17As at the end of March 2021:(i) There were 32,236 households in the Private rented sector in receipt of Universal Credit(ii) Of these, 25,941 were in receipt of an LHA-capped housing payment that was lower than their declared rent(iii) The average shortfall between rent and LHA was £120.17As at the end of May 2020 (please note May 2020 figures used as due to large influx of claims in March/April 2020 related to Covid there were a substantial number of unverified housing costs that were causing anomalies in the data)(i) There were 26,742 households in the Private rented sector in receipt of Universal Credit(ii) Of these, 20,904 were in receipt of an LHA-capped housing payment that was lower than their declared rent(iii) The average shortfall between rent and LHA was £122.41

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment on the effectiveness of routine HPV screening in the prevention of cervical cancer.
answer_text: The National Screening Committee (NSC) have advised that Human papillomavirus (HPV) screening is effective and cost effective in the longer term.  Following this consideration, the Northern Ireland Screening Committee (NISC) has recommended a move to HPV screening in Northern Ireland and it will be introduced as part of the implementation of the Cancer Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the Western Health and Social Care Trust conducts HPV screening as part of their Cervical Screening Programme.
answer_text: Human papillomavirus (HPV) screening as part of the Cervical Screening Programme has not yet been introduced in Northern Ireland.The Cancer Strategy for Northern Ireland launched in March 2022, includes the action that HPV testing will be introduced into Northern Ireland’s Cervical Screening Programme, with project work beginning in 2022/23. My Department is currently working with the Public Health Agency (PHA) and Regional Pathology Network to progress this action.  It is anticipated that this will be partially completed in 2022/2023, but is dependent on the confirmation of budgets to support this service change.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether consideration has been given to treating Maternity Allowance as earned income, similar to the policy applied to Statutory Maternity Pay, for people in receipt of Universal Credit.
answer_text: If the North decides to take a different approach regarding the treatment of Maternity Allowance in Universal Credit, it will require Executive approval as any additional costs incurred will fall to the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what engagement she has had with her counterparts in the UK Goverment about (i) improving the rate of Maternity Allowance; and (ii) ensuring that claimants who are also in receipt of Universal Credit are not left financially worse off.
answer_text: I refer you to response to AQW 180/22-27 in relation to the increase of all benefits.  You will also be aware that the continued absence of a functioning Executive and agree Executive budget is have serious consequences for all Departments. Therefore, all options continue to be explored.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether she has considered the introduction of a Child Payment, similar to the provision made in Scotland, to assist low-income families with a child under the age of six years.
answer_text: I appointed an independent panel to complete a comprehensive review of existing welfare mitigation measures and to consider a future mitigation package. As part of this work the independent panel were specifically asked to consider the impact of welfare reforms on household income, including on children.The panel engaged with a wide range of people who access the social security system and with key stakeholders so that their voices are heard on these important issues. The panel has provided its final report to my Department and this will be published at the earliest opportunity.The implementation of any new payment schemes will require Executive agreement on the allocation of necessary funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether she has considered replacing the Sure Start Maternity Grant with a more comprehensive support package, similar to the Best Start Grant available in Scotland.
answer_text: I appointed an independent panel to complete a comprehensive review of existing welfare mitigation measures and to consider a future mitigation package. As part of this work the independent panel were specifically asked to consider the impact of welfare reforms on household income, including on children.The independent panel has provided its final report to my Department and this will be published at the earliest opportunity.The implementation of any new payment schemes will require Executive agreement on the allocation of necessary funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what further engagement she has had with her counterparts in the UK Government to restore the Local Housing Allowance to the 30th percentile.
answer_text: The Secretary of State for Work and Pensions announced that Local Housing Allowance (LHA) rates would be frozen at the rate determined for 2020/21 and would remain at this level in future years.  I wrote to Therese Coffrey, Secretary of State for Work and Pensions, requesting that urgent consideration be given to increasing the rates.The Member can be assured that I will continue to highlight the absolute need to urgently increase LHA rates, with DWP..

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the established Community Perinatal Mental Health team in the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Recruitment is underway for the community perinatal mental health team in the Western Health and Social Care Trust. A Team Lead and Consultant Psychiatrist have been appointed and recruitment for the remaining core team is continuing.  Once these positions have been appointed, the community perinatal service will be in a position to commence accepting referrals and offer a consultancy service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the 0.5-1.5 per cent pay increase announced in January 2022 for Health Social Care staff will be implemented.
answer_text: BSO Payroll team currently anticipate that payment will be made in August salaries.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether consideration has been given to increasing the mileage allowance for volunteers at HM Prison Maghaberry.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Prison Service engages a range of voluntary organisations that provide volunteers for work with people during their sentence and in the important period post release.  Volunteers prove invaluable in the assistance of reducing offending through supporting the management and rehabilitation of offenders.  Of these volunteers only one group, the Independent Monitoring Board (IMB) Members, have terms and conditions that fall under the remit of the Department of Justice.IMB members are entitled to recover expenses relating to travel and subsistence in line with the Northern Ireland Civil Service Travel and Subsistence Policy, which falls within the remit of the Department of Finance.  The member may therefore wish to direct his question to the Minister of Finance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the total amount of money sought by Northern Ireland Housing Executive from leaseholders for maintenance schemes programmed for (i) 2021-22; and (ii) 2022-23.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has provided the following figures; for the total value of actual costs invoiced to leaseholders for payment: 1/4/2021 to 31/3/2022 - £763,1751/4/2022 to 31/5/2022 - £428,762        The total value of estimated costs for works carried out for leaseholders, where actual costs have not been invoiced:1/4/2021 to 31/3/2022 - £1,365,5261/4/2022 to 31/5/2022 - £213,586It should be noted that the estimated costs of works are applicable to the works which are still due to take place, or have not yet been completed. The list of estimates provided are for those that have been sent out to leaseholders for the period in question for consultation purposes, this allows the leaseholders opportunity to contact their local office to discuss and review should it be necessary.  The Housing Executive does not invoice leaseholders until completion of works.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the support available in each Health and Social Care Trust to parents who have lost a child to stillbirth or neonatal death, including follow up support services.
answer_text: A wide range of support services for bereaved parents are available across each Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust and delivered in partnership between clinicians, bereavement coordinators and the community and voluntary sector. In cases of pregnancy loss, stillbirth or early neonatal death, all Trusts in Northern Ireland have a bereavement midwife who provides support and guidance for bereaved women, partners and their families and ensures that parents receive care that is clinically skilled, emotionally intelligent, consistent and authentically caring.  The midwife also works to ensure that all staff within the relevant health settings are educated, supported and enabled to provide sensitive, safe, effective, compassionate, kind and respectful care for families who experience such a loss. Each Trust has also had a Trust Bereavement Coordinator in place since 2006, when the HSC Bereavement Network was established to provide information, guidance and resources for those who care for and support people around the time of death and bereavement, as well as a Bereavement Forum which meets regularly with a wide membership comprising staff and service users. The HSC Bereavement Network was expanded in April 2021 to become the Northern Ireland Bereavement Network, comprising representation from across Departmental and community organisations and agencies, including two representatives from each HSC Trust, as well as third sector organisations such as SANDS, Cruse, NI Children’s Hospice and NICVA.  While the new Network’s website is under development, information on bereavement support continues to be available on the HSC Bereavement Network website at https://hscbereavementnetwork.hscni.net/ and on individual Trust websites, which provide guidance and support on a range of issues and contact information for a number of organisations which specialise in supporting bereaved parents.The vast majority of parents who experience stillbirth or neonatal death will experience normal grief and adjustment difficulties.  They are most appropriately helped by psychological input provided by primary care services, or counselling and other supports located in the community and voluntary sector.  Implementation of specialist perinatal mental health services is being implemented on a phased approach, with phase one focussing on the roll out of community perinatal mental health teams across the region.  Services in the Belfast Trust and the South Eastern Trust are operational and accepting referrals, with work being progressed in the other Trust areas to commence service delivery as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the recently announced mileage allowance increase will apply to carers and staff in the independent health sector.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the continuing impact that the increase in living costs and fuel prices is having across Northern Ireland, and particularly in relation to those who use their vehicles to deliver services as part of their day to day roles, whether that be in the statutory or the independent sector.I was pleased to recently be able to announce a mileage rate increase for Agenda for Change (AfC) staff, including Trust employed domiciliary care workers and district nurses, however this particular increase does not apply to the independent health and social care sector. Unlike the statutory sector, there is no regional rate for mileage in regards to independent sector providers, which is part of the terms and conditions offered by individual employers. The Department cannot mandate what levels of pay, or terms and conditions private employers should offer, including in regards to mileage or travel costs.However, you will be aware that on 24 November I announced a support package of up to £23m, which allowed independent domiciliary care providers to improve recruitment, employee retention, and Terms and Conditions for staff. This investment has already allowed providers to deliver enhanced rates of pay and improve other terms and conditions, which may also include increased mileage rates, at individual employers’ discretion.Despite a very challenging financial climate I have ensured that sufficient funding was provided to Trusts to ensure this uplift was carried through to the current year and made recurrent.I have tasked officials to continue engaging with the independent sector on ongoing challenges including the travel and other cost of living pressures facing staff. However, given the financial pressures and budgetary uncertainty, the room for manoeuvre in terms of additional funding is limited.

answer_text: Official measures of relative poverty are derived from the Family Resources Survey (FRS). The number of pensioners estimated to be living in relative poverty are presented in the table below.Pensioner Poverty Type2015/162016/172017/182018/192019/20Relative Poverty Before Housing Costs52,00057,00045,00043,00052,000Relative Poverty After Housing Costs35,00044,00036,00031,00038,000Further information regarding the Family Resources Survey (Households Below Average Income) can be found at the link below.https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/households-below-average-income-northern-ireland-201920

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 786/22-27, whether she has explored options to extend the provision of the JobStart scheme.
answer_text: With the continued situation of not having a functioning Executive or agreed Executive Budget, all options for the Department are under consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether she has engaged with her counterparts in Westminster on a further suspension of the Minimum Income Floor.
answer_text: I wrote to Thérèse Coffey MP, Secretary of State Department for Work and Pensions, and again in a joint letter along with the other devolved administrations, raising concerns about the restoration of the Minimum Income Floor.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the impact that shortages in PSNI staffing and resources has on the investigation of serious crime.
answer_text: Matters relating to investigations of serious crime by the PSNI, including the allocation of resources, are ultimately an operational matter for the Chief Constable of the PSNI, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board (NIPB).  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the NIPB.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether transitional payments or arrangements will be put in place for claimants impacted by plans to migrate a quarter of a million legacy benefit claims to Universal Credit by December 2024.
answer_text: Transitional Protection will provide a top-up payment equivalent to the difference between a person’s entitlement on a legacy benefit and their entitlement on Universal Credit to ensure that no one will be worse off at the point of transition to Universal Credit through Move to UC.People here claiming one of the benefits/credits being replaced by Universal Credit do not need to do anything unless their circumstances change as they will be contacted when it is time for them to move to Universal Credit.Anyone thinking about making a claim to Universal Credit can seek independent advice from the Advice NI Helpline on 0800 915 4604 and use benefit calculators on nidirect to check their entitlement.My Department’s Make the Call service can also support people with a free and confidential needs assessment, by calling 0800 232 1271; texting ADVICE to 079 8440 5248; visiting nidirect.gov.uk/makethecall (external link opens in a new window / tab) or emailing makethecall@dfcni.gov.uk. 	Income Support, Income Based Jobseeker’s Allowance, Income Related Employment & Support Allowance, Housing Benefit (Rental), tax credits

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the review of the statutory framework for the inspection of Probation Approved Premises.
answer_text: Approved Premises, which are funded and administered by the Housing Executive on behalf of the Department for Communities, play an important role in supporting rehabilitation and resettlement, as well as in safeguarding the community and mitigating risk. Officials are currently undertaking a review of the statutory framework for the inspection of those premises by Criminal Justice Inspection Northern Ireland, in consultation with the Department for Communities led Supporting People Programme Policy Group, who are similarly looking at the regulation of the Supporting People Programme more generally.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) how many people in receipt of a Severe Disability Premium have migrated from legacy benefits to Universal Credit; and (ii) how many of those were not entitled to a transitional payment, for each of the last five years.
answer_text: Between 8 October 2019 and 21 January 2021, 513 Universal Credit customers, who had previously been in receipt of Severe Disability Premium (SDP) whist on a legacy benefit, received a transitional payment. Over the same period, 6 Universal Credit customers previously in receipt of SDP were not eligible for a transitional payment.  Following a change to process in January 2021, a total of 1,408 people in receipt of an SDP moved from legacy benefits to Universal Credit. 1,300 of those received a transitional element within their UC award. 108 were not eligible for the transitional element. Due to the change in process the figures for these two periods are not directly comparable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many people in Northern Ireland are currently in receipt of a Severe Disability Premium.
answer_text: The numbers of Income Support and Pension Credit customers in receipt of a Severe Disability Premium are set out in the table below. BenefitTotal Severe Disability Premium in paymentIncome Support 2,260Pension Credit 30,920The Department does not hold data in respect of Employment Support Allowance and Jobseekers Allowance customers in receipt of a Severe Disability Premium.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what consideration she has given to establishing a Warm Discount Scheme or equivalent initiative.
answer_text: The Warm Homes Discount Scheme which currently operates in England, Scotland and Wales is funded through a Public Service Obligation placed on energy companies.  I have been advised by the Minister for the Economy that the underpinning legislation does not extend the Warm Homes Discount Scheme here.It would be for the Economy Minister, with responsibility for energy, to lead on determining whether an equivalent to the Warm Homes Discount Scheme could also be established here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many people diagnosed with an eating disorder had to travel outside Northern Ireland to access specialist hospital care, for each of the last five years.
answer_text: A total of 26 patients with a primary diagnosis of eating disorder were funded by HSC to access inpatient treatment outside of Northern Ireland over the five year period 2017/18 to 2021/22.  The DOH must be mindful of its duty to protect the privacy of its patients. Given the very small number of patients travelling each year to access treatment from highly specialist eating disorder facilities, to provide a breakdown each year has the potential to identify an individual and impact on their privacy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health which Health and Social Care Trusts are currently carrying out routine HPV screening as part of their Cervical Screening Programme.
answer_text: The Cancer Strategy for Northern Ireland launched in March 2022 and included the action that HPV testing would be introduced into Northern Ireland’s Cervical Screening Programme, with project work beginning in 2022/23. My Department is currently working with the Public Health Agency and Regional Pathology Network to progress this action.  It is anticipated that this will be partially completed in 2022/2023, but is dependent on the confirmation of budgets to support this service change.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of people that are currently in probation approved temporary or emergency accommodation in each constituency.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has advised that it does not keep specific details of an applicant’s criminal record beyond the assessment process.  The Housing Executive is mindful of the Data Protection Regulations 2018 and in compliance with this is not in a position to record details of specific offending history on any of its systems. Housing Executive staff responsible for the allocation of accommodation are aware of the need to carry out relevant checks, where applicable, as part of the Public Protection Arrangements (PPANI).  As part of PPANI the Housing Executive works with key stakeholders, including the Police Service, Probation Board (PBNI) and Prison Service, to manage the risks posed by certain sexual and violent offenders when they are released from prison into the community.PBNI within DoJ would be best placed to provide more detail on accommodation checks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) what steps have been taken by the Northern Ireland Housing Executive to resolve issues surrounding the Federation of Master Builders not providing building warranties to contractors before grant works commence; and (ii) what impact this has had on adaptation works as part of the Disabled Facilities Grant.
answer_text: The Housing Executive are acutely aware of the issues being caused for both its grant customers and contractors, and it is  engaging as a matter of urgency with FMB to resolve these matters.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the implementation of Recommendation 155 of the Gillen Review.
answer_text: I fully support Sir John Gillen’s conclusion in his Review of the Law and Procedures in Serious Sexual Offences that legislative steps need to be taken to underpin the key principle of consent as defined in Article 3 of the Sexual Offences (Northern Ireland) Order 2008: “A person consents if he agrees by choice, and has the freedom and capacity to make that choice”. The Review made a number of recommendations as to how this might be achieved. The complexities of the issues involved in taking forward these recommendations, and in developing effective legislative proposals, require significant consultation with criminal justice partners, key stakeholders and the Department’s legal advisers. A challenging legislative programme in the previous, much truncated, mandate meant that this work could not be progressed. I intend that relevant legislative proposals will be brought forward in this mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of specialist endometriosis surgeons currently employed; and (ii) for an update on the availability of excision surgery for endometriosis patients.

To ask the Minister for Communities (i) why the Northern Ireland Housing Executive only accept building warranties from contractors registered with Federation of Master Builders or Contractors Insurance Guarantee Services NI; and; (ii) whether consideration has been given to expanding the list of accepted insurance providers.
answer_text: The primary purpose of the Warranted Builders Scheme is to facilitate Housing Executive grant applicants by providing them with insurance backed protection through the warranty schemes for grant aided works.  All grant approvals with a value exceeding £5,000 must use a contractor who is registered with one of the two approved schemes.In November 2018 the Housing Executive re-advertised in the local press, its intention to continue with its established register of Warranted Builders Schemes for a further three year period to facilitate the operation of the Home Improvement Grants Scheme.  The invitation to apply was open to all companies who could meet the criteria. Following this renewal exercise the two Warranted Builder Schemes, who had already been operating under the scheme, re-applied, providing the relevant evidence to meet the criteria.  Following assessment of the submissions, including the financial standing of the companies, the two applicants were accepted into the scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 28213/17-22, for an update on the possible extension of the 12-months free dental treatment provision for expectant mothers, in light of the accessibility issues posed by the pandemic.
answer_text: The accessibility issues posed by the pandemic have been felt by all dental patients, with many still having to wait longer than normal for treatment.Under the current General Dental Services Regulations, access to Health Service dental treatment is free of charge if, on the date of the contract or arrangement for services, the person was an expectant mother, or had a baby within the previous 12 months.  This means, that exemption from dental charges is indeed for a period longer than 12 months, when pregnancy and the post-natal period are taken into consideration.An extension of free treatment for patients who were pregnant during the height of the pandemic has been considered, but the complexity of the regulatory and administrative changes required to achieve this temporary measure has precluded it from happening. The same arrangements are in place across all of the United Kingdom, ensuring equality of access regardless of the Devolved Area in which the person resides. The Department is presently focussed on increasing dental activity and access for patients.  Specific efforts are focused on addressing availability of treatment for children and unregistered patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what steps she is taking to improve the time taken for child sexual abuse cases to go to trial.
answer_text: I recognise the impact that delay in the criminal justice system can have on children, particularly those children who have suffered sexual and physical abuse.  The impact of child sexual abuse is often significant and in many cases lasts long into adulthood and I remain committed to addressing the unacceptable delays in progressing these cases.   	In order to reduce the adverse impact of delay on child victims of sexual offences, a judge-led voluntary protocol was introduced in September 2019 to expedite serious sexual offence cases involving children under 13 in the Belfast Crown Court.  This has made a positive difference to the children and their families, and has recently been extended to September 2022.  		In co-operation with justice partners and the Lady Chief Justice’s Office, my Department is building on the lessons being learned from the judge-led voluntary protocol and is leading work to develop a permanent protocol that will ensure that these benefits can be extended to all child witnesses of serious sexual offences in Northern Ireland.  However, implementation of the permanent protocol and its wider roll out will be subject to additional funding being made available. Like many other areas of public service, COVID has had a significant impact on efforts to speed up the justice system and has resulted in significant backlogs, which will take time to clear.  I recognise that the justice system not only needs to recover, but needs to address delay more generally.  In addition to the protocol which I have referred to, my Department is also collaborating with criminal justice partners on an extensive programme of work to address avoidable delay in the criminal justice system including:the development of a strategy to address the issues raised in the Northern Ireland Audit Office report which highlighted a number of structural issues within the justice system;Case Progression Officers (CPOs) have been introduced to ensure that all procedural or administrative matters in relation to Crown Court cases are progressed by the relevant agencies and that, as far as possible, any blockages or impediments to effective case progression are promptly addressed; the Disclosure Improvement Plan for NI, signed by the Police Service of NI and Public Prosecution Service, provides the framework for resolving disclosure issues that will improve public confidence in the justice system; to improve the quality of prosecution files, PSNI and PSS have agreed file standards with dedicated PSNI staff to oversee file quality and disclosure in serious sexual offence cases before submission to the PPS;the ongoing review of the Indictable Case Process for sexual offences; andthe introduction of The Criminal Justice (Committal Reform) Act, which has received Royal Assent and will see any offence triable only on indictment brought within the direct committal arrangements.Reducing the time it takes to complete criminal cases is a challenging and complex issue and reforms

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of reported child sexual abuse cases for each of the last five years.
answer_text: The recording of information on offences reported to the police is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.The Police Service of Northern Ireland publish statistics on recorded crime on a monthly basis. You may, therefore, wish to direct any question relating to the number of recorded offences involving child sexual abuse to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of sexual crimes reported; (ii) the number of cases currently awaiting trial; and (iii) the average waiting time between initial report and cases going to trial, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: The following information is provided in relation to the different parts of this question:The recording of information on offences reported to the police is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.The Police Service of Northern Ireland publish statistics on recorded crime on a monthly basis. You may, therefore, wish to direct any question relating to the number of recorded offences involving sexual offences to the PSNI.Given the question posed at i) related data for sexual offences has been provided for the answers to parts ii) and iii) of the question posed.Figures sourced from Management Information Systems indicate there were 39 cases at 1 June 2022 where the principal offence at prosecution was a sexual offence, and where a decision to prosecute had been taken by PPS but first court date after PPS decision had not yet been reached.Figures for the average waiting time between initial report and cases going to trial are as follows:Average (Median) time in days from date offence reported to first court date after PPS decision, for cases where main offence was a sexual offence, 2019/20 – 2021/222019/20     444 2020/21     311 2021/22     407 	Note:1. Figures relate to the number of calendar days between the listed dates for each table.  Court recess dates are not taken into account.2. Figures relate to cases brought on behalf of the PSNI, Harbour and Airport police in Northern Ireland that resulted in a court disposal.  Appeals are not included.3. Figures relate to the period from when an offence was reported to, or detected by, the police until the first court date after PPS decision, for cases which were dealt with at courts in a 12 month period, from 1 April to 31 March, in the years specified.4. Figures for 2021/22 are provisional.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what steps he has taken to ensure NHS patients can access dental care; and (ii) what measures are being considered to assist children who have been unable to register with a dental surgery.
answer_text: To address the issue of access the Department has continued to provide significant financial support to the sector, with changes made in recent months to more closely link support to activity.  £76m has been paid to independent GDPs over the past two years in return for increasing levels of activity.A £5m Revenue Grant Scheme was launched on 27 January 2022 for dental practices to support the cost of improving and increasing their delivery of General Dental Services. This funding is designed to improve patient safety, practice efficiency and improve the resilience of practices in order to mitigate against future transmission of aerosol based respiratory diseases, and other infectious diseases.Recent easing of Infection Prevention and Control procedures within all health care settings, along with the introduction of the Rebuilding Support Scheme (RSS) in April 2022 has already resulted in an increase in capacity within practices.The Department is also increasing the availability of appointments for non-registered patients who have urgent and emergency treatments needs. Patient registration timeframes have also been extended since the beginning of the pandemic to ensure that patients registration status is unaffected by any difficulties encountered in attending. In addition, Urgent Dental Care Centres continue to provide cover for patients with dental emergencies who cannot be seen in practice, and these centres will be reverting to Relief of Dental Pain Clinics, with increased capacity, in the coming weeks. The Chief Dental Officer is currently developing an enhanced scheme to assist unregistered children with registering with a general dental practitioner.  However, the recent General Dental Services statistics for Northern Ireland 2021/22, published on 23 June 2022, show that 72% of children in Northern Ireland are currently registered with a dentist. Indeed, almost 76,000 children received dental treatment in excess of basic care, which is a rise of 47% on 2020/21.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether people who have an open Universal Credit claim, but who do not currently receive a payment, will be entitled to the £650 Cost of Living payment.
answer_text: Information on the Cost of Living payments for people can be found at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/cost-living-support. For the first Cost of Living Payment of £326, a person must have an entitlement to a payment of Universal Credit of at least 1p in an assessment period that ended in the period 26 April 2022 to 25 May 2022. The second payment of £324 will be made in the autumn and although the qualifying assessment period has not yet been announced, it too will require an entitlement to a payment of Universal Credit of at least 1p.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the £150 Cost of Living payment for people on disability benefits, applies to claimants under the age of 18.
answer_text: The disability additional payment of £150 will be paid to all eligible claimants, including those under the age of 18.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when ophthalmology services will return to full capacity.
answer_text: The pandemic has had a negative impact on all health care and medical specialities, including ophthalmology services. Strenuous efforts are currently underway to rebuild activity in ophthalmology services including securing additional capacity from independent sector providers. Ophthalmology service provision is the responsibility of the Belfast and Western Health and Social Care Trusts with services provided to the remaining HSC Trusts on an ‘outreach’ basis. The Western Health and Social Care Trust (WHSCT) have advised the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) that they have increased all ophthalmology clinics and hope to return to pre-covid capacity by September 2022.  The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust have advised that capacity within ophthalmology services is currently impacted by IP+C measures and are unable to confirm a date or timeframe for a return to pre-covid capacity and activity for ophthalmology services. The commissioning by SPPG of new primary care optometry services and models of care (in the glaucoma and acute eye care pathways) has assisted in managing demand for ophthalmology services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many patients are currently on the waiting list for an appointment at a long Covid clinic, in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Information on the number of people who are on the waiting list for their first appointment at a long COVID clinic is not available centrally in the Department. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether HPV primary screening will continue to be carried out as part of the Cervical Screening Programme.
answer_text: The Cancer Strategy, launched in March 2022 included the action that HPV testing would be introduced into Northern Ireland’s Cervical Screening Programme and it was envisaged that project work would begin in 2022/23. My Department is currently working with the Public Health Agency and Regional Pathology Network to progress this action.  It is anticipated that this will be partially completed within the next 12 months, but is dependent on confirmation of budgets to support this service change.I am very conscious of pressing demands for extra funding across many areas of health and social care.  As Minister, I will continue to do the best I can to deliver for the public with the resources I have available.  Amid ongoing budget uncertainty, I am on record as warning that funding pressures in health may be significant by the second half of 2022/23. The financial situation will undoubtedly be constrained whatever the final budget settlement.  My ability to plan strategically is being significantly impaired by the ongoing budgetary uncertainty. Funding elements of the health service on a month to month or quarter to quarter basis is not an efficient or effective way to build for the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the absence of an Executive will impact the proposed one-off £300 Winter Fuel payment for pensioner households.
answer_text: Despite the absence of a functioning Executive or agreed Executive budget, my department will continue to engage with DWP and make the necessary arrangements to ensure the proposed one-off £300 Winter Fuel payment for pensioner households is available here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline the protocol for naming or renaming government owned buildings.
answer_text: There is no formal protocol for naming or renaming government owned buildings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on her Department's work to develop further reforms within the private rented sector, including rent controls.
answer_text: The Department is currently taking forward work to bring all Sections of the Private Tenancies Act into operation. Further exploration of future reform is ongoing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for an update on the Engage II Programme; and (ii) whether this will continue beyond the end of this academic year.
answer_text: The Engage programme has been extremely successful to date, with more than £24 million provided to 1,700 educational settings this academic year.  Engage has ensured that our children and young people have been provided with a range of support to reduce the impact which the Covid-19 pandemic has had on their educational experience and learning. I issued a Direction to my officials for the Engage programme to continue to December 2022.  The Department of Finance approved that Direction on 6 July 2022, and I am delighted that I have been able to allocate £10million to continue the programme until the end of December 2022.   I have recently written to schools and settings to inform them.It is my intention that the programme will continue until the end of the academic year. However, as this remains subject to the necessary funding being made available, settings are not able to commit to expenditure beyond December 2022. I will update schools on the budget position for the rest of the academic year as soon as I am in a position to do so.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for her assessment of the current Discretionary Support provision; and when her Department will implement the recommendations of the Discretionary Support Review Panel.
answer_text: Since the introduction of Discretionary Support the number of people supported and the amount awarded has increased significantly with provisional data for the 2021/22 financial year showing that almost 78,000 awards of Discretionary Support were made with a value of £28.1 million.The report of the independent review of Discretionary Support has been published on my Department’s website.  I hope to make an announcement soon on how I propose to improve Discretionary Support by making it more accessible and ensuring that emergency financial support is there for people when they need it most.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the number of (i) successful; and (ii) unsuccessful applications made to Discretionary Support, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: Information can be found via the following linkhttps://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/welfare-supplementary-payments-discretionary-support-standards-advice-assistance-and-sanctions

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of people in emergency accommodation for each of the last five years, broken down by (i) constituency; (ii) accommodation type and; (iii) households with children.
answer_text: The Housing Executive which has statutory responsibility for responding to Homelessness does not record the requested data by Parliamentary constituency, but records data on the basis of local council area. Due to the large volume of data requested, tables outlining the number of people in emergency accommodation for each of the last five years, broken down by (i) council area (ii) accommodation type and; (iii) households with children have been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the associated projected costs with the renaming of his Department's headquarters from Ballykelly House to Jubilee House.
answer_text: The costs associated with the renaming of my headquarters building from Ballykelly House to Jubilee House are approximately £1,000.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people presenting to emergency departments seeking treatment for complications arising from cosmetic procedures, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust for each of the last five years.
answer_text: Information on the number of people presenting to emergency departments seeking treatment for complications arising from cosmetic procedures is not available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how much has been spent on Discretionary Support in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Information can be found at https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/welfare-supplementary-payments-discretionary-support-standards-advice-assistance-and-sanctions

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the current standards and protections within the non-surgical cosmetic industry.
answer_text: Under the Independent Health Care Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2005, the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA) regulate clinics providing certain laser or intense pulsed light cosmetic treatments and these providers are required to undergo the appropriate training etc. However, those carrying out treatments such as Botox or dermal fillers are not required to register with RQIA.  My Department’s Medicines Regulatory Group (MRG) carried out an enforcement and compliance programme into the provision of Botox over a 6 month period. Initial investigations and assessments have been conducted into over 200 individual providers of Botulinum Toxin Type A.  Those in breach of legislation have been issued formal advice and warning notices where appropriate and have moved into legal compliance in the vast majority of cases investigated.  In addition, formal referrals have been made to professional bodies, e.g. GMC where appropriate.  The MRG continues to monitor the situation.My Department is aware of the changing health and social care environment, notably around cosmetic treatments, and has carried out a fundamental review of health and social care regulatory framework.  My officials have developed a new draft regulatory policy that includes the principles of regulation, along with the broad scope of services to be regulated and the proposal that the regulator should have wider powers of enforcement etc.   This work has been the first phase of the process before moving on to phase 2. As part of my Department’s response to the pandemic the launch of the consultation on phase 1 was delayed.  Before a draft policy is consulted upon it will be subject to further review taking into account any additional lessons from the pandemic and any other regulatory and quality issues that have emerged over the past few years.It is my intention that a Review of Regulation will be taken forward during the current mandate of the Assembly.  This Review of Regulation will also include an assessment of current standards in order to consider whether any further assurances are required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps he has taken to improve autism spectrum disorder services for adults.
answer_text: My Department is aware that services and support for adults who are waiting for assessment or who have received an autism diagnosis within the health and social care sector must be addressed.  This was highlighted as an action in the Autism – Interim Strategy for the years 2021 and 2022 and a review of services was to be undertaken by the Health and Social Care Board and recommendations made to the Department. Whilst this work has been delayed as a result of the pandemic, my Department will be engaging with Health and Social Care colleagues to obtain a clear picture regarding how consistency in provision of service and support across the region can be improved. However, it is clear that additional resource, both in funding and workforce, will be required to progress activity alongside a strategic planning and commission approach to understanding population need, current service provision, gaps in service and alternative models of care and support for adults with presenting need.It should also be noted that where support and advice is required by an individual, Trusts can signpost to service providers and support groups within their respective area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail what steps she has taken to increase resources within (i) special schools;  and (ii) special education provision in mainstream schools.
answer_text: Since the formation of the most recent Executive in January 2020 through to March 2022, my Department has allocated c£178.2m of additional resource funding to meet Special Educational Needs (SEN).  This additional funding has directly benefited special schools by c£27.7m (c£24.6m funding allocated via the Education Authority’s (EA) Block Grant and £3.1m of earmarked funds for the implementation of the new SEN Framework and the requirements of the SEND Act NI 2016).Special education provision in mainstream schools has benefited from c£117.4m of additional funding (c£61.2m funding allocated via the EA Block Grant and £56.2m of earmarked funds for the implementation of the new SEN Framework and the requirements of the SEND Act NI 2016). The balance of the £178.2m, being c£33.1m, allocated via the EA Block Grant to address SEN pressures for Pupil Support and SEN Transport.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many pension households are entitled to, but not claiming, (i) Pension Credit; and (ii) Housing Benefit.
answer_text: My Department released experimental statistics on the uptake of Pension Credit in October 2020. This publication highlighted that for 2017/18, it is estimated that there were 30,900 families potentially entitled to Pension Credit and not receiving it. The full report can be found here: https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/system/files/publications/communities/dfc-pension-credit-estimates-of-benefit-take-up-oct20.pdfFurther research on barriers to Pension Credit uptake, is due to report in the Autumn 2022.Information is not held on how many pension households are entitled to but not claiming Housing Benefit.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the number of people in receipt of Pension Credit.
answer_text: The information requested is included in the supplementary tables of the latest Pension Credit summary statistics and can be found here: 	https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/system/files/publications/communities/bss-pension-credit-feb-2022.xlsx

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether she has considered introducing a carer's supplement for people in receipt of Carer's Allowance, similar to the provision in Scotland.
answer_text: An Independent Advisory Panel was been appointed to complete a comprehensive review of welfare mitigations. The panel engaged with a wide range of people who access the social security system and with key stakeholders (including carers) so that their voices are heard on these important issues. The panel has provided its final report to my Department and this will be published at the earliest opportunity.The implementation of any new payment schemes will require Executive agreement on the allocation of necessary funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what engagement she has had with the Department for Work and Pensions with regards to reviewing the qualifying date for the Winter Fuel Payment.
answer_text: DWP administers and manages the Winter Fuel Payment on behalf of the Department for Communities.I continue to consider any additional ways to help people, and this includes ongoing engagement with DWP.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether she has given further consideration to reviewing the current rate of the Winter Fuel Payment.
answer_text: I continue to consider any additional ways to help people through this cost of living crisis.  However, any considerations that impact the Block Grant require Executive approval which unfortunately cannot be facilitated due to the continued absence of a functioning Executive and agree Executive budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether she will consider the inclusion of a specific aim which addresses regional disparity as part of the development process of a future arts and culture strategy.
answer_text: The development of a new strategy is underway through a co-design process.  The strategy will be underpinned by promoting equality of opportunity and access for all.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when gynaecology services will resume at full capacity.
To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 398/22-27, when the pay award will be implemented.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has advised that it anticipates that the pay award will be applied in the June 2022 salary. The revised rates apply from 1 April 2021 and any backpay will also be included in the June salary.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what measures are in place to support the mental wellbeing of unplaced post-primary school children and their parents.
answer_text: Emotional health and wellbeing is high priority for my Department and this has been even more so the case since the pandemic which has significantly impacted on the lives of our children and young people.In February 2021, my Department and the Department of Health (DoH) published the ‘Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework’ which aims to:-Ensure children and young people are empowered and assisted to understand and take care/manage their emotional health and wellbeing.Identify needs early and address effectively when required.Establish an integrated model that supports an early help, support and intervention focus.Reduce the numbers of children and young people requiring specialist intervention from Mental Health Services.A number of initiatives under the Framework may be particularly beneficial to support children’s mental health at key points such as the transition from primary to post-primary school and can provide support for parents as well.  These include:-Text A Nurse service - a secure and confidential text messaging service for young people aged 11-19 providing access to a school nurse.  REACH – ‘Resilience in Education, Assisting Change to Happen’ programme for 6-19 year olds to develop positive emotional health, increase their readiness for learning and build their resilience.Healthy Happy Minds Therapeutic and Counselling Services pilot – providing therapeutic and counselling support for primary school pupils. School pastoral care teams and the Education Authority’s (EA) Youth Service Youth Online are also available to provide emotional help and support in a safe and secure way.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when gastrointestinal surgeries will resume in the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Western HSC Trust has confirmed that these surgeries did not cease, albeit they reduced due to the pandemic.  The Western HSC Trust is working on a reset of all services, actively engaging with the independent sector to maximise capacity for all day-case outpatients across all areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of unplaced post-primary school children this year, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) publishes details in respect of children for whom a post-primary application was submitted, but who are yet to secure a place.  The information is updated regularly and can be found by using the following link: https://www.eani.org.uk/publications/breakdown-of-statistics-re-p7-pupils-who-have-yet-to-be-placed-0

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail what steps she will take to increase the number of places at girls only Catholic Maintained post-primary schools, particularly in Foyle.
answer_text: My Department is continuing to work with schools in areas where there is pressure on Year 8 places to secure additional capacity to cater for demand.  This includes at single-sex girls’ schools in the denominational sector where there is a need.  A total of 96 additional Year 8 places have been provided in Foyle to date, with 52 of these at denominational girls’ schools.
