question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) what engagement she has had with the Chancellor on the cost of living support package; and (ii) whether her Department is in the process of developing future support schemes.
answer_text: Finance Minister, Conor Murphy is committed to working with Treasury to ensure the Energy Bills Support Scheme will provide assistance to citizens here given the absence of a functioning Executive and agreed Executive budget.I am deeply frustrated that the lack of a functioning Executive and agreed budget prevents any further cost of living support measures being introduced. However, I remain determined that my Department will continue to deliver support for those who are in need and I have tasked my officials to fully explore and expedite all options to address both the immediate needs of, and medium to longer term solutions to, the cost of living crisis.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the 150 Community and Voluntary organisations that will benefit from the £1,500 energy payment.
answer_text: A list of those organisations who received the £1,500 energy payments provided through my Department has been published on the DfC website at:https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/energy-payment-provided-grassroots-organisations-impacted-by-soaring-costs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what consideration he has given to developing a lifelong learning bill which would allow those with caring responsibilities to access skills development and education throughout their lives.
answer_text: Whilst I currently have no plans to introduce a lifelong learning bill, I fully recognise the role education and skills development plays in improving social equality. My Department’s new Skills Strategy, ‘Skills for a 10x Economy’, has lifelong learning at its heart and highlights the need to improve access to education and training opportunities, by alleviating cost and time constraints, both of which I appreciate are key concerns for those with caring responsibilities.As such, policy objective two of the Strategy has been designated as ‘Creating a Culture of Lifelong Learning’, with the key aim of creating a Lifelong Learning Action Plan. My officials will be working with colleagues in the Department for Communities and other Departments to scope out the how the recommendations underpinning the Action Plan can be taken forward and to determine key priorities. A broad range of provision already exists in this area and so our recommendations do not focus on the creation of new programmes, but on increasing awareness of existing provision and improving access to it. The Strategy covers the next ten years and will be implemented through two, five and ten year plans and therefore activities will need to be slotted into these plans depending on available resources. A sub-Committee of the Northern Ireland Skills Council has also been established to consider Equality, Diversity and Inclusion issues and will be reporting to the main Council, with a view to advising on how skills needs in relation to social inclusion more generally can be addressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the total number of individuals on the social housing waiting list for ground floor accommodation, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has provided the following table detailing the number of applicants on the social housing waiting list for ground floor accommodation, broken down by parliamentary constituency, as at 31st March 2022.Parliamentary ConstituencyNo. of Applicants who Require Ground Floor AccommodationBelfast East315Belfast East/South86Belfast North576Belfast South322Belfast South/Strangford13Belfast West518Belfast West/North19Belfast West/South<10East Antrim485East Derry484Fermanagh South Tyrone342Foyle669Lagan Valley358Mid Ulster214Newry & Armagh429North Antrim489North Down615South Antrim310South Down451Strangford386Upper Bann706West Tyrone443Please note that where there are less than 10 applicants these have been listed as <10 in compliance with Data Protection requirements. The following Common Landlord Areas (CLAs) cross Parliamentary Constituency Boundaries:Housing Exec                                                        CLA                                                        Parliamentary ConstituencyCregagh Castlereagh                                       Belfast East/SouthWillowfield/Upper Castlereagh Road               Belfast East/SouthCarryduff                                                          Belfast South/Strangford Ainsworth                                                         Belfast West/NorthTwaddell/Upper Woodvale                               Belfast West/NorthHamill St / John St                                            Belfast West/SouthCarrick Hill / Unity Flats                                    Belfast West/NorthBridge End / Rotterdam Court                          Belfast East/South

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether she will consider the introduction of a Children's Future Fund which would provide £500 to every child at birth, with an additional top up of £500 when they reach ten years old.
answer_text: I appointed an independent panel to complete a comprehensive review of existing welfare mitigation measures and to consider a future mitigation package. As part of this work the independent panel were specifically asked to consider the impact of welfare reforms on household income, including on children.	The implementation of any new payment schemes will require Executive agreement on the allocation of necessary funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health if he will consider improving support provided to expectant mothers to include (i) ante-natal nurturing programmes; (ii) support groups for new parents; and (iii) direct referral to additional services where required.
To ask the Minister for Communities for her assessment of the effectiveness of the JobStart Scheme in helping young people aged 16–24 years who are at risk of long term unemployment.
answer_text: My Department’s JobStart Scheme has been effective in helping young people aged 16-24 who are at risk of long term unemployment. Since its launch, 2,139 employer applications have been approved by my Department offering 4,218 JobStart opportunities. At its peak there were over 1,500 opportunities available on my Department’s vacancy portal representing all business sectors and all council areas providing a wide variety of jobs for young people to choose from.As of 31 May 2022, 1,427 young people had started the Scheme. Early destination data for those completing the scheme is positive with 75% of total completers sustaining employment with their JobStart employer or using their skills and experience gained in their JobStart opportunity to move straight into employment with another employer.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to provide an update on (i) the expansion of Ulster University Magee Campus; and (ii) the removal of the Maximum Student Number cap.
answer_text: (i) Ulster University welcomed its first cohort of students to its Graduate Entry Medical School in August 2021, and will enrol a further intake for Academic Year 2022/23 as well as students on its undergraduate Health Sciences programme which is relocating from the Jordanstown campus.My Department is in discussion with the University in relation to its strategic objectives for growth across all its campuses.  (ii) Given the budgetary situation there is no, nor likely to be in the near future, funding available to remove the Maximum Student Number cap which acts as a cost control measure.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether households here will be entitled to the full £400 energy bill discount announced by the Chancellor as part of the Cost of Living Support package.
answer_text: The Chancellor announced that Treasury would deliver equivalent support to people in the North in relation to the Energy Bill Support Scheme.Following discussions with the Chief Secretary to the Treasury I have committed to working with him to ensure the Energy Bills Support Scheme will deliver equivalent support to people here given the absence of an Executive. Discussions are ongoing between my officials and Treasury about the support to be provided to households in relation to their energy bills.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how the cost of living support package announced by Chancellor on 26 May 2022 will be distributed in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I refer the member to the answer provided to AQW 626/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what steps he has taken to ring-fence funds generated from the Soft Drinks Industry Levy, for obesity prevention and treatment.
answer_text: As I have previously advised, revenue raised from the Soft Drinks Industry Levy is not formally linked by the British Government to any specific spending. Spending in Whitehall departments for children’s food and to promote children’s health is allocated through Spending Reviews. The Executive receives a Barnett consequential on any changes in funding provided to Whitehall departments. For Spending Reviews this is provided at a departmental level rather than by specific programme. Therefore my department is not informed of how much the Executive receives as a result of the Levy. It would be for an Executive to agree to ring-fence funding for obesity prevention and treatment and to determine the quantum of funding to be allocated. As Finance Minister I remain willing to consider proposals that will help address this issue; however, in the absence of an Executive, it is not possible to agree to such ring-fencing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for her assessment of the 40 per cent increase in food bank use in Northern Ireland since 2019.
answer_text: My Department has been running a Social Supermarket Pilot Programme at five sites since October 2017. A Social Supermarket provides holistic support through the provision of food alongside wraparound services to address the root causes of poverty. Following positive evaluation I have approved the expansion of the social supermarket approach and a budget of £1.5m has been allocated to support this work in 2022/23.  This is being taken forward through a co-design process with each Council and their local stakeholders to design a model that best meets local need.I am deeply frustrated that the lack of a functioning Executive and agreed budget prevents any further cost of living support measures being introduced. However, I remain determined that my Department will continue to deliver support for those who are in need and I have tasked my officials to fully explore and expedite all options to address both the immediate needs of, and medium to longer term solutions to, the cost of living crisis.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the number of people from outside the Foyle constituency (i) who have been allocated temporary accommodation in Foyle since 2020; and (ii) who have subsequently secured permanent housing in the area.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has advised that it does not record the data in the format requested.  The Housing Executive has advised that it does not record placements made by location (in this case local council area) as an identifier on its Housing Management System.  All placements are identified by the Housing Solutions team from which the placement is made.As placements are not recorded by the area to which they are made in the same manner, a case by case examination of each placement would be required in order to answer this question and it would not be possible to do so without incurring disproportionate costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the £500 Health and Social Care Special Recognition Payment has been paused; and to outline the rationale for this decision.
answer_text: Phase 2 had been temporarily paused to allow officials to seek clarity on a number of issues raised relating to the impact of the increase in national insurance contributions which are included as part of the payment provided to the former employer.  A further letter was issued to employers on 14 April 2022 providing clarity on the issues raised and confirming recommencement of the payment process. Claims are being processed by BSO in date order when all relevant information is provided by the claimants. No date has been set for the closure of the scheme and claims will continue to be processed by the Business Services Organisation during 2022/23. I am not in a position to provide a date as to when all payments will be made.I can confirm that as at 26 May 2022, a total of over £16.6m has been paid out under Phase 1 to independent sector care home and domiciliary care providers covering 28,457 individuals. As at the same date over £3.3m has been paid out to 7,999 individuals under Phase 2; these payments include staff previously employed in independent sector care home and domiciliary care providers who have since moved employer or left the sector, and staff employed with eligible day opportunity providers and buildings-based day services.In addition we have also opened the Scheme to eligible applicants in the following groups, Personal Assistants employed by Independent Living Fund recipients, Thalidomide Health Grant recipients, and Personal Assistants employed by those in receipt of Direct Payments from HSC Trusts. This element of the scheme was launched on 23 March and is being delivered by ILF Scotland for the payments, Scotland Excel who set up and run the online portal, and SDS Scotland who manage the dedicated helpline. These delivery partners were heavily involved in delivering the equivalent scheme for the Scottish Government.  To date £300k has been paid to 863 individuals. To encourage uptake on this element Trusts have now written to all individuals in receipt of direct payments. My officials are also finalising arrangements to include eligible staff whose employers are no longer trading and I hope to be in a position to include this cohort of individuals in the coming weeks.A FAQ on the Special Recognition Payment has been published on the Department’s website as below:-https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/news/hsc-staff-recognition-payment-faqsThis continues to be updated with relevant information as each phase of the claims process progresses. I remain personally committed to ensuring the recognition payment will be paid to independent sector staff as soon as it is practicable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what consideration he has given to (i) increasing the domiciliary care rate for Direct Payment clients; and (ii) addressing the position of personal assistants, as part of the £23 million domiciliary care funding package announced in November 2021.
answer_text: (i) As you will be aware, Direct Payment funding is provided in lieu of statutory provided services and is calculated on the basis of individual assessed need. Direct Payment hourly rates are adjusted each April to reflect changes such as increases to the National Minimum Wage.The £23m support package which I announced in November was primarily designed as a response to the pressures facing domiciliary care, which has resulted in providers being unable to deliver some care packages as well as having a knock on effect on primary care services. Staffing pressures have also continued to be experienced in care homes. This funding helped to stabilise the sector and support staff retention during a very difficult period. The funding package that has been rolled out does not extend to direct payments. However, I am considering if there would be scope to address direct payment rates which are an extremely important part of our care system, particularly in allowing for a person centred approach, whilst also ensuring that an individual’s assessed social care needs are fulfilled.As you are aware, we face and are likely to continue to face, an extremely challenging fiscal environment and the absence of an agreed three year budget makes this position even more challenging.(ii) In regards to the position of personal assistants, more widely, the Department has recently launched a consultation on the Reform of Adult Social Care. The consultation document recognises that there is further scope for self-directed support to achieve its full potential. It therefore makes a number of proposals in relation to direct payments and support for personal assistants. I look forward to seeing what views emerge on these issues as the consultation progresses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people diagnosed with an eating disorder in each of the last three years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The number of referrals accepted to adults eating disorder services and to Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS) across Northern Ireland for the previous three years are presented in Table 1. This information is not aggregated at constituency and to do so would be at a disproportionate cost; therefore, the figures have been broken down by Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust.Table 1: Number of referrals accepted to adults eating disorder services and CAMHS by HSC Trust (2019/20 – 2021/22)HSC TrustYear ending 31 March202020212022Belfast*223222235Northern**165144167Southern182193261Western171170183Northern Ireland741729846Source: Monthly Non Inpatient Mental Health Returns and Monthly CAMHS MDS Returns from HSC Trusts, SPPG.*The Belfast HSC Trust provides an eating disorder service for residents of the South Eastern HSC Trust.**Proxy figures for 2021 and 2022 have been provided for the Northern HSC Trust due to limitations in data availability as a result of ongoing Information System migration.Note 1: The figures provided are in relation to referrals, not 'people'; therefore, it is possible one individual was referred multiple times.Note 2: It is not possible to disaggregate referrals received for eating disorder services in children as this is through a single point of entry for all CAMHS Services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps have been taken to improve access to eating disorder services and specialist treatments.
answer_text: Options for improving eating disorder service provision across Northern Ireland have been examined by the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG), formally the Health and Social Care Board, through the Regional Eating Disorders Network Group.  The findings of the review shaped the strategic policy direction reflected in the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 that I published in June 2021. 		Action 29(d) of the Strategy commits to enhancing the regional eating disorder service and a business case to support this action is currently being developed by SPPG.  This will be a significant first step in improving mental health services and ensuring that those with eating disorders get the care and treatment they need.	My Department also provided an additional £500k in 2021/22 to increase specialist staff across all Trusts and to implement new initiatives to improve support for young people at the earliest opportunity who are affected by eating disorders.  The initiatives implemented to date include the piloting of an intensive day treatment programme for children with mild to moderate presentations of eating disorder or eating difficulties within the Belfast Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many people under 18 years old have received treatment for eating disorders in each of the last three years.
answer_text: The number of new contacts seen within Eating Disorder Services in Children and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS) for Northern Ireland in the previous three years are presented in Table 1.Table 1: Number of new contacts seen within eating disorder services (CAMHS) in Northern Ireland (2019/20 – 2021/22)Northern Ireland Year ending 31 March202020212022New Contacts Seen within Eating Disorder Services (CAMHS)237254349Source: Monthly CAMHS MDS Returns from HSC Trusts, SPPG.Note: The figures provided are in relation to new contacts seen and not ‘people’; therefore, it is possible one young person was referred multiple times. It may also include a small number of patients aged 18 and over who attended a new contact appointment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the current provision of eating disorder treatment services in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Each Health and Social Care Trust within Northern Ireland has a specialised community based Eating Disorder Service for both young people and adults.  Inpatient services for young people with an eating disorder are based in Beechcroft Hospital, Belfast Trust and inpatient services for adults with an eating disorder are based in local Trust Mental Health Acute Inpatient Units.All Trusts work to the same regional ‘You in Mind Eating Disorder Care Pathway (2015)’ which uses a stepped model of care, commissioned to deal with the severe, enduring, and treatment resistant cases of Anorexia Nervosa, Bulimia Nervosa, and associated atypical eating disorders.  Services can range from targeted prevention, early intervention and supported self-help, specialist physical and mental health services, specialist eating disorder services, to highly intensive mental health services, which are usually intensive daily support and hospital based care.CAMHS and Adult Eating Disorder Services are part of the Regional Eating Disorder Network Group (REDNG), which includes clinicians and senior managers from all Trust Eating Disorder Teams, DoH Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG), Public Health Agency and Service User representation from the Eating Disorder Association. Options for improving eating disorder service provision across Northern Ireland have been examined by the SPPG through REDNG.  The findings of that review shaped the strategic policy direction reflected in the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 that I published on 29 June 2021.  Action 29(d) of the Strategy commits to enhancing the regional eating disorder service and a business case to support this action is currently being developed by SPPG.  This will be a significant first step in improving mental health services and ensuring that those with eating disorders get the care and treatment they need.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what steps her Department has taken to review the rate of the Winter Fuel Payment.
answer_text: The Chancellor’s announcement on 26 May means that eligible older people will receive an extra winter fuel payment of £300, but more needs to be done.I have written to the Secretary of State for Work and Pensions on this basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the implementation of the 1.75 per cent National Joint Council pay increase for Northern Ireland Housing Executive staff.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to my answer to AQW 398/22-27 on the same matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the potential increase of the basic mileage rate allowances for nursing and other Health and Social Care staff.
answer_text: In light of the recent significant increases in fuel costs, my Department, Employers and Trade Unions have been involved in national and local discussions regarding a potential review of the current Agenda for Change (AfC) Mileage Rates.The national discussions have been led through the NHS Staff Council and have been exploring the options in relation to mileage reimbursement rates. Unfortunately it has not been possible to conclude a National agreement on an increase in fuel reimbursement rates.In light of no nationally agreed position, temporary increases have been agreed in Scotland and Wales until the end of July and June respectively. At the time of those agreements, no decision could be made locally due to election period restrictions. My Department is, however, considering options for Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the amount the Northern Ireland Housing Executive has requested from leaseholders for improvement and maintenance schemes in the last year.
answer_text: Housing Executive has advised that it has requested £767,952 from leaseholders for improvement and maintenance schemes from the 1st April 2021 to 31st March 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether she has considered establishing a dedicated service to improve the uptake in benefits for people of pension age.
answer_text: My Department’s Make the Call Service aims to ensure that all individuals and their families are receiving the benefits, supports and services they are entitled to claim. This Service offers free and confidential needs assessments for individuals to check for entitlement to social security benefits, additional supports and services. Make the Call also has an extensive range of partnerships with community-based organisations and other government departments which extends its reach and accessibility. Sitting alongside this partnership offer, the Make the Call community-based Outreach Officers work with local community, charitable and voluntary groups, to raise awareness of potential benefit entitlements and provide practical help in accessing them.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an update on the review of social housing allocation policy; and (ii) how many of the proposals set out in the December 2020 Consultation Outcome Report have been implemented.
answer_text: My Department has been working with the Housing Executive to implement 18 of the 20 proposals set out in the December 2020 Consultation Outcome Report on “A Fundamental Review of Social Housing Allocations”. The implementation will be carried out using a 3 phased approach.The Housing Executive’s project team is currently progressing the following proposals within Phase 1 of the Review:  Proposal 1.  An independent, tenure-neutral housing advice     service - has been implemented.Proposal 5.  A greater choice of areas for all applicants for a social home – in progress for completion by October 2022.Proposal 16. Social landlords may withdraw an offer of accommodation in specified circumstances - in progress for completion by October 2022.Proposal 15. An applicant may receive two reasonable offers of accommodation.My officials are engaging closely with the Housing Executive particularly where proposals directly impact customers.   These changes will make the Housing Selection Scheme fairer and better at responding to objective need.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the inclusion of the Northern Ireland Housing Executive in the end of the Right to Buy scheme.
answer_text: As per my previous response to you on 24 November 2021 (AQW 25566-17-22), I have sought the Executive’s agreement to a consultation on this subject which still remains outstanding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many applications have been submitted to the Right to Buy scheme since 2020, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has provided the table below detailing house sales scheme applications received 2019/20 - 2021/22 broken down by the Housing Executive Region & District. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what engagement she has had with the Department for Work and Pensions on reversing the decision to change Support for Mortgage Interest from a benefit to a loan.
answer_text: My Department is constantly reviewing social security policy.  As part of this constant policy review, officials will continue to review Support for Mortgage Interest loan policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether she will seek additional funding or reallocate resources to (i) allow for further collaboration between the homeless sector and schools to evaluate, update and promote resources; and (ii) prepare for Homelessness Awareness week.
answer_text: Education and awareness raising on homelessness are key in addressing this very important issue. It is for that reason that DE and the Council for Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) make an ongoing contribution to the delivery of Interdepartmental Homelessness Action Plan (IDHAP), which is led by the Department for Communities. CCEA was commissioned in 2019 to produce a series of resources for schools on homelessness and these are available on the CCEA web-site along with further resources that were developed on financial capability and wellbeing.Opportunities identified by CCEA for further support, subject to funding, are set out in in the IDHAP as follows:Further collaboration with the homeless sector and schools;Updating of resources;Promotion and evaluation of resources; andWork / preparation for Homelessness Awareness week.In the absence of additional funding from Department for Communities, CCEA’s agreed action for Year 3 of the IDHAP is to maintain the existing resources and produce an analysis of their use by schools by June 2022.My Department is committed to continued cross-departmental collaboration in tackling homelessness in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Complex Lives Project; and what plans are in place for its future development beyond Belfast.
answer_text: As detailed in the recently published Homelessness Strategy 2022-27 which can be accessed at https://www.nihe.gov.uk/Documents/Homelessness-Strategy-2022-2027/Ending-Homelessness-Together-Homelessness-Strategy.aspx the intention is to evaluate and, subject to a successful evaluation, extend the Complex Lives approach.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what steps she has taken to introduce a mortgage support scheme for homeowners finding it difficult to make payments.
answer_text: During the pandemic the Executive put in place a package of measures working alongside mortgage lenders, temporary measures were implemented to assist homeowners to manage their mortgage repayments and thereby avoid mortgage arrears. Repossession proceedings were also suspended. Whilst the mortgage payment deferral scheme has ended lenders continue to provide tailored forbearance and support to borrowers who need help.  As we continue to work through the effects of the pandemic and face many economic uncertainties due to the rise in the cost of living, I am determined that my Department will do everything it can to help those in society who need our help the most.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many people, recorded as persons experiencing homelessness, presented at emergency departments in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Information on the number of people experiencing homelessness that attend emergency departments is not available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether consideration has been given to extending the Happy Healthy Minds Therapeutic and Counselling Service Pilot beyond June 2022.
answer_text: Following conclusion of the Healthy Happy Minds Therapeutic and Counselling Service Pilot in June 2022, transitional arrangements will apply to ensure no child is left unsupported. An independent evaluation of the pilot will commence in July 2022 and is expected to report by late Autumn 2022. Once received, this evaluation will inform future provision of therapeutic and counselling support at primary school level.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) whether work is underway to update the Empty Homes Strategy and Action Plan; and (ii) what consideration has been given to providing an incentive for owners to register their properties as empty.
answer_text: A Review of the Empty Homes Strategy and Action Plan was completed and published on the Department’s website in 2021.  As a result of this work the Department is now much better informed about the complexities of this issue and the reasons why so many properties appear to be vacant.It is intended that the Housing Supply Strategy will continue work to make the best use of existing assets and that there will be specific actions around housing led regeneration and adaptive reuse of existing buildings to generate additional supply. The registration of vacant domestic properties is a matter for the Department of Finance’s Land & Property Services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of vacant domestic properties in each constituency, for each of the last five years.
answer_text: The table below shows the number of vacant domestic properties in each Parliamentary Constituency recorded as vacant at the end of each of the last five rating years.Parliamentary Constituency Number of Vacant Domestic properties by rating year17/1818/1919/2020/2121/22Belfast East976841854926875Belfast North1,044997958985968Belfast South1,4411,3391,3481,3951,347Belfast West706682701718680East Antrim835863905978943East Londonderry1,3201,2871,2861,3261,297Fermanagh and South Tyrone2,1672,1542,1622,1532,148Foyle699710763787724Lagan Valley972955933973876Mid Ulster1,3571,3771,4201,4721,496Newry and Armagh1,5331,5481,6011,5921,659North Antrim1,0411,0571,1541,1681,139North Down869857886821746South Antrim781765753797729South Down1,4531,4741,5171,5361,544Strangford920916934953914Upper Bann1,0181,0209639861,050West Tyrone1,5721,5591,5921,6081,710Unallocated1,0371,0981,1641,1471,107Total21,74121,49921,89422,32121,952Since the introduction of the Rating of Empty Homes legislation on 1st October 2011, the rate liability for vacant domestic properties has been assessed at 100%.  There is currently no requirement for ratepayers to inform Land & Property Services (LPS) that their property is vacant, nor is there any financial advantage in doing so.  As a result, the information in the rating system on the number of empty domestic properties may not be current or complete.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what action she has taken to ensure an uprate in benefits is applied based on the nine per cent inflation rate.
answer_text: My Department has no power to set different rates of benefit in the annual Up-rating Order. I have spoken with Chloe Smith, the Minister for Disabled People, Health and Work as I do not believe that one-off payments go far enough. I have also written to Thérèse Coffey MP, Secretary of State for Work and Pensions stating that more must be done, including an uplift to benefits in line with inflation.I have written to the Scottish Cabinet Secretary for Social Justice, Housing and Local Government and the Welsh Minister for Social Justice seeking their support in submitting a joint request for further action to be taken.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what consideration has been given to establishing paediatric gastroenterology provision in the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Paediatric Gastroenterology is provided by Belfast Health and Social Care Trust and is located in the Children’s Hospital.   The service model is centralised in Belfast consistent with safety and sustainability requirements inherent in the provision of a highly specialist paediatric service within a population base of 1.9m.  On this basis it would not be possible to establish an additional service in a second Trust in Northern Ireland.Whilst the service is centralised in Belfast there is intention to provide support to local provision where it is safe and sustainable to do so.  This includes specialist advice and guidance to paediatricians with an interest in gastroenterology allowing children to be managed by their local hospital.  It can also include a level of outreach working in conjunction with the local clinical team delivering specialist care for children closer to home.The Belfast service is commissioned to support 3 consultants and supporting infrastructure.  Given the very specialist nature of the service it has been challenging to maintain the 3 person team and as such arrangements for outreach provision specifically are constrained at this time due to a vacancy in the consultant team.  The service did previously outreach to the Western area and SPPG will aim to review this once the third consultant appointment is progressed.  There may also be emerging opportunities given the learning from Covid-19 particularly in relation to virtual and telemedicine approaches which could further improve local access to this specialist service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide an update on the full reopening of day centres and respite facilities.
answer_text: In February 2022, the Chief Social Work Officer wrote to Trusts to outline my decision to remobilise respite services. To date, significant efforts have been made by Trusts to implement the Public Health Agency Pathway to manage risk, while increasing service provision on a phased basis, throughout a period of high community prevalence of Covid-19. The latest position reported by Trusts shows that the majority have increased availability in adult day care to at least 70% of pre-pandemic capacity. However, this has not been reflected in the volume of attendances as a number of service users have been unable to attend due to health protection guidance that outlines that close contacts should not attend a Health & Social Care setting for a period of 10 days. Short Breaks provision across statutory health & social care settings has significantly increased. However, overall capacity has reduced as a number of respite beds available in care homes have been withdrawn by independent sector providers. Transport continues to be provided on a reduced basis as there is limited scope to increase capacity while adhering to 1m physical distancing guidelines, aligned to recommendations by the World Health Organisation.While it is clear that the Pathway has been an enabler to remobilise services, there remains further adjustments to public health guidance required before services can be fully restore to pre-pandemic levels. Therefore, my officials have written to the PHA asking them to commence an urgent formal review of the Pathway and associated health protection guidelines. Aligned to this process, the Department has requested performance and capacity data across all Trusts, which will be published on the Department’s website in June 2022. I have clearly outlined that this data will be collected and published on a regular basis to monitor remobilisation against rebuild timeframes. Furthermore, I will commit to issuing a periodic communication to all MLAs to outline progress made by the Trusts. It is regrettable that services have yet to be fully restored to pre-pandemic capacity and I sincerely apologise to those families that continue to receive a partial service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the implementation of an updated Maternity Strategy.
To ask the Minister of Health how many applications have been accepted as part of the Cross Border Healthcare Directive since 2020.

To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) the number of (a) children; and (b) adults currently waiting to access overnight respite care; and (ii) the number of overnight respite beds available in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Department does not hold this information centrally and it was requested from the Health and Social Care Trusts. Please find information detailed below. Northern HSC Trust were unable to provide a response in the timeframe provided. Please note that the figures provided from the HSC Trusts are compiled from varying categories of care, and some categories of care are only available for some of the years requested. Belfast HSC Trust provided information for children’s, learning disability, physical and sensory disability and older people’s respite services, and recorded that the service was not applicable to mental health provision in their Trust. South Eastern HSC Trust provided information for adult disability, older people’s services, and mental health respite services. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Sexual Orientation Strategy and ban on conversion therapy.
answer_text: My Department continues to lead a significant programme of work in the co-design of the Executive’s four social inclusion strategies including the Sexual Orientation (LGBTQI+) Strategy. The drafting of the Strategy is ongoing in readiness for presentation to an incoming Executive. I am committed to the delivery of a meaningful strategy.		I agreed to take the lead in the process to bring forward legislation to ban so called conversion practices here and work is progressing. 		We need to get this policy right, and we need to ensure we produce effective legislation. We need to consider how we can help and support those who have been most impacted and how we can start to repair the damage so called conversion therapy has done.			Engagement is continuing with key stakeholders to ensure the legislation is robust in protecting those we wish to protect.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) how many applicants availed of the Emergency Fuel Support Scheme; and (ii) whether her Department intends to reopen this provision given the cost of living emergency,
answer_text: (i) 20,142 applicants received support from this Scheme operated by Bryson Care. This information has been provided by Bryson Care(ii) I am fully committed to supporting people in need. I would like this scheme to reopen and I have been assured by the Finance Minister there is money available, however this requires Executive approval. I am deeply frustrated that the absence of a functioning Executive prevents support for this essential scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the amount spent on agency nursing staff in each Health and Social Care Trust, in each of the last three years.
To ask the Minister of Health whether consideration has been given to extending the Cross-Border Healthcare Directive beyond 30 June 2022.
answer_text: You will be aware that in June 2021, I announced a new Cross-Border Healthcare scheme (ROI Reimbursement Scheme) for a 12 month period from 30th June 2021 as the Cross Border Healthcare Directive no longer applies to the UK.  This initiative, part of the Elective Care Framework, is aimed at reducing Northern Ireland waiting lists.  My officials are preparing a paper for me to consider options for the end of the 12 month scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has committed to a review of the Hospital Travel Scheme to include parents on low incomes whose children require inpatient hospital care.
answer_text: My Department intends to review both the Help with Health Costs and the Hospital Travel Costs schemes.  This review will be primarily to take forward the necessary legislative amendments due to the introduction of Universal Credit.  Part of the review will involve comparisons with other GB jurisdictions and will give us the opportunity to consider if the schemes require further amendments to support people on low incomes with their families healthcare costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the New Decade, New Approach commitment to provide three funded cycles of IVF treatment.
answer_text: An essential first step towards implementing this welcome NDNA commitment has been the stabilisation of waiting times for first cycles of IVF.  Non-recurrent funding from my Department has enabled the Regional Fertility Centre (RFC) to restore these to pre-pandemic levels through a successful waiting list initiative in 2021/22. However, I have always been clear that providing up to three cycles of IVF treatment for those who are eligible will require sustained investment to enable the RFC to deliver greater volumes of treatment.  Whilst work is ongoing to develop the necessary business case and implementation plan for this, the member will be aware that long term affordability remains a major issue in the absence of an agreed Executive budget for the next three years. Once funding is in place, it is likely that a phased approach to firstly two, and then three cycles will be adopted.  This is to ensure that the service is not overwhelmed by demand and that waiting lists do not increase significantly, as well as the need to ensure there is adequate capacity to deliver, including within the independent sector initially. I recognise that many women are keen to get clarity on their individual position.  Once there is clarity on future funding and implementation, a Frequently Asked Questions guide will be developed to assist patients to understand the new arrangements, including eligibility.
